instruction: Can you create an image of a fantasy knight in a dramatic portrait style?
description: Illustrious XL is an illustration-focused Stable Diffusion XL model, continued from Kohaku XL Beta 5, trained by the OnomaAI Research Team. The model is fine-tuned on the large-scale annotated Danbooru2023 dataset, which includes a wide variety of character designs, styles, and artistic knowledge, making it suitable for creative and artistic AI generation tasks. Two model variants are released: Illustrious-XL-v0.1, an untuned base model intended for future model variants and research purposes, and Illustrious-XL-v0.1-GUIDED, a minimally safety-controlled model recommended for general use. The model is released under the fair public AI license, with a discouragement of usage for monetization or closed-source purposes. Users must agree to the license terms, which prohibit the use of generated content for harmful, illegal, or unethical activities. The model supports quality tags such as 'worst quality,' 'bad quality,' 'average quality,' 'good quality,' 'best quality,' and 'masterpiece (quality).' It does not have a default style, as intended for the base model. The model is not tuned for aesthetics or preferences and is designed to allow for future training of LoRAs or adapters. Safety control is recommended, and the GUIDED variant is suggested to reduce the risk of generating harmful or unintended content. The model is intended for open-source use, and any derivative models must also be open source under the same license. Usage recommendations include avoiding overuse of critical composition tags and using specific sampling methods such as Euler a with 20–28 steps and a CFG of 5–7.5. The model supports composition tags like 'upper body,' 'cowboy shot,' 'portrait,' and 'full body.' The model versions include v0.1 (May 2024), v1.0 (July 2024), v1.1 (August 2024), v2.0 (September 2024), v3 (November 2024), and v3.5 (a special variant incorporating Google’s v-parameterization). The development of the Illustrious XL series aims to improve natural language composition and image generation, with future plans including true 2K-resolution generation and better natural language alignment. The model is designed with backward compatibility in mind, supporting fine-tuning and the training of LoRAs. The Onoma AI team plans to progressively open-source previous versions and release new models with comprehensive explanations and insights.

instruction: I want to build a system that can detect if two Japanese sentences are paraphrased versions of each other. How should I proceed?
description: Sarashina-Embedding-v2-1B is a Japanese text embedding model based on a Japanese large language model. It was trained using multi-stage contrastive learning and achieved a state-of-the-art average score across 28 datasets in the Japanese Massive Text Embedding Benchmark as of July 28, 2025. The model maps sentences and paragraphs into a 1792-dimensional dense vector space and supports applications such as semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, and clustering. It is a Sentence Transformer model with a maximum sequence length of 8,192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with LlamaModel and a Pooling layer that uses the last token for pooling. The model is designed to work with Japanese text. For usage, it can be loaded using the sentence-transformers library version 4.0.2. To enhance performance, the model was created through a three-stage learning process: first, weakly-supervised learning using contrastive learning with web-crawled and open datasets; second, supervised fine-tuning with higher-quality data and multiple model variations; and third, model merging by linearly combining the weights of the top-performing models from the second stage. Evaluation results as of July 28, 2025, show an average score of 76.38 across all tasks, with 76.48 in Retrieval, 84.22 in STS, 77.14 in Classification, 86.28 in Reranking, and 52.56 in Clustering. The model is licensed under an unspecified license, and commercial use requires contacting the developers.

instruction: 이 채팅 대화에서 상대방의 감정 변화를 추적해줘: '처음엔 화났는데, 점점 말투가 부드러워졌어.'

instruction: Can you create a written summary of this audio file where the speaker is reading a poem in English?
description: Whisper is a state-of-the-art model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation, proposed in a paper by Alec Radford et al. from OpenAI. It is trained on over 5 million hours of labeled data and demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to many datasets and domains in a zero-shot setting. Whisper large-v3-turbo is a finetuned version of a pruned model. It is the exact same model, except that the number of decoding layers has been reduced from 32 to 4. As a result, the model is significantly faster, at the expense of a minor quality degradation. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. There are two flavors of Whisper model: English-only and multilingual. The English-only models were trained on the task of English speech recognition. The multilingual models were trained simultaneously on multilingual speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions to a different language from the audio. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are available as English-only and multilingual. The largest checkpoints are multilingual only. All ten of the pre-trained checkpoints are available on the Hugging Face Hub. The pre-trained Whisper model demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to different datasets and domains. However, its predictive capabilities can be improved further for certain languages and tasks through fine-tuning. Whisper is supported in Hugging Face Transformers. The model can be used with the pipeline class to transcribe audios of arbitrary length. It can transcribe local audio files by passing the path to the audio file when calling the pipeline. Multiple audio files can be transcribed in parallel by specifying them as a list and setting the batch_size parameter. Transformers is compatible with all Whisper decoding strategies, such as temperature fallback and condition on previous tokens. Whisper predicts the language of the source audio automatically. If the source audio language is known a-priori, it can be passed as an argument to the pipeline. By default, Whisper performs the task of speech transcription, where the source audio language is the same as the target text language. To perform speech translation, where the target text is in English, set the task to 'translate'. The model can be made to predict timestamps. For sentence-level timestamps, pass the return_timestamps argument. And for word-level timestamps, pass return_timestamps='word'. The above arguments can be used in isolation or in combination. For example, to perform the task of speech transcription where the source audio is in French, and we want to return sentence-level timestamps, the following can be used. For more control over the generation parameters, use the model + processor API directly. Whisper has a receptive field of 30 seconds. To transcribe audios longer than this, one of two long-form algorithms are required: sequential or chunked. The sequential long-form algorithm should be used in either of the following scenarios: when transcription accuracy is the most important factor, and speed is less of a consideration, or when transcribing batches of long audio files, in which case the latency of sequential is comparable to chunked, while being up to 0.5% WER more accurate. Conversely, the chunked algorithm should be used when transcription speed is the most important factor or when transcribing a single long audio file. By default, Transformers uses the sequential algorithm. To enable the chunked algorithm, pass the chunk_length_s parameter to the pipeline. The Whisper forward pass is compatible with torch.compile for 4.5x speed-ups. Note that torch.compile is currently not compatible with the chunked long-form algorithm or Flash Attention 2. We recommend using Flash Attention 2 if your GPU supports it and you are not using torch.compile. To do so, first install flash-attn. Then pass attn_implementation='flash_attention_2' to from_pretrained. If your GPU does not support Flash Attention, we recommend making use of PyTorch's scale-product-attention (SDPA). This attention implementation is activated by default for PyTorch versions 2.1.1 or greater. Once a valid PyTorch version is installed, SDPA is activated by default. It can also be set explicitly by specifying attn_implementation='sdpa'. The pre-trained Whisper model demonstrates a strong ability to generalize to different datasets and domains. However, its predictive capabilities can be improved further for certain languages and tasks through fine-tuning. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers studying robustness, generalization, capabilities, biases, and constraints of the current model. However, Whisper is also potentially quite useful as an ASR solution for developers, especially for English speech recognition. We recognize that once models are released, it is impossible to restrict access to only 'intended' uses or to draw reasonable guidelines around what is or is not research. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks. They show strong ASR results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities, particularly if fine-tuned on certain tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. We strongly recommend that users perform robust evaluations of the models in a particular context and domain before deploying them. In particular, we caution against using Whisper models to transcribe recordings of individuals taken without their consent or purporting to use these models for any kind of subjective classification. We recommend against use in high-risk domains like decision-making contexts, where flaws in accuracy can lead to pronounced flaws in outcomes. The models are intended to transcribe and translate speech, use of the model for classification is not only not evaluated but also not appropriate, particularly to infer human attributes. Our studies show that, over many existing ASR systems, the models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, as well as zero shot translation from multiple languages into English; and that accuracy on speech recognition and translation is near the state-of-the-art level. However, because the models are trained in a weakly supervised manner using large-scale noisy data, the predictions may include texts that are not actually spoken in the audio input (i.e. hallucination). We hypothesize that this happens because, given their general knowledge of language, the models combine trying to predict the next word in audio with trying to transcribe the audio itself. Our models perform unevenly across languages, and we observe lower accuracy on low-resource and/or low-discoverability languages or languages where we have less training data. The models also exhibit disparate performance on different accents and dialects of particular languages, which may include higher word error rate across speakers of different genders, races, ages, or other demographic criteria. The models also exhibit disparate performance on different accents and dialects of particular languages, which may include higher word error rate across speakers of different genders, races, ages, or other demographic criteria. The sequence-to-sequence architecture of the model makes it prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be mitigated to some degree by beam search and temperature scheduling but not perfectly. Further analysis on these limitations are provided in the paper. It is likely that this behavior and hallucinations may be worse on lower-resource and/or lower-discoverability languages. We anticipate that Whisper models’ transcription capabilities may be used for improving accessibility tools. While Whisper models cannot be used for real-time transcription out of the box – their speed and size suggest that others may be able to build applications on top of them that allow for near-real-time speech recognition and translation. The real value of beneficial applications built on top of Whisper models suggests that the disparate performance of these models may have real economic implications. There are also potential dual use concerns that come with releasing Whisper. While we hope the technology will be used primarily for beneficial purposes, making ASR technology more accessible could enable more actors to build capable surveillance technologies or scale up existing surveillance efforts, as the speed and accuracy allow for affordable automatic transcription and translation of large volumes of audio communication. Moreover, these models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals out of the box, which in turn presents safety concerns related both to dual use and disparate performance. In practice, we expect that the cost of transcription is not the limiting factor of scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: Please give me an English summary of the main content from this Arabic research paper.
description: mmBERT is a modern multilingual encoder that significantly outperforms previous generation models like XLM-R on classification, embedding, and retrieval tasks. Built on the ModernBERT architecture with novel multilingual training innovations, mmBERT demonstrates that low-resource languages can be effectively learned during the decay phase of training. It is also significantly faster than any previous multilingual encoder. mmBERT represents the first significant advancement over XLM-R for massively multilingual encoder models. Key features include massive language coverage, trained on over 1800 languages with a progressive inclusion strategy. The model is built on the ModernBERT foundation with Flash Attention 2 and unpadding techniques. It introduces a novel training recipe with inverse mask scheduling and temperature sampling. The training data is publicly available, consisting of 3T+ tokens. The model uses bidirectional attention with masked language modeling objectives, optimized specifically for multilingual understanding and cross-lingual transfer. The training process includes a progressive language addition strategy, starting with 60 high-resource languages, expanding to 110 mid-resource languages, and including all 1833 languages in the decay phase. An inverse mask schedule reduces the mask ratio from 30% to 15% to 5% across training phases for progressively refined learning. Inverse temperature sampling adjusts multilingual sampling from high-resource bias (τ=0.7) to uniform sampling (τ=0.3). Model merging combines English-focused, high-resource, and all-language decay variants using TIES merging. The model family includes mmBERT-small and mmBERT-base, with mmBERT-small having 140M total parameters and 42M non-embed parameters, and mmBERT-base having 307M total parameters and 110M non-embed parameters. Both models support 1800+ languages and have a max sequence length of 8192. The model is trained on a carefully curated 3T+ token multilingual dataset, including 2.3T tokens for 60 languages in the pre-training phase, 600B tokens for 110 languages in the mid-training phase, and 100B tokens for 1833 languages in the decay phase. Data sources include Filtered DCLM (English), FineWeb2 (multilingual), FineWeb2-HQ (20 high-resource languages), Wikipedia (MegaWika), code repositories (StarCoder, ProLong), academic papers (ArXiv, PeS2o), and community discussions (StackExchange).

instruction: What are the recommended quant versions for achieving the best performance?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a model, including imatrix and IQ quants, which are available for download. The quants are sorted by size, though not necessarily by quality, with IQ quants often being preferable to similar-sized non-IQ quants. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S, i1-IQ1_M, i1-IQ2_XXS, i1-IQ2_XS, i1-IQ2_S, i1-IQ2_M, i1-Q2_K_S, i1-Q2_K, i1-IQ3_XXS, i1-IQ3_XS, i1-Q3_K_S, i1-IQ3_S, i1-IQ3_M, i1-Q3_K_M, i1-Q3_K_L, i1-IQ4_XS, i1-Q4_0, i1-IQ4_NL, i1-Q4_K_S, i1-Q4_K_M, i1-Q4_1, i1-Q5_K_S, i1-Q5_K_M, and i1-Q6_K. Each quant type has a specified size in gigabytes and associated notes, such as 'for the desperate', 'very low quality', or 'recommended'. An imatrix file is also provided for creating custom quants. Users are advised to refer to external resources for detailed instructions on using GGUF files and concatenating multi-part files. The model's quantization work was made possible through the use of company servers and a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: Can you visualize a peaceful garden with flowers, a fountain, and a small bridge over a pond?
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family is the fastest image generation model to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture. It delivers state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Designed for real-time image generation without sacrificing quality, it runs on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is fully open under Apache 2.0 and can run on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090/4070. The model supports text-to-image, image editing, and multi-reference editing at quality that exceeds its size. It is suitable for local development, edge deployment, and production use. The repository includes an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B, with the main repository containing full BF16 weights. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives. The model is available via the BFL API and in other formats. It can be used with the Diffusers Python library by installing or upgrading diffusers and using the Flux2KleinPipeline. The model is not intended to provide factual information and may produce inaccurate or distorted text. As a statistical model, it may represent or amplify biases from the training data and may fail to generate output that matches prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate deceptive or harmful content, to generate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm individuals, to harass or abuse individuals or groups, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high-risk applications that adversely impact legal rights. Black Forest Labs is committed to responsible development and has implemented pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material and nonconsensual intimate imagery. Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with partnerships to filter known CSAM. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit harmful behaviors and suppress certain concepts. Ongoing evaluations were conducted with internal and external third-party assessments to identify and address risks. The final checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs and were released under Apache 2.0 for the 4B model and a non-commercial license for the 9B model. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content are included in the repository, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations. Content provenance features include pixel-layer watermarking and C2PA metadata to indicate AI-generated content. Policies govern acceptable use, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy. Black Forest Labs provides a dedicated email address for safety feedback and maintains relationships with organizations to address emerging risks.

instruction: Which of the two headlines is more relevant to the article about artificial intelligence?
description: This is a model finetuned from Alibaba-NLP/gte-multilingual-reranker-base using the Sentence Transformers library. It is a Cross Encoder model designed to compute scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model has a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and produces one output label. It can be loaded and used with the Sentence Transformers library for inference, where it can calculate scores for text pairs or rank texts based on similarity to a single text. The model was trained on a dataset of 24,588 samples, with each sample containing two sentences and a label. The training used a non-default set of hyperparameters, including a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, 5 training epochs, and FP16 precision. During training, the model achieved a Spearman correlation of 0.8709 on the validation dataset. The training loss decreased over time, and the Spearman correlation on the validation set improved from 0.7581 at step 100 to 0.8709 at step 3200. The model was trained using PyTorch 2.8.0+cu128, Sentence Transformers 5.1.2, and Transformers 4.57.1. The training data included a variety of sentence pairs, with sentence lengths ranging from 3 to 998 characters for the first sentence and 73 to 352 characters for the second sentence. The labels ranged from 0.0 to 1.0, with a mean of 0.41. The model uses a linear learning rate scheduler with no warmup steps and a learning rate of 5e-05. The training process included logging of training loss and validation Spearman correlation at each step.

instruction: Which document contains a diagram explaining the structure of a neural network?
description: ColQwen2.5-Omni is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy derived from Omnimodal Language Models, designed to efficiently index documents using their visual features. It is an extension of Qwen2.5-Omni-3B that generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may alter the aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to produce at most 1024 image patches, with experiments showing improvements in performance with more patches, though at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained using colpali-engine version 0.3.11 and uses the same data as described in the ColPali paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, with 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with pseudo-questions generated by a Vision Language Model (VLM). The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to avoid contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. The audio retrieval capabilities are acquired in a zero-shot manner, as the training data is purely image-text matching, with the audio and vision towers frozen during training. The model supports tasks involving the retrieval of documents based on visual and audio features. It is intended to be used with the colpali-engine version 0.3.11 or higher. The model is evaluated using a dataset of 500 audio samples from the 'dailytalk-conversations-grouped' dataset, and retrieval results are obtained by scoring query embeddings against document embeddings. The model is available for use with specific installation and processing instructions, including the use of a processor for audio and query processing. The model is developed by Manuel Faysse and Antonio Loison, and its methodology is described in a paper available on arXiv. If used in research, the model should be cited according to the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: List the protein entities mentioned in this drug interaction study.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene and protein entity recognition in biomedical texts. It is optimized for high-precision identification of gene and protein mentions in clinical and research documents, supporting applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is trained on the BC2GM dataset, a foundational corpus for gene and protein name recognition in biomedical literature, which contains manually annotated gene and protein mentions from MEDLINE abstracts. It identifies and classifies entities using the `B-GENE/PROTEIN` and `I-GENE/PROTEIN` labels. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. It achieves an F1 score of 0.81, with a precision of 0.80, recall of 0.81, and accuracy of 0.95. It is compatible with the Hugging Face ecosystem and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction, including `none`, `simple`, `first`, `average`, and `max`. The model is suitable for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in both clinical and research applications with production-ready reliability. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Which version should I choose if I prioritize speed over precision?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the Voxtral-Small-24B-2507 model by MistralAI, using the llama.cpp release b6014 for quantization. The model is intended for use with llama.cpp or other llama.cpp-based projects. The quantizations are generated using the imatrix option and a dataset provided by the original model. The model supports various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The available quantizations include BF16, Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_1, Q4_K_L, Q4_K_M, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q3_K_XL, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, IQ3_M, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, Q2_K_L, IQ3_XXS, Q2_K, IQ2_M, IQ2_S, and IQ2_XS. Each quantization is described with its quality level and file size. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Some quantizations, such as Q4_0, support online repacking for improved performance on ARM and AVX hardware. The model includes information on performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system, showing speed improvements for certain quantizations. Users are advised to consider their hardware capabilities when selecting a quantization, balancing speed, quality, and memory usage. I-quants (IQX_X) are recommended for lower quantization levels when using cuBLAS or rocBLAS, while K-quants (QX_K_X) are generally suitable for most use cases. The model includes credits to contributors for dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: How does the processing time vary when using different precision settings like FP8 or FP16?
description: ComfyUI-SeedVR2_VideoUpscaler is an official release for ComfyUI that enables video and image upscaling. It provides high-quality upscaling and is suitable for any video length when the appropriate settings are used. The model supports both 3B and 7B versions, with FP8 and FP16 precision options. It requires a GPU with substantial VRAM, with at least 18GB recommended for the 3B version and more than 24GB for optimal performance with the 7B version. The model automatically downloads into the `models/SEEDVR2` directory. Users must configure parameters such as model version, seed, new resolution, batch size, and VRAM preservation settings. Temporal consistency requires a batch size of at least 5, and higher batch sizes improve results but increase VRAM usage. Input and output resolutions impact VRAM consumption, and users may need to adjust these if they encounter out-of-memory errors. The model has been benchmarked on various hardware, including NVIDIA H100 and RTX4090 GPUs, with performance metrics showing improvements in speed and VRAM efficiency over time. The 7B FP8 model has quality issues and is recommended to be used in FP16 if possible. The model has undergone several updates to improve speed, reduce VRAM usage, fix memory leaks, and allow process interruption. It is compatible with the latest ComfyUI version and Python 3.12.9. The model has high VRAM requirements and processing speed depends on GPU capabilities. Contributions are welcome, and users are encouraged to submit pull requests for improvements. The code is released under the MIT license.

instruction: Can you check if this audio clip sounds like it was generated artificially?
description: The DF Arena 1B is a universal antispoofing model trained on traditional speech antispoofing datasets as well as singing and environmental deepfake data. It is designed to detect audio deepfakes and determine whether an audio sample is spoofed or bonafide. The model supports antispoofing tasks and can be used to classify audio inputs into spoof or bonafide categories. It is based on a neural network architecture optimized for audio deepfake detection. The model has been evaluated on multiple datasets, achieving an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.00% on the dfadd dataset, 11.54% on add_2023_round_2, 8.37% on codecfake, 4.66% on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.91% on in_the_wild, 1.14% on asvspoof_2019, 22.21% on add_2022_track_1, 2.92% on fake_or_real, 17.25% on asvspoof_2024, 2.20% on add_2022_track_3, 5.08% on add_2023_round_1, 0.15% on librisevoc, 1.75% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 1.09% on sonar. The average EER across all datasets is 5.919%, and the pooled EER is 9.52%. In terms of F1-score, the model achieves 0.9993 on dfadd, 0.9188 on add_2023_round_2, 0.8695 on codecfake, 0.8037 on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.9928 on in_the_wild, 0.9473 on asvspoof_2019, 0.6678 on add_2022_track_1, 0.9711 on fake_or_real, 0.6615 on asvspoof_2024, 0.9357 on add_2022_track_3, 0.9639 on add_2023_round_1, 0.9958 on librisevoc, 0.7577 on asvspoof_2021_df, and 0.9903 on sonar. The average F1-score is 0.8863, and the pooled F1-score is 0.81. For accuracy, the model achieves 99.97% on dfadd, 88.46% on add_2023_round_2, 91.63% on codecfake, 95.34% on asvspoof_2021_la, 99.10% on in_the_wild, 98.86% on asvspoof_2019, 77.79% on add_2022_track_1, 97.11% on fake_or_real, 82.75% on asvspoof_2024, 97.80% on add_2022_track_3, 94.92% on add_2023_round_1, 99.84% on librisevoc, 98.25% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 98.89% on sonar. The average accuracy is 94.079%, and the pooled accuracy is 90.47%. The model is distributed under a non-commercial license. For questions or issues, users are encouraged to open an issue on the model repository or contact the developers via email. The model can be cited using the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Can you implement a basic linked list in Python with insert and delete methods?
description: Maincoder is a code-focused language model optimized for code generation and completion tasks. It is designed to perform well on coding benchmarks while maintaining a compact size suitable for local deployment. The model is optimized for Python code completion and generation and features a modern transformer architecture with RoPE embeddings, grouped-query attention, QK normalization, and a high depth-to-width ratio. It is pre-trained and mid-trained on custom data mixes developed for high-performance coding and is fine-tuned using a specialized reinforcement learning policy optimization algorithm to improve training stability and accelerate convergence. Maincoder achieves state-of-the-art performance on Python coding benchmarks HumanEval, HumanEval+, and MBPP+, with scores of 0.7622, 0.7256, and 0.7090 respectively. The model has 1 billion parameters and includes features such as rotary position embeddings with a theta of 1,000,000, RMSNorm for pre-normalization, grouped query attention with a 4:1 ratio of query to key-value heads, QK normalization with RMSNorm applied to attention queries and keys, and SwiGLU MLP with SiLU activation. The model has a hidden size of 1536, 32 layers, 16 attention heads (4 key-value heads), a head dimension of 96, a vocabulary size of 151,936, a context length of 2,048 tokens, and uses bfloat16 precision. It supports code completion tasks such as function completion, class completion, and algorithm implementation. The model has a context length limit of 2,048 tokens and is primarily optimized for Python, with performance potentially varying on other languages. It may generate code with bugs or security issues, so generated code should always be reviewed. The model has not undergone any alignment or safety tuning, and outputs may be unsafe or biased. Appropriate safeguards and careful evaluation are recommended for use. The model is released under a license and can be cited as Maincoder-1B: A High-Performance 1B Parameter Coding Model by the Maincode Team in 2025.

instruction: I need the content of this image converted into plain text with proper formatting.
description: OCRFlux-3B is a preview release of a multimodal large language model toolkit designed for converting PDFs and images into clean, readable plain Markdown text. It is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct using private document datasets and some additional data. The model is based on a 3B parameter vision-language model (VLM), allowing it to run on a GTX 3090 GPU. OCRFlux-3B is intended to push the current state-of-the-art in document parsing to a significantly higher level. It supports superior parsing quality on each page, achieving higher Edit Distance Similarity (EDS) scores on the OCRFlux-bench-single benchmark compared to baseline models such as olmOCR-7B-0225-preview, Nanonets-OCR-s, and MonkeyOCR. Specifically, it achieves 0.095 higher EDS for English pages (from 0.872 to 0.967), 0.109 higher EDS for Chinese pages (from 0.858 to 0.967), and 0.187 higher EDS overall (from 0.780 to 0.967). The model also provides native support for cross-page table and paragraph merging, a feature not previously available in open-source projects. It can be used via an API for direct integration into code or through a Docker container setup, which requires Docker with GPU support and a pre-downloaded model. The model supports processing millions of documents at scale using an efficient inference setup via vllm. It includes a pipeline for generating Markdown files from JSONL outputs and offers evaluation capabilities for single-page parsing, table parsing, and cross-page merging detection and merging tasks. OCRFlux-3B is evaluated on two comprehensive benchmarks for single-page parsing and two for cross-page table/paragraph merging. In single-page parsing, it achieves an average EDS of 0.967, outperforming other models. In cross-page merging detection, it achieves a precision of 0.996, recall of 0.976, F1 score of 0.986, and accuracy of 0.986. For cross-page table merging, it achieves an average Tree Edit Distance-based Similarity (TEDS) of 0.950. The benchmarks used for evaluation are not included in the model's training or evaluation data. The model is designed to handle complex scenarios such as cross-page tables with repeated headers, vertically split tables, and multi-line table cells. It is also capable of merging paragraphs across pages by concatenation.

instruction: List the protein names mentioned in this section of a drug discovery report.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene and protein entity recognition in biomedical texts. It is optimized for high-precision identification of gene and protein mentions in clinical and research documents, supporting applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is trained on the BC2GM dataset, a foundational corpus for gene and protein name recognition in biomedical literature, which contains manually annotated gene and protein mentions from MEDLINE abstracts. It identifies and classifies entities using the `B-GENE/PROTEIN` and `I-GENE/PROTEIN` labels. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. It achieves an F1 score of 0.81, with a precision of 0.80, recall of 0.81, and accuracy of 0.95. It is compatible with the Hugging Face ecosystem and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction, including `none`, `simple`, `first`, `average`, and `max`. The model is suitable for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in both clinical and research applications with production-ready reliability. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Generate a 3D segmentation of the pancreas using the text 'pancreas with diffuse enlargement and heterogeneous enhancement' in this abdominal MRI.
description: VoxTell is a state-of-the-art 3D vision-language segmentation model that directly maps free-form text prompts to volumetric masks in medical imaging. Unlike traditional segmentation models that require predefined class labels, VoxTell accepts natural language descriptions ranging from single words to full clinical sentences, enabling intuitive and flexible medical image analysis. The model is designed for both anatomical and pathological structures across multiple imaging modalities (CT, PET, MRI), being trained on 1000+ familiar concepts while maintaining strong generalization to related unseen classes through its multi-stage vision-language fusion architecture. VoxTell supports free-text prompting to generate 3D segmentation masks using natural language descriptions, multi-modality support across CT, PET, and MRI imaging modalities, comprehensive anatomy coverage including brain, thorax, abdomen, pelvis, musculoskeletal system, and extremities, and flexible granularity from coarse anatomical labels to fine-grained pathological findings. VoxTell v1.1 is the recommended version, trained on all datasets from the paper and additional sources (190 datasets, ~68,500 volumes), with a 95% probability of sampling from the semantic datasets corpus and 5% probability from image-text-mask triplets from instance-focused datasets. It is recommended for general application, inference, and fine-tuning, maximizing supervision and concept coverage for stronger general-purpose performance. VoxTell v1.0 is deprecated and not recommended for general use, having been used for the experiments in the paper but containing known issues that have been fixed in v1.1. VoxTell employs a multi-stage vision-language fusion approach, including an image encoder that processes 3D volumetric input into latent feature representations, a prompt encoder using a frozen model to embed text prompts, a prompt decoder that transforms text queries and image latents into multi-scale text features, and an image decoder that fuses visual and textual information at multiple resolutions using MaskFormer-style query-image fusion with deep supervision. The model is intended for research in vision-language models for medical image analysis, text-promptable or automated segmentation of anatomical structures in medical imaging, and identification and delineation of pathological findings. It is not intended for clinical diagnosis without expert radiologist review, real-time emergency medical decision-making, or commercial use. VoxTell achieves state-of-the-art performance on anatomical and pathological segmentation tasks across multiple medical imaging benchmarks. Performance may vary on imaging modalities or anatomical regions underrepresented in training data. Prompting structures absent from the image and never seen on this modality may lead to undesired results. Text prompt quality and specificity affects segmentation accuracy. The model is not validated for direct clinical use without expert review. Images must be in RAS orientation for correct anatomical localization, and performance may degrade on images with very uncommon voxel spacings. The model does not resample images to a standardized spacing for faster inference. Known issues include compatibility problems with PyTorch 2.9.0 causing OOM errors during inference, which should be avoided by using PyTorch 2.8.0 or earlier.

instruction: Can you help me search for similar items in a catalog using a product name and photo?
description: The embedding model trained by Jina AI is a version of the Qwen2.5-VL model with a retrieval adapter merged into the base weights. This modification allows the model to be natively compatible with vLLM without the need for custom adapter-handling code. The model is designed for multimodal multilingual retrieval tasks, supporting both text and image inputs. It can encode text prompts and image prompts to generate embeddings. For text, prompts are structured with either a 'Query' or 'Passage' prefix. For images, a 'user' instruction is provided alongside the image data. The model processes these prompts and generates embeddings, which are pooled and normalized. Vision tokens are isolated for image prompts by identifying specific token IDs marking the start and end of the image content. The embeddings for text-only prompts are derived from all tokens. The model uses PyTorch for computation and supports float16 data type for efficiency. The model is intended for use in retrieval systems where both textual and visual information may be relevant.

instruction: 이 카페 리뷰에서 고객의 감정을 분석해줘: '서비스도 좋고 분위기도 너무 좋았어요!'
description: 이 모델은 한국어 텍스트를 6가지 감정인 분노, 불안, 슬픔, 평온, 당황, 기쁨으로 분류하도록 설계되었습니다. `klue/roberta-base` 모델을 기반으로 파인튜닝되었습니다. 감정 분류를 수행하는 데 사용되며, 각 감정에 대해 정밀도, 재현율, F1 점수를 평가했습니다. 분노의 경우 정밀도 0.9801, 재현율 0.9788, F1 점수 0.9795를 기록했으며, 불안은 정밀도 0.9864, 재현율 0.9848, F1 점수 0.9856를 보였습니다. 슬픔의 정밀도는 0.9837, 재현율 0.9854, F1 점수 0.9845이며, 평온의 정밀도는 0.9782, 재현율 0.9750, F1 점수 0.9766입니다. 당황의 정밀도는 0.9607, 재현율 0.9668, F1 점수 0.9652이며, 기쁨의 정밀도는 0.9857, 재현율 0.9886, F1 점수 0.9872입니다. 전체 정확도는 0.9831이며, 매크로 평균 정밀도는 0.9791, 재현율은 0.9804, F1 점수는 0.9798입니다. 가중 평균 정밀도는 0.9831, 재현율은 0.9831, F1 점수는 0.9831입니다.

instruction: What part of the image matches the description 'a group of people walking on a sunny sidewalk'?
description: OneRef is a unified one-tower model for expression grounding and segmentation, introduced in the paper 'OneRef: Unified One-tower Expression Grounding and Segmentation with Mask Referring Modeling' presented at NeurIPS 2024. The model is developed by Linhui Xiao, Xiaoshan Yang, Fang Peng, Yaowei Wang, and Changsheng Xu. OneRef introduces a novel paradigm called Mask Referring Modeling (MRefM), which effectively models the referential relationship between visual and language modalities. Unlike previous approaches, OneRef uses a minimalist one-tower framework that unifies visual and linguistic feature spaces, eliminating the need for modality interaction modules, fusion en-/decoders, and special grounding tokens. The model is validated across three referring tasks on five datasets, achieving state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance. OneRef supports both referring expression comprehension (REC) and referring expression segmentation (RES) tasks. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is compatible with Python 3.9.10 and PyTorch 2.0.1. It has been tested on multiple PyTorch versions and is easy to deploy in various environments. The model is trained using a referring-aware dynamic image masking strategy, which focuses on the referred region rather than using fixed ratios or random masking. The model is available in both Base and Large variants, with pre-trained checkpoints provided for single-dataset fine-tuning and dataset-mixed grounding pre-training settings. The MRefM pre-training for REC is conducted using a mixture of RefCOCO/+/g and ReferIt datasets, while for RES it is conducted using RefCOCO/+/g datasets. The model is built upon the BEiT-3 architecture and includes Sentencepiece tokenizer and VQKD model for pre-training. The model has been shown to achieve significant performance improvements in mixed pre-training settings, although the performance gains in single-dataset settings are less pronounced. The model is available for download from HuggingFace and Google Drive, with detailed training and evaluation scripts provided. The model has been tested on datasets such as RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg, ReferItGame, and Flickr30K. The model's performance is evaluated using standard metrics such as mIoU and oIoU for segmentation tasks and standard accuracy metrics for comprehension tasks. The model is noted for its energy efficiency and computational cost advantages compared to existing approaches. The model's architecture and training methodology are described in detail in the paper, with visualizations and qualitative results provided for various datasets.

instruction: Can you imagine a vibrant underwater world with colorful coral and a sunlit surface above?
description: Quantized GGUF versions of the model by Tongyi-Mai. The available models include Z-Image Turbo GGUF and Qwen3-4B (Text Encoder). The model can be used with tools provided by city96. The model is designed for image generation tasks and supports detailed text-to-image synthesis. It utilizes a transformer-based architecture and can be loaded using the Diffusers library with specific quantization configurations. The model can be run on CUDA-enabled devices with options for attention backend selection, model compilation, and CPU offloading to optimize performance and memory usage. The model is evaluated for efficiency and inference speed, particularly with the Turbo variant requiring zero guidance and a reduced number of inference steps. Users are advised to consult the original model card for detailed information. The repository follows the same license as the original model.

instruction: Create a fantasy tavern scene with a bard playing a lute and a crowd of curious onlookers.
description: NetaYume Lumina is a text-to-image model fine-tuned from a high-quality anime-style image generation model developed by an unspecified entity. It is based on an open-source base model released by the team at Shanghai AI Laboratory. The model was trained to generate both realistic human images and high-quality anime-style images, while retaining a significant amount of knowledge from its base model. Key features include the ability to generate detailed anime-style images with sharp outlines, vibrant colors, and smooth shading. It also demonstrates improved character understanding, particularly for characters from the Danbooru dataset, resulting in more coherent and accurate character representations. Additionally, it enhances the generation of fine details such as accessories, clothing textures, hairstyles, and background elements with greater clarity. The model file, NetaYume_Lumina_v2_all_in_one.safetensors, is an all-in-one file that includes the necessary weights for the VAE, text encoder, and image backbone for use with ComfyUI. The model uses a pre-trained Gemma-2-2b text encoder, a pre-trained Flux.1 dev's VAE, and a fine-tuned NetaLumina image backbone. It is recommended to use a system prompt to help generate desired images by aligning with user prompts. For anime-style images using Danbooru tags, the model is described as an assistant designed to generate anime images based on textual prompts and user prompts with Danbooru tags. Recommended settings for optimal performance include a CFG of 4–7, sampling steps of 40–50, and samplers such as Euler a with a normal scheduler or res_multistep with a linear_quadratic scheduler. The model acknowledges the use of the Danbooru dataset, the creators of a wonderful model, and a team for openly sharing an awesome model.

instruction: Create a 10-second visual of a snowy mountain with a cabin and a forest in the background.
description: The GGUF version of TurboWan2.2-I2V-A14B-720P is provided for use with ComfyUI. This model is part of the TurboDiffusion framework, which accelerates video diffusion models by 100-200 times. The model is described in the research paper titled 'TurboDiffusion: Accelerating Video Diffusion Models by 100-200 Times' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025. Additional related research includes 'SageAttention: Accurate 8-Bit Attention for Plug-and-play Inference Acceleration' by Zhang, Jintao and others, presented at the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) in 2025, and 'SLA: Beyond Sparsity in Diffusion Transformers via Fine-Tunable Sparse-Linear Attention' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025. Further contributions include 'Large Scale Diffusion Distillation via Score-Regularized Continuous-Time Consistency' by Zheng, Kaiwen and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025, and 'Sageattention2: Efficient attention with thorough outlier smoothing and per-thread int4 quantization' by Zhang, Jintao and others, presented at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) in 2025. Additional research includes 'Sageattention2++: A more efficient implementation of sageattention2' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025, and 'SageAttention3: Microscaling FP4 Attention for Inference and An Exploration of 8-Bit Training' by Zhang, Jintao and others, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: 有没有办法将中文文本转换成数值表示，以支持跨文档的语义比较？
description: This is a CoSENT (Cosine Sentence) model named shibing624/text2vec-base-chinese. It maps sentences to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for tasks like sentence embeddings, text matching, or semantic search. The model is trained using the CoSENT method based on the hfl/chinese-macbert-base architecture on the Chinese STS-B dataset. It achieves good performance on the Chinese STS-B test set. The model is available on the Hugging Face model hub and is recommended for general Chinese semantic matching tasks. The model outputs a vector that captures semantic information and can be used for information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a max sequence length of 128 and a Pooling layer that uses mean token pooling. The model is fine-tuned using a contrastive objective, where cosine similarity is computed for all possible sentence pairs in a batch, and a rank loss is applied by comparing true and false pairs. The training dataset is unspecified, and the maximum sequence length is set to 128. The best performance was achieved at epoch 5, and the sentence embedding dimension is 768. The model can be used with the text2vec library, HuggingFace Transformers, or the sentence-transformers library. It supports acceleration via ONNX and OpenVINO backends, with ONNX providing no performance loss on GPU and OpenVINO providing a 1.12x speedup on CPU. Quantization with OpenVINO (int8) results in a small performance hit on some tasks and a minor gain on others, with performance on CPU also improved. The model is intended for use as a sentence and short paragraph encoder.

instruction: Is it possible to create a video where a character's body language matches the emotion in a provided audio file?
description: The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model is designed for audio-driven cinematic video generation. It was introduced in the Wan-S2V paper, which addresses the challenge of achieving film-level character animation by proposing an audio-driven model built upon Wan. The model significantly enhances expressiveness and fidelity in cinematic contexts compared to existing approaches. It outperforms state-of-the-art models such as Hunyuan-Avatar and Omnihuman in extensive experiments. The model supports applications in long-form video generation and precise video lip-sync editing. Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video models, incorporating a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into video diffusion models. This architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4. This model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available, suitable for both industrial and academic use. The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model supports video generation at 480P and 720P resolutions. It can generate videos from audio input combined with a reference image and optional text prompt. The model also supports pose-driven generation, allowing it to follow specific pose sequences while generating videos synchronized with audio input. The model can be run on a single GPU with at least 80GB VRAM or using multi-GPU inference with FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design tailored to the denoising process of diffusion models: a high-noise expert for early stages and a low-noise expert for later stages. Each expert model has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching between the high-noise and low-noise experts. The effectiveness of the MoE architecture is validated through comparisons with baseline and other MoE-based variants, showing that the final version achieves the lowest validation loss. Wan2.2 also explores a high-compression design with a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE. This model achieves a T×H×W compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, ranking among the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It natively supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a single unified framework. Wan2.2 is compared with leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0, demonstrating superior performance across multiple crucial dimensions. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, granting users the freedom to use the generated content while ensuring compliance with the license provisions. Users are fully accountable for their use of the models, which must not involve sharing any content that violates applicable laws, causes harm to individuals or groups, disseminates personal information intended for harm, spreads misinformation, or targets vulnerable populations.

instruction: Please analyze the satellite image and indicate where the buildings are located.
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of facebook/mask2former-swin-base-ade-semantic for semantic segmentation of satellite and aerial imagery. It uses the Mask2Former architecture with a Swin Transformer backbone and is designed for semantic segmentation, specifically land cover classification. The model classifies pixels into nine land cover categories: Background, Bareland, Grass, Pavement, Road, Tree, Water, Cropland, and Building. It was trained for 17 epochs and achieved a best mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) of 0.5202 and a validation loss of 44.21. The model is intended for land cover classification from satellite and aerial imagery, with applications in urban planning, environmental monitoring, geographic information systems (GIS), and remote sensing research. However, it has several limitations: it exhibits geographic bias due to being primarily trained on imagery from specific regions in the OpenEarthMap dataset, performs best on imagery with resolution similar to the training data, may require fine-tuning for different satellite sensors or aerial platforms, and its performance may vary with seasonal or weather conditions.

instruction: Could you locate some authoritative sources on the history of the Roman Empire?
description: inf-retriever-v1-pro is a specialized retrieval component of the INF-X-Retriever framework, designed to distill the core retrieval intent from complex, verbose, or reasoning-intensive queries. It is built upon the inf-retriever-v1 foundation and further trained to serve as the retriever within a RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) system. The model transforms raw user queries into concise, search-optimized queries for dense retrieval systems. In experiments, a single canonical query-writing prompt was applied across all datasets to ensure consistency and reproducibility. The task is defined as 'Given a web search query, retrieve relevant passages that answer the query.' This model is a key enabler for INF-X-Retriever's state-of-the-art performance, currently holding the No. 1 position on the (as of Dec 17, 2025). The BRIGHT (Benchmark for Reasoning-Intensive Grounded HT) is a rigorous text retrieval benchmark designed to evaluate the capability of retrieval models in handling questions that require intensive reasoning and cross-document synthesis. It is collected from real-world sources such as StackExchange, competitive programming platforms, and mathematical competitions, and comprises complex queries spanning diverse domains like mathematics, coding, biology, economics, and robotics. On the BRIGHT benchmark, INF-X-Retriever achieves state-of-the-art results (as of Dec 20, 2025). For short document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 68.3 on StackExchange, 55.3 on Coding, and 57.7 on Theorem-based categories. For detailed results across 12 datasets, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 79.8 in Bio., 70.9 in Earth., 69.9 in Econ., 73.3 in Psy., 57.7 in Rob., 64.3 in Stack., 61.9 in Sus., 56.1 in Leet., 54.5 in Pony, 51.9 in AoPS, 53.1 in TheoQ., and 67.9 in TheoT. For long document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 54.6, with 73.2 in Bio., 59.6 in Earth., 69.3 in Econ., 12.1 in Pony, 74.3 in Psy., 55.9 in Rob., 27.8 in Stack., and 64.8 in Sus. The model is implemented using the transformers library version 4.51.0 and can be used with the SentenceTransformer or the Transformers library for encoding queries and documents and computing retrieval scores.

instruction: Is there a way to know if this video of a face is real or generated?
description: This model is a powerful deepfake video detection system that combines a ResNext50 network for spatial feature extraction with an LSTM network for temporal analysis. It is designed to classify video authenticity by identifying visual artifacts and temporal inconsistencies. The model achieves 87% accuracy on internal test datasets and is built using PyTorch. It is trained on a custom dataset of real and deepfake videos and provides a binary classification (real/fake) along with a confidence score. The architecture is optimized for video clips of 20 frames and performs best on human face-focused content. Its performance may be affected by video quality, such as heavy compression or low resolution. The model is intended for ethical use in combating misinformation and verifying media authenticity. It is integrated with the Hugging Face pipeline for easy inference and is available as a Docker-ready deployment with a Hugging Face Space.

instruction: Which substances are identified in this extract from a medical case report?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.98. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is suitable for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: What is the license number and RTO code on the back of this driving license?
description: The Indian ID Validator is a computer vision pipeline designed for classifying, detecting, and extracting text from Indian identification documents, including Aadhaar, PAN Card, Passport, Voter ID, and Driving License. The system is powered by YOLO11 models and PaddleOCR, and it supports both front and back images for Aadhaar and Driving License. The pipeline performs three main tasks: classifying the type of ID document using the Id_Classifier model, detecting specific fields on the document using type-specific YOLO11 detection models, and extracting text from the detected fields via PaddleOCR with preprocessing steps such as upscaling, denoising, and contrast enhancement. The supported ID types include Aadhaar (front and back), PAN Card (front), Passport (front), Voter ID (front and back), and Driving License (front and back). The pipeline includes the following models: Id_Classifier, which classifies the type of Indian ID document with a Top-1 accuracy of 0.995 and Top-5 accuracy of 1.0; Aadhaar, which detects fields such as Aadhaar Number, DOB, Gender, Name, and Address, with mAP50 of 0.795, mAP50-95 of 0.553, Precision of 0.777, Recall of 0.774, and Fitness of 0.577; Driving_License, which detects fields such as Address, Blood Group, DL No, DOB, Name, Relation With, RTO, State, and Vehicle Type, with mAP50 of 0.690, mAP50-95 of 0.524, Precision of 0.752, and Recall of 0.669; Pan_Card, which detects fields such as PAN, Name, Father's Name, DOB, and Pan Card, with mAP50 of 0.924, mAP50-95 of 0.686, Precision of 0.902, and Recall of 0.901; Passport, which detects fields such as Address, Code, DOB, DOI, EXP, Gender, MRZ1, MRZ2, Name, Nationality, Nation, and POI, with mAP50 of 0.987, mAP50-95 of 0.851, Precision of 0.972, and Recall of 0.967; and Voter_Id, which detects fields such as Address, Age, DOB, Card Voter ID 1 Back, Card Voter ID 2 Front, Card Voter ID 2 Back, Card Voter ID 1 Front, Date of Issue, Election, Father, Gender, Name, Point, Portrait, Symbol, and Voter ID, with mAP50 of 0.917, mAP50-95 of 0.772, Precision of 0.922, and Recall of 0.873. The models are available in various formats such as PyTorch, ONNX, and TensorRT for deployment in different environments. The system can be used via a Python API or command-line interface, and it provides end-to-end processing for classification, detection, and OCR. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Could you summarize the content of this long article and highlight the key points?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It handles long contexts and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling full recall and second-level indexing for books and hours-long video. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and ModelScope, with generation hyperparameters available for both visual and text tasks. The model is described in technical reports and preprints available on arXiv.

instruction: Explain the connection between the image and the explanation provided in the text below it.
description: InternVL3.5 is a new family of open-source multimodal models that significantly advances versatility, reasoning capability, and inference efficiency in the InternVL series. A key innovation is the Cascade Reinforcement Learning (Cascade RL) framework, which enhances reasoning through a two-stage process: offline RL for stable convergence and online RL for refined alignment. This coarse-to-fine training strategy leads to substantial improvements on downstream reasoning tasks, such as MMMU and MathVista. To optimize efficiency, the Visual Resolution Router (ViR) dynamically adjusts the resolution of visual tokens without compromising performance. Coupled with ViR, the Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates the vision encoder and language model across different GPUs, effectively balancing computational load. These contributions collectively enable InternVL3.5 to achieve up to a +16.0% gain in overall reasoning performance and a 4.05 × inference speedup compared to its predecessor, InternVL3. In addition, InternVL3.5 supports novel capabilities such as GUI interaction and embodied agency. The largest model, InternVL3.5-241B-A28B, attains state-of-the-art results among open-source MLLMs across general multimodal, reasoning, text, and agentic tasks, narrowing the performance gap with leading commercial models like GPT-5. InternVL3.5 follows the 'ViT–MLP–LLM' paradigm, initializing the language model using the Qwen3 series and GPT-OSS, and the vision encoder using InternViT-300M and InternViT-6B. The Dynamic High Resolution strategy from InternVL1.5 is also retained. The Flash version of InternVL3.5 further integrates ViR, enabling efficient variants suitable for resource-constrained scenarios. InternVL3.5-Flash compresses visual tokens down to 64 tokens per patch using a higher compression rate pixel shuffle module, determined by a patch router assessing semantic richness. This patch-aware compression mechanism reduces the number of visual tokens by 50% while maintaining nearly 100% of the performance of InternVL3.5. The training pipeline comprises four stages: Multimodal Continual Pre-Training (CPT), Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and Cascade Reinforcement Learning (CascadeRL). In CascadeRL, the model is first fine-tuned using Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO) under an offline RL setting, followed by GSPO under an online RL setting. For the Flash version, an additional training stage called Visual Consistency Learning (ViCO) is introduced to integrate ViR and reduce inference cost. InternVL3.5-Flash is the result of this process. The model supports a Thinking mode, which encourages step-by-step reasoning for complex problems, and a Best-of-N (BoN) strategy for parallel thinking, improving reasoning depth and breadth. The Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates vision and language processing, with the vision subsystem batching and processing images to produce compact feature embeddings, which are then transmitted to the language subsystem for fusion with the text context prior to decoding. This separation alleviates blocking and brings multimodal prefilling performance closer to that of pure language models. InternVL3.5 is evaluated on a wide range of tasks, including multimodal reasoning and mathematics, OCR, chart and document understanding, multi-image understanding, real-world comprehension, comprehensive multimodal understanding, multimodal hallucination evaluation, visual grounding, multimodal multilingual understanding, video understanding, GUI tasks, embodied tasks, and SVG tasks. The model is also evaluated on language capability. InternVL3.5 is available in various sizes, including 1B, 2B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 38B, 20B-A4B, 30B-A3B, and 241B-A28B, with both GitHub and HuggingFace formats. The model is publicly released under the Apache-2.0 License.

instruction: I want to enhance my search functionality to support both text and images. Any suggestions?
description: This is a W4A16 quantized version of a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model. The quantization was performed using AutoRound with the AutoAWQ backend. The quantized model achieves approximately 3.5 GB memory usage, compared to 8.4 GB for the original model, enabling deployment on consumer GPUs while maintaining competitive retrieval performance. The model has 4.0B parameters and uses W4A16 quantization (4-bit weights, 16-bit activations). The calibration sequence length used during quantization is 1024, and the quantization configuration includes 4 bits, a group size of 128, and symmetric quantization. The calibration dataset used is NeelNanda/pile-10k, with 560 samples and 1000 iterations, and a batch size of 80. A total of 252 layers were quantized, while 105 vision-related layers remain in FP16/BF16 to preserve visual feature quality. The embedding dimension is 320, and the model supports up to 1280 visual tokens. On the ViDoRe benchmark, the model achieves an average NDCG@5 of 0.69768 across all languages, representing a -0.36% change from the original FP16 model. For English-only evaluation, the model achieves an average NDCG@5 of 0.74582, a -0.21% change from the original. Overall, the model shows quality retention of approximately 99.6%, with 17 benchmarks improved and 23 degraded. The model is suitable for deployment on GPUs with limited memory, with 8 GB GPUs supporting a batch size of approximately 64, 12 GB GPUs supporting a batch size of approximately 256, and 16 GB and 24 GB GPUs supporting high batch sizes. The calibration sequence length refers to the length used during quantization and not the maximum sequence length supported by the model. The model is designed for tasks involving retrieval, particularly in multimodal settings. The model supports inference using the AutoProcessor and AutoModel from the Transformers library, with specific dependencies including torch, torchvision, auto-round, autoawq, transformers, pillow, and requests. The model is released under a license consistent with the original model. Limitations include reduced precision due to 4-bit quantization, the non-quantized vision encoder, and performance dependence on the inference backend. The model is based on an original model by Alibaba, with quantization performed using tools developed by Intel. If used, the model should be cited along with the original model in a bibtex format.

instruction: How can I control the number of frames in the final video output?
description: This repository contains quantized models for HunyuanVideo-1.5, optimized for use with the LightX2V framework. The quantized models are designed to significantly reduce memory usage while maintaining high-quality video generation performance. The available models include two Diffusion Transformer (DIT) models for 720p video generation: one for image-to-video (I2V) and one for text-to-video (T2V) tasks, as well as a quantized text encoder based on the Qwen2.5-VL LLM architecture. These models use FP8-E4M3 quantization with the SGL (SGLang) kernel scheme, which provides up to 50% reduction in VRAM usage, minimal quality degradation compared to full precision models, and faster inference through optimized kernels. The quantized models are intended to be used in conjunction with the original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights and require specific configurations to be applied before the generator is created. Performance benefits include lower VRAM requirements, enabling use on GPUs with less memory, faster inference due to optimized kernels, and preserved visual quality. The models support both T2V and I2V tasks with specific parameters such as frame count, guidance scale, and aspect ratio. The original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights are still required for operation. For best performance, SageAttention 2 is recommended as the attention mode. The models are released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Can you render this fantasy creature as a realistic animal with accurate textures and colors?
description: Anything2Real 2601 A is a version of the Anything2Real family of LoRA models, designed to transform non-photorealistic images such as illustrations, anime, cartoons, and paintings into convincing photographs while preserving the original composition and content. It is based on Anything2Real 2601 and incorporates adjustments to the layer weights of anything2real alpha to achieve a more balanced performance between maintaining the original image and generating a realistic environment. Anything2Real 2601 A addresses weaknesses in previous versions, such as the limited effectiveness of Anything2Real 2601 in transforming eyes and the limited color performance of anything2real alpha. The model is built on the Qwen Edit 2511 base model and is recommended to be used with a strength of 1 to 0.75. A prompt template is suggested: 'change the picture 1 to realistic photograph, [description of your image]'. Including detailed descriptions in the prompt helps the model better understand the content and produce superior transformations, although it can still function without them. The model is used in a workflow involving 21 nodes, including CLIPLoader, ConditioningZeroOut, ImageScaleBy, KSampler, LoadImage, LoraLoaderModelOnly, and specialized Qwen Edit nodes, and is powered by an RTX 4090 Series GPU. Realism is subjective, and it is recommended to modulate strength or switch base models rather than increasing the LoRA weight if realism is insufficient. If realism remains inadequate, it is suggested to blend with an additional photorealistic LoRA and adjust to taste. The model was created to bridge the gap between artistic creations and photorealistic results without requiring users to start from scratch. It is intended for artists who want to see their characters in realistic form or for anyone interested in transforming their favorite art styles into photographs. Users are encouraged to share their results and suggestions for future improvements. For best results, it is recommended to follow the workflow demonstrated in the RunningHub example. Users can reach out with questions or collaboration ideas via Twitter, email, QQ Group, WeChat, or CivitAI.

instruction: Is it possible to recognize the different blocks in this user interface design?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform a task related to layout processing, though specific intended uses and limitations are not provided. The model was trained for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, and an evaluation batch size of 8. The optimizer used is ADAMW_TORCH with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and an epsilon of 1e-08. The learning rate scheduler type is reduce_lr_on_plateau with a warmup ratio of 0.06. The random seed used during training is 42. During training, the training loss decreased from 3.2231 at epoch 0.7692 to 0.0001 at epoch 10. The validation loss also decreased from 0.7135 at epoch 0.7692 to 0.0001 at epoch 10. The model was developed using Transformers 4.48.0, Pytorch 2.4.1.post100, Datasets 3.2.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.0.

instruction: I have a voxel-based idea for a sculpture—can you create a 3D version of it?
description: Hunyuan3D-Omni is a unified framework for the controllable generation of 3D assets, built on Hunyuan3D 2.1. It introduces a unified control encoder to incorporate additional control signals, including point cloud, voxel, skeleton, and bounding box. The model supports multi-modal conditional control, allowing 3D model generation constrained by 3D bounding boxes, creation of 3D human models with specific skeletal poses, generation guided by input point clouds, and creation from voxel representations.
