Hunyuan3D-Omni uses a single cross-modal architecture to unify all signals, rather than separate heads for each modality. It is trained with a progressive, difficulty-aware sampling strategy that selects one control modality per example, favoring harder signals such as skeletal pose and downweighting easier ones like point clouds. This approach encourages robust multi-modal fusion and graceful handling of missing inputs. Experiments show that these additional controls improve generation accuracy, enable geometry-aware transformations, and increase robustness for production workflows. The model requires 10 GB VRAM for generation and has a size of 3.3B parameters.

instruction: Which country has the largest population according to the latest statistics?
description: This model is a TAPAS medium model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_medium_reset` checkpoint, and the non-default version corresponds to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_medium`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step of intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings instead. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The model's performance on the development set for the medium size with reset position embeddings is 0.4324 accuracy. The model is intended for answering questions related to tables. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, and this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Is the market currently signaling a period of high or low volatility for NIFTY 50?
description: This is a production-ready XGBoost model for predicting market regimes (RISK_ON/RISK_OFF) in Indian financial markets, specifically for the NIFTY 50 index. It is part of a complete MLOps pipeline that includes experiment tracking, data versioning, and automated deployment. The model performs binary classification to predict whether the market is in a RISK_ON or RISK_OFF regime. RISK_ON indicates favorable market conditions with lower volatility and bullish momentum, suitable for aggressive trading, while RISK_OFF indicates cautious conditions with higher volatility, where defensive positioning is advised. The model uses four key technical indicators as features: India VIX (a volatility index), RSI (14-day) for momentum, 50-Day MA for short-term trend, and 200-Day MA for long-term trend. It is trained using XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) with the binary logistic objective function and logloss as the evaluation metric. The model is evaluated using both ML metrics and finance metrics, with production thresholds requiring an F1 score of at least 0.65 and a Sharpe Ratio of at least 0.5. The model is integrated into an MLOps system that includes MLflow for experiment tracking and model registry, DVC for data version control, GitHub Actions for automated CI/CD pipeline, FastAPI for REST API serving, Docker for containerized deployment, and Hugging Face Spaces for cloud deployment. The training pipeline includes data preprocessing, feature engineering, train/test split with stratification, XGBoost training with hyperparameters, model evaluation, backtesting on historical data, and model registration in MLflow. The model is retrained weekly via GitHub Actions, with automatic promotion to production if performance thresholds are met. The model is version-controlled in MLflow Model Registry under the name market_regime_classifier, version 1, in the Production stage, with run ID 43544e1ed8eb4c2a9268948b6795bdf5. The dataset used is synthetic market data for NIFTY 50, with an 80/20 train/test split. The model can be used via MLflow or a REST API endpoint. This project demonstrates MLOps pipeline architecture, experiment tracking, model registry management, data versioning, CI/CD automation, model serving, financial metrics and backtesting, Docker containerization, and cloud deployment. It is licensed under the MIT License for free use in learning and projects.

instruction: Are there any protein-related terms in this drug development proposal that need classification?
description: This model is a state-of-the-art fine-tuned transformer designed to deliver enterprise-grade accuracy for biomedical entity recognition. It is specialized for identifying and extracting various biomedical entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is production-ready and suitable for clinical and research applications. The model can identify and classify biomedical entities including B-protein, B-protein_complex, B-protein_enum, B-protein_familiy_or_group, B-protein_variant, I-protein, I-protein_complex, I-protein_enum, I-protein_familiy_or_group, and I-protein_variant. It is trained on the FSU dataset, a biomedical NER dataset focused on protein interactions and molecular biology entities for systems biology research. The dataset includes annotations for proteins, protein complexes, protein families, protein variants, and molecular interaction entities relevant to systems biology and biochemistry research. The model is based on the deberta-v3-small architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and validated through cross-validation on a held-out test set. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.97. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Is there a way to align audio tokens with text transcripts to improve speech generation accuracy?
description: X-Codec2 is a neural audio codec designed to improve speech synthesis and general audio generation for large language model (LLM) pipelines. It extends the original X-Codec by refining how semantic and acoustic information is integrated and tokenized, enabling efficient and high-fidelity audio representation. The architecture of X-Codec2 is based on X-Codec with several major differences. It uses a unified semantic-acoustic tokenization approach, fusing outputs from a semantic encoder (e.g., Wav2Vec2-BERT) and an acoustic encoder into a single embedding, capturing both high-level meaning (e.g., text content, emotion) and low-level audio details (e.g., timbre). It employs a single-stage vector quantization (VQ) using a single-layer Feature-Space Quantization (FSQ), which provides stability and compatibility with causal, autoregressive LLMs, in contrast to the multi-layer residual VQ used in most approaches. X-Codec2 includes semantic supervision during training, adding a semantic reconstruction loss to ensure that the discrete tokens preserve meaningful linguistic and emotional information, which is crucial for TTS tasks. Its 1D token structure is designed to align with the autoregressive modeling in LLMs like LLaMA, improving training efficiency and downstream compatibility. The model supports encoding and decoding of audio, with the ability to extract audio codes and reconstruct audio values. It is intended for use in LLM pipelines and is compatible with the Transformers library, though it is not yet merged into the main branch. Users can install it from source and use it with Python code to load the model, process audio data, and perform encoding and decoding operations. The model was contributed by external contributors, and the original code and checkpoints are available for reference.

instruction: Show me a realistic image of a café with the words 'Now Hiring' written on a chalkboard near the entrance.
description: LongCat-Image is an open-source and bilingual (Chinese-English) foundation model for image generation, designed to address core challenges in multilingual text rendering, photorealism, deployment efficiency, and developer accessibility prevalent in current leading models. With 6B parameters, LongCat-Image surpasses numerous open-source models that are several times larger across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating the immense potential of efficient model design. The model demonstrates superior accuracy and stability in rendering common Chinese characters compared to existing state-of-the-art open-source models and achieves industry-leading coverage of the Chinese dictionary. Through an innovative data strategy and training framework, LongCat-Image achieves remarkable photorealism in generated images. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, the target text must be enclosed within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: Can you provide a language-agnostic embedding for this text so I can compare it to others?
description: mmBERT is a modern multilingual encoder that significantly outperforms previous generation models like XLM-R on classification, embedding, and retrieval tasks. Built on the ModernBERT architecture with novel multilingual training innovations, mmBERT demonstrates that low-resource languages can be effectively learned during the decay phase of training. It is also significantly faster than any previous multilingual encoder. The model uses bidirectional attention with masked language modeling objectives, optimized specifically for multilingual understanding and cross-lingual transfer. Key features include massive language coverage, with training on over 1800 languages using a progressive inclusion strategy. The model is built on the ModernBERT foundation with Flash Attention 2 and unpadding techniques. It introduces novel training methods such as inverse mask scheduling and temperature sampling. The training data is publicly available, consisting of over 3T tokens. The model demonstrates effective learning of low-resource languages in the final training phase. The training data is composed of 2.3T tokens in the first pre-training phase with 60 languages, followed by an extension phase and a final pre-training phase. The mid-training phase includes 600B tokens across 110 languages, and the decay phase includes 100B tokens across 1833 languages. Data sources include Filtered DCLM, FineWeb2, FineWeb2-HQ, code repositories, academic papers, Wikipedia, and community discussions. The model family includes mmBERT-small and mmBERT-base, with varying parameter counts and language coverage. mmBERT supports tasks such as masked language modeling, cross-lingual embeddings, dense retrieval, cross-lingual classification, and multilingual reranking. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be fine-tuned for specific downstream tasks. The model is publicly available for download and use under an open license. If used in research, it should be cited with the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Is there a way to make a video where the depth effect simulates a shallow focus lens, highlighting only the subject?
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 text-to-video generation system, specifically for depth-based control. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet modules for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and depth control frames. The architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, a transformer model, and an autoencoder. The model is evaluated through inference examples that demonstrate its use in generating videos with specific visual characteristics, such as a close-up shot of a young woman's face with a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model is trained and optimized for high-resolution video generation with a focus on depth perception and visual quality. The model has been developed by Karachev Denis and is available on Github. Users are advised to raise issues directly in the repository and can contact welcomedenk@gmail.com for professional support and recommendations.

instruction: I want this English marketing email translated into Indonesian for a campaign in Southeast Asia.
description: Seed-X is a series of open-source multilingual translation language models, including an instruction model, a reinforcement learning model, and a reward model, designed to push the boundaries of translation capabilities within a 7 billion parameter scale. Seed-X exhibits state-of-the-art translation capabilities, on par with or outperforming ultra-large models like Gemini-2.5, Claude-3.5, and GPT-4, as validated by human evaluations and automatic metrics. With a compact 7B parameter count and mistral architecture, Seed-X offers outstanding translation performance in a lightweight and efficient package, ideal for deployment and inference. It excels on a highly challenging translation test set spanning diverse domains, including the internet, science and technology, office dialogues, e-commerce, biomedicine, finance, law, literature, and entertainment. The Seed-X-PPO-7B-GPTQ-Int8 model is a causal language model that supports multilingual translation among 28 languages, including Arabic, Czech, Danish, German, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian Bokmal, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, and Chinese. It is trained through pretraining and post-training stages and is quantized using GPTQ 8-bit. The model is specialized in multilingual translation and is not expected to support other tasks. The language tags at the end of the prompt are necessary for PPO training. There is no chat template, so the model should not be prompted in a multi-round conversation format. The model is evaluated on a diverse set of translation benchmarks, including FLORES-200, WMT-25, and a publicly released benchmark, accompanied by human evaluations. The model is recommended to be used with vllm version 0.8.0 and transformers version 4.51.3. The model is licensed under OpenMDW. It is recommended to use the Seed-X-PPO model, as its translation performance is superior to Seed-X-Instruct. Unofficial quantized versions for local deployment are not recommended. An official quantized model and a demo on Hugging Face Space are planned for release.

instruction: Make a video of a person hiking up a mountain with the sun rising behind them.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video capabilities. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model includes a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with a transition point determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). The A14B model series, part of Wan2.2, has two experts with approximately 14B parameters each, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. Wan2.2 also features a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. TI2V-5B supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. The model is trained on significantly larger data compared to its predecessor, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. Wan2.2 achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The repository also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions with a MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, with integration into ComfyUI and Diffusers. Computational efficiency tests show that Wan2.2 models perform well on different GPUs, with specific parameter settings for multi-GPU and single-GPU inference. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: Can you help me create a mask for this image to use in a video editing project?
description: U-2-Netp is a lightweight version of the U2Net model designed for efficient and effective image segmentation tasks, especially for generating masks. It retains the core architectural design of U2Net while being optimized for faster inference times and reduced memory usage. The model is based on a simplified version of the original U2Net architecture, designed to be more lightweight while still achieving high performance in segmentation tasks. The model consists of several stages with down-sampling and up-sampling paths, using Residual U-blocks (RSU) for enhanced feature representation. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 to match the original U-2-Net model.

instruction: I need to forecast the monthly revenue for a small business using historical sales data. How should I approach this?
description: Mitra regressor is a tabular foundation model that is pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random regressors. It is based on a 12-layer Transformer with 72 million parameters, pre-trained by incorporating an in-context learning paradigm. The model is designed for regression tasks and supports inference and training on tabular data. To use the model, users can install AutoGluon and follow a workflow that includes loading a dataset, splitting it into training and test sets, converting it into a TabularDataset, and creating a TabularPredictor configured for regression. The model can be trained on a subset of the training data, and its performance can be evaluated using a leaderboard on the test data. The model is available under the Apache-2.0 License. The development of Mitra is described in the paper titled 'Mitra: Mixed synthetic priors for enhancing tabular foundation models,' published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: How do I handle VRAM constraints when upscaling large video files?
description: ComfyUI-SeedVR2_VideoUpscaler is a high-quality video and image upscaling model designed for use with ComfyUI. It supports both ComfyUI integration and standalone CLI operation, enabling users to upscale images and videos with diffusion-based techniques. The model is available in multiple variants, including 3B and 7B parameter models, with options for FP16, FP8, and GGUF quantization to accommodate different VRAM requirements. It features temporal consistency to maintain coherence across video frames, multi-format support for RGB and RGBA, and any video length compatibility. The model includes memory optimization techniques such as BlockSwap technology, VAE tiling, and intelligent offloading to reduce VRAM usage. Performance features include torch.compile integration for speed improvements, multi-GPU CLI support, and model caching for faster batch processing. Quality control options include advanced color correction methods and noise injection controls. The model can be installed via ComfyUI Manager or manually, with models automatically downloaded from HuggingFace. It supports a modular node architecture in ComfyUI, including dedicated nodes for DiT model, VAE model, torch.compile settings, and the main upscaler. The standalone CLI provides batch processing capabilities, multi-GPU support, and sophisticated optimization options. The model has specific limitations, including a required batch_size following the 4n+1 formula and potential VRAM bottlenecks during VAE encoding/decoding. Best practices include starting with debug mode enabled, using appropriate optimization settings for VRAM constraints, and testing with short clips before processing long videos.

instruction: Make a video of a rainstorm transitioning into a rainbow over a small village.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model is trained on significantly larger datasets, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. It can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is among the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions, built with an MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, with each expert having about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching. Validation results show that the MoE-based Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality. The TI2V-5B model supports 720P video generation at 24 FPS and can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks in a unified framework. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and inference code and model weights are available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope. Computational efficiency tests have been conducted on different GPUs, with parameter settings and testing methodologies detailed in the documentation. The model has been compared to leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, demonstrating superior performance across multiple dimensions.

instruction: How can I adjust the perspective of an object in a photo to make it look more natural?
description: This repository contains the FP8 quantized version of the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It is designed for efficient inference while maintaining high-quality image editing capabilities. The model supports single-model image editing workflows and is compatible with standard pipelines such as Diffusers and custom ComfyUI nodes. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 significantly improves character consistency, allowing for imaginative edits based on an input portrait while preserving the identity and visual characteristics of the subject. It further enhances consistency in multi-person group photos, enabling high-fidelity fusion of two separate person images into a coherent group shot. The model integrates selected popular LoRAs directly into the base model, unlocking their effects without extra tuning. For example, it supports realistic lighting control and the generation of new viewpoints directly with the base model. It is also suitable for industrial design applications, such as batch industrial product design and material replacement for industrial components. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 introduces stronger geometric reasoning capability, such as directly generating auxiliary construction lines for design or annotation purposes. The model is distributed under the Apache-2.0 license. For usage, dependencies such as torch, diffusers, and safetensors must be installed, and the FP8 model can be loaded using the QwenImageEditPipeline from the diffusers library.

instruction: How many people are visible in this group photo from a party?
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. Molmo2 models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, a collection of datasets with highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. It has state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models with a similar size. Molmo2-8B is based on a vision backbone and outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science. The Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2 (training code, evaluations, intermediate checkpoints) will be made available at a later date, furthering the commitment to open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. It is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Please review the sources to determine if this model is appropriate for your use case. Molmo2-8B achieves an average score of 63.1 on 15 academic benchmarks.

instruction: Can you look up documents on the economic effects of automation in manufacturing?
description: This is a learned sparse retrieval model designed for efficient and effective document search. It encodes documents into 30522-dimensional sparse vectors, where the non-zero dimensions represent token indices in the vocabulary, and the corresponding weights indicate the importance of each token. For queries, it uses a tokenizer and a weight look-up table to generate sparse vectors. The similarity between query and document vectors is calculated using the inner product. The model is intended to be used with OpenSearch's neural sparse feature, which supports learned sparse retrieval using a Lucene inverted index. The model can also be used outside the OpenSearch cluster with HuggingFace's API. The model is trained on a variety of datasets, including MS MARCO, eli5_question_answer, squad_pairs, WikiAnswers, yahoo_answers_title_question, gooaq_pairs, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_body_body, wikihow, S2ORC_title_abstract, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_title-body_title-body, yahoo_answers_question_answer, searchQA_top5_snippets, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_title_title, yahoo_answers_title_answer, fever, fiqa, hotpotqa, nfcorpus, and scifact. The model's performance is benchmarked on a subset of the BEIR benchmark, including TrecCovid, NFCorpus, NQ, HotpotQA, FiQA, ArguAna, Touche, DBPedia, SCIDOCS, FEVER, Climate FEVER, SciFact, and Quora. The v3 series of models demonstrates better search relevance, efficiency, and inference speed compared to the v1 and v2 series, though specific advantages and disadvantages may vary across datasets. The model has an average NDCG@10 score of 0.546 and an average FLOPS of 1.7. The model is inference-free for retrieval and has 133M parameters. The model is available on the HuggingFace Hub and can be used with the Sentence Transformers library. The model's performance varies across different datasets, with the highest NDCG@10 scores observed on TrecCovid (0.734) and Quora (0.873), and the lowest on SCIDOCS (0.167).

instruction: I need this menu translated from Italian to Arabic—can you ensure the food terms are accurate?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and include 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios. It supports terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. HY-MT1.5-1.8B, despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, delivers comparable translation performance, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and supports real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. It supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios compared to its September open-source version. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models are available in multiple quantization formats, including FP8 and Int4. The models support a wide range of languages, including Chinese, English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, and Uyghur. The models can be used with the transformers library, and specific parameters are recommended for inference. The models do not have a default system prompt. The HY-MT1.5-1.8B and HY-MT1.5-7B models were open-sourced on Hugging Face in December 2025 and September 2025, respectively. The technical report provides more experimental results and analysis.

instruction: Could you help me edit this photo to remove any noise or graininess?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning is a collection of optimized models designed for image editing tasks, utilizing step distillation and quantization techniques to achieve high-efficiency inference performance. The model suite includes three core model files, each with distinct characteristics. One model, named Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-bf16.safetensors, is a 4-step distilled LoRA model with BF16 precision, offering a lightweight and fast inference option. Another model, Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-fp32.safetensors, is also a 4-step distilled LoRA model but with FP32 precision, providing high accuracy. The third model, qwen_image_edit_2511_fp8_e4m3fn_scaled_lightning.safetensors, is an FP8 quantized model with e4m3fn scaled precision, fused with a 4-step distilled LoRA and optimized for low-memory deployment. The models support two mainstream usage frameworks: the Qwen-Image-Lightning framework and the LightX2V lightweight video/image generation inference framework. The LoRA models are optimized through step distillation, reducing the original inference steps to just 4 steps, which results in a speedup of approximately 10 times faster than standard 40-step inference while maintaining image editing quality. Additionally, the FP8 quantized model balances performance and resource efficiency, reducing GPU memory usage by about 50% compared to FP32 while preserving editing fidelity. For technical support, users can open issues in the respective repositories for Qwen framework-specific questions or LightX2V integration questions.

instruction: This image has vertical text in Traditional Chinese. Can you extract it properly?
description: PP-OCRv5_server_rec is one of the PP-OCRv5_rec models, which are the latest generation text line recognition models developed by the PaddleOCR team. It is designed to efficiently and accurately support the recognition of four major languages—Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, and Japanese—as well as complex text scenarios such as handwriting, vertical text, pinyin, and rare characters using a single model. The model achieves key accuracy metrics across various text recognition tasks, including 0.5807 for Handwritten Chinese, 0.5806 for Handwritten English, 0.9013 for Printed Chinese, 0.8679 for Printed English, 0.7472 for Traditional Chinese, 0.6039 for Ancient Text, 0.7372 for Japanese, 0.5946 for General Scenario, 0.8384 for Pinyin, 0.7435 for Rotation, 0.9314 for Distortion, 0.6397 for Artistic Text, and an overall average of 0.8401. These metrics are calculated by marking an entire line as incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong, ensuring higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks through the PaddleOCR framework, either as a standalone module or as part of an OCR pipeline that includes document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition modules. It supports integration into projects via Python code and command-line tools, and it can be used with GPU devices for model inference. The model is also compatible with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline for layout analysis, which includes layout detection, general OCR, document image preprocessing, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The default model used in the PP-StructureV3 pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, and users can also specify a local model file if needed.

instruction: How can I create a search engine that understands the meaning behind user questions rather than just keywords?
description: EmbeddingGemma is a 300M parameter open embedding model developed by Google DeepMind, built from Gemma 3 with T5Gemma initialization and the same research and technology used to create Gemini models. It produces vector representations of text, making it suitable for search and retrieval tasks, including classification, clustering, and semantic similarity search. The model was trained on data in over 100 spoken languages, and its small size and on-device focus allow it to be deployed in environments with limited resources such as mobile phones, laptops, or desktops. EmbeddingGemma supports input text strings with a maximum context length of 2048 tokens and outputs numerical vector representations with a default embedding dimension size of 768. Smaller embedding sizes (512, 256, or 128) are available via Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL), which allows users to truncate the output embedding and re-normalize it for efficient and accurate representation. The model was trained on a dataset of approximately 320 billion tokens, including web documents, code and technical documents, and synthetic and task-specific data. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content, including CSAM and personal information. Training was conducted using the latest generation of TPUv5e hardware. The model was evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB (Multilingual, English, and Code versions), with performance metrics varying by embedding dimension and quantization configuration. EmbeddingGemma can be used for various tasks such as semantic similarity, classification, clustering, retrieval (document and query), question answering, fact verification, and code retrieval. Users are advised to be aware of potential limitations, including biases or gaps in training data, and the model's potential challenges in understanding language ambiguity and nuance. Ethical considerations include the risk of bias perpetuation, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to use de-biasing techniques, adhere to privacy regulations, and monitor for misuse. EmbeddingGemma is intended for responsible AI development and has demonstrated superior performance compared to similarly sized open models.

instruction: Detect and outline all the cars parked in this lot.
description: The OWLv2 model, short for Open-World Localization, was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby. It is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that can be used to query an image with one or multiple text queries. The model uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To use CLIP for detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is enabled by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with the class-name embeddings obtained from the text model. The authors first train CLIP from scratch and fine-tune it end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. One or multiple text queries per image can be used to perform zero-shot text-conditioned object detection. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is hoped that this model will enable researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. It is also hoped it can be used for interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers. The model is primarily imagined to be used by researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data. This was done through a combination of crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, which means that the data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets.

instruction: Would you transcribe this Russian language voice recording into text?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the wav2vec2-large-xlsr-53-russian model on the Common Voice 17.0 dataset for the Russian language. It is designed for automatic speech recognition tasks, specifically for converting audio input into transcribed text. The model was trained using the CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) approach and is based on the wav2vec2 architecture. It was trained for 5 epochs with a learning rate of 0.0001, a training batch size of 8, and an evaluation batch size of 4. The training used the ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, and a linear learning rate scheduler. Mixed precision training with Native AMP was enabled. The model was evaluated on the Common Voice 17.0 test set, achieving a final word error rate (WER) of 22.3989, a character error rate (CER) of 4.8036, and a sentence error rate (SER) of 75.4264. During training, the model's performance was monitored at regular intervals, with validation loss and error rates recorded at each step. The training process involved preprocessing audio data to a sampling rate of 16000 Hz, removing non-essential metadata columns, and using a custom data collator for padding and batching. The model was implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers library, PyTorch 2.6.0, and related dependencies including Datasets 3.6.0 and Tokenizers 0.21.1. The model is intended for speech recognition tasks in Russian but no specific limitations or caveats are explicitly stated in the provided text.

instruction: How does this image relate to the text 'a bustling street in a major city'?
description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE leverages a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale well for downstream applications. PE core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE core currently comes in three sizes: PE core G is the main checkpoint, with L and B models distilled from it. The L and B models include a class token for global aggregation in addition to an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower. PE core achieves strong results on zero-shot image classification and retrieval, as well as zero-shot video classification and retrieval. It performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a contrastive pretraining approach and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model is available for use through the OpenCLIP library and can be accessed via the provided GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated technical report and research papers.

instruction: Determine if the provided article is relevant to the question about the benefits of exercise for seniors.
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is built upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series and inherits their multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills. The Qwen3 Embedding series includes various sizes of embedding and reranking models (0.6B, 4B, and 8B), offering a comprehensive range of options for different use cases. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4 billion parameters and supports a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it can accept user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The model supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on the MTEB-R benchmark, 75.94 on the CMTEB-R benchmark, 72.74 on the MMTEB-R benchmark, 69.97 on the MLDR benchmark, 81.20 on the MTEB-Code benchmark, and 14.84 on the FollowIR benchmark. These results are based on top-100 candidates retrieved by a dense embedding model. The model is recommended to be used with customized instructions, as this can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. Developers are advised to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English. The model can be used with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher, or with the vLLM library version 0.8.5 or higher. It is recommended to enable flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory saving. The model processes input pairs consisting of a query and a document, and outputs a score indicating the relevance of the document to the query. The model is evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB, CMTEB, MMTEB, and MTEB-Code, and demonstrates strong performance across these tasks.

instruction: What's the predicted resin usage for this model based on its mesh statistics?
description: MiniRes is a small Python library and pretrained ensemble model designed to predict resin usage in grams for pre-supported 3D printed miniatures. It accepts tabular features exported from slicing and analysis tools such as UVtools, PrusaSlicer, and trimesh, and returns a single float per part representing the estimated resin usage. The model combines a Keras neural network with an XGBoost regressor. Model weights are hosted on Hugging Face and automatically downloaded and cached. The training data was generated from STL files provided by multiple miniature artists, which were sliced using PrusaSlicer with a consistent profile and a resin density of 1.1/g. UVtools was used to extract resin usage statistics, including the label of grams used, while trimesh extracted mesh-level features such as volume, surface area, implied mass, Euler characteristic, and bounding box sizes. Feature engineering added interaction and ratio features such as volume × mass, surface/volume, surface/mass, bounding-box/volume, and similar. MiniRes is trained on this tabular data to predict resin usage based on these engineered features. The model expects a fixed set of feature columns used during training, which can be inspected at runtime. Required features include kb, volume, surface_area, bbox_area, euler_number, scale, and surface_volume_ratio. The model raises an error if any required columns are missing. MiniRes includes two command-line scripts: stl-scanner.py, which scans STL files and exports geometric features to a CSV using trimesh, and mini-pricing.py, which calculates the total cost of each file based on resin usage and user-defined cost parameters. The model is distributed under the MIT License, which permits use, modification, and distribution of the software without restriction, provided the copyright and permission notices are included. The software is provided 'as is', without warranty of any kind, and the authors or copyright holders are not liable for any damages arising from its use.

instruction: How can you turn this transcript into a realistic audio conversation?
description: VibeVoice is a novel open-source text-to-speech (TTS) model designed to generate expressive, long-form, multi-speaker conversational audio, such as podcasts, from text. It addresses key limitations in traditional TTS systems, including scalability, speaker consistency, and natural turn-taking. A core innovation is the use of continuous speech tokenizers—Acoustic and Semantic—operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz. These tokenizers preserve audio fidelity while improving computational efficiency for long sequences. VibeVoice employs a next-token diffusion framework, combining a Large Language Model (LLM) to understand textual context and dialogue flow with a diffusion head to generate high-fidelity acoustic details. The model can synthesize speech up to 90 minutes long with up to 4 distinct speakers, surpassing the typical 1-2 speaker limits of many prior models. The LLM is integrated with specialized acoustic and semantic tokenizers and a diffusion-based decoding head. The Acoustic Tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks, achieving 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. Encoder and decoder components are approximately 340M parameters each. The Semantic Tokenizer mirrors the Acoustic Tokenizer's architecture (without VAE components) and is trained with an ASR proxy task. The Diffusion Head is a lightweight module with 4 layers and ~123M parameters, conditioned on LLM hidden states, and uses a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process with Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 65,536 tokens. Training involves separate pre-training of the Acoustic and Semantic tokenizers, followed by training of the LLM and diffusion head with a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k -> 16K -> 32K -> 64K). The LLM used is Qwen2.5, which typically uses its own text tokenizer. Audio is tokenized via the acoustic and semantic tokenizers. The VibeVoice-1.5B model has a context length of 64K and can generate up to ~90 minutes of audio. The model is intended for research purposes only and is not recommended for commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. It is limited to English and Chinese data, and outputs in other languages may be unintelligible or offensive. The model does not handle background noise, music, or other sound effects and does not generate overlapping speech segments in conversations. It may inherit biases, errors, or omissions from its base model (Qwen2.5 1.5b). High-quality synthetic speech can be misused for impersonation, fraud, or disinformation. Users are expected to use the model lawfully and to disclose the use of AI when sharing AI-generated content. An audible disclaimer is embedded in every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added for provenance verification. Inference requests are logged (hashed) for abuse pattern detection and to publish aggregated statistics quarterly. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and ethically, including securing appropriate rights and anonymizing data prior to use. The model is developed by members of Microsoft Research, and feedback or reports of issues can be sent to VibeVoice@microsoft.com.

instruction: Given a batch of images from a moving car, can you reconstruct the camera positions and surrounding geometry?
description: Pi-Cubed ($\pi^3$) is a feed-forward neural network designed for visual geometry reconstruction without the need for a fixed reference view. Traditional methods often rely on a designated reference frame, which can lead to instability if the reference is suboptimal. In contrast, $\pi^3$ uses a fully permutation-equivariant architecture to directly predict affine-invariant camera poses and scale-invariant local point maps from an unordered set of images. This design allows the model to be robust to input ordering and highly scalable. An emergent property of the model's simple, bias-free design is the learning of a dense and structured latent representation of the camera pose manifold. Without complex priors or training schemes, $\pi^3$ achieves state-of-the-art performance on tasks such as camera pose estimation, monocular and video depth estimation, and dense point map estimation. The model takes a tensor of images as input and outputs a dictionary containing the reconstructed geometry, including a global point cloud, per-view local point maps, confidence scores for local points, and camera-to-world transformation matrices. The model is implemented in PyTorch and can be run on a batch of images, with optional support for mixed precision inference on compatible GPUs. The model is available for academic use under a 2-clause BSD License, and commercial use requires contacting the authors.

instruction: Generate a 12-second clip of a baby elephant playing with a water spout in a savannah setting.
description: HunyuanVideo-1.5 is a lightweight video generation model with 8.3 billion parameters, designed to deliver state-of-the-art visual quality and motion coherence while enabling efficient inference on consumer-grade GPUs. It supports both text-to-video (T2V) and image-to-video (I2V) generation across multiple durations and resolutions. The model integrates an advanced DiT (Diffusion Transformer) architecture with a 3D causal VAE, achieving compression ratios of 16× in spatial dimensions and 4× along the temporal axis. A key innovation is the SSTA (Selective and Sliding Tile Attention) mechanism, which prunes redundant spatiotemporal key-value blocks, significantly reducing computational overhead for long video sequences and accelerating inference. HunyuanVideo-1.5 also features a video super-resolution network that enhances sharpness and corrects distortions, allowing outputs to be upscaled to 1080p. The model is trained using a multi-stage, progressive strategy that refines motion coherence, aesthetic quality, and human preference alignment. It supports various inference optimizations, including step-distilled models for faster generation (e.g., 8 or 12 steps for 480p I2V), sparse attention, and caching techniques like deepcache, teacache, and taylorcache. The model is available on Hugging Face Diffusers and supports integration with tools like ComfyUI and LightX2V. It is trained using the Muon optimizer, which accelerates convergence and improves training stability. Evaluation results show that HunyuanVideo-1.5 achieves professional-grade content generation and sets a new state-of-the-art among open-source models. The model is accessible for developers and creators, with code, weights, and training scripts open-sourced to lower the cost of video creation and research.

instruction: Is it possible to extract the English text from this image of a printed page?
description: en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is a text line recognition model developed by the PaddleOCR team as part of the PP-OCRv5_rec series. It is designed to efficiently and accurately recognize English text. The model achieves an accuracy of 85.3 percent, where a line is considered incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong. This strict evaluation ensures higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks and is part of a larger OCR pipeline that includes modules for document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition. The en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec model is intended to be used in conjunction with other models in the pipeline to address complex real-world OCR challenges. The model supports integration into projects through the PaddleOCR library, which requires installation of PaddlePaddle and PaddleOCR. The model can be run using a command-line interface or integrated into Python code for inference. The default model in the pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, so specifying en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is necessary for use in the pipeline. The model can also be used with a local model file if provided.

instruction: Create a video of a space shuttle launching into orbit, with synchronized rocket engine sounds and ambient space noise.
description: LTX-2 is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is based on a diffusion approach, supporting English language inputs. LTX-2 is intended for tasks involving the generation of audio-visual content, including image-to-video generation. The model is available in multiple versions, including a full model, quantized variants (fp8, nvfp4), a distilled version, and LoRA versions. Additional components include spatial and temporal upscalers for increasing resolution and frame rate in multi-stage pipelines. The model can be used locally with a direct use license, and is supported in the PyTorch framework and the Diffusers library. The codebase is compatible with Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. The base model is fully trainable, and training for specific aspects such as motion, style, or likeness can be completed in under an hour in many settings. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify societal biases, may not perfectly match prompts, is influenced by prompting style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and may produce lower quality audio when no speech is present.

instruction: Would it be possible to remove the background from this portrait and keep the hair details?
description: BiRefNet is a bilateral reference network designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation, particularly for matting tasks. The model is adaptable to arbitrary input shapes, ranging from 256x256 to 2304x2304, and demonstrates robust performance on inputs of any shape. It was trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS (Dichotomous Image Segmentation), HRSOD (High-Resolution Salient Object Detection), and COD (Complementary Object Detection). The architecture of BiRefNet is not explicitly detailed in the text, but it supports inference on arbitrary resolutions without the need for resizing. The model can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or via local code and weights. It is implemented in PyTorch and supports GPU inference. The model is associated with the research paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model is available through a GitHub repository and HuggingFace, and online inference demos are provided. The authors acknowledge support from Freepik and Fal for GPU resources and from Not-lain for deployment assistance on HuggingFace.

instruction: What is the most likely scene depicted in this picture?
description: MobileNet V1 is a pre-trained model on ImageNet-1k at a resolution of 192x192. It was introduced by Howard et al and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide a model card. MobileNets are designed to be small, low-latency, and low-power models that can be adapted for various use cases by adjusting their parameters to meet resource constraints. They support tasks such as classification, detection, embeddings, and segmentation, similar to other large-scale models like Inception. MobileNets are optimized to run efficiently on mobile devices and balance trade-offs between latency, size, and accuracy while performing favorably compared to other models in the literature. The model can be used for image classification, predicting one of 1001 classes, which includes the 1000 ImageNet classes plus an additional 'background' class. The model and its feature extractor are currently supported in PyTorch.

instruction: Find the page in this research paper that mentions the results of the second experiment.
description: ColPali is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model was introduced in a paper and first released in a version trained with a batch size of 256 for 3 epochs on the same data as the original ColPali model. This version is trained with right padding for queries to fix unwanted tokens in the query encoding and stems from the fixed `vidore/colpaligemma-3b-pt-448-base` to guarantee deterministic projection layer initialization. It was trained for 5 epochs, with in-batch negatives and hard mined negatives, and a warmup of 1000 steps to help reduce non-English language collapse. The data used is the same as the ColPali data described in the paper. The model is built iteratively starting from an off-the-shelf model. It is fine-tuned to create and fed the patch-embeddings output by SigLIP to an LLM to create. One benefit of inputting image patch embeddings through a language model is that they are natively mapped to a latent space similar to textual input (query). This enables leveraging the strategy to compute interactions between text tokens and image patches, which enables a step-change improvement in performance compared to BiPali. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made up of pages from web-crawled PDF documents augmented with VLM-generated (Claude-3 Sonnet) pseudo-questions. The training set is fully English by design, enabling the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used both in the training and validation sets to prevent evaluation contamination. A validation set is created with 2% of the samples to tune hyperparameters. Multilingual data is present in the pretraining corpus of the language model (Gemma-2B) and may occur during PaliGemma-3B's multimodal training. All models are trained for 1 epoch on the train set in bfloat16 format, using low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model, as well as the final randomly initialized projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is done on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, potentially limiting its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieving derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (PaliGemma) is under the Gemma license, while the adapters attached to the model are under the MIT license.

instruction: Please check this video and let me know if it contains any violent content.
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 600 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 28 videos of abuse, 18 of arrest, 16 of arson, 62 of assault, 120 of burglary, 54 of explosion, 48 of fighting, 58 of road accidents, 184 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, 46 of stealing, and 72 of vandalism. The dataset was split into 384 training videos, 96 validation videos, and 120 test videos. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. It processes 16 frames per video clip at an input resolution of 224x224 pixels per frame. The model was trained for 15 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and the AdamW optimizer. On the validation set, it achieved a loss of 1.4159, accuracy of 0.7188, precision of 0.7207, recall of 0.7188, and F1 score of 0.7190. On the test set, it achieved a loss of 1.7586, accuracy of 0.6833, precision of 0.6963, recall of 0.6833, and F1 score of 0.6802. The model was trained in 69.5 minutes and achieved a best accuracy of 0.7188. The model is intended for use in research and development in video analysis, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes in computer vision and AI safety. It is not suitable for unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, or privacy violations. The model may have limitations in generalizing to all types of violence, may miss context in longer sequences due to its 16-frame clip approach, and may be affected by environmental factors such as lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It may also misclassify intense but non-violent activities. The model was developed using the HuggingFace Transformers framework and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. It is based on the MCG-NJU/videomae-base model and is available for use with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model was trained on 2025-06-02 11:52:12 UTC and is intended for use by researchers, security professionals, and developers.

instruction: Which quant types are best suited for systems with limited storage space?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a model, including imatrix and IQ quants, which are available for download. The quants are sorted by size, though not necessarily by quality, with IQ quants often being preferable to similar-sized non-IQ quants. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S, i1-IQ1_M, i1-IQ2_XXS, i1-IQ2_XS, i1-IQ2_S, i1-IQ2_M, i1-Q2_K_S, i1-Q2_K, i1-IQ3_XXS, i1-IQ3_XS, i1-Q3_K_S, i1-IQ3_S, i1-IQ3_M, i1-Q3_K_M, i1-Q3_K_L, i1-IQ4_XS, i1-Q4_0, i1-IQ4_NL, i1-Q4_K_S, i1-Q4_K_M, i1-Q4_1, i1-Q5_K_S, i1-Q5_K_M, and i1-Q6_K. Each quant type has a specified size in gigabytes and associated notes, such as 'for the desperate', 'very low quality', or 'recommended'. An imatrix file is also provided for creating custom quants. Users are advised to refer to external resources for detailed instructions on using GGUF files and concatenating multi-part files. The model's quantization work was made possible through the use of company servers and a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: How can I detect the gender of the speaker in a phone call recording?
description: Common-Voice-Gender-Detection is a fine-tuned version of `facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h` for binary audio classification, specifically trained to detect speaker gender as female or male. This model uses the `Wav2Vec2ForSequenceClassification` architecture for efficient and accurate voice-based gender classification. The model achieves a precision of 0.9705 for female and 0.9943 for male, with a recall of 0.9916 for female and 0.9799 for male. The F1-scores are 0.9809 for female and 0.9870 for male, with an overall accuracy of 0.9846 across 6545 samples. The model is intended for use in speech analytics, conversational AI personalization, voice dataset curation, research applications, and multimedia content tagging. It is designed to assist in analyzing speaker demographics in call centers or customer service recordings, adjust tone or dialogue in voice assistants based on gender detection, automatically tag or filter voice datasets by speaker gender, enable linguistic and acoustic research involving gender-specific speech patterns, and automate metadata generation for gender identification in podcasts, interviews, or video content.

instruction: I want to optimize the audio for a podcast. Can you suggest a way to make it smaller but still clear?
description: Distill-NeuCodec is a version of NeuCodec that features a compatible, distilled encoder. The distilled encoder is 10 times smaller in parameter count and uses approximately 7.5 times fewer MACs at inference time. The distilled model includes the following adjustments: the notoriously slow acoustic encoder is replaced with a new acoustic encoder, reducing the parameter count from 70 million to 36 million; the semantic encoder is replaced, reducing the parameter count from 600 million to 21 million. The model is developed by Neuphonic and is categorized as a Neural Audio Codec. It is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model was trained using the same data as the full model, with an additional distillation loss that computes the mean squared error between the outputs of the distilled and original encoders.

instruction: Is there a non-compete clause in this employment contract?
description: This model is designed for the task of Question Answering on Legal Documents. It is a RoBERTa-based model developed by Mohammed Rakib and is shared under the MIT license. The model is intended to be used for contractual language understanding, particularly for making legal contracts more comprehensible to non-legal professionals. It is trained on the Contract Understanding Atticus Dataset (CUAD), which is the largest open-source contract dataset available. The model has been optimized through various pre-processing experiments and hyperparameter tuning, achieving an AUPR score of 46.6% compared to 42.6% for the original RoBERTa-base model on the same dataset. The model is part of an end-to-end contract understanding application that can highlight relevant clauses in a contract and provide descriptions to aid in due diligence before signing. The system can process both digital and scanned (non-searchable) contracts using Tesseract OCR. The model was developed using V100/P100 hardware from Google Colab Pro, with Python and the Transformers library as the software stack. The model is described in the paper titled 'An Open Source Contractual Language Understanding Application Using Machine Learning' by Nawar, Rakib, Hai, and Haq, published in 2022.

instruction: I’m building an app and need to automatically flag inappropriate content. Can you analyze this image?
description: This model is a vision transformer based on the EVA architecture, fine-tuned for NSFW content classification. It has been trained to detect four categories (neutral, low, medium, high) of visual content using 100,000 synthetically labeled images. The model can be used as a binary (true/false) classifier or to obtain full output probabilities. It outperforms other publicly available models in internal benchmarks, offering the ability to select the NSFW level that suits the use case. The model was trained for 3 epochs on 3 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs using two sets: training and validation. No images with a cosine similarity higher than 0.92 were included to avoid duplicates and biases between training and validation. A custom loss was created to minimize predictions that are lower than the true class. For example, it is very rare for an image labeled as 'high' to be predicted as 'neutral' (this only happens 0.46% of the time). The model supports efficient batch processing for multiple images and is recommended to be used in bf16 for optimal performance. Using torch tensors is especially indicated in cases such as use with text to image models or similar because the output is already in tensor format. The model has been evaluated on a manually labeled dataset with careful attention to avoiding biases (gender, ethnicity, etc.). In global performance comparisons, the model outperforms AdamCodd and Falconsai in accuracy, particularly on high and neutral labels. It offers greater granularity, performing excellently at identifying low and medium NSFW content. Falconsai may classify some medium and low images as not NSFW or as safe for work (SFW), which could lead to unexpected results. AdamCodd classifies both low and medium categories as NSFW, which may not be desirable depending on the use case. Furthermore, 10% of images in low and medium are considered SFW. In AI-generated content detection, the model is the best option to detect NSFW content, outperforming other models. Falconsai should be avoided for AI-generated content to prevent prediction errors. The model is licensed under the MIT License and is based on the EVA architecture as described in the paper 'EVA-02: A Visual Representation for Neon Genesis'.

instruction: I want to build a system that uses both text and images for search. How can I create embeddings for these inputs?
description: The embedding model trained by Jina AI is a version of the Qwen2.5-VL model with the code adapter merged into the base weights. This architecture modification enables native compatibility with vLLM without requiring custom adapter-handling code. The model is designed for multimodal multilingual retrieval and supports tasks involving text and image inputs. It is intended to be used for encoding prompts, including text and image data, into embeddings. The model can process text prompts for queries and passages, as well as image prompts with associated descriptions. When processing image prompts, the model identifies and extracts embeddings specifically from the vision tokens. For text-only prompts, it uses all tokens to generate embeddings. The embeddings are pooled and normalized to produce the final output. The model is compatible with the vLLM framework and can be initialized with specific configurations such as data type and pooling settings. It is important to note that the model requires a specific method to extract and process embeddings, particularly for image data, which involves identifying vision token ranges and applying normalization.

instruction: Extract the text and layout elements from this document while preserving the original language and formatting.
description: dots.ocr is a multilingual document layout parsing model that unifies layout detection and content recognition within a single vision-language model while maintaining good reading order. Built on a compact 1.7B-parameter LLM foundation, it achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance for text, tables, and reading order, with formula recognition results comparable to much larger models like Doubao-1.5 and Gemini2.5-Pro. It demonstrates robust parsing capabilities for low-resource languages, achieving decisive advantages across both layout detection and content recognition on in-house multilingual document benchmarks. By leveraging a single vision-language model, dots.ocr offers a significantly more streamlined architecture than conventional methods that rely on complex, multi-model pipelines. Switching between tasks is accomplished simply by altering the input prompt, proving that a VLM can achieve competitive detection results compared to traditional detection models like DocLayout-YOLO. Built upon a compact 1.7B LLM, dots.ocr provides faster inference speeds than many other high-performing models based on larger foundations. It supports a range of layout categories, including 'Caption', 'Footnote', 'Formula', 'List-item', 'Page-footer', 'Page-header', 'Picture', 'Section-header', 'Table', 'Text', and 'Title'. Text extraction and formatting rules specify that 'Picture' elements omit text, 'Formula' elements are formatted as LaTeX, 'Table' elements as HTML, and all others as Markdown. The output text must be the original text from the image, with no translation, and all layout elements must be sorted according to human reading order. The final output is a single JSON object. dots.ocr is not yet perfect for high-complexity tables and formula extraction, and pictures in documents are currently not parsed. The model may fail to parse under certain conditions, such as when the character-to-pixel ratio is excessively high or when continuous special characters are present. It is not yet optimized for high-throughput processing of large PDF volumes. Future work includes achieving more accurate table and formula parsing, enhancing OCR capabilities for broader generalization, and developing a more general-purpose perception model based on VLMs that integrates general detection, image captioning, and OCR tasks into a unified framework. Parsing the content of pictures in documents is also a key priority for future work.

instruction: Show me a motion clip of a person doing a high-intensity workout routine.
description: Semantic Motion Generation (SMoG) is a state-of-the-art text-to-motion synthesis model that leverages CLIP semantics and Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-enhanced transformers to generate realistic, diverse 3D human motions from textual descriptions. Unlike conventional approaches, SMoG replaces linear layers in transformer architectures with KANLayers, enabling adaptive nonlinear feature learning and superior motion-text alignment. It generates the sequence of SMPL body model movement for each frame. SMoG is built on MotionCLIP — a 3D motion autoencoder trained to reconstruct poses using natural language. It employs a latent space representing abstract, compressed data features non-trivially present in the input space. This approach reduces reliance on classical data labeling by using contrastive learning to distinguish similarity, identity, or difference between text-motion pairs. During training, action-text pairs are matched for similarity (positive) or dissimilarity (negative), maximizing positive pair similarity and minimizing negative pair similarity. The latent space enables semantic alignment between input text and generated motion, producing meaningful interpretations. For example, inputting 'wings' or a cultural reference could generate a wing-like arm motion without explicit training examples. The model is trained not only to reconstruct motions but also to align their latent representations with text labels in CLIP space. The KANLayer, core to Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KAN), implements a hybrid function approximation approach based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem. This theorem states that any multivariate continuous function can be represented as a superposition of univariate functions, achieved in KANLayer via linear transformations and nonlinear B-spline components. Structurally, the layer has two parallel branches: a base branch performing linear transformation with activation (default: SiLU), and a spline branch adding nonlinearity through adaptive B-splines.
