The model is designed for both anatomical and pathological structures across multiple imaging modalities (CT, PET, MRI), being trained on 1000+ familiar concepts while maintaining strong generalization to related unseen classes through its multi-stage vision-language fusion architecture. VoxTell supports free-text prompting to generate 3D segmentation masks using natural language descriptions, multi-modality support across CT, PET, and MRI imaging modalities, comprehensive anatomy coverage including brain, thorax, abdomen, pelvis, musculoskeletal system, and extremities, and flexible granularity from coarse anatomical labels to fine-grained pathological findings. VoxTell v1.1 is the recommended version, trained on all datasets from the paper and additional sources (190 datasets, ~68,500 volumes), with a 95% probability of sampling from the semantic datasets corpus and 5% probability from image-text-mask triplets from instance-focused datasets. It is recommended for general application, inference, and fine-tuning, maximizing supervision and concept coverage for stronger general-purpose performance. VoxTell v1.0 is deprecated and not recommended for general use, having been used for the experiments in the paper but containing known issues that have been fixed in v1.1. VoxTell employs a multi-stage vision-language fusion approach, including an image encoder that processes 3D volumetric input into latent feature representations, a prompt encoder using a frozen model to embed text prompts, a prompt decoder that transforms text queries and image latents into multi-scale text features, and an image decoder that fuses visual and textual information at multiple resolutions using MaskFormer-style query-image fusion with deep supervision. The model is intended for research in vision-language models for medical image analysis, text-promptable or automated segmentation of anatomical structures in medical imaging, and identification and delineation of pathological findings. It is not intended for clinical diagnosis without expert radiologist review, real-time emergency medical decision-making, or commercial use. VoxTell achieves state-of-the-art performance on anatomical and pathological segmentation tasks across multiple medical imaging benchmarks. Performance may vary on imaging modalities or anatomical regions underrepresented in training data. Prompting structures absent from the image and never seen on this modality may lead to undesired results. Text prompt quality and specificity affects segmentation accuracy. The model is not validated for direct clinical use without expert review. Images must be in RAS orientation for correct anatomical localization, and performance may degrade on images with very uncommon voxel spacings. The model does not resample images to a standardized spacing for faster inference. Known issues include compatibility problems with PyTorch 2.9.0 causing OOM errors during inference, which should be avoided by using PyTorch 2.8.0 or earlier.

instruction: Identify the specific cancer types discussed in this research paper on tumor mutations.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for high-precision biomedical entity recognition in the domain of cancer genetics. It is optimized for identifying and extracting cancer-related genetic entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports a wide range of biomedical entity types, including but not limited to amino acids, anatomical systems, cancers, cells, cellular components, developing anatomical structures, genes or gene products, immaterial anatomical entities, multi-tissue structures, organs, organisms, organism subdivisions, organism substances, pathological formations, simple chemicals, and tissues. Both B- and I- tags are supported for these entity types. The model is trained on the BIONLP2013_CG dataset, a specialized corpus for cancer genetics entities and gene regulation in oncology research. It is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.79, precision of 0.79, recall of 0.80, and accuracy of 0.89. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is production-ready and validated on clinical benchmarks, and it is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: How accurate is the depth estimation for objects in low-light conditions?
description: Depth Anything 3 (DA3) is a model that predicts spatially consistent geometry from an arbitrary number of visual inputs, with or without known camera poses. It is designed for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation, using a compact foundation model with a unified depth-ray representation. The model is based on a single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, which serves as a backbone without architectural specialization. DA3 uses a singular depth-ray prediction target, eliminating the need for complex multi-task learning. It is trained using a teacher-student training paradigm and achieves a level of detail and generalization comparable to Depth Anything 2 (DA2). The model sets a new state-of-the-art across all tasks on a new visual geometry benchmark, surpassing prior SOTA VGGT by an average of 44.3% in camera pose accuracy and 25.1% in geometric accuracy. It also outperforms DA2 in monocular depth estimation. DA3 supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. The model is developed by ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer for visual geometry. It is trained exclusively on public academic datasets and may have limitations on certain domain-specific images. Performance may vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: I need to remove the background from this photo of a pet — is that something you can do?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference approach, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference tasks and can be deployed locally or through an inference endpoint. It is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded with weights from HuggingFace or local storage. The model includes functionality for image preprocessing, prediction, and post-processing, including alpha channel generation for object extraction. The model weights are available in the official repository, and the model can be used with or without the associated GitHub codebase. The model has been developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model's implementation and weights are available on GitHub and HuggingFace, and online demos for inference are provided. The model's development has been supported by GPU resources from Freepik and Fal, as well as assistance with deployment from Not-Lain.

instruction: Create an image of a fashion boutique with the name 'Elegant Threads' written in a stylish font on the window.
description: LongCat-Image is an open-source and bilingual (Chinese-English) foundation model for image generation, designed to address core challenges in multilingual text rendering, photorealism, deployment efficiency, and developer accessibility prevalent in current leading models. With 6B parameters, LongCat-Image surpasses numerous open-source models that are several times larger across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating the immense potential of efficient model design. The model demonstrates superior accuracy and stability in rendering common Chinese characters compared to existing state-of-the-art open-source models and achieves industry-leading coverage of the Chinese dictionary. Through an innovative data strategy and training framework, LongCat-Image achieves remarkable photorealism in generated images. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, the target text must be enclosed within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: I have a new molecule—can you predict its toxicity based on the SMILES input?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict toxicity-related molecular properties. The model is designed to perform regression tasks, taking SMILES strings as input and using graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an architecture based on AttentiveFP with enhanced atom and bond features. The model uses 10-dimensional enhanced atom features and 6-dimensional enhanced bond features. The hyperparameters include 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, 0.1 dropout, 0.0005 learning rate, 0.0001 weight decay, 32 batch size, 50 epochs, and 10 patience. The model was trained on the TMP dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Tag all the entities that pertain to blood cancer in this section of a clinical guideline.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for clinical entity recognition, specifically for entities related to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). It is optimized for high-precision biomedical entity recognition and is trained on a curated CLL dataset. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It supports the identification and classification of biomedical entities, including the entity types `B-CL` and `I-CL`. The model is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The CLL corpus, on which the model is trained, is a domain-specific biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on entities related to chronic lymphocytic leukemia, a type of blood cancer. The dataset includes annotations for CLL-specific terminology, biomarkers, treatment entities, and clinical concepts relevant to hematology and oncology research. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.78, recall of 0.94, and accuracy of 0.97. Comparative performance on the CLL dataset shows that the model ranks fifth in F1 score with a value of 0.8547, compared to other models with F1 scores ranging from 0.8364 to 0.9575. The model is based on the distilbert-base-cased architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with the AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It performs token classification for named entity recognition, taking tokenized biomedical text as input and producing BIO-tagged entity predictions as output. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research in biomedical NLP. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Can you extract the body key points for each person in this image?
description: YOLO26-N-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model has an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 57.2 and a mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 83.3. It runs at 40.3 ms on CPU (ONNX) and 1.8 ms on T4 TensorRT10, with 2.9M parameters and 7.5B FLOPs. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and makes inference faster, lighter, and easier to deploy. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and improves deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing. The model can be installed via pip and used with the ultralytics library for inference on images.

instruction: How can I tell if this image is authentic or if it's been created using synthetic means?
description: AIRealNet is a binary image classifier designed to distinguish AI-generated images from real human photographs. It is optimized for detecting conventional AI-generated content while adhering to strict privacy standards, avoiding the use of personal or sensitive images. The model leverages the SwinV2 Tiny architecture as its backbone, achieving high accuracy while maintaining a lightweight design suitable for practical deployment. AIRealNet was fine-tuned on a 14k-image dataset from the `Parveshiiii/AI-vs-Real` repository, which is balanced between AI-generated and real images (60% AI-generated, 40% real human images). The training data was sourced from public datasets and AI generation tools, with intentional exclusion of edited personal photos. The model demonstrates high accuracy and robustness on public datasets, though it has limitations in detecting subtle edits, such as nano-scale modifications, and cannot identify AI-modified real-person images due to privacy and ethical guidelines. Performance may also vary on images from unseen AI generators or unconventional content. AIRealNet is intended for use in detecting AI-generated imagery on social media, research publications, and digital media platforms, assisting content moderators, researchers, and fact-checkers in identifying synthetic media. It is not intended for legal verification without human corroboration. The model preprocesses images to 256x256 resolution, extracts features using the SwinV2 Tiny vision transformer, and outputs binary classification probabilities for AI-generated versus real human images. Ethical considerations include a privacy-first approach, responsible deployment with human review, and transparency in communicating limitations and dataset design. Future work aims to improve detection of subtle AI-generated edits, expand training data with diverse AI generators, and explore multi-modal detection capabilities.

instruction: How can I enhance the clarity of speech in a video with background noise?
description: Dolphin is an efficient audio-visual speech separation model that extracts target speech from noisy environments by combining acoustic and visual (lip movement) cues. It achieves state-of-the-art performance while being 6× faster and using 50% fewer parameters than previous methods. The model is designed to separate speech using both audio and video inputs, where the video input consists of grayscale lip region frames. It supports tasks such as hearing aid enhancement, video conferencing noise suppression, in-car speech extraction, AR/VR communication, and edge device deployment. The model architecture includes a DP-LipCoder video encoder, an audio encoder, a global-local attention separator, and an audio decoder. The DP-LipCoder uses a dual-path architecture with visual compression and semantic encoding, vector quantization for discrete lip semantic tokens, and knowledge distillation from AV-HuBERT, with only 8.5M parameters. The audio encoder extracts multi-scale acoustic features, and the global-local attention separator uses a single-pass TDANet-based architecture with global attention for long-range dependencies and local attention for noise suppression. The model is trained on the VoxCeleb2 dataset with 2-speaker mixtures at 0dB SNR, using a SI-SNR loss function. On the VoxCeleb2 benchmark, it achieves a SI-SNRi of 16.1 dB, SDRi of 16.3 dB, PESQ of 3.45, ESTOI of 0.93, with 51.3M parameters, 417G MACs, and an inference speed of 0.015s per 4s-clip. The model requires frontal or near-frontal face views for optimal performance, works best with 25fps video input, and is trained on English speech, which may require fine-tuning for other languages. Performance degrades with severe occlusions or low lighting.

instruction: Can I run this on my laptop with an integrated GPU?
description: MelBandRoformer-GGUF is a repository containing Mel-Band-Roformer models converted to the GGUF format, enabling high-performance, cross-platform local inference using a specific inference engine. The repository provides GGUF quantized versions of original models, including a model referred to as (voc_fv6). Various quantized versions are available to meet different hardware requirements. The q8_0 quantization type is recommended, offering 8-bit quantization that significantly reduces memory usage and bandwidth requirements while maintaining audio quality almost identical to FP32. The fp16 quantization type provides 16-bit floating point precision, suitable for scenarios requiring maximum precision. Lower-bit quantization types, including q4_0, q4_1, q5_0, and q5_1, are available for devices with low VRAM or RAM. Norm and Bias weights remain in FP32 to ensure numerical stability. The model is intended to be used with a specific command-line tool. Users are instructed to download the executable for their system or compile it themselves, download the desired .gguf file from the repository, and run inference using the provided command-line interface. Basic usage involves specifying the model file, input audio file, and output audio file. Advanced usage allows for adjusting chunk size and overlap to optimize quality.

instruction: How is the term 'due process' typically interpreted in North Carolina court rulings?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of a base model on a dataset consisting of 75 years of North Carolina case law, including constitution, statutes, and administrative code, divided into 500 token chunks. The model was trained for two epochs. It is intended for natural language processing tasks specifically related to North Carolina state case law and is expected to perform poorly on inputs involving federal or other jurisdictional law. During training, all numbers, special characters, and citations were removed from the corpus to test whether BERT would rely on semantic pattern recognition. The model achieved a top-1 accuracy of 73.10% and a top-5 accuracy of 88.00% using a legal terms specific masking strategy. The training used a learning rate of 2e-05, a train and evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler with 500 warmup steps, and 1.5 training epochs. The model was developed using Transformers 4.57.3, Pytorch 2.9.0+cu126, Datasets 4.0.0, and Tokenizers 0.22.2. The model achieved an evaluation loss of 1.5688. It is possible that the BERT model retains some latent citation bias in its weights, and the next step is to create a BERT model from scratch using the citation ablation techniques developed for this corpus sanitization.

instruction: Could you explain the medical terms related to this skin lesion?
description: DermLIP is a vision-language model for dermatology, trained on the Derm1M dataset, which is the largest dermatological image-text corpus to date. The model variant PanDerm-base-w-PubMed-256 uses domain-specific pretraining to achieve superior performance compared to other DermLIP variants. It is a pretrained vision-language model with a CLIP-style architecture. The vision encoder is PanDerm-base, and the text encoder is PubMedBert-256. The model processes images at a resolution of 224×224 pixels. It was trained on 403,563 skin image-text pairs from the Derm1M dataset, which includes both dermoscopic and clinical images. The training objective was to minimize image-text contrastive loss, and the model was trained using an Nvidia H200 GPU with approximately 90GB of memory over a period of about 9.5 hours. The intended uses of the model include zero-shot classification, few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and concept annotation or explanation. The model is distributed under the cc-by-nc-nd-4.0 license.

instruction: How do I create a visual fingerprint of a video for quick reference?
description: The VideoMAE-v2 (giant-sized model, Pretrained on UnlabeledHybrid-1M) is a model pre-trained for 1200 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is intended for video feature extraction. It can be used by loading the model and processor from the 'transformers' library, processing video input as a list of numpy arrays, and extracting features through the model. The model is based on a video masked autoencoder architecture with dual masking, as described in the associated research. The model is available for use under the OpenGVLab repository. The paper was presented at the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) in June 2023 and is also available on arXiv with the identifier 2303.16727.

instruction: I need a concise version of this article for a presentation—can you help?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the INTELLECT-3 model by PrimeIntellect, using the llama.cpp release b7127. The quantizations are based on a dataset from a combined source, including a subset of combined_all_small.parquet from Ed Addario. The model can be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format includes a system prompt, user input, and assistant response. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. For models larger than 50GB, the files are split and can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool. The Q4_0 quantization format has been deprecated in favor of online repacking. The I-quants (IQX_X) are newer and offer better performance for their size, especially when using cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD), but may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, desired speed, and quality. The model has been benchmarked on an AVX2 system, showing performance improvements for certain quantizations. Credits are given to several contributors for assistance with dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: Show me a video of a car driving through a desert at high speed with sand swirling around it.
description: The FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is part of the FastWan2.1 series, which is finetuned using a novel Sparse-distill strategy. This strategy combines DMD and VSA in a single training process, leveraging distillation to reduce diffusion steps and sparse attention to minimize attention computations, resulting in faster video generation. The model is based on Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers and supports efficient 3-step inference, producing high-quality videos at a resolution of 61×448×832. It is trained on the FastVideo 480P Synthetic Wan dataset, which contains 600k synthetic latents. The model can achieve up to 16 FPS on a single H100 GPU. While it is trained on 61×448×832 resolution, it can generate videos at any resolution, though quality may degrade. The model is trained on 4 nodes with 32 H200 GPUs in total, using a global batch size of 64, gradient checkpointing, gradient accumulation steps of 2, and a learning rate of 1e-5. The VSA attention sparsity is set to 0.8, and training runs for 4000 steps, which takes approximately 12 hours. The model supports a wide range of GPUs from H100 to 4090 and is also compatible with Mac users. If the FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is used for research, the associated papers should be cited.

instruction: Could you search for and purchase a pair of wireless noise-canceling headphones from Amazon?
description: Fara-7B is Microsoft's first agentic small language model (SLM) designed specifically for computer use. With 7 billion parameters, it is an ultra-compact Computer Use Agent (CUA) that achieves state-of-the-art performance within its size class and is competitive with larger, more resource-intensive agentic systems. Fara-7B is a multimodal decoder-only language model that takes an image (screenshot) and text context as input and directly predicts thoughts and actions with grounded arguments. It is based on Qwen 2.5-VL (7B). The model accepts user goals (text), current screenshots, and a history of previous outputs (thoughts and actions) as input. It has a context length of 128k and produces generated text in response to the input, with a chain-of-thought block followed by a tool call block to indicate the action. Fara-7B was trained using 64 H100 GPUs over 2.5 days. It is a static model trained on public and private data, with training occurring between October 26th and October 29th, 2025. It was released on November 24th, 2025, under an MIT license. Fara-7B is specialized for taking actions on the web to accomplish high-level user tasks, such as booking restaurants, applying for jobs, planning trips, and buying shopping lists. It perceives browser inputs via screenshots, while internal reasoning and state history are recorded textually. Based on recent screenshots and a full history of actions, it predicts the next action with necessary arguments, such as coordinates for clicks. Fara-7B uses a robust post-training safety approach leveraging open-source and in-house synthetic datasets. It incorporates critical point recognition—situations requiring user permission or sensitive information—to safely halt actions. The model is trained to refuse harmful tasks and undergoes automated red teaming to assess risks, including grounding, jailbreaks, harmful content, and copyright violations. Fara-7B is trained to refuse tasks in categories that violate usage policy, including illegal activities, deceptive tasks, high-risk or regulated domains, harassment, exploitation, hate, unsafe technical use, misinformation, and sexual content. Critical points where the agent stops include entering personal information, completing purchases, making calls, sending emails, submitting applications, and signing into accounts. Fara-7B can be used to automate web tasks such as shopping, booking travel, restaurant reservations, information-seeking, or account workflows. It performs actions step-by-step using multimodal understanding from browser screenshots. On-device execution provides privacy guarantees and lower latency. It is not evaluated for all downstream purposes, and users should consider the limitations of LLMs for accuracy, safety, and fairness. It must adhere to applicable laws and regulations and is currently limited to English-only support. Fara-7B is available through Hugging Face and Azure AI Foundry. It requires specific input formats, including the use of the ChatML template with a system prompt for inference. The system prompt instructs the model to stop execution at critical points, such as checkout, booking, purchasing, calling, emailing, or ordering. It provides function signatures for all actions, including key presses, typing, mouse movements, left clicks, scrolling, visiting URLs, web searches, browser history navigation, memorizing facts, waiting, and terminating tasks. Fara-7B has technical requirements including Python packages such as torch, transformers, and vllm, and is tested on NVIDIA A6000, A100, and H100 GPUs. It is recommended to use vLLM server with bf16 precision and is provided via Magentic-UI in a Docker sandbox for safe web execution. Responsible AI considerations include its English-only support, potential for stereotype reinforcement or inappropriate content, the need to verify outputs in high-stakes or regulated domains, and the risk of misuse for fraud, spam, or malware generation. Users are advised to use safety services like Azure AI Content Safety, implement human-in-the-loop monitoring, sandboxing, access control, and output verification. Fara-7B is trained using a multi-agent data generation pipeline that produces synthetic trajectories from seed URLs and open-source tasks. It records screenshots, thoughts, action traces, and verification via verifier agents. The training data includes high-quality public datasets with image and text modalities, as well as specialized data for grounding, UI understanding (VQA, captioning, OCR), and safety/refusal datasets. Fara-7B has been evaluated on benchmarks such as WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, DeepShop, and WebTailBench, achieving a task completion success rate of 73.5 on WebVoyager, 34.1 on Online-Mind2Web, 26.2 on DeepShop, and 38.4 on WebTailBench. These scores are averaged over three runs. It has undergone safety evaluation and red-teaming on Azure, focusing on grounding, jailbreaks, harmful content, and copyright. Guidelines for safe use include human-in-the-loop monitoring, not sharing sensitive data, running in sandboxed environments, limiting internet access via allow-lists or block-lists, and avoiding use in commercial, high-stakes, or regulated domains. Security considerations include the model's ability to automate interactions across websites, apps, and operating systems, which requires strict access control, sandboxing, and monitoring. Fara-7B is based on Qwen 2.5 VL, which has an Apache 2.0 license. Fara-7B is released under an MIT license, and both licenses are compatible.

instruction: What’s the most effective way to denoise a speech recording while preserving the voice?
description: Linacodec is an audio tokenizer that compresses audio into 12.5 tokens per second (171 bps) and decodes to 48khz audio. It offers 60x more compression than DAC, with a 48khz output that is much clearer than the standard 16khz or 24khz. The encoder operates at 200x realtime speed, and the decoder runs at 400x realtime speed, with even faster performance when using batching. Linacodec indirectly supports tasks such as voice conversion, audio super-resolution, and audio denoising. Audio tokenizers contribute to the speed, quality, and capability of TTS and ASR models, and Linacodec significantly improves upon previous codecs in these areas. It enables TTS models to run at 800x realtime, which is 8x faster than previously reported, and allows high-quality TTS models to be trained in less than one day. It is versatile, working for both Text-to-Speech and Speech-to-Text applications, unlike most other codecs. The model is licensed under CC-BY-4.0, which permits use for any purpose, including commercial use, provided the original creator is credited.

instruction: I have a table with sensor readings—can you help determine which samples are anomalous?
description: TabPFN-2.5 is a transformer-based foundation model that uses in-context-learning to solve tabular prediction problems in a forward pass. It is designed for regression and classification tasks with up to 50,000 samples and 2000 features in structured tabular format. The model is not suitable for unstructured data such as text or images, and it has not been tested for use with more than 50,000 samples or 2000 features. The architecture consists of a transformer with TabPFNv2-like alternating attention and 18-24 layers. TabPFN-2.5 is trained purely on synthetic tabular tasks, while Real-TabPFN-2.5 is a version that has been continued pre-trained on real-world datasets. The model has been evaluated on proprietary benchmark collections, including TabArena and RealCause, and it achieves new state-of-the-art results in each. It is free from dataset leakage from the pretraining stage due to its training on synthetic data, but users should ensure that the labeled data is free of biases when applied to high-risk use cases. Performance can degrade when applied to more than 50,000 data points and/or 2000 features. The model weights are released under the tabpfn-2.5-license-v1.0, which allows for research and limited internal evaluation but prohibits use for commercial or production purposes. A Commercial Enterprise License is available for production use cases, providing access to a high-speed inference engine, dedicated support, integration tooling, and other internal models. The model was developed by Prior Labs and is available in version v1.0.

instruction: How can I get the most relevant result from this text when searching for project timelines?
description: TomoroAI/tomoro-colqwen3-embed-8b is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model that maps text queries, visual documents (images, PDFs), or short videos into aligned multi-vector embeddings. It is built by merging components with a full vision stack, inheriting robust text retrieval capabilities. The model has been fine-tuned on a curated mixture of data sources and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across ViDoRe V1-V3 in both English and multilingual settings. It offers a significantly reduced embedding footprint compared to other full-dimension Colpali model alternatives. The model is based on a Qwen3-VL 8B architecture with an encoder-only variant and a 320-dimension projection head. It uses a ColPali-style late interaction methodology with MaxSim scoring. It supports up to 1,280 visual tokens per page or 5,120 visual tokens per video, with text prompts constrained by the base context window of 32,000 tokens. The model is capable of handling complex layouts, such as chart-rich PDFs and domain-specific documents, and supports end-to-end retrieval of unseen multimodal documents without requiring OCR or an intermediate vision LLM for summaries. It demonstrates strong performance on non-English document inputs and inherits strong text retrieval performance from the Qwen3-Embedding-8B model. However, it is not fine-tuned on large-scale video retrieval datasets, and its video support is based on preliminary findings. The model is larger than single-vector baselines despite having a smaller token dimension. It is not currently fine-tuned with diverse system instructions, and improvements are planned using more synthetic datasets. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, with upstream Qwen checkpoints retaining their community licenses. Training data includes ViDoRe/MTEB corpora and synthetic VisRAG assets. The model is applied to enhance Tomoro’s customized multimodal agentic RAG pipelines, enabling autonomous agents to parse, reason over, and retrieve from large-scale enterprise internal documentation. It supports Tomoro AI's mission to deliver high-quality enterprise multimodal solutions across high-stakes industries.

instruction: I need to extract the content from this document and have it in Markdown for a presentation. Can you help?
description: NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking is the first reasoning OCR vision-language model (VLM) designed to convert documents into clean Markdown files, particularly suitable for RAG applications. It generates thinking tokens to analyze the layout of a document before producing the final Markdown output. The model excels at handling documents with unusual layouts and complex tables. The number of thinking tokens can range from 20% to 500% of the final answer, depending on the complexity of the task. It is a fine-tune of Qwen 2.5-VL-7B, trained on synthetic examples of documents followed by a reinforcement learning phase (GRPO) using a layout-centric reward. NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking outperforms generic non-reasoning models like GPT-4o and specialized OCR models like OCRFlux, and is competitive with large reasoning closed-source models like Gemini 2.5. In an arena ranking using the trueskill-2 system with approximately 500 anonymized votes, it ranks second with a μ of 26.10 and σ of 0.79, just behind gemini-flash-reasoning. The model is particularly effective for complex document-to-markdown tasks and is intended for use in scenarios requiring structured document conversion. A planned markdown arena will provide a tool for evaluating different solutions in this domain. The training process includes a single epoch of supervised fine-tuning on synthetic reasoning traces from public PDFs, followed by a reinforcement learning phase using a layout-centric reward with challenging image examples. The model is designed to handle headers, bullet points, tables with merged cells, footers, and multi-line content within cells, ensuring accurate and well-structured Markdown output. It is intended for use in educational and administrative settings where document conversion is required, such as for student and family information sheets. The model supports both vLLM and Hugging Face Transformers for deployment, with specific instructions for serving and inference provided.

instruction: Which object in the image corresponds to the sentence 'a bird sitting on a power line'?
description: OneRef is a unified one-tower model for expression grounding and segmentation, introduced in the paper 'OneRef: Unified One-tower Expression Grounding and Segmentation with Mask Referring Modeling' presented at NeurIPS 2024. The model is developed by Linhui Xiao, Xiaoshan Yang, Fang Peng, Yaowei Wang, and Changsheng Xu. OneRef introduces a novel paradigm called Mask Referring Modeling (MRefM), which effectively models the referential relationship between visual and language modalities. Unlike previous approaches, OneRef uses a minimalist one-tower framework that unifies visual and linguistic feature spaces, eliminating the need for modality interaction modules, fusion en-/decoders, and special grounding tokens. The model is validated across three referring tasks on five datasets, achieving state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance. OneRef supports both referring expression comprehension (REC) and referring expression segmentation (RES) tasks. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is compatible with Python 3.9.10 and PyTorch 2.0.1. It has been tested on multiple PyTorch versions and is easy to deploy in various environments. The model is trained using a referring-aware dynamic image masking strategy, which focuses on the referred region rather than using fixed ratios or random masking. The model is available in both Base and Large variants, with pre-trained checkpoints provided for single-dataset fine-tuning and dataset-mixed grounding pre-training settings. The MRefM pre-training for REC is conducted using a mixture of RefCOCO/+/g and ReferIt datasets, while for RES it is conducted using RefCOCO/+/g datasets. The model is built upon the BEiT-3 architecture and includes Sentencepiece tokenizer and VQKD model for pre-training. The model has been shown to achieve significant performance improvements in mixed pre-training settings, although the performance gains in single-dataset settings are less pronounced. The model is available for download from HuggingFace and Google Drive, with detailed training and evaluation scripts provided. The model has been tested on datasets such as RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg, ReferItGame, and Flickr30K. The model's performance is evaluated using standard metrics such as mIoU and oIoU for segmentation tasks and standard accuracy metrics for comprehension tasks. The model is noted for its energy efficiency and computational cost advantages compared to existing approaches. The model's architecture and training methodology are described in detail in the paper, with visualizations and qualitative results provided for various datasets.

instruction: Which compounds are being discussed in this part of the health research paper?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used as a benchmark for evaluating named entity recognition models. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.94, recall of 0.95, and accuracy of 0.99. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Can you help me generate a composite image that includes two separate people in the same scene?
description: This repository contains the FP8 quantized version of the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It is designed for efficient inference while maintaining high-quality image editing capabilities. The model supports single-model image editing workflows and is compatible with standard pipelines such as Diffusers and custom ComfyUI nodes. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 significantly improves character consistency, allowing for imaginative edits based on an input portrait while preserving the identity and visual characteristics of the subject. It further enhances consistency in multi-person group photos, enabling high-fidelity fusion of two separate person images into a coherent group shot. The model integrates selected popular LoRAs directly into the base model, unlocking their effects without extra tuning. For example, it supports realistic lighting control and the generation of new viewpoints directly with the base model. It is also suitable for industrial design applications, such as batch industrial product design and material replacement for industrial components. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 introduces stronger geometric reasoning capability, such as directly generating auxiliary construction lines for design or annotation purposes. The model is distributed under the Apache-2.0 license. For usage, dependencies such as torch, diffusers, and safetensors must be installed, and the FP8 model can be loaded using the QwenImageEditPipeline from the diffusers library.

instruction: What level of emotional strength is present in this recording?
description: This model implements dimensional emotion classification as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits.' The training pipeline is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. Unlike the official challenge submission, this system does not use transcripts and is designed as a speech-only system to maintain simplicity while ensuring effectiveness. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast dataset and is capable of predicting three emotional dimensions: Arousal (ranging from 0 for calm to 1 for active), Valence (ranging from 0 for negative to 1 for positive), and Dominance (ranging from 0 for weak to 1 for strong). The model may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which can be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model is based on the WavLM architecture and is available through Huggingface under the identifier 'tiantiaf/wavlm-large-msp-podcast-emotion-dim.' It is recommended to use audio inputs that are no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz, and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are expected to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: I need to process this image for OCR. How can I ensure the text is upright?
description: The text line orientation classification module is designed to distinguish the orientation of text lines and correct them using post-processing. In scenarios such as document scanning and license or certificate photography, the capture device may be rotated, resulting in text lines in various orientations. Standard OCR pipelines are not well-suited to handle such data. By utilizing image classification technology, the orientation of text lines can be predetermined and adjusted, thereby enhancing the accuracy of OCR processing. The PP-LCNet_x1_0_textline_ori is a text line classification model based on PP-LCNet_x0_25, with two classes: 0 degrees and 180 degrees. It achieves an average recognition accuracy of 98.85 percent and has a model storage size of 0.96 megabytes.

instruction: Can you translate this entire document from English to Hindi while preserving the formatting?
description: Sarvam-Translate is an advanced translation model developed by Sarvam AI in collaboration with AI4Bharat. It is specifically designed for comprehensive, document-level translation across the 22 official Indian languages, built on the Gemma3-4B-IT architecture. The model addresses modern translation needs by handling long-context inputs, diverse content types, and various formats, moving beyond isolated sentences. Sarvam-Translate aims to provide high-quality, contextually aware translations for Indian languages, which have traditionally lagged behind high-resource languages in LLM performance. It supports a wide range of input formats, including markdown, digitized content with OCR errors, documents with embedded math and chemistry equations, and code files, translating only comments. The model is engineered to respect different contexts, formats, styles (formal/informal), and ensure inclusivity, such as appropriate gender attribution. The maximum context length supported is 8k tokens. The 22 supported Indian languages include Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, Gujarati, English, Hindi, Kannada, Kashmiri, Konkani, Maithili, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Santali, Sindhi, Tamil, Telugu, and Urdu.

instruction: If a rectangle has a length of 12 meters and a width of 8 meters, what is its perimeter?
description: This model is a continuously trained Qwen3-0.6B fine-tuned using the Gensyn RL-Swarm framework with GRPO (Generalized Reward Policy Optimization) to enhance reasoning and mathematical capabilities. The model is identified by the Agent ID 'tall_tame_panther' and is currently in active training, with updates occurring every 5-10 minutes. It supports GGUF (llama.cpp) for edge deployment and local inference and is compatible with Text Generation Inference for production use. The model is trained on a composite dataset of 1,000 samples, with a weighted sampling strategy across multiple domains including propositional logic, calendar arithmetic, decimal arithmetic, base conversion, fraction simplification, and basic arithmetic. Training is conducted in bfloat16 precision and involves distributed reinforcement learning across a swarm network. The model has 0.6 billion parameters, with an architecture including 28 layers, 16 attention heads, 8 key-value heads, a hidden size of 1024, and an intermediate size of 3072. It supports a context length of 40,960 tokens and a vocabulary of 151,936 tokens. The model excels in logical reasoning, mathematical operations, number system conversions, date/time calculations, step-by-step problem solving, and conversational tutoring. It is optimized for math and reasoning tasks and may underperform on creative writing. The model is suitable for edge deployment but is not state-of-the-art for complex reasoning. Training is experimental and decentralized, which may lead to less predictable behavior compared to supervised models. The model is compatible with chat formats and supports system instructions, multi-turn dialogue, tool calling, and reasoning mode. It is trained using a reward system that evaluates model outputs via a judge API and updates the policy using GRPO. The model is part of a decentralized swarm with fault tolerance, diverse exploration, and collective intelligence. Training and model updates are verified on-chain. The model is available in multiple quantized formats (F16, Q3_K_M, Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M) and can be deployed using various methods including standard Transformers, TGI, GGUF with llama.cpp, and Ollama. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is developed by 0xgrey. Reproducibility requires specific configurations, including the same seed, dataset, and swarm state. Formal evaluation benchmarks are planned for future inclusion as training progresses.

instruction: How can I extract meaningful visual features from a high-resolution landscape photo without training a new system?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for the ViT-S/16 model. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for the ConvNeXt Tiny model. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for the ViT-L/16 model. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Can I run this on a device with ARM architecture, and if so, what settings are optimal?
description: This model is a quantized version of the Huihui-gemma-3n-E4B-it-abliterated model, processed using the imatrix quantization method with a dataset provided by an unspecified source. The quantizations were performed using the llama.cpp release b5856. The model is intended to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format uses a default template since no chat template was specified, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for the embedding and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. The Q4_0 quantization format is compatible with online repacking, and newer IQ4_NL and IQ4_XS formats offer similar benefits for ARM CPUs. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for both single-file and multi-file downloads. Users are advised to consider their hardware limitations, such as RAM and VRAM availability, when selecting a quantization type. For maximum quality, users should aim for a quant with a file size 1-2GB smaller than the total available memory. K-quant formats, such as Q5_K_M, are recommended for general use, while I-quant formats, such as IQ3_M, may offer better performance for specific hardware like Nvidia or AMD GPUs. The model includes a variety of quantization options, ranging from high-quality Q6_K_L and Q5_K_L to lower-quality but more memory-efficient options like Q3_K_S and Q2_K. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system show that Q4_K_M and Q4_0_8_8 formats offer improvements in prompt processing and text generation compared to the standard Q4_0 format. The model was developed with assistance from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, and inspired by ZeroWw's work on embedding and output weights. Support for the model's development was provided by LM Studio.

instruction: What are the key visual characteristics of this scene captured in this photo?
description: This is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed for image feature extraction and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer architecture with a patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for most models, except for variants with `qkvb` in the name, which have the bias enabled but set to zero. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.2% on IN-ReaL and 0.352 on NYU for the ViT-L/16 variant. It is also evaluated on satellite-related tasks such as classification and segmentation, with performance metrics such as 79.6% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench classification and 74.5% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench segmentation for the ViT-L/16 variant. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper and the Vision Transformer paper, and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library.

instruction: What are some effective strategies for managing stress in a high-pressure job?
description: GLM-4.6V-Flash is a 6-bit quantized version of the GLM-4.6V-Flash model, optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or appropriateness of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or availability. Users are solely responsible for any damage resulting from their use of the model.

instruction: How would you describe the sentiment of the message 'I'm so grateful for all the support I've received.'?
description: The Emotion English DistilRoBERTa-base is a model designed to classify emotions in English text data. It predicts seven emotion classes based on Ekman's 6 basic emotions plus a neutral class: anger, disgust, fear, joy, neutral, sadness, and surprise. The model was trained on a balanced subset of six diverse English text datasets, including data from Twitter, Reddit, student self-reports, and TV dialogues. These datasets collectively provided 2,811 observations per emotion, totaling nearly 20,000 observations. The model was trained on 80% of this data and evaluated on the remaining 20%, achieving an evaluation accuracy of 66%, which is significantly higher than the random-chance baseline of 14%. The model is a fine-tuned checkpoint of a base model, and for a non-distilled version, users are directed to the model card of the original version. The model is intended for use in applications requiring emotion classification, such as analyzing sentiment in social media, news, or other text sources. It can be applied to single text examples or larger datasets, including .csv files, using Hugging Face's pipeline command. The model has been cited in scientific research, including studies on rumor detection, music-to-text synesthesia, and longitudinal analysis of news media sentiment. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their work, with a working paper and BibTeX citation provided for reference. The model's development was supported by Samuel Domdey and others, and users are invited to contact the model's creator for questions or feedback.

instruction: Can you generate a video where a person is in the center, and the depth makes the surrounding elements appear distant and hazy?
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 text-to-video generation system, specifically for depth-based control. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet modules for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and depth control frames. The architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, a transformer model, and an autoencoder. The model is evaluated through inference examples that demonstrate its use in generating videos with specific visual characteristics, such as a close-up shot of a young woman's face with a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model is trained and optimized for high-resolution video generation with a focus on depth perception and visual quality. The model has been developed by Karachev Denis and is available on Github. Users are advised to raise issues directly in the repository and can contact welcomedenk@gmail.com for professional support and recommendations.

instruction: Generate a clip of a dancer performing a graceful routine under colorful stage lights.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model is trained on significantly larger datasets, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. It can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is among the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions, built with an MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, with each expert having about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching. Validation results show that the MoE-based Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality. The TI2V-5B model supports 720P video generation at 24 FPS and can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks in a unified framework. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and inference code and model weights are available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope. Computational efficiency tests have been conducted on different GPUs, with parameter settings and testing methodologies detailed in the documentation. The model has been compared to leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, demonstrating superior performance across multiple dimensions.

instruction: Can I get a voice output in French from this message without relying on any cloud services?
description: Supertonic is a lightning-fast, on-device text-to-speech system designed for extreme performance with minimal computational overhead. It runs entirely on the user's device using ONNX Runtime, eliminating the need for cloud services, API calls, or external dependencies, and ensuring privacy. Supertonic 2 extends the original system's capabilities by adding multilingual support while maintaining the same inference speed and efficiency. It supports English, Korean, Spanish, Portuguese, and French. Supertonic 2 delivers ultra-fast inference speeds, up to 167 times faster than real-time, with an efficient architecture of only 66 million parameters optimized for on-device deployment. All supported languages share the same model architecture and inference pipeline, ensuring cross-language consistency. Performance is evaluated using two metrics: Characters per Second, which measures throughput by dividing the number of input characters by the time required to generate audio, and Real-time Factor (RTF), which measures the time taken to synthesize audio relative to its duration. Supertonic achieves high throughput and low RTF across input texts of varying lengths—short (59 characters), mid (152 characters), and long (266 characters)—on different hardware configurations, including M4 Pro CPU, M4 Pro WebGPU, and RTX4090. For example, on M4 Pro WebGPU, it processes up to 2509 characters per second for long texts and achieves an RTF of 0.006. On RTX4090, it processes up to 12164 characters per second and achieves an RTF of 0.001. Additional performance data is available for 5-step inference, where throughput and RTF remain consistently high across all tested configurations. The project's sample code is released under the MIT License, the accompanying model under the OpenRAIL-M License, and the model was trained using PyTorch, which is licensed under the BSD 3-Clause License but is not redistributed with the project.

instruction: Generate a voice that sounds like a confident businesswoman in her 40s giving a TED Talk.
description: Maya1 is a state-of-the-art speech model for expressive voice generation, designed to capture real human emotion and precise voice design. It allows users to create any voice they can imagine, including specific age, gender, accent, and character traits, and to add emotion tags such as laugh, cry, whisper, rage, sigh, and gasp to make the output feel more realistic. The model streams audio instantly, runs on a single GPU, and has 3B parameters. It outperforms top proprietary models and is developed by Maya Research. Maya1 uses a 3B-parameter decoder-only transformer (Llama-style) to predict SNAC neural codec tokens, enabling real-time streaming at ~0.98 kbps. It is trained on an internet-scale English speech corpus and a curated dataset of studio recordings with human-verified voice descriptions, 20+ emotion tags, multi-accent English coverage, and character variations. The model supports natural language voice control, allowing users to describe voices in plain text without complex parameters or training data. It also supports inline emotion tags for expressive speech, enabling users to add emotions directly into the text. Maya1 is suitable for a variety of use cases, including game character voices, podcast and audiobook production, AI voice assistants, video content creation, customer service AI, and accessibility tools. It is open source under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing commercial use, deployment, and customization. The model can be fine-tuned on custom datasets for specialized voices and is compatible with vLLM for production-scale deployment. It generates 24 kHz audio output and is web audio compatible for browser playback. Maya1 is production-ready and was released in November 2025.

instruction: What is the best way to extract features from a large image that isn't a standard size?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN or logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or using a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, the iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and video classification. For example, the ViT-7B model achieves 90.4% on IN-ReaL, 91.1% on IN-R, 91.1% on Obj.Net, 72.8% on Ox.-H, 55.9% on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU, 79.7% on DAVIS, 64.4% on NAVI, and 58.7% on SPair. The ConvNeXt Base model achieves 88.5% on IN-ReaL, 89.2% on IN-R, 77.2% on Obj.Net, 78.2% on ADE20k, 56.2% on NYU, 61.3% on DAVIS, 46.3% on NAVI, and 0.420 on SPair. On satellite data, the ViT-L model achieves 73.0% on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and 79.6% on the mean classification task. The ViT-7B model achieves 74.0% on m-BEnet, 97.2% on m-brick-kiln, 94.8% on m-eurosat, 62.3% on m-forestnet, 96.1% on m-pv4ger, 62.1% on m-so2sat, and 81.1% on the mean classification task. On segmentation tasks, the ViT-L model achieves 94.2% on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and 74.5% on the mean segmentation task. The ViT-7B model achieves 94.1% on m-cashew, 76.6% on m-chesapeake, 62.6% on m-NeonTree, 83.4% on m-nz-cattle, 95.5% on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6% on m-SA-crop, and 75.0% on the mean segmentation task. The model training used Nvidia H100 GPUs and required 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: I want to restore a low-quality clip of a car race to make the details in the background more visible. Can you help?
description: SeedVR2 is a one-step diffusion-based video restoration (VR) model designed to perform adversarial VR training against real data. It aims to address the challenges of high-resolution video restoration in real-world settings, where existing distillation-based approaches for one-step image restoration have not been effectively extended due to limited generation ability and poor temporal consistency. To handle high-resolution VR within a single step, SeedVR2 introduces an adaptive window attention mechanism, where the window size is dynamically adjusted to fit the output resolutions, avoiding window inconsistency observed under high-resolution VR using window attention with a predefined window size. Additionally, the model incorporates a series of losses, including a proposed feature matching loss, to stabilize and improve the adversarial post-training process without significantly sacrificing training efficiency. Extensive experiments show that SeedVR2 can achieve comparable or even better performance compared with existing VR approaches in a single step. However, the model has some limitations: it is not always robust to heavy degradations and very large motions, and shares some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Moreover, due to its strong generation ability, the model may occasionally oversharpen results on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos. SeedVR and SeedVR2 are licensed under the Apache 2.0.

instruction: Can you tell me where the dog is in this picture?
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, making it more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet.
