The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is primarily intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model is designed to enable researchers to explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection and to support interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training.

instruction: What changes are expected in the environment over the next 10 seconds based on the current situation?
description: HERMES is a unified Driving World Model designed for simultaneous 3D scene understanding and generation in autonomous driving scenarios. It integrates scene understanding, which involves interpreting and reasoning about the driving environment, with future scene evolution (generation). HERMES uses a Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representation to consolidate multi-view spatial information while preserving geometric relationships and interactions. The model introduces world queries that incorporate world knowledge into BEV features through causal attention in a Large Language Model (LLM), enabling contextual enrichment for both understanding and generation tasks. HERMES was evaluated on the nuScenes and OmniDrive-nuScenes datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance with a 32.4% reduction in generation error and an 8.0% improvement in understanding metrics such as CIDEr. The model is based on prior works including BEVFormer v2, InternVL, UniPAD, OmniDrive, and DriveMonkey. HERMES is open-sourced, with code, pretrained weights, and processed data available. A demo has also been released.

instruction: Puoi individuare gli occhi in questa foto scattata in condizioni di scarsa luce?
description: Il modello implementa una rete neurale convoluzionale per la detection degli occhi basata su YOLOv12, ottimizzata per single-class detection, real-time processing, alta precisione e recall, e robusta performance in diverse condizioni di illuminazione. L'architettura utilizza YOLOv12n, una versione nano per performance ottimali, con input size configurabile a 640x640 pixel e output in forma di bounding boxes con confidence scores. Il modello è addestrato su un dataset custom di high-quality disponibile su HuggingFace, con una pipeline completa di training, validazione e test. Le metriche di performance includono una precisione superiore a 0.85, un recall superiore a 0.80, un F1 Score superiore a 0.82, un mAP@0.5 superiore a 0.80 e un mAP@0.5:0.95 superiore a 0.70. Il modello supporta predizioni su immagini, video e webcam in tempo reale, con configurazione flessibile tramite file YAML e monitoraggio avanzato con integrazione WandB. Il training include data augmentation con variazioni HSV, trasformazioni geometriche, mosaic augmentation e flipping orizzontale. Il modello è progettato per applicazioni come medical imaging, human-computer interaction, security systems, research applications e quality control. Tuttavia, presenta limitazioni in caso di posizioni non naturali degli occhi, ad esempio volto ruotato di 90°.

instruction: What are the benefits of practicing mindfulness meditation on a daily basis?
description: Next-1B is a 1-billion parameter causal language model based on Gemma 3, designed for efficiency, low-resource deployment, and reasoning-focused natural language understanding. It is optimized for text reasoning, summarization, and creative generation. The model supports Turkish natively while remaining multilingual. It is open-source and transparent for research and applications. Next-1B is ideal for developers, students, and organizations needing fast, reliable, and low-resource text generation. The model features a lightweight architecture optimized for low VRAM usage, making it suitable for small GPUs or CPU deployment. It is capable of logical chain-of-thought for question-answering and problem-solving, and it provides reliable and reproducible results across multiple runs. The model was fine-tuned using instruction fine-tuning (SFT) with Turkish and multilingual datasets. It is quantization-ready for q8, f16, and f32 formats. The model supports a range of use cases, including text generation, summarization, question and answer, creative writing, and reasoning tasks. Next-1B has demonstrated strong performance on benchmarks, achieving 87.3% on MMLU (5-shot), 69.2% on MMLU-Pro, 90.5% on GSM8K, and 70.1% on MATH. The model is available under the MIT License, which allows for free use, modification, and distribution with attribution appreciated.

instruction: Can you explain the basics of how the internet works in simple terms?
description: Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 is an updated version of the Qwen3-30B-A3B non-thinking mode model. It features significant improvements in general capabilities, including instruction following, logical reasoning, text comprehension, mathematics, science, coding, and tool usage. The model also shows substantial gains in long-tail knowledge coverage across multiple languages and is better aligned with user preferences in subjective and open-ended tasks, enabling more helpful responses and higher-quality text generation. It supports 256K long-context understanding and has a context length of 262,144 tokens natively. The model is a causal language model with 30.5B total parameters and 3.3B activated parameters. It has 48 layers, 32 attention heads for queries and 4 for key-value pairs, and uses a mixture-of-experts architecture with 128 experts and 8 activated experts. It supports only non-thinking mode and does not generate code blocks in its output. The model has been evaluated on various benchmarks, showing strong performance in knowledge, reasoning, coding, alignment, and multilingual tasks. For ultra-long context processing up to 1 million tokens, the model integrates Dual Chunk Attention and sparse attention mechanisms, which improve generation quality and inference efficiency. Users require approximately 240 GB of GPU memory to process 1 million tokens effectively. The model can be launched using vLLM or SGLang with specific configurations for long-context support. Best practices include using sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and setting an output length of 16,384 tokens for most queries. The model is recommended for use with standardized output formats for benchmarking, including specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions.

instruction: Could you provide the log KOA prediction for the compound with the SMILES string C1=CC=C(C=C1)C2=CC=CC=C2?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the octanol-air partition coefficient (log KOA). This property measures a compound's tendency to partition between octanol and air, indicating volatility and environmental transport potential. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the KOA dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What is the classification for the test sample (0.6, -0.4)?
description: This is a 2-qubit Quantum Neural Network (QNN) designed for binary classification tasks, specifically trained on the Two Moons dataset to demonstrate quantum machine learning on real quantum hardware. The model uses a hardware-efficient ansatz and has been tested on IBM Quantum's `ibm_fez` backend. It is a pure quantum model that uses quantum circuits for feature encoding and classification, optimized for NISQ-era quantum devices. The QNN has a total of 6 parameters, including 2 input parameters and 4 trainable parameters, with a circuit depth of 4 layers. The circuit includes 6 Ry gates and 1 CNOT gate, arranged in a linear entanglement topology. The model architecture consists of an encoding layer, two variational layers, and an entanglement layer, followed by a parity measurement for classification. The model was trained using the COBYLA optimizer and cross-entropy loss function on 8 training samples and tested on 4 test samples, achieving a train accuracy of approximately 87.5% and a test accuracy ranging from 0% to 75%. The low test accuracy is attributed to the small dataset size, quantum noise, and the limitations of NISQ devices. The model is intended as a proof-of-concept and educational tool, not for production use. It includes a parity measurement strategy to create a nonlinear decision boundary in feature space. The model is provided with pre-trained weights and a quantum circuit in Qiskit QPY format, along with helper functions for inference. It requires access to IBM Quantum hardware for execution and is distributed under the Apache License 2.0. The model has several limitations, including a small dataset, low capacity, binary classification only, and the need for quantum access. It is not suitable for multi-class problems or large-scale applications. The model is intended for educational, research, and development purposes, serving as a template for quantum machine learning projects and a benchmark for comparing quantum and classical models. Future improvements include increasing the dataset size, testing on multiple backends, and implementing error mitigation techniques.

instruction: Can you detect if there is any background music in this clip?
description: MiDashengLM is an efficient audio-language model that achieves holistic audio understanding through caption-based alignment. It is designed to perform a range of audio understanding tasks, including audio captioning, audio and paralinguistic classification, speech recognition, and question answering. The model is optimized for high inference efficiency, delivering a 3.2× throughput speedup and supporting batch sizes up to 512. MiDashengLM is based on a 7B parameter architecture and is available in both FP32 and BF16 versions, with the BF16 version recommended for most applications to achieve optimal performance and efficiency. The model has been evaluated on multiple audio understanding benchmarks and has demonstrated state-of-the-art performance. For example, on the MusicCaps dataset, it achieves an FENSE score of 59.71, outperforming Qwen2.5-Omni-7B and Kimi-Audio-Instruct. On the VoxCeleb1 dataset, it achieves an accuracy of 92.36, significantly higher than the other models tested. In speech recognition tasks, it achieves a word error rate of 3.7 on the LibriSpeech test-clean dataset and 6.2 on the test-other dataset. The model is available under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in both research and business applications. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their work.

instruction: Could you extract the relevant entities for chronic lymphocytic leukemia from this clinical observation log?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for clinical entity recognition, specifically for entities related to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). It is optimized for high-precision biomedical entity recognition and is trained on a curated CLL dataset. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It supports the identification and classification of biomedical entities, including the entity types `B-CL` and `I-CL`. The model is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The CLL corpus, on which the model is trained, is a domain-specific biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on entities related to chronic lymphocytic leukemia, a type of blood cancer. The dataset includes annotations for CLL-specific terminology, biomarkers, treatment entities, and clinical concepts relevant to hematology and oncology research. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.78, recall of 0.94, and accuracy of 0.97. Comparative performance on the CLL dataset shows that the model ranks fifth in F1 score with a value of 0.8547, compared to other models with F1 scores ranging from 0.8364 to 0.9575. The model is based on the distilbert-base-cased architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with the AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It performs token classification for named entity recognition, taking tokenized biomedical text as input and producing BIO-tagged entity predictions as output. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research in biomedical NLP. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Is it possible to estimate the height of a building from a single photo using visual geometry techniques?
description: DA3 Nested is a model that combines the any-view Giant model with the metric Large model for metric-scale visual geometry reconstruction. It is designed to perform a range of visual geometry tasks, including relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, 3D Gaussian generation, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. The model is based on a plain transformer architecture with a unified depth-ray representation, and it uses a vision transformer for visual geometry. It was developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. The model has 1.40B parameters and is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. It significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations on domain-specific images and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face Hub and can be accessed via a Python API or command line interface for inference and export in various formats such as glb, npz, ply, mini_npz, gs_ply, and gs_video.

instruction: How likely is this user to churn given their activity and engagement metrics?
description: sap-rpt-1-oss is a deep learning model designed for tabular in-context learning (ICL) that combines semantic understanding and alignment within a table-native ICL framework. The model is intended to perform classification and regression tasks on tabular data. It accepts input in the form of a pandas DataFrame or a NumPy array, with no preprocessing required. Column names and cell values are automatically embedded using a background large language model, and missing values are handled appropriately. The model is trained on large-scale real-world tabular data and is competitive with state-of-the-art models across a broad set of benchmarks, particularly excelling on the semantically rich CARTE benchmark. It employs specialized embeddings for different data modalities to better leverage the semantics and world knowledge in real-world data. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use a GPU with at least 80 GB of memory, a context size of 8192, and a bagging factor of 8. For lower resource requirements, the context size can be reduced to 2048 and the bagging factor to 1. The model checkpoints are available for download from Hugging Face and require login access. The model is licensed under the Apache Software License, version 2.0, and is intended for research purposes only. The model checkpoints inherit restrictions from the training data, which is a subset of another dataset. The model was previously known as ConTextTab, but the code and repository have been updated to reflect the new name, while the model checkpoint and functionality remain unchanged.

instruction: Group these customer support tickets into categories based on the issues described.
description: The all-mpnet-base-v2 model maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space, enabling tasks such as clustering or semantic search. It is designed to function as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, producing vectors that capture semantic information for use in information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 384 word pieces is truncated by default. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs. The training process involved computing cosine similarity between all possible sentence pairs in a batch and applying cross-entropy loss based on true pairs. The model was fine-tuned for 100,000 steps on a TPU v3-8 with a batch size of 1024 (128 per TPU core), a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length limit of 128 tokens. The training data was a combination of multiple datasets, including citation pairs, duplicate question pairs, question-answer pairs, and image captions, among others. The total number of training tuples across all datasets is 1,170,060,424. The model was developed during a project organized by Hugging Face, with support from Google's Flax, JAX, and Cloud teams. It is intended for use with the Sentence-Transformers library, HuggingFace Transformers, or the Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) system. When using the HuggingFace Transformers library, the model requires a pooling operation to be applied on top of the contextualized word embeddings. The model can be deployed via a Docker container for CPU or NVIDIA GPU environments, and embeddings can be generated by sending a request to the /v1/embeddings endpoint.

instruction: Highlight all the species entities in this biomedical text for me.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying species names from the Species-800 dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated SPECIES800 dataset, which contains 800 manually annotated abstracts for organism mentions. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the distilroberta-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It has an F1 score of 0.72, precision of 0.72, recall of 0.73, and accuracy of 0.95. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research settings. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with different aggregation strategies for entity prediction, including `none`, `simple`, `first`, `average`, and `max`. For large-scale processing, it supports batch and dataset-level operations with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities.

instruction: Is there a way to detect depth in this scene using visual features?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperform specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the models outperform previous models on multiple benchmarks. For example, the ViT-7B model achieves 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair. The ConvNeXt Large model achieves 88.9 on IN-ReaL, 89.4 on IN-R, 81.3 on Obj.Net, 82.4 on ADE20k, 59.3 on NYU, 65.2 on DAVIS, 47.8 on NAVI, and 0.403 on SPair. On satellite data, the ViT-7B model achieves 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for classification tasks, and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for segmentation tasks. The model architecture includes Vision Transformer models with varying parameters and ConvNeXt models with different sizes. The training of the ViT-7B model required 61,440 hours of compute time on Nvidia H100 GPUs, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The models were trained using PyTorch 2.7 on private infrastructure in the USA.

instruction: Generate a video of a futuristic city with glowing architecture and smooth transitions.
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 text-to-video generation system, specifically tailored for canny edge detection. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet modules for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and control signals derived from canny edge maps. The model architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, which is integrated with the Wan2.2 base model. The model is evaluated through inference examples that demonstrate its ability to generate high-resolution video outputs with specific visual characteristics, such as close-up shots with soft lighting and detailed imagery. The model is provided with a Python-based implementation that includes loading the base model, setting up the pipeline, and processing video frames with canny detection. The model is available for use under the terms of the associated GitHub repository. The model has been developed by Karachev Denis and is published on GitHub. For professional support and recommendations, users are directed to contact welcomedenk@gmail.com.

instruction: Could you locate and label the different types of vehicles in this traffic image?
description: YOLOS is a Vision Transformer (ViT) trained using the DETR loss. It is designed for object detection and is capable of achieving 42 AP on COCO validation 2017, similar to DETR and more complex frameworks such as Faster R-CNN. The model is trained using a 'bipartite matching loss,' where the predicted classes and bounding boxes of each of the N = 100 object queries are compared to the ground truth annotations, padded up to the same length N. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between each of the N queries and each of the N annotations. Standard cross-entropy is used for the classes, and a linear combination of the L1 and generalized IoU loss is used for the bounding boxes to optimize the parameters of the model. The YOLOS model was pre-trained for 300 epochs on ImageNet-1k and fine-tuned for 300 epochs on COCO, a dataset consisting of 118k/5k annotated images for training/validation respectively. This model achieves an AP (average precision) of 28.7 on COCO 2017 validation. The model is intended for use in object detection tasks and supports PyTorch for both the feature extractor and model. The team releasing YOLOS did not write a model card for this model, so this model card has been written by the Hugging Face team.

instruction: Which of these two headlines better captures the essence of the article?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is based on the Bradley-Terry model, with improvements in training data scale and quality to achieve superior performance. Compared with the first generation of Skywork-Reward, the Skywork-Reward-V2 series is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, consisting of 26 million preference pairs curated via a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series offers state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. The series includes models of multiple sizes, with the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, nearly matching the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2. The largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The models are available in eight variants, including Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-1B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-1.7B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-4B, and Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series outperforms models in both Bradley-Terry and Generative categories with much smaller model sizes. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and inference should be performed within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The model Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M is considered an experimental variant, trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for commercial use, with conditions for attribution and modification. Models derived from Qwen3 follow the Qwen3 license, while those derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct follow the Llama license.

instruction: Generate a summary of this detailed travel guide, making sure each recommendation is backed by the original content.
description: sui-1 (Summarization with Unique Identifiers) is a specialized model for high-quality summarization of very long texts with built-in source grounding. Every claim in the summary can be traced back to its source sentence, enabling verification and reducing hallucination risk. The model handles up to 128k tokens natively, with a two-step iterative approach for documents up to 2 million tokens. The FP8 variant runs on a single A100 40GB or A6000 48GB GPU, and the iterative approach enables deployment on even more modest hardware. sui-1 significantly outperforms all tested open-weight baselines, including models with 3x more parameters. It is fine-tuned for English, German, Spanish, French, and Italian, and inherits 20+ additional languages from Mistral Small 3.2. The model is built using a sophisticated data generation pipeline that produced 22,000+ training examples from parliamentary documents, web sources, and Wikipedia using chain-of-thought reasoning with multi-stage verification. It features a built-in citation mechanism that links each claim to its source sentence for full traceability. The model follows a three-phase approach: a planning phase that analyzes the input and plans the summary structure, a reference selection phase that identifies the most important sentences to cite, and a grounded generation phase that produces a summary with inline citations to source sentences. Citations use XML tags assigned during preprocessing, enabling deterministic verification of each claim. The input text must be preprocessed with XML sentence tags, each sentence wrapped in a unique 8-character hexadecimal tag. The model outputs a JSON object with three keys: 'structure', 'xml_tags', and 'summary'. The 'structure' key contains internal reasoning about the content and planned summary structure, the 'xml_tags' key is a list of XML tags cited in the summary, and the 'summary' key is the actual summary with inline XML tag references. The model supports a 128k token context window natively and an iterative approach for longer documents. For documents exceeding the context limit, a two-step iterative approach is used that preserves grounding quality. The first step involves creating partial summaries of document chunks, and the second step merges these into a final summary. The iterative approach maintains source grounding through careful XML tag propagation, ensuring that every claim in the final summary traces back to a specific source sentence. The model provides two variants: bfloat16 (full precision, ~48GB weights) and FP8 (quantized, ~24GB weights). The FP8 version preserves high quality, scoring 81.05% overall on the benchmark, nearly identical to bfloat16. The bfloat16 variant requires ~55GB VRAM for 8k context, scaling to ~76GB for 128k. The FP8 variant requires ~38GB for 8k and ~50GB for 128k. The model is evaluated using an LLM-as-a-Judge methodology, where a strong judge model evaluates summary quality across multiple criteria, including factual accuracy, coverage and completeness, specificity and informativeness, format compliance, and custom instruction adherence. The evaluation was conducted on 100 diverse test samples covering multiple languages and document types, with scoring using binary pass/fail per criterion, aggregated to success rates. Grounding quality is validated using structural checks for tag uniqueness, tag validity, and tag usage. The model is a contribution to the open-source community and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It has limitations, including the requirement for preprocessing of input text with XML tags, a maximum single-pass context of 128k tokens, and occasional JSON output parsing failures, for which retry logic is recommended in production use.

instruction: How have changes in U.S. trade policy affected global supply chain financing?
description: LLM Pro Finance is a multilingual AI model designed for finance, economics, and business professionals. It is trained on a vast corpus of high-quality financial and economic data, including market reports, regulatory filings, central bank publications, and academic research. The model is fluent in English, French, and German, with a nuanced understanding of financial terminology across these languages. It provides well-structured, factually grounded explanations, summaries, and analyses tailored to professional standards. The model is built with a focus on reliability, accuracy, and transparency, using low-carbon computing infrastructure. It supports a range of use cases, including financial analysis, regulatory compliance, investment research, economic forecasting, and client communication. The training data is composed of 54.4% financial content, 19.8% translation tasks, 15.6% general knowledge, 8.0% retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and 2.2% reasoning, math, and code. This composition ensures strong domain expertise in banking, investment, and economic reasoning, while retaining versatility for general and multilingual use cases. The model is based on Llama 3 and is fine-tuned for domain-specific performance. It was trained in a low-carbon datacenter powered by renewable energy. Rigorous red teaming exercises were conducted to ensure the model maintains high standards of safety and ethical integrity. These exercises confirmed that the model maintains a significantly low toxicity score, consistently avoids biased or stereotypical language, and resists generating misinformation, prioritizing factual accuracy and contextual relevance in its responses.

instruction: Can you help restore a low-quality home video of my family from the 90s to make it look clearer and more vibrant?
description: SeedVR2 is a one-step diffusion-based video restoration (VR) model designed to perform adversarial VR training against real data. It aims to address the challenges of high-resolution video restoration in real-world settings, where existing distillation-based approaches for one-step image restoration have not been effectively extended due to limited generation ability and poor temporal consistency. To handle high-resolution VR within a single step, SeedVR2 introduces an adaptive window attention mechanism, where the window size is dynamically adjusted to fit the output resolutions, avoiding window inconsistency observed under high-resolution VR using window attention with a predefined window size. Additionally, the model incorporates a series of losses, including a proposed feature matching loss, to stabilize and improve the adversarial post-training process without significantly sacrificing training efficiency. Extensive experiments show that SeedVR2 can achieve comparable or even better performance compared with existing VR approaches in a single step. However, the model has some limitations: it is not always robust to heavy degradations and very large motions, and shares some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Moreover, due to its strong generation ability, the model may occasionally oversharpen results on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos. SeedVR and SeedVR2 are licensed under the Apache 2.0.

instruction: I need the skin in this image to look more consistent with natural lighting—what can be done?
description: The qwen-edit-skin is a finetuned Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) model designed to enhance the realism and detail of human skin in images. It is built on top of the Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 base model, which is known for its advanced image editing capabilities, particularly in maintaining consistency in single-image edits and preserving personal identity in multi-image editing. The LoRA was trained for 5000 steps on an NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU using the AI-Toolkit. The training dataset was curated by taking real images of subject portraits with exposed skin, captioning them as target images, and then editing them in Photoshop to reduce skin texture, tone, and pore visibility to create control images. The model was trained at resolutions of 512, 768, and 1024 pixels using bf16 precision. Key training parameters include a batch size of 1, gradient accumulation of 1, a learning rate of 1.0e-04, and the adamw8bit optimizer, which helps reduce memory usage during training. The noise scheduler used is flowmatch, which contributes to efficient training and high-quality image generation. The LoRA architecture features linear and convolutional ranks and alphas of 16, ensuring a balanced scaling of learned adaptations to prevent overfitting. To use the model, the base Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 model must be loaded, followed by the qwen-edit-skin LoRA weights. The recommended weight strength is between 1 and 1.5, though higher values can be used to observe the effect of the LoRA. The model is intended for creative and artistic purposes, such as enhancing the realism of human skin in digital images for digital artists, photographers, and AI art enthusiasts. It may carry inherent biases from the base model and the training dataset, which focused on improving skin details and may not represent all skin tones and types equally. Users are encouraged to use descriptive and inclusive language in their prompts and to use the model responsibly. The model is not intended for unethical or harmful use, and users are responsible for the content they generate. A trigger phrase to activate the model's effect is 'make the subjects skin details more prominent and natural.'

instruction: I want to create an audiobook from my notes—can this be done locally on my machine?
description: Pocket TTS is a lightweight text-to-speech (TTS) application designed to run efficiently on CPUs. It eliminates the need for GPUs or web APIs by allowing audio generation through a simple pip install and function call. The model supports Python versions 3.10 through 3.14 and requires PyTorch 2.5 or higher, but does not require the GPU version of PyTorch. It is a CPU-based model with a small size of 100 million parameters. It supports audio streaming with low latency, approximately 200 milliseconds to produce the first audio chunk, and operates at about six times real-time speed on the CPU of a MacBook Air M4. It uses only two CPU cores and provides both a Python API and a command-line interface (CLI). The model supports voice cloning and can handle infinitely long text inputs. It is currently limited to English language support. Users can try the model through a web interface without installation, or use it via the CLI or as a Python library. The model can be run as a local server to generate audio via HTTP requests, keeping the model in memory between requests for faster performance. The model is not supported for GPU execution, as no speed improvements were observed compared to CPU execution due to the use of a batch size of 1 and a small model size. Contributions are welcome, and development instructions are available in the relevant documentation. The model must be used in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations, and its use must not involve illegal, harmful, deceptive, fraudulent, or unauthorized activities. Prohibited uses include voice impersonation or cloning without explicit and lawful consent, misinformation or disinformation, and the generation of unlawful, harmful, or privacy-invasive content.

instruction: Can you create a video of a glowing firefly flying through a forest with a transparent background?
description: Wan-Alpha is a framework for high-quality text-to-video generation that includes an alpha channel to represent transparency. It is designed to generate transparent videos by jointly learning both RGB and alpha channels. The model uses an effective variational autoencoder (VAE) that encodes the alpha channel into the RGB latent space. To support the training of the diffusion transformer, a high-quality and diverse RGBA video dataset was constructed. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, Wan-Alpha demonstrates superior performance in visual quality, motion realism, and transparency rendering. It can generate a wide variety of semi-transparent objects, glowing effects, and fine-grained details such as hair strands. The model is available for download and use, with open-sourced weights and inference code for version 1.0. Users can generate videos by specifying a transparent background, visual style, shot type, and a description of the main subject in their prompts. The model supports both Chinese and English input. For usage, users must download and organize specific model files, including the Wan DiT base model, text encoder, LightX2V model, and VAE decoders for both RGB and alpha channels. These files should be placed in the appropriate directories for use with the ComfyUI interface. The model can be tested using a provided script that requires specifying paths to the model weights and other parameters. The output includes RGBA videos with a checkerboard background and PNG frames. The model is built upon several open-source projects related to training, inference, and acceleration. The authors request citation of their paper if the model is used in research.

instruction: Can you retrieve the most relevant code examples for a Python-based web scraper?
description: ModernBERT is a modernized bidirectional encoder-only Transformer model (BERT-style) pre-trained on 2 trillion tokens of English and code data with a native context length of up to 8,192 tokens. It incorporates architectural improvements such as Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) for long-context support, Local-Global Alternating Attention for efficiency on long inputs, and Unpadding and Flash Attention for efficient inference. ModernBERT is designed for tasks requiring processing of long documents, such as retrieval, classification, and semantic search within large corpora. It is suitable for a wide range of downstream tasks, including code retrieval and hybrid (text + code) semantic search. The model is available in two sizes: one with 22 layers and 149 million parameters, and another with 28 layers and 395 million parameters. ModernBERT was evaluated across a range of tasks, including natural language understanding (GLUE), general retrieval (BEIR), long-context retrieval (MLDR), and code retrieval (CodeSearchNet and StackQA). On GLUE, ModernBERT-base surpasses other similarly-sized encoder models, and ModernBERT-large is second only to Deberta-v3-large. For general retrieval tasks, ModernBERT performs well on BEIR in both single-vector (DPR-style) and multi-vector (ColBERT-style) settings. Thanks to the inclusion of code data in its training mixture, ModernBERT achieves new state-of-the-art code retrieval results on CodeSearchNet and StackQA. ModernBERT’s strong results, coupled with its efficient runtime on long-context inputs, demonstrate that encoder-only models can be significantly improved through modern architectural choices and extensive pretraining on diversified data sources. The model uses an encoder-only, Pre-Norm Transformer with GeGLU activations. It was pre-trained up to 1,024 tokens and then extended to 8,192 tokens. The training data consists of 2 trillion tokens of English text and code. The model was trained using the StableAdamW optimizer with trapezoidal LR scheduling and 1-sqrt decay, on 8x H100 GPUs. ModernBERT’s training data is primarily English and code, so performance may be lower for other languages. While it can handle long sequences efficiently, using the full 8,192 tokens window may be slower than short-context inference. Like any large language model, ModernBERT may produce representations that reflect biases present in its training data. Verify critical or sensitive outputs before relying on them. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Please examine this audio to determine if it contains fluent or disfluent speech segments.
description: This model implements a speech fluency classification system based on the Whisper Large v3 architecture, as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits'. The model is designed to classify speech segments as either 'fluent' or 'disfluent' using a 3-second window with a 1-second step size. When disfluent speech is detected, the model further classifies the type of disfluency into one of the following categories: 'Block', 'Prolongation', 'Sound Repetition', 'Word Repetition', or 'Interjection'. The model is intended for use in characterizing speech traits and is not suitable for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive uses, or commercial purposes. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are required to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions.

instruction: Would you be able to transcribe a Russian voice message I received?
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Russian. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe Russian speech into text. Evaluation can be performed on the mozilla-foundation/common_voice_6_0 dataset with the test split, and on the speech-recognition-community-v2/dev_data dataset with the validation split. The model has been tested on a set of Russian speech samples, and some reference and predicted transcriptions are provided, showing varying degrees of accuracy. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face Transformers library. It is important to note that the model may not always produce perfect transcriptions, as demonstrated by the discrepancies between reference and predicted sentences in the evaluation examples.

instruction: What is the main event being described in this long-form documentary clip?
description: The SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT from allenai is a 4B-parameter vision-language model fine-tuned from Qwen/Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. It serves as the core decision-maker in the SAGE (Smart Any-Horizon Agent) system for long video reasoning. The model operates in two stages: Stage-1 analyzes initial sampled frames and metadata to classify queries as either single-turn (immediate answer) or multi-turn (tool-required). Stage-2 iteratively calls tools, processes observations, and refines context until resolution. The model generates JSON-formatted actions for specialized tools, including web-search for external knowledge, transcribe-speech for automatic speech recognition on timestamps, ground-event for temporal localization, extract-video-parts for high-resolution frames or subclips, and analyze for detailed visual breakdown. This enables robust handling of extended videos beyond fixed horizons through dynamic tool orchestration. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license for research and educational use, in accordance with Ai2 guidelines. It requires the SAGE GitHub runtime for tool execution and parsing and supports GGUF quantizations for efficient deployment. The model excels in video question-answering tasks across domains such as sports, narratives, and events.

instruction: How can I determine which of these videos is the best match for this sentence?
description: The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-8B is a multimodal reranking model designed for cross-modal understanding and information retrieval. It is part of the Qwen3-VL model series, which includes the Qwen3-VL-Embedding and Qwen3-VL-Reranker models. These models are built upon the Qwen3-VL foundation and are specifically engineered to handle diverse input modalities, including text, images, screenshots, and videos, as well as combinations of these. The Embedding model generates high-dimensional vectors for tasks like retrieval and clustering, while the Reranker model refines these results by taking a (query, document) pair as input and outputting a precise relevance score. The two models are typically used together in a two-stage pipeline, where the Embedding model performs initial recall and the Reranker improves retrieval accuracy. The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-8B supports over 30 languages and is capable of processing inputs with arbitrary single or mixed modalities. It has 8 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and supports quantization, multilingual representation learning (MRL), and instruction-aware customization. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-VL-Reranker-8B outperforms baseline models and the base embedding model across multiple retrieval tasks, including image-text retrieval, video-text matching, and visual document retrieval. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for specific tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance. The model is designed for integration into existing pipelines and supports flexible vector dimensions and quantized embeddings for practical deployment.

instruction: What is the most probable label for the subject in this picture?
description: LSE-DINOv2 is a DINOv2 Vision Transformer enhanced with a Deep Equilibrium Model (DEM) based local scale adaptation mechanism to improve scale equivariance, ensuring local scale consistency. The model learns to adaptively scale different image regions based on their content using fixed-point iteration to find optimal deformation parameters. This adaptation is applied at multiple transformer layers, and the model is fine-tuned on the ImageNet-1K dataset for image classification. The base architecture is a ViT-Base model with a 14×14 patch size, 224×224 image size, 768 embedding dimension, 12 transformer blocks, 12 attention heads, and 4 register tokens. The DEM adapter includes 3 phi layers, local scaling grids of [16x16, 8x8, 8x8], 2 DEQ layers, CNN channels of [3, 64, 128], 5 DEQ iterations, and is applied at the input, Layer 4, and Layer 8. The model was trained on ImageNet-1K for 20 epochs with a batch size of 200, using the AdamW optimizer with a backbone learning rate of 5e-5 and a DEM learning rate of 1e-5. The model introduces some computational overhead due to DEQ iterations and requires higher memory usage compared to standard DINOv2. It also depends on the `timm` library and optionally the `torchdeq` library. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license and is described in the paper titled 'Local Scale Equivariance with Latent Deep Equilibrium Canonicalizer' by Rahman et al., published in the Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision in 2025.

instruction: Which of these research papers talks the most about quantum computing advancements?
description: jina-reranker-v3 is a 0.6B parameter multilingual document reranker with a novel last but not late interaction architecture. Unlike ColBERT's separate encoding with multi-vector matching, this model performs causal self-attention between query and documents within the same context window, extracting contextual embeddings from the last token of each document. Built on Qwen3-0.6B with 28 transformer layers and a lightweight MLP projector (1024→512→256), it processes up to 64 documents simultaneously within 131K token context. The model achieves state-of-the-art BEIR performance with 61.94 nDCG@10 while being 10× smaller than generative listwise rerankers. It is designed to rerank documents based on their relevance to a given query, supporting multilingual retrieval tasks. The model can be used for reranking in applications such as information retrieval, document search, and question answering. The model is available for local inference using the transformers library and can also be accessed via Jina AI's API for faster integration. The rerank function takes a query and a list of documents, returning a list of documents sorted by relevance score. The model outputs include the document text, relevance score, and optionally, document embeddings. The model is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 for non-commercial use. For commercial usage inquiries, contact the appropriate licensing authority. The model is listed on AWS and Azure platforms.

instruction: Can you summarize a 100-page research paper in a concise and clear way?
description: Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is a next-generation foundation model designed to support increasingly powerful and agentic artificial intelligence through innovative model architecture. It is the first installment in the Qwen3-Next series and features a hybrid attention mechanism that combines Gated DeltaNet and Gated Attention to enable efficient context modeling for ultra-long context lengths. The model also incorporates a high-sparsity Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design, which achieves an extreme low activation ratio in MoE layers, drastically reducing FLOPs per token while preserving model capacity. Additional stability optimizations include zero-centered and weight-decayed layernorm, and other enhancements for robust pre-training and post-training. The model also supports Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), which boosts pretraining performance and accelerates inference. Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is a causal language model with 80B total parameters and 3B activated parameters, trained on 15T tokens. It has 48 layers with a hybrid layout of 12 repetitions of (3 repetitions of (Gated DeltaNet -> MoE) -> 1 repetition of (Gated Attention -> MoE)). The Gated Attention component has 16 Q heads and 2 KV heads, with a head dimension of 256 and a rotary position embedding dimension of 64. The Gated DeltaNet has 32 V heads and 16 QK heads, with a head dimension of 128. The MoE component includes 512 experts, with 10 activated experts and 1 shared expert, and an expert intermediate dimension of 512. The model natively supports a context length of 262,144 tokens and is extensible up to 1,010,000 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct outperforms Qwen3-32B-Base on downstream tasks with 10% of the training cost and 10 times the inference throughput for contexts over 32K tokens. It performs on par with Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507 on certain benchmarks while demonstrating significant advantages in handling ultra-long-context tasks up to 256K tokens. The model supports instruct (non-thinking) mode and does not generate code blocks in its output. It is recommended to use dedicated inference frameworks such as SGLang or vLLM for optimal efficiency and throughput. The model is capable of handling a wide range of tasks, including knowledge-based reasoning, coding, alignment, agent-based interactions, and multilingualism. For ultra-long text processing, the model can be configured with YaRN to handle context lengths up to 1 million tokens. Best practices for using the model include setting sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, using an output length of 16,384 tokens for most queries, and standardizing output formats for benchmarking. The model is available through Hugging Face Transformers and can be deployed using SGLang or vLLM to create an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. It is important to note that the efficiency or throughput improvement depends highly on the implementation, and the use of a dedicated inference framework is recommended. Additionally, the default context length is 256K, and it may be necessary to reduce the context length if the server fails to start. The model is also recommended for agentic use with Qwen-Agent, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity.

instruction: Can you turn my description of a fantasy castle into a 5-second animated scene?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a 16×16×4 compression ratio, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at 480P and 720P resolutions. It delivers outstanding video generation quality and surpasses leading commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark. The model is built with a two-expert MoE design: a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, each with about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching. The model also features a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a T×H×W compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. The TI2V-5B model supports 720P video generation at 24 FPS and can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU. It natively supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a single framework. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating harmful or illegal content.

instruction: What are the key elements in this photograph, and can you outline their positions?
description: The Deformable Detection Transformer (DETR) model is an encoder-decoder transformer architecture with a ResNet-50 convolutional backbone. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model incorporates two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class label prediction and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding box regression. Object detection is performed using object queries, with 100 queries specified for the COCO dataset. During training, a bipartite matching loss is used, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes from each of the 100 object queries to the ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is applied to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations. The model is optimized using cross-entropy for class prediction and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding box regression. The model is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch for both the feature extractor and model. The model was introduced in a paper by Zhu et al. and is available for use through the Hugging Face platform. A disclaimer notes that the model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original model developers did not provide one.

instruction: Please process this document and provide a summary of the main sections and their content.
description: olmOCR-2-7B-1025 is a full BF16 version of a model fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct using a specific dataset. It has been further fine-tuned using GRPO RL training to enhance its performance on math equations, tables, and other challenging OCR cases. The model is designed to process document images, particularly those with complex layouts, and extract structured metadata. It is recommended to use the FP8 version for most practical applications, except for further fine-tuning. The model is best used via a dedicated toolkit that includes an efficient inference setup using VLLM, capable of handling millions of documents at scale. The model expects a single document image as input, with the longest dimension rendered to 1288 pixels. The prompt must include additional metadata from the document, which can be generated using the provided methods. The model scores 82.3 ± 1.1 on the olmOCR-Bench, with specific performance metrics including 82.9 on ArXiv, 82.1 on Old Scans Math, 84.3 on Tables, 48.3 on Old Scans, 95.7 on Headers and Footers, 84.3 on Multi column, 81.4 on Long tiny text, and 99.7 on Base. The FP8 version of the model scores 82.4 ± 1.1 on the same benchmark, with slightly improved performance on most categories. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2's policies.

instruction: Is the voice in this recording more nasal or clear?
description: This model implements voice (sounding) quality classification based on the Vox-Profile framework, which is designed to characterize diverse speaker and speech traits. It is specifically trained to classify speech samples into a set of predefined voice quality attributes. The model evaluates voice characteristics across five categories: pitch, texture, volume, clarity, and rhythm. The classification labels include 'shrill', 'nasal', 'deep', 'silky', 'husky', 'raspy', 'guttural', 'vocal-fry', 'booming', 'authoritative', 'loud', 'hushed', 'soft', 'crisp', 'slurred', 'lisp', 'stammering', 'singsong', 'pitchy', 'flowing', 'monotone', 'staccato', 'punctuated', 'enunciated', and 'hesitant'. The model is based on the Whisper Large v3 architecture and is fine-tuned for voice quality classification. Performance is evaluated using speaker-level Macro-F1 scores, calculated by averaging the Macro-F1 scores across speakers. The speaker-level score is derived from five randomly sampled utterances per speaker, with the stratification process repeated 20 times. The final performance metric is the unweighted average of speaker-level Macro-F1 scores between the VoxCeleb and Expresso datasets. EARS is excluded from ParaSpeechCaps due to the limited number of samples in the holdout set. The model is intended for use with audio inputs that are no shorter than 3 seconds and no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. Predictions are made using a sigmoid function on the model's output logits, with a threshold of 0.7 used to determine the presence of each voice quality label. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Can you transcribe this audio file of a French podcast into written text?
description: parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is a 600-million-parameter multilingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) model designed for high-throughput speech-to-text transcription. It extends a previous model by expanding language support from English to 25 European languages. The model automatically detects the language of the audio and transcribes it without requiring additional prompting. It is part of a series of models that leverage a multilingual corpus as their primary training dataset. The supported languages include Bulgarian, Croatian, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Estonian, Finnish, French, German, Greek, Hungarian, Italian, Latvian, Lithuanian, Maltese, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Slovak, Slovenian, Spanish, Swedish, Russian, and Ukrainian. The model is ready for commercial and non-commercial use. Key features include automatic punctuation and capitalization, accurate word-level and segment-level timestamps, long audio transcription supporting up to 24 minutes with full attention or up to 3 hours with local attention, and a permissive CC BY 4.0 license. The model is based on the FastConformer-TDT architecture and has 600 million parameters. It accepts 16kHz audio in .wav and .flac formats and produces text output with punctuation and capitalization included. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, achieving faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. It was trained using the NeMo toolkit, initialized from a CTC multilingual checkpoint pretrained on the Granary dataset, and further trained for 150,000 steps on 128 A100 GPUs. Stage 2 fine-tuning was performed for 5,000 steps on 4 A100 GPUs using approximately 7,500 hours of high-quality, human-transcribed data from NeMo ASR Set 3.0. A unified SentencePiece Tokenizer with a vocabulary of 8,192 tokens was used during training. The model was trained on a combination of the Granary dataset and the NeMo ASR Set 3.0, which includes 10,000 hours of human-transcribed data and 660,000 hours of pseudo-labeled data. Evaluation datasets include Fleurs, MLS, CoVoST, and the Hugging Face Open ASR Leaderboard. The model's multilingual ASR performance is summarized by word error rate (WER) across different datasets, with an average WER of 11.97% on Fleurs, 7.83% on MLS, and 11.98% on CoVoST. On the Hugging Face Open-ASR-Leaderboard, the model achieves an average WER of 6.34%. Performance is evaluated across different Signal-to-Noise Ratios (SNR), with increasing WER as SNR decreases. The model is intended for use by developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is licensed under a permissive CC BY 4.0 license. Technical limitations include potential inaccuracies in transcripts, with accuracy varying based on language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy may vary based on the context of the input text.

instruction: Find the most relevant articles about renewable energy from a given text collection.
description: This is a model finetuned from distilbert/distilbert-base-uncased. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and an output dimensionality of 768 dimensions. The similarity function used is Cosine Similarity. The full model architecture includes a Transformer with a DistilBertModel architecture and a Pooling layer with a word embedding dimension of 768. The pooling configuration includes pooling_mode_mean_tokens set to True and all other pooling modes set to False. The model was trained using Python 3.12.8, Sentence Transformers 5.1.0, Transformers 4.55.4, PyTorch 2.8.0, Accelerate 1.5.2, Datasets 3.5.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.1.

instruction: Can you evaluate how this compound might behave in a biological system?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict teratogenic bioaccumulation potential, a property that may relate to developmental toxicity or bioaccumulation scoring. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the TBP dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Can you extract all gene-related terms from this biomedical research summary?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model has achieved an F1 score of 1.00, with precision, recall, and accuracy also at 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows that it ranks among the top models, with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with the AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and it is validated using cross-validation on a held-out test set.

instruction: I need to extract species from this text for a biodiversity monitoring project.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting species and organism names from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the LINNAEUS dataset, a curated biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on species name identification and organism recognition in scientific literature. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.78, precision of 0.72, recall of 0.84, and accuracy of 0.98. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: How likely is it that this card will gain popularity and see a price rise?
description: This model is a PyTorch-based Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) designed to analyze features of Pokémon cards to identify those with potential for a significant price increase. It is a tabular binary classification model that takes various card features—such as rarity, type, and historical price data—as input and outputs a single logit. A sigmoid function can be applied to this logit to obtain a probability score for the likelihood of a price rise. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is part of a custom dataset derived from the PokemonTCG/pokemon-tcg-data repository, augmented with pricing history. The model is trained to predict a 30% price increase over a 6-month period. It is important to note that the model is trained on historical data and may not accurately predict future trends, especially in a volatile market. Additionally, the model is not trained for other thresholds or timeframes, and the dataset may contain biases related to card popularity, set releases, or data collection artifacts. SHAP values were computed to provide model explainability, showing the average impact of each feature on the model's output magnitude.

instruction: Can you translate this Polish message into English for me?
description: The Hunyuan Translation Model includes a translation model, Hunyuan-MT-7B, and an ensemble model, Hunyuan-MT-Chimera. The translation model is used to translate source text into the target language, while the ensemble model integrates multiple translation outputs to produce a higher-quality result. It primarily supports mutual translation among 33 languages, including five ethnic minority languages in China. In the WMT25 competition, the model achieved first place in 30 out of the 31 language categories it participated in. Hunyuan-MT-7B achieves industry-leading performance among models of comparable scale. Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B is the industry’s first open-source translation ensemble model, elevating translation quality to a new level. A comprehensive training framework for translation models has been proposed, spanning from pretrain → cross-lingual pretraining (CPT) → supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → translation enhancement → ensemble refinement, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results for models of similar size. The model supports translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple non-Chinese languages. Specific supported languages include Chinese, English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, Uyghur, and Cantonese. The model can be used with the transformers library, and specific instructions are provided for loading and applying the model, including adjustments for fp8 quantized versions. The model is available for download on Hugging Face, and users are encouraged to use version v4.56.0 of the transformers library. For inference, a set of recommended parameters is provided, including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The model does not have a default system_prompt. The model was open-sourced in September 2025.

instruction: What is happening in the highlighted part of this picture?
description: The VLM-FO1_Qwen2.5-VL-3B-v01 model is an implementation of the VLM-FO1 framework built on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B base model. VLM-FO1 is a novel plug-and-play framework designed to bridge the gap between the high-level reasoning of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and the need for fine-grained visual perception. It endows pre-trained VLMs with superior fine-grained perception without compromising their inherent high-level reasoning and general understanding capabilities. The framework operates as a plug-and-play module that can be integrated with any existing VLM, preserving its original weights and capabilities. VLM-FO1 excels at a wide range of fine-grained perception tasks, including Object Grounding, Region Generative Understanding, and Visual Region Reasoning. The framework is designed as a set of enhancement modules that can be seamlessly integrated with any pre-trained VLM. A novel Dual-Vision Encoder architecture, called the Hybrid Region Encoder (HFRE), fuses semantic-rich features with perception-enhanced features, creating powerful region tokens that capture both high-level meaning and fine-grained spatial detail. VLM-FO1 achieves state-of-the-art results across a diverse suite of benchmarks. A two-stage training strategy ensures that fine-grained perception is gained without causing catastrophic forgetting of the base model's powerful general visual understanding abilities.

instruction: Can you run the quantum circuit on a new data point and return the classification?
description: This is a 2-qubit Quantum Neural Network (QNN) designed for binary classification tasks, specifically trained on the Two Moons dataset to demonstrate quantum machine learning on real quantum hardware. The model uses a hardware-efficient ansatz and has been tested on IBM Quantum's `ibm_fez` backend.
