It is a pure quantum model that uses quantum circuits for feature encoding and classification, optimized for NISQ-era quantum devices. The QNN has a total of 6 parameters, including 2 input parameters and 4 trainable parameters, with a circuit depth of 4 layers. The circuit includes 6 Ry gates and 1 CNOT gate, arranged in a linear entanglement topology. The model architecture consists of an encoding layer, two variational layers, and an entanglement layer, followed by a parity measurement for classification. The model was trained using the COBYLA optimizer and cross-entropy loss function on 8 training samples and tested on 4 test samples, achieving a train accuracy of approximately 87.5% and a test accuracy ranging from 0% to 75%. The low test accuracy is attributed to the small dataset size, quantum noise, and the limitations of NISQ devices. The model is intended as a proof-of-concept and educational tool, not for production use. It includes a parity measurement strategy to create a nonlinear decision boundary in feature space. The model is provided with pre-trained weights and a quantum circuit in Qiskit QPY format, along with helper functions for inference. It requires access to IBM Quantum hardware for execution and is distributed under the Apache License 2.0. The model has several limitations, including a small dataset, low capacity, binary classification only, and the need for quantum access. It is not suitable for multi-class problems or large-scale applications. The model is intended for educational, research, and development purposes, serving as a template for quantum machine learning projects and a benchmark for comparing quantum and classical models. Future improvements include increasing the dataset size, testing on multiple backends, and implementing error mitigation techniques.

instruction: Show me a video of a train passing through a tunnel with lights flickering on the walls.
description: Mochi 1 is a state-of-the-art video generation model with high-fidelity motion and strong prompt adherence in preliminary evaluation. It is an open-source model that significantly closes the gap between closed and open video generation systems. The model is released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license. Mochi 1 is based on a 10 billion parameter diffusion model built on the Asymmetric Diffusion Transformer (AsymmDiT) architecture. It is the largest video generative model ever openly released and features a simple, hackable design. The model is trained entirely from scratch and includes an efficient context parallel implementation for inference. Mochi 1 uses an AsymmVAE video compression model with an asymmetric encoder-decoder structure to compress videos to a 128x smaller size, using 8x8 spatial and 6x temporal compression to a 12-channel latent space. The AsymmDiT architecture processes user prompts and compressed video tokens by streamlining text processing and focusing neural network capacity on visual reasoning. It jointly attends to text and visual tokens with multi-modal self-attention and uses separate MLP layers for each modality. The visual stream has nearly four times as many parameters as the text stream, with non-square QKV and output projection layers to unify the modalities in self-attention. Mochi 1 encodes prompts using a single T5-XXL language model, rather than multiple pretrained language models. The model supports both multi-GPU and single-GPU operation, with single-GPU operation requiring approximately 60GB VRAM. ComfyUI can optimize the model to run on less than 20GB VRAM, but this implementation prioritizes flexibility over memory efficiency. It is recommended to use at least one H100 GPU when using the repository. Mochi 1 is a research preview and is a living and evolving checkpoint. It currently generates videos at 480p resolution and may exhibit minor warping and distortions in some edge cases with extreme motion. The model is optimized for photorealistic styles and does not perform well with animated content. It is expected that the community will fine-tune the model to suit various aesthetic preferences. Genmo video models are general text-to-video diffusion models that reflect the biases and preconceptions found in their training data. While steps have been taken to limit NSFW content, organizations should implement additional safety protocols and careful consideration before deploying these model weights in any commercial services or products.

instruction: I want to understand the emotional dynamics in this podcast excerpt.
description: This model implements dimensional emotion classification based on the approach described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits.' The training pipeline is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. Unlike the official challenge submission, this system does not use transcripts and is designed as a speech-only system to maintain simplicity while ensuring effectiveness. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast dataset and may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which could be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model outputs three continuous values in the range of 0 to 1, representing Arousal (0 calm, 1 active), Valence (0 negative, 1 positive), and Dominance (0 weak, 1 strong). The model is based on the Whisper-large v3 architecture. The model is intended for use with audio data that is no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license and is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use. Users are expected to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and to comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions.

instruction: I want to organize these customer support tickets by the likelihood of resolving the issue quickly.
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and provides a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports over 100 languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on MTEB-R, 75.94 on CMTEB-R, 72.74 on MMTEB-R, 69.97 on MLDR, 81.20 on MTEB-Code, and 14.84 on FollowIR. The model supports instruction-aware reranking, and using instructions typically improves performance by 1% to 5% compared to not using them. Developers are advised to create tailored instructions specific to their tasks and scenarios. In multilingual contexts, instructions should be written in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: Draw a realistic scene of a cat sitting on a windowsill with sunlight streaming in and a garden visible outside.
description: Qwen-Image-2512 is the December update of Qwen-Image’s text-to-image foundational model. It is designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts, with a focus on enhancing realism, detail, and text integration. Compared to the base Qwen-Image model released in August, Qwen-Image-2512 features enhanced human realism, finer natural detail, and improved text rendering. The model significantly reduces the “AI-generated” look and substantially enhances overall image realism, especially for human subjects. It delivers more detailed rendering of landscapes, animal fur, and other natural elements. The model also improves the accuracy and quality of textual elements, achieving better layout and more faithful multimodal (text + image) composition. Over 10,000 rounds of blind model evaluations have been conducted, and the results show that Qwen-Image-2512 is currently the strongest open-source model and remains highly competitive among closed-source models. The model supports a variety of tasks, including generating images with specific aspect ratios, rendering detailed human portraits, landscapes, and complex infographics. It is capable of accurately capturing age cues, facial expressions, and environmental context, as well as rendering fine details such as hair strands, fur, and water flow. The model also supports the generation of text-rich images, including PPT slides, infographics, and educational posters, with improved accuracy, layout, and multimodal integration. The model is implemented using the diffusers library and can be loaded and used with Python code. It is available for use on platforms such as Hugging Face and ModelScope. The model is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on CUDA-enabled devices for faster inference. The model is suitable for a wide range of applications, including but not limited to, content creation, design, and educational materials. However, the model may still have limitations in certain scenarios, and users are encouraged to test and evaluate its performance for their specific use cases.

instruction: Can you help me reorder these search results to find the most relevant ones for a research paper?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B is a text reranking model part of the Qwen3 Embedding series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is a modified version of a foundational model, adapted as a sequence classification model. The Qwen3 Embedding series builds upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series, offering a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). This series inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of its foundational model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these two modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B has 0.6B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports 100+ languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B achieves a score of 65.80 on MTEB-R, 71.31 on CMTEB-R, 66.36 on MMTEB-R, 67.28 on MLDR, 73.42 on MTEB-Code, and 5.41 on FollowIR. Developers are advised to customize the input instructions according to their specific tasks and scenarios, as using instructions typically yields an improvement of 1% to 5% in performance. In multilingual contexts, it is recommended to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: I have an image of a car—can you build a 3D version of it with textures?
description: AIEXX GenAI Image to 3D is a professional, GPU-accelerated AI system for generating high-quality 3D models from text prompts or 2D images. It is built on cutting-edge deep learning technologies and delivers production-ready 3D assets in seconds. The system supports both text-to-3D generation and image-to-3D conversion. For text-to-3D, it uses natural language prompts to generate 3D models, integrating Stable Diffusion 1.5 and supporting advanced negative prompting. This process takes 20-60 seconds per model. For image-to-3D, it converts a single image into a full 3D mesh, with support for multi-view reconstruction and automated texture generation, taking 15-30 seconds to process. The system is optimized for NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPUs, using CUDA 12.8 and cuDNN for acceleration, and supports BF16 mixed precision training and Flash Attention v2. It is implemented in Python 3.11.9 with PyTorch 2.7.0 and includes TripoSR for 3D reconstruction. The system provides professional output in GLB/GLTF format with PBR material support and configurable mesh resolution, compatible with Blender, Unity, and Unreal. It supports local, offline processing with no compilation required, and includes a drag-and-drop interface. The system requires Windows 10/11 (64-bit), an NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPU with 8GB VRAM, 16GB minimum RAM (32GB recommended), and ~55GB of free storage. It offers three workflows: a basic TripoSR pipeline for quick prototypes, an intermediate prompt-to-3D pipeline using Stable Diffusion 1.5, and an advanced InstantMesh pipeline for high-quality output. Performance benchmarks indicate processing times of 15-30 seconds for image-to-3D, 25-40 seconds for prompt-to-3D with TripoSR, and 45-90 seconds for prompt-to-3D with InstantMesh. The system is built upon open-source projects including ComfyUI, Stable Diffusion, TripoSR, PyTorch, and torch_scatter. It is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: What types of content filtering were applied during the training of this system?
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, even though the raw parameter count of the model is 8B. This is achieved by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator, resulting in a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 4B model. The models also use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the E4B model. One sub-model is provided, or users can access a spectrum of custom-sized models. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens in length. They are trained on a dataset totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio, ensuring a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and formats. Rigorous data filtering methods were applied, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional content quality and safety filtering. Gemma 3n models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. Evaluation results show strong performance across a variety of benchmarks, including reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional metrics. The models demonstrated safe levels of performance in child safety, content safety, and representational harms evaluations, with minimal policy violations and significant improvements over previous Gemma models. However, the models have limitations, including potential biases from training data, challenges with complex or open-ended tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and the risk of generating incorrect or outdated factual statements. Ethical considerations include the potential for bias, misinformation, misuse, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to implement content safety safeguards, adhere to privacy regulations, and use the models responsibly. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, education, and data extraction. They offer superior performance compared to other similarly sized open models, making them a high-performance option for responsible AI development.

instruction: Could you calculate the volatility indicator for the molecule represented by the SMILES string C1=CC=CC=C1?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the octanol-air partition coefficient (log KOA). This property measures a compound's tendency to partition between octanol and air, indicating volatility and environmental transport potential. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the KOA dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Can you tag all the chemical terms in this clinical research document?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition, specifically for identifying chemical entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 4,409 annotated chemical entities. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-CHEM` and `I-CHEM`. It is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.93, recall of 0.96, and accuracy of 0.99. It is ranked among the top models on the BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, with an F1 score of 0.9614, precision of 0.9520, recall of 0.9710, and accuracy of 0.9892. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with the Hugging Face pipeline API, and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. For efficient processing of large datasets, the model supports batch processing with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical named entity recognition across multiple public datasets.

instruction: Can you help me edit a photo by adding a sunset background in a few quick steps?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning is a collection of optimized models designed for image editing tasks, utilizing step distillation and quantization techniques to achieve high-efficiency inference performance. The model suite includes three core model files, each with distinct characteristics. One model, named Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-bf16.safetensors, is a 4-step distilled LoRA model with BF16 precision, offering a lightweight and fast inference option. Another model, Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Lightning-4steps-V1.0-fp32.safetensors, is also a 4-step distilled LoRA model but with FP32 precision, providing high accuracy. The third model, qwen_image_edit_2511_fp8_e4m3fn_scaled_lightning.safetensors, is an FP8 quantized model with e4m3fn scaled precision, fused with a 4-step distilled LoRA and optimized for low-memory deployment. The models support two mainstream usage frameworks: the Qwen-Image-Lightning framework and the LightX2V lightweight video/image generation inference framework. The LoRA models are optimized through step distillation, reducing the original inference steps to just 4 steps, which results in a speedup of approximately 10 times faster than standard 40-step inference while maintaining image editing quality. Additionally, the FP8 quantized model balances performance and resource efficiency, reducing GPU memory usage by about 50% compared to FP32 while preserving editing fidelity. For technical support, users can open issues in the respective repositories for Qwen framework-specific questions or LightX2V integration questions.

instruction: Can you help identify if this video involves a road accident or something else?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 300 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 34 videos of abuse, 36 of arrest, 46 of arson, 36 of assault, 70 of burglary, 24 of explosion, 30 of fighting, 86 of road accidents, 98 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, and 62 of stealing. The dataset was split into 192 training videos, 48 validation videos, and 60 test videos. The model achieved a validation accuracy of 0.6250, precision of 0.6351, recall of 0.6250, and F1 score of 0.6114. On the test set, it achieved an accuracy of 0.6667, with equal precision, recall, and F1 score of 0.6667. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. Training was conducted for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and AdamW optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08. The model processes 16 frames per video clip at a resolution of 224x224 pixels and outputs binary classification results. It was trained using the HuggingFace Transformers framework with PyTorch 2.0.1+ and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. The model has several limitations, including potential generalization issues due to dataset scope, possible false positives with non-violent but intense activities, and environmental bias from lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It is intended for research, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes. Prohibited uses include unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, and privacy violations. The model was developed by a research team and is available for use under the HuggingFace Transformers framework.

instruction: I have a video of a busy market. Can you enhance the lighting to simulate a more vibrant atmosphere?
description: TC-Light is a one-shot model designed to manipulate the illumination distribution of video and achieve realistic world transfer. It is particularly suitable for high-dynamic videos, such as those with motion-rich actions and frequent switching of foreground and background objects. The model is distinguished by its outstanding temporal consistency on highly dynamic scenarios and superior computational efficiency, enabling long video processing, such as 300 frames at a resolution of 1280x720 on a 40G A100 GPU. These features make it valuable for sim2real and real2real augmentation for embodied agents or for preparing video pairs to train stronger video relighting models. TC-Light is a novel generative renderer that addresses the limitations of existing models, which are often constrained by the domain of training data or face bottlenecks in temporal consistency and computational efficiency, especially with complex dynamics and long durations. The model operates in two stages: the first stage optimizes appearance embedding to align global illumination, and the second stage optimizes the Unique Video Tensor (UVT), a canonical video representation, to align fine-grained texture and lighting. To evaluate performance, a long and highly dynamic video benchmark was established. Extensive experiments show that TC-Light enables physically plausible re-rendering results with superior temporal coherence and low computational cost. The model tokenizes input latents in the xy plane and yt plane separately, and the predicted noises are combined for denoising. The output undergoes the two-stage optimization process. The model works better on video resolutions over 512x512, as this is the minimum resolution used to train the related IC-Light model. Higher resolutions help maintain the consistency of image intrinsic properties. It performs relatively better on realistic scenes than on synthetic ones, in terms of both temporal consistency and physical plausibility. However, it struggles to drastically change the illumination of night scenarios or hard shadows, as demonstrated in IC-Light.

instruction: What is the expected 3D shape of the protein encoded by the sequence 'QVAGG'?
description: ESM-2 is a state-of-the-art protein model trained on a masked language modelling objective. It predicts protein structures from amino acid sequences, leveraging a transformer-based architecture for accurate 3D modeling. It is suitable for fine-tuning on a wide range of tasks that take protein sequences as input. This version of the ESM-2 model is optimized with NVIDIA's library. It is based on the original ESM-2 model from Facebook Research, and (within numerical precision) has identical weights and outputs. Slight numerical differences may be observed between the original model and the optimized version. The model is ready for commercial and non-commercial use. It is not owned or developed by NVIDIA but has been developed and built to a third-party's requirements for this application and use case. ESM-2 is licensed under a specified license. It is available for global deployment and is intended for use in protein structure prediction, specifically predicting 3D protein structures from amino acid sequences. The model was released on Hugging Face on 07/29/2025. The model architecture is based on a transformer with the ESM-2 network architecture. It has 1.5 x 10^10 model parameters. The input type is text in the form of protein sequences, formatted as a string, with a one-dimensional input. The input consists of a protein sequence represented as a string of canonical amino acids, with a maximum length of 1022. Longer sequences are automatically truncated to this length. The output type is embeddings at the amino acid and sequence level, formatted as a vector with one-dimensional parameters. The output is a numeric vector with floating-point values corresponding to an embedding for each amino acid in the input protein sequence, with a maximum output length of 1022 embeddings. The model is designed and/or optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware (e.g., GPU cores) and software frameworks (e.g., CUDA libraries) to achieve faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. The model integrates with the Hugging Face Transformers runtime engine and is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Blackwell, and Hopper hardware microarchitectures. It is supported on Linux operating systems. Integration of foundation and fine-tuned models into AI systems requires additional testing using use-case-specific data to ensure safe and effective deployment. Iterative testing and validation at both unit and system levels are essential to mitigate risks, meet technical and functional requirements, and ensure compliance with safety and ethical standards before deployment. Several ESM-2 checkpoints are available with varying sizes. Larger checkpoints have better accuracy but require more memory and time to train. The training datasets include UniRef90 and UniRef50 clusters generated from UniRef100 seed sequences using the MMseqs2 algorithm. The data modality is text in the form of protein sequences, with a training data size ranging from 1 billion to 10 trillion tokens. The data collection method is human, and no labeling method is used. The evaluation datasets include data collected from sequences of protein structures about to be released by the Protein Data Bank (PDB) and from CASP14 targets. The benchmark scores for these datasets are 0.72 and 0.55, respectively. The data collection method for both is human, and no labeling method is used. The model is accelerated using the Hugging Face Transformers engine and has been tested on A100, H100, H200, and GB200 hardware. NVIDIA believes Trustworthy AI is a shared responsibility and has established policies and practices to enable development for a wide array of AI applications. When downloaded or used in accordance with the terms of service, developers should work with their internal model team to ensure the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. Users are responsible for ensuring the physical properties of model-generated molecules are appropriately evaluated and comply with applicable safety regulations and ethical standards.

instruction: Generate a photo of a modern kitchen with stainless steel appliances and a bright, open layout.
description: Z-Image is a powerful and highly efficient image generation model with 6B parameters. It includes three variants: Z-Image-Turbo, Z-Image-Base, and Z-Image-Edit. Z-Image-Turbo is a distilled version that matches or exceeds leading competitors with only 8 NFEs (Number of Function Evaluations). It offers sub-second inference latency on enterprise-grade H800 GPUs and fits within 16G VRAM consumer devices. It excels in photorealistic image generation, bilingual text rendering (English and Chinese), and robust instruction adherence. Z-Image-Base is the non-distilled foundation model, intended for community-driven fine-tuning and custom development. Z-Image-Edit is a variant fine-tuned for image editing tasks, supporting creative image-to-image generation with strong instruction-following capabilities for precise edits based on natural language prompts. The model uses a Scalable Single-Stream DiT (S3-DiT) architecture, where text, visual semantic tokens, and image VAE tokens are concatenated at the sequence level to serve as a unified input stream, maximizing parameter efficiency compared to dual-stream approaches. According to Elo-based Human Preference Evaluation, Z-Image-Turbo shows highly competitive performance against other leading models and achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source models. The model is supported by the Decoupled-DMD algorithm, a core few-step distillation method that enables the 8-step Z-Image model. Decoupled-DMD is based on the insight that the success of existing DMD methods results from two independent mechanisms: CFG Augmentation (CA), which drives the distillation process, and Distribution Matching (DM), which ensures the stability and quality of the generated output. By decoupling these mechanisms, the model's distillation process is optimized for performance. Building on Decoupled-DMD, the model also incorporates DMDR, which fuses DMD with Reinforcement Learning (RL) during post-training. DMDR improves semantic alignment, aesthetic quality, and structural coherence, while producing images with richer high-frequency details. The model is available for download and use with the Hugging Face diffusers library, with support added via pull requests merged into the latest official release. Users are advised to install diffusers from source to access the latest features and Z-Image support.

instruction: What is the impact of using a pre-trained setup for short-term training tasks?
description: This is a training adapter designed for fine-tuning, originally intended for use with a specific trainer but potentially compatible with others. It can also be used as a general de-distillation LoRA for inference to remove the 'Turbo' component from 'Z-Image-Turbo'. Training directly on a step distilled model can cause the distillation to break down quickly, leading to unpredictable loss of the step distillation and reduced speed. This adapter significantly slows down the distillation breakdown, allowing for short training runs while preserving the speed benefits of step distillation. However, it is important to note that this is a workaround rather than a complete solution. The distillation will still degrade over time, and long training runs may result in the distillation breaking down to the point where artifacts are produced when the adapter is removed. The adapter was created by generating thousands of images at various sizes and aspect ratios, then training a LoRA on those images at a low learning rate (1e-5). This process allowed the distillation to break down while preserving the model's existing knowledge. When using this adapter, the distillation is already broken down, so any LoRA trained on top of it will not cause further distillation breakdown. This means the new LoRA will only learn the subject being trained. During inference, the adapter is removed, leaving the new information on the distilled model, which allows the new information to run at the faster distilled speeds.

instruction: What is the structure of this piece—verse, chorus, bridge, or something else?
description: Music Flamingo (MF) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) designed to advance music understanding in foundational audio models. It supports deep music understanding across songs and instrumentals, and is capable of generating rich, theory-aware captions and answering questions about harmony, structure, timbre, lyrics, and cultural context. The model is trained using reasoning-centric methods that include chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning with custom rewards for step-by-step reasoning. It is also capable of long-form song reasoning over full-length, multicultural audio with extended context. Evaluations have confirmed its effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 10 public music understanding and reasoning tasks. The model is based on a Transformer architecture and includes an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder from Audio Flamingo 3, an MLP-based audio adaptor, and a decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B). It has 8 billion parameters. The model is intended for non-commercial research purposes only and is supported in HuggingFace Transformers. It processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample, and longer inputs are truncated. The model accepts music (song or instrumental) and text as input, with audio in WAV/MP3/FLAC formats and text in UTF-8. It can handle up to 20 minutes of audio and 24,000 text tokens. The output is text in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum of 2048 tokens. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100) and is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers. It supports Linux-based operating systems and is intended for use in research and development for applications such as music question answering, long-context music comprehension, and interactive music design assistants. The model was trained on music data collected from various sources, with training data ranging from 10,000 to 1 million hours. The training data was collected using human methods, while labeling was a mix of human and automated methods. Testing was performed on datasets with human-collected data, with labeling also a mix of human and automated methods. The model is a preview version, and a better base and a thinking version are expected to be released in the near future. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets industry and use-case requirements and addressing potential misuse. The model is subject to NVIDIA's terms of service and policies on Trustworthy AI.

instruction: Can you recognize the hand holding a piece in this chessboard image?
description: This repository contains fine-tuned models for a Chess Move Tracking pipeline. The first model, yolo11s_pose_chessboard.pt, is based on the YOLO11s-pose architecture and is designed for keypoint detection, specifically for localizing the four corners of a chessboard (a1, h1, a8, h8). It operates on raw video frames, which may be rotated or occluded, and identifies one class, the chessboard. The second model, yolo11m_pieces.pt, is based on the YOLO11m architecture and is designed for object detection, identifying 13 classes: Hand, bB, bK, bN, bP, bQ, bR, wB, wK, wN, wP, wQ, and wR. It operates on warped top-down chessboard images of size 640x640. These models are intended to be used with the ultralytics library, which must be installed via pip. The chessboard corner localization model is used to detect the four semantic corners of the chessboard to assist with perspective warping. The chess pieces detection model is used to detect the 12 chess pieces and the hand on a warped top-down view of the board.

instruction: Can you convert this article and its accompanying image into a single numerical representation for comparison?
description: The embedding model trained by Jina AI is a version of the Qwen2.5-VL model with the code adapter merged into the base weights. This architecture modification enables native compatibility with vLLM without requiring custom adapter-handling code. The model is designed for multimodal multilingual retrieval and supports tasks involving text and image inputs. It is intended to be used for encoding prompts, including text and image data, into embeddings. The model can process text prompts for queries and passages, as well as image prompts with associated descriptions. When processing image prompts, the model identifies and extracts embeddings specifically from the vision tokens. For text-only prompts, it uses all tokens to generate embeddings. The embeddings are pooled and normalized to produce the final output. The model is compatible with the vLLM framework and can be initialized with specific configurations such as data type and pooling settings. It is important to note that the model requires a specific method to extract and process embeddings, particularly for image data, which involves identifying vision token ranges and applying normalization.

instruction: How can I represent this sentence in a way that preserves its meaning for cross-lingual comparison?
description: The Qwen3-Embedding-4B is a text embedding model from the Qwen3 Embedding model series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is part of a comprehensive range of models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B) that provide text embeddings and reranking capabilities. The model inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the Qwen3 series. It supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and offers robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 4 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and an embedding dimension of up to 2560, with support for user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 2560. It is instruction-aware, allowing users to customize input instructions for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, achieving a score of 69.45 on the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of May 24, 2025), and it supports flexible vector definitions and seamless integration with other modules. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal performance in multilingual contexts. The model can be used with frameworks such as Sentence Transformers, Transformers, and vLLM, and it is compatible with deployment via Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on both GPU and CPU devices.

instruction: Show a peaceful sunset over a quiet lake with a small boat drifting by.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with each expert having approximately 14B parameters and a total of 27B parameters. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a defined threshold step for switching. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also introduces a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. This model can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at both 480P and 720P resolutions and delivers outstanding video generation quality. On the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, the model surpasses leading commercial models across most key evaluation dimensions. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: I’m planning a home renovation. Can you generate a 3D scene of the updated layout from my sketch?
description: SpatialGen is a multi-view, multi-modal diffusion model designed to generate 3D indoor scenes conditioned on either a reference image or a textual description, given a 3D semantic layout. The model supports tasks such as image-to-3D scene generation and text-to-3D scene generation. It is intended for use in 3D scene generation applications and is based on a diffusion model architecture. The model has been developed as part of a research project and is associated with a paper published in the International Conference on 3D Vision in 2026. The model is currently available for inference, with a dataset containing 48 rooms and 4.8K rendered images provided for multi-view diffusion inference. The model is derived from another project and is licensed under the same license as that project. The model is tested with Python 3.10, PyTorch 2.3.1, and CUDA Version 12.1. Training instructions and a full dataset release are planned for future updates.

instruction: How can I animate this person in the picture so that their form stays consistent throughout the movement?
description: SteadyDancer is a model designed for harmonized and coherent human image animation with first-frame preservation. It is intended to animate human images while maintaining the structure and appearance of the first frame. The model is associated with the Multimedia Computing Group at Nanjing University and the Platform and Content Group (PCG) at Tencent. This repository provides checkpoints for the model described in the paper titled 'SteadyDancer: Harmonized and Coherent Human Image Animation with First-Frame Preservation.' The model offers lower computational cost compared to alternatives. For users encountering a 'RuntimeError: expected scalar type Float but found BFloat16' issue, it is recommended to pull the latest code of Kijai ComfyUI. For each quantized version of the model, two sets of weights are provided: one derived from fp16 format and another from fp32 format. Both sets of weights are available and yield largely consistent results. The team is currently working to determine the final version of the model to be retained.

instruction: Can you determine the angle of a person's elbow in a captured image for a rehabilitation app?
description: The Person Pose Detection 480x640 model, developed by Synaptics, is a lightweight quantized tflite model designed for the SR100 processor in the Synaptics Astra™ SR MCU Series. It is intended to generate bounding boxes and identify 17 keypoints for each person, corresponding to various body parts. Each keypoint is accompanied by a confidence score that indicates the reliability of the detection, enabling accurate estimation of the person's pose. The model is designed to fit in Flash and is also intended to fit in SRAM. It is a pose detection model with an input size of 480x640 and outputs, for each detected person, bounding box coordinates, 17 keypoint locations, and confidence scores. The model is designed for a single class (person) and is specifically intended for pose estimation of people. It is optimized for deployment on the Astra™ SR100 MCU, with INT8 quantization and preprocessing that requires input images to be resized and quantized to match model requirements. The model is intended for real-time person pose estimation on embedded, resource-constrained edge devices. Typical use cases include human activity recognition and gesture control, fitness and wellness tracking, smart home automation based on body posture, and interactive applications and user interfaces. The model can be evaluated and tested in a hosted environment optimized for the Synaptics SR110 MCU, which provides a seamless sandbox for model evaluation using hardware-specific quantization and runtime settings. The model is distributed under a license that allows flexible use, modification, and distribution.

instruction: I need to create a budget spreadsheet for the upcoming month. Can you suggest some templates and help me fill them in?
description: Fara-7B is Microsoft's first agentic small language model (SLM) designed specifically for computer use. With 7 billion parameters, it is an ultra-compact Computer Use Agent (CUA) that achieves state-of-the-art performance within its size class and is competitive with larger, more resource-intensive agentic systems. Fara-7B is a multimodal decoder-only language model that takes an image (screenshot) and text context as input and directly predicts thoughts and actions with grounded arguments. It is based on Qwen 2.5-VL (7B). The model accepts user goals (text), current screenshots, and a history of previous outputs (thoughts and actions) as input. It has a context length of 128k and produces generated text in response to the input, with a chain-of-thought block followed by a tool call block to indicate the action. Fara-7B was trained using 64 H100 GPUs over 2.5 days. It is a static model trained on public and private data, with training occurring between October 26th and October 29th, 2025. It was released on November 24th, 2025, under an MIT license. Fara-7B is specialized for taking actions on the web to accomplish high-level user tasks, such as booking restaurants, applying for jobs, planning trips, and buying shopping lists. It perceives browser inputs via screenshots, while internal reasoning and state history are recorded textually. Based on recent screenshots and a full history of actions, it predicts the next action with necessary arguments, such as coordinates for clicks. Fara-7B uses a robust post-training safety approach leveraging open-source and in-house synthetic datasets. It incorporates critical point recognition—situations requiring user permission or sensitive information—to safely halt actions. The model is trained to refuse harmful tasks and undergoes automated red teaming to assess risks, including grounding, jailbreaks, harmful content, and copyright violations. Fara-7B is trained to refuse tasks in categories that violate usage policy, including illegal activities, deceptive tasks, high-risk or regulated domains, harassment, exploitation, hate, unsafe technical use, misinformation, and sexual content. Critical points where the agent stops include entering personal information, completing purchases, making calls, sending emails, submitting applications, and signing into accounts. Fara-7B can be used to automate web tasks such as shopping, booking travel, restaurant reservations, information-seeking, or account workflows. It performs actions step-by-step using multimodal understanding from browser screenshots. On-device execution provides privacy guarantees and lower latency. It is not evaluated for all downstream purposes, and users should consider the limitations of LLMs for accuracy, safety, and fairness. It must adhere to applicable laws and regulations and is currently limited to English-only support. Fara-7B is available through Hugging Face and Azure AI Foundry. It requires specific input formats, including the use of the ChatML template with a system prompt for inference. The system prompt instructs the model to stop execution at critical points, such as checkout, booking, purchasing, calling, emailing, or ordering. It provides function signatures for all actions, including key presses, typing, mouse movements, left clicks, scrolling, visiting URLs, web searches, browser history navigation, memorizing facts, waiting, and terminating tasks. Fara-7B has technical requirements including Python packages such as torch, transformers, and vllm, and is tested on NVIDIA A6000, A100, and H100 GPUs. It is recommended to use vLLM server with bf16 precision and is provided via Magentic-UI in a Docker sandbox for safe web execution. Responsible AI considerations include its English-only support, potential for stereotype reinforcement or inappropriate content, the need to verify outputs in high-stakes or regulated domains, and the risk of misuse for fraud, spam, or malware generation. Users are advised to use safety services like Azure AI Content Safety, implement human-in-the-loop monitoring, sandboxing, access control, and output verification. Fara-7B is trained using a multi-agent data generation pipeline that produces synthetic trajectories from seed URLs and open-source tasks. It records screenshots, thoughts, action traces, and verification via verifier agents. The training data includes high-quality public datasets with image and text modalities, as well as specialized data for grounding, UI understanding (VQA, captioning, OCR), and safety/refusal datasets. Fara-7B has been evaluated on benchmarks such as WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, DeepShop, and WebTailBench, achieving a task completion success rate of 73.5 on WebVoyager, 34.1 on Online-Mind2Web, 26.2 on DeepShop, and 38.4 on WebTailBench. These scores are averaged over three runs. It has undergone safety evaluation and red-teaming on Azure, focusing on grounding, jailbreaks, harmful content, and copyright. Guidelines for safe use include human-in-the-loop monitoring, not sharing sensitive data, running in sandboxed environments, limiting internet access via allow-lists or block-lists, and avoiding use in commercial, high-stakes, or regulated domains. Security considerations include the model's ability to automate interactions across websites, apps, and operating systems, which requires strict access control, sandboxing, and monitoring. Fara-7B is based on Qwen 2.5 VL, which has an Apache 2.0 license. Fara-7B is released under an MIT license, and both licenses are compatible.

instruction: What is the most prominent color in this image, and what does it suggest?
description: MobileCLIP2-S3-OpenCLIP is a variant of the MobileCLIP2 model, adapted from the original Apple model and configured using OpenCLIP settings and weight naming conventions. MobileCLIP2 was introduced in a publication featured in TMLR August 2025 by Fartash Faghri, Pavan Kumar Anasosalu Vasu, Cem Koc, Vaishaal Shankar, Alexander T Toshev, Oncel Tuzel, and Hadi Pouransari. The MobileCLIP2-S3 checkpoint is included in this repository. MobileCLIP2-S4 matches the accuracy of SigLIP-SO400M/14 with 2x fewer parameters and surpasses DFN ViT-L/14 at 2.5x lower latency measured on iPhone12 Pro Max. MobileCLIP-S3 and S4 are new architectures trained on the MobileCLIP training dataset, DataCompDR-1B. The smallest variant, MobileCLIP-S0, achieves similar zero-shot performance as ViT-B/16 while being 4.8x faster and 2.8x smaller. MobileCLIP-S2 achieves better average zero-shot performance than ViT-B/16 while being 2.3x faster and 2.1x smaller, and was trained with 3x fewer seen samples. MobileCLIP-B (LT) attains a zero-shot ImageNet performance of 77.2%, which is significantly better than recent works with similar architectures or even others. The model is designed for tasks involving image and text processing, including zero-shot classification and cross-modal feature encoding. It is based on a vision-language architecture and is optimized for performance on mobile devices. The model's performance is evaluated on the ImageNet-1k dataset and 38 other datasets, with results including zero-shot top-1 accuracy and average performance metrics. The model supports inference and model exporting, with optional reparameterization for improved performance. It uses a tokenizer and preprocessing steps for image and text inputs, and outputs label probabilities based on encoded features.

instruction: Can you find documents that discuss the same concept as the one I provided?
description: This is a model finetuned from a base model. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 8192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. It is designed for the English language and is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a ModernBertModel, a Pooling layer with mean token pooling, and a Normalize layer. The model was trained on 2,828 triplet samples with anchor, positive, and negative text examples. Training used a batch size of 16, a learning rate of 2e-05, and one training epoch with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was evaluated on 489 samples, achieving a cosine accuracy of 0.9959 on the dev dataset. The model is intended for use with the Sentence Transformers library and supports inference for generating embeddings and computing similarity scores between text inputs.

instruction: Can you make a video of a dragon soaring over a misty valley at sunrise?
description: Self Forcing is a method for training autoregressive video diffusion models by simulating the inference process during training, using autoregressive rollout with KV caching. This approach addresses the train-test distribution mismatch and allows for real-time, streaming video generation on a single RTX 4090 while maintaining the quality of state-of-the-art diffusion models. The model is designed to generate high-quality video content and supports both GUI and CLI-based inference. It is trained using a data-free algorithm, meaning no video data is required, and instead uses text prompts and an ODE-initialized checkpoint. The model can be trained using multiple GPUs, with a training run of 600 iterations completing in under 2 hours using 64 H100 GPUs. Performance can be optimized by enabling `torch.compile` or using FP8 Linear layers, though these may affect quality. The model works best with long, detailed prompts, and it is recommended to use third-party LLMs like GPT-4o to extend prompts before input. The model supports chunk-wise autoregressive generation and is evaluated using prompts from the MovieGenVideoBench dataset. The codebase is based on open-source implementations from other repositories. The model is available on HuggingFace and requires an Nvidia GPU with at least 24 GB memory, a Linux operating system, and 64 GB RAM for optimal performance.

instruction: Is it possible to identify the position of a person in this image for a movement analysis?
description: YOLO26-S-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection, or pose estimation. It is designed for edge and low-power devices, with a focus on simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. The model operates with an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 63.0 for pose estimation at 50-95 and 86.6 at 50. It runs at 85.3 milliseconds on CPU (ONNX) and 2.7 milliseconds on T4 TensorRT10, with 10.4 million parameters and 23.9 billion FLOPs. The architecture is end-to-end, eliminating the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which reduces latency and simplifies deployment. It features the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which improves stability and convergence. Additional features include Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for faster inference, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing. The model can be used for pose estimation tasks and is intended to be deployed in real-world systems where low latency and hardware efficiency are critical. It is available for download and inference via the Hugging Face Hub and the Ultralytics library.

instruction: What do you see in this photo of a tropical forest with sunlight filtering through the trees?
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed for both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is pretrained on the COCO dataset using a base architecture with a Vision Transformer (ViT) base backbone. The model supports conditional and unconditional image captioning, allowing users to generate captions based on an image or a given text prompt. BLIP is designed to transfer flexibly to a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval, image captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). It achieves state-of-the-art results, with improvements of +2.7% in average recall@1 for image-text retrieval, +2.8% in CIDEr for image captioning, and +1.6% in VQA score. Additionally, BLIP demonstrates strong generalization ability when applied to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model utilizes noisy web data by bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. This release is intended for research purposes in support of an academic paper. The model, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model is released under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license.

instruction: What type of laboratory activity is being shown in this recorded clip?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of VideoMAE-v2 vit_base_patch16_224 for video classification on the FineBio dataset. The base model is VideoMAE-v2 (vit_base_patch16_224), and it has been fine-tuned on the FineBio dataset for a total of 32 classes. The model processes video clips consisting of 16 frames, each with a resolution of 224x224 pixels. The training configuration includes the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001, a batch size of 8 (effective: 24), and 50 training epochs. Additional training parameters include a weight decay of 0.05, a layer decay of 0.75, a drop path rate of 0.1, and 10 warmup epochs. The dataset used is a custom video dataset for laboratory action recognition, which contains 32 classes, but the sizes of the training, validation, and test sets are not provided. The best validation accuracy is not available. The model is intended for video classification tasks and is based on the VideoMAE-v2 architecture. Users are advised to cite the associated research paper if they use this model. For any questions or issues, users should open an issue in the model repository.

instruction: What are some practical strategies for improving focus and productivity while working remotely?
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two open models are available: gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b. The gpt-oss-120b is intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters. The gpt-oss-20b is intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and should only be used with the harmony format, as they will not work correctly otherwise. The gpt-oss models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them ideal for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, facilitating easier debugging and increased trust in outputs, though this is not intended to be shown to end users. The models are fine-tunable, allowing for full customization to specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning. They support agentic capabilities, including function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The gpt-oss-20b model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware, while the gpt-oss-120b model can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node. The models support web browsing using built-in tools, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks. The reasoning level can be set in the system prompts to low, medium, or high, depending on the task requirements. The gpt-oss models can be used with various frameworks and tools, including Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. Model weights can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI.

instruction: How can I get a detailed breakdown of the visual elements present in this 512x512 satellite image?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained following the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. These models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens, image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2. However, a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on various tasks. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU↓, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16; 88.0 on IN-ReaL, 68.8 on IN-R, 54.6 on Obj.Net, 50.0 on Ox.-H, 48.8 on ADE20k, 0.399 on NYU↓, 75.5 on DAVIS, 57.1 on NAVI, and 55.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S+/16; 89.3 on IN-ReaL, 76.7 on IN-R, 64.1 on Obj.Net, 58.5 on Ox.-H, 51.8 on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU↓, 77.2 on DAVIS, 58.8 on NAVI, and 57.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-B/16; 90.2 on IN-ReaL, 88.1 on IN-R, 74.8 on Obj.Net, 63.1 on Ox.-H, 54.9 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.9 on DAVIS, 62.3 on NAVI, and 61.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; 90.3 on IN-ReaL, 90.0 on IN-R, 78.6 on Obj.Net, 64.5 on Ox.-H, 54.8 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.3 on DAVIS, 63.3 on NAVI, and 56.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-H+/16; and 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU↓, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU↓, and 0.448 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny; 87.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 88.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 44.8 on NYU↓, and 0.432 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Small; 88.5 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.2 on IN-ReaL @512px, 77.2 on Obj.Net @256px, 78.2 on Obj.Net @512px, 56.2 on ADE20k @256px, 61.3 on ADE20k @512px, 46.3 on NYU↓, and 0.420 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Base; and 88.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.4 on IN-ReaL @512px, 81.3 on Obj.Net @256px, 82.4 on Obj.Net @512px, 59.3 on ADE20k @256px, 65.2 on ADE20k @512px, 47.8 on NYU↓, and 0.403 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Large. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite (SAT-493M), the results include 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the results include 94.2 on m-cashew, 75.6 on m-chesapeake, 61.8 on m-NeonTree, 83.7 on m-nz-cattle, 95.2 on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 on m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model training for the ViT-7B model used 61,440 hours of compute on Nvidia H100 GPUs in a private infrastructure in the USA, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-S+ (29M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; ViT-B (86M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 768, 4 register tokens, 12 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-L (300M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1024, 4 register tokens, 16 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-H+ (840M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1280, 4 register tokens, 20 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; and ViT-7B (6716M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 4096, 4 register tokens, 32 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Design an image of a mystical forest with glowing mushrooms, fireflies, and ancient ruins.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model. The unsloth/FLUX.2-klein-9B-GGUF model uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision and tooling from city96. The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality. FLUX.2 [klein] 9B is a 9 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is the flagship small model, defining the Pareto frontier for quality versus latency across text-to-image, single-reference editing, and multi-reference generation. It matches or exceeds models five times its size in under half a second. Built on a 9B flow model with an 8B Qwen3 text embedder, it is step-distilled to four inference steps. The model is a distilled model for sub-second image generation with outstanding quality. It supports text-to-image and image-to-image multi-reference editing in a single unified model. It is great for real-time generation and integration into applications. It is ideal for creative exploration with excellent prompt adherence and output diversity. It is available for non-commercial use. A reference implementation of FLUX.2 [klein] 9B, as well as sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository. Developers and creatives looking to build on top of FLUX.2 [klein] 9B are encouraged to use this as a starting point. The FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model is available via the BFL API and is also available in both quantized and full-precision versions. To use FLUX.2 [klein] 9B with the Diffusers Python library, first install or upgrade diffusers. Then you can use Flux2KleinPipeline to run the model. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under. The FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model fits in approximately 29GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and above. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of its models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in its model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation included multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with text-only prompts, a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision included a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks, and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license, and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models were approved for release under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on the company's hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Policies governing acceptable use of the models and access to the API are set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes.
