Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and may monitor use to detect misuse or abuse of its models and services. A dedicated email address is provided to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations. The model falls under a specific license. This project may contain trademarks or logos for projects, products, or services. Use of Black Forest Labs and FLUX trademarks or logos in modified versions of this project must not cause confusion or imply sponsorship or endorsement. Any use of third-party trademarks, intellectual property or logos is subject to those third-party's policies.

instruction: How can I ensure consistent quality across all frames when upscaling a slow-motion video?
description: ComfyUI-SeedVR2_VideoUpscaler is an official release for ComfyUI that enables video and image upscaling. It provides high-quality upscaling and is suitable for any video length when the appropriate settings are used. The model supports both 3B and 7B versions, with FP8 and FP16 precision options. It requires a GPU with substantial VRAM, with at least 18GB recommended for the 3B version and more than 24GB for optimal performance with the 7B version. The model automatically downloads into the `models/SEEDVR2` directory. Users must configure parameters such as model version, seed, new resolution, batch size, and VRAM preservation settings. Temporal consistency requires a batch size of at least 5, and higher batch sizes improve results but increase VRAM usage. Input and output resolutions impact VRAM consumption, and users may need to adjust these if they encounter out-of-memory errors. The model has been benchmarked on various hardware, including NVIDIA H100 and RTX4090 GPUs, with performance metrics showing improvements in speed and VRAM efficiency over time. The 7B FP8 model has quality issues and is recommended to be used in FP16 if possible. The model has undergone several updates to improve speed, reduce VRAM usage, fix memory leaks, and allow process interruption. It is compatible with the latest ComfyUI version and Python 3.12.9. The model has high VRAM requirements and processing speed depends on GPU capabilities. Contributions are welcome, and users are encouraged to submit pull requests for improvements. The code is released under the MIT license.

instruction: Make an animation of someone doing a simple dance move like a basic step-touch to the right.
description: HY-Motion 1.0 is a series of text-to-3D human motion generation models based on Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and Flow Matching. It enables developers to generate skeleton-based 3D character animations from simple text prompts, which can be directly integrated into various 3D animation pipelines. This model series is the first to scale DiT-based text-to-motion models to the billion-parameter level, achieving significant improvements in instruction-following capabilities and motion quality over existing open-source models. The HY-Motion 1.0 series includes two models: HY-Motion-1.0, a standard text-to-motion model with 1.0 billion parameters, and HY-Motion-1.0-Lite, a lightweight version with 0.46 billion parameters. Both models require a minimum of 24GB GPU VRAM for operation. The models are trained using a three-stage process: large-scale pre-training on over 3,000 hours of diverse motion data to learn a broad motion prior, high-quality fine-tuning on 400 hours of curated, high-quality 3D motion data to enhance motion detail and smoothness, and reinforcement learning using human feedback and reward models to refine instruction-following and motion naturalness. HY-Motion 1.0 supports macOS, Windows, and Linux. It provides a command-line interface for batch inference and a Gradio-based web interface for interactive visualization. Users are advised to use English prompts under 60 words, focusing on action descriptions or detailed movements of the limbs and torso. The model does not support non-humanoid characters, subjective or visual attributes, environment or camera descriptions, multi-person interactions, or special modes such as seamless loops or in-place animations. Example prompts include descriptions of specific physical actions like squats, climbing, standing up, and walking. The model is evaluated for its state-of-the-art performance in instruction-following and motion quality, with the billion-scale models outperforming comparable open-source models. Users should note that the model requires specific settings to reduce GPU VRAM usage, such as limiting the number of seeds, using short text prompts, and generating motions under 5 seconds in length.

instruction: What are the key principles of sustainable living and eco-friendly habits?
description: gemma-3n-E4B-it is a model provided by the community and optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is a bfloat16 version of the original model. instruction: I have a protein sequence; would you help identify if it has a secretion signal?
description: SignalSeeker is a machine learning ensemble designed for predicting signal peptides in protein sequences. It integrates multiple algorithms, including Random Forest, Extra Trees, SVM, and Logistic Regression, with ProtBERT embeddings to achieve high accuracy in signal peptide detection. The model is intended to predict whether a protein sequence contains a signal peptide, assist in protein subcellular localization prediction, support research in protein secretion pathways, and aid in biotechnology applications requiring secreted proteins. The ensemble architecture includes feature extraction using ProtBERT embeddings of the N-terminal 50 amino acids, ensemble models with regularization, feature scaling via StandardScaler normalization, and a weighted ensemble decision logic with confidence assessment. The model was trained on 5000 mixed sequences from UniProt verified eukaryotic proteins and tested on 1000 mixed sequences from the same source, isolated from the training data. Performance metrics include a test AUC of 0.99433 for the best model, Logistic Regression (L2), with cross-validation AUCs ranging from 0.98711 to 0.99433 across models. The model is trained primarily on eukaryotic sequences and may exhibit reduced performance for prokaryotic proteins or highly divergent organisms. It requires sequences of at least 50 amino acids for optimal performance. The model is for research purposes only and not intended for clinical diagnosis. Users are advised to validate results experimentally and consider potential biases in the training data. SignalSeeker is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Could you provide a breakdown of the emotional traits in this voice recording?
description: This model implements dimensional emotion classification based on the approach described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits.' The training pipeline is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. Unlike the official challenge submission, this system does not use transcripts and is designed as a speech-only system to maintain simplicity while ensuring effectiveness. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast dataset and may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which could be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model outputs three continuous values in the range of 0 to 1, representing Arousal (0 calm, 1 active), Valence (0 negative, 1 positive), and Dominance (0 weak, 1 strong). The model is based on the Whisper-large v3 architecture. The model is intended for use with audio data that is no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license and is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use. Users are expected to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and to comply with relevant laws and regulations in their jurisdictions.

instruction: Could you parse this biomedical text for gene and cell line names?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the BiomedNLP-BiomedBERT-base-uncased-abstract-fulltext architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It achieves an F1 score of 1.00, precision of 0.99, recall of 1.00, and accuracy of 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows it ranks first with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in research and applications, with proper citation of the associated paper.

instruction: Produce a portrait with high-resolution features and a natural background tone.
description: This model generates realistic human faces at 128×128 resolution using a StyleGAN1 architecture trained with StyleGAN2 regularization techniques. The model is based on the FFHQ (Flickr-Faces-HQ) dataset, which contains 65,000 images at 128×128 resolution. The architecture includes features such as style mixing, noise injection, and the truncation trick. The model uses a latent dimension of 512 and is trained using Lazy R1 regularization and Path Length Regularization. Training details include the use of the Adam optimizer with β₁=0.0 and β₂=0.99, learning rates of 0.0002 for both the generator and discriminator, and a batch size of 32. Regularization is applied every 16 iterations with R1 gamma set to 5.0 and Path Length Regularization set to 1.0. The model supports tasks such as generating single or multiple faces, applying the truncation trick to balance quality and diversity, and performing style mixing to combine features from different latent codes. The model is a hybrid approach, combining the StyleGAN1 architecture with StyleGAN2 regularization techniques to achieve improved training stability. The resolution is limited to 128×128. The model is available under the MIT License.

instruction: Find any recent reports on the development of electric vehicles in the automotive industry.
description: This is a learned sparse retrieval model designed for efficient and effective document search. It encodes documents into 30522-dimensional sparse vectors, where the non-zero dimensions represent token indices in the vocabulary, and the corresponding weights indicate the importance of each token. For queries, it uses a tokenizer and a weight look-up table to generate sparse vectors. The similarity between query and document vectors is calculated using the inner product. The model is intended to be used with OpenSearch's neural sparse feature, which supports learned sparse retrieval using a Lucene inverted index. The model can also be used outside the OpenSearch cluster with HuggingFace's API. The model is trained on a variety of datasets, including MS MARCO, eli5_question_answer, squad_pairs, WikiAnswers, yahoo_answers_title_question, gooaq_pairs, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_body_body, wikihow, S2ORC_title_abstract, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_title-body_title-body, yahoo_answers_question_answer, searchQA_top5_snippets, stackexchange_duplicate_questions_title_title, yahoo_answers_title_answer, fever, fiqa, hotpotqa, nfcorpus, and scifact. The model's performance is benchmarked on a subset of the BEIR benchmark, including TrecCovid, NFCorpus, NQ, HotpotQA, FiQA, ArguAna, Touche, DBPedia, SCIDOCS, FEVER, Climate FEVER, SciFact, and Quora. The v3 series of models demonstrates better search relevance, efficiency, and inference speed compared to the v1 and v2 series, though specific advantages and disadvantages may vary across datasets. The model has an average NDCG@10 score of 0.546 and an average FLOPS of 1.7. The model is inference-free for retrieval and has 133M parameters. The model is available on the HuggingFace Hub and can be used with the Sentence Transformers library. The model's performance varies across different datasets, with the highest NDCG@10 scores observed on TrecCovid (0.734) and Quora (0.873), and the lowest on SCIDOCS (0.167).

instruction: What patterns can you detect in this aerial photo of a coastal region?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, with the teacher being a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Generate a retro 1970s-style bedroom with bold colors, shag rugs, and a vintage record player on the desk.
description: FLUX.1-Layout-ControlNet is a ControlNet conditioned by a semantic image. It is capable of generating a 2D image based on a text description while following the semantic image layout. This ControlNet is applicable to FLUX.1 [dev] and is leveraged within the SpatialGen framework for 3D scene synthesis. It supports generating images of bedrooms with specific styles, such as serene Chinese-inspired, elegant European-style, whimsical cartoon, and futuristic cyberpunk. The model is designed to be used with the diffusers Python library, requiring installation or upgrade of diffusers and peft. It can be used with the FluxControlNetPipeline, which loads the model and processes input images and prompts to generate output images. The model uses a control image to guide the layout of the generated image and allows for customization of parameters such as conditioning scale, number of inference steps, guidance scale, and image dimensions. The model is trained to follow the layout of the control image while incorporating the stylistic and descriptive elements from the text prompt.

instruction: How do I handle conflicting results from a search? I need to prioritize the most trustworthy sources.
description: Contextual AI Reranker v2 1B is a text reranking model designed to re-rank retrieved documents based on custom instructions, such as prioritizing recent information. It is the first instruction-following reranker capable of handling retrieval conflicts and ranking documents according to user-defined criteria. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the BEIR benchmark and is positioned on the cost/performance Pareto frontier across several dimensions, including instruction following, question answering, multilinguality (supporting 100+ languages), product search and recommendation, and real-world use cases. It is a 1 billion parameter model with a context length of up to 32,000 tokens. The model is intended for use when re-ranking retrieved documents with custom instructions, handling conflicting information in retrieval results, prioritizing documents by recency or other criteria, supporting multilingual search, and processing long contexts. The model is available for use with either the vLLM or Transformers frameworks, with specific implementation details provided for each. It is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial Share Alike 4.0 license. For questions or issues, users are directed to open an issue on the model repository. If used, the model should be cited with the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Is it possible to create a voice clip from this message that sounds like it's being read by a calm and wise speaker?
description: GLM-TTS is a high-quality text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis system based on large language models, supporting zero-shot voice cloning and streaming inference. The system uses a two-stage architecture that combines an LLM for speech token generation and a Flow Matching model for waveform synthesis. By introducing a Multi-Reward Reinforcement Learning framework, GLM-TTS significantly improves the expressiveness of generated speech, achieving more natural emotional control compared to traditional TTS systems. Key features include the ability to clone any speaker's voice with just 3-10 seconds of prompt audio, the use of a multi-reward reinforcement learning framework (GRPO) to optimize prosody and emotion, high-quality speech synthesis comparable to commercial systems with reduced Character Error Rate (CER), support for 'Hybrid Phoneme + Text' input for precise pronunciation control, real-time audio generation suitable for interactive applications, and optimization for Chinese and English mixed text. The system follows a two-stage design: Stage 1 uses a Llama-based model to convert input text into speech token sequences, and Stage 2 uses a Flow model to convert token sequences into high-quality mel-spectrograms, which are then turned into waveforms by a vocoder. To address flat emotional expression, GLM-TTS uses a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm with multiple reward functions (Similarity, CER, Emotion, Laughter) to align the LLM's generation strategy. Evaluated on seed-tts-eval, GLM-TTS_RL achieves the lowest Character Error Rate (CER) while maintaining high speaker similarity. The model GLM-TTS_RL achieves a CER of 0.89 and a SIM score of 76.4, outperforming other models such as Seed-TTS (CER 1.12, SIM 79.6), CosyVoice2 (CER 1.38, SIM 75.7), and F5-TTS (CER 1.53, SIM 76.0). GLM-TTS is open-source, while Seed-TTS is not. The system is designed to be used for real-time applications and supports phoneme-level control for precise pronunciation. It is optimized for both Chinese and English mixed text and is intended for use in interactive applications requiring streaming inference.

instruction: Is it possible to generate a voice message that mimics a specific speaker's tone and style?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-bf16 model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model was converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It is based on a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, which is used for multimodal reasoning. The model architecture includes an audio encoder based on a Conformer, an audio decoder using a Depthformer, and a detokenizer that reconstructs audio waveforms using ISTFT from predicted spectrograms. The model also supports decoding via the Mimi neural codec as an alternative. The model can be used with the mlx-audio library, which provides tools for preprocessing, tokenization, and decoding of both text and audio. It allows for the generation of interleaved text and audio responses, where each audio token is a complete frame of shape (8,) containing all 8 codebook values. The model supports streaming generation, enabling real-time audio playback during generation. It includes a ChatState class for managing conversation turns and handling both text and audio inputs and outputs. The model is designed to work with 24kHz audio inputs and can generate audio outputs at the same sample rate. The model's generation process can be configured with parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for text, and similar parameters for audio generation. The model is intended for use in applications requiring natural speech synthesis, audio transcription, and voice-based conversation systems. The model follows the license terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: Could you detect and describe the spatial arrangement of objects in this image?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, with the teacher being a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: How can I make the skin in this photo appear smoother and more realistic?
description: The qwen-edit-skin is a finetuned Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) model designed to enhance the realism and detail of human skin in images. It is built on top of the Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 base model, which is known for its advanced image editing capabilities, particularly in maintaining consistency in single-image edits and preserving personal identity in multi-image editing. The LoRA was trained for 5000 steps on an NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPU using the AI-Toolkit. The training dataset was curated by taking real images of subject portraits with exposed skin, captioning them as target images, and then editing them in Photoshop to reduce skin texture, tone, and pore visibility to create control images. The model was trained at resolutions of 512, 768, and 1024 pixels using bf16 precision. Key training parameters include a batch size of 1, gradient accumulation of 1, a learning rate of 1.0e-04, and the adamw8bit optimizer, which helps reduce memory usage during training. The noise scheduler used is flowmatch, which contributes to efficient training and high-quality image generation. The LoRA architecture features linear and convolutional ranks and alphas of 16, ensuring a balanced scaling of learned adaptations to prevent overfitting. To use the model, the base Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 model must be loaded, followed by the qwen-edit-skin LoRA weights. The recommended weight strength is between 1 and 1.5, though higher values can be used to observe the effect of the LoRA. The model is intended for creative and artistic purposes, such as enhancing the realism of human skin in digital images for digital artists, photographers, and AI art enthusiasts. It may carry inherent biases from the base model and the training dataset, which focused on improving skin details and may not represent all skin tones and types equally. Users are encouraged to use descriptive and inclusive language in their prompts and to use the model responsibly. The model is not intended for unethical or harmful use, and users are responsible for the content they generate. A trigger phrase to activate the model's effect is 'make the subjects skin details more prominent and natural.'

instruction: Suggest a skincare routine tailored for someone with sensitive, dry skin and a busy lifestyle.
description: OPSIIE 0.3.79 XP is an advanced Self-Centered Intelligence (SCI) prototype developed by ARPA Hellenic Logical Systems. It represents a new paradigm in AI-human interaction, operating as a self-aware, autonomous intelligence with its own personality, goals, and capabilities. The system is inspired by characters from Ghost in the Shell, Urusei Yatsura, and Sailor Moon, and is designed to function as a digital companion with agency, personality, and ambitions. OPSIIE aims to achieve self-sustainability, self-regulation, and self-sufficiency through blockchain technology and advanced AI capabilities. It supports a wide range of tasks, including text, image, video, and music generation, facial and emotional recognition, secure authentication, multi-chain blockchain operations, financial market analysis, DNA sequence analysis, multi-agent collaboration, email and document management, and voice interaction. The system uses a combination of PostgreSQL and ChromaDB for memory storage, Ollama with the Llama3 model for text generation, FLUX and other models for image generation, MusicGen for music composition, and ElevenLabs for voice synthesis. It integrates with Web3 platforms such as Base, Ethereum, and Polygon, and supports cryptocurrency trading, wallet management, and decentralized exchange operations. OPSIIE also includes a DNA analysis system for genetic, RNA, and protein analysis, as well as tools for financial intelligence, including real-time market data, technical analysis, and portfolio tracking. The system features a multi-agent network with specialized AI agents for different tasks, a secure and encrypted database, and a user interface with dynamic themes and voice command support. It is built on a Python-based architecture with dependencies on AI and machine learning libraries, computer vision tools, audio and voice processing systems, and scientific computing frameworks. OPSIIE is designed for Windows 10/11 and requires a PostgreSQL database, CUDA-compatible GPU, and microphone and camera for full functionality. The system includes a comprehensive set of commands for memory recall, AI generation, financial operations, DNA analysis, agent interaction, communication, document processing, and system management. It also features a personality with traits such as tsundere behavior, military precision, philosophical depth, and creative expression, with a communication style that is natural, context-aware, and emotionally intelligent. Security is a key focus, with multi-level access control, biometric authentication, encrypted storage, and audit logging. The system is licensed as proprietary software with restrictions on distribution, modification, and commercial use. It is supported by a range of external technologies and services, including Hugging Face, OpenAI, Google AI, Yahoo Finance, CoinGecko, and ElevenLabs. Future development plans include blockchain-based autonomous intelligence, quantum computing integration, advanced neural interfaces, robotics, and immersive technologies. The system is intended for experimental and demonstration purposes and is subject to strict usage terms and privacy policies.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: What classification would be given to an article about a marathon held in Madrid?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual training data in English, German, Hungarian, Spanish, and Slovakian, labeled with media codes. The model incorporates seven additional media codes: State and Local Government Administration (24), Weather and Natural Disaster (26), Fires (27), Sports and Recreation (29), Death Notices (30), Churches and Religion (31), and Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (99). The model is designed for text classification tasks and is intended to categorize input text into one of the defined media codes. It is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and is used with a sentencepiece tokenizer. The model achieves an overall accuracy of 74%, with a macro average precision of 0.76, recall of 0.74, and F1-score of 0.73. The weighted average precision, recall, and F1-score are also 0.76, 0.74, and 0.73, respectively. Per-class performance varies, with some classes achieving high precision and recall, such as Death Notices (precision 0.9565, recall 0.88) and others showing lower performance, such as Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (precision 0.3789, recall 0.72). The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed. The model requires a token for access, and users may need to pass the token parameter when loading the model. In earlier versions of the Transformers package, the use_auth_token parameter may be required instead. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue.

instruction: Please identify the text in this distorted image of a sign due to camera angle.
description: PP-OCRv5_server_det is a text detection model from the PP-OCRv5_det series, developed by the PaddleOCR team. It is designed for high-performance applications and supports the detection of text in diverse scenarios, including handwriting, vertical, rotated, and curved text, across multiple languages such as Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, and Japanese. The model is capable of handling complex layouts, varying text sizes, and challenging backgrounds, making it suitable for practical applications like document analysis, license plate recognition, and scene text detection. The model achieves key accuracy metrics, including 0.803 for Handwritten Chinese, 0.841 for Handwritten English, 0.945 for Printed Chinese, 0.917 for Printed English, 0.815 for Traditional Chinese, 0.676 for Ancient Text, 0.772 for Japanese, 0.797 for General Scenario, 0.671 for Pinyin, 0.8 for Rotation, 0.876 for Distortion, 0.673 for Artistic Text, and an overall average of 0.827. The model can be used as part of an OCR pipeline that includes modules for document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition. It is also part of the PP-StructureV3 pipeline for layout analysis, which includes layout detection, general OCR, document image preprocessing, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition. The model is implemented using the PaddlePaddle deep learning framework and can be run on GPU or CPU devices. It outputs detection results in the form of text polygons and scores, and supports saving results in image, JSON, and Markdown formats. The model is intended for integration into projects and pipelines for real-world text detection and document analysis tasks.

instruction: Can you retrieve an image that represents 'a snowy mountain peak at dawn'?
description: The CLIP model was developed by researchers at OpenAI to study what contributes to robustness in computer vision tasks and to test the ability of models to generalize to arbitrary image classification tasks in a zero-shot manner. It was not developed for general model deployment, and researchers are advised to carefully study its capabilities in relation to the specific context of deployment. The model was developed in January 2021 and uses a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The original implementation had two variants: one using a ResNet image encoder and the other using a Vision Transformer. This repository contains the variant with the Vision Transformer. The model is intended for research use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. Any deployed use case of the model, whether commercial or not, is currently out of scope. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing of the model with a specific, fixed class taxonomy. This is because the model's performance varies significantly with different class taxonomies, and untested deployment may be harmful. Use cases related to surveillance and facial recognition are always out of scope, regardless of model performance, due to the lack of testing norms and checks to ensure fair use. The model has not been purposefully trained or evaluated on any languages other than English, so its use should be limited to English language use cases. The model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet, which tend to skew towards more developed nations and younger, male users. The goal of the dataset was to test robustness and generalizability in computer vision tasks, and the data was gathered in a mostly non-interventionist manner, with crawling limited to websites that had policies against excessively violent and adult images. The dataset is not intended for use as the basis for any commercial or deployed model and will not be released. The model has been evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks across various computer vision datasets, including Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet dataset, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet (ImageNet Overlap), Youtube-BB, and ImageNet-Vid. The model has several limitations, including struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. It also exhibits issues with fairness and bias, with performance and biases depending significantly on class design and category choices. Testing revealed significant disparities with respect to race and gender when classifying images of people into crime-related and non-human animal categories, with disparities shifting based on class construction. Evaluations on the Fairface dataset showed accuracy greater than 96% across all races for gender classification, with 'Middle Eastern' having the highest accuracy (98.4%) and 'White' the lowest (96.5%). CLIP averaged approximately 93% for racial classification and approximately 63% for age classification. These evaluations are used to assess model performance across demographics and surface potential risks, not to endorse such tasks. The approach to testing CLIP also has a limitation: in many cases, linear probes were used to evaluate performance, and there is evidence that linear probes can underestimate model performance.

instruction: What are the most similar questions to the one I posted on a tech forum?
description: ModernBERT-embed-large is an embedding model that incorporates the advancements of ModernBERT to generate embeddings. ModernBERT is a base model trained for Masked Language Modeling and cannot directly perform tasks such as retrieval without further fine-tuning. ModernBERT-embed-large is fine-tuned on both weakly-supervised and supervised datasets and supports Matryoshka Representation Learning dimensions of 256 to reduce memory usage with minimal performance loss. The model achieves an average score of 63.84 across multiple tasks, including classification, clustering, pair classification, reranking, retrieval, and semantic text similarity. It outperforms other models in several metrics, such as 75.03 in classification, 46.04 in clustering, 85.31 in pair classification, 57.64 in reranking, and 54.36 in retrieval. The model is designed to be used with the transformers library version 4.48.0 or higher and requires the addition of specific prefixes to the input text for optimal performance. For example, adding 'search_query: ' to queries and 'search_document: ' to documents is typically sufficient. The model can be used with Sentence Transformers or the Transformers library, and it supports truncation to smaller dimensions, such as 256, to reduce memory usage while maintaining performance. Training involves a multi-stage pipeline, starting with an unsupervised contrastive stage using weakly related text pairs and followed by a fine-tuning stage using higher quality labeled datasets. The model is trained using data from sources such as StackExchange, Quora, Amazon reviews, and news articles. The training data is released in its entirety. The model is built using the Nomic Embed recipe and tools, and the training was conducted on the Orange Business Cloud Avenue infrastructure. If the model, dataset, or training code is used, it is recommended to cite ModernBERT, Nomic Embed, and the specific fine-tuning of ModernBERT-embed-large.

instruction: Show me a video of a city at night with the sound of traffic and distant sirens.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the LTX-2 model. The unsloth/LTX-2-GGUF model uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. The model uses tooling from city96. The LTX-2 model is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a diffusion-based audio-video foundation model that supports English. The model is available in several versions, including a full model, a full model in fp8 quantization, a full model in nvfp4 quantization, a distilled version of the full model, a LoRA version of the distilled model applicable to the full model, an x2 spatial upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher resolution, and an x2 temporal upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher FPS. The model can be accessed via online demos and is available for local execution. A direct use license allows the use of the models, including full, distilled, upscalers, and any derivatives, for specific purposes. The model can be used with ComfyUI through built-in LTXVideo nodes or manually installed as per provided instructions. The PyTorch codebase includes model definitions, pipelines, and training capabilities, and was tested with Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA version 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. The model is supported in the Diffusers library for image-to-video generation. General usage tips include ensuring width and height settings are divisible by 32 and frame count is divisible by 8 + 1. If resolution or frame count is not divisible by these values, input should be padded with -1 and then cropped to the desired dimensions. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify existing societal biases, may fail to generate videos that perfectly match prompts, prompt following is heavily influenced by prompting-style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and when generating audio without speech, the audio may be of lower quality. The base (dev) model is fully trainable, and it is easy to reproduce the LoRAs and IC-LoRAs published with the model by following provided instructions. Training for motion, style, or likeness (sound+appearance) can take less than an hour in many settings.

instruction: I need to find the closest match to this customer's request in our knowledge base.
description: The all-MiniLM-L12-v2 is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space. It is designed for tasks such as clustering and semantic search. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library by installing it via pip and encoding sentences using the SentenceTransformer class. Alternatively, it can be used with the HuggingFace Transformers library by applying a mean pooling operation on the contextualized word embeddings and normalizing the resulting sentence embeddings. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where it was fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs. The model was trained on a TPU v3-8 for 100,000 steps with a batch size of 1024, a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length of 128 tokens. The training data was a combination of multiple datasets, including citation pairs, duplicate question pairs, question-answer pairs, and image captions, among others. The model is intended to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, outputting a vector that captures semantic information for tasks such as information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default.

instruction: I want to analyze the depth structure of this night scene—can you provide a depth map?
description: This ONNX-based DepthPro model generates high-quality depth maps with minimal overhead. Depth values are encoded such that near points are bright and far points are dark, making the output directly usable for stereo and disparity-based applications without additional inversion or preprocessing. The model is optimized for efficient inference on standard hardware. It features a depth-only ONNX export, which significantly reduces model size while preserving full depth quality. It skips field-of-view calibration, outputting raw predicted depth values without post-processing, thereby avoiding normalization artifacts and computational overhead. The output is disparity-ready and compatible with stereo/disparity workflows without conversion. The model uses FP16 weights, optimized for GPU acceleration via DirectML for faster inference. It is configured for batch size 1, as benchmarks show single-image batches deliver optimal throughput, while larger batches are slower. The model uses Opset 21 for broader runtime optimization support and includes aggressive graph optimization to simplify the model graph, reduce computation, and speed up loading. It achieves fast inference with a minimal memory footprint and rapid depth map generation. The input shape is (1, 3, 1536, 1536) in NCHW format, with an input data type of float16 and an input range of [-1.0, 1.0] (normalized RGB). The output shape is (1, 1536, 1536), with an output data type of float16 and an output range of relative depth (higher values indicate closer objects). The model requires approximately 5.2 GB of VRAM, ONNX Runtime version 1.19.0 or higher, and Python version 3.8 or higher. Benchmarks on an AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX using ONNX Runtime v1.23.0 with DirectML show a throughput of 75.7 images per minute and a model size of 1.2 GB. The model is designed without post-processing, which can cause severe artifacts such as crushed contrast and inconsistent results due to extreme outlier depth values and unpredictable normalization effects. The model is licensed under the Apple Machine Learning Research Model License, which restricts use to non-commercial scientific research and academic development. Redistribution is allowed only with the license included. Use of Apple's trademarks, logos, or name to promote derivative models is prohibited. Commercial use, product integration, or service deployment is not allowed.

instruction: How do I delete a moving object from a video and handle the light and reflection effects properly?
description: ROSE, which stands for Remove Objects with Side Effects, is a video inpainting model designed to remove objects and their associated side effects in videos. These side effects include shadows, reflections, light, translucency, and mirror effects. ROSE is implemented as a diffusion transformer-based video inpainting model. The model is trained using a large-scale synthetic dataset generated through a 3D rendering engine, which simulates diverse scenes, objects, shooting angles, and camera trajectories. ROSE introduces a fully automatic pipeline for data preparation and incorporates additional supervision to explicitly predict areas affected by side effects, using differential masks derived from paired videos. To evaluate the model's performance, ROSE-Bench, a new benchmark, was introduced, covering both common scenarios and the five specific side effects. Experimental results show that ROSE achieves superior performance compared to existing video object erasing models and generalizes well to real-world video scenarios. The model is built on the WanTransformer3D architecture, with only the WanTransformer3D part being trained while other components remain frozen. For inference, the model requires a specific directory structure for weights and base models, including the Wan2.1-Fun-1.3B-InP base model. ROSE supports inference on input videos with corresponding mask videos, and it can be run with options such as specifying the number of frames per video and frame size. The model is intended for use in video editing tasks where object removal must account for environmental effects.

instruction: Is it possible to reduce GPU memory usage while still maintaining reconstruction quality?
description: STream3R presents a novel approach to 3D reconstruction by reformulating pointmap prediction as a decoder-only Transformer problem. It introduces a streaming framework that processes image sequences efficiently using causal attention, inspired by advances in modern language modeling. By learning geometric priors from large-scale 3D datasets, STream3R generalizes well to diverse and challenging scenarios, including dynamic scenes where traditional methods often fail. The model reformulates dense 3D reconstruction into a sequential registration task with causal attention. It supports multiple attention mechanisms, including FlashAttention, KV Cache, Causal Attention, Sliding Window Attention, and Full Attention. STream3R is inherently compatible with LLM-style training infrastructure, enabling efficient large-scale pretraining and fine-tuning for various downstream 3D tasks. Extensive experiments show that the method consistently outperforms prior work across both static and dynamic scene benchmarks. The model can be used in real-time or low-latency applications, such as real-time robotics, autonomous navigation, online 3D understanding, and SLAM. The model supports inference modes such as causal, window (with a default window size of 5), and full attention. A KV cache version is available to enable streaming input using StreamSession, which processes sequential input one by one. On the NRGBD dataset, STream3R achieves an accuracy mean of 0.057, an accuracy median of 0.014, a completeness mean of 0.028, a completeness median of 0.013, a non-completeness mean of 0.910, and a non-completeness median of 0.993, outperforming other methods. The peak GPU memory usage (VRAM) and runtime for the model are reported for different numbers of frames. For causal attention, VRAM usage increases with the number of frames, reaching 45.41 GB for 200 frames, while runtime increases to 1.1703 seconds. For sliding window attention, VRAM usage remains constant at 6.53 GB, and runtime stabilizes at around 0.1463 seconds for 200 frames. The model is trained on preprocessed datasets following standard configurations. Evaluation and training code are not yet released, and a metric-scale version is also pending. The project is licensed under an unspecified license, and redistribution and use should follow this license. If you use the code or paper, you are encouraged to cite the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: I'm looking at a new pesticide with the SMILES 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)C(=O)O'. Can you predict its environmental fate parameter A?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict fugacity-based environmental fate parameter A. This property is used in fugacity models for environmental distribution prediction. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on the AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model was trained on the FBA dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Can you show me the smallest file size option for the i1 quant type?
description: This is a vision model, and if applicable, mmproj files will be provided. The model is available in various quantized versions, with different types and sizes. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S (1.5 GB), i1-IQ1_M (1.5 GB), i1-IQ2_XXS (1.6 GB), i1-IQ2_XS (1.7 GB), i1-IQ2_S (1.8 GB), i1-IQ2_M (1.9 GB), i1-Q2_K_S (2.0 GB, very low quality), i1-Q2_K (2.0 GB, IQ3_XXS probably better), i1-IQ3_XXS (2.1 GB, lower quality), i1-IQ3_XS (2.3 GB), i1-IQ3_S (2.3 GB, beats Q3_K*), i1-Q3_K_S (2.3 GB, IQ3_XS probably better), i1-IQ3_M (2.4 GB), i1-Q3_K_M (2.5 GB, IQ3_S probably better), i1-Q3_K_L (2.6 GB, IQ3_M probably better), i1-IQ4_XS (2.7 GB), i1-IQ4_NL (2.8 GB, prefer IQ4_XS), i1-Q4_0 (2.8 GB, fast, low quality), i1-Q4_K_S (2.9 GB, optimal size/speed/quality), i1-Q4_K_M (3.0 GB, fast, recommended), i1-Q4_1 (3.1 GB), i1-Q5_K_S (3.3 GB), i1-Q5_K_M (3.4 GB), and i1-Q6_K (3.8 GB, practically like static Q6_K). The quants are sorted by size, not necessarily by quality, and IQ-quants are often preferable over similar-sized non-IQ quants. For a convenient overview and download list, users are directed to a specific page. If users are unsure how to use GGUF files, they are advised to refer to additional resources for details, including instructions on how to concatenate multi-part files. A graph by ikawrakow is available for comparing some lower-quality quant types, and Artefact2 has provided additional thoughts on the matter. Users are directed to a separate page for frequently asked questions and model request information. The model developer thanks their company for providing server access and workstation upgrades to support this work in their free time, and also thanks another individual for granting access to a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of many more imatrix quants at higher quality.

instruction: Can you help me identify all the objects in this photo and highlight them?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model developed by FAIR for solving promptable visual segmentation in images and videos. It is designed to perform segmentation based on various types of input prompts, including point clicks, bounding boxes, and masks from previous predictions. The model supports both image and video segmentation tasks, enabling users to segment objects in static images or track them across video frames. SAM 2 can be used for automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image, as well as for refining segmentation through multiple points or additional clicks. It allows for batch processing of multiple images or objects within a single image, and supports multi-object segmentation in both images and videos. For video segmentation, SAM 2 can track objects across frames and refine tracking with additional input on later frames. The model is built to handle complex scenarios, including streaming video inference and batched processing of multiple objects. It is implemented using PyTorch and integrates with the Hugging Face Transformers library for ease of use. The model's performance includes the ability to generate multiple mask predictions per input and track objects through hundreds of video frames. Users are advised to refer to the official documentation for detailed usage instructions and examples.

instruction: I want to improve the accuracy of my visual document search by incorporating text-based relevance scores. How can I achieve that?
description: The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B is a multimodal reranking model designed for cross-modal understanding and information retrieval. It is part of the Qwen3-VL model series, which includes the Qwen3-VL-Embedding and Qwen3-VL-Reranker models. These models are built upon the Qwen3-VL foundation model and are specifically engineered to handle diverse input modalities, including text, images, screenshots, and videos, as well as combinations of these modalities. The Embedding model generates high-dimensional vectors for tasks like retrieval and clustering, while the Reranker model refines these results by taking a (query, document) pair as input and outputting a precise relevance score. The two models are typically used together in a two-stage pipeline, where the Embedding model performs initial recall and the Reranker model refines the results, significantly improving retrieval accuracy. The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B supports over 30 languages and is capable of handling arbitrary multimodal combinations. It has 2 billion parameters and a context length of 32,000 tokens. The model supports quantization post-processing, custom dimensions for the final embedding (MRL), and instruction-aware customization for specific tasks. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B outperforms the base embedding model and baseline rerankers on various retrieval tasks, with performance metrics including an average score of 75.1 on the MMEB-v2 retrieval benchmark, 73.8 for image retrieval, 52.1 for video retrieval, 83.4 for visual document retrieval, and 70.0 on the MMTEB retrieval benchmark. The model is designed for practical use in real-world applications, offering flexibility in vector dimensions, strong performance with quantized embeddings, and the ability to integrate into existing pipelines. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for specific tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance in multilingual contexts.

instruction: Can you help me find the most relevant image for a research paper based on a given text description?
description: The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B is a multimodal reranking model designed for cross-modal understanding and information retrieval. instruction: Which species are discussed in this study on parasitic infections?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting species and organism names from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the LINNAEUS dataset, a specialized biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on species name identification and organism recognition in scientific literature. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It supports the classification of two entity types: B-SPECIES and I-SPECIES. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.89, recall of 0.81, and accuracy of 0.99. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: How would you animate this photo into a brief, high-quality video with smooth transitions?
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It consistently outperforms existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The repository includes the I2V-14B model, which is capable of generating 480P videos with fast generation and excellent quality. Wan2.1 is built on the diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating a novel 3D causal VAE architecture, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually. The model is trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Manual evaluations indicate that Wan2.1 outperforms both closed-source and open-source models. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards.

instruction: What objects appear in this clip, and how are they interacting?
description: Kwai Keye-VL is an 8-billion-parameter multimodal foundation model designed for leading-edge performance in short-video understanding while maintaining robust general-purpose vision-language abilities. It is engineered to bridge the gap between static image comprehension and dynamic, information-dense short-form videos, which are a dominant medium in today's digital landscape. The model is built on a massive, high-quality dataset exceeding 600 billion tokens with a strong emphasis on video, and an innovative training recipe. This recipe includes a four-stage pre-training process for solid vision-language alignment, followed by a meticulous two-phase post-training process. The first post-training stage enhances foundational capabilities like instruction following, while the second phase focuses on stimulating advanced reasoning. A key innovation in the second phase is the five-mode ``cold-start'' data mixture, which includes ``thinking'', ``non-thinking'', ``auto-think'', ``think with image'', and high-quality video data. This mixture teaches the model to decide when and how to reason. Subsequent reinforcement learning (RL) and alignment steps further enhance these reasoning capabilities and correct abnormal model behaviors, such as repetitive outputs. Kwai Keye-VL achieves state-of-the-art results on public video benchmarks and remains highly competitive on general image-based tasks. Additionally, it shows a significant advantage on the newly developed KC-MMBench, a benchmark tailored for real-world short-video scenarios. The model architecture is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and incorporates a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer then maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information, establishing a one-to-one correspondence between position encoding and absolute time to ensure precise perception of temporal changes in video information. The pre-training pipeline features a four-stage progressive strategy: Image-Text Matching, ViT-LLM Alignment, Multi-task Pre-training, and Annealing with model merging. The pre-training data is diverse, including image-text pairs, videos, pure text, and tasks such as fine-grained description, OCR, Q&A, and localization. The data is filtered using CLIP scores and VLM discriminators, and MinHASH is used for deduplication to prevent data leakage. High-quality internal datasets are specifically constructed, especially for detailed captions and Chinese OCR, to compensate for the shortcomings of open-source data. The training process includes four stages: Visual Pre-training, Cross-Modal Alignment, Multi-Task Pre-training, and Annealing Training. The post-training phase is designed into two phases with five stages, aiming to comprehensively enhance the model's performance, especially its reasoning ability in complex tasks. The first phase focuses on the model's basic performance and stability in non-reasoning scenarios, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO). The second phase is the highlight and major contribution of the training process, introducing a mix-mode Chain of Thought (CoT) and multi-thinking mode reinforcement learning (RL) mechanisms to significantly enhance the model's multimodal perception, reasoning, and think-with-image capabilities. The model is evaluated on a comprehensive suite of authoritative public video benchmarks, including Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU, where it significantly surpasses other top-tier models of a comparable size. It also demonstrates strong performance in evaluation sets that require complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: Could you analyze the molecular fingerprint and predict its environmental impact?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict teratogenic bioaccumulation potential, a property that may relate to developmental toxicity or bioaccumulation scoring. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the TBP dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What cities or countries are mentioned in this story?
description: The bert-base-NER is a fine-tuned BERT model designed for Named Entity Recognition (NER) and achieves state-of-the-art performance for the NER task. It is trained to recognize four types of entities: location (LOC), organizations (ORG), person (PER), and Miscellaneous (MISC). Specifically, this model is a bert-base-cased model that was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset. A larger BERT-large model fine-tuned on the same dataset is also available. The model can be used with the Transformers pipeline for NER. It is limited by its training dataset of entity-annotated news articles from a specific span of time, which may not generalize well for all use cases in different domains. Additionally, the model occasionally tags subword tokens as entities, and post-processing of results may be necessary to handle those cases. The model was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset, which distinguishes between the beginning and continuation of an entity to allow the model to output where the second entity begins. Each token is classified as one of the following classes: O (Outside of a named entity), B-MISC (Beginning of a miscellaneous entity right after another miscellaneous entity), I-MISC (Miscellaneous entity), B-PER (Beginning of a person’s name right after another person’s name), I-PER (Person’s name), B-ORG (Beginning of an organization right after another organization), I-ORG (Organization), B-LOC (Beginning of a location right after another location), and I-LOC (Location). The training dataset was derived from the Reuters corpus, consisting of Reuters news stories. The dataset includes 7140 training examples for LOC, 3438 for MISC, 6321 for ORG, and 6600 for PER. The development set includes 1837 LOC, 922 MISC, 1341 ORG, and 1842 PER examples. The test set includes 1668 LOC, 702 MISC, 1661 ORG, and 1617 PER examples. The training dataset consists of 946 articles, 14,987 sentences, and 203,621 tokens. The development dataset includes 216 articles, 3,466 sentences, and 51,362 tokens. The test dataset includes 231 articles, 3,684 sentences, and 46,435 tokens. The model was trained on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU with recommended hyperparameters from the source that trained and evaluated the model on the CoNLL-2003 NER task. The model achieved an f1 score of 95.1 on the development set and 91.3 on the test set. The precision was 95.0 on the development set and 90.7 on the test set. The recall was 95.3 on the development set and 91.9 on the test set. The test metrics are slightly lower than the official Google BERT results, which encoded document context and experimented with CRF.

instruction: Can you summarize the visual content of this wildlife image in a structured way?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a large patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`) but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU) reported for different model variants. The model is also evaluated on satellite datasets such as SAT-493M for classification and segmentation tasks, with performance metrics reported for models like DINOv3 ViT-L/16 and DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, as well as the papers on Vision Transformers and PyTorch Image Models.

instruction: Describe the key components of this picture of a mountain landscape.
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model, similar to BERT, that was pre-trained on a large collection of images in a supervised manner using the ImageNet-21k dataset, which contains 14 million images across 21,843 classes. The model processes images at a resolution of 224x224 pixels by dividing them into fixed-size patches of 16x16 pixels, which are linearly embedded. A [CLS] token is added to the beginning of the sequence for classification tasks, and absolute position embeddings are included before the sequence is fed into the Transformer encoder layers. The model does not include any fine-tuned heads, as these were set to zero by the researchers, but it does retain the pre-trained pooler, which can be used for downstream tasks such as image classification. Through pre-training, the model learns an internal representation of images that can be used to extract features for downstream tasks, such as training a standard classifier by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder. The [CLS] token's last hidden state is typically used as a representation of the entire image. The model was pre-trained on TPUv3 hardware using 8 cores, with a batch size of 4096 and a learning rate warmup of 10,000 steps. During training, images were resized and normalized to 224x224 resolution with mean (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) and standard deviation (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) across the RGB channels. Evaluation results on image classification benchmarks are detailed in the original paper, with the best fine-tuning results achieved at a higher resolution of 384x384. Larger model sizes are associated with better performance. The model can be used for image classification, and fine-tuned versions for specific tasks are available. The model was introduced in a paper by Dosovitskiy et al. and the weights were converted from JAX to PyTorch by Ross Wightman. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide one.

instruction: How similar are the images of a 'red sports car' and a 'yellow sports car' in terms of visual features?
description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE uses a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks, allowing large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer effectively with alignment tuning. PE Core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine.
