PE Core comes in three sizes: B, L, and G, with G being the main checkpoint and B and L models distilled from it. The G/14 variant has 1.88 billion parameters for the vision tower, a width of 1536, a depth of 50, an MLP size of 8960, 16 attention heads, a CLIP dimension of 1280, and a resolution of 448px. The text tower for G/14 has 0.47 billion parameters, a width of 1280, a depth of 24, an MLP size of 5120, 20 attention heads, and a CLIP dimension of 1280, with a context length of 72 tokens. All PE Core models use an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower, and the B and L models include an additional class token for global aggregation. PE Core achieves strong results on zero-shot image and video classification and retrieval tasks, with the G/14 variant achieving 85.4% accuracy on ImageNet-1k, 80.2% on ImageNet-v2, 92.6% on ImageNet-A, 88.2% on ObjectNet, 58.1% on COCO-T2I, 76.9% on Kinetics-400, and 51.2% on VTT-T2I. PE Core performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a vision-language architecture and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model can be used for encoding images and text, computing similarity scores, and generating label probabilities. The model is available through the OpenCLIP and original GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated research paper.

instruction: Produce a clip of a waterfall with the sound of rushing water and birdsong.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the LTX-2 model. The unsloth/LTX-2-GGUF model uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. The model uses tooling from city96. The LTX-2 model is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a diffusion-based audio-video foundation model that supports English. The model is available in several versions, including a full model, a full model in fp8 quantization, a full model in nvfp4 quantization, a distilled version of the full model, a LoRA version of the distilled model applicable to the full model, an x2 spatial upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher resolution, and an x2 temporal upscaler for the ltx-2 latents used in multi-stage pipelines for higher FPS. The model can be accessed via online demos and is available for local execution. A direct use license allows the use of the models, including full, distilled, upscalers, and any derivatives, for specific purposes. The model can be used with ComfyUI through built-in LTXVideo nodes or manually installed as per provided instructions. The PyTorch codebase includes model definitions, pipelines, and training capabilities, and was tested with Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA version 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. The model is supported in the Diffusers library for image-to-video generation. General usage tips include ensuring width and height settings are divisible by 32 and frame count is divisible by 8 + 1. If resolution or frame count is not divisible by these values, input should be padded with -1 and then cropped to the desired dimensions. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify existing societal biases, may fail to generate videos that perfectly match prompts, prompt following is heavily influenced by prompting-style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and when generating audio without speech, the audio may be of lower quality. The base (dev) model is fully trainable, and it is easy to reproduce the LoRAs and IC-LoRAs published with the model by following provided instructions. Training for motion, style, or likeness (sound+appearance) can take less than an hour in many settings.

instruction: Create an audio snippet with background music and a voiceover introducing a new product launch.
description: Higgs Audio v2 is a powerful audio foundation model pretrained on over 10 million hours of audio data and a diverse set of text data. It is designed for expressive audio generation and excels in this area despite having no post-training or fine-tuning. The model demonstrates strong performance in expressive audio generation due to its deep language and acoustic understanding. On a specific evaluation, it achieves win rates of 75.7% and 55.7% over 'gpt-4o-mini-tts' on the 'Emotions' and 'Questions' categories, respectively. It also obtains state-of-the-art performance on traditional TTS benchmarks like Seed-TTS Eval and Emotional Speech Dataset (ESD). The model demonstrates capabilities such as automatic prosody adaptation during narration, zero-shot generation of natural multi-speaker dialogues in multiple languages, melodic humming with the cloned voice, and simultaneous generation of speech and background music. Higgs Audio v2 adopts a 'generation variant' architecture. Its strong performance is driven by three key technical innovations: an automated annotation pipeline that leverages multiple ASR models, sound event classification models, and an in-house audio understanding model, which was finetuned on top of Higgs Audio v1 Understanding; a unified audio tokenizer trained from scratch that captures both semantic and acoustic features; and the DualFFN architecture, which enhances the LLM’s ability to model audio tokens with minimal computational overhead. The audio tokenizer runs at 25 frames per second while maintaining or improving audio quality compared to tokenizers with twice the bitrate. It is the first to train on 24 kHz data covering speech, music, and sound events in one unified system. It uses a simple non-diffusion encoder/decoder for fast, batch inference and achieves state-of-the-art performance in semantic and acoustic evaluations. The DualFFN architecture acts as an audio-specific expert, boosting the LLM's performance with minimal computational overhead. The implementation preserves 91% of the original LLM’s training speed with the inclusion of DualFFN, which has 2.2B parameters. The total number of parameters for Higgs Audio v2 is 3.6B (LLM) + 2.2B (Audio Dual FFN), and it has the same training / inference FLOPs as Llama-3.2-3B. Ablation studies show that the model equipped with DualFFN consistently outperforms its counterpart in terms of word error rate (WER) and speaker similarity. Higgs Audio v2 is evaluated on four benchmarks: Seed-TTS Eval, ESD, EmergentTTS-Eval, and a custom Multi-speaker Eval. On Seed-TTS Eval and ESD, it achieves a WER of 2.44 and a SIM of 67.70 on SeedTTS-Eval, and a WER of 1.78 and a SIM (emo2vec) of 86.13 on ESD. On the EmergentTTS-Eval, it achieves win rates of 75.71% and 55.71% on the 'Emotions' and 'Questions' categories, respectively. On the Multi-speaker Eval, it achieves a WER of 18.88 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 51.95 on the 'two-speaker-conversation' subset, a WER of 11.89 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 67.92 on the 'small talk (ref)' subset, and a WER of 14.65 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 55.28 on the 'small talk (no ref)' subset. The model is open-sourced and can be used for tasks such as text-to-speech generation, multi-speaker dialogue generation, and audio generation with specific emotional or prosodic characteristics. It is available through an open-source repository and can be installed and run using provided code snippets. The model is licensed under a specific license, which can be found in the repository.

instruction: What does the speaker say in this Russian audio file?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the wav2vec2-large-xlsr-53-russian model on the Common Voice 17.0 dataset for the Russian language. It is designed for automatic speech recognition tasks, specifically for converting audio input into transcribed text. The model was trained using the CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) approach and is based on the wav2vec2 architecture. It was trained for 5 epochs with a learning rate of 0.0001, a training batch size of 8, and an evaluation batch size of 4. The training used the ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08, and a linear learning rate scheduler. Mixed precision training with Native AMP was enabled. The model was evaluated on the Common Voice 17.0 test set, achieving a final word error rate (WER) of 22.3989, a character error rate (CER) of 4.8036, and a sentence error rate (SER) of 75.4264. During training, the model's performance was monitored at regular intervals, with validation loss and error rates recorded at each step. The training process involved preprocessing audio data to a sampling rate of 16000 Hz, removing non-essential metadata columns, and using a custom data collator for padding and batching. The model was implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers library, PyTorch 2.6.0, and related dependencies including Datasets 3.6.0 and Tokenizers 0.21.1. The model is intended for speech recognition tasks in Russian but no specific limitations or caveats are explicitly stated in the provided text.

instruction: How likely is the compound with SMILES C1CCNCC1 to degrade in natural conditions?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict biodegradability potential, which classifies whether a compound is readily biodegradable in the environment. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP, and it performs a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an architecture based on AttentiveFP with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 64, two layers, two timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.2, a learning rate of 0.001, a weight decay of 1e-05, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the tbiodeg dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What settings should I consider when aiming for a balance between video clarity and dynamism?
description: The Wan2.2-Fun-Reward-LoRAs are pre-trained models in the form of LoRAs (Low-Rank Adaptations) designed to enhance the alignment of generated videos with human preferences through the use of reward backpropagation techniques. These LoRAs can be used as plug-ins to improve the performance of the corresponding base model or to train custom reward LoRAs. The models include Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-MPS, and Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-MPS. Each LoRA is trained with a batch size of 8 for varying numbers of steps: 5,000 steps for the high-noise HPS2.1 and high-noise MPS models, 2,700 steps for the low-noise HPS2.1 model, and 4,500 steps for the low-noise MPS model. The HPS2.1 and MPS reward LoRAs have a rank of 128 and a network alpha of 64. It is noted that the MPS reward LoRA for the low-noise model converges significantly more slowly and may not deliver satisfactory results, so the HPSv2.1 reward LoRA is recommended for the low-noise model. The models are intended to be used in conjunction with the base model Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP. During training, the reward continues to increase after a certain point, but the quality of the generated videos does not improve further, as the model may learn to exploit shortcuts such as adding artifacts in the background. Additionally, there is a lack of suitable preference models for video generation, and using image preference models leads to a decrease in the dynamism of generated videos, although this can be partially mitigated by computing the reward using only the first frame of the decoded video.

instruction: I want to translate this science article from French to Arabic for a wider audience.
description: Seed-X is a series of open-source multilingual translation language models, including an instruction model, a reinforcement learning model, and a reward model. It is designed to push the boundaries of translation capabilities within a 7 billion parameter scale. Seed-X is intended as an accessible, off-the-shelf tool to support the community in advancing translation research and applications. It exhibits state-of-the-art translation capabilities, on par with or outperforming ultra-large models like Gemini-2.5, Claude-3.5, and GPT-4, as validated by human evaluations and automatic metrics. With a compact 7B parameter count and mistral architecture, Seed-X offers outstanding translation performance in a lightweight and efficient package, ideal for deployment and inference. It excels on a highly challenging translation test set spanning diverse domains, including the internet, science and technology, office dialogues, e-commerce, biomedicine, finance, law, literature, and entertainment. The Seed-X-PPO model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It supports multilingual translation among 28 languages, including Arabic, Czech, Danish, German, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Norwegian Bokmal, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, and Chinese. The model is specialized in multilingual translation and is not expected to support other tasks. The language tags at the end of the prompt are necessary for translation, as used in PPO training. The model does not have a chat template, so users should avoid prompting it in a multi-round conversation format. The model is evaluated on a diverse set of translation benchmarks, including FLORES-200, WMT-25, and a publicly released benchmark, accompanied by human evaluations. The model is licensed under OpenMDW. Users are recommended to use the Seed-X-PPO model, as its translation performance is superior to Seed-X-Instruct. Unofficial quantized versions for local deployment are not recommended, as the team plans to release an official quantized model and develop a demo on Hugging Face Space.

instruction: Is there an audio compression method that preserves speech clarity while minimizing file size?
description: NeuCodec is a lightweight neural audio codec designed for encoding speech at a bit-rate of 0.8 kbps. It is intended for use in training high-quality text-to-speech models and is optimized for real-world applications, including commercial use. The model uses Finite Scalar Quantisation (FSQ) to produce a single codebook, which is ideal for downstream modeling with Speech Language Models. It operates by taking 16kHz audio input and upscaling it to 24kHz using an upsampling decoder. The codec processes audio at 50 tokens per second with 16 bits per token, resulting in the 0.8kbps bit-rate. The FSQ encoding scheme provides bit-level error resistance, making it suitable for use in unreliable or noisy communication channels. NeuCodec was trained on a variety of datasets, including Emilia-YODAS, MLS, LibriTTS, Fleurs, CommonVoice, HUI, and an additional proprietary set. Publicly available data was covered by either the CC-BY-4.0 or CC0 license. The model is released under the Apache-2.0 license and is available for use in tools or products. Pre-encoded datasets are provided, including a version of Emilia-YODAS compressed from 1.7TB to 41GB using NeuCodec, which significantly reduces the compute requirements for training. The model is developed by Neuphonic and is available for installation via PyPI.

instruction: Is it possible to process multiple images at once for segmentation?
description: EdgeTAM is an on-device executable variant of the SAM 2 model designed for promptable segmentation and tracking in videos. It runs 22× faster than SAM 2 and achieves 16 FPS on iPhone 15 Pro Max without quantization. EdgeTAM supports automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image. It can be used for basic image segmentation through single point clicks, multiple points for refinement, and bounding box inputs. The model allows for segmentation of multiple objects simultaneously. It supports batch inference for multiple images and multiple objects per image, including complex batch scenarios with multiple points per object. EdgeTAM can use masks from previous predictions as input to refine segmentation. A key strength of EdgeTAM is its ability to track objects across video frames. It supports basic video tracking, multi-object video tracking, and refining video segmentation through additional clicks on any frame. For real-time applications, EdgeTAM supports streaming video inference, processing video frames as they arrive. It also supports video batch processing for multiple objects, allowing multiple objects to be tracked simultaneously in video by adding them all at once.

instruction: Please find and mark the disease-related entities in this excerpt from a clinical document.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for disease entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC5CDR_DISEASE dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 5,818 annotated disease entities. The model supports the identification and classification of two entity types: B-DISEASE and I-DISEASE. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem and is validated on clinical benchmarks for production-ready reliability. Performance metrics for the model include an F1 score of 0.87, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.88, and accuracy of 0.97. The model is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: What does this dermatological image suggest about the skin condition?
description: This repository contains a drop-in, Hugging Face–compatible checkpoint converted from an original model. It can be loaded using the same code as the original model, without requiring any additional conversion steps. The model is based on an architecture that includes an embedding layer (embed_tokens) and an output layer (lm_head), both of which may change in shape when the vocabulary size is expanded. However, the attention mechanism remains unaffected, as it operates on the hidden state dimensions, which stay consistent. Expanding the vocabulary increases the number of token embeddings but does not alter the attention calculation logic. The only change in the model is the addition of new token embeddings, which are initialized and used during the first forward pass. The output layer (lm_head) changes in shape but not in functional logic. The model supports tasks involving both image and text inputs, such as answering questions about medical images. It can be loaded using the LlavaForConditionalGeneration class from the transformers library and an AutoProcessor for input processing. Example inference involves constructing a prompt with an image and a text question, tokenizing the input, and generating a response using the model's generate method. The model is compatible with multi-GPU setups and uses the bfloat16 data type for efficient computation. It also supports the flash_attention_2 implementation for attention calculations.

instruction: Can you detect and extract rotated tables from this document?
description: MinerU2.5 is a 1.2B-parameter vision-language model for document parsing that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with high computational efficiency. It uses a two-stage parsing strategy: first performing efficient global layout analysis on downsampled images, then conducting fine-grained content recognition on native-resolution crops for text, formulas, and tables. The model is supported by a large-scale, diverse data engine for pretraining and fine-tuning, and it consistently outperforms both general-purpose and domain-specific models across multiple benchmarks while maintaining low computational overhead. MinerU2.5 provides comprehensive and granular layout analysis, preserving non-body elements like headers, footers, and page numbers to ensure full content integrity, and it employs a refined and standardized labeling schema for a clearer, more structured representation of elements such as lists, references, and code blocks. It delivers high-quality parsing of complex, lengthy mathematical formulae and accurately recognizes mixed-language (Chinese-English) equations. The model also demonstrates enhanced robustness in table parsing, handling challenging cases such as rotated tables, borderless tables, and tables with partial borders. For inference, MinerU2.5 can be used with the `mineru-vl-utils` Python package, which simplifies the process of sending requests and handling responses. The `vllm-async-engine` is recommended for inference, as it can achieve a concurrent inference speed of 2.12 fps on one A100. The model can be installed using pip with either the `transformers` or `vllm` backend. MinerU2.5 is designed to extract document content in two steps, using a client interface that supports both synchronous and asynchronous operations. The model is based on a vision-language architecture and is intended for high-resolution document parsing tasks. The model's performance has been evaluated on the OmniDocBench benchmark across different elements and document types. The model is open-source and available for use under the terms of the provided citation. The authors acknowledge contributions from various individuals and organizations for providing valuable code and models. If the model is used in research, the authors request that it be cited using the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: Could you extract segmentation masks and joint positions from this image simultaneously?
description: The BBox-Mask-Pose (BMP) method integrates detection, pose estimation, and segmentation into a self-improving loop by conditioning these tasks on each other. This approach enhances all three tasks simultaneously. Using segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes improves performance in crowded scenarios, making top-down methods competitive with bottom-up approaches. The method includes MaskPose, a pose estimation model conditioned by segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes, which boosts performance in dense scenes without adding parameters. The BBox-MaskPose (BMP) method links bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and poses to simultaneously address multi-body detection, segmentation, and pose estimation. A fine-tuned RTMDet is adapted for iterative detection, ignoring 'holes'. The system supports multi-dataset training of ViTPose, previously implemented in the official ViTPose repository but absent in MMPose. The ViTPose-b multi-dataset model uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. The model was trained on multiple datasets and performs better in multi-body and crowded scenarios than COCO-trained ViTPose. The MaskPose-b model also uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) and estimated instance segmentation as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It uses training data including SAM-estimated instance masks. Compared to ViTPose, MaskPose takes instance segmentation as input and is better at distinguishing instances in multi-body scenes without computational overhead. The fine-tuned RTMDet-l model uses a CSPNeXt-P5 backbone, CSPNeXtPAFPN neck, and RTMDetInsSepBN head. It takes RGB images as input and outputs detected instances, including bounding boxes, instance masks, and class for each. It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMDetection framework. It was trained for 10 epochs with a batch size of 16 and a learning rate of 2e-2 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It was trained on data with randomly masked-out instances and is designed for iterative detection, especially effective in multi-body scenes where background would not be detected otherwise.

instruction: What section of this research paper addresses the limitations of current AI models?
description: TomoroAI/tomoro-colqwen3-embed-8b is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model that maps text queries, visual documents (images, PDFs), or short videos into aligned multi-vector embeddings. It is built by merging components with a full vision stack, inheriting robust text retrieval capabilities. The model has been fine-tuned on a curated mixture of data sources and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across ViDoRe V1-V3 in both English and multilingual settings. It offers a significantly reduced embedding footprint compared to other full-dimension Colpali model alternatives. The model is based on a Qwen3-VL 8B architecture with an encoder-only variant and a 320-dimension projection head. It uses a ColPali-style late interaction methodology with MaxSim scoring. It supports up to 1,280 visual tokens per page or 5,120 visual tokens per video, with text prompts constrained by the base context window of 32,000 tokens. The model is capable of handling complex layouts, such as chart-rich PDFs and domain-specific documents, and supports end-to-end retrieval of unseen multimodal documents without requiring OCR or an intermediate vision LLM for summaries. It demonstrates strong performance on non-English document inputs and inherits strong text retrieval performance from the Qwen3-Embedding-8B model. However, it is not fine-tuned on large-scale video retrieval datasets, and its video support is based on preliminary findings. The model is larger than single-vector baselines despite having a smaller token dimension. It is not currently fine-tuned with diverse system instructions, and improvements are planned using more synthetic datasets. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, with upstream Qwen checkpoints retaining their community licenses. Training data includes ViDoRe/MTEB corpora and synthetic VisRAG assets. The model is applied to enhance Tomoro’s customized multimodal agentic RAG pipelines, enabling autonomous agents to parse, reason over, and retrieve from large-scale enterprise internal documentation. It supports Tomoro AI's mission to deliver high-quality enterprise multimodal solutions across high-stakes industries.

instruction: Could you generate an audio version of this message with a more formal and polite tone?
description: Dia is a 1.6B parameter text-to-speech model developed by Nari Labs. It directly generates highly realistic dialogue from a transcript and allows conditioning the output on audio, enabling control over emotion and tone. The model can also produce nonverbal communications such as laughter, coughing, and clearing the throat. To support research, the model provides access to pretrained checkpoints and inference code. The model weights are hosted on a platform, and currently, the model only supports English generation. A comparison of the model to other models is also provided. The model was not fine-tuned on a specific voice, so different voices are generated with each run. Speaker consistency can be maintained by adding an audio prompt or fixing the seed. The model supports dialogue generation using [S1] and [S2] tags, and it can recognize non-verbal tags like (laughs), (clears throat), (sighs), (gasps), (coughs), (singing), (sings), (mumbles), (beep), (groans), (sniffs), (claps), (screams), (inhales), (exhales), (applause), (burps), (humming), (sneezes), (chuckle), and (whistles). Voice cloning is supported, and more information is available. The model can be used as a Python library or as part of the transformers library. It has been tested on GPUs with PyTorch 2.0+ and CUDA 12.6, and CPU support is planned. On enterprise GPUs, the model can generate audio in real-time, while on older GPUs, inference is slower. On an A4000 GPU, the model generates approximately 40 tokens per second, with 86 tokens corresponding to one second of audio. The full version requires around 10GB of VRAM, and a quantized version is planned for the future. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. It is intended for research and educational use, and the following uses are strictly forbidden: identity misuse, deceptive content, and illegal or malicious use. The developers are not responsible for any misuse and oppose unethical usage. Future work includes Docker support, inference speed optimization, and memory-efficient quantization. The project is maintained by a small team and welcomes contributions. The developers acknowledge the support of computation resources, inspiration from other works, and the HuggingFace ZeroGPU Grant. The name 'Nari' is a Korean word for lily, and Jason Y. is thanked for assistance with data filtering.

instruction: Can you process this image of a table and return the data in a clean HTML table format?
description: Chandra is an OCR model that outputs markdown, HTML, and JSON. It is highly accurate at extracting text from images and PDFs, while preserving layout information. Chandra supports converting documents to markdown, HTML, or JSON with detailed layout information. It has good handwriting support and can reconstruct forms accurately, including checkboxes. It also provides good support for tables, math, and complex layouts. The model extracts images and diagrams, including captions and structured data, and supports 40+ languages. Chandra has been evaluated using the olmocr benchmark, which is considered the most reliable current OCR benchmark in testing. In the benchmark, Chandra v0.1.0 achieved an overall score of 83.1 ± 0.9, with scores of 82.2 on ArXiv, 80.3 on Old Scans Math, 88.0 on Tables, 50.4 on Old Scans, 90.8 on Headers and Footers, 81.2 on Multi column, and 92.3 on Long tiny text. It outperformed several other models in multiple categories. Chandra can be used via a command-line interface, an interactive streamlit app, or through code using the HuggingFace or VLLM methods. The model is available for installation via pip and can be accessed through a hosted API or a free playground.

instruction: What is the most likely genre of the audio based on the sound?
description: MiDashengLM is an efficient audio-language model that achieves holistic audio understanding through caption-based alignment. It is designed to perform a range of audio understanding tasks, including audio captioning, audio and paralinguistic classification, speech recognition, and question answering. The model is optimized for high inference efficiency, delivering a 3.2× throughput speedup and supporting batch sizes up to 512. MiDashengLM is based on a 7B parameter architecture and is available in both FP32 and BF16 versions, with the BF16 version recommended for most applications to achieve optimal performance and efficiency. The model has been evaluated on multiple audio understanding benchmarks and has demonstrated state-of-the-art performance. For example, on the MusicCaps dataset, it achieves an FENSE score of 59.71, outperforming Qwen2.5-Omni-7B and Kimi-Audio-Instruct. On the VoxCeleb1 dataset, it achieves an accuracy of 92.36, significantly higher than the other models tested. In speech recognition tasks, it achieves a word error rate of 3.7 on the LibriSpeech test-clean dataset and 6.2 on the test-other dataset. The model is available under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in both research and business applications. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their work.

instruction: Generate a quick audio version of this message so I can review it on the go.
description: Soprano is an ultra-lightweight, on-device text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for expressive, high-fidelity speech synthesis at unprecedented speed. It supports up to 2000x real-time generation on GPU and 20x real-time on CPU. The model enables lossless streaming and is designed for use in various environments, including WebUI, CLI, Python scripts, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Soprano allows for basic inference, batched inference, and streaming inference with customizable sampling parameters such as temperature, top_p, and repetition_penalty. It can be configured with different cache sizes and decoder batch sizes to optimize inference speed, though this may increase memory usage. The model is currently English-only and does not support voice cloning. It was trained on 1,000 hours of audio, which is approximately 100 times less than other TTS models, and as a result, mispronunciation of uncommon words may occur. This limitation is expected to improve with additional training data. Users are advised that Soprano is now outdated and should use a newer version instead. On Windows with CUDA, users must reinstall PyTorch explicitly with the correct CUDA wheel after installing Soprano to ensure CUDA support. If Soprano produces unsatisfactory results, users can regenerate the output for a new generation or adjust sampling settings for more varied results. Improper grammar, such as the lack of contractions or multiple spaces, should be avoided for optimal performance.

instruction: Is there a way to improve the OCR results of this image by adjusting the layout?
description: UVDoc is a model designed to perform geometric transformations on images to correct document distortion, inclination, and perspective deformation, thereby improving the accuracy of subsequent text recognition. The model achieved a Character Error Rate (CER) of 0.179 on the DocuNet benchmark dataset. UVDoc is intended to be used as part of the TextImageUnwarping module within the PaddleOCR framework. It can be integrated into projects for document image correction and is compatible with PaddlePaddle versions 3.0.0 for CUDA 11.8, CUDA 12.6, and CPU environments. The model supports inference through command-line tools and Python code, allowing users to process images and save results in various formats, including images and JSON. UVDoc can also be used in conjunction with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline, which includes layout detection, OCR, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The pipeline can be enabled with the use_doc_unwarping parameter to enhance document processing in real-world scenarios.

instruction: Can you detect any outliers in the customer spending data and explain their possible impact?
description: JT-DA-8B is a specialized large language model designed for complex table reasoning tasks across diverse real-world scenarios. It is built upon the open-sourced JT-Coder-8B model, an 8B-parameter decoder-only foundation model trained from scratch. The model architecture follows a mainstream Transformer decoder design, including RMS layer normalization, RoPE, and GQA mechanism. The model is trained on a comprehensive and diverse training corpus, which includes 29 public table QA datasets, over 300 domain-specific non-QA tables, and 1 million rule-based table reasoning QA pairs. A structured pipeline is used to generate realistic multi-step analytical tasks involving reasoning patterns like correlation, anomaly detection, and hypothesis testing. The training data is extensively cleaned and processed, including short answer filtering, repetitive thinking filtering, low information density filtering, and LLM-based scoring to ensure high-quality, table-centric data. The training data composition includes six core capabilities with 34 subtasks, covering natural language understanding, table understanding, table basic operation, table computational operation, data analysis, and advanced data analysis. The model is trained using a distributed parallel strategy on 96 NPUs, with a high-bandwidth, low-latency communication network and shared high-performance storage. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is conducted to enhance the model's capability in structured table reasoning, using a teacher-forcing approach to minimize the cross-entropy loss between predicted and ground-truth tokens. A four-stage table reasoning workflow is proposed, including table preprocessing, table sensing, tool-integrated reasoning, and prompt engineering, to improve model interpretability and execution accuracy. The model is evaluated on the TReB benchmark, which covers six core capabilities and 26 subtasks, using three inference modes: Textual Chain-of-Thought (TCoT), Program-of-Thought (PoT), and Interleaved Chain-of-Thought (ICoT). The evaluation results demonstrate that the model excels among models of the same size, showcasing outstanding performance in table understanding, querying, computation, and data analysis tasks. The model is capable of performing multi-step reasoning, handling inconsistent and noisy data types, and generating executable code for data analysis. It supports tasks such as table summarization, descriptive analysis, statistical metric calculation, variable relationship modeling, and result interpretation. The model also supports data visualization, including the generation of bar charts to illustrate data patterns. The model is distributed under a license and includes a disclaimer that it may still produce content that is inaccurate, biased, or inappropriate, and users are advised to exercise discretion when interpreting the model’s outputs and assume full responsibility for any outcomes arising from its use.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Could you help me find the paragraph that introduces the concept of transfer learning?
description: ColQwen2 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy derived from Vision Language Models (VLMs), designed to efficiently index documents using their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images, extending the capabilities of VLMs. This version is an untrained base model that ensures deterministic initialization of the projection layer. The model accepts dynamic image resolutions without resizing, preserving the aspect ratio, with a maximum resolution that allows up to 768 image patches. Larger numbers of image patches improve performance but increase memory requirements. It was trained using colpali-engine version 0.3.1 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the associated paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with pseudo-questions generated by a VLM (Claude-3 Sonnet). The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to avoid contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. All models are trained for one epoch using bfloat16 format, low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the language model's transformer layers and the final projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model is intended for use with the colpali-engine version 0.3.1 or higher and requires transformers version greater than 4.45.0. It supports processing of images and queries, generating embeddings for multi-vector retrieval. Limitations include a primary focus on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit generalization to other document types or less represented languages. The model relies on multi-vector retrieval based on the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require additional engineering to adapt to standard vector retrieval frameworks. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2-VL) is licensed under Apache 2.0, while the attached adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: Compare the provided skin lesion image to the text 'malignant melanoma with irregular pigmentation'?
description: MedSigLIP is a variant of SigLIP (Sigmoid Loss for Language Image Pre-training) designed to encode medical images and text into a common embedding space. It contains a 400M parameter vision encoder and a 400M parameter text encoder, supporting 448x448 image resolution with up to 64 text tokens. MedSigLIP was trained on a combination of de-identified medical image and text pairs, including chest X-rays, dermatology images, ophthalmology images, histopathology slides, and slices of CT and MRI volumes, along with associated descriptions or reports. This training data was combined with natural (non-medical) image and text pairs to retain the model's ability to parse natural images. MedSigLIP is recommended for medical image interpretation applications that do not require text generation, such as data-efficient classification, zero-shot classification, and semantic image retrieval. For medical applications that require text generation, another model is recommended. MedSigLIP accepts images and text as inputs, producing image embeddings, text embeddings, and similarity scores between the image and text. It was evaluated across a range of medical image modalities, including chest X-ray, pathology, dermatology, and ophthalmology. Key performance metrics include zero-shot AUCs for chest X-ray findings, where MedSigLIP outperformed ELIXR in several categories, and AUCs for dermatology, ophthalmology, and pathology findings, where MedSigLIP showed competitive performance compared to existing HAI-DEF embedding models. MedSigLIP is based on SigLIP-400M and uses the same encoder that powers image interpretation in a generative model. It is intended for use by software developers and researchers to facilitate the creation of healthcare applications involving medical images and text. MedSigLIP is not a finished product and requires adaptation and validation for specific use cases. Developers should consider potential biases in validation data and data contamination concerns when evaluating the model's generalization capabilities. MedSigLIP was trained on diverse public datasets, including MIMIC-CXR, Slake-VQA, PAD-UFES-20, SCIN, TCGA, CAMELYON, PMC-OA, and Mendeley Digital Knee X-Ray, as well as de-identified, licensed datasets or datasets collected internally at Google from consented participants. The model was created on July 9, 2025, and is version 1.0.0. When using this model, please cite the MedGemma Technical Report by Sellergren et al. (2025).

instruction: What information is displayed on this image of a weather radar map?
description: Llama Nemotron Nano VL is a document intelligence vision language model (VLM) designed to query and summarize images from the physical or virtual world. It is deployable in the data center, cloud, and at the edge, including Jetson Orin and laptops, using AWQ 4bit quantization through the TinyChat framework. The model was trained on commercial images for all three stages of training and supports single image inference. Key findings include the necessity of interleaved image-text data over image-text pairs, the importance of unfreezing the language model during interleaved pre-training for in-context learning, and the benefit of re-blending text-only instruction data to enhance both VLM and text-only performance. The model uses a Transformer architecture with a vision encoder and a Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct language encoder. It accepts image and text inputs, with images in RGB format and text in string format. Input images are constrained by a 12-tile layout, with each tile being 512 × 512 pixels, supporting various aspect ratios. The model processes 2D images and 1D text, with a total input and output token limit of 16K. Output is text in string format. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA hardware and software for faster training and inference. It is compatible with the TensorRT-LLM runtime engine and the H100 SXM 80GB microarchitecture, and supports Linux operating systems. The model was trained and evaluated using the NV-Pretraining and NV-CosmosNemotron-SFT datasets, which include a mix of internal and public datasets, as well as synthetic data for specific tasks. Evaluation benchmarks include scores on MMMU Val (48.2%), AI2D (85.0%), ChartQA (86.3%), InfoVQA Val (77.4%), OCRBench (839), OCRBenchV2 English (60.1%), OCRBenchV2 Chinese (37.9%), DocVQA val (91.2%), and VideoMME (54.7%). The model supports use cases such as image summarization, text-image analysis, optical character recognition, interactive Q&A on images, and text chain-of-thought reasoning. It is intended for AI foundry enterprise customers and is available in the Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-VL-8B-V1 version. The model is governed by the Llama 3.1 Community Model License and is built with Llama. Ethical considerations include the responsibility of users to ensure safe integration, including the implementation of guardrails and other safety mechanisms, as outputs may contain political content, misleading information, content security issues, or unwanted bias. Users are responsible for model inputs and outputs and should work with their internal model teams to ensure compliance with industry and use case requirements.

instruction: My video has some strange noise and artifacts. Can you smooth them out?
description: SeedVR2 is a one-step diffusion-based video restoration model designed to perform adversarial video restoration training against real data. It aims to address the challenges of high-resolution video restoration in real-world settings, where existing distillation-based approaches for one-step image restoration have not been effectively extended. SeedVR2 introduces several architectural and training enhancements to handle high-resolution video restoration within a single step. These include an adaptive window attention mechanism, where the window size is dynamically adjusted to fit the output resolution, preventing window inconsistency observed in high-resolution video restoration using fixed window sizes. Additionally, a series of losses are verified for their effectiveness in stabilizing and improving adversarial post-training, including a proposed feature matching loss that does not significantly sacrifice training efficiency. Extensive experiments show that SeedVR2 can achieve comparable or better performance than existing video restoration approaches in a single step. However, the model has some limitations: it may not be perfectly aligned with the paper due to being a prototype, and it is not always robust to heavy degradations or very large motions. It shares some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Moreover, due to its strong generation ability, it may occasionally oversharpen results on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos.

instruction: Can you create a snowflake image that has a subtle gradient or color variation?
description: This model is an unconditional DDPM U-Net (EMA) trained from scratch to generate images of snowflakes. It operates on latent representations produced by a pre-trained Variational Autoencoder (VAE), which is not part of this model. The model learns the visual structure of real snowflakes from macro photography, enabling the generation of novel, synthetic snowflake images that resemble real crystalline forms. The input to the model is Gaussian noise, and the output consists of snowflake images after VAE decoding, with a resolution of 512×512. The model is used in the open-source application Snowflakeizer for snowflake image generation. It was trained on a dataset of macro photos of real snowflakes by Alexey Kljatov, which is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY). The model weights are licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0). When using this model, proper attribution to the original dataset author is required. The model is intended for research in generative modeling, image synthesis experiments, artistic and creative applications, and educational purposes related to diffusion models. It has limitations, including being trained on a single-domain dataset of snowflake macro photography, and generated images may not correspond to physically accurate crystal growth processes. The model may also produce artifacts or unrealistic structures. The training data consists of non-sensitive macro photographs of natural objects, with no known privacy, biometric, or identity-related concerns. Generated images should not be presented as real scientific observations.

instruction: Can you determine the sentiment of this judicial opinion?
description: GEM-RoBERTa HQ Legal is a RoBERTa-base model pre-trained from scratch on a strategically curated 21GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. The model employs a quality-based data repetition strategy, where higher-quality legal sources are repeated multiple times during training to enhance the model's understanding of premium legal terminology and concepts. This model was trained as part of a research project and has been optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering within the legal field. The RoBERTa architecture provides enhanced performance through dynamic masking and removal of the Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) task, focusing entirely on Masked Language Modeling (MLM). The model was pre-trained on a comprehensive corpus of Greek text compiled from various legal and governmental sources, with a quality-based data repetition strategy that increases exposure to higher-quality legal content. The original 16.75GB corpus was expanded to 21.12GB through strategic repetition. The quality-based repetition strategy enhances the model's exposure to premium legal terminology from the Raptarchis Legal Dictionary (4x repetition), high-quality judicial reasoning from Supreme Court reports (3x repetition), and EU legal concepts from Eur-Lex content (2x repetition). This approach ensures the model develops a stronger understanding of sophisticated legal language while maintaining exposure to the broader legal corpus. The model uses the RoBERTa-base architecture with a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, 12 hidden layers, and approximately 125 million parameters. The model was trained using a custom ByteLevelBPE tokenizer with a vocabulary of 50,264 tokens optimized for Greek legal terminology. The data was processed into fixed-size chunks of 512 tokens, respecting document boundaries to ensure contextual coherence. Higher-quality sources were strategically repeated during the data preparation phase. The model was pre-trained from scratch for 150,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, using BFloat16 (bf16) mixed-precision for stability and speed. The training took approximately 81 hours and 39 minutes to complete. The key hyperparameters used were a learning rate of 1.5e-4 (0.00015) with a linear warmup of 9,000 steps, an effective batch size of 2,688 (per_device_train_batch_size: 48, gradient_accumulation_steps: 7), the AdamW optimizer with standard parameters, a weight decay of 0.01, a max sequence length of 512, a max of 150,000 steps, 9,000 warmup steps, an MLM probability of 0.15, and a max gradient norm of 1.0. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.617 and a final evaluation loss of 0.573035. The training infrastructure consisted of 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, with a total of 150,000 training steps and a total training time of 66 hours 39 minutes. The train/validation split was 95%/5%, and the effective training data was 21.12GB, incorporating the quality-based repetition strategy.

instruction: What are the main causes of the French Revolution and its impact on European history?
description: Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 is an updated version of the Qwen3-4B non-thinking mode, featuring significant improvements in general capabilities such as instruction following, logical reasoning, text comprehension, mathematics, science, coding, and tool usage. It demonstrates substantial gains in long-tail knowledge coverage across multiple languages and is better aligned with user preferences in subjective and open-ended tasks, enabling more helpful responses and higher-quality text generation. The model supports 256K long-context understanding and has 4.0B parameters, with 3.6B non-embedding parameters, 36 layers, and 32 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It has a context length of 262,144 tokens natively. This model is designed for non-thinking mode only and does not generate code blocks in its output. It is reported to outperform previous versions and other models on a range of benchmarks, including MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux, GPQA, SuperGPQA, AIME25, HMMT25, ZebraLogic, LiveBench, LiveCodeBench, MultiPL-E, Aider-Polyglot, IFEval, Arena-Hard v2, Creative Writing v3, WritingBench, BFCL-v3, TAU1-Retail, TAU1-Airline, TAU2-Retail, TAU2-Airline, TAU2-Telecom, MultiIF, MMLU-ProX, INCLUDE, and PolyMATH. The model is compatible with Hugging Face transformers and can be used with vLLM or SGLang for creating an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and to set an output length of 16,384 tokens. The model supports agentic use through Qwen-Agent, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity. Users can define available tools using an MCP configuration file or integrate other tools manually. The model is supported by various local applications, including Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, and KTransformers. If out-of-memory issues occur, reducing the context length to 32,768 is advised. For benchmarking, it is recommended to standardize output formats using specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions.

instruction: Could you generate a 3D human figure with a detailed posture based on my instructions?
description: Hunyuan3D-Omni is a unified framework for the controllable generation of 3D assets, built on Hunyuan3D 2.1. It introduces a unified control encoder to incorporate additional control signals, including point cloud, voxel, skeleton, and bounding box. The model supports multi-modal conditional control, allowing 3D model generation constrained by 3D bounding boxes, creation of 3D human models with specific skeletal poses, generation guided by input point clouds, and creation from voxel representations. Hunyuan3D-Omni uses a single cross-modal architecture to unify all signals, rather than separate heads for each modality. It is trained with a progressive, difficulty-aware sampling strategy that selects one control modality per example, favoring harder signals such as skeletal pose and downweighting easier ones like point clouds. This approach encourages robust multi-modal fusion and graceful handling of missing inputs. Experiments show that these additional controls improve generation accuracy, enable geometry-aware transformations, and increase robustness for production workflows. The model requires 10 GB VRAM for generation and has a size of 3.3B parameters.

instruction: Сгенерируй объект, который будет выглядеть как сфера, но с элементами кубической геометрии и красным цветом.
description: alpha3D-v1 — это экспериментальная легковесная нейросеть для генерации 3D-объектов по текстовому описанию. Модель преобразует текстовые эмбеддинги в цветные облака точек. Она обучена на синтетическом датасете геометрических примитивов. Модель поддерживает генерацию форм, таких как куб и сфера, а также цветов: красный, зеленый и синий. Она умеет смешивать формы и цвета, например, создавать фиолетовый кубо-шар. Вывод модели представляет собой облако из 1000 точек, каждая из которых содержит координаты XYZ и значения цвета RGB. Архитектура модели — это многослойный перцептрон (MLP), в котором входной one-hot вектор размерностью 5 (для цветов и форм) проходит через последовательность слоев: 128, 512, 1024 нейронов, и заканчивается выходным слоем размерностью 6000, соответствующим 1000 точкам с 6 значениями. Модель обучалась с использованием функции потерь MSE. Датасет для обучения был сгенерирован синтетически: сферы создавались по спирали Фибоначчи, а кубы — по равномерной сетке. Модель способна на морфинг между объектами, например, при смешивании входных векторов можно получить промежуточные формы, такие как раздутый куб или фиолетовый цвет. Модель распространяется под лицензией Apache 2.0, что позволяет использовать, модифицировать и распространять код, в том числе в коммерческих целях.

instruction: Can you extract features from this image that are robust to lighting and orientation changes?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Generate an image of a steampunk-inspired airship flying over a Victorian city.
description: HiDream-I1 is a new open-source image generative foundation model with 17B parameters that achieves state-of-the-art image generation quality within seconds. It produces exceptional results across multiple styles including photorealistic, cartoon, and artistic. The model achieves a state-of-the-art HPS v2.1 score, which aligns with human preferences. It also achieves industry-leading scores on the GenEval and DPG benchmarks, outperforming all other open-source models. The model is released under the MIT license to foster scientific advancement and enable creative innovation. Generated images can be freely used for personal projects, scientific research, and commercial applications. The model includes a VAE component from FLUX.1 [schnell], licensed under Apache 2.0, and text encoders from google/t5-v1_1-xxl (licensed under Apache 2.0) and meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (licensed under the Llama 3.1 Community License Agreement). The Transformer models in the repository are licensed under the MIT License. Users own all content they create with this model and can use their generated content freely, but must comply with the license agreement. Users are responsible for how they use the models and must not create illegal content, harmful material, personal information that could harm others, false information, or content targeting vulnerable groups.

instruction: Could you help me decide which of these two versions of a product review is more trustworthy and detailed?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs, using a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series is based on the Bradley-Terry model and offers significant improvements over the first generation of Skywork-Reward. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, which contributes to its superior performance. The models are available in eight different sizes, ranging from the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, to the largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B. The smallest model nearly matches the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2, while the largest model surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and it is recommended to perform inference within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M model is considered an experimental variant, trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The model series is available under different licenses depending on the base model. Models derived from Qwen3 support commercial use and permit modifications and the creation of derivative works under the Apache 2.0 License, provided that all conditions are met and proper attribution is given. Models derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct follow the Meta License. The model can be used for inference in Hugging Face Transformers or via distributed inference using SGLang for optimal throughput with a large number of conversations. The model repository, including the model weights and code, is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for Qwen3-derived models and the Meta License for Llama-derived models. If you have any questions, you can contact the authors at yuhao.liuu at kunlun-inc dot com and liang.zeng at kunlun-inc dot com. If you find the work useful, please cite it as follows: @article{liu2025skywork, title={Skywork-Reward-V2: Scaling Preference Data Curation via Human-AI Synergy}, author = {Liu, Chris Yuhao and Zeng, Liang and Xiao, Yuzhen and He, Jujie and Liu, Jiacai and Wang, Chaojie and Yan, Rui and Shen, Wei and Zhang, Fuxiang and Xu, Jiacheng and Liu, Yang and Zhou, Yahui}, journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.01352}, year={2025}}

instruction: I need a video where a character is performing a dialogue, with their movements and expressions reflecting the audio input.
description: The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model is designed for audio-driven cinematic video generation. It was introduced in the Wan-S2V paper, which addresses the challenge of achieving film-level character animation by proposing an audio-driven model built upon Wan. The model significantly enhances expressiveness and fidelity in cinematic contexts compared to existing approaches. It outperforms state-of-the-art models such as Hunyuan-Avatar and Omnihuman in extensive experiments. The model supports applications in long-form video generation and precise video lip-sync editing. Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video models, incorporating a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into video diffusion models. This architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 includes a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4. This model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available, suitable for both industrial and academic use. The Wan2.2-S2V-14B model supports video generation at 480P and 720P resolutions. It can generate videos from audio input combined with a reference image and optional text prompt. The model also supports pose-driven generation, allowing it to follow specific pose sequences while generating videos synchronized with audio input. The model can be run on a single GPU with at least 80GB VRAM or using multi-GPU inference with FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design tailored to the denoising process of diffusion models: a high-noise expert for early stages and a low-noise expert for later stages. Each expert model has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined for switching between the high-noise and low-noise experts. The effectiveness of the MoE architecture is validated through comparisons with baseline and other MoE-based variants, showing that the final version achieves the lowest validation loss. Wan2.2 also explores a high-compression design with a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE. This model achieves a T×H×W compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, ranking among the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It natively supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a single unified framework. Wan2.2 is compared with leading closed-source commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0, demonstrating superior performance across multiple crucial dimensions. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, granting users the freedom to use the generated content while ensuring compliance with the license provisions. Users are fully accountable for their use of the models, which must not involve sharing any content that violates applicable laws, causes harm to individuals or groups, disseminates personal information intended for harm, spreads misinformation, or targets vulnerable populations.

instruction: Can you help me prioritize the most relevant Polish government documents for a given query?
description: The polish-reranker-roberta-v3 is a Polish language reranker model designed for use in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines. It is a general-purpose reranker that supports a wide range of document types and domains. This model is an updated version of the previous polish-reranker-roberta-v2, with the key improvement being the ability to handle long contexts of up to 8192 tokens, a 16x increase from the previous 512 tokens. This enhancement significantly improves reranking quality for datasets with longer documents. The model was trained using knowledge distillation, with a large language model-based reranker as the teacher model. The training process used the same loss function and largely the same training corpus as the previous version, with the addition of a new dataset of approximately 40,000 passages from the public administration domain, sourced from Polish government and local municipality websites. Questions for these passages were generated synthetically using DeepSeek-V3-0324. The model has 443 million parameters and delivers performance competitive with large LLM-based rerankers. For example, it outperforms the multilingual state-of-the-art reranker in 18 out of 41 tasks while having only 5% of its parameters. On the PIRB benchmark, the average score increased from 64.49 to 65.17 when using the sdadas/mmlw-retrieval-roberta-largeretriever, or from 65.30 to 66.21 when using the BAAI/bge-multilingual-gemma2retriever. For short and medium-length texts, the model achieves comparable or slightly better results than the previous version, but for very short texts, a slight decrease in quality was observed. The model was specifically improved for the public administration domain, with training data expanded to include questions and documents from this area. Evaluation results on three datasets from this domain show strong performance, with the model achieving NDCG@10 scores of 86.3 on opi-urzedowe, 80.1 on ezd-qa, and 86.7 on ezd-ir-chunked. The model can be used with Huggingface Transformers or Sentence-Transformers libraries. It achieves an NDCG@10 score of 66.21 in the Rerankers category of the Polish Information Retrieval Benchmark.

instruction: What's the best play on 4th and 3 from the 15-yard line in the final 30 seconds of a game tied at 20?
description: The NFL 4th-Down Decision Model is a small multi-task MLP that recommends whether to go for it, kick a field goal, or punt on 4th down by predicting the change in win probability (ΔWP) for each action. It is trained on NFL play-by-play data from 2016 to 2024, with labels generated from historical probabilities. The model takes 23 input features, including quarter, game and half seconds remaining, yardline, yards-to-go, score differential, timeouts, weather, point spread, and one-hot encoded roof and surface indicators, along with derived features such as field goal distance and whether the field goal is in range. The model outputs ΔWP for the three actions (go, field goal, punt) as the main head, and also predicts auxiliary heads for field goal make probability and 4th-down conversion probability. The recommendation is to choose the action with the highest ΔWP. The model was trained using a split of 2016–2021 for training, 2022 for validation, and 2023–2024 for testing. The training objective combines Huber loss for ΔWP with 0.2 times the mean squared error for auxiliary heads. Training used the AdamW optimizer with BatchNorm, Dropout, and L2 regularization. The best model weights are saved as `mlp_dwp_plus_aux_best.keras`. On the 2023–2024 test set, the model achieved a policy agreement of 0.794, a mean regret (ΔWP) of 0.0023, a late and close (≤600 seconds, absolute score ≤8) agreement of 0.744, and a late and close mean regret of 0.0099. ΔWP is reported in win-probability points, with lower regret indicating better performance. The model is intended as an educational decision assistant for 4th-down scenarios and is not suitable for wagering or real-time coaching. It relies on historical probabilities and inherits assumptions from the nfl4th project. Simplifications in weather and wp_punt imputation may affect accuracy, and domain judgment should be applied when using the model's recommendations.

instruction: Can you help me identify all the disease-related terms mentioned in this medical research paper?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for disease entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC5CDR_DISEASE dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 5,818 annotated disease entities. The model supports the identification and classification of two entity types: B-DISEASE and I-DISEASE. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the BiomedNLP-BiomedELECTRA-large-uncased-abstract architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.90, precision of 0.89, recall of 0.91, and accuracy of 0.98. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research applications where accurate disease entity identification is crucial for medical informatics and healthcare analytics. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: I have some encoded speech data from a game project—can you decode it into high-quality Japanese audio?
description: Anime‑XCodec2 is a fine‑tuned variant of HKUSTAudio/xcodec2, trained on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese anime/game-style voices. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, so speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for downstream systems that already work with XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model is a neural speech codec or speech tokenizer model based on XCodec2 with a decoder fine-tuned for Japanese speech, particularly anime/game-style voices. The training scope involved decoder-only fine-tuning on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese data, while the encoder and codebook are frozen. Because the encoder and codebook are unchanged, speech tokens produced at encode time are identical with the original XCodec2. Any downstream model expecting XCodec2 codes can use Anime‑XCodec2 as a drop-in decoder. The model operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz. The intended use of the model is to decode XCodec2 speech tokens, such as from Llasa or other autoregressive token generators trained on XCodec2 codes, into Japanese speech with improved naturalness for anime/game-style voices. It is also intended for the reconstruction of Japanese speech from XCodec2 tokens when analyzing or building Japanese-focused speech pipelines. The model is optimized for Japanese, and performance on other languages may degrade compared to the baseline XCodec2.
