It operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz, and inputs must be resampled to 16 kHz before encoding; decoding assumes 16 kHz. The model is tuned toward anime/game-style voices, and out-of-domain speech may not benefit. The training data consists of approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese speech, with a focus on anime/game-style voices, including acting and character voices. Data preparation included resampling to 16 kHz and standard loudness/peak checks where appropriate. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the components `generator.backbone`, `generator.head`, and `fc_post_a`, while all other non-listed components were frozen. The goal of the training was to preserve token compatibility with HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 while improving reconstruction quality for Japanese anime/game-style speech. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, the same as the original XCodec2 license. The model acknowledges the original HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 and thanks contributors and the community around Japanese speech resources.

instruction: What features can be extracted from this video that represent its core content?
description: The VideoMAE-v2 (Large-sized model, Pretrained on UnlabeledHybrid-1M) is a model pre-trained for 800 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is intended for video feature extraction. It can be used by loading the model and processor from the 'transformers' library, processing video inputs into tensors, and extracting features through the model. The model is based on the VideoMAE architecture and incorporates dual masking for training. The model is available under the identifier 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Large'.

instruction: Create a painting of a desert landscape with a lone cactus and a distant mirage.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family is the fastest image generation model to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture. It delivers state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Designed for real-time image generation without sacrificing quality, it runs on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is fully open under Apache 2.0 and can run on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090/4070. The model supports text-to-image, image editing, and multi-reference editing at quality that exceeds its size. It is suitable for local development, edge deployment, and production use. The model is available via the BFL API and can be used with the Diffusers Python library. Limitations include the inability to provide factual information, potential inaccuracies in rendered text, possible amplification of biases from training data, and the possibility of failing to generate output that matches prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate deceptive or harmful content, to generate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm individuals, to harass or abuse individuals or groups, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high-risk applications that adversely impact legal rights. Black Forest Labs is committed to responsible development and has implemented pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material and nonconsensual intimate imagery. These include filtering pre-training data for NSFW and CSAM content, targeted fine-tuning to inhibit harmful behaviors, and ongoing internal and external evaluations. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs and were released under an Apache 2.0 license. The model includes filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs, and implements pixel-layer watermarking and C2PA metadata for content provenance. Acceptable use is governed by policies including the FLUX Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy. Black Forest Labs maintains a safety email address and reporting relationships with organizations to address emerging risks and develop mitigations.

instruction: Which of these software documentation pages best explains how to set up a local server?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and offers a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model. The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is optimized for reranking in text retrieval scenarios and supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, providing robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 8 billion parameters and a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it supports customizing input instructions according to different tasks, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most downstream tasks. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal results, as most training instructions were in English. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including MTEB-R, CMTEB-R, MMTEB-R, MLDR, MTEB-Code, and FollowIR, where it achieves strong performance, particularly in multilingual and code-related tasks. The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is available for use with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher and requires specific formatting for input queries and documents. It is recommended to use the model with flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory efficiency. The model is part of a series that includes both embedding and reranking models, allowing developers to combine these modules for diverse use cases.

instruction: Please evaluate these two pieces of text and determine which is more relevant to the topic of cybersecurity threats.
description: The granite-embedding-reranker-english-r2 is a 149M parameter dense cross-encoder model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 768 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. The model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. It uses a cross-encoder architecture to compute relevance scores between queries and documents by jointly encoding their text, enabling precise reranking based on contextual alignment. The model is trained with ranking-specific loss functions such as pListMLE and model merging techniques to enhance performance. It shows strong performance on standard information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, MIRACL), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR), and enterprise use cases. The model is based on the ModernBERT architecture, with an embedding size of 768, 22 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1152, a GeGLU activation function, a vocabulary size of 50368, and a maximum sequence length of 8192. It is designed to compute relevance scores for query-document pairs, making it well-suited for reranking tasks in information retrieval and search applications. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and can be used to jointly encode pairs of text to compute relevance scores. It is also compatible with the Huggingface Transformers library and can be used to encode pairs of text. The model can be used in conjunction with a retriever to produce an end-to-end search system. The model is trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the model does not use the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing information about content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing, usage restrictions, data acquisition, and an assessment of sensitive information. The model is trained using IBM's BlueVela Cluster, which is outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80gb GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is fine-tuned on English and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: How can I extract all the time references from this diary entry?
description: This repository hosts the ONNX version of NuNerZero, a zero-shot named entity recognition (NER) model optimized for fast inference using ONNX Runtime. This conversion aims to provide efficient and production-ready performance while maintaining the original capabilities of the NuNerZero model. The model is part of Zink, a zero-shot anonymizer that currently uses the onnx NuNERZero model for anonymization. The repository includes the primary ONNX model file used for inference, configuration settings for the model, additional tokens required by the tokenizer, mapping for special tokens, tokenizer vocabulary and configuration files, and the SentencePiece model file used by the tokenizer. The model requires Python 3.7 or higher and the GLiNER package, with the onnx version created on 'gliner==0.2.3'. The model file is approximately 1.85 GB in size, so sufficient bandwidth and disk space are required for downloading. Leveraging the ONNX format can significantly accelerate inference compared to the original PyTorch implementation. Contributions, suggestions, or bug reports are welcome through opening an issue or submitting a pull request for improvements. The model is available under the citation 10.57967/hf/4902.

instruction: Show me an anime-style scene of a character preparing for a big exam.
description: Wan2.2-Anime-Unlimited (v1.0 Stable Toy) is the first truly open-source, unrestricted anime video generation model. It is based on the Wan2.2-5B architecture and can run on 6GB VRAM. The model is uncensored and designed to generate anime-style video content. The v1.0 version is described as a 'Qualified Toy' and is fully stable, serving as a proof of concept that a 5B model, when fine-tuned with 531 high-quality polished clips, can produce impressive anime content. It specializes in character interactions and basic movements. The model is currently in a stable state and is intended to be a fun and usable tool for generating anime videos. The development roadmap includes future versions such as v2.x, which will focus on battle scenes and slice-of-life content with support for 8s and 10s video generation, and v3.0, which will expand the range of anime concepts and art styles. The v5.0 to v6.0 versions will introduce a new audio model, transforming the system into a multimodal audio-video model with sound alignment and unrestricted creativity. The model is being developed with the support of the open-source community and is intended to surpass the performance of the 27B base model in the anime domain. The model is currently being trained on 4,000+ high-quality videos for the v2.x series, and further development depends on community support and funding.

instruction: What are the coordinates of the tables in this document?
description: This repository hosts three YOLOv11 models (nano, small, and medium) fine-tuned for high-performance Document Layout Analysis. The models are designed to accurately detect and classify key layout elements in a document, such as text, tables, figures, and titles. This is a fundamental task for document understanding and information extraction pipelines. The models are trained on the DocLayNet dataset to recognize 11 distinct layout types: Text, Title, Section-header, Table, Picture, Caption, List-item, Formula, Page-header, Page-footer, and Footnote. The recommended model is the `yolo11n` (nano) model, which offers an exceptional balance of speed and accuracy, making it ideal for production environments. The nano, small, and medium variants allow users to choose the best model for their use case. The nano model provides the best trade-off between speed and accuracy, while the small model is slightly more accurate, and the medium model provides the highest accuracy with a corresponding increase in computational cost. Performance gains are marginal when moving from the small to the medium model, making the nano and small variants the most practical choices. A deeper analysis revealed that the `train4` nano model stands out for its superior localization quality. It delivered significantly more accurate bounding boxes, with a +9.0% precision improvement for the `title` class and strong gains for `section-header` and `table`. It also achieved a +2.4% mAP50 and +2.05% mAP50-95 improvement for the difficult `footnote` class. The models were trained at 1280x1280 resolution, as initial tests at the default 640x640 resulted in a significant performance drop, especially for smaller elements like `footnote` and `caption`.

instruction: Can you walk me through solving this equation step by step: 4x + 7 = 23?
description: This model is a continuously trained Qwen3-0.6B fine-tuned using the Gensyn RL-Swarm framework with GRPO (Generalized Reward Policy Optimization) to enhance reasoning and mathematical capabilities. The model is identified by the Agent ID 'tall_tame_panther' and is currently in active training, with updates occurring every 5-10 minutes. It supports GGUF (llama.cpp) for edge deployment and local inference and is compatible with Text Generation Inference for production use. The model is trained on a composite dataset of 1,000 samples, with a weighted sampling strategy across multiple domains including propositional logic, calendar arithmetic, decimal arithmetic, base conversion, fraction simplification, and basic arithmetic. Training is conducted in bfloat16 precision and involves distributed reinforcement learning across a swarm network. The model has 0.6 billion parameters, with an architecture including 28 layers, 16 attention heads, 8 key-value heads, a hidden size of 1024, and an intermediate size of 3072. It supports a context length of 40,960 tokens and a vocabulary of 151,936 tokens. The model excels in logical reasoning, mathematical operations, number system conversions, date/time calculations, step-by-step problem solving, and conversational tutoring. It is optimized for math and reasoning tasks and may underperform on creative writing. The model is suitable for edge deployment but is not state-of-the-art for complex reasoning. Training is experimental and decentralized, which may lead to less predictable behavior compared to supervised models. The model is compatible with chat formats and supports system instructions, multi-turn dialogue, tool calling, and reasoning mode. It is trained using a reward system that evaluates model outputs via a judge API and updates the policy using GRPO. The model is part of a decentralized swarm with fault tolerance, diverse exploration, and collective intelligence. Training and model updates are verified on-chain. The model is available in multiple quantized formats (F16, Q3_K_M, Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M) and can be deployed using various methods including standard Transformers, TGI, GGUF with llama.cpp, and Ollama. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is developed by 0xgrey. Reproducibility requires specific configurations, including the same seed, dataset, and swarm state. Formal evaluation benchmarks are planned for future inclusion as training progresses.

instruction: What is the role of the AdamW optimizer in the training process?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of sberbank-ai/ruBert-large designed for the task of document question answering. It has been adapted specifically for extracting answers from a provided document context and fine-tuned on a custom JSON dataset containing context, question, and answer triples. The model was fine-tuned using the Transformers library with a custom training pipeline. A loader reads a JSON file containing context, question, and answer triples. The script tokenizes the document and question with a sliding window approach to handle long texts. The training process leverages mixed precision training and the AdamW optimizer to improve optimization. The training script evaluates model performance on a validation set, saves checkpoints, and employs early stopping based on validation loss. The model was trained for 20 epochs with a batch size of 4 per device, using 0.1 of total steps for warmup. FP16 training was enabled if CUDA was available. Training was performed on an 1xRTX 3070. The input is formed by concatenating the question and the document context, guiding the model to focus on the relevant segments. This model is ideal for interactive document question answering tasks, making it a powerful tool for applications such as customer support, document search, and automated Q&A systems. While primarily focused on Russian texts, the model also supports English language inputs. The model also supports English language, but its support was not tested.

instruction: Could you help me create a voice message that reacts to an audio input in real time?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-bf16 model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model was converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It is based on a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, which is used for multimodal reasoning. The model architecture includes an audio encoder based on a Conformer, an audio decoder using a Depthformer, and a detokenizer that reconstructs audio waveforms using ISTFT from predicted spectrograms. The model also supports decoding via the Mimi neural codec as an alternative. The model can be used with the mlx-audio library, which provides tools for preprocessing, tokenization, and decoding of both text and audio. It allows for the generation of interleaved text and audio responses, where each audio token is a complete frame of shape (8,) containing all 8 codebook values. The model supports streaming generation, enabling real-time audio playback during generation. It includes a ChatState class for managing conversation turns and handling both text and audio inputs and outputs. The model is designed to work with 24kHz audio inputs and can generate audio outputs at the same sample rate. The model's generation process can be configured with parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for text, and similar parameters for audio generation. The model is intended for use in applications requiring natural speech synthesis, audio transcription, and voice-based conversation systems. The model follows the license terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: Can you help me locate a specific paragraph in a document using a natural language question?
description: TomoroAI/tomoro-colqwen3-embed-4b is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model that maps text queries, visual documents (images, PDFs), or short videos into aligned multi-vector embeddings. It is built by merging components with a full vision stack, inheriting robust text retrieval capabilities. The model has been fine-tuned on a curated mixture of data sources and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across ViDoRe V1-V3 in both English and multilingual settings. It offers a significantly reduced embedding footprint compared to other full-dimension Colpali model alternatives. The architecture is based on Qwen3-VL 4B (encoder-only variant) with a 320-dimension projection head. It uses a ColPali-style late interaction method with MaxSim scoring. The model supports up to 1,280 visual tokens per page or 5,120 visual tokens per video, with text prompts constrained by the base context window of 32k. It is capable of handling complex layouts, such as chart-rich PDFs and domain-specific documents, and supports end-to-end retrieval of unseen multimodal documents without requiring OCR or an intermediate vision LLM for summaries. It demonstrates strong performance on non-English document inputs and inherits strong text retrieval performance from the Qwen3-Embedding-8B model. The model generalizes to video retrieval but is not fine-tuned on large-scale video retrieval datasets, and improvements are planned. It is larger than single-vector baselines despite the smaller token dimension. The model is not currently fine-tuned with diverse system instructions, and future improvements are intended through synthetic datasets. It is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, with upstream Qwen checkpoints retaining their community licenses. The training data includes ViDoRe/MTEB corpora and synthetic VisRAG assets. The model is applied to enhance Tomoro’s customized multimodal agentic RAG pipelines, enabling autonomous agents to parse, reason over, and retrieve from large-scale enterprise internal documentation. It supports Tomoro AI's mission to deliver high-quality enterprise multimodal solutions across high-stakes industries.

instruction: Could you provide a written version of this German speech, including timestamps for each segment?
description: parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is a 600-million-parameter multilingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) model designed for high-throughput speech-to-text transcription. It extends a previous model by expanding language support from English to 25 European languages. The model automatically detects the language of the audio and transcribes it without requiring additional prompting. It is part of a series of models that leverage a multilingual corpus as their primary training dataset. The supported languages include Bulgarian, Croatian, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Estonian, Finnish, French, German, Greek, Hungarian, Italian, Latvian, Lithuanian, Maltese, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Slovak, Slovenian, Spanish, Swedish, Russian, and Ukrainian. The model is ready for commercial and non-commercial use. Key features include automatic punctuation and capitalization, accurate word-level and segment-level timestamps, long audio transcription supporting up to 24 minutes with full attention or up to 3 hours with local attention, and a permissive CC BY 4.0 license. The model is based on the FastConformer-TDT architecture and has 600 million parameters. It accepts 16kHz audio in .wav and .flac formats and produces text output with punctuation and capitalization included. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, achieving faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. It was trained using the NeMo toolkit, initialized from a CTC multilingual checkpoint pretrained on the Granary dataset, and further trained for 150,000 steps on 128 A100 GPUs. Stage 2 fine-tuning was performed for 5,000 steps on 4 A100 GPUs using approximately 7,500 hours of high-quality, human-transcribed data from NeMo ASR Set 3.0. A unified SentencePiece Tokenizer with a vocabulary of 8,192 tokens was used during training. The model was trained on a combination of the Granary dataset and the NeMo ASR Set 3.0, which includes 10,000 hours of human-transcribed data and 660,000 hours of pseudo-labeled data. Evaluation datasets include Fleurs, MLS, CoVoST, and the Hugging Face Open ASR Leaderboard. The model's multilingual ASR performance is summarized by word error rate (WER) across different datasets, with an average WER of 11.97% on Fleurs, 7.83% on MLS, and 11.98% on CoVoST. On the Hugging Face Open-ASR-Leaderboard, the model achieves an average WER of 6.34%. Performance is evaluated across different Signal-to-Noise Ratios (SNR), with increasing WER as SNR decreases. The model is intended for use by developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is licensed under a permissive CC BY 4.0 license. Technical limitations include potential inaccuracies in transcripts, with accuracy varying based on language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy may vary based on the context of the input text.

instruction: What is the predicted income category for a person in the education sector with a master’s degree and 15 years of experience?
description: This model is a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) that uses Categorical Embeddings to classify income levels as either greater than 50K or less than or equal to 50K based on census data. It is a more complex and robust approach compared to models that use standard one-hot encoding. The architecture includes specialized Embedding layers for categorical features and has 13.1k parameters. It contains two hidden layers with dimensions 128 and 64, both using ReLU activation. A key feature of the model is its use of dense representations (embeddings) for categorical variables instead of one-hot encoding. The preprocessing pipeline includes Log-transformation for numerical stability and Standard Scaling. The model achieves an accuracy of 0.806 and an F1-Score of 0.768. While slightly more conservative in raw scores compared to base models, it demonstrates excellent generalization. The Embedding architecture is noted for its training stability, as the evaluation loss curves show that the model remains flatter and more controlled compared to base models, which tend to overfit more aggressively.

instruction: Could you detect and outline all the objects in this image of a cluttered room?
description: The DETR (DEtection TRansformer) model is an encoder-decoder transformer with a convolutional backbone, specifically a ResNet-50 backbone. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model was introduced in a paper by Carion et al. and features two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class labels and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding boxes. Object detection is performed using object queries, with 100 queries for the COCO dataset. The model is trained using a bipartite matching loss, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes to ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create a one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations, followed by optimization using cross-entropy for classes and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding boxes. The model achieves an average precision (AP) of 42.0 on the COCO 2017 validation set. It is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch. The model can be used with an image processor and object detection pipeline to extract and interpret detection results. The training process involved 300 epochs on 16 V100 GPUs, with a batch size of 64. Images were preprocessed by resizing and normalizing with ImageNet mean and standard deviation values. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original DETR team did not provide one.

instruction: Can I generate a natural-sounding voice reply to an audio message in under a second?
description: LFM2-Audio-1.5B is the first end-to-end audio foundation model from Liquid AI. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, it enables seamless conversational interaction at 1.5 billion parameters, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. LFM2-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model that does not require separate ASR and TTS components. The model consists of a pretrained LFM2 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and a RQ-transformer generating discrete Mimi tokens as audio output. LFM2-Audio supports two distinct generation routines: interleaved generation, which enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities where audio generation latency is key, and sequential generation, which is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters in the language model component, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolutional and attention-based backbone, an audio tokenizer using 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. It is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. The model achieves a VoiceBench score of 56.78, with AlpacaEval 3.71, CommonEval 3.49, WildVoice 3.17, SD-QA 30.56, MMSU 31.95, OBQA 44.40, BBH 30.54, IFEval 98.85, and ADVBench 67.33. For ASR, it has a word error rate (WER) of 7.24, with Open 15.58, AMI 10.67, GigaSpeech 2.01, LibriSpeech-clean 4.39, LibriSpeech-other 3.56, and TED-LIUM 3.56. The model is suitable for multi-turn, multi-modal chat, where it can switch between audio and text modalities. It is available via the `liquid-audio` package and can be run using a Gradio demo interface. The model is intended for use with the LFM Open License v1.0, and the audio encoder is based on code licensed under other terms. The model is described in the LFM2 Technical Report, arXiv:2511.23404.

instruction: Find the best information on how blockchain technology is transforming the financial sector.
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-17m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 17 million parameters and a projection dimension of 48. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. On the BEIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.490, with specific scores of 0.416 on MS MARCO, 0.719 on SciFact, 0.316 on Touche, 0.326 on FiQA, 0.713 on TREC-COVID, 0.551 on NQ, and 0.410 on DBPedia. On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.847 for 32k tokens and 0.776 for 4k tokens. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the provided BibTeX entries for the associated tech report, projection variants report, and PyLate library.

instruction: I need to find all the gene names mentioned in this abstract. Can you do that?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the BiomedNLP-BiomedBERT-base-uncased-abstract-fulltext architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It achieves an F1 score of 1.00, precision of 0.99, recall of 1.00, and accuracy of 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows it ranks first with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in research and applications, with proper citation of the associated paper.

instruction: How do the meanings of 'sustainable development' in English and Chinese compare?
description: The `llama-embed-nemotron-8b` is a text embedding model developed by NVIDIA, optimized for tasks such as retrieval, reranking, semantic similarity, and classification. It is designed for multilingual and cross-lingual text retrieval and serves as a foundational component in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. The model is based on the Llama-3.1-8B architecture with a bidirectional attention mechanism and consists of 32 hidden layers and an embedding size of 4096. It was trained on a combination of public and synthetically generated datasets, with a total of 16.4 million query-passage pairs. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MTEB (Multilingual, v2) benchmark as of October 21, 2025, with a Borda rank of 1 and 39,573 Borda votes, along with a mean task performance of 69.46. The model is instruction-aware, meaning it supports custom instructions to improve performance for specific use cases. For retrieval tasks, queries must be accompanied by a task instruction in the format `Instruct: {task_instruction}\nQuery: {query}`, while documents do not require special handling. The model outputs embedding vectors of dimension 4096 for each text input and supports input sequences up to 32768 tokens in length. It is compatible with NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, including architectures such as Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, Pascal, Turing, and Volta, and is optimized for use with software frameworks like TensorRT and Triton. The model is intended for non-commercial and research use only and is available on Hugging Face as of October 21, 2025. It is recommended to use the model with transformers version 4.51.0 and flash-attention for GPU processing. The model can be integrated using libraries such as Sentence Transformers or Hugging Face Transformers. NVIDIA encourages users to ensure the model meets industry and use-case requirements and to report any concerns regarding model quality, risk, or security vulnerabilities.

instruction: Generate a series of frames showing a cityscape transitioning from morning to evening with changing lighting conditions.
description: next-scene-qwen-image-lora-2509 is a LoRA adapter fine-tuned on Qwen-Image-Edit (build 2509), designed to generate cinematic image sequences with natural visual progression from frame to frame. This model enables Qwen Image Edit to understand camera dynamics, visual composition, and narrative continuity, creating shots that flow seamlessly into one another. It is trained on a large-scale proprietary cinematic dataset and is intended to advance the story by preserving spatial relationships, lighting consistency, and emotional resonance across sequential frames. The model supports tasks such as storyboard generation for film and animation pre-production, cinematic AI video pipelines requiring frame-to-frame coherence, sequential narrative workflows, concept art evolution, and visual storytelling for creative projects. It is not optimized for static portraits, single-image illustration tasks, or non-sequential edits, and is best suited for multi-frame workflows with narrative progression. The model is based on the Qwen-Image-Edit (build 2509) architecture and is compatible with platforms such as ComfyUI and Automatic1111 (with Qwen support). A recommended LoRA strength is 0.7–0.8. The model is available in two versions: V1 (`next-scene_lora_v1-3000.safetensors`) and V2 (`next-scene_lora_v2-3000.safetensors`), with V2 trained on higher quality data for improved results, better command responsiveness, and fixed black bar artifacts. The model is released under the MIT License for research, educational, and creative use, with commercial applications requiring independent testing and proper attribution.

instruction: What is the sequence of actions in this video, and how are they visually represented?
description: This is an updated version of a previous model, offering improved abilities in multimodal reasoning, visual perception, video understanding, and high-resolution image processing. The 2506 version achieves better accuracy on multimodal reasoning benchmarks, including 56.9 on MathVision (+20.1), 80.1 on MathVista (+8.4), 46.3 on MMMU-Pro (+3.3), and 64.0 on MMMU (+2.1), while requiring 20% fewer tokens for thinking. It also demonstrates strong performance on general visual perception and understanding tasks, such as MMBench-EN-v1.1 (84.4), MMStar (70.4), RealWorldQA (70.0), and MMVet (78.4), matching or surpassing the abilities of non-thinking models. The model extends to video scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art results on VideoMMMU (65.2) and maintaining strong general video understanding on Video-MME (71.9). It supports 3.2 million total pixels in a single image, four times the resolution of the previous version, leading to improvements on high-resolution perception and OS-agent grounding benchmarks such as V* Benchmark (83.2), ScreenSpot-Pro (52.8), and OSWorld-G (52.5). The model is designed for long-decode inference, generating up to 32K tokens, and is recommended to be used with VLLM for efficient performance. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires specific versions of Python, PyTorch, and Transformers for optimal use. The model is evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks, including MMLU (82.0), MMLU-Pro (68.5), MATH (91.8), and GPQA-Diamond (42.3), and is compared with other large open-source models such as Qwen2.5-VL-32B, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, and Gemma3-27B-IT. The model is available for use with specific installation instructions and inference code examples provided for both VLLM and Hugging Face Transformers. The model is described in a technical report and is available for citation through the arXiv repository.

instruction: Translate this English user feedback into Vietnamese for analysis in our product team.
description: Seed-X is a series of open-source multilingual translation language models, including an instruction model, a reinforcement learning model, and a reward model, designed to push the boundaries of translation capabilities within a 7 billion parameter scale. Seed-X exhibits state-of-the-art translation capabilities, on par with or outperforming ultra-large models like Gemini-2.5, Claude-3.5, and GPT-4, as validated by human evaluations and automatic metrics. With a compact 7B parameter count and mistral architecture, Seed-X offers outstanding translation performance in a lightweight and efficient package, ideal for deployment and inference. It excels on a highly challenging translation test set spanning diverse domains, including the internet, science and technology, office dialogues, e-commerce, biomedicine, finance, law, literature, and entertainment. The Seed-X-PPO-7B-GPTQ-Int8 model is a causal language model that supports multilingual translation among 28 languages, including Arabic, Czech, Danish, German, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian Bokmal, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, and Chinese. It is trained through pretraining and post-training stages and is quantized using GPTQ 8-bit. The model is specialized in multilingual translation and is not expected to support other tasks. The language tags at the end of the prompt are necessary for PPO training. There is no chat template, so the model should not be prompted in a multi-round conversation format. The model is evaluated on a diverse set of translation benchmarks, including FLORES-200, WMT-25, and a publicly released benchmark, accompanied by human evaluations. The model is recommended to be used with vllm version 0.8.0 and transformers version 4.51.3. The model is licensed under OpenMDW. It is recommended to use the Seed-X-PPO model, as its translation performance is superior to Seed-X-Instruct. Unofficial quantized versions for local deployment are not recommended. An official quantized model and a demo on Hugging Face Space are planned for release.

instruction: Please turn this newsletter into a concise overview so I can read it quickly during my lunch break.
description: This is a fine-tuned T5-small model specialized for email summarization. The model can generate both brief (one-line) and detailed (comprehensive) summaries of emails, and is robust to messy, informal inputs with typos and abbreviations. The base model is T5-small, and it was fine-tuned by the Wordcab Team. It is a T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) model designed for English language processing and is released under the Apache 2.0 License. The model supports dual-mode summarization through the use of `summarize_brief:` and `summarize_full:` prefixes. It is optimized for fast inference and real-world email processing. The intended uses include summarizing professional and casual emails, email inbox management and prioritization, meeting invitation processing, customer support email triage, and newsletter summarization. Limitations include a maximum input length of 512 tokens (~2500 characters), support for English language only, potential difficulty in capturing nuanced context in very long email threads, and unsuitability for legal or medical document summarization. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing 364,000 email-summary pairs, including professional business emails, casual informal communications, meeting invitations and updates, technical discussions, and customer service interactions. The training data was augmented with subtle typos and misspellings in 50% of examples, common abbreviations, and various formatting styles. Training was conducted for one epoch with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 3e-4, using a validation split of 1%. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GPU with mixed precision (fp16) using the HuggingFace Transformers 4.36.0 framework. Emails were formatted as `Subject: [subject]. Body: [content]`, and two training examples were created per email (brief and full summaries). Data augmentation was applied to 50% of examples. The model achieved a ROUGE-L score of 0.42, an average inference time of 0.63 seconds for brief summaries and 0.71 seconds for full summaries on a T4 GPU, and a coherence score of 80% on messy inputs. It successfully differentiates between brief and full summaries, with a 2.5x length difference, and is robust in handling informal text and typos. The model is based on the T5 architecture, which includes an encoder and decoder with multiple T5 blocks, embedding layers, and a linear language model head. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 License and is based on the T5 model. It was fine-tuned on a dataset and trained using HuggingFace Transformers. Special thanks are given to the open-source community.

instruction: What is the speaker saying in this audio, and can you translate it into Spanish?
description: Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The languages that each modal supports are the following: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model is intended for broad multilingual and multimodal commercial and research use. It provides uses for general purpose AI systems and applications which require memory/compute constrained environments, latency bound scenarios, strong reasoning (especially math and logic), function and tool calling, general image understanding, optical character recognition, chart and table understanding, multiple image comparison, multi-image or video clip summarization, speech recognition, speech translation, speech QA, and audio understanding. The model is designed to accelerate research on language and multimodal models, for use as a building block for generative AI powered features. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. This release of Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is based on valuable user feedback from the Phi-3 series. Previously, users could use a speech recognition model to talk to the Mini and Vision models. To achieve this, users needed to use a pipeline of two models: one model to transcribe the audio to text, and another model for the language or vision tasks. This pipeline means that the core model was not provided the full breadth of input information – e.g. cannot directly observe multiple speakers, background noises, jointly align speech, vision, language information at the same time on the same representation space. With Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, a single new open model has been trained across text, vision, and audio, meaning that all inputs and outputs are processed by the same neural network. The model employed new architecture, larger vocabulary for efficiency, multilingual, and multimodal support, and better post-training techniques were used for instruction following and function calling, as well as additional data leading to substantial gains on key multimodal capabilities. It is anticipated that Phi-4-multimodal-instruct will greatly benefit app developers and various use cases. The enthusiastic support for the Phi-4 series is greatly appreciated. Feedback on Phi-4 is welcomed and crucial to the model's evolution and improvement. The model has 5.6B parameters and is a multimodal transformer model. The model has the pretrained Phi-4-Mini-Instruct as the backbone language model, and the advanced encoders and adapters of vision and speech. The model's inputs include text, image, and audio. It is best suited for prompts using the chat format. The model's context length is 128K tokens. The model was trained on 512 A100-80G GPUs for 28 days. The training data includes 5 trillion text tokens, 2.3M speech hours, and 1.1T image-text tokens. The model's outputs are generated text in response to the input. The model was trained between December 2024 and January 2025. The model is a static model trained on offline datasets with the cutoff date of June 2024 for publicly available data. The model supports the following languages: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model was released in February 2025. The model's training data includes a wide variety of sources, totaling 5 trillion text tokens, and is a combination of publicly available documents filtered for quality, selected high-quality educational data, and code; newly created synthetic, “textbook-like” data for the purpose of teaching math, coding, common sense reasoning, general knowledge of the world (e.g., science, daily activities, theory of mind, etc.); high quality human labeled data in chat format; selected high-quality image-text interleave data; synthetic and publicly available image, multi-image, and video data; anonymized in-house speech-text pair data with strong/weak transcriptions; selected high-quality publicly available and anonymized in-house speech data with task-specific supervisions; selected synthetic speech data; and synthetic vision-speech data. Focus was placed on the quality of data that could potentially improve the reasoning ability for the model, and the publicly available documents were filtered to contain a preferred level of knowledge. As an example, the result of a game in premier league on a particular day might be good training data for large foundation models, but such information was removed for the Phi-4-multimodal-instruct to leave more model capacity for reasoning for the model's small size. The data collection process involved sourcing information from publicly available documents, with a focus on filtering out undesirable documents and images. To safeguard privacy, image and text data sources were filtered to remove or scrub potentially personal data from the training data. The decontamination process involved normalizing and tokenizing the dataset, then generating and comparing n-grams between the target dataset and benchmark datasets. Samples with matching n-grams above a threshold were flagged as contaminated and removed from the dataset. A detailed contamination report was generated, summarizing the matched text, matching ratio, and filtered results for further analysis. The model is licensed under the . The model is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under.

instruction: I want to classify a Japanese sentence as either formal or informal. How can I do that?
description: ModernBERT-Ja-310M is a variant of the BERT model that combines local and global attention mechanisms, enabling it to handle long sequences while maintaining high computational efficiency. It incorporates modern architectural improvements. The model is trained on a high-quality corpus of Japanese and English text comprising 4.09T tokens, with a vocabulary size of 102,400 and a sequence length of 8,192 tokens. It is designed for masked language modeling and is intended to be fine-tuned for downstream tasks. It is not suitable for text generation, and users are advised to use a dedicated text generation model for such purposes. The model uses a unigram language model tokenizer with byte fallback, and does not apply pre-tokenization for Japanese, allowing raw sentences to be input directly. However, the token boundaries often do not align with morpheme boundaries, which may lead to poor performance in token classification tasks such as named entity recognition and span extraction. The model was constructed through a three-stage training process: pre-training with 3.51T tokens using a sequence length of 1,024 and a masking rate of 30%, followed by two phases of context length extension. Phase 1 used 430B tokens with a sequence length of 8,192 and a masking rate of 30%, and Phase 2 used 150B tokens with a sequence length of 8,192 and a reduced masking rate of 15%. The model's configuration includes a vocabulary size of 102,400, a head dimension of 64, and an activation function of GELU. The attention mechanism consists of one global attention layer and two local attention layers, with a sliding window attention window context size of 128. The global_rope_theta is set to 160,000 and the local_rope_theta to 10,000. The model was evaluated on 12 datasets, including JGLUE, across various tasks such as knowledge-based tasks, Japanese linguistic acceptability classification, natural language inference, semantic textual similarity, and various classification tasks. The evaluation used a maximum sequence length not exceeding 512, with settings aligned to existing models. Hyperparameters such as learning rate and number of epochs were explored, and the model was fine-tuned with a classification head consisting of a linear layer, a GELU activation function, and another linear layer. The embedding of the special token at the beginning of the sentence was used for classification, as the model does not perform next sentence prediction during pretraining. Evaluations were conducted using 5-fold cross-validation, with optimal hyperparameters determined based on validation set performance and final scores reported on test sets. For datasets without predefined splits, 10% of the data was set aside as a test set, and for datasets with only train and validation sets, the validation set was treated as the test set. The model achieved state-of-the-art performance across evaluation tasks, even when compared with much larger models. Despite being a long-context model capable of processing sequences of up to 8,192 tokens, it also exhibited strong performance in short-sequence evaluations. The model may produce representations that reflect biases, and when used for masked language modeling, it may generate biased or harmful expressions.

instruction: I need a panoramic image that includes both intrinsic material properties and semantic labels—can you produce that?
description: OmniX is a family of flow matching generative models designed for unified panoramic perception, generation, and completion. It enables the creation of graphics-ready 3D scenes suitable for physically based rendering, relighting, and simulation. OmniX repurposes 2D generative models for panoramic perception of geometry, textures, and PBR materials, offering a versatile and unified framework. It is based on a lightweight and efficient cross-modal adapter structure that reuses 2D generative priors for a broad range of panoramic vision tasks. OmniX supports tasks such as panoramic image generation from text or image prompts, panoramic perception of geometric, intrinsic, and semantic properties, and the combination of generation and perception to produce panoramic images with multiple property annotations. The model is evaluated using a large-scale synthetic panorama dataset containing high-quality multimodal panoramas from diverse indoor and outdoor scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of OmniX in panoramic visual perception and graphics-ready 3D scene generation. The model is still under development for certain functionalities, such as graphics-ready scene reconstruction and generation, which are noted as being in progress.

instruction: I have a text that includes a mix of languages. Can you translate it into English while keeping the original language parts intact?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes a 1.8B translation model, HY-MT1.5-1.8B, and a 7B translation model, HY-MT1.5-7B. Both models focus on supporting mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporating 5 ethnic and dialect variations. Among them, HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with newly added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers translation performance comparable to its larger counterpart, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves the industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. HY-MT1.5-1.8B supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B, compared to its September open-source version, has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation.

instruction: What is the most important detail you can extract from this video?
description: Keye-VL-1.5 is a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) designed to address fundamental challenges in video comprehension. It incorporates a novel Slow-Fast video encoding strategy, a progressive four-stage pre-training methodology that extends context length up to 128K tokens, and a comprehensive post-training pipeline focused on reasoning enhancement and human preference alignment. The model demonstrates significant improvements in video understanding tasks and maintains competitive performance on general multimodal benchmarks. Keye-VL-1.5 is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and includes a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports SlowFast video encoding and native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information. The post-training process includes a non-reasoning stage composed of SFT and MPO training, and a reasoning stage consisting of three key steps: CoT Cold Start, General RL, and Alignment RL. Keye-VL-1.5-8B establishes itself with powerful, state-of-the-art perceptual abilities that are competitive with leading models. It demonstrates exceptional proficiency in video understanding across authoritative public video benchmarks such as Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU. In evaluation sets requiring complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, Keye-VL-1.5-8B displays strong performance, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: How can I improve the accuracy of visual search results for a multilingual dataset?
description: This is a W4A16 quantized version of a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model. The quantization was performed using AutoRound with the AutoAWQ backend. The quantized model achieves approximately 3.5 GB memory usage, compared to 8.4 GB for the original model, enabling deployment on consumer GPUs while maintaining competitive retrieval performance. The model has 4.0B parameters and uses W4A16 quantization (4-bit weights, 16-bit activations). The calibration sequence length used during quantization is 1024, and the quantization configuration includes 4 bits, a group size of 128, and symmetric quantization. The calibration dataset used is NeelNanda/pile-10k, with 560 samples and 1000 iterations, and a batch size of 80. A total of 252 layers were quantized, while 105 vision-related layers remain in FP16/BF16 to preserve visual feature quality. The embedding dimension is 320, and the model supports up to 1280 visual tokens. On the ViDoRe benchmark, the model achieves an average NDCG@5 of 0.69768 across all languages, representing a -0.36% change from the original FP16 model. For English-only evaluation, the model achieves an average NDCG@5 of 0.74582, a -0.21% change from the original. Overall, the model shows quality retention of approximately 99.6%, with 17 benchmarks improved and 23 degraded. The model is suitable for deployment on GPUs with limited memory, with 8 GB GPUs supporting a batch size of approximately 64, 12 GB GPUs supporting a batch size of approximately 256, and 16 GB and 24 GB GPUs supporting high batch sizes. The calibration sequence length refers to the length used during quantization and not the maximum sequence length supported by the model. The model is designed for tasks involving retrieval, particularly in multimodal settings. The model supports inference using the AutoProcessor and AutoModel from the Transformers library, with specific dependencies including torch, torchvision, auto-round, autoawq, transformers, pillow, and requests. The model is released under a license consistent with the original model. Limitations include reduced precision due to 4-bit quantization, the non-quantized vision encoder, and performance dependence on the inference backend. The model is based on an original model by Alibaba, with quantization performed using tools developed by Intel. If used, the model should be cited along with the original model in a bibtex format.

instruction: Could you help me by adding real-time descriptions to a nature video?
description: CASA-Qwen2_5-VL-3B-LiveCC is a Qwen-2.5VL model adapted from token insertion to a cross-attention-based architecture using CASA layers. It has been further fine-tuned for live video captioning. The model is designed to process video inputs and generate real-time subtitles that are embedded at the timestamp they are generated. It supports tasks involving video frame processing at 2fps and the generation of subtitles in real-time. The model is based on a cross-attention-based architecture and is optimized for efficient vision-language fusion. It has been evaluated on the Animal Kingdom dataset, where it generates descriptive captions for video content. For example, it can describe a fox in the Arctic, noting environmental details and the animal's behavior. The model requires the use of the `transformers` library and specific dependencies such as `ffmpeg`. It is available for use with model weights provided in the associated repository. Users are advised to refer to the repository for detailed instructions on running the model and generating live captions. The model is part of a research project and should be cited in any academic work that uses it.

instruction: How can I get a video output that’s both visually rich and compact in file size from a single image?
description: The Wan2.2 Distilled LoRA Models are high-performance video generation models that enable 4-step inference using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) techniques. These models are designed to provide excellent generation quality while supporting flexible deployment options. The LoRA weights are extracted from Wan2.2 distilled models and can be combined with base models either through offline merging or online loading during inference. The models support multiple frameworks, including LightX2V and ComfyUI. They offer dual noise control, allowing users to choose between high noise for more creative and diverse outputs or low noise for more stable and input-faithful results. The LoRA models are compact in size, with a rank of 64, and can be further optimized by combining them with quantization techniques for storage efficiency and easy sharing. The 4-step inference process ensures ultra-fast generation of high-quality videos while maintaining the benefits of distilled models. The available LoRA models include both high noise and low noise variants for image-to-video (I2V) tasks. These models must be used in conjunction with the Wan2.2 I2V base model and require additional components such as T5 text encoder, CLIP vision encoder, VAE encoder/decoder, and tokenizer for full functionality. When using 4-step inference, the recommended denoising step list is [1000, 750, 500, 250].

instruction: Edit a photo to make it look like it was taken during the golden hour, even if it was shot at noon.
description: FLUX.2 [dev] is a 32 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating, editing, and combining images based on text instructions. It is designed to perform state-of-the-art open text-to-image generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing. The model supports character, object, and style reference without the need for additional training. It was trained using guidance distillation, which makes it more efficient. The model is available for personal, scientific, and commercial use under the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License. Developers and creatives can use a reference implementation and sampling code provided in a dedicated repository. For local deployment on consumer-grade graphics cards, such as an RTX 4090 or RTX 5090, users can follow instructions on the GitHub page. The model includes filters for NSFW and IP-infringing content at input and output, and these filters or manual review must be used under the terms of the license. Black Forest Labs has implemented multiple pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). These include filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM, targeted fine-tuning to suppress harmful content, and ongoing internal and external evaluations. The final FLUX.2 [dev] checkpoint demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and showed higher resilience than leading open-weight models. The model includes content provenance features such as pixel-layer watermarking and links to the Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity (C2PA) standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 Pro applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to output content. Use of the model is governed by the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License and other policies that prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Black Forest Labs is monitoring for patterns of violative use after release and may issue takedown requests or ban users who repeatedly violate policies. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains reporting relationships with organizations such as the Internet Watch Foundation and the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.

instruction: Detect and separate the list items and their parent sections in this technical report.
description: dots.ocr is a multilingual document layout parsing model that unifies layout detection and content recognition within a single vision-language model while maintaining good reading order. Built on a compact 1.7B-parameter LLM foundation, it achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance for text, tables, and reading order, with formula recognition results comparable to much larger models like Doubao-1.5 and Gemini2.5-Pro. It demonstrates robust parsing capabilities for low-resource languages, achieving decisive advantages across both layout detection and content recognition on in-house multilingual document benchmarks. By leveraging a single vision-language model, dots.ocr offers a significantly more streamlined architecture than conventional methods that rely on complex, multi-model pipelines. Switching between tasks is accomplished simply by altering the input prompt, proving that a VLM can achieve competitive detection results compared to traditional detection models like DocLayout-YOLO. Built upon a compact 1.7B LLM, dots.ocr provides faster inference speeds than many other high-performing models based on larger foundations. It supports a range of layout categories, including 'Caption', 'Footnote', 'Formula', 'List-item', 'Page-footer', 'Page-header', 'Picture', 'Section-header', 'Table', 'Text', and 'Title'. Text extraction and formatting rules specify that 'Picture' elements omit text, 'Formula' elements are formatted as LaTeX, 'Table' elements as HTML, and all others as Markdown. The output text must be the original text from the image, with no translation, and all layout elements must be sorted according to human reading order. The final output is a single JSON object. dots.ocr is not yet perfect for high-complexity tables and formula extraction, and pictures in documents are currently not parsed. The model may fail to parse under certain conditions, such as when the character-to-pixel ratio is excessively high or when continuous special characters are present. It is not yet optimized for high-throughput processing of large PDF volumes. Future work includes achieving more accurate table and formula parsing, enhancing OCR capabilities for broader generalization, and developing a more general-purpose perception model based on VLMs that integrates general detection, image captioning, and OCR tasks into a unified framework. Parsing the content of pictures in documents is also a key priority for future work.

instruction: Which platform is likely to be used by a westbound train arriving at Alewife at 6:30 AM?
description: This model predicts which MBTA commuter rail track or platform a train will use. It is a small tabular neural-network ensemble trained on historical track assignments. The model performs multi-class classification across 13 track classes. It is implemented using Keras with a TensorFlow backend and consists of six diverse dense neural networks with embeddings and cyclical time features. At inference, the softmax outputs of the six models are averaged to produce the final prediction. The model accepts several inputs, including categorical features such as `station_id`, `route_id`, and `direction_id`, as well as cyclical time features like `hour_sin`, `hour_cos`, `minute_sin`, `minute_cos`, `day_sin`, and `day_cos`, and a continuous feature `scheduled_timestamp` (normalized in-model). The output is a probability distribution over the 13 track labels. The model is licensed under the MIT license. The ensemble includes six individual models, each saved as a `.keras` file, and best checkpoints from training are also available. Training metrics include an average validation loss of 1.2251 and an average validation accuracy of 0.5957, with individual model accuracies ranging from 0.5812 to 0.6049. The ensemble accuracy has a standard deviation of 0.0087. The training dataset contains 24,832 records, with 310 training steps and 77 validation steps per epoch. The model expects integer indices for `station_id` and `route_id`, and consistent vocabulary mappings are required for accurate inference. The training pipeline uses a dataset loader to define the exact feature mapping, and users must ensure the same vocabulary is used during inference. The model is intended for assisting real-time track/platform assignment predictions for MBTA commuter rail but is not a safety system and should not replace official dispatch or operations. It is sensitive to concept drift and unseen stations or routes. Consistent categorical index mapping between training and inference is required for reliable performance.

instruction: Which of these articles provides the clearest explanation of blockchain technology?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and offers a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model. The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is optimized for reranking in text retrieval scenarios and supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, providing robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 8 billion parameters and a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it supports customizing input instructions according to different tasks, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most downstream tasks. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal results, as most training instructions were in English. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including MTEB-R, CMTEB-R, MMTEB-R, MLDR, MTEB-Code, and FollowIR, where it achieves strong performance, particularly in multilingual and code-related tasks. The Qwen3-Reranker-8B is available for use with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher and requires specific formatting for input queries and documents. It is recommended to use the model with flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory efficiency. The model is part of a series that includes both embedding and reranking models, allowing developers to combine these modules for diverse use cases.

instruction: Can you find the section number that discusses safety guidelines in this document?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed to perform document question-answering tasks. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a random seed of 42, the Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and an epsilon of 1e-08, a linear learning rate scheduler, and a total of 20 training epochs. During training, the model achieved a final training loss of 0.0053 at step 4500 and a final validation loss of 4.7623. The model was developed using the Transformers 4.44.2 library, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Datasets 3.0.2, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the specific training and evaluation data used.

instruction: What are some common challenges when training video generation systems with reward-based feedback?
description: The Wan2.2-Fun-Reward-LoRAs are pre-trained models in the form of LoRAs (Low-Rank Adaptations) designed to enhance the alignment of generated videos with human preferences through the use of reward backpropagation techniques. These LoRAs can be used as plug-ins to improve the performance of the corresponding base model or to train custom reward LoRAs. The models include Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-HPS2.1, Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-high-noise-MPS, and Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP-low-noise-MPS. Each LoRA is trained with a batch size of 8 for varying numbers of steps: 5,000 steps for the high-noise HPS2.1 and high-noise MPS models, 2,700 steps for the low-noise HPS2.1 model, and 4,500 steps for the low-noise MPS model. The HPS2.1 and MPS reward LoRAs have a rank of 128 and a network alpha of 64. It is noted that the MPS reward LoRA for the low-noise model converges significantly more slowly and may not deliver satisfactory results, so the HPSv2.1 reward LoRA is recommended for the low-noise model. The models are intended to be used in conjunction with the base model Wan2.2-Fun-A14B-InP. During training, the reward continues to increase after a certain point, but the quality of the generated videos does not improve further, as the model may learn to exploit shortcuts such as adding artifacts in the background. Additionally, there is a lack of suitable preference models for video generation, and using image preference models leads to a decrease in the dynamism of generated videos, although this can be partially mitigated by computing the reward using only the first frame of the decoded video.

instruction: Can you determine if the speaker in this recording is real or synthetic?
description: The DF Arena 1B is a universal antispoofing model trained on traditional speech antispoofing datasets as well as singing and environmental deepfake data. It is designed to detect audio deepfakes and determine whether an audio sample is spoofed or bonafide. The model supports antispoofing tasks and can be used to classify audio inputs into spoof or bonafide categories. It is based on a neural network architecture optimized for audio deepfake detection. The model has been evaluated on multiple datasets, achieving an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.00% on the dfadd dataset, 11.54% on add_2023_round_2, 8.37% on codecfake, 4.66% on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.91% on in_the_wild, 1.14% on asvspoof_2019, 22.21% on add_2022_track_1, 2.92% on fake_or_real, 17.25% on asvspoof_2024, 2.20% on add_2022_track_3, 5.08% on add_2023_round_1, 0.15% on librisevoc, 1.75% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 1.09% on sonar. The average EER across all datasets is 5.919%, and the pooled EER is 9.52%. In terms of F1-score, the model achieves 0.9993 on dfadd, 0.9188 on add_2023_round_2, 0.8695 on codecfake, 0.8037 on asvspoof_2021_la, 0.9928 on in_the_wild, 0.9473 on asvspoof_2019, 0.6678 on add_2022_track_1, 0.9711 on fake_or_real, 0.6615 on asvspoof_2024, 0.9357 on add_2022_track_3, 0.9639 on add_2023_round_1, 0.9958 on librisevoc, 0.7577 on asvspoof_2021_df, and 0.9903 on sonar. The average F1-score is 0.8863, and the pooled F1-score is 0.81. For accuracy, the model achieves 99.97% on dfadd, 88.46% on add_2023_round_2, 91.63% on codecfake, 95.34% on asvspoof_2021_la, 99.10% on in_the_wild, 98.86% on asvspoof_2019, 77.79% on add_2022_track_1, 97.11% on fake_or_real, 82.75% on asvspoof_2024, 97.80% on add_2022_track_3, 94.92% on add_2023_round_1, 99.84% on librisevoc, 98.25% on asvspoof_2021_df, and 98.89% on sonar. The average accuracy is 94.079%, and the pooled accuracy is 90.47%. The model is distributed under a non-commercial license. For questions or issues, users are encouraged to open an issue on the model repository or contact the developers via email. The model can be cited using the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: ຂ້ອຍຕ້ອງການຄໍາຄຳຖາມເພື່ອສຳຫຼວດຜູ້ໃຊ້ງານກ່ຽວກັບການນໍາໃຊ້ດິຈິຕອນໃນການສຶກສາ, ທ່ານສາມາດຊ່ວຍໄດ້ບໍ?
description: This is a Lao language summarization model fine-tuned on a dataset using the base model Gemma 3-12B Instruct. The model is designed to generate concise summaries from Lao language text. It is specifically adapted to understand and generate responses in Lao language.
