It was trained using a curated dataset of over 5,000 high-quality Lao input-output pairs, primarily focused on AIDC (Artificial Intelligence and Digital Content) topics. The model supports summarization, question answering, and general chat tasks. The fine-tuning was performed using LoRA with PEFT (Unsloth). The training dataset consists of 10,000 Laos supervised samples, with a sequence length of 2048 and a batch size of 2 (with gradient accumulation). The model was trained for 3–5 epochs with early stopping enabled. The model is available in GGUF format, with F16 and Q8_0 versions available. The model is based on Google's Gemma 3-12B Instruct model and is suitable for Lao language generation. The model uses the Apache 2.0 license. The evaluation metric used is the BLEU score, which assesses the quality of generated summaries against reference summaries in the dataset.

instruction: What is the main action or event in this video, and what are the key visual details that support it?
description: This is an updated version of a previous model, offering improved abilities in multimodal reasoning, visual perception, video understanding, and high-resolution image processing. The 2506 version achieves better accuracy on multimodal reasoning benchmarks, including 56.9 on MathVision (+20.1), 80.1 on MathVista (+8.4), 46.3 on MMMU-Pro (+3.3), and 64.0 on MMMU (+2.1), while requiring 20% fewer tokens for thinking. It also demonstrates strong performance on general visual perception and understanding tasks, such as MMBench-EN-v1.1 (84.4), MMStar (70.4), RealWorldQA (70.0), and MMVet (78.4), matching or surpassing the abilities of non-thinking models. The model extends to video scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art results on VideoMMMU (65.2) and maintaining strong general video understanding on Video-MME (71.9). It supports 3.2 million total pixels in a single image, four times the resolution of the previous version, leading to improvements on high-resolution perception and OS-agent grounding benchmarks such as V* Benchmark (83.2), ScreenSpot-Pro (52.8), and OSWorld-G (52.5). The model is designed for long-decode inference, generating up to 32K tokens, and is recommended to be used with VLLM for efficient performance. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers library and requires specific versions of Python, PyTorch, and Transformers for optimal use. The model is evaluated on a wide range of benchmarks, including MMLU (82.0), MMLU-Pro (68.5), MATH (91.8), and GPQA-Diamond (42.3), and is compared with other large open-source models such as Qwen2.5-VL-32B, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, and Gemma3-27B-IT. The model is available for use with specific installation instructions and inference code examples provided for both VLLM and Hugging Face Transformers. The model is described in a technical report and is available for citation through the arXiv repository.

instruction: What does the chart suggest about the performance differences across the categories?
description: This model is a specialized version of Qwen3-VL-2B-Thinking, fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on the ChartQA dataset for advanced chart understanding, data extraction, and visual reasoning. It is designed to perform 'System 2' thinking, generating step-by-step reasoning traces before providing the final answer, which makes the decision process regarding chart interpretation transparent and verifiable. The model uses tags to articulate the visual analysis process, such as identifying axes and comparing bars, before concluding. It is optimized for bar charts, line graphs, and pie charts found in the ChartQA dataset. The model is fine-tuned with LoRA, involving approximately 2% trainable parameters, while retaining the base model's general capabilities. It is trained to return answers in a strict JSON format {\

instruction: Show me a fantasy castle surrounded by misty mountains and ancient ruins.
description: NetaYume Lumina is a text-to-image model fine-tuned from a high-quality anime-style image generation model developed by an unspecified entity. It is based on an open-source base model released by the team at Shanghai AI Laboratory. The model was trained to generate both realistic human images and high-quality anime-style images, while retaining a significant amount of knowledge from its base model. Key features include the ability to generate detailed anime-style images with sharp outlines, vibrant colors, and smooth shading. It also demonstrates improved character understanding, particularly for characters from the Danbooru dataset, resulting in more coherent and accurate character representations. Additionally, it enhances the generation of fine details such as accessories, clothing textures, hairstyles, and background elements with greater clarity. The model file, NetaYume_Lumina_v2_all_in_one.safetensors, is an all-in-one file that includes the necessary weights for the VAE, text encoder, and image backbone for use with ComfyUI. The model uses a pre-trained Gemma-2-2b text encoder, a pre-trained Flux.1 dev's VAE, and a fine-tuned NetaLumina image backbone. It is recommended to use a system prompt to help generate desired images by aligning with user prompts. For anime-style images using Danbooru tags, the model is described as an assistant designed to generate anime images based on textual prompts and user prompts with Danbooru tags. Recommended settings for optimal performance include a CFG of 4–7, sampling steps of 40–50, and samplers such as Euler a with a normal scheduler or res_multistep with a linear_quadratic scheduler. The model acknowledges the use of the Danbooru dataset, the creators of a wonderful model, and a team for openly sharing an awesome model.

instruction: Please detect and outline the body key points for all people visible in the image.
description: This model combines an object detector with a custom PyTorch keypoint head and is trained on a 500-person subset of the COCO dataset. The system detects people using DETR and then predicts 17 COCO-style keypoints using heatmap regression. The model is designed to perform keypoint estimation in a top-down approach, where person bounding boxes are first detected, and then individual keypoint heatmaps are generated for each detected person. The architecture processes an input image through DETR to detect person bounding boxes, crops and resizes the detected regions, and feeds them into a CNN keypoint head to produce 17 heatmaps corresponding to the COCO keypoints. The model is trained on 500 images from the COCO val2017 dataset that contain visible persons, with 17 annotated keypoints per person. The model files include trained PyTorch weights, a Colab notebook for training and inference, a configuration file, and a project description. For inference, the model can be loaded using PyTorch, and the DETR object detector can be used to extract person bounding boxes from an input image, which are then used to generate keypoint heatmaps. The model supports optional fine-tuning on custom datasets by converting the data to COCO format and using the provided notebook. The model is based on the DETR architecture for object detection and a custom CNN for keypoint estimation. The system is intended for use in applications requiring human pose estimation from images.

instruction: Which of these paragraphs provides the clearest explanation of the concept of blockchain?
description: This is a model finetuned using the Sentence Transformers library. It computes scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model is a Cross Encoder with a maximum sequence length of 1024 tokens and produces one output label. It is designed to work with multilingual text and is licensed under cc-by-nc-4.0. The model can be loaded using the Sentence Transformers library and supports tasks such as computing scores for text pairs and ranking texts based on similarity to a single text. It was trained on a dataset of 16,862 samples with query, document, and label columns, and evaluated on a dataset of 808 samples. The training used a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, a learning rate of 2e-05, and 2 training epochs with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained using bf16 precision. Evaluation results on the affiliation-val dataset show a mean average precision (map) of 0.9307, a mean reciprocal rank at 10 (mrr@10) of 0.9307, and a normalized discounted cumulative gain at 10 (ndcg@10) of 0.9502. The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer with a linear learning rate scheduler and a weight decay of 0.0. The training loss decreased from 0.577 at the first step to 0.2783 at step 1000, with a validation loss of 0.528 at the saved checkpoint. The model was developed using Python 3.12.12, Sentence Transformers 5.2.0, Transformers 4.57.3, PyTorch 2.9.1+cu128, Accelerate 1.12.0, Datasets 4.4.2, and Tokenizers 0.22.1.

instruction: I need to verify the authenticity of this voice message. Is there a way to tell if it's real or AI-made?
description: This is a fine-tuned Wav2Vec2 transformer model for binary audio classification, designed to distinguish between real human speech and AI-generated speech from text-to-speech and voice cloning software. The model is trained to detect audio generated by platforms including ElevenLabs, Amazon Polly, Hexgrad Kokoro, Hume AI, Speechify, and Luvvoice. It is based on a pre-trained Wav2Vec2-XLSR model that was already fine-tuned for deepfake audio detection, and it uses transfer learning with selective layer freezing. The feature extractor and bottom 12 transformer encoder layers are frozen, while the top 12 transformer encoder layers and a classification head are trained. The model has approximately 160 million trainable parameters. It is optimized for 2.5-13 second audio clips, resampled to 16kHz and converted to mono. The model outputs logits for two classes: real (human) audio and fake (AI-generated) audio, which can be converted to probabilities using softmax. The model achieves high performance on the validation set, with an accuracy of 97.9%, a ROC-AUC of 0.998, and a balanced accuracy of 97.9%. However, it is important to note that the high performance is due to the base model's existing deepfake detection capabilities and the similarity of the training data to the validation set. The model may not generalize well to novel TTS engines, advanced voice cloning systems, real-time voice manipulation, or low-quality recordings. The model is intended for use on audio files in WAV, MP3, or FLAC formats, and it is recommended to test it on TTS engines not included in the training data for true generalization assessment.

instruction: List all the proteins referenced in this pharmacology research summary.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for biomedical entity recognition, specifically for identifying proteins, DNA, RNA, cell lines, and cell types. It is optimized for high precision and is trained on the JNLPBA dataset, a curated corpus for biomedical named entity recognition. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem, enabling easy integration into production environments. It supports the classification of biomedical entities such as B-DNA, B-RNA, B-cell_line, B-cell_type, B-protein, I-DNA, I-RNA, I-cell_line, I-cell_type, and I-protein. The model is suitable for applications including drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It achieves an F1 score of 0.80, with a precision of 0.76, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.93. The JNLPBA dataset, derived from the GENIA corpus, contains annotations for five entity types and is widely used as a benchmark for evaluating biomedical NER systems. The model is based on the roberta-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It performs token classification for named entity recognition, taking tokenized biomedical text as input and producing BIO-tagged entity predictions as output. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: How would you estimate the distance between two trees in a park photo?
description: DA3 Large is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation, designed as a foundation model with a unified depth-ray representation. It is part of the Any-view Model series, contains 0.35B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture for visual geometry, using a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation. The model is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to perform without the need for complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images. Performance can also vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: What is the relationship between the text and the chart shown in this document?
description: InternVL3.5 is a new family of open-source multimodal models that significantly advances versatility, reasoning capability, and inference efficiency in the InternVL series. A key innovation is the Cascade Reinforcement Learning (Cascade RL) framework, which enhances reasoning through a two-stage process: offline RL for stable convergence and online RL for refined alignment. This coarse-to-fine training strategy leads to substantial improvements on downstream reasoning tasks, such as MMMU and MathVista. To optimize efficiency, the Visual Resolution Router (ViR) dynamically adjusts the resolution of visual tokens without compromising performance. Coupled with ViR, the Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates the vision encoder and language model across different GPUs, effectively balancing computational load. These contributions collectively enable InternVL3.5 to achieve up to a +16.0% gain in overall reasoning performance and a 4.05 × inference speedup compared to its predecessor, InternVL3. In addition, InternVL3.5 supports novel capabilities such as GUI interaction and embodied agency. The largest model, InternVL3.5-241B-A28B, attains state-of-the-art results among open-source MLLMs across general multimodal, reasoning, text, and agentic tasks, narrowing the performance gap with leading commercial models like GPT-5. InternVL3.5 follows the 'ViT–MLP–LLM' paradigm, initializing the language model using the Qwen3 series and GPT-OSS, and the vision encoder using InternViT-300M and InternViT-6B. The Dynamic High Resolution strategy from InternVL1.5 is also retained. The Flash version of InternVL3.5 further integrates ViR, enabling efficient variants suitable for resource-constrained scenarios. InternVL3.5-Flash compresses visual tokens down to 64 tokens per patch using a higher compression rate pixel shuffle module, determined by a patch router assessing semantic richness. This patch-aware compression mechanism reduces the number of visual tokens by 50% while maintaining nearly 100% of the performance of InternVL3.5. The training pipeline comprises four stages: Multimodal Continual Pre-Training (CPT), Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and Cascade Reinforcement Learning (CascadeRL). In CascadeRL, the model is first fine-tuned using Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO) under an offline RL setting, followed by GSPO under an online RL setting. For the Flash version, an additional training stage called Visual Consistency Learning (ViCO) is introduced to integrate ViR and reduce inference cost. InternVL3.5-Flash is the result of this process. The model supports a Thinking mode, which encourages step-by-step reasoning for complex problems, and a Best-of-N (BoN) strategy for parallel thinking, improving reasoning depth and breadth. The Decoupled Vision-Language Deployment (DvD) strategy separates vision and language processing, with the vision subsystem batching and processing images to produce compact feature embeddings, which are then transmitted to the language subsystem for fusion with the text context prior to decoding. This separation alleviates blocking and brings multimodal prefilling performance closer to that of pure language models. InternVL3.5 is evaluated on a wide range of tasks, including multimodal reasoning and mathematics, OCR, chart and document understanding, multi-image understanding, real-world comprehension, comprehensive multimodal understanding, multimodal hallucination evaluation, visual grounding, multimodal multilingual understanding, video understanding, GUI tasks, embodied tasks, and SVG tasks. The model is also evaluated on language capability. InternVL3.5 is available in various sizes, including 1B, 2B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 38B, 20B-A4B, 30B-A3B, and 241B-A28B, with both GitHub and HuggingFace formats. The model is publicly released under the Apache-2.0 License.

instruction: Could you generate a depth map for a desert landscape to help with terrain mapping?
description: DepthPro is a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation, designed to generate high-resolution depth maps with remarkable sharpness and fine-grained details. It employs a multi-scale Vision Transformer (ViT)-based architecture, where images are downsampled, divided into patches, and processed using a shared Dinov2 encoder. The extracted patch-level features are merged, upsampled, and refined using a DPT-like fusion stage, enabling precise depth estimation. The model synthesizes high-resolution depth maps with unparalleled sharpness and high-frequency details. The predictions are metric, with absolute scale, without relying on the availability of metadata such as camera intrinsics. The model is fast, producing a 2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a standard GPU. These characteristics are enabled by an efficient multi-scale vision transformer for dense prediction, a training protocol that combines real and synthetic datasets to achieve high metric accuracy alongside fine boundary tracing, dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in estimated depth maps, and state-of-the-art focal length estimation from a single image. The model was developed by Aleksei Bochkovskii, Amaël Delaunoy, Hugo Germain, Marcel Santos, Yichao Zhou, Stephan R. Richter, and Vladlen Koltun. It is licensed under the Apple-ASCL license. The model uses a DepthProEncoder for encoding the input image and a FeatureFusionStage for fusing the output features from the encoder. The DepthProEncoder further uses two encoders: a patch_encoder and an image_encoder. The input image is scaled with multiple ratios, split into smaller patches of size patch_size with overlapping areas, and processed by the patch_encoder. The input image is also rescaled to patch_size and processed by the image_encoder. Both encoders can be configured via patch_model_config and image_model_config, both of which are separate Dinov2Model by default. Outputs from both encoders (last_hidden_state) and selected intermediate states (hidden_states) from the patch_encoder are fused by a DPT-based FeatureFusionStage for depth estimation. The network is supplemented with a focal length estimation head. A small convolutional head ingests frozen features from the depth estimation network and task-specific features from a separate ViT image encoder to predict the horizontal angular field-of-view. Images are preprocessed by being rescaled by 1/225, normalized with mean=[0.5, 0.5, 0.5] and std=[0.5, 0.5, 0.5], and resized to 1536x1536 pixels.

instruction: Could you summarize the content of this educational video about the solar system?
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. Molmo2 models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, a collection of datasets with highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. It has state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models with a similar size. Molmo2-8B is based on a vision backbone and outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science. The Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2 (training code, evaluations, intermediate checkpoints) will be made available at a later date, furthering the commitment to open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. It is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Please review the sources to determine if this model is appropriate for your use case. Molmo2-8B achieves an average score of 63.1 on 15 academic benchmarks.

instruction: Which university has the highest acceptance rate among top-ranked institutions?
description: This model is a TAPAS medium model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_medium_reset` checkpoint, and the non-default version corresponds to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_medium`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step of intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings instead. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The model's performance on the development set for the medium size with reset position embeddings is 0.4324 accuracy. The model is intended for answering questions related to tables. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model, and this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: How has social media sentiment changed over the past week regarding $ORBX?
description: Orbital X ($ORBX) is a next-generation crypto token that integrates AI-driven insights to optimize and enhance the cryptocurrency experience. Built on a foundation of powerful, specially trained Large Language Models (LLMs), $ORBX aims to give investors and enthusiasts an edge in the fast-paced world of digital assets. Orbital X bridges the gap between complex data analysis and accessible insights, offering users interactive, personalized guidance, predictive analytics, and investment tools informed by real-time market data. At the heart of $ORBX is a highly trained LLM that processes large volumes of data, including market trends, historical price movements, and social sentiment, generating forecasts for asset volatility and identifying patterns and price indicators. The LLM continuously adapts to incoming information, offering data-driven predictions that factor in emerging patterns, sentiment shifts, and market dynamics. $ORBX’s LLM provides users with an interactive interface where they can ask investment-related questions in plain language. Whether users are looking to refine a trading strategy, understand a token’s performance, or gain insights on recent market trends, $ORBX’s AI responds with personalized, actionable advice. The AI model learns from user inputs and tailors responses to individual preferences, helping them make informed decisions with accurate, user-centered insights. $ORBX’s neural network scans vast amounts of social and market data, detecting shifts in sentiment and interpreting the impact on market behavior. This information enables the LLM to generate real-time analytics on market sentiment, helping investors stay ahead of emerging trends. By correlating news events, social sentiment, and market fluctuations, the LLM identifies major influences on $ORBX’s price and trading volume, delivering insights into the broader context of market changes. The $ORBX platform includes a 3D neural network visualization, allowing users to explore how AI interprets data, patterns, and predictions. The interactive display of neural connections simulates the token’s AI-driven decision-making, providing a clear view into the backend processing. Charts and graphs based on predictive models provide an easy-to-understand visualization of current and forecasted market conditions. The $ORBX AI is powered by a unique Large Language Model trained specifically for the financial and blockchain industries. $ORBX’s LLM is trained on financial data, blockchain transaction histories, social sentiment from platforms like Twitter, and relevant news sources, ensuring a holistic view of market influences. The LLM analyzes market data in real time, identifying trends and forecasting price shifts. It uses sophisticated neural network models to correlate social and market data with predictive power. Through its natural language interface, the LLM delivers custom insights, allowing users to ask questions and receive answers contextualized by recent data trends and personal investment history. The development of the core LLM and neural network for baseline prediction capabilities and sentiment analysis is part of the project’s roadmap, followed by the launch of an interactive dashboard, 3D neural network visualization, and AI-driven advisor. The project also plans to expand AI capabilities with enhanced NLP responses, improved predictive accuracy, and user-tailored insights. $ORBX - Orbital X is licensed under the MIT License.

instruction: How would you classify the objects present in this high-resolution image?
description: The vit_huge_plus_patch16_dinov3.lvd1689m is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model designed as an image feature encoder. It is distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is intended for tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It has 840.5 million parameters, 224.9 GMACs, and 193.6 million activations, with an input image size of 256 x 256. The model uses a ViT architecture and is trained using the DINOv3 method. The original model weights have all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some variants have `qkvb` in the name with `qkv_bias=True` but zero values to align with the `transformers` library and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.3 IN-ReaL, 90.0 IN-R, 78.6 Obj.Net, 64.5 Ox.-H, 54.8 ADE20k, 0.352 NYU, 79.3 DAVIS, 63.3 NAVI, and 56.3 SPair. It is also evaluated on GEO-Bench for satellite imagery tasks, including classification and segmentation, with metrics such as 73.0 m-BEnet, 96.5 m-brick-kiln, 94.1 m-eurosat, 60.6 m-forestnet, 96.0 m-pv4ger, 57.4 m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for classification, and 94.2 m-cashew, 75.6 m-chesapeake, 61.8 m-NeonTree, 83.7 m-nz-cattle, 95.2 m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for segmentation. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the ViT paper, and the PyTorch Image Models repository.

instruction: Can you transcribe this audio file and summarize the key points in English?
description: Voxtral Small 1.0 (24B) is an enhancement of Mistral Small 3, incorporating state-of-the-art audio input capabilities while retaining best-in-class text performance. It is designed to excel at speech transcription, translation, and audio understanding. Voxtral can operate in a pure speech transcription mode to maximize performance, automatically predicting the source audio language and transcribing the text accordingly. It supports long-form context with a 32k token context length, handling audios up to 30 minutes for transcription or 40 minutes for understanding. The model supports built-in Q&A and summarization, allowing users to ask questions directly through audio and generate structured summaries without the need for separate ASR and language models. It is natively multilingual, with automatic language detection and state-of-the-art performance in widely used languages including English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Hindi, German, Dutch, and Italian. Voxtral enables function-calling straight from voice, allowing direct triggering of backend functions, workflows, or API calls based on spoken user intents. It retains the text understanding capabilities of its language model backbone, Mistral Small 3.1. The model can be used with frameworks such as vLLM and Transformers. For vLLM, it is recommended to use version 0.10.0 or higher. The model supports multiple audios per message, multiple user turns with audio, and function calling, though system prompts are not yet supported. Running Voxtral-Small-24B-2507 on GPU requires approximately 55 GB of GPU RAM in bf16 or fp16. The model can be used for audio instruct, transcription, and function calling. It supports text-only, audio-only, and multi-modal inputs, as well as batched inference. The model can be run in a server/client setting using vLLM or natively with Transformers version 4.54.0 or higher. It requires mistral-common version 1.8.1 or higher with audio dependencies installed.

instruction: How can I turn a series of photos into a 3D animation?
description: AnySplat is a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting model designed to process input from unconstrained views. It is intended to reconstruct 3D scenes from a set of 2D images by inferring camera poses and generating Gaussian representations of the scene. The model supports inference tasks, including the estimation of extrinsic and intrinsic camera parameters and the generation of interpolated video outputs based on the reconstructed 3D data. The architecture is implemented in Python using PyTorch and is designed to be used in a pipeline that includes image preprocessing, model inference, and post-processing for video output. The model is trained to handle multiple input images, which are processed and stacked into a tensor for inference. During inference, the model outputs Gaussian representations of the scene along with predicted camera poses. The model is evaluated using standard practices in 3D reconstruction and is intended for use in applications requiring 3D scene understanding from arbitrary camera views. The model is provided with a license, and users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper for any use of the model or its outputs.

instruction: Could you determine the acid dissociation constant for the SMILES 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)C(=O)OC'? I need this for a dissolution study.
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the acid dissociation constant (pKa) for acidic groups. This property predicts the pH at which acidic functional groups donate protons, affecting ionization state and bioavailability. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the pKa_acidic dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What are the top three articles that best explain the impact of climate change on coastal cities?
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-32m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 32 million parameters and a projection dimension of 64. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. Evaluation results on the BEIR benchmark show that it achieves an average score of 0.521, with strong performance on tasks such as MS MARCO (0.450), SciFact (0.740), and TREC-COVID (0.775). On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.849 for documents up to 32,000 tokens. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the associated tech report, and if its projection heads are specifically discussed, an additional report on projection variants should also be cited. Additionally, if PyLate is used in conjunction with the model, it should be cited separately.

instruction: Can you track the movement of a bouncing ball in this video using a text description?
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images and videos, as well as promptable visual segmentation (PVS) for images and videos. For image PCS, the model takes text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returns segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It also provides semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. For video PCS, the model detects and tracks all matching object instances across video frames using text prompts. It supports both pre-loaded and streaming video inference, with the latter being suitable for real-time applications. However, streaming inference may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks compared to pre-loaded inference. For image PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It is an updated version of SAM2 that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. For video PVS, the model tracks a specific object instance per prompt across video frames using interactive visual prompts. It is an updated version of SAM2 Video that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. The model supports batched inference with text prompts and mixed prompts for different images in the same batch. It also supports multi-object video tracking, allowing multiple objects to be tracked simultaneously across video frames. The model can be used with the 🤗 Transformers library and provides various helper functions for post-processing results, such as overlaying masks on images.

instruction: Is it possible to produce a 44.1kHz audio file with a natural-sounding anime-style voice?
description: Anime-XCodec2-44.1kHz is a fine-tuned variant of NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2, designed to produce 44.1kHz high-fidelity Japanese speech in an anime/game-style. It incorporates upsampling layers and RMS loss inspired by the Inworld TTS-1 paper. The model was trained on approximately 22,000 hours of Japanese speech data. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, ensuring speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for systems already using XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model accepts 16kHz input for encoding and outputs 44.1kHz audio. It is intended for decoding XCodec2 speech tokens into high-fidelity Japanese speech, upgrading existing Anime-XCodec2 pipelines to 44.1kHz output, and potentially for audio super-resolution, though this use case is untested. The model modifies the original XCodec2 architecture by adding upsampler blocks and requires a custom xcodec2 library for inference, as the standard library will not work. The model is optimized for Japanese and anime/game-style voices, and it is not compatible with the original xcodec2 library. The training procedure involved integrating an UpSamplerBlock into the decoder with parameters hop_length=147, upsample_factors=[3, 2], and kernel_sizes=[7, 6]. RMS Loss was adopted in addition to the original losses. The encoder and codebook were frozen, while the generator's backbone, head, upsampler, and fc_post_a were fine-tuned. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, inherited from XCodec2 and Anime-XCodec2. It acknowledges the original HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 model and Inworld AI for their contributions to the upsampler architecture and RMS Loss.

instruction: Could you generate a version of this photo with a vintage film filter applied?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of an image-editing model based on advances in complex text rendering. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is an image-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is quantized from an unspecified base model. The data type for the model is INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). Two rank configurations are available: r32 for faster inference and r128 for better quality but slower inference. Standard inference speed models are provided for general use. Additionally, 4-step distilled models are available, fused with unspecified components using LoRA strength = 1.0. These include models with data types INT4 and NVFP4, and ranks r32 and r128. Similarly, 8-step distilled models are available, also fused with unspecified components using LoRA strength = 1.0. The model sources include an unspecified inference engine, quantization library, paper, and demo. The model is described in the paper titled 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models,' presented at The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025.

instruction: I have an image of a Chinese invoice—can you read the text for me?
description: POINTS-Reader is a vision-language model designed for end-to-end document conversion. It is a streamlined model that follows the structure of POINTS1.5, with the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct replaced by Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct. The input to POINTS-Reader consists of a fixed prompt and a document image, and the output is a string of text extracted from the document image, delivered directly to the user without any post-processing. The model supports extraction from both Chinese and English documents, achieving scores of 0.133 for English and 0.212 for Chinese on OmniDocBench. It is optimized for high throughput by using a ViT with a moderate number of parameters (600M NaViT) and supports deployment with SGLang. The model's development includes a two-stage data augmentation strategy: the first stage uses automated data to provide basic document extraction capabilities, and the second stage employs continuous self-evolution to improve the quality of generated data. POINTS-Reader has been accepted for presentation at the EMNLP 2025 Main Conference and is available on Hugging Face Spaces. It can struggle with complex layouts, such as newspapers, and may produce repeated or missing content. It also has difficulty handling handwritten inputs, such as receipts or notes, which can lead to recognition errors or omissions. Currently, it supports only English and Chinese, limiting its effectiveness on other languages.

instruction: List the organisms or species referenced in this comparative oncology study.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for high-precision biomedical entity recognition in the domain of cancer genetics. It is optimized for identifying and extracting cancer-related genetic entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports a wide range of biomedical entity types, including but not limited to amino acids, anatomical systems, cancers, cells, cellular components, developing anatomical structures, genes or gene products, immaterial anatomical entities, multi-tissue structures, organs, organisms, organism subdivisions, organism substances, pathological formations, simple chemicals, and tissues. Both B- and I- tags are supported for these entity types. The model is trained on the BIONLP2013_CG dataset, a specialized corpus for cancer genetics entities and gene regulation in oncology research. It is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.79, precision of 0.79, recall of 0.80, and accuracy of 0.89. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is production-ready and validated on clinical benchmarks, and it is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Please create a visual summary of this image based on the extracted features.
description: This is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed for image feature extraction and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer architecture with a patch size of 16. The model has 21.6 million parameters, 6.3 GMACs, and 17.0 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`), but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models keep RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization. This results in some numerical differences, but the `timm` approach is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well, if not slightly better, for fine-tuning. To match the original model outputs, the `model.rope.periods` can be converted to `bfloat16` and then to `float32`. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 87.0% on IN-ReaL, 60.4% on IN-R, 50.9% on Obj.Net, 49.5% on Ox.-H, 47.0% on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7% on DAVIS, 56.3% on NAVI, and 50.4% on SPair. The model is also evaluated on satellite datasets such as SAT-493M, with performance metrics including 73.0% on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and a mean of 79.6%. For segmentation tasks, the model achieves 94.2% on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and a mean of 74.5%. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, as well as the papers on Vision Transformers for image recognition at scale and PyTorch Image Models.

instruction: Can you convert this scanned form into a clean Markdown format while preserving the table structure?
description: NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking is the first reasoning OCR vision-language model (VLM) designed to convert documents into clean Markdown files, particularly suitable for RAG applications. It generates thinking tokens to analyze the layout of a document before producing the final Markdown output. The model excels at handling documents with unusual layouts and complex tables. The number of thinking tokens can range from 20% to 500% of the final answer, depending on the complexity of the task. It is a fine-tune of Qwen 2.5-VL-7B, trained on synthetic examples of documents followed by a reinforcement learning phase (GRPO) using a layout-centric reward. NuMarkdown-8B-Thinking outperforms generic non-reasoning models like GPT-4o and specialized OCR models like OCRFlux, and is competitive with large reasoning closed-source models like Gemini 2.5. In an arena ranking using the trueskill-2 system with approximately 500 anonymized votes, it ranks second with a μ of 26.10 and σ of 0.79, just behind gemini-flash-reasoning. The model is particularly effective for complex document-to-markdown tasks and is intended for use in scenarios requiring structured document conversion. A planned markdown arena will provide a tool for evaluating different solutions in this domain. The training process includes a single epoch of supervised fine-tuning on synthetic reasoning traces from public PDFs, followed by a reinforcement learning phase using a layout-centric reward with challenging image examples. The model is designed to handle headers, bullet points, tables with merged cells, footers, and multi-line content within cells, ensuring accurate and well-structured Markdown output. It is intended for use in educational and administrative settings where document conversion is required, such as for student and family information sheets. The model supports both vLLM and Hugging Face Transformers for deployment, with specific instructions for serving and inference provided.

instruction: What's the best way to generate high-quality images of a room from just one photo?
description: This is a float16 conversion of the SHARP model for use with Swift/MLX implementations. SHARP is an approach to photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. Given a single photograph, SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of the depicted scene. This is done in less than a second on a standard GPU via a single feedforward pass through a neural network. The 3D Gaussian representation produced by SHARP can then be rendered in real time, yielding high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The representation is metric, with absolute scale, supporting metric camera movements. Experimental results demonstrate that SHARP delivers robust zero-shot generalization across datasets. It sets a new state of the art on multiple datasets, reducing LPIPS by 25–34% and DISTS by 21–43% versus the best prior model, while lowering the synthesis time by three orders of magnitude. The results are 3D Gaussian splats (3DGS) in the output folder. The 3DGS .ply files are compatible with various public 3DGS renderers. The model follows the OpenCV coordinate convention (x right, y down, z forward). The 3DGS scene center is roughly at (0, 0, +z). When dealing with third-party renderers, please scale and rotate to re-center the scene accordingly. Additionally, you can render videos with a camera trajectory. While the Gaussian prediction works for all CPU, CUDA, and MPS, rendering videos via the --render option currently requires a CUDA GPU. The gsplat renderer takes a while to initialize at the first launch.

instruction: I need help drafting a persuasive letter to my local representative about climate change.
description: Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is a next-generation foundation model designed to support increasingly powerful and agentic artificial intelligence through innovative model architecture. It is the first installment in the Qwen3-Next series and features a hybrid attention mechanism that combines Gated DeltaNet and Gated Attention to enable efficient context modeling for ultra-long context lengths. The model also incorporates a high-sparsity Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design, which achieves an extreme low activation ratio in MoE layers, drastically reducing FLOPs per token while preserving model capacity. Additional stability optimizations include zero-centered and weight-decayed layernorm, and other enhancements for robust pre-training and post-training. The model also supports Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), which boosts pretraining performance and accelerates inference. Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is a causal language model with 80B total parameters and 3B activated parameters, trained on 15T tokens. It has 48 layers with a hybrid layout of 12 repetitions of (3 repetitions of (Gated DeltaNet -> MoE) -> 1 repetition of (Gated Attention -> MoE)). The Gated Attention component has 16 Q heads and 2 KV heads, with a head dimension of 256 and a rotary position embedding dimension of 64. The Gated DeltaNet has 32 V heads and 16 QK heads, with a head dimension of 128. The MoE component includes 512 experts, with 10 activated experts and 1 shared expert, and an expert intermediate dimension of 512. The model natively supports a context length of 262,144 tokens and is extensible up to 1,010,000 tokens using RoPE scaling techniques. Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct outperforms Qwen3-32B-Base on downstream tasks with 10% of the training cost and 10 times the inference throughput for contexts over 32K tokens. It performs on par with Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507 on certain benchmarks while demonstrating significant advantages in handling ultra-long-context tasks up to 256K tokens. The model supports instruct (non-thinking) mode and does not generate code blocks in its output. It is recommended to use dedicated inference frameworks such as SGLang or vLLM for optimal efficiency and throughput. The model is capable of handling a wide range of tasks, including knowledge-based reasoning, coding, alignment, agent-based interactions, and multilingualism. For ultra-long text processing, the model can be configured with YaRN to handle context lengths up to 1 million tokens. Best practices for using the model include setting sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, using an output length of 16,384 tokens for most queries, and standardizing output formats for benchmarking. The model is available through Hugging Face Transformers and can be deployed using SGLang or vLLM to create an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. It is important to note that the efficiency or throughput improvement depends highly on the implementation, and the use of a dedicated inference framework is recommended. Additionally, the default context length is 256K, and it may be necessary to reduce the context length if the server fails to start. The model is also recommended for agentic use with Qwen-Agent, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity.

instruction: How emotionally charged is the speaker in this clip?
description: This model implements dimensional emotion classification as described in the paper 'Vox-Profile: A Speech Foundation Model Benchmark for Characterizing Diverse Speaker and Speech Traits.' The training pipeline is the top-performing solution (SAILER) in the INTERSPEECH 2025—Speech Emotion Challenge. Unlike the official challenge submission, this system does not use transcripts and is designed as a speech-only system to maintain simplicity while ensuring effectiveness. The model is trained on the MSP-Podcast dataset and is capable of predicting three emotional dimensions: Arousal (ranging from 0 for calm to 1 for active), Valence (ranging from 0 for negative to 1 for positive), and Dominance (ranging from 0 for weak to 1 for strong). The model may be sensitive to content information when making emotion predictions, which can be a beneficial feature for classifying emotions from online content. The model is based on the WavLM architecture and is available through Huggingface under the identifier 'tiantiaf/wavlm-large-msp-podcast-emotion-dim.' It is recommended to use audio inputs that are no longer than 15 seconds, sampled at 16kHz, and in mono channel format. The model is released under the Open RAIL license, and users are expected to respect the privacy and consent of data subjects and comply with relevant laws and regulations. The model is not intended for clinical or diagnostic applications, surveillance, privacy-invasive applications, or commercial use.

instruction: Modify the video so the character is holding a large umbrella and walking in the rain.
description: Lucy Edit Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of editing tasks, including clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. The model is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, which includes a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, allowing for easy adaptation of existing scripts and workflows. It is designed to preserve motion and composition perfectly, enabling precise edits. Compared to common inference-time methods, Lucy Edit Dev offers more reliable editing results. It supports changes to wardrobe and accessories, such as adding glasses, earrings, or hats. It can also perform character changes, such as replacing a person with a monster, animal, or known character. Additionally, it supports scene transformations, such as changing a real-world scene into a 2D cartoon. The model operates using pure text instructions, requiring no finetuning or masks for common edits. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of the Wan2.2 5B architecture. Lucy Edit Dev is the first open-source instruction-guided video editing model. It is designed to be used with descriptive text prompts, with best results achieved using prompts of around 20–30 words. Trigger words such as 'change,' 'add,' 'replace,' and 'transform to' help the model understand the user's intent. The model excels at clothing changes, human or character replacement, and object replacement, with strong results for structure-preserving swaps. Color changes have mixed reliability and work best with precise descriptions. Adding objects is most effective for wearable or handheld props. Global transformations can alter the environment or style but may also affect the subject's identity. Lucy Edit Dev is particularly strong in identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and adherence to prompts. Longer, detailed prompts improve results, and 81-frame generations provide better temporal consistency than shorter clips. The model is available for use with the diffusers library and is integrated with the LucyEditPipeline. It is also available via a hosted API. Lucy Edit Dev is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges the contributions of the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face. The model was first released on 2025-09-18, with a diffusers integration merged on 2025-09-16. Future plans include local inference ComfyUI nodes, LoRA and fine-tuning scripts, and additional technical documentation.

instruction: What might a visual assistant predict about the movement of a bouncing ball?
description: KAIROS-MM-Qwen2.5-VL-7B-SFT is a multimodal reasoning model based on Qwen2.5-VL, designed to enable robots and vision AI agents to reason about the real world using physics understanding, prior knowledge, and common sense. The model understands space, time, and fundamental physical principles, and is well suited for planning, decision making, and long horizon video reasoning in embodied and agentic systems. It serves as a planning and reasoning backbone for embodied agents, allowing them to infer what actions to take next based on visual observations, temporal context, and physical constraints.

instruction: Identify and tag the species names in this biomedical passage.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying species names from the Species-800 dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated SPECIES800 dataset, which contains 800 manually annotated abstracts for organism mentions. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the distilroberta-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It has an F1 score of 0.72, precision of 0.72, recall of 0.73, and accuracy of 0.95. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research settings. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with different aggregation strategies for entity prediction, including `none`, `simple`, `first`, `average`, and `max`. For large-scale processing, it supports batch and dataset-level operations with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities.

instruction: How accurate is it to determine gender from this voice recording?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed for gender classification and achieves a loss of 0.1142 and an accuracy of 0.9639 on the evaluation set. The model was trained for three epochs using a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 256, and an evaluation batch size of 256. The random seed used was 42. The optimizer used is ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED with betas of (0.9, 0.999), an epsilon of 1e-08, and no additional optimizer arguments. The learning rate scheduler type is linear with a warmup ratio of 0.05. During training, the training loss decreased from 0.2321 to 0.1002 over 400 training steps, while the validation loss stabilized at 0.1142. The accuracy on the validation set increased from 0.9434 to 0.9658 and then stabilized at 0.9639. The model was developed using Transformers 4.57.3, Pytorch 2.9.0+cu126, Datasets 4.4.2, and Tokenizers 0.22.1.

instruction: Can you tell me which of these two descriptions of a feature is more compelling for potential users?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is based on the Bradley-Terry model, with improvements in training data scale and quality to achieve superior performance. Compared to the first generation of Skywork-Reward, the Skywork-Reward-V2 series includes the following major improvements: it is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, consisting of 26 million preference pairs curated via a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. The series includes eight models across multiple sizes, with the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, nearly matching the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2. The largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The models are available in various sizes, including Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-1B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-1.7B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-4B, and Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series outperforms models in both Bradley-Terry and Generative categories with much smaller model sizes. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and inference should be performed within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M model is considered experimental and is trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The model repository, including the model weights and code, is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for models derived from Qwen3, and under the Meta License for models derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct. If you have any questions, you can contact the authors at yuhao.liuu at kunlun-inc dot com and liang.zeng at kunlun-inc dot com. If you find the work useful, please cite it as follows: @article{liu2025skywork, title={Skywork-Reward-V2: Scaling Preference Data Curation via Human-AI Synergy}, author = {Liu, Chris Yuhao and Zeng, Liang and Xiao, Yuzhen and He, Jujie and Liu, Jiacai and Wang, Chaojie and Yan, Rui and Shen, Wei and Zhang, Fuxiang and Xu, Jiacheng and Liu, Yang and Zhou, Yahui}, journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.01352}, year={2025}}

instruction: Make a video of a person painting a landscape with a brush, slowly bringing the scene to life.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) generation. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 is trained on significantly larger data, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The model includes a 5B version with a 16×16×4 compression ratio, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and is capable of running on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at 480P and 720P resolutions. It delivers outstanding video generation quality and surpasses leading commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: I have a dataset of customer support tickets. Can you help me categorize them based on the issues mentioned?
description: Sarashina-Embedding-v2-1B is a Japanese text embedding model based on a Japanese large language model. It was trained using multi-stage contrastive learning and achieved a state-of-the-art average score across 28 datasets in the Japanese Massive Text Embedding Benchmark as of July 28, 2025. The model maps sentences and paragraphs into a 1792-dimensional dense vector space and supports applications such as semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, and clustering. It is a Sentence Transformer model with a maximum sequence length of 8,192 tokens and uses cosine similarity as the similarity function. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with LlamaModel and a Pooling layer that uses the last token for pooling. The model is designed to work with Japanese text. For usage, it can be loaded using the sentence-transformers library version 4.0.2. To enhance performance, the model was created through a three-stage learning process: first, weakly-supervised learning using contrastive learning with web-crawled and open datasets; second, supervised fine-tuning with higher-quality data and multiple model variations; and third, model merging by linearly combining the weights of the top-performing models from the second stage. Evaluation results as of July 28, 2025, show an average score of 76.38 across all tasks, with 76.48 in Retrieval, 84.22 in STS, 77.14 in Classification, 86.28 in Reranking, and 52.56 in Clustering. The model is licensed under an unspecified license, and commercial use requires contacting the developers.

description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities, leveraging data and model sizes at scale. The model is intended to represent any video (and image) to perform video classification, retrieval, or as a video encoder for vision-language models. V-JEPA 2 is designed to process videos by sampling a specific number of frames, with this model using 64 frames. For image processing, the image is copied to the desired number of frames. The model supports loading and processing video and image data using the Hugging Face Transformers library. Performance and evaluation results are not explicitly detailed in the provided text. The model is associated with the technical report titled 'V-JEPA~2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning' by Assran et al., published in 2025 by FAIR at Meta.

instruction: How can I automatically detect if a document is upside down or sideways?
description: The Document Image Orientation Classification Module is designed to identify the orientation of document images and correct them through post-processing. It is used to determine the orientation of documents or IDs containing text regions before OCR processing, which helps improve OCR accuracy. The model is based on PP-LCNet_x1_0 and classifies document images into four orientation categories: 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270°. It achieves an average recognition accuracy of 99.06% and has a model storage size of 7 MB. The model can be used as part of a larger pipeline that includes document orientation classification and geometric distortion correction. The pipeline supports document preprocessing for OCR, digital document management, and scenarios requiring improved document image quality. It offers flexible deployment options across multiple hardware platforms and supports secondary development through fine-tuning on custom datasets. The model can be integrated into projects using the PaddleOCR library and is compatible with both command-line and programmatic usage. The model is optional within the pipeline and can be enabled or disabled as needed.

instruction: Produce a series of frames that show a castle under siege, with the battle intensifying and the environment reacting to the chaos.
description: next-scene-qwen-image-lora-2509 is a LoRA adapter fine-tuned on Qwen-Image-Edit (build 2509), designed to generate cinematic image sequences with natural visual progression from frame to frame. This model enables Qwen Image Edit to understand camera dynamics, visual composition, and narrative continuity, creating shots that flow seamlessly into one another. It is trained on a large-scale proprietary cinematic dataset and is intended to advance the story by preserving spatial relationships, lighting consistency, and emotional resonance across sequential frames. The model supports tasks such as storyboard generation for film and animation pre-production, cinematic AI video pipelines requiring frame-to-frame coherence, sequential narrative workflows, concept art evolution, and visual storytelling for creative projects. It is not optimized for static portraits, single-image illustration tasks, or non-sequential edits, and is best suited for multi-frame workflows with narrative progression. The model is based on the Qwen-Image-Edit (build 2509) architecture and is compatible with platforms such as ComfyUI and Automatic1111 (with Qwen support). A recommended LoRA strength is 0.7–0.8. The model is available in two versions: V1 (`next-scene_lora_v1-3000.safetensors`) and V2 (`next-scene_lora_v2-3000.safetensors`), with V2 trained on higher quality data for improved results, better command responsiveness, and fixed black bar artifacts. The model is released under the MIT License for research, educational, and creative use, with commercial applications requiring independent testing and proper attribution.

instruction: Which of these candidate answers seems most accurate for the question about historical events?
description: This is a model finetuned using the Sentence Transformers library. It computes scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model is a Cross Encoder with a maximum sequence length of 1024 tokens and produces one output label. It is designed to work with multilingual text and is licensed under cc-by-nc-4.0. The model can be loaded using the Sentence Transformers library and supports tasks such as computing scores for text pairs and ranking texts based on similarity to a single text. It was trained on a dataset of 16,862 samples with query, document, and label columns, and evaluated on a dataset of 808 samples. The training used a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, a learning rate of 2e-05, and 2 training epochs with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The model was trained using bf16 precision. Evaluation results on the affiliation-val dataset show a mean average precision (map) of 0.9307, a mean reciprocal rank at 10 (mrr@10) of 0.9307, and a normalized discounted cumulative gain at 10 (ndcg@10) of 0.9502. The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer with a linear learning rate scheduler and a weight decay of 0.0. The training loss decreased from 0.577 at the first step to 0.2783 at step 1000, with a validation loss of 0.528 at the saved checkpoint. The model was developed using Python 3.12.12, Sentence Transformers 5.2.0, Transformers 4.57.3, PyTorch 2.9.1+cu128, Accelerate 1.12.0, Datasets 4.4.2, and Tokenizers 0.22.1.

instruction: What is the likely setting or location shown in this photograph?
description: MobileCLIP2-S3-OpenCLIP is a variant of the MobileCLIP2 model, adapted from the original Apple model and configured using OpenCLIP settings and weight naming conventions. MobileCLIP2 was introduced in a publication featured in TMLR August 2025 by Fartash Faghri, Pavan Kumar Anasosalu Vasu, Cem Koc, Vaishaal Shankar, Alexander T Toshev, Oncel Tuzel, and Hadi Pouransari. The MobileCLIP2-S3 checkpoint is included in this repository. MobileCLIP2-S4 matches the accuracy of SigLIP-SO400M/14 with 2x fewer parameters and surpasses DFN ViT-L/14 at 2.5x lower latency measured on iPhone12 Pro Max. MobileCLIP-S3 and S4 are new architectures trained on the MobileCLIP training dataset, DataCompDR-1B. The smallest variant, MobileCLIP-S0, achieves similar zero-shot performance as ViT-B/16 while being 4.8x faster and 2.8x smaller. MobileCLIP-S2 achieves better average zero-shot performance than ViT-B/16 while being 2.3x faster and 2.1x smaller, and was trained with 3x fewer seen samples. MobileCLIP-B (LT) attains a zero-shot ImageNet performance of 77.2%, which is significantly better than recent works with similar architectures or even others. The model is designed for tasks involving image and text processing, including zero-shot classification and cross-modal feature encoding. It is based on a vision-language architecture and is optimized for performance on mobile devices. The model's performance is evaluated on the ImageNet-1k dataset and 38 other datasets, with results including zero-shot top-1 accuracy and average performance metrics. The model supports inference and model exporting, with optional reparameterization for improved performance. It uses a tokenizer and preprocessing steps for image and text inputs, and outputs label probabilities based on encoded features.

instruction: What are the most prominent features in this satellite image of a city?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification.
