For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours used for ViT-7B model training, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Could you locate documents that compare different approaches to renewable energy policy?
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-17m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 17 million parameters and a projection dimension of 48. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. On the BEIR benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.490, with specific scores of 0.416 on MS MARCO, 0.719 on SciFact, 0.316 on Touche, 0.326 on FiQA, 0.713 on TREC-COVID, 0.551 on NQ, and 0.410 on DBPedia. On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.847 for 32k tokens and 0.776 for 4k tokens. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the provided BibTeX entries for the associated tech report, projection variants report, and PyLate library.

instruction: Can you predict the category for these new entries using the data patterns you've observed?
description: Mitra classifier is a tabular foundation model that is pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random classifiers. It is based on a 12-layer Transformer with 72 million parameters, pre-trained by incorporating an in-context learning paradigm. The model is designed for use in tabular data classification tasks and supports both inference and fine-tuning. For inference, the model can be used without fine-tuning, and for fine-tuning, a specified number of training steps can be applied. The model is implemented within the AutoGluon.tabular framework and can be trained and evaluated using standard machine learning workflows, including train/test splits and performance evaluation. The model is licensed under the Apache-2.0 License. The model's development and methodology are described in the research paper titled 'Mitra: Mixed synthetic priors for enhancing tabular foundation models' by Zhang et al., published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: What is the most relevant classification for this Greek legal case summary?
description: GEM-ELECTRA Legal is an improved ELECTRA-base model pre-trained from scratch on a 17GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. This second version includes refined training hyperparameters for enhanced performance and stability. It is designed to understand the complex vocabulary and context of the legal domain in Greece and the European Union. The model was developed as part of a research project and optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering within the legal field. The ELECTRA architecture enables more efficient pre-training compared to masked language models like BERT by using a generator-discriminator approach. The training data was compiled from various legal and governmental sources, including the FEK - Greek Government Gazette (11.0 GB), Greek Parliament Proceedings (2.9 GB), Political Reports of the Supreme Court (1.2 GB), Eur-Lex (Greek Content, 0.92 GB), Europarl (Greek Content, 0.38 GB), and Raptarchis Legal Dictionary (0.35 GB), totaling approximately 16.75 GB. The text was tokenized using a custom ByteLevelBPE tokenizer trained from scratch on the Greek legal corpus, with an uncased vocabulary of 50,264 tokens. The data was processed into fixed-size chunks of 512 tokens, respecting document boundaries to ensure contextual coherence. The model was pre-trained for 200,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs using BFloat16 mixed-precision. Key hyperparameters included a learning rate of 1e-4 with a linear warmup of 12,000 steps, an effective batch size of 3,840, AdamW optimizer with beta1=0.9, beta2=0.98, and epsilon=1e-6, weight decay of 0.01, max sequence length of 512, and equal loss weights for the generator and discriminator of 50.0. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.0056 and a final evaluation loss of 0.0054.

instruction: Can you help me find the most relevant image that matches this product description?
description: The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-8B is a multimodal reranking model designed for cross-modal understanding and information retrieval. It is part of the Qwen3-VL model series, which includes the Qwen3-VL-Embedding and Qwen3-VL-Reranker models. These models are built upon the Qwen3-VL foundation and are specifically engineered to handle diverse input modalities, including text, images, screenshots, and videos, as well as combinations of these. The Embedding model generates high-dimensional vectors for tasks like retrieval and clustering, while the Reranker model refines these results by taking a (query, document) pair as input and outputting a precise relevance score. The two models are typically used together in a two-stage pipeline, where the Embedding model performs initial recall and the Reranker improves retrieval accuracy. The Qwen3-VL-Reranker-8B supports over 30 languages and is capable of processing inputs with arbitrary single or mixed modalities. It has 8 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and supports quantization, multilingual representation learning (MRL), and instruction-aware customization. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-VL-Reranker-8B outperforms baseline models and the base embedding model across multiple retrieval tasks, including image-text retrieval, video-text matching, and visual document retrieval. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for specific tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance. The model is designed for integration into existing pipelines and supports flexible vector dimensions and quantized embeddings for practical deployment.

instruction: What should I do if the processing is too slow on my machine?
description: MelBandRoformer-GGUF is a repository containing Mel-Band-Roformer models converted to the GGUF format, enabling high-performance, cross-platform local inference using a specific inference engine. The repository provides GGUF quantized versions of original models, including a model referred to as (voc_fv6). Various quantized versions are available to meet different hardware requirements. The q8_0 quantization type is recommended, offering 8-bit quantization that significantly reduces memory usage and bandwidth requirements while maintaining audio quality almost identical to FP32. The fp16 quantization type provides 16-bit floating point precision, suitable for scenarios requiring maximum precision. Lower-bit quantization types, including q4_0, q4_1, q5_0, and q5_1, are available for devices with low VRAM or RAM. Norm and Bias weights remain in FP32 to ensure numerical stability. The model is intended to be used with a specific command-line tool. Users are instructed to download the executable for their system or compile it themselves, download the desired .gguf file from the repository, and run inference using the provided command-line interface. Basic usage involves specifying the model file, input audio file, and output audio file. Advanced usage allows for adjusting chunk size and overlap to optimize quality.

instruction: Can you identify the DNA-related terms in this medical research abstract?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for biomedical entity recognition, specifically for identifying proteins, DNA, RNA, cell lines, and cell types. It is optimized for high precision and is trained on the JNLPBA dataset, a curated corpus for biomedical named entity recognition. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem, enabling easy integration into various applications. It supports the classification of biomedical entities such as B-DNA, B-RNA, B-cell_line, B-cell_type, B-protein, I-DNA, I-RNA, I-cell_line, I-cell_type, and I-protein. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.81, with a precision of 0.75, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.93. It is suitable for applications including drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the deberta-v3-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: I have a mono audio file—can you assign speaker IDs to each segment?
description: This model is a streaming version of the Sortformer diarizer, designed for speaker diarization in conversations. It is a novel end-to-end neural model trained with unconventional objectives compared to existing end-to-end diarization models. The model employs an Arrival-Order Speaker Cache (AOSC) to store frame-level acoustic embeddings of previously observed speakers. Sortformer resolves the permutation problem in diarization by following the arrival-time order of the speech segments from each speaker. The model can be used to recognize speakers in conversations. The streaming version of Sortformer employs a pre-encode layer in the Fast-Conformer to generate a speaker-cache, which is filtered at each step to retain only high-quality speaker cache vectors. Aside from the speaker-cache management part, the streaming Sortformer follows the architecture of the offline version of Sortformer. The offline version consists of an L-size (17 layers) encoder based on a Fast-Conformer, followed by an 18-layer Transformer encoder with a hidden size of 192, and two feedforward layers with 4 sigmoid outputs for each frame input at the top layer. The model is available for use in the NeMo Framework and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or for fine-tuning on another dataset. The model accepts single-channel (mono) audio sampled at 16,000 Hz. The input tensor is a Ns x 1 matrix for each audio clip, where Ns is the number of samples in the time-series signal. The output of the model is an T x S matrix, where S is the maximum number of speakers (in this model, S = 4), and T is the total number of frames, including zero-padding. Each frame corresponds to a segment of 0.08 seconds of audio. Each element of the T x S matrix represents the speaker activity probability in the [0, 1] range. The model operates in a streaming mode (online mode) and can detect a maximum of 4 speakers; performance degrades on recordings with 5 and more speakers. While the model is designed for long-form audio and can handle recordings that are several hours long, performance may degrade on very long recordings. The model was trained on publicly available speech datasets, primarily in English, and performance may degrade on non-English speech and on out-of-domain data, such as recordings in noisy conditions. The model was trained on a combination of 2445 hours of real conversations and 5150 hours of simulated audio mixtures generated by a specific method. The datasets include Fisher English (LDC), AMI Meeting Corpus, VoxConverse-v0.3, ICSI, AISHELL-4, Third DIHARD Challenge Development (LDC), 2000 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation, split1 (LDC), DiPCo, AliMeeting, 2004-2010 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation (LDC), and Librispeech. The model was trained on 8 nodes of 8×NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs using 90-second long training samples and a batch size of 4. The model can be trained using specific tools and scripts. Inference can be performed with post-processing algorithms using inference tools. The model's performance is evaluated using the Diarization Error Rate (DER) on various datasets, including DIHARD III Eval, CALLHOME-part2, and CH109. Evaluation results show varying DER values depending on the latency and whether post-processing is applied. The model is deployed via NVIDIA RIVA ASR, an accelerated speech AI SDK deployable on-prem, in all clouds, multi-cloud, hybrid, on edge, and embedded. Riva provides world-class out-of-the-box accuracy for the most common languages, best-in-class accuracy with run-time word boosting, and customization of acoustic model, language model, and inverse text normalization. It also provides streaming speech recognition, Kubernetes compatible scaling, and enterprise-grade support. The model is licensed under the CC-BY-4.0 license.

instruction: What should a team do on 4th and 2 from the opponent's 10-yard line with 30 seconds left and a 2-point lead?
description: The NFL 4th-Down Decision Model is a small multi-task MLP that recommends whether to go for it, kick a field goal, or punt on 4th down by predicting the change in win probability (ΔWP) for each action. It is trained on NFL play-by-play data from 2016 to 2024, with labels generated from historical probabilities. The model takes 23 input features, including quarter, game and half seconds remaining, yardline, yards-to-go, score differential, timeouts, weather, point spread, and one-hot encoded roof and surface indicators, along with derived features such as field goal distance and whether the field goal is in range. The model outputs ΔWP for the three actions (go, field goal, punt) as the main head, and also predicts auxiliary heads for field goal make probability and 4th-down conversion probability. The recommendation is to choose the action with the highest ΔWP. The model was trained using a split of 2016–2021 for training, 2022 for validation, and 2023–2024 for testing. The training objective combines Huber loss for ΔWP with 0.2 times the mean squared error for auxiliary heads. Training used the AdamW optimizer with BatchNorm, Dropout, and L2 regularization. The best model weights are saved as `mlp_dwp_plus_aux_best.keras`. On the 2023–2024 test set, the model achieved a policy agreement of 0.794, a mean regret (ΔWP) of 0.0023, a late and close (≤600 seconds, absolute score ≤8) agreement of 0.744, and a late and close mean regret of 0.0099. ΔWP is reported in win-probability points, with lower regret indicating better performance. The model is intended as an educational decision assistant for 4th-down scenarios and is not suitable for wagering or real-time coaching. It relies on historical probabilities and inherits assumptions from the nfl4th project. Simplifications in weather and wp_punt imputation may affect accuracy, and domain judgment should be applied when using the model's recommendations.

instruction: Could you generate a feature map for this image to highlight important regions?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a base configuration and a patch size of 16. The model has 85.6 million parameters, 23.6 GMACs, and 34.1 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`), but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models keep RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU). For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data (LVD-1689M), the model achieves 89.3% accuracy on IN-ReaL, 76.7% on IN-R, 64.1% on Obj.Net, 58.5% on Ox.-H, 51.8% on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU, 77.2% on DAVIS, 58.8% on NAVI, and 57.2% on SPair. For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data (SAT-493M), the model achieves 73.0% mean accuracy on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and 79.6% mean accuracy. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the model achieves 94.2% mean accuracy on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and 74.5% mean accuracy. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the paper on Vision Transformers for image recognition at scale, and the PyTorch Image Models repository. The model is available through the `timm` library and can be used for image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used with the `timm.create_model` function and the `eval` method, along with model-specific transforms. For feature map extraction, the model can be used with the `features_only=True` argument. For image embeddings, the model can be used with the `num_classes=0` argument to remove the classifier, or with the `forward_features` and `forward_head` methods to obtain the embeddings. The model has some caveats, including the handling of QKV biases and RoPE periods, which may affect numerical stability and compatibility with certain hardware.

instruction: How can I get the structured data from this image of a form, including checkboxes and labels?
description: Chandra is an OCR model that outputs markdown, HTML, and JSON. It is highly accurate at extracting text from images and PDFs, while preserving layout information. Chandra supports converting documents to markdown, HTML, or JSON with detailed layout information. It has good handwriting support and can reconstruct forms accurately, including checkboxes. It also provides good support for tables, math, and complex layouts. The model extracts images and diagrams, including captions and structured data, and supports 40+ languages. Chandra has been evaluated using the olmocr benchmark, which is considered the most reliable current OCR benchmark in testing. In the benchmark, Chandra v0.1.0 achieved an overall score of 83.1 ± 0.9, with scores of 82.2 on ArXiv, 80.3 on Old Scans Math, 88.0 on Tables, 50.4 on Old Scans, 90.8 on Headers and Footers, 81.2 on Multi column, and 92.3 on Long tiny text. It outperformed several other models in multiple categories. Chandra can be used via a command-line interface, an interactive streamlit app, or through code using the HuggingFace or VLLM methods. The model is available for installation via pip and can be accessed through a hosted API or a free playground.

instruction: Can you create a 3D-like effect based on this 2D photo?
description: DPT-Large, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in March 2022. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. The model is designed for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and can be used by anyone performing such tasks. It is typically intended to be fine-tuned for specific applications and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used, with random horizontal flips for data augmentation. The model was evaluated on several benchmarks, including DIW WHDR, ETH3D AbsRel, Sintel AbsRel, KITTI δ>1.25, NYU δ>1.25, and TUM δ>1.25, achieving results of 10.82, 0.089, 0.270, 8.46, 8.32, and 9.97, respectively. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. The training and inference were conducted on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown, and no additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development. The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing.

instruction: Please extract the text from this picture and provide a simplified version of it.
description: DeepSeek-OCR is a model designed for visual-text compression. It is intended to explore the boundaries of this domain. The model supports inference using Huggingface transformers on NVIDIA GPUs, with requirements tested on Python 3.12.9 and CUDA 11.8. It includes specific dependencies such as torch version 2.6.0, transformers version 4.46.3, tokenizers version 0.20.3, and additional libraries like einops, addict, and easydict. The model also supports the use of flash-attn version 2.7.3. It provides an inference function with parameters for tokenizer, prompt, image file, output path, base size, image size, crop mode, test compression, and save results. Different configurations are available for Tiny, Small, Base, Large, and Gundam settings, each with specific base and image sizes and crop mode settings. The model is now officially supported in upstream vLLM, allowing for model inference acceleration and PDF processing. It includes a vLLM-based implementation that supports batched input with image files and custom sampling parameters. The model acknowledges the contributions of various entities for their valuable models and ideas, as well as specific benchmarks. It is described in the paper titled 'DeepSeek-OCR: Contexts Optical Compression' by Wei, Haoran, Sun, Yaofeng, and Li, Yukun, published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: I need to understand what was said in a background conversation. Can you extract the text?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-8bit model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model is based on the LFM2.5-Audio architecture and has been converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It features a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, a Conformer-based audio encoder for processing input audio, a Depthformer-based audio decoder for generating audio codes, and an ISTFT-based neural detokenizer for reconstructing audio waveforms. The model also supports an alternative decoding method using the Mimi neural codec. It is capable of handling both text and audio inputs and outputs, with the ability to generate responses that include text, audio, or a combination of both. The model is trained to process audio at a 24kHz sample rate and provides configuration options for generation parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for both text and audio outputs. The model includes a ChatState system for managing conversation turns and supports streaming generation for real-time audio playback. The model is available for use via the mlx-audio library, which can be installed using pip. The model's license follows the terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: Is there a way to tell if the voice sample is from a man or a woman?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It is designed for gender classification and achieves a loss of 0.1142 and an accuracy of 0.9639 on the evaluation set. The model was trained for three epochs using a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 256, and an evaluation batch size of 256. The random seed used was 42. The optimizer used is ADAMW_TORCH_FUSED with betas of (0.9, 0.999), an epsilon of 1e-08, and no additional optimizer arguments. The learning rate scheduler type is linear with a warmup ratio of 0.05. During training, the training loss decreased from 0.2321 to 0.1002 over 400 training steps, while the validation loss stabilized at 0.1142. The accuracy on the validation set increased from 0.9434 to 0.9658 and then stabilized at 0.9639. The model was developed using Transformers 4.57.3, Pytorch 2.9.0+cu126, Datasets 4.4.2, and Tokenizers 0.22.1.

instruction: Is it possible to convert this black-and-white image into a vibrant color version?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a high-performance image generation model. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is an image-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is quantized from an unspecified base model. The data type used is INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). The model supports different ranks for inference: r32 for faster inference, r128 for better quality but slower inference, and r256 for the highest quality (slowest inference). Standard inference speed models are available for general use. The model is based on an unspecified inference engine and quantization library. A paper titled 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models' by Li, Muyang, Lin, Yujun, Zhang, Zhekai, Cai, Tianle, Li, Xiuyu, Guo, Junxian, Xie, Enze, Meng, Chenlin, Zhu, Jun-Yan, and Han, Song, presented at The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025, is associated with the model.

instruction: Produce a discriminative embedding for this document to identify similar content.
description: UME-R1 is a model that has undergone a cold-start SFT stage and an RL stage of training. It is capable of embedding text, images, multiple images, and videos. The model can generate either discriminative or generative embeddings as needed, and the generative embeddings possess the potential for test-time scaling. UME-R1 significantly outperforms discriminative embeddings and can provide either type of representation as required. Its oracle performance—selecting the best between discriminative and generative—far exceeds using either mode alone. In addition, UME-R1 can produce improved embedding representations through repeated sampling, indicating that generative embeddings also hold strong promise for inference-time scaling.

instruction: Identify all the chemical compounds in this paragraph on drug metabolism.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used as a benchmark for evaluating named entity recognition models. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.94, recall of 0.95, and accuracy of 0.99. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: How does the molecule 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)O' behave in terms of atmospheric reactivity?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the reaction rate constant with hydroxyl radicals (log kAOH). This property is related to atmospheric degradation and environmental persistence. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It processes SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the kAOH dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: I recorded a family event, but the lighting was poor. Can you adjust the video to look better?
description: SeedVR2 is a one-step diffusion-based video restoration model designed to perform adversarial video restoration training against real data. It aims to address the challenges of high-resolution video restoration in real-world settings, where existing distillation-based approaches for one-step image restoration have not been effectively extended. SeedVR2 introduces several architectural and training enhancements to handle high-resolution video restoration within a single step. These include an adaptive window attention mechanism, where the window size is dynamically adjusted to fit the output resolution, preventing window inconsistency observed in high-resolution video restoration using fixed window sizes. Additionally, a series of losses are verified for their effectiveness in stabilizing and improving adversarial post-training, including a proposed feature matching loss that does not significantly sacrifice training efficiency. Extensive experiments show that SeedVR2 can achieve comparable or better performance than existing video restoration approaches in a single step. However, the model has some limitations: it may not be perfectly aligned with the paper due to being a prototype, and it is not always robust to heavy degradations or very large motions. It shares some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Moreover, due to its strong generation ability, it may occasionally oversharpen results on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos.

instruction: I need a short version of this news article about Greece’s response to the global pandemic—can you help?
description: GreekBART is a Greek sequence-to-sequence pretrained model designed for generative tasks such as abstractive summarization. It is based on a reconstruction approach, where the model learns to reconstruct a corrupted input sentence. The pretraining was conducted using a corpus of 76.9GB of Greek raw text. Unlike existing BERT-based Greek language models like GreekBERT, GreekBART includes both an encoder and a decoder that are pretrained, making it well-suited for generation tasks. The base version of GreekBART has 12 layers and 165 million parameters. In addition to the base model, it is fine-tuned for three specific tasks: generating an abstract from a Greek news article (`greekbart-news24-abstract`), generating a title from a Greek news article (`greekbart-news24-title`), and performing binary sentiment classification (`greekbart-sentiment-classification`). The model was trained and evaluated at École Polytechnique by Iakovos Evdaimon, Hadi Abdine, Christos Xypolopoulos, Stamatis Outsios, Michalis Vazirgiannis, and Giorgos Stamou.

instruction: I need to extract key themes from a large number of social media posts—what’s an efficient way to do this?
description: EmbeddingGemma is a 300M parameter open embedding model developed by Google DeepMind, built from Gemma 3 with T5Gemma initialization and the same research and technology used to create Gemini models. It produces vector representations of text, making it suitable for search and retrieval tasks, including classification, clustering, and semantic similarity search. The model was trained on data in over 100 spoken languages, and its small size and on-device focus allow it to be deployed in environments with limited resources such as mobile phones, laptops, or desktops. EmbeddingGemma supports input text strings with a maximum context length of 2048 tokens and outputs numerical vector representations with a default embedding dimension size of 768. Smaller embedding sizes (512, 256, or 128) are available via Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL), which allows users to truncate the output embedding and re-normalize it for efficient and accurate representation. The model was trained on a dataset of approximately 320 billion tokens, including web documents, code and technical documents, and synthetic and task-specific data. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content, including CSAM and personal information. Training was conducted using the latest generation of TPUv5e hardware. The model was evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB (Multilingual, English, and Code versions), with performance metrics varying by embedding dimension and quantization configuration. EmbeddingGemma can be used for various tasks such as semantic similarity, classification, clustering, retrieval (document and query), question answering, fact verification, and code retrieval. Users are advised to be aware of potential limitations, including biases or gaps in training data, and the model's potential challenges in understanding language ambiguity and nuance. Ethical considerations include the risk of bias perpetuation, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to use de-biasing techniques, adhere to privacy regulations, and monitor for misuse. EmbeddingGemma is intended for responsible AI development and has demonstrated superior performance compared to similarly sized open models.

instruction: Can you predict the lipophilicity of a compound based on its structure using the SMILES code 'C=O'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the octanol-water partition coefficient (log KOW/log P), a fundamental property that indicates lipophilicity and affects bioaccumulation potential. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using PyTorch Geometric and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, 0.1 dropout, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the KOW dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What is the high-level description of this image based on its visual content?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a base configuration and a patch size of 16. The model has 85.6 million parameters, 23.6 GMACs, and 34.1 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`), but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models keep RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU). For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data (LVD-1689M), the model achieves 89.3% accuracy on IN-ReaL, 76.7% on IN-R, 64.1% on Obj.Net, 58.5% on Ox.-H, 51.8% on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU, 77.2% on DAVIS, 58.8% on NAVI, and 57.2% on SPair. For ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data (SAT-493M), the model achieves 73.0% mean accuracy on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and 79.6% mean accuracy. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the model achieves 94.2% mean accuracy on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and 74.5% mean accuracy. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the paper on Vision Transformers for image recognition at scale, and the PyTorch Image Models repository. The model is available through the `timm` library and can be used for image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used with the `timm.create_model` function and the `eval` method, along with model-specific transforms. For feature map extraction, the model can be used with the `features_only=True` argument. For image embeddings, the model can be used with the `num_classes=0` argument to remove the classifier, or with the `forward_features` and `forward_head` methods to obtain the embeddings. The model has some caveats, including the handling of QKV biases and RoPE periods, which may affect numerical stability and compatibility with certain hardware.

instruction: Detect any cell line names in this abstract about gene expression analysis.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting gene-related entities from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated GELLUS dataset, which is focused on gene recognition and genetics entities for genomics and molecular biology applications. The model supports the classification of biomedical entities such as 'B-Cell-line-name' and 'I-Cell-line-name'. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model has achieved an F1 score of 1.00, with precision, recall, and accuracy also at 1.00. Comparative performance on the GELLUS dataset shows that it ranks among the top models, with an F1 score of 0.9976, precision of 0.9977, recall of 0.9975, and accuracy of 0.9989. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with the AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and it is validated using cross-validation on a held-out test set.

instruction: Could you identify the biological organisms in this section of a clinical report?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying species names from the Species-800 dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated SPECIES800 dataset, which contains 800 manually annotated abstracts for organism mentions. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.82, precision of 0.81, recall of 0.82, and accuracy of 0.96. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the Bio_Discharge_Summary_BERT architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Can you evaluate which of these two website landing pages is more persuasive?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is based on the Bradley-Terry model, with improvements in training data scale and quality to achieve superior performance. Compared with the first generation of Skywork-Reward, the Skywork-Reward-V2 series is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, consisting of 26 million preference pairs curated via a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series offers state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. The series includes models of multiple sizes, with the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, nearly matching the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2. The largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The models are available in eight variants, including Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-1B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-1.7B, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-4B, and Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-8B. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series outperforms models in both Bradley-Terry and Generative categories with much smaller model sizes. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and inference should be performed within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The model Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M is considered an experimental variant, trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The models are licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for commercial use, with conditions for attribution and modification. Models derived from Qwen3 follow the Qwen3 license, while those derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct follow the Llama license.

instruction: I'd like to see a video of a child playing with a robot in a futuristic playground.
description: This model is a controlnet module designed for the Wan2.2 model. It follows the same approach as the controlnet implementation for Wan2.2. The model is intended for use with ComfyUI and supports inference tasks for generating video content based on text prompts and control frames. The model architecture includes a custom controlnet module integrated with the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers base model. Inference is performed using a pipeline that incorporates a controlnet, a transformer, and an autoencoder. The model processes video frames with Gaussian blur and resizing to generate output video sequences. Performance is evaluated through inference examples, including a detailed prompt for generating a video of a young woman blowing a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model includes parameters for guidance scale, controlnet weight, and frame resolution. The model is available on GitHub, and users are encouraged to raise issues directly in the repository. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: Can you help me create a voice memo from a written note that I can play back later?
description: LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B is an updated end-to-end audio foundation model. Key improvements include a custom, LFM-based audio detokenizer, `llama.cpp` compatible GGUFs for CPU inference, and better ASR and TTS performance. LFM2.5-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model, and as such does not require separate ASR and TTS components. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, at only 1.5 billion parameters LFM2.5-Audio enables seamless conversational interaction, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. Our model consists of a pretrained LFM2.5 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and an RQ-transformer generating discrete tokens coupled with a lightweight audio detokenizer for audio output. LFM2.5-Audio supports two distinct generation routines, each suitable for a set of tasks. Interleaved generation enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities, where audio generation latency is key. Sequential generation is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters for the language model only, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolution and attention-based backbone, an audio detokenizer compatible with 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. The model is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. Performance on VoiceBench (audio input) includes scores of 3.76 on AlpacaEval, 3.53 on CommonEval, 3.15 on WildVoice, 30.92 on SD-QA, 33.04 on MMSU, 44.40 on OBQA, 49.54 on BBH, 28.48 on IFEval, 99.04 on Overall. For ASR, the model has a word error rate (WER) average of 7.53, with 15.63 on AMI, 14.56 on Earnings22, 10.47 on GigaSpeech, 1.95 on LibriSpeech-clean, 4.30 on LibriSpeech-other, 2.76 on SPGISpeech, 3.47 on TED-LIUM, and 7.13 on VoxPopuli. The model is intended for use with the `liquid-audio` package, which provides a lower-level interface for custom use cases, including multi-turn, multi-modal chat. The model is suitable for tasks such as multi-turn chat, ASR, and TTS. The model has some caveats, including that it is currently limited to English and that performance may vary depending on the specific use case and deployment environment.

instruction: How well does the video convey the intended message from the prompt?
description: VideoScore2 is a multi-dimensional, interpretable, and human-aligned framework designed to evaluate AI-generated videos across three dimensions: visual quality, text-to-video alignment, and physical/common-sense consistency. The model produces detailed chain-of-thought rationales to support its evaluations. It is trained on a large-scale dataset called VideoFeedback2, which contains 27,168 human-annotated videos with scores and reasoning traces across the three evaluation dimensions. The training process uses a two-stage pipeline involving supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance analytical robustness. Extensive experiments show that VideoScore2 achieves 44.35 (+5.94) accuracy on the in-domain benchmark VideoScore-Bench-v2 and an average performance of 50.37 (+4.32) across four out-of-domain benchmarks, including VideoGenReward-Bench and VideoPhy2. The model is intended for evaluating AI-generated videos and provides both hard scores and soft, normalized scores based on token-level probabilities. It is designed to be used with a specific inference pipeline that includes video preprocessing, model generation, and score parsing. The model supports evaluation of visual quality (clarity, smoothness, artifacts), text-to-video alignment (fidelity to the prompt), and physical/common-sense consistency (naturalness and physics plausibility).

instruction: Could you help me extract the areas marked as impervious surfaces from this image?
description: This model is a Mask2Former-Large (Swin-L backbone) model fine-tuned on the DLR SkyScapes aerial imagery dataset for 20-class semantic segmentation. It is designed for high-resolution urban and peri-urban environments and produces detailed semantic masks for roads, buildings, vegetation, vehicles, and more. The architecture is based on the Mask2Former framework with a Transformer-based Swin-L backbone. The base checkpoint used is `facebook/mask2former-swin-large-ade-semantic`, and the model is trained for the task of semantic segmentation in the domain of high-resolution aerial imagery. It supports 20 semantic classes from the DLR SkyScapes dataset, which is a subset of the original 31 classes. The model uses the CrossEntropyLoss function with an ignore index of 255 and is preprocessed using the Hugging Face `AutoImageProcessor`. For full reproducibility, it is recommended to use `use_fast=False`. The model weights are stored in `model.safetensors`. The DLR SkyScapes dataset is a high-resolution aerial dataset created by the German Aerospace Center (DLR), providing high-quality pixel-level annotations for detailed scene understanding in urban environments. The dataset contains 16 images with a resolution of 5616 × 3744 pixels and a ground sampling distance of 13 cm per pixel. It covers an aerial area of approximately 5.69 km², including both urban and rural regions, with 70,346 annotated instances. The 20 semantic classes used in this model include low vegetation, paved road, non paved road, paved parking, non paved parking, bikeways, sidewalks, entrance/exit, danger area, lane markings, building, car, trailer, van, truck, long truck, bus, clutter, impervious surface, and tree. These classes correspond to the class IDs used during training. The intended applications of this model include urban mapping and GIS, HD map generation, scene understanding for robotics, autonomous driving (aerial-based priors), environmental monitoring, and remote sensing research. However, it is not suitable for safety-critical deployment without human supervision.

instruction: Can you help me identify slow-moving products that may need a sales push?
description: The Smart Inventory Advisor for Retail Sales (Invense) is a machine learning model trained to predict daily sales for retail products and provide actionable inventory recommendations. The model is a Random Forest Regressor implemented using scikit-learn, designed to forecast the number of units sold for a given product based on a variety of features. It generates a 'Store Owner's Action Plan' that identifies products at risk of stocking out and recommends a reorder quantity. The model is intended for use as a decision-support tool for small to medium-sized retail business owners, helping them determine which products to restock and how many units to order. It is not suitable for real-time stock market prediction or long-term financial forecasting, as its predictions are based on historical patterns and may not account for drastic changes in market conditions. The model's performance is dependent on the quality and patterns of the training data, and it was trained on a synthetic dataset containing over 73,000 daily records across multiple stores and products. The final model achieved a Testing R² score of 0.34, indicating that about 66% of the variance in daily sales is due to unpredictable factors not captured in the data. The model was trained using a preprocessing pipeline that included feature engineering, label encoding of categorical features, and standard scaling of numerical features. Hyperparameters were tuned using RandomizedSearchCV, with the best settings including 200 estimators, a minimum of 10 samples to split, a minimum of 4 samples per leaf, 'sqrt' for max features, and a max depth of 20. The model was evaluated on a 20% holdout set using R-squared, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) as metrics. The Testing R² was 0.3402, RMSE was 88.39, and MAE was 69.27. Users are advised to treat the model's recommendations as data-driven suggestions and to apply their own domain knowledge, particularly when considering external factors not included in the dataset. The model should not be used for automated financial decisions without human oversight. The model is provided under the MIT license and was developed by jerewy (NexusCrew).

instruction: Could you analyze the structure of this architectural photo and provide a summary?
description: This is a DINOv3 ViT model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed to extract image features and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture with a large patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`) but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as accuracy and mean intersection over union (mIoU) reported for different model variants. The model is also evaluated on satellite datasets such as SAT-493M for classification and segmentation tasks, with performance metrics reported for models like DINOv3 ViT-L/16 and DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, as well as the papers on Vision Transformers and PyTorch Image Models.

instruction: Could you evaluate and compare these two versions of a product description for clarity and appeal?
description: Skywork-Reward-V2 is a series of eight reward models designed for versatility across a wide range of tasks. These models are trained on a mixture of 26 million carefully curated preference pairs, using a large-scale human-LLM synergistic pipeline. The series is based on the Bradley-Terry model and offers significant improvements over the first generation of Skywork-Reward. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series is trained on a significantly larger and higher-quality preference data mixture, which contributes to its superior performance. The models are available in eight different sizes, ranging from the smallest 0.6B variant, Skywork-Reward-V2-Qwen3-0.6B, to the largest 8B version, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B. The smallest model nearly matches the average performance of the previous best model, Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2, while the largest model surpasses all existing reward models across all benchmarks on average. The top experimental model, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M, outperforms all existing reward models on every benchmark. The Skywork-Reward-V2 series achieves state-of-the-art performance on seven major reward model benchmarks, including RewardBench v1, RewardBench v2, PPE Preference, PPE Correctness, RMB, RM-Bench, and JudgeBench. For most use cases, Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B is recommended, with smaller variants suggested for low-resource settings. All models are trained on preference data with a maximum length of 16,384 tokens, and it is recommended to perform inference within this limit. System prompts should not be included when using chat templates. The Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.1-8B-40M model is considered an experimental variant, trained on the complete set of 40 million preference pairs, with about one third of the chosen-rejected pairs flipped. It is recommended for research or non-production purposes only. The model series is available under different licenses depending on the base model. Models derived from Qwen3 support commercial use and permit modifications and the creation of derivative works under the Apache 2.0 License, provided that all conditions are met and proper attribution is given. Models derived from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct follow the Meta License. The model can be used for inference in Hugging Face Transformers or via distributed inference using SGLang for optimal throughput with a large number of conversations. The model repository, including the model weights and code, is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License for Qwen3-derived models and the Meta License for Llama-derived models. If you have any questions, you can contact the authors at yuhao.liuu at kunlun-inc dot com and liang.zeng at kunlun-inc dot com. If you find the work useful, please cite it as follows: @article{liu2025skywork, title={Skywork-Reward-V2: Scaling Preference Data Curation via Human-AI Synergy}, author = {Liu, Chris Yuhao and Zeng, Liang and Xiao, Yuzhen and He, Jujie and Liu, Jiacai and Wang, Chaojie and Yan, Rui and Shen, Wei and Zhang, Fuxiang and Xu, Jiacheng and Liu, Yang and Zhou, Yahui}, journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.01352}, year={2025}}

instruction: What web pages are most closely related to the query about climate change mitigation?
description: Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is a 47M parameter dense biencoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. Compared to most other open-source models, this model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. The r2 models show strong performance across standard and IBM-built information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, ClapNQ), code retrieval (COIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables), and on many enterprise use cases. These models use a bi-encoder architecture to generate high-quality embeddings from text inputs such as queries, passages, and documents, enabling seamless comparison through cosine similarity. Built using retrieval-oriented pretraining, contrastive finetuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is optimized to ensure strong alignment between query and passage embeddings. The latest granite embedding r2 release introduces two English embedding models, both based on the ModernBERT architecture: granite-embedding-english-r2 (149M parameters) with an output embedding size of 768, replacing granite-embedding-125m-english; and granite-embedding-small-english-r2 (47M parameters), a reduced-size model with 8192 context length support, fewer layers, and a smaller output embedding size (384), replacing granite-embedding-30m-english. The model is designed to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. For efficient decoding, these models use Flash Attention 2. Installing it is optional but can lead to faster inference. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and is very easy to use. It can also be used with the Huggingface Transformers library and PyTorch. The granite embedding r2 models show strong performance across diverse tasks, including MTEB Retrieval (i.e., BEIR), MTEB-v2, code retrieval (CoIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), and table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables). The average speed to encode documents on a single H100 GPU using a sliding window with 512 context length chunks is also reported. Nearing encoding speed of 200 documents per second, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 demonstrates speed and efficiency while maintaining competitive performance. The model has 47 million parameters, an embedding size of 384, 12 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, uses the GeGLU activation function, has a vocabulary size of 50368, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and is based on the ModernBERT architecture. The granite embedding r2 models incorporate key enhancements from the ModernBERT architecture, including alternating attention lengths to accelerate processing, rotary position embeddings for extended sequence length, a newly trained tokenizer optimized with code and text data, Flash Attention 2.0 for improved efficiency, and streamlined parameters, eliminating unnecessary bias terms. The models are trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the popular MS-MARCO retrieval dataset is not used in the training corpus due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing critical information about the data, including content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing information, usage restrictions, how the data will be acquired, and an assessment of sensitive information (i.e., personal information). The models were trained using IBM's computing cluster, BlueVela Cluster, outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs. Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 leverages both permissively licensed open-source and select proprietary data for enhanced performance. The training data for the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is trained only for English texts and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: I have a set of tokens from a previous system—can they be used to create a 44.1kHz output?
description: Anime-XCodec2-44.1kHz is a fine-tuned variant of NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2, designed to produce 44.1kHz high-fidelity Japanese speech in an anime/game-style. It incorporates upsampling layers and RMS loss inspired by the Inworld TTS-1 paper. The model was trained on approximately 22,000 hours of Japanese speech data. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, ensuring speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for systems already using XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model accepts 16kHz input for encoding and outputs 44.1kHz audio. It is intended for decoding XCodec2 speech tokens into high-fidelity Japanese speech, upgrading existing Anime-XCodec2 pipelines to 44.1kHz output, and potentially for audio super-resolution, though this use case is untested. The model modifies the original XCodec2 architecture by adding upsampler blocks and requires a custom xcodec2 library for inference, as the standard library will not work. The model is optimized for Japanese and anime/game-style voices, and it is not compatible with the original xcodec2 library. The training procedure involved integrating an UpSamplerBlock into the decoder with parameters hop_length=147, upsample_factors=[3, 2], and kernel_sizes=[7, 6]. RMS Loss was adopted in addition to the original losses. The encoder and codebook were frozen, while the generator's backbone, head, upsampler, and fc_post_a were fine-tuned. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, inherited from XCodec2 and Anime-XCodec2. It acknowledges the original HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 model and Inworld AI for their contributions to the upsampler architecture and RMS Loss.

instruction: I need to know if this video shows a hand opening a door or pushing it shut.
description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities by leveraging large-scale data and model sizes. This specific model variant is based on the ViT-g 384 architecture and includes a video classification head. It was pretrained on the Something-Something-V2 dataset. The model is designed to support video classification tasks and is intended for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library. Performance results are not explicitly detailed in the provided text. The model includes a warning that the code is released but no further caveats or limitations are specified.

instruction: Can you help identify the dominant visual features in this photo for a downstream task?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained following the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. These models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones providing multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens, image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2. However, a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on various tasks. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU↓, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16; 88.0 on IN-ReaL, 68.8 on IN-R, 54.6 on Obj.Net, 50.0 on Ox.-H, 48.8 on ADE20k, 0.399 on NYU↓, 75.5 on DAVIS, 57.1 on NAVI, and 55.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S+/16; 89.3 on IN-ReaL, 76.7 on IN-R, 64.1 on Obj.Net, 58.5 on Ox.-H, 51.8 on ADE20k, 0.373 on NYU↓, 77.2 on DAVIS, 58.8 on NAVI, and 57.2 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-B/16; 90.2 on IN-ReaL, 88.1 on IN-R, 74.8 on Obj.Net, 63.1 on Ox.-H, 54.9 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.9 on DAVIS, 62.3 on NAVI, and 61.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; 90.3 on IN-ReaL, 90.0 on IN-R, 78.6 on Obj.Net, 64.5 on Ox.-H, 54.8 on ADE20k, 0.352 on NYU↓, 79.3 on DAVIS, 63.3 on NAVI, and 56.3 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-H+/16; and 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU↓, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web (LVD-1689M), the results include 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU↓, and 0.448 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny; 87.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 88.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 44.8 on NYU↓, and 0.432 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Small; 88.5 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.2 on IN-ReaL @512px, 77.2 on Obj.Net @256px, 78.2 on Obj.Net @512px, 56.2 on ADE20k @256px, 61.3 on ADE20k @512px, 46.3 on NYU↓, and 0.420 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Base; and 88.9 on IN-ReaL @256px, 89.4 on IN-ReaL @512px, 81.3 on Obj.Net @256px, 82.4 on Obj.Net @512px, 59.3 on ADE20k @256px, 65.2 on ADE20k @512px, 47.8 on NYU↓, and 0.403 on SPair for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Large. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite (SAT-493M), the results include 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. For segmentation tasks on satellite data, the results include 94.2 on m-cashew, 75.6 on m-chesapeake, 61.8 on m-NeonTree, 83.7 on m-nz-cattle, 95.2 on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 on m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16; and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for DINOv3 ViT-7B/16. The model training for the ViT-7B model used 61,440 hours of compute on Nvidia H100 GPUs in a private infrastructure in the USA, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-S+ (29M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 384, 4 register tokens, 6 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; ViT-B (86M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 768, 4 register tokens, 12 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-L (300M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1024, 4 register tokens, 16 heads, MLP FFN, RoPE; ViT-H+ (840M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 1280, 4 register tokens, 20 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE; and ViT-7B (6716M parameters) with patch size 16, embedding dimension 4096, 4 register tokens, 32 heads, SwiGLU FFN, RoPE. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Can you create a voice recording in Telugu that matches the style and inflection of the example provided?
description: IndicF5 is a high-quality, near-human polyglot Text-to-Speech (TTS) model trained on 1417 hours of high-quality speech data. It supports 11 Indian languages: Assamese, Bengali, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Malayalam, Marathi, Odia, Punjabi, Tamil, and Telugu. The model is designed to synthesize speech based on three inputs: the text to be spoken, a reference prompt audio that guides the model's prosody and speaker characteristics, and the transcript of the reference prompt audio. IndicF5 is intended for use with explicit permission for voice cloning, and unauthorized use is strictly prohibited. Users are responsible for any misuse of the model. The model is based on prior work by other researchers in the field of text-to-speech synthesis. If used in research or projects, it should be cited appropriately.

instruction: How do I make a speech-to-text system more efficient with better audio processing?
description: Linacodec is an audio tokenizer that compresses audio into 12.5 tokens per second (171 bps) and decodes to 48khz audio. It offers 60x more compression than DAC, with a 48khz output that is much clearer than the standard 16khz or 24khz. The encoder operates at 200x realtime speed, and the decoder runs at 400x realtime speed, with even faster performance when using batching. Linacodec indirectly supports tasks such as voice conversion, audio super-resolution, and audio denoising. Audio tokenizers contribute to the speed, quality, and capability of TTS and ASR models, and Linacodec significantly improves upon previous codecs in these areas. It enables TTS models to run at 800x realtime, which is 8x faster than previously reported, and allows high-quality TTS models to be trained in less than one day. It is versatile, working for both Text-to-Speech and Speech-to-Text applications, unlike most other codecs. The model is licensed under CC-BY-4.0, which permits use for any purpose, including commercial use, provided the original creator is credited.

instruction: I have a database of legal documents. Can I sort them to find the most relevant ones faster?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is based on the Qwen3 series and provides a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). It inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the foundational Qwen3 model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios.
