The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports over 100 languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on MTEB-R, 75.94 on CMTEB-R, 72.74 on MMTEB-R, 69.97 on MLDR, 81.20 on MTEB-Code, and 14.84 on FollowIR. The model supports instruction-aware reranking, and using instructions typically improves performance by 1% to 5% compared to not using them. Developers are advised to create tailored instructions specific to their tasks and scenarios. In multilingual contexts, instructions should be written in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: Please find all instances of legal terminology in this text.
description: GEM-ModernBERT HQ Legal is a ModernBERT-base model pre-trained from scratch on a 21GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. The model incorporates ModernBERT's architectural innovations, including Flash Attention 2, StableAdamW optimizer, 1024-token context length, and advanced memory optimization techniques. It is designed for superior performance on Greek legal document understanding tasks. The model uses a quality-based data repetition strategy, with 30% masking probability, trapezoidal learning rate scheduling, and optimized batch sizing to enhance convergence and performance. It supports extended 1024-token context windows for handling longer legal documents while maintaining computational efficiency. The model is optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering in the legal domain. The training data includes a 21.12GB corpus, expanded from an original 16.75GB through strategic repetition of higher-quality legal content. The model uses 1024-token sequences to process complete legal articles, full court decisions, complex legislative texts, and parliamentary debates. The model architecture includes a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, 12 hidden layers, and approximately 139 million parameters. It has a vocabulary size of 50,373 tokens and supports 1024 max position embeddings. The model was pre-trained for 150,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs using BFloat16 mixed-precision. Training took 97 hours and 9 minutes to complete. Key training optimizations include a per-device batch size of 16, gradient accumulation steps of 8, and an effective batch size of 1,024. The StableAdamW optimizer was configured with a learning rate of 0.0002, weight decay of 0.1, and trapezoidal learning rate scheduling with 9,000 warmup steps and square-root decay. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.7648 and a final evaluation loss of 0.7751. Training used a 90%/10% train/validation split and 21.12GB of effective training data. Advanced training infrastructure included Flash Attention 2 optimizations, memory optimization settings, and distributed training with NCCL backend and BFloat16 mixed precision. The model supports downstream tasks with extended context and is compatible with the ModernBERT tokenizer.

instruction: Produce a full set of views for a fantasy weapon using a text prompt and a reference image.
description: MV-AR is a diffusion-based model designed for generating multi-view consistent images. It addresses the limitations of existing methods that rely on a specific reference view for predicting subsequent views, which can lead to poor image quality and inconsistency when there is minimal overlap between the reference and predicted views. MV-AR instead conditions on the preceding view with significant overlap, improving the quality and consistency of the generated multi-view images. The model supports tasks such as text-to-multi-view image generation, image-to-multi-view image generation, and text-plus-geometry-to-multi-view image generation. The model is based on prior work, and its implementation is available for reproduction with specific pretrained models and datasets. Generated images are saved in designated directories following execution of provided shell scripts. The model's development and methodology are described in an arXiv preprint titled 'Auto-Regressively Generating Multi-View Consistent Images' by Hu, JiaKui and others.

instruction: What is the average salary for employees in the engineering department?
description: This model is a TAPAS large model fine-tuned on WikiTable Questions (WTQ). It has two versions: the default version corresponds to the `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large_reset` checkpoint, and the other version is `no_reset`, corresponding to `tapas_wtq_wikisql_sqa_inter_masklm_large`. The model was pre-trained on masked language modeling (MLM) and an additional step called intermediate pre-training, followed by fine-tuning on SQA, WikiSQL, and finally WTQ. It uses relative position embeddings, resetting the position index at every cell of the table. The non-default version uses absolute position embeddings. TAPAS is a BERT-like transformer model pre-trained on a large corpus of English data from Wikipedia in a self-supervised fashion. It was pre-trained with two objectives: masked language modeling, where 15% of the words in a flattened table and associated context are randomly masked and the model must predict them, and intermediate pre-training, where the model is trained to classify whether a sentence is supported or refuted by the contents of a table using a balanced dataset of syntactically created training examples. This allows the model to learn a bidirectional representation of tables and associated texts. The model can be used for tasks such as answering questions about a table or determining whether a sentence is entailed or refuted by the contents of a table. Fine-tuning involves adding a cell selection head and aggregation head on top of the pre-trained model and jointly training these with the base model. The model was fine-tuned on 32 Cloud TPU v3 cores for 50,000 steps with a maximum sequence length of 512 and a batch size of 512. The optimizer used is Adam with a learning rate of 1.93581e-5 and a warmup ratio of 0.128960. An inductive bias is added to ensure the model only selects cells of the same column. The preprocessing involves lowercasing and tokenizing the texts using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. The input format is [CLS] Question [SEP] Flattened table [SEP]. The model's performance on the development set for the LARGE version with reset is 0.5097, and with noreset is 0.5062. The model is intended for answering questions related to tables, and users are directed to the HuggingFace documentation for code examples. The team releasing TAPAS did not write a model card for this model; this model card was written by the Hugging Face team and contributors.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Produce a video of a child riding a bicycle through a colorful autumn forest.
description: The FastWan2.2-TI2V-5B-FullAttn-Diffusers model is part of the FastWan2.2 series, which is finetuned using a novel Sparse-distill strategy. This strategy combines DMD and VSA in a single training process, leveraging distillation to shorten diffusion steps and sparse attention to reduce attention computations, enabling faster video generation. The model is built upon Wan-AI/Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers and supports efficient 3-step inference, producing high-quality videos at 121×704×1280 resolution. The model is trained using simulated forward for the generator model, making the training process data-free. The current FastWan2.2-TI2V-5B-FullAttn-Diffusers model is trained using only DMD. The model is trained on 121×704×1280 resolution but can generate videos with any resolution, although quality may degrade. Training was conducted on 8 nodes with 64 H200 GPUs in total, using a global batch size of 64, and training runs for 3000 steps, which takes approximately 12 hours. If the model is used for research, the associated papers should be cited.

instruction: How would you handle depth estimation in a scene with varying lighting conditions?
description: DA3 Nested is a model that combines the any-view Giant model with the metric Large model for metric-scale visual geometry reconstruction. It is the recommended model that integrates all available capabilities. The model belongs to the Nested series and contains 1.40B parameters. It is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, 3D Gaussians, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture with a plain transformer backbone and a unified depth-ray representation. The model is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. It significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images, and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is intended for non-commercial use only due to its license.

instruction: Could you help me read what's being said in this Russian audio recording?
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Russian. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe Russian speech into text. Evaluation can be performed on the mozilla-foundation/common_voice_6_0 dataset with the test split, and on the speech-recognition-community-v2/dev_data dataset with the validation split. The model has been tested on a set of Russian speech samples, and some reference and predicted transcriptions are provided, showing varying degrees of accuracy. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face Transformers library. It is important to note that the model may not always produce perfect transcriptions, as demonstrated by the discrepancies between reference and predicted sentences in the evaluation examples.

instruction: Could you determine the posture of each person in this group photo?
description: YOLO26-S-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection, or pose estimation. It is designed for edge and low-power devices, with a focus on simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. The model operates with an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 63.0 for pose estimation at 50-95 and 86.6 at 50. It runs at 85.3 milliseconds on CPU (ONNX) and 2.7 milliseconds on T4 TensorRT10, with 10.4 million parameters and 23.9 billion FLOPs. The architecture is end-to-end, eliminating the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which reduces latency and simplifies deployment. It features the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which improves stability and convergence. Additional features include Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for faster inference, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing. The model can be used for pose estimation tasks and is intended to be deployed in real-world systems where low latency and hardware efficiency are critical. It is available for download and inference via the Hugging Face Hub and the Ultralytics library.

instruction: Transform this photo of a desert into a video that shows a caravan passing by at sunset.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and an efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video (TI2V) system. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The high-noise expert handles early-stage layout, while the low-noise expert refines details in later stages. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defining the switch. The A14B model series, which uses this MoE design, has 27B total parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, maintaining low inference cost and GPU memory usage. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, improving generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The TI2V-5B model, built with the advanced Wan2.2-VAE, achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling 720P video generation at 24fps on consumer-grade GPUs like the RTX 4090. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model can run on a single GPU with at least 24GB VRAM and can be optimized for higher VRAM GPUs to improve speed. Wan2.2 is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their use complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model is open-sourced and supports both industrial and academic applications.

instruction: Can you calculate the average sales per region from the provided table and identify the region with the highest performance?
description: JT-DA-8B is a specialized large language model designed for complex table reasoning tasks across diverse real-world scenarios. It is built upon the open-sourced JT-Coder-8B model, an 8B-parameter decoder-only foundation model trained from scratch. The model architecture follows a mainstream Transformer decoder design, including RMS layer normalization, RoPE, and GQA mechanism. The model is trained on a comprehensive and diverse training corpus, which includes 29 public table QA datasets, over 300 domain-specific non-QA tables, and 1 million rule-based table reasoning QA pairs. A structured pipeline is used to generate realistic multi-step analytical tasks involving reasoning patterns like correlation, anomaly detection, and hypothesis testing. The training data is extensively cleaned and processed, including short answer filtering, repetitive thinking filtering, low information density filtering, and LLM-based scoring to ensure high-quality, table-centric data. The training data composition includes six core capabilities with 34 subtasks, covering natural language understanding, table understanding, table basic operation, table computational operation, data analysis, and advanced data analysis. The model is trained using a distributed parallel strategy on 96 NPUs, with a high-bandwidth, low-latency communication network and shared high-performance storage. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is conducted to enhance the model's capability in structured table reasoning, using a teacher-forcing approach to minimize the cross-entropy loss between predicted and ground-truth tokens. A four-stage table reasoning workflow is proposed, including table preprocessing, table sensing, tool-integrated reasoning, and prompt engineering, to improve model interpretability and execution accuracy. The model is evaluated on the TReB benchmark, which covers six core capabilities and 26 subtasks, using three inference modes: Textual Chain-of-Thought (TCoT), Program-of-Thought (PoT), and Interleaved Chain-of-Thought (ICoT). The evaluation results demonstrate that the model excels among models of the same size, showcasing outstanding performance in table understanding, querying, computation, and data analysis tasks. The model is capable of performing multi-step reasoning, handling inconsistent and noisy data types, and generating executable code for data analysis. It supports tasks such as table summarization, descriptive analysis, statistical metric calculation, variable relationship modeling, and result interpretation. The model also supports data visualization, including the generation of bar charts to illustrate data patterns. The model is distributed under a license and includes a disclaimer that it may still produce content that is inaccurate, biased, or inappropriate, and users are advised to exercise discretion when interpreting the model’s outputs and assume full responsibility for any outcomes arising from its use.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you detect any electronic devices in this image?
description: The OWLv2 model, short for Open-World Localization, was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby. It is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that can be used to query an image with one or multiple text queries. The model uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To use CLIP for detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is enabled by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with the class-name embeddings obtained from the text model. The authors first train CLIP from scratch and fine-tune it end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. One or multiple text queries per image can be used to perform zero-shot text-conditioned object detection. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is hoped that this model will enable researchers to better understand and explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection. It is also hoped it can be used for interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training. The primary intended users of these models are AI researchers. The model is primarily imagined to be used by researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data. This was done through a combination of crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, which means that the data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets.

instruction: What are the top five products I should restock based on upcoming sales trends?
description: The Smart Inventory Advisor for Retail Sales (Invense) is a machine learning model trained to predict daily sales for retail products and provide actionable inventory recommendations. The model is a Random Forest Regressor implemented using scikit-learn, designed to forecast the number of units sold for a given product based on a variety of features. It generates a 'Store Owner's Action Plan' that identifies products at risk of stocking out and recommends a reorder quantity. The model is intended for use as a decision-support tool for small to medium-sized retail business owners, helping them determine which products to restock and how many units to order. It is not suitable for real-time stock market prediction or long-term financial forecasting, as its predictions are based on historical patterns and may not account for drastic changes in market conditions. The model's performance is dependent on the quality and patterns of the training data, and it was trained on a synthetic dataset containing over 73,000 daily records across multiple stores and products. The final model achieved a Testing R² score of 0.34, indicating that about 66% of the variance in daily sales is due to unpredictable factors not captured in the data. The model was trained using a preprocessing pipeline that included feature engineering, label encoding of categorical features, and standard scaling of numerical features. Hyperparameters were tuned using RandomizedSearchCV, with the best settings including 200 estimators, a minimum of 10 samples to split, a minimum of 4 samples per leaf, 'sqrt' for max features, and a max depth of 20. The model was evaluated on a 20% holdout set using R-squared, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) as metrics. The Testing R² was 0.3402, RMSE was 88.39, and MAE was 69.27. Users are advised to treat the model's recommendations as data-driven suggestions and to apply their own domain knowledge, particularly when considering external factors not included in the dataset. The model should not be used for automated financial decisions without human oversight. The model is provided under the MIT license and was developed by jerewy (NexusCrew).

instruction: I recorded a voice note in a car—can you help eliminate the engine noise and improve the clarity?
description: DPDFNet is a family of causal, single-channel speech enhancement models designed for real-time noise suppression in challenging everyday environments. It extends the DeepFilterNet2 enhancement framework by incorporating Dual-Path RNN (DPRNN) blocks into the encoder, which enhances long-range temporal and cross-band modeling while maintaining a compact, streaming-friendly design. The models are optimized for deployment on mobile and edge devices using TensorFlow Lite (TFLite). The 16kHz models include baseline, dpdfnet2, dpdfnet4, and dpdfnet8, while a 48kHz model, dpdfnet2_48khz_hr, is available for fullband enhancement. These models are characterized by their causal and low-latency design, suitable for streaming applications such as telephony, conferencing, and embedded devices. The Dual-Path RNN integration improves temporal context and frequency-domain interactions, enabling more robust enhancement in difficult noise conditions. The model family is scalable, allowing users to choose between baseline and dpdfnet2/4/8 to balance quality and computational requirements. The models are intended for real-time, single-channel speech enhancement and are targeted for deployment on mobile devices, embedded NPUs, and edge platforms. The 16kHz models process 16kHz mono noisy speech waveforms and output 16kHz mono enhanced speech waveforms, while the 48kHz model processes and outputs 48kHz mono waveforms. Typical applications include voice calls, video conferencing, always-on voice interfaces, and audio devices such as wearables and earbuds. The models were trained using a mixture of public speech and noise datasets, including DNS4 (downsampled), MLS, MUSAN, and FSD50K. The baseline model has 2.31 million parameters, 0.36 gigaflops of multiply-accumulate operations, and a TFLite size of 8.5 MB. DPDFNet-2 has 2.49 million parameters, 1.35 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 10.7 MB. DPDFNet-4 has 2.84 million parameters, 2.36 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 12.9 MB. DPDFNet-8 has 3.54 million parameters, 4.37 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 17.2 MB. The 48kHz model, DPDFNet-2 HR, has 2.58 million parameters, 2.42 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 11.6 MB. The models are distributed under the Apache-2.0 license.

instruction: Take this image of a cat and animate it walking across the room in a cartoonish style.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video capabilities. The MoE architecture in Wan2.2 separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The model includes a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with a transition point determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). The A14B model series, part of Wan2.2, has two experts with approximately 14B parameters each, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. Wan2.2 also features a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 4×16×16, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. TI2V-5B supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. The model is trained on significantly larger data compared to its predecessor, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. Wan2.2 achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The repository also includes the I2V-A14B model for image-to-video generation, supporting 480P and 720P resolutions with a MoE architecture for more stable video synthesis and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, with integration into ComfyUI and Diffusers. Computational efficiency tests show that Wan2.2 models perform well on different GPUs, with specific parameter settings for multi-GPU and single-GPU inference. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: Can I expect better quality from the larger file sizes, and how much difference does that make?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the gemma-3n-E4B-it model by Google, using the llama.cpp release b5760 for quantization. The original model is quantized using the imatrix option with a dataset provided. The quantizations are intended to be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. No chat template is specified, so a default prompt format is used, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The quantizations vary in quality and file size, with options ranging from Q8_0 (7.35GB, extremely high quality) to IQ3_XS (3.17GB, lower quality). Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for embed and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, particularly for the Q4_0 and IQ4_NL quantizations, which can improve performance on compatible hardware. The Q4_0 quantization has been deprecated in favor of the Q4_0 format with online repacking. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system (EPYC7702) show that the Q4_K_M quantization offers improved tokens per second compared to Q4_0 in most cases, while the Q4_0_8_8 quantization provides a performance boost for prompt processing and text generation. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, with recommendations for maximizing speed or quality based on RAM and VRAM availability. I-quants (e.g., IQ3_M) are newer and offer better performance for their size on cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD) systems, though they may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants (e.g., Q5_K_M). The model includes contributions from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, ZeroWw for inspiration on embed/output quantization, and LM Studio for sponsorship.

instruction: Can you assist in identifying the most relevant product descriptions for a given query?
description: The Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B is a text embedding model from the Qwen3 Embedding series, designed for tasks such as text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. It is part of a series that includes models of various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B) and provides both embedding and reranking capabilities. The model is built upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series and inherits their multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills. It supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and offers robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B has 0.6 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and supports embedding dimensions up to 1024, with user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 1024. The model is instruction-aware, meaning it supports customizing input instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. Evaluation results show that the 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard with a score of 70.58 (as of June 5, 2025), and the Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B achieves a mean score of 64.33 on the MTEB multilingual benchmark. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for their tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance, as most training instructions were in English. The model can be used with frameworks such as Sentence Transformers, Transformers, and vLLM, and supports deployment via Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on both GPU and CPU devices. The model is available for use with specific versions of libraries and requires attention to compatibility with older versions of Transformers. The Qwen3 Embedding series is described as offering exceptional versatility and comprehensive flexibility, with a full spectrum of sizes and features to cater to diverse use cases.

instruction: I want to convert a recorded message in Greek into text for better clarity. Can you assist?
description: The parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is an on-device multilingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) model converted to Core ML for Apple platforms. It powers FluidAudio’s batch ASR and is the same model used in the backend. The model supports 25 European languages and is optimized for low-latency, private, offline transcription. It is based on the Parakeet TDT v3 architecture, a Token Duration Transducer with 0.6 billion parameters. The model accepts 16 kHz, mono, Float32 PCM audio input in the range [-1, 1]. It is implemented in mixed precision optimized for Core ML execution on Apple Neural Engine (ANE) and CPU. The model achieves a real-time factor (RTF) of approximately 110× on M4 Pro in batch mode, meaning 1 minute of audio takes about 0.5 seconds to transcribe. Performance, including throughput and latency, varies depending on the device, input duration, and compute units used. The model is intended for batch transcription of complete audio files on macOS and iOS, local dictation and note-taking applications where privacy and latency are important, and embedded ASR in production apps via the FluidAudio Swift framework. It is supported on macOS 14+ (Apple Silicon recommended) and iOS 17+. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. It is primarily optimized for European languages, and performance may degrade for non-European languages.

instruction: Is the camera panning left or right in this recording?
description: The model is a variant that has been fine-tuned for multi-label video classification, specifically for camera motion classification on an internal dataset. It is designed to predict 18 different camera motion classes, including 'arc_left', 'arc_right', 'dolly_in', 'dolly_out', 'pan_left', 'pan_right', 'pedestal_down', 'pedestal_up', 'roll_left', 'roll_right', 'static', 'tilt_down', 'tilt_up', 'truck_left', 'truck_right', 'undefined', 'zoom_in', and 'zoom_out', as well as 3 shot type classes: 'pov', 'shake', and 'track'. The model is trained to associate the entire video with camera motion labels, rather than frame-level motions, meaning it outputs one or more labels for the whole video. If a camera motion is not present throughout the entire video, the model should predict 'undefined'. The model processes 16 frames per input clip, extracted uniformly from the video, regardless of its original duration. For videos longer than 2 seconds, it is recommended to split the video into non-overlapping 2-second segments, run inference on each segment independently, and then post-process the results. The model achieves an accuracy of 75% on an internal test dataset of 2-second videos, and approximately 84% when excluding the 'pov', 'shake', and 'track' classes.

instruction: Can you create a realistic 3D living room based on a photo I have of my own?
description: SpatialGen is a multi-view, multi-modal diffusion model designed to generate 3D indoor scenes conditioned on either a reference image or a textual description, given a 3D semantic layout. The model supports tasks such as image-to-3D scene generation and text-to-3D scene generation. It is intended for use in 3D scene generation applications and is based on a diffusion model architecture. The model has been developed as part of a research project and is associated with a paper published in the International Conference on 3D Vision in 2026. The model is currently available for inference, with a dataset containing 48 rooms and 4.8K rendered images provided for multi-view diffusion inference. The model is derived from another project and is licensed under the same license as that project. The model is tested with Python 3.10, PyTorch 2.3.1, and CUDA Version 12.1. Training instructions and a full dataset release are planned for future updates.

instruction: Can you detect any patterns in the rhythm of this 1-minute percussion track?
description: Audio Flamingo 3 (AF3) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) that advances reasoning and understanding across speech, sounds, and music. AF3 builds on previous work with innovations in unified audio representation learning (speech, sound, music), flexible, on-demand chain-of-thought reasoning, long-context audio comprehension (up to 10 minutes), multi-turn, multi-audio conversational dialogue (AF3-Chat), and voice-to-voice interaction (AF3-Chat). Extensive evaluations confirm AF3’s effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 20 public audio understanding and reasoning tasks. This model is for non-commercial research purposes only. AF3 uses an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder, an MLP-based audio adaptor, a Decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B), and a Streaming TTS module (AF3-Chat). Audio Flamingo 3 can take up to 10 minutes of audio inputs. The model is supported in 🤗 Transformers and processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample. Longer inputs are truncated. AF3 supports single-turn audio and text instruction, multi-turn chat, batch multiple conversations, text-only and audio-only prompts, and transcription of local or remote files. It also supports think-mode reasoning with a PEFT adapter (AF-Think) and training or fine-tuning. AF3 can be optimized for inference using vLLM for up to 5-7x speedup, Flash Attention 2, Torch compile, or PyTorch SDPA. The model is designed to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100) and is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers. AF3 accepts audio in WAV, MP3, or FLAC formats and text in UTF-8, with a maximum audio length of 10 minutes and a maximum text length of 16000 tokens. It outputs text in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum text length of 1024 tokens, and can optionally produce speech in the form of a streaming TTS waveform. AF3 is trained entirely on open-source audio data, organized into four novel, large-scale collections. The data collection method for all training datasets is human, with labeling methods varying between automated and human. AF3 is evaluated on the test split of multiple datasets, with data collection methods also being human for most datasets, and labeling methods varying. The model is intended for researchers and developers to explore audio question answering and reasoning, long-context audio comprehension, interactive sound/music design assistants, and multi-turn (voice) chat. AF3 was released on 07/10/2025 via GitHub and HuggingFace. The model is released under a license, with portions of the dataset generation also subject to other licenses and OpenAI’s terms. NVIDIA encourages responsible use of the model and asks users to report any security vulnerabilities or AI concerns.

instruction: Can you determine the category of this diving performance?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta, extending the pretraining objectives of its predecessors to achieve state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities. It leverages large-scale data and model sizes to enhance performance. The model is based on a ViT-L architecture with a 256-pixel input size and includes a video classification head. It is pretrained on the Diving 48 dataset, which consists of diving-related video content. The model is designed to support video classification tasks, enabling the prediction of class labels for input videos. Performance is demonstrated through inference examples, where the model outputs top predicted class names along with their probabilities. The model requires specific preprocessing steps, including frame sampling and tensor conversion, to prepare input data for inference. Users are advised to install the latest version of the transformers library and use the provided code snippet to load the model and perform video classification. The model's configuration includes a frames_per_clip parameter, which defines the number of frames to sample from each video clip. The model outputs logits, which are then converted into probabilities using softmax to determine the top predicted classes. The model's training and evaluation are detailed in a technical report published by FAIR at Meta in 2025.

instruction: Is the following sentence likely to be sarcastic or sincere: 'Oh, of course, another brilliant idea'?
description: This is an ONNX-optimized version of a RoBERTa-base model for efficient inference. The repository contains a regular ONNX exported model and an INT8 dynamically quantized model for faster inference with minimal accuracy loss. The model is optimized for zero-shot classification tasks across multiple languages. The quantized model provides faster inference, with a speedup of approximately 2-3x compared to PyTorch, a smaller size due to INT8 quantization, and maintained accuracy with less than 1% loss compared to the original model. The base model is RoBERTa-base, and it is designed for zero-shot classification or natural language inference tasks. The model was converted from PyTorch to ONNX format and uses dynamic quantization with INT8 weights. It is implemented using ONNX Runtime with Optimum. The model is licensed under the MIT License. If used, it should be cited as part of the original RoBERTa Base Zero-Shot Classification model by Laurer, Atteveldt, Casas, and Welbers in 2022. This ONNX optimization was created for efficient deployment in production environments, with special thanks to the original model authors and the Hugging Face Optimum team.

instruction: Can you confirm if the person in this audio is speaking clearly or if it’s too noisy?
description: Ultravox is a multimodal Speech LLM that can consume both speech and text as input, such as a text system prompt and a voice user message. The input to the model is given as a text prompt with a special pseudo-token, and the model processor replaces this token with embeddings derived from the input audio. Using the merged embeddings as input, the model generates output text as usual. In the v0.6 series, Ultravox models are trained on expanded Hindi speech data, resulting in significantly improved speech understanding performance on Hindi and modest degradation on other languages. Additionally, the v0.6 models are trained on noise datasets for improved noise robustness and the ability to output a special string '((noise))' if the input audio is too noisy or doesn't contain clear speech. In a future revision of Ultravox, the plan is to expand the token vocabulary to support the generation of semantic and acoustic audio tokens, which can then be fed to a vocoder to produce voice output. No preference tuning has been applied to this revision of the model. Ultravox is built around a pretrained LLM (Llama, Gemma, Qwen, etc) and a speech encoder backbone. The multi-modal adapter is trained, the Whisper encoder is fine-tuned, and the LLM is kept frozen. The model uses a knowledge-distillation loss where Ultravox is trying to match the logits of the text-based LLM backbone. The training dataset is a mix of ASR datasets, extended with continuations generated by Llama 3.1 8B, speech translation datasets, and noise datasets. The training procedure involves supervised speech instruction finetuning via knowledge-distillation. The training regime uses BF16 mixed precision training on 8x H100 GPUs. The current version of Ultravox, when invoked with audio content, has a time-to-first-token (TTFT) of approximately 150ms, and a tokens-per-second rate of ~50-100 when using an A100-40GB GPU, all using a text-based LLM (Llama, Gemma, or Qwen) backbone. Evaluations are conducted on covost2 (speech translation measured in BLEU), fleurs and ultravox_calls (speech recognition measured in WER), big bench audio (audio reasoning measured in accuracy), as well as musan and ultravox_unintelligible (noise/unintelligible speech detection measured in recall). The model is developed by Fixie.ai and is licensed under the MIT license. It can be used as a voice agent, for speech-to-speech translation, and for the analysis of spoken audio.

instruction: I need a segmentation of the person’s legs and torso in this fashion image.
description: The FASHN Human Parser is a SegFormer-B4 model fine-tuned for human parsing with 18 semantic classes, optimized for fashion and virtual try-on applications. It segments human images into 18 semantic categories, including body parts such as face, hair, arms, hands, legs, feet, and torso; clothing items such as top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, and scarf; and accessories such as bag, hat, glasses, and jewelry. The model uses a SegFormer-B4 architecture, which includes an MIT-B4 encoder and an MLP decoder. It accepts input images of size 384 x 576 and produces 18-class semantic segmentation masks. The model was fine-tuned on a proprietary dataset curated and annotated by FASHN AI, specifically designed for virtual try-on applications. The 18-class label schema was developed to capture the semantic regions most relevant for clothing transfer and human body understanding in fashion contexts. The model is optimized for single-person images with clear visibility and performs best on fashion or e-commerce style photography. Input images are resized to 384x576, and very small subjects may lose detail. The label definitions include background, face, hair, top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, bag, hat, scarf, glasses, arms, hands, legs, feet, torso, and jewelry. For virtual try-on applications, the categories are mapped as follows: Tops (upper body) include top, dress, and scarf; Bottoms (lower body) include skirt, pants, and belt; and One-pieces (full body) include top, dress, scarf, skirt, pants, and belt. Labels typically preserved during virtual try-on include face, hair, jewelry, bag, glasses, and hat.

instruction: Draw a futuristic city at night with holographic billboards and autonomous cars.
description: The Stable Diffusion v2-1-base is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model designed to generate and modify images based on text prompts. It is a latent diffusion model that combines an autoencoder with a diffusion model trained in the latent space of the autoencoder. The model uses a fixed, pretrained text encoder for processing text prompts. It was developed by Robin Rombach and Patrick Esser and is primarily intended for research purposes. The model supports tasks such as the safe deployment of generative models, understanding their limitations and biases, artistic creation, and educational or creative tool development. The model is not intended for misuse, including the generation of harmful, offensive, or discriminatory content, or the creation of mis- and disinformation. The model has several limitations, including the inability to achieve perfect photorealism, difficulties in rendering legible text, challenges with compositional tasks, and potential issues with generating faces and people. It was trained mainly on English captions and may not perform well with non-English prompts. The model was trained on a subset of the LAION-5B dataset, which contains adult, violent, and sexual content, and was partially filtered using LAION's NSFW detector. The model's training procedure involves encoding images into latent representations using an autoencoder with a downsampling factor of 8, and encoding text prompts using the OpenCLIP-ViT/H text encoder. The text encoder's output is fed into the UNet backbone of the latent diffusion model via cross-attention, with a reconstruction objective and the v-objective used for training. The model was trained using 32 x 8 x A100 GPUs, the AdamW optimizer, a batch size of 2048, and a learning rate that warmed up to 0.0001 over 10,000 steps. Evaluation results show relative improvements of the checkpoints using different classifier-free guidance scales and 50 steps of DDIM sampling. The model's environmental impact includes an estimated CO2 emission of 15,000 kg eq. based on 200,000 hours of A100 GPU usage on AWS in the US-east region.

instruction: How confident are you in determining the prognosis from this biopsy sample?
description: Digepath is a self-supervised foundation model for intelligent gastrointestinal pathology image analysis. The model is a Vision Transformer Large/16 with DINO-V2 self-supervised pre-training on 353 million multi-scale images from 210,043 H&E-stained gastrointestinal related slides. Gastrointestinal (GI) diseases represent a clinically significant burden, necessitating precise diagnostic approaches to optimize patient outcomes. Conventional histopathological diagnosis suffers from limited reproducibility and diagnostic variability. To overcome these limitations, Digepath was developed as a specialized foundation model for GI pathology. The framework introduces a dual-phase iterative optimization strategy combining pretraining with fine-screening, specifically designed to address the detection of sparsely distributed lesion areas in whole-slide images. Digepath was initially pretrained on a large-scale dataset comprising over 353 million multi-scale images derived from 210,043 H&E-stained slides of GI diseases. It was subsequently fine-tuned on 471,443 carefully selected regions of interest (ROIs) in the second stage. It attains state-of-the-art performance on 32 out of 33 tasks related to GI pathology, including pathological diagnosis, protein expression status prediction, gene mutation prediction, and prognosis evaluation. Digepath demonstrates broad applicability across diverse clinical tasks, highlighting its potential for reliable deployment in real-world pathology workflows.

instruction: What is the main subject in this image of a forest, and what other elements are visible?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It handles long contexts and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling full recall and second-level indexing for books and hours-long video. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and ModelScope, with generation hyperparameters available for both visual and text tasks. The model is described in technical reports and preprints available on arXiv.

instruction: I recorded a conversation in Swedish and need it transcribed quickly. Is that possible?
description: The parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is an on-device multilingual automatic speech recognition (ASR) model converted to Core ML for Apple platforms. It powers FluidAudio’s batch ASR and is the same model used in the backend. The model supports 25 European languages and is optimized for low-latency, private, offline transcription. It is based on the Parakeet TDT v3 architecture, a Token Duration Transducer with 0.6 billion parameters. The model accepts 16 kHz, mono, Float32 PCM audio input in the range [-1, 1]. It is implemented in mixed precision optimized for Core ML execution on Apple Neural Engine (ANE) and CPU. The model achieves a real-time factor (RTF) of approximately 110× on M4 Pro in batch mode, meaning 1 minute of audio takes about 0.5 seconds to transcribe. Performance, including throughput and latency, varies depending on the device, input duration, and compute units used. The model is intended for batch transcription of complete audio files on macOS and iOS, local dictation and note-taking applications where privacy and latency are important, and embedded ASR in production apps via the FluidAudio Swift framework. It is supported on macOS 14+ (Apple Silicon recommended) and iOS 17+. The model is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. It is primarily optimized for European languages, and performance may degrade for non-European languages.

instruction: Identify any objects in this image that might be considered uncommon in a typical traffic scene.
description: The `fisheye8k_facebook_deformable-detr-detic` model is a component of the **Mcity Data Engine**, an open-source system designed for iterative model improvement through open-vocabulary data selection. This engine provides modules for the complete data-based development cycle, from data acquisition to model deployment, specifically addressing the challenge of detecting long-tail and novel classes in large amounts of unlabeled data, particularly in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). The model is a fine-tuned version of the `deformable-detr-detic` base model, specializing in object detection within the context of the Mcity Data Engine's workflows. It enables the identification of objects even for classes not seen during its initial fine-tuning, through its open-vocabulary capabilities. The model is primarily intended for **zero-shot object detection** within Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) and related domains. It is designed to assist researchers and practitioners in identifying rare and novel classes of interest from raw visual data, facilitating the continuous improvement of AI models. Potential use cases include detecting various types of road users and vehicles in complex traffic scenarios, identifying long-tail or previously unseen objects in automotive perception datasets, and serving as a component within larger data curation and model training pipelines. Limitations include potential variability in performance on highly out-of-distribution scenarios, the requirement for a powerful GPU for optimal utilization of the Mcity Data Engine workflows, and possible differences in performance on standard perspective images, as the model was fine-tuned on fisheye camera data. The model was fine-tuned on the Fisheye8K dataset, with the Mcity Data Engine leveraging data selection processes to focus on detecting long-tail and novel classes, which is crucial for ITS applications. The model's `config.json` indicates it was fine-tuned for classes such as 'Bus', 'Bike', 'Car', 'Pedestrian', and 'Truck'. During training, the following hyperparameters were used: learning_rate of 5e-05, train_batch_size of 1, eval_batch_size of 8, seed of 0, optimizer of ADAMW_TORCH with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, lr_scheduler_type of cosine, num_epochs of 36, and mixed_precision_training using Native AMP. Training loss values decreased over the course of training, with a final training loss of 1.5963 at epoch 16. The validation loss reached a minimum of 1.8862 at epoch 11, and the final validation loss was 2.1348. The model was developed using Transformers 4.48.3, Pytorch 2.5.1+cu124, Datasets 3.2.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.0. Mcity acknowledges Amazon Web Services (AWS) for their pivotal role in providing the cloud infrastructure on which the Data Engine depends. Special thanks are given to contributors to the Mcity Data Engine.

instruction: Which of these Indonesian documents best answers the question about health and nutrition?
description: The Multilingual-E5-base model is a multilingual text embedding model with 12 layers and an embedding size of 768. It is designed to encode queries and passages for tasks such as passage retrieval, semantic similarity, and bitext mining. The model supports 100 languages, including English, Chinese, and others, but performance may degrade for low-resource languages. It is initialized from a multilingual foundation and trained on a mixture of multilingual datasets. The first stage of training involves contrastive pre-training with weak supervision using a large number of text pairs from various sources, including titles, page content, news content, translation pairs, and more. The second stage involves supervised fine-tuning on labeled datasets, primarily in English and Chinese. The model is evaluated on benchmark tasks and achieves an average MRR@10 score of 65.9, outperforming baselines such as BM25 and mDPR. It is also evaluated on the MTEB benchmark. The model requires input texts to be prefixed with 'query: ' or 'passage: ' to maintain performance, as this is how it was trained. Cosine similarity scores typically range from 0.7 to 1.0 due to the use of a low temperature in the InfoNCE contrastive loss, but the relative order of scores is what matters for tasks like text retrieval. Long texts are truncated to a maximum of 512 tokens.

instruction: Can you estimate the toxicity level of this chemical compound based on its SMILES string?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict toxicity-related molecular properties. The model is designed to perform regression tasks, taking SMILES strings as input and using graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an architecture based on AttentiveFP with enhanced atom and bond features. The model uses 10-dimensional enhanced atom features and 6-dimensional enhanced bond features. The hyperparameters include 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, 0.1 dropout, 0.0005 learning rate, 0.0001 weight decay, 32 batch size, 50 epochs, and 10 patience. The model was trained on the TMP dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Create a scene where a cat is sitting on a windowsill watching the rain fall outside.
description: This model is a controlnet module designed for the Wan2.2 model. It follows the same approach as the controlnet implementation for Wan2.2. The model is intended for use with ComfyUI and supports inference tasks for generating video content based on text prompts and control frames. The model architecture includes a custom controlnet module integrated with the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers base model. Inference is performed using a pipeline that incorporates a controlnet, a transformer, and an autoencoder. The model processes video frames with Gaussian blur and resizing to generate output video sequences. Performance is evaluated through inference examples, including a detailed prompt for generating a video of a young woman blowing a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model includes parameters for guidance scale, controlnet weight, and frame resolution. The model is available on GitHub, and users are encouraged to raise issues directly in the repository. Professional support can be requested via email.

instruction: Please translate this email from German to French, keeping the formal tone and structure consistent.
description: The Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporate 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers translation performance comparable to its larger counterpart, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. HY-MT1.5-1.8B supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B, compared to its September open-source version, has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models support translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple languages, including English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, Uyghur, and Cantonese. The models can be used with llama.cpp and ollama for inference, with recommended parameters including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The models are available in different quantization versions, including FP8 and Int4, and can be downloaded from Hugging Face.

instruction: Can you provide a detailed analysis of the road signs and lane markings visible in the scene?
description: HERMES is a unified Driving World Model designed for simultaneous 3D scene understanding and generation in autonomous driving scenarios. It integrates scene understanding, which involves interpreting and reasoning about the driving environment, with future scene evolution (generation). HERMES uses a Bird's-Eye View (BEV) representation to consolidate multi-view spatial information while preserving geometric relationships and interactions. The model introduces world queries that incorporate world knowledge into BEV features through causal attention in a Large Language Model (LLM), enabling contextual enrichment for both understanding and generation tasks. HERMES was evaluated on the nuScenes and OmniDrive-nuScenes datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance with a 32.4% reduction in generation error and an 8.0% improvement in understanding metrics such as CIDEr. The model is based on prior works including BEVFormer v2, InternVL, UniPAD, OmniDrive, and DriveMonkey. HERMES is open-sourced, with code, pretrained weights, and processed data available. A demo has also been released.

instruction: Rewrite a poem in Japanese into English, ensuring the poetic structure is maintained.
description: LMT-60 is a suite of Chinese-English-centric multilingual machine translation (MMT) models trained on 90B tokens, including both monolingual and bilingual data, covering 60 languages across 234 translation directions. The model achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among models with similar language coverage. Both the CPT and SFT versions of LMT-60 are available in four sizes: 0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, and 8B. The supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data are publicly released. The model supports translation tasks across a wide range of languages, including 13 high-resource languages, 18 medium-resource languages, and 29 low-resource languages. The high-resource languages include Arabic, English, Spanish, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Turkish, and Chinese. The medium-resource languages include Bulgarian, Bengali, Czech, Danish, Modern Greek, Persian, Finnish, Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Korean, Norwegian, Romanian, Slovak, Swedish, Thai, Ukrainian, and Vietnamese. The low-resource languages include Amharic, Azerbaijani, Tibetan, Modern Hebrew, Croatian, Armenian, Icelandic, Javanese, Georgian, Kazakh, Central Khmer, Kirghiz, Lao, Chinese Mongolian, Marathi, Malay, Burmese, Nepali, Pashto, Sinhala, Swahili, Tamil, Telugu, Tajik, Tagalog, Uighur, Urdu, Uzbek, and Yue Chinese. The model is designed to perform translation tasks and is available for use through the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model is described in a paper titled 'Beyond English: Toward Inclusive and Scalable Multilingual Machine Translation with LLMs,' authored by Yingfeng Luo, Ziqiang Xu, Yuxuan Ouyang, Murun Yang, Dingyang Lin, Kaiyan Chang, Tong Zheng, Bei Li, Peinan Feng, Quan Du, Tong Xiao, and Jingbo Zhu, and available on arXiv with the identifier 2511.07003.

instruction: Could you create a high-resolution version of a scene viewed from a slightly different angle?
description: This is a Core ML (.mlpackage) version of SHARP, a model for photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of a scene from a single photograph, which can then be rendered in real time to produce high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The model is metric, with absolute scale, and supports metric camera movements. The Core ML version is optimized for CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine inference on macOS and iOS. The model accepts a 3-channel RGB image in uint8 format with shape (1, 3, H, W) and a scalar disparity factor representing the ratio of focal length to image width. It outputs five tensors representing a 3D Gaussian splat representation: 3D positions, scale parameters, orientation quaternions, linear RGB colors, and opacity values. The total number of Gaussians is approximately 1,179,648 for the default model. The Core ML model has been validated against the original PyTorch implementation, with all outputs matching within 0.01% mean error. Quaternion angular errors are below 1° for 99% of Gaussians. Inference on an Apple M4 Max takes approximately 1.9 seconds. The model is designed to be used with the provided inference script, which includes features such as automatic model compilation and caching, decimation to reduce point cloud size while preserving visual fidelity, and compatibility with standard RGB input images. The model is not affiliated with the original research paper titled 'Sharp Monocular View Synthesis in Less Than a Second' by Lars Mescheder, Wei Dong, Shiwei Li, Xuyang Bai, Marcel Santos, Peiyun Hu, Bruno Lecouat, Mingmin Zhen, Amaël Delaunoy, Tian Fang, Yanghai Tsin, Stephan Richter, and Vladlen Koltun.

instruction: Design a multi-angle view of a fantasy landscape using a single image and a description of the surroundings.
description: MV-AR is a diffusion-based model designed for generating multi-view consistent images. It addresses the limitations of existing methods that rely on a specific reference view for predicting subsequent views, which can lead to poor image quality and inconsistency when there is minimal overlap between the reference and predicted views. MV-AR instead conditions on the preceding view with significant overlap, improving the quality and consistency of the generated multi-view images. The model supports tasks such as text-to-multi-view image generation, image-to-multi-view image generation, and text-plus-geometry-to-multi-view image generation. The model is based on prior work, and its implementation is available for reproduction with specific pretrained models and datasets. Generated images are saved in designated directories following execution of provided shell scripts. The model's development and methodology are described in an arXiv preprint titled 'Auto-Regressively Generating Multi-View Consistent Images' by Hu, JiaKui and others.

instruction: How can I determine if these two technical documents are semantically equivalent?
description: The all-MiniLM-L12-v2 is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384-dimensional dense vector space. It is designed for tasks such as clustering and semantic search. The model can be used with the Sentence-Transformers library by installing it via pip and encoding sentences using the SentenceTransformer class. Alternatively, it can be used with the HuggingFace Transformers library by applying a mean pooling operation on the contextualized word embeddings and normalizing the resulting sentence embeddings. The model was developed using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective, where it was fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs. The model was trained on a TPU v3-8 for 100,000 steps with a batch size of 1024, a learning rate of 2e-5, and a sequence length of 128 tokens. The training data was a combination of multiple datasets, including citation pairs, duplicate question pairs, question-answer pairs, and image captions, among others. The model is intended to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder, outputting a vector that captures semantic information for tasks such as information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default.

instruction: Can you segment every bird in this video using the phrase 'a flying animal with wings'?
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images and videos, as well as promptable visual segmentation (PVS) for images and videos. For image PCS, the model takes text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returns segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It supports text-only prompts, single or multiple bounding box prompts (positive and negative), and combined prompts (text + negative box). It also supports batched inference with text prompts and batched mixed prompts. For video PCS, the model takes text as prompts and detects and tracks all matching object instances across video frames. It supports pre-loaded video inference and streaming video inference. For image PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It supports single point clicks, multiple points for refinement, bounding box input, and multiple objects segmentation. It also supports batch inference. For video PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to track a specific object instance per prompt across video frames. It supports basic video tracking, multi-object video tracking, and streaming video inference. SAM 3 also provides semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. The model is an updated version of SAM2 and SAM2 Video, maintaining the same API while providing improved performance, making it a drop-in replacement for SAM2 workflows. It is available through the Transformers library and supports both image and video processing. For best results, pre-loaded video inference is recommended when all frames are available, as streaming inference may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks due to the disabling of hotstart heuristics.

instruction: What motion is the person performing in this clip from a sports demonstration?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities by leveraging large-scale data and model sizes. This specific model variant is based on the ViT-g 384 architecture and includes a video classification head. It was pretrained on the Something-Something-V2 dataset. The model is designed to support video classification tasks and is intended for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library. Performance results are not explicitly detailed in the provided text. The model includes a warning that the code is released but no further caveats or limitations are specified.

instruction: Generate a short story based on this photo of a forest at sunset.
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. The raw parameter count of the model is 8B, but the architecture design allows the model to be run with a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 4B model by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator. The models use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the E4B model. One sub-model is provided, or a spectrum of custom-sized models can be accessed. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens, subtracting the request input tokens. Input images are normalized to 256x256, 512x512, or 768x768 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. Audio data is encoded to 6.25 tokens per second from a single channel. The models were trained on a dataset totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to the training data, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. The models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways, which allow for efficient training of large models. The models were evaluated at full precision (float32) against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of content generation. Evaluation results show performance improvements over previous Gemma models in categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. The models have certain limitations, including potential biases or gaps in the training data, challenges with open-ended or highly complex tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and the possibility of generating incorrect or outdated factual statements. Ethical considerations include the risk of perpetuating biases, generating harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to implement appropriate content safety safeguards and adhere to privacy regulations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, and education. They provide superior performance compared to other, similarly sized open model alternatives.

instruction: I want to know which parts of the image match the phrase 'a red bicycle'. Can you show me?
description: This tiny model is intended for debugging purposes. It is randomly initialized using a configuration adapted from a source model. The model is designed to perform instance segmentation tasks, supporting inputs such as images, text prompts, and bounding box annotations. It includes a vision encoder, a text encoder, a geometry encoder, a DETR encoder, a DETR decoder, and a mask decoder. The vision encoder processes images through a vision transformer with a patch embedding layer, multiple transformer layers, and a feature pyramid network. The text encoder is based on a CLIP text model with projection layers. The geometry encoder processes box and label information with attention and pooling operations. The DETR encoder and decoder handle cross-modal interactions between vision and text, while the mask decoder generates segmentation masks. The model supports negative box annotations to exclude specific regions from segmentation. The architecture includes multiple attention mechanisms, layer normalization, and feed-forward networks. The model is randomly initialized and not trained on any specific dataset, and as such, it is not intended for production use or real-world applications.

instruction: Create a 3D CT image of a brain with a well-defined tumor in the frontal lobe.
description: This model is a 3D latent diffusion model (RFlow) combined with a 3D VAE and a CLIP3D text encoder, designed for text-to-CT generation. It is intended for research and experimentation in text-conditioned 3D CT synthesis, including the generation of synthetic data for benchmarking or augmentation. The model supports tasks such as CT volume generation from textual descriptions. The architecture includes a 3D VAE for latent compression and decoding, a diffusion UNet trained with rectified flow in latent space, and a CLIP3D text encoder for report embeddings. The model was trained on the CT-RATE dataset, which includes CT volumes and reports, and the CLIP3D component was trained for vision-language alignment on this dataset. The diffusion UNet was trained using rectified flow in latent space, conditioned on text embeddings. The model is not validated for clinical use and may encode dataset biases. Synthetic outputs may contain artifacts and should not be used for patient care. Evaluation results are detailed in the associated paper. The model is released under an Apache 2.0 license. The model weights include an autoencoder checkpoint, a diffusion UNet checkpoint, and CLIP3D weights for report embeddings. The model is not intended for clinical decision-making, diagnostic use, or deployment without proper validation and approvals. Training was conducted using a multi-GPU setup, though environmental impact was not reported.

instruction: Can you create a short animation showing a sunrise over a mountain range in slow motion?
description: The FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is part of the FastWan2.1 series, which is finetuned using a novel Sparse-distill strategy. This strategy combines DMD and VSA in a single training process, leveraging distillation to reduce diffusion steps and sparse attention to minimize attention computations, resulting in faster video generation. The model is based on Wan-AI/Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers and supports efficient 3-step inference, producing high-quality videos at a resolution of 61×448×832. It is trained on the FastVideo 480P Synthetic Wan dataset, which contains 600k synthetic latents. The model can achieve up to 16 FPS on a single H100 GPU. While it is trained on 61×448×832 resolution, it can generate videos at any resolution, though quality may degrade. The model is trained on 4 nodes with 32 H200 GPUs in total, using a global batch size of 64, gradient checkpointing, gradient accumulation steps of 2, and a learning rate of 1e-5. The VSA attention sparsity is set to 0.8, and training runs for 4000 steps, which takes approximately 12 hours. The model supports a wide range of GPUs from H100 to 4090 and is also compatible with Mac users.
