If the FastWan2.1-T2V-1.3B-Diffusers model is used for research, the associated papers should be cited.

instruction: How would you classify a news piece that discusses both a natural disaster and government response?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual training data in English, German, Hungarian, Spanish, and Slovakian, labeled with media codes. The model incorporates seven additional media codes: State and Local Government Administration (24), Weather and Natural Disaster (26), Fires (27), Sports and Recreation (29), Death Notices (30), Churches and Religion (31), and Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (99). The model is designed for text classification tasks and is intended to categorize input text into one of the defined media codes. It is based on the xlm-roberta-large architecture and is used with a sentencepiece tokenizer. The model achieves an overall accuracy of 74%, with a macro average precision of 0.76, recall of 0.74, and F1-score of 0.73. The weighted average precision, recall, and F1-score are also 0.76, 0.74, and 0.73, respectively. Per-class performance varies, with some classes achieving high precision and recall, such as Death Notices (precision 0.9565, recall 0.88) and others showing lower performance, such as Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (precision 0.3789, recall 0.72). The model is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool designed to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed. The model requires a token for access, and users may need to pass the token parameter when loading the model. In earlier versions of the Transformers package, the use_auth_token parameter may be required instead. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue.

instruction: Can you recommend a tune that fits the description 'a nostalgic walk down memory lane'?
description: MuQ is a large music foundation model pre-trained via Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), achieving state-of-the-art performance in various MIR (Music Information Retrieval) tasks. MuQ-MuLan is a music-text joint embedding model trained via contrastive learning, supporting both English and Chinese texts. MuQ is based on a self-supervised learning approach using Mel Residual Vector Quantization and is designed to extract music audio features. MuQ-MuLan is designed to extract both music and text embeddings and calculate their similarity. MuQ has approximately 300 million parameters and was trained on the Million Song Dataset. MuQ-MuLan has approximately 700 million parameters and was trained on music-text pairs. It is important to note that the open-sourced MuQ model may not achieve the same level of performance as reported in the paper due to differences in dataset size.

instruction: Can you help me find the best version of the software for my computer's hardware?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the gemma-3n-E2B-it model by Google, processed using the llama.cpp release b5760. The quantizations are based on a dataset provided for calibration. The model is intended to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. No specific chat template is provided, so a default prompt format is used, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The model offers a variety of quantization options, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights, which may differ from the default quantization method. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, particularly for the Q4_0 and IQ4_NL quantizations, which can improve performance on compatible hardware. The Q4_0 quantization has been deprecated in favor of newer formats that support online repacking. For users with AVX2 systems, benchmarks show that the Q4_K_M quantization provides a significant performance improvement over Q4_0 in prompt processing, while Q4_0_8_8 offers a moderate improvement in both prompt processing and text generation. When choosing a quantization, users should consider their available RAM and VRAM, as well as whether they prioritize speed or quality. K-quants, such as Q5_K_M, are generally recommended for most use cases, while I-quants, such as IQ3_M, may offer better performance for smaller quantizations when using cuBLAS or rocBLAS. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with options to download specific files or entire branches. The model was created with assistance from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, and inspired by ZeroWw's work on embedding and output weights. Support for the model's development was provided by LM Studio.

instruction: Make an image of a modern office building at night with all the windows lit up.
description: Z-Image is a powerful and highly efficient image generation model with 6B parameters. There are three variants: Z-Image-Turbo, Z-Image-Base, and Z-Image-Edit. Z-Image-Turbo is a distilled version that matches or exceeds leading competitors with only 8 NFEs (Number of Function Evaluations). It offers sub-second inference latency on enterprise-grade H800 GPUs and fits within 16G VRAM consumer devices. It excels in photorealistic image generation, bilingual text rendering (English and Chinese), and robust instruction adherence. Z-Image-Base is the non-distilled foundation model, intended for community-driven fine-tuning and custom development. Z-Image-Edit is a variant fine-tuned for image editing tasks, supporting creative image-to-image generation with strong instruction-following capabilities, allowing for precise edits based on natural language prompts. The model uses a Scalable Single-Stream DiT (S3-DiT) architecture, where text, visual semantic tokens, and image VAE tokens are concatenated at the sequence level to serve as a unified input stream, maximizing parameter efficiency compared to dual-stream approaches. According to the Elo-based Human Preference Evaluation, Z-Image-Turbo shows highly competitive performance against other leading models and achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source models. The model is supported by the Decoupled-DMD algorithm, a core few-step distillation method that enables the 8-step Z-Image model. Decoupled-DMD is based on the insight that the success of existing DMD methods results from two independent mechanisms: CFG Augmentation, which drives the distillation process, and Distribution Matching, which ensures the stability and quality of the generated output. By decoupling these mechanisms, the model's distillation process is optimized for performance. Building on Decoupled-DMD, the model also incorporates DMDR, which fuses DMD with Reinforcement Learning to further improve semantic alignment, aesthetic quality, and structural coherence, while producing images with richer high-frequency details. DMDR integrates RL and DMD during the post-training of few-step models, demonstrating that RL unlocks the performance of DMD while DMD effectively regularizes RL.

instruction: How does the abliteration process affect the model's refusal rate and overall performance?
description: Gemma 3n E4B IT is a fine-tuned version of the Gemma 3n model, produced using a modified abliteration engine. It has the best abliteration scores and can be used without a specialized system prompt for removing censorship. Use it with extreme caution and care. This repository contains static quants, while the quants have been moved to a separate repository and regenerated with a larger multilingual dataset. Vision support has been merged into llama.cpp, requiring a git pull and rebuild. Abliteration scores include 11 refusals out of 100 and a KL divergence of 0.1422. The model has 26 abliterated layers, 9 skipped layers, and modifications to 8 attn.o_proj matrices and 26 mlp.down_proj matrices. Gemma 3n models are a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. They are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and can handle multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, generating text outputs. These models were trained with data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters. The models support input in the form of text strings, images normalized to specific resolutions, audio data encoded at a specific rate, and a total input context of 32K tokens. Outputs include generated text in response to the input, with a total output length up to 32K tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content. Gemma was trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. The models were evaluated on a range of benchmarks, showing strong performance in reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional categories. Ethics and safety evaluations were conducted, covering child safety, content safety, and representational harms. The models have limitations related to training data, context and task complexity, language ambiguity, factual accuracy, and common sense. Ethical considerations include bias and fairness, misinformation and misuse, transparency and accountability, and privacy. The models are intended for use in content creation, research, and education, with guidelines for responsible use provided.

instruction: Create an embedding for this sentence that can be used to find similar documents.
description: UME-R1 is a model that has undergone a cold-start SFT stage and an RL stage of training. It is capable of embedding text, images, multiple images, and videos. The model can generate either discriminative or generative embeddings as needed, and the generative embeddings possess the potential for test-time scaling. UME-R1 significantly outperforms discriminative embeddings and can provide either type of representation as required. Its oracle performance—selecting the best between discriminative and generative—far exceeds using either mode alone. In addition, UME-R1 can produce improved embedding representations through repeated sampling, indicating that generative embeddings also hold strong promise for inference-time scaling.

instruction: Make a 3D CT reconstruction of the abdomen with a focus on the pancreas and surrounding structures.
description: This model is a 3D latent diffusion model (RFlow) combined with a 3D VAE and a CLIP3D text encoder, designed for text-to-CT generation. It is intended for research and experimentation in text-conditioned 3D CT synthesis, including the generation of synthetic data for benchmarking or augmentation. The model supports tasks such as CT volume generation from textual descriptions. The architecture includes a 3D VAE for latent compression and decoding, a diffusion UNet trained with rectified flow in latent space, and a CLIP3D text encoder for report embeddings. The model was trained on the CT-RATE dataset, which includes CT volumes and reports, and the CLIP3D component was trained for vision-language alignment on this dataset. The diffusion UNet was trained using rectified flow in latent space, conditioned on text embeddings. The model is not validated for clinical use and may encode dataset biases. Synthetic outputs may contain artifacts and should not be used for patient care. Evaluation results are detailed in the associated paper. The model is released under an Apache 2.0 license. The model weights include an autoencoder checkpoint, a diffusion UNet checkpoint, and CLIP3D weights for report embeddings. The model is not intended for clinical decision-making, diagnostic use, or deployment without proper validation and approvals. Training was conducted using a multi-GPU setup, though environmental impact was not reported.

instruction: Can you detect the language spoken in this audio and translate it to English?
description: Ultravox is a multimodal Speech LLM that can consume both speech and text as input, such as a text system prompt and a voice user message. The input to the model is given as a text prompt with a special pseudo-token, and the model processor will replace this token with embeddings derived from the input audio. Using the merged embeddings as input, the model will then generate output text as usual. In a future revision of Ultravox, the token vocabulary will be expanded to support the generation of semantic and acoustic audio tokens, which can then be fed to a vocoder to produce voice output. No preference tuning has been applied to this revision of the model. The model uses a pre-trained backbone as well as the encoder part of another model. The multi-modal adapter is trained, the Whisper encoder is fine-tuned, while the Llama model is kept frozen. A knowledge-distillation loss is used where Ultravox is trying to match the logits of the text-based Llama backbone. The training dataset is a mix of ASR datasets, extended with continuations generated by Llama 3.1 8B, and speech translation datasets, which yield a modest improvement in translation evaluations. The model is trained using supervised speech instruction finetuning via knowledge-distillation. The training is performed using BF16 mixed precision training on 8x H100 GPUs. The current version of Ultravox, when invoked with audio content, has a time-to-first-token (TTFT) of approximately 150ms, and a tokens-per-second rate of ~50-100 when using an A100-40GB GPU, all using a Llama 3.1 8B backbone. The model can be used as a voice agent, for speech-to-speech translation, and for the analysis of spoken audio. The model is developed by Fixie.ai and is licensed under the MIT license. Evaluation results show that Ultravox 0.5 8B achieves a BLEU score of 12.99 on covost2 en_ar, 31.54 on covost2 en_ca, 28.70 on covost2 en_de, 40.19 on covost2 es_en, 42.13 on covost2 ru_en, 17.22 on covost2 zh_en, and 66.54 on big bench audio.

instruction: I want to extract all moving vehicles from a traffic surveillance video. How can I do that?
description: SeC is a concept-driven segmentation framework for video object segmentation that integrates Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to provide robust, object-centric representations. The framework dynamically balances semantic reasoning with feature matching, adaptively adjusting computational efforts based on scene complexity to achieve optimal segmentation performance. SeC is designed to support video object segmentation tasks and is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, including SA-V, LVOS v2, MOSE, DAVIS 2017, YTVOS 2019, and the newly proposed SeCVOS benchmark, which is specifically designed to evaluate segmentation in complex and challenging scenarios. On these benchmarks, SeC achieves a SA-V validation score of 82.7, a SA-V test score of 81.7, an LVOS v2 validation score of 86.5, a MOSE validation score of 75.3, a DAVIS 2017 validation score of 91.3, a YTVOS 2019 validation score of 88.6, and a SeCVOS score of 70.0. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of SeC in handling complex video object segmentation tasks. The model is intended for use in scenarios where accurate and adaptive segmentation is required, particularly in complex visual environments.

instruction: Can you visualize a city where every building is shaped like a musical instrument?
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the FLUX.2 [dev] model, which uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. FLUX.2 [dev] is a 32 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating, editing, and combining images based on text instructions. It is designed for open text-to-image generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing, allowing character, object, and style reference without the need for additional training. The model is trained using guidance distillation, making it more efficient. It has open weights to support scientific research and empower artists to develop innovative workflows. Generated outputs can be used for personal, scientific, and commercial purposes as described in the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives to build upon the model. FLUX.2 [dev] is available in both quantized and non-quantized versions. For local deployment on consumer-grade graphics cards, such as an RTX 4090 or RTX 5090, instructions are available on the GitHub page. Black Forest Labs has evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in the model, including the generation of unlawful content such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with collaboration from the Internet Watch Foundation. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit certain behaviors and suppress concepts that could lead to synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluations, including internal and external third-party testing, were conducted to identify and address residual risks. The final FLUX.2 [dev] checkpoint demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and showed higher resilience than leading open-weight models. The model includes filters for NSFW and IP-infringing content at input and output, and developers must use these filters or manual review processes under the terms of the FLUX.2 [dev] Non-Commercial License. Inference filters are applied to the API for FLUX.2 Pro, using both in-house and third-party filters to prevent CSAM and NCII outputs. The model also includes content provenance features, such as pixel-layer watermarking and links to the C2PA standard for metadata. The FLUX.2 Pro API applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to output content. Use of the model and access to the API are governed by the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy, which prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. The model is monitored for patterns of violative use after release, and takedown requests are issued to websites or businesses that misuse the model. Users or developers who repeatedly violate policies may be banned. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains reporting relationships with organizations such as the Internet Watch Foundation and the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.

instruction: What media code would you assign to an article about a personal finance tip for saving money?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on English training data labeled with 18 media codes. The media codes include categories such as State and Local Government Administration, Weather, Fires, emergencies and natural disasters, Crime and trials, Arts, culture, entertainment and history, Style and fashion, Food, Travel, Wellbeing and learning, Personal finance and real estate, Personal technology and popular science, Churches and Religion, Celebrities and human interest, Obituaries and death notices, Sports, Crosswords, puzzles, comics, Media production/internal, letters, and Advertisements. The model is designed for text classification tasks and is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool intended to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. The model was evaluated on a test set of 67,681 English examples, achieving an accuracy, precision, recall, and weighted average F1-score of 0.8. Performance metrics for individual labels include precision, recall, and F1-score values ranging from 0 to 1, with specific values provided for each of the 18 media codes and additional categories. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions, and users must pass the token parameter when loading the model. In earlier versions of the Transformers package, the use_auth_token parameter may be required instead. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users running the model before transformers version 4.27 may need to install it manually. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed at poltextlab@poltextlab.com.

instruction: What is the peak RAM usage for a 1024-word input on the GPU?
description: This model provides several variants of the embedding model published in the document, which are optimized for deployment on Android or iOS using the specified tools or frameworks. The Gecko embedding model can be used on Android through the provided SDK, which is available for download or can be installed directly from Maven. A sample application is also available for demonstration. The model uses the sentencepiece model as its tokenizer. Performance benchmarks are provided for a Samsung S23 Ultra device, with measurements taken for different backends, sequence lengths, initialization times, inference times, memory usage, and model sizes. The model size is measured by the size of the .tflite flatbuffer, which is the serialization format for LiteRT models. Memory usage is an indicator of peak RAM consumption. Inference on the CPU is accelerated using the LiteRT delegate with 4 threads, while inference on the GPU is accelerated using the LiteRT GPU delegate. The benchmarks assume that the XNNPACK cache is enabled. The dynamic_int8 variant refers to a quantized model with int8 weights and float activations. For a sequence length of 256, the CPU backend has an initialization time of 11.03 ms, an inference time of 147.6 ms, and uses 126.3 MB of memory, with a model size of 114 MB. For the same sequence length, the GPU backend has an initialization time of 1306.06 ms, an inference time of 76.2 ms, and uses 604.5 MB of memory, with a model size of 114 MB. For a sequence length of 512, the CPU backend has an initialization time of 30.04 ms, an inference time of 353.1 ms, and uses 225.6 MB of memory, with a model size of 120 MB. For the same sequence length, the GPU backend has an initialization time of 1363.38 ms, an inference time of 173.2 ms, and uses 604.6 MB of memory, with a model size of 120 MB. For a sequence length of 1024, the CPU backend has an initialization time of 79.17 ms, an inference time of 954 ms, and uses 619.5 MB of memory, with a model size of 145 MB. For the same sequence length, the GPU backend has an initialization time of 1419.87 ms, an inference time of 397 ms, and uses 871.1 MB of memory, with a model size of 145 MB.

instruction: Can you generate a detailed landscape using the structure from my line art?
description: ControlNet is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. This specific checkpoint is conditioned on Canny edges, which are represented as a monochrome image with white edges on a black background. The model was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala and is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model. It is intended to be used in combination with other models, particularly those based on Stable Diffusion. The model supports various input conditions, including Canny edge detection, Midas depth estimation, HED edge detection, M-LSD line detection, normal maps, OpenPose bone images, human scribbles, and semantic segmentation. The Canny edge model was trained on 3 million edge-image, caption pairs for 600 GPU-hours using an Nvidia A100 80G and Stable Diffusion 1.5 as the base model. The model is licensed under a framework adapted from responsible AI licensing practices. It is recommended to use this checkpoint with the Stable Diffusion 1.5 model, although it can also be used with other diffusion models such as dreamboothed stable diffusion. To process an image for the auxiliary conditioning, external dependencies such as OpenCV, diffusers, and related packages are required. The model is described in the paper titled 'Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala, available on arXiv with the identifier 2302.05543.

instruction: Which passage most directly answers the question about the causes of the French Revolution?
description: The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is optimized for providing a logit score that represents how relevant a document(s) is to a given query. The model was fine-tuned for multilingual, cross-lingual text question-answering retrieval, with support for long documents (up to 8192 tokens). This model was evaluated on 26 languages: English, Arabic, Bengali, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Persian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, and Turkish. This model is a component in a text retrieval system to improve the overall accuracy. A text retrieval system often uses an embedding model (dense) or lexical search (sparse) index to return relevant text passages given the input. A reranking model can be used to rerank the potential candidate into a final order. The reranking model has the question-passage pairs as an input and therefore, can process cross attention between the words. It’s not feasible to apply a Ranking model on all documents in the knowledge base, therefore, ranking models are often deployed in combination with embedding models. This model is ready for commercial use. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is a part of the NeMo Retriever collection of NIM, which provide state-of-the-art, commercially-ready models and microservices, optimized for the lowest latency and highest throughput. It features a production-ready information retrieval pipeline with enterprise support. The models that form the core of this solution have been trained using responsibly selected, auditable data sources. With multiple pre-trained models available as starting points, developers can also readily customize them for their domain-specific use cases, such as information technology, human resource help assistants, and research & development research assistants. We are excited to announce the open sourcing of this commercial embedding model. For users interested in deploying this model in production environments, it is also available via the model API in NVIDIA Inference Microservices (NIM). The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is most suitable for users who want to improve their multilingual retrieval tasks by reranking a set of candidates for a given question. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is a transformer cross-encoder fine-tuned with contrastive learning. We employ bi-directional attention when fine-tuning for higher accuracy. The last embedding output by the decoder model is used with a mean pooling strategy, and a binary classification head is fine-tuned for the ranking task. Ranking models for text ranking are typically trained as a cross-encoder for sentence classification. This involves predicting the relevancy of a sentence pair (for example, question and chunked passages). The CrossEntropy loss is used to maximize the likelihood of passages containing information to answer the question and minimize the likelihood for (negative) passages that do not contain information to answer the question. We trained the model on public datasets described in the Dataset and Training section. The model takes a pair of texts as input, formatted as a list of text pairs. The input parameters are one-dimensional. The model was trained on question and answering over text documents from multiple languages. It was evaluated to work successfully with up to a sequence length of 8192 tokens. Longer texts are recommended to be either chunked or truncated. The model outputs floats, formatted as a list of floats, with one-dimensional parameters. Each value corresponds to a raw logit. Users can choose to apply a Sigmoid activation function to the logits to convert them into probabilities during model usage. The model requires transformers version 4.47.1. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, NVIDIA Hopper, and NVIDIA Lovelace hardware microarchitectures, and it supports Linux as the operating system. The Llama Nemotron Reranking 1B model is part of the NeMo Retriever collection of NIM and is identified by the short name llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2. The model was trained on 800k samples from public datasets. The evaluation datasets are based on three TextQA datasets, the TechQA dataset, MIRACL, MLDR, and MLQA multilingual retrieval datasets, which are all public datasets. The sizes range between 10,000s up to 5M depending on the dataset. The pipeline llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2 + llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 provides high BEIR+TechQA accuracy with multilingual and crosslingual support. The llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2 ranking model is 3.5x smaller than the nv-rerankqa-mistral-4b-v3 model. The pipeline achieves an average Recall@5 of 73.64% on the NQ, HotpotQA, FiQA, and TechQA datasets. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 65.80% on the MIRACL multilingual datasets. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 86.83% on the MLQA dataset with different languages. The model achieves an average Recall@5 of 70.69% on the MLDR dataset. The model’s max sequence length is 8192. Therefore, the longer text inputs should be truncated. The model does not always guarantee to retrieve the correct passage(s) for a given query. Use of this model is governed by the license. Additional information is available. NVIDIA believes Trustworthy AI is a shared responsibility and has established policies and practices to enable development for a wide array of AI applications. Developers should work with their supporting model team to ensure this model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. For more detailed information on ethical considerations for this model, please see the Explainability, Bias, Safety, and Privacy sections. Please report security vulnerabilities or NVIDIA AI Concerns. The model is intended for passage ranking for question and answer retrieval. It is designed for use by generative AI creators working with conversational AI models—users who want to build a multilingual question and answer application over a large text corpus, leveraging the latest dense retrieval technologies. The model transforms the tokenized input text into a dense vector representation. The model's output is an array of float numbers (dense vector representation for the input text). The model's performance is measured in terms of accuracy, throughput, and latency. The model does not always guarantee to retrieve the correct passage(s) for a given query. The model’s max sequence length is 8192. Therefore, the longer text inputs should be truncated. The model has not been tested to deliver comparable outcomes for any adversely impacted groups. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards. The model does not generate or reverse engineer personally-identifiable information (PII). No personal data was used to create this model. The dataset is reviewed before every release. There is no mechanism in place to honor data subject right of access or deletion of personal data. Personal data was not collected for the development of this model by NVIDIA. Data labeling (annotation, metadata) complies with privacy laws. Data is not compliant with data subject requests for data correction or removal, if such a request was made. The model application is text reranking for retrieval. There is no physical safety impact. Use of this model is governed by the license. Additional information is available. The Principle of least privilege (PoLP) is applied limiting access for dataset generation and model development. Restrictions enforce dataset access during training, and dataset license constraints are adhered to.

instruction: Could you help me create a transparent version of this animal for use in a digital artwork?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference approach, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference tasks and can be deployed locally or through an inference endpoint. It is implemented using PyTorch and can be loaded with weights from HuggingFace or local storage. The model includes functionality for image preprocessing, prediction, and post-processing, including alpha channel generation for object extraction. The model weights are available in the official repository, and the model can be used with or without the associated GitHub codebase. The model has been developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model's implementation and weights are available on GitHub and HuggingFace, and online demos for inference are provided. The model's development has been supported by GPU resources from Freepik and Fal, as well as assistance with deployment from Not-Lain.

instruction: Can you tell me which category best fits a news report about a city council meeting?
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on English training data labeled with 18 media codes. The media codes include categories such as State and Local Government Administration, Weather, Fires, emergencies and natural disasters, Crime and trials, Arts, culture, entertainment and history, Style and fashion, Food, Travel, Wellbeing and learning, Personal finance and real estate, Personal technology and popular science, Churches and Religion, Celebrities and human interest, Obituaries and death notices, Sports, Crosswords, puzzles, comics, Media production/internal, letters, and Advertisements. The model is designed for text classification tasks and is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool intended to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. The model was evaluated on a test set of 67,681 English examples, achieving an accuracy, precision, recall, and weighted average F1-score of 0.8. Performance metrics for individual labels include precision, recall, and F1-score values ranging from 0 to 1, with specific values provided for each of the 18 media codes and additional categories. The model requires a token for access due to gated access restrictions, and users must pass the token parameter when loading the model. In earlier versions of the Transformers package, the use_auth_token parameter may be required instead. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and users running the model before transformers version 4.27 may need to install it manually. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and contributions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed at poltextlab@poltextlab.com.

instruction: I want to use this image on a billboard—can you upscale it for that purpose?
description: This is a model for high-definition magnification of images, trained on Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511. It is primarily used for losslessly enlarging images to approximately 2K size and is intended for use in ComfyUI. The model works with a modified version of Comfy's Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 workflow, which includes adding a Qwen/Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 LoRA node connected to the base model. The model can be used with a QwenImageEditPipeline to load the base model and trained LoRA weights for in-scene editing. An input image is loaded, and an in-scene editing prompt is defined, such as 'Upscale this picture to 4K resolution.' The model generates an edited image with enhanced scene understanding using specified parameters, including a prompt, generator seed, true_cfg_scale, negative_prompt, and num_inference_steps. The model weights are available in Safetensors format. There is no fixed trigger word, and the specific removal prompt may require further testing. The model was trained by the AI Laboratory of Chongqing Valiant Cat Technology Co., LTD.

instruction: Generate a clip of a floating crystal orb with swirling colors and a see-through center.
description: Wan-Alpha is a framework for high-quality text-to-video generation that includes an alpha channel to represent transparency. It is designed to generate transparent videos by jointly learning both RGB and alpha channels. The model uses an effective variational autoencoder (VAE) that encodes the alpha channel into the RGB latent space. To support the training of the diffusion transformer, a high-quality and diverse RGBA video dataset was constructed. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, Wan-Alpha demonstrates superior performance in visual quality, motion realism, and transparency rendering. It can generate a wide variety of semi-transparent objects, glowing effects, and fine-grained details such as hair strands. The model is available for download and use, with open-sourced weights and inference code for version 1.0. Users can generate videos by specifying a transparent background, visual style, shot type, and a description of the main subject in their prompts. The model supports both Chinese and English input. For usage, users must download and organize specific model files, including the Wan DiT base model, text encoder, LightX2V model, and VAE decoders for both RGB and alpha channels. These files should be placed in the appropriate directories for use with the ComfyUI interface. The model can be tested using a provided script that requires specifying paths to the model weights and other parameters. The output includes RGBA videos with a checkerboard background and PNG frames. The model is built upon several open-source projects related to training, inference, and acceleration. The authors request citation of their paper if the model is used in research.

instruction: Can you separate the primary speaker's voice from overlapping voices in this clip?
description: Dolphin is an efficient audio-visual speech separation model that extracts target speech from noisy environments by combining acoustic and visual (lip movement) cues. It achieves state-of-the-art performance while being 6× faster and using 50% fewer parameters than previous methods. The model is designed to separate speech using both audio and video inputs, where the video input consists of grayscale lip region frames. It supports tasks such as hearing aid enhancement, video conferencing noise suppression, in-car speech extraction, AR/VR communication, and edge device deployment. The model architecture includes a DP-LipCoder video encoder, an audio encoder, a global-local attention separator, and an audio decoder. The DP-LipCoder uses a dual-path architecture with visual compression and semantic encoding, vector quantization for discrete lip semantic tokens, and knowledge distillation from AV-HuBERT, with only 8.5M parameters. The audio encoder extracts multi-scale acoustic features, and the global-local attention separator uses a single-pass TDANet-based architecture with global attention for long-range dependencies and local attention for noise suppression. The model is trained on the VoxCeleb2 dataset with 2-speaker mixtures at 0dB SNR, using a SI-SNR loss function. On the VoxCeleb2 benchmark, it achieves a SI-SNRi of 16.1 dB, SDRi of 16.3 dB, PESQ of 3.45, ESTOI of 0.93, with 51.3M parameters, 417G MACs, and an inference speed of 0.015s per 4s-clip. The model requires frontal or near-frontal face views for optimal performance, works best with 25fps video input, and is trained on English speech, which may require fine-tuning for other languages. Performance degrades with severe occlusions or low lighting.

instruction: How does the optimizer configuration affect the model's ability to generalize?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of an unspecified base model on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 5.1124 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: learning rate of 5e-05, training batch size of 4, evaluation batch size of 8, random seed of 42, Adam optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, linear learning rate scheduler, and 20 training epochs. During training, the model's training loss and validation loss were recorded at various steps and epochs. The training process was implemented using Transformers 4.44.2, PyTorch 2.5.0+cu121, Datasets 3.1.0, and Tokenizers 0.19.1. More information is needed regarding the model's intended uses, limitations, and the training and evaluation data.

instruction: Can you detect and categorize the types of vehicles in this image?
description: The vit_huge_plus_patch16_dinov3.lvd1689m is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model designed as an image feature encoder. It is distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is intended for tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It has 840.5 million parameters, 224.9 GMACs, and 193.6 million activations, with an input image size of 256 x 256. The model uses a ViT architecture and is trained using the DINOv3 method. The original model weights have all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some variants have `qkvb` in the name with `qkv_bias=True` but zero values to align with the `transformers` library and original models. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.3 IN-ReaL, 90.0 IN-R, 78.6 Obj.Net, 64.5 Ox.-H, 54.8 ADE20k, 0.352 NYU, 79.3 DAVIS, 63.3 NAVI, and 56.3 SPair. It is also evaluated on GEO-Bench for satellite imagery tasks, including classification and segmentation, with metrics such as 73.0 m-BEnet, 96.5 m-brick-kiln, 94.1 m-eurosat, 60.6 m-forestnet, 96.0 m-pv4ger, 57.4 m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for classification, and 94.2 m-cashew, 75.6 m-chesapeake, 61.8 m-NeonTree, 83.7 m-nz-cattle, 95.2 m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8 m-SA-crop, and 74.5 mean for segmentation. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, the ViT paper, and the PyTorch Image Models repository.

instruction: Can you isolate the subject in this image so I can use it in a different layout?
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. It is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. The model has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The accuracy, efficiency, and versatility of the model currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. Developed by BRIA AI, RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use. Bria RMBG2.0 is available as source-code and weights, ComfyUI nodes, or API endpoints. Commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. The model is released under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license for non-commercial use. BRIA RMBG-2.0 is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset. The model output includes a single-channel 8-bit grayscale alpha matte, where each pixel value indicates the opacity level of the corresponding pixel in the original image. This non-binary output approach offers developers the flexibility to define custom thresholds for foreground-background separation, catering to varied use cases and enhancing integration into complex pipelines. The Bria-RMBG model was trained with over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The training data includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The distribution of images is as follows: 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects/animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types is 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The distribution of background types is 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The distribution of foreground content is 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with a proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for background-removal tasks. If you use this model in your research, please cite the article titled 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Zheng, Peng and Gao, Dehong and Fan, Deng-Ping and Liu, Li and Laaksonen, Jorma and Ouyang, Wanli and Sebe, Nicu, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model requires the following libraries: torch, torchvision, pillow, kornia, and transformers. To use the model, load the model from the 'briaai/RMBG-2.0' repository, preprocess the input image by resizing it to 1024x1024, normalizing it, and converting it to a tensor. Perform inference using the model, apply a sigmoid function to the output, and convert the result to a PIL image. Resize the mask to the original image size and apply it as an alpha channel to the image. Save the resulting image with the background removed.

instruction: Generate a scene where the background depth shifts to simulate a miniature aquarium behind a person's face.
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 A14B text-to-video generation system, specifically for depth-based control. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet implementations for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and depth control inputs. The architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, a transformer model, and an autoencoder. Performance is demonstrated through inference examples using a CLI and code, where the model processes a video input and generates a controlled video output based on a detailed prompt. The model is evaluated using a prompt that describes a complex visual scene involving a close-up shot of a young woman's face with a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model is trained on unspecified data and is available for use with specific model paths. Caveats include the requirement for specific software versions and hardware, such as CUDA-compatible devices, and the need for manual installation of dependencies. The model is provided under a GitHub repository, and users are directed to raise issues or seek professional support through the repository or via email.

instruction: Is the sentence 'Light travels faster than sound' a scientifically supported claim?
description: This is an ONNX-optimized version of the DeBERTa-v3-large model for efficient inference. The repository contains a regular ONNX exported model and an INT8 dynamically quantized model for faster inference with minimal accuracy loss. The model is optimized for zero-shot classification tasks across multiple languages. The quantized model provides faster inference, with a speedup of approximately 2-3x compared to PyTorch, a smaller size due to INT8 quantization, and maintained accuracy with less than 1% loss compared to the original model. The base model is DeBERTa-v3-large, and it is designed for zero-shot classification and natural language inference tasks. The model was converted from PyTorch to ONNX format and uses dynamic quantization with INT8 weights. It is implemented using ONNX Runtime with Optimum. The model is licensed under the MIT License. If used, it should be cited as part of the original DeBERTa-v3-large Zero-Shot Classification model by Laurer, Atteveldt, Casas, and Welbers in 2022. This ONNX optimization was created for efficient deployment in production environments, with special thanks to the original model authors and the Hugging Face Optimum team.

instruction: Can you point out the trees in this landscape picture?
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/16 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, making it more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is primarily intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model is designed to enable researchers to explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection and to support interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training.

instruction: What medications are discussed in this patient's electronic health record?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.93, precision of 0.92, recall of 0.94, and accuracy of 0.99. The model is based on the ModernBERT-large architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is suitable for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Extract any RNA-related terms from this section on viral replication.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for biomedical entity recognition, specifically for identifying proteins, DNA, RNA, cell lines, and cell types. It is optimized for high precision and is trained on the JNLPBA dataset, a curated corpus for biomedical named entity recognition. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem, enabling easy integration into various applications. It supports the classification of biomedical entities such as B-DNA, B-RNA, B-cell_line, B-cell_type, B-protein, I-DNA, I-RNA, I-cell_line, I-cell_type, and I-protein. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.81, with a precision of 0.75, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.93. It is suitable for applications including drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the deberta-v3-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical and research settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: Could you predict whether the next major global sports event will be held in a non-traditional host country by 2030?
description: OpenForecaster-8B is a specialized language model for open-ended forecasting and predicting future events. It is post-trained from Qwen3-8B using reinforcement learning. The model is trained to make calibrated predictions on open-ended questions about future events, including providing calibrated confidence estimates when asked, reasoning about uncertainty and future scenarios, and leveraging retrieved information in context to improve predictions. OpenForecaster-8B was trained on the OpenForesight dataset, which contains over 52,000 forecasting questions generated from global news events, using GRPO to optimize a joint reward function combining accuracy and brier score. The model's knowledge cutoff is at best until April 2025, with the base model's cutoff being around June 2024, meaning it has no knowledge of events occurring after that date. Therefore, if asked about events in 2026 or later without recent developments or relevant context, the model's answers may not be helpful or up to date. OpenForecaster-8B achieves competitive performance with larger models like DeepSeek-v3 and Qwen3-235B-A22B on forecasting benchmarks, with key improvements in accuracy, calibration of confidence estimates, and consistency in predictions. Performance on a benchmark in July-August 2025 on non-numeric questions (86 questions) showed that OpenForecaster-8B has much higher accuracy than 100B+ models, with a comparison limited to models released before April 2025 for a fair, equal knowledge cutoff. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: This image has a mix of text and a chart. Can you identify and represent the chart in Markdown?
description: HunyuanOCR is an end-to-end OCR expert vision-language model (VLM) built using Hunyuan's native multimodal architecture. With a lightweight 1B parameter design, it has achieved state-of-the-art performance across multiple industry benchmarks. The model is capable of complex multilingual document parsing and is designed for practical applications such as text spotting, open-field information extraction, video subtitle extraction, and photo translation. It supports tasks including spotting, document parsing, general parsing, information extraction, and translation. For document parsing, it can recognize formulas in LaTeX format, parse tables into HTML, and represent charts using Mermaid or Markdown formats. It can extract document content in markdown, ignoring headers and footers, and represent formulas in LaTeX and tables in HTML. For information extraction, it can extract key-value pairs, specific fields, and subtitles in JSON format. For translation, it can extract and translate text into English, ignoring headers and footers, and represent formulas in LaTeX and tables in HTML. The model is available for deployment and inference using the Transformers library or vLLM, with specific installation and usage instructions provided. It is recommended to use the clean_repeated_substrings function to process output text. The model has been developed by the Hunyuan Vision Team and is described in the HunyuanOCR Technical Report, which is available on arXiv. The team acknowledges the contributions of other researchers and models, as well as the benchmarks used in the development and evaluation of the model.

instruction: How can the relevance of documents be improved in a STEM-focused search engine?
description: The zeroentropy/zerank-1-small is a 1.7B parameter reranker model, serving as a smaller version of the flagship model. Despite being over 2x smaller, it maintains nearly the same level of performance, outperforming other popular rerankers and showing significant accuracy improvements over traditional vector search. The model is designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems and is intended for use in search engines. It is released under the open-source Apache 2.0 license to support the open-source community and advance the capabilities of open-source models. The model can be used for reranking tasks and is compatible with the sentence_transformers library as a CrossEncoder. Evaluations show that the model achieves high NDCG@10 scores across various tasks, including Code, Conversational, Finance, Legal, Medical, and STEM. For example, in the Code task, it achieves an NDCG@10 score of 0.730, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.678 and other rerankers. In the Conversational task, it achieves 0.556, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.250. In the Finance task, it achieves 0.861, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.839. In the Legal task, it achieves 0.817, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.703. In the Medical task, it achieves 0.773, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.619. In the STEM task, it achieves 0.680, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.401. The model also outperforms other rerankers such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and Salesforce/Llama-rank-v1 in these tasks. The model can be used in conjunction with an initial retriever, such as OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small, to rerank the top 100 candidate documents. The model can be used for inference via ZeroEntropy's endpoint.

instruction: Could you translate this customer support message from Portuguese to Chinese, ensuring the response is polite and accurate?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporate 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers comparable translation performance, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs, and supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios. HY-MT1.5-7B has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios compared to its September open-source version. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models are available in multiple quantization formats, including FP8 and Int4. The models support translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among the 33 supported languages. The models are designed to be used with the transformers library, and specific prompt templates are provided for different translation tasks, including terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models do not have a default system prompt, and specific inference parameters are recommended for optimal performance. The models were open-sourced on Hugging Face in December 2025 and September 2025.

instruction: Can you make a 3D object of a dental implant for a personalized treatment plan?
description: Shap-MeD is a text-to-3D object generative model specialized in the biomedical domain. It is designed to assist in the 3D modeling of medical objects, aiming to reduce development time. 3D modeling in medicine has various applications, including surgical procedure simulation and planning, the design of personalized prosthetic implants, medical education, the creation of anatomical models, and the development of research prototypes. Shap-MeD is based on Shap-e, an open-source text-to-3D generative model developed by OpenAI, and is fine-tuned using a dataset of biomedical objects. The model achieved a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.089 in latent generation on the evaluation set, compared to Shap-e's MSE of 0.147. A qualitative evaluation was also conducted, comparing Shap-MeD with other models in the generation of biomedical objects. The results indicate that Shap-MeD demonstrates higher structural accuracy in biomedical object generation.

instruction: Generate a whimsical scene of a cat wearing a hat and glasses, sitting on a bookshelf reading a book.
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model. The unsloth/FLUX.2-klein-9B-GGUF model uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision and tooling from city96. The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality. FLUX.2 [klein] 9B is a 9 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is the flagship small model, defining the Pareto frontier for quality versus latency across text-to-image, single-reference editing, and multi-reference generation. It matches or exceeds models five times its size in under half a second. Built on a 9B flow model with an 8B Qwen3 text embedder, it is step-distilled to four inference steps. The model is a distilled model for sub-second image generation with outstanding quality. It supports text-to-image and image-to-image multi-reference editing in a single unified model. It is great for real-time generation and integration into applications. It is ideal for creative exploration with excellent prompt adherence and output diversity. It is available for non-commercial use. A reference implementation of FLUX.2 [klein] 9B, as well as sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository. Developers and creatives looking to build on top of FLUX.2 [klein] 9B are encouraged to use this as a starting point. The FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model is available via the BFL API and is also available in both quantized and full-precision versions. To use FLUX.2 [klein] 9B with the Diffusers Python library, first install or upgrade diffusers. Then you can use Flux2KleinPipeline to run the model. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under. The FLUX.2 [klein] 9B model fits in approximately 29GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and above. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of its models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in its model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation included multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with text-only prompts, a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision included a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks, and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license, and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models were approved for release under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on the company's hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Policies governing acceptable use of the models and access to the API are set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and may monitor use to detect misuse or abuse of its models and services. A dedicated email address is provided to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations. The model falls under a specific license. This project may contain trademarks or logos for projects, products, or services. Use of Black Forest Labs and FLUX trademarks or logos in modified versions of this project must not cause confusion or imply sponsorship or endorsement. Any use of third-party trademarks, intellectual property or logos is subject to those third-party's policies.

instruction: Can you recognize if the speaker is speaking in a formal or casual tone?
description: SEW-D-tiny is a base model pretrained on 16kHz sampled speech audio. It is designed to be fine-tuned on downstream tasks such as Automatic Speech Recognition, Speaker Identification, Intent Classification, and Emotion Recognition. The model is part of the SEW (Squeezed and Efficient Wav2vec) architecture, which is optimized for performance and efficiency in pre-trained models for automatic speech recognition. SEW-D-tiny demonstrates significant improvements in both performance and efficiency compared to wav2vec 2.0. For example, under the 100h-960h semi-supervised setup on LibriSpeech, SEW achieves a 1.9x inference speedup compared to wav2vec 2.0, with a 13.5% relative reduction in word error rate. With a similar inference time, SEW reduces word error rate by 25-50% across different model sizes. When using the model, it is important to ensure that the speech input is also sampled at 16kHz. Evaluation results on LibriSpeech's 'clean' and 'other' test data show a word error rate (WER) of 10.47 and 22.73, respectively.

instruction: What activity is happening in the video I just uploaded?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta, extending the pretraining objectives to achieve state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities, leveraging large-scale data and model sizes. The model is based on a ViT-L 256 architecture with a video classification head and is pretrained on the Something-Something-V2 dataset. It is designed to support video classification tasks and is intended for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model processes video inputs by sampling frames according to its configuration, preprocessing them, and running inference to produce classification logits. Performance is demonstrated through an example where the model predicts top 5 class labels with corresponding probabilities. The model is available for use with the latest version of the Transformers library and requires a compatible environment for execution. The model's development and evaluation are detailed in a technical report published by FAIR at Meta in 2025.

instruction: How can I get a clean mask for the object described as 'a person wearing a red jacket' in this image?
description: Talk2DINO is a hybrid model that combines the spatial accuracy of DINOv2 with the language understanding capabilities of CLIP to address the task of Open-Vocabulary Segmentation (OVS). OVS involves segmenting images based on free-form textual concepts without predefined training classes. While CLIP can generate segmentation masks using Vision Transformers, it struggles with spatial localization due to global alignment of image and text features. On the other hand, self-supervised models like DINO excel in fine-grained visual encoding but lack language integration. Talk2DINO bridges this gap by aligning CLIP's textual embeddings with DINOv2's patch-level features through a learned mapping function, without fine-tuning the underlying backbones. During training, the model uses DINOv2's attention maps to selectively align local visual patches with textual embeddings. This approach enhances the segmentation process by producing more natural and less noisy segmentations and effectively distinguishing foreground objects from the background. Experimental results show that Talk2DINO achieves state-of-the-art performance on several unsupervised OVS benchmarks.

instruction: Can you add a soft focus transition between these two video clips?
description: Ditto is a framework designed to address the scarcity of high-quality training data for instruction-based video editing. It introduces a novel data generation pipeline that combines the creative diversity of a leading image editor with an in-context video generator. The framework employs an efficient, distilled model architecture augmented by a temporal enhancer to reduce computational overhead and improve temporal coherence. An intelligent agent drives the entire pipeline, crafting diverse instructions and filtering output to ensure quality control at scale. Using this framework, the Ditto-1M dataset was created, consisting of one million high-fidelity video editing examples. The Editto model was trained on this dataset using a curriculum learning strategy, demonstrating superior instruction-following ability and establishing a new state-of-the-art in instruction-based video editing. Editto is a model designed for video editing based on textual instructions. It supports tasks such as applying editing instructions to input videos to generate output videos. The model can be used with the DiffSynth framework or the ComfyUI interface. When using ComfyUI, it is noted that due to the use of quantized and distilled models, there may be some quality degradation compared to full-precision models. The model requires specific model weights and dependencies to be downloaded and set up in designated directories. The model is licensed under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 and is provided for academic research purposes only.

instruction: Can you rank these images from most to least relevant to the query 'a group of people dancing at a party'?
description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE uses a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale for downstream tasks, allowing large-scale contrastive pretraining to transfer effectively with alignment tuning. PE Core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE Core comes in three sizes: B, L, and G, with G being the main checkpoint and B and L models distilled from it. The G/14 variant has 1.88 billion parameters for the vision tower, a width of 1536, a depth of 50, an MLP size of 8960, 16 attention heads, a CLIP dimension of 1280, and a resolution of 448px. The text tower for G/14 has 0.47 billion parameters, a width of 1280, a depth of 24, an MLP size of 5120, 20 attention heads, and a CLIP dimension of 1280, with a context length of 72 tokens. All PE Core models use an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower, and the B and L models include an additional class token for global aggregation. PE Core achieves strong results on zero-shot image and video classification and retrieval tasks, with the G/14 variant achieving 85.4% accuracy on ImageNet-1k, 80.2% on ImageNet-v2, 92.6% on ImageNet-A, 88.2% on ObjectNet, 58.1% on COCO-T2I, 76.9% on Kinetics-400, and 51.2% on VTT-T2I. PE Core performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A.
