The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a vision-language architecture and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model can be used for encoding images and text, computing similarity scores, and generating label probabilities. The model is available through the OpenCLIP and original GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated research paper.

instruction: Can you extract just the vocals from this music track so I can use them for a karaoke version?
description: Mini-BS-RoFormer-V2 is a model designed for the music source separation task. It has been improved upon previous versions. The model has a total of 46.8 million parameters and uses BF16 weight precision. On the validation set of the MUSDB18HQ dataset, the model achieves an SDR score of 10.03 for overall tracks, 9.68 for bass, 10.58 for drums, 8.99 for other, and 10.86 for vocals. The model uses time dimension downsampling to significantly reduce resource consumption. The computational cost for inference on 30 seconds of audio is 8343.55 GFLOPs. The model requires the transformers library version 4.55.4, as well as the soundfile, einops, and librosa libraries to function properly. The model can separate audio into bass, drums, other, and vocal tracks. If only vocals and instrumental tracks are desired, the bass, drums, and other tracks can be merged. The model was trained using the train and test sets of the MUSDB18HQ dataset. Training was conducted for 310k steps with a constant learning rate of 5e-4 and a batch size of 16. The Muon optimizer was used for training the transformer layers, while the AdamW optimizer was used for other network layers. Key improvements in Mini-BS-RoFormer-V2 compared to previous versions include training with bf16-true precision, using the Muon optimizer for faster convergence, changing the n_fft for the STFT operation from 2048 to 4096 for input audio, switching from 62 frequency bands to 80 mel-based frequency bands, time dimension downsampling with a step size of 4, and additional code modifications.

instruction: Can you extract all the drug names mentioned in this medical research paper?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition, specifically for identifying chemical entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 4,409 annotated chemical entities. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-CHEM` and `I-CHEM`. It is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the xlm-roberta-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.93, recall of 0.96, and accuracy of 0.99. It is ranked among the top models on the BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, with an F1 score of 0.9614, precision of 0.9520, recall of 0.9710, and accuracy of 0.9892. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare applications. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model can be used with the Hugging Face pipeline API, and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. For efficient processing of large datasets, the model supports batch processing with adjustable batch sizes depending on hardware capabilities. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical named entity recognition across multiple public datasets.

instruction: I want to extract the text from this document and preserve the structure of all tables and headers.
description: OCRFlux-3B is a preview release of a multimodal large language model toolkit designed for converting PDFs and images into clean, readable plain Markdown text. It is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct using private document datasets and some additional data. The model is based on a 3B parameter vision-language model (VLM), allowing it to run on a GTX 3090 GPU. OCRFlux-3B is intended to push the current state-of-the-art in document parsing to a significantly higher level. It supports superior parsing quality on each page, achieving higher Edit Distance Similarity (EDS) scores on the OCRFlux-bench-single benchmark compared to baseline models such as olmOCR-7B-0225-preview, Nanonets-OCR-s, and MonkeyOCR. Specifically, it achieves 0.095 higher EDS for English pages (from 0.872 to 0.967), 0.109 higher EDS for Chinese pages (from 0.858 to 0.967), and 0.187 higher EDS overall (from 0.780 to 0.967). The model also provides native support for cross-page table and paragraph merging, a feature not previously available in open-source projects. It can be used via an API for direct integration into code or through a Docker container setup, which requires Docker with GPU support and a pre-downloaded model. The model supports processing millions of documents at scale using an efficient inference setup via vllm. It includes a pipeline for generating Markdown files from JSONL outputs and offers evaluation capabilities for single-page parsing, table parsing, and cross-page merging detection and merging tasks. OCRFlux-3B is evaluated on two comprehensive benchmarks for single-page parsing and two for cross-page table/paragraph merging. In single-page parsing, it achieves an average EDS of 0.967, outperforming other models. In cross-page merging detection, it achieves a precision of 0.996, recall of 0.976, F1 score of 0.986, and accuracy of 0.986. For cross-page table merging, it achieves an average Tree Edit Distance-based Similarity (TEDS) of 0.950. The benchmarks used for evaluation are not included in the model's training or evaluation data. The model is designed to handle complex scenarios such as cross-page tables with repeated headers, vertically split tables, and multi-line table cells. It is also capable of merging paragraphs across pages by concatenation.

instruction: Compare the similarity between the phrases 'Artificial intelligence' and 'Machine learning' to determine their relatedness.
description: The gte-v1.5 series is an upgraded version of the gte embeddings, supporting a context length of up to 8192 while further enhancing model performance. These models are built upon the transformer++ encoder, which combines BERT, RoPE, and GLU components. The gte-v1.5 series achieves state-of-the-art scores on the MTEB benchmark within the same model size category and provides competitive results on the LoCo long-context retrieval tests. The models are developed by the Institute for Intelligent Computing, Alibaba Group, and are designed for text embeddings. The gte-v1.5 series includes multiple models with varying language support, model sizes, maximum sequence lengths, and embedding dimensions. For example, one model supports multiple languages with a model size of 7720 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 32768, and an embedding dimension of 4096, achieving an MTEB-en score of 67.34 and a LoCo score of 87.57. Another model is specifically for English with a model size of 434 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and an embedding dimension of 1024, achieving an MTEB-en score of 65.39 and a LoCo score of 86.71. A third English model has a model size of 137 million parameters, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and an embedding dimension of 768, achieving an MTEB-en score of 64.11 and a LoCo score of 87.44. The training process involves a multi-stage strategy to support the long context length of 8192. The model first undergoes masked language modeling (MLM) pre-training on the c4-en dataset, followed by weak-supervised contrastive pre-training (CPT) and supervised contrastive fine-tuning. The MLM pre-training includes stages with different learning rates, batch sizes, and sequence lengths. The model is evaluated on the MTEB and LoCo benchmarks, achieving high scores across various tasks. The gte-v1.5 model is available for use with the transformers and sentence-transformers libraries, and can be accessed via the model path 'Alibaba-NLP/gte-base-en-v1.5'. It is recommended to install xformers and enable unpadding for acceleration. The model can be used for tasks such as text embedding generation, similarity computation, and retrieval. The model is described as a SOTA instruction-tuned multi-lingual embedding model that ranked 2nd in MTEB and 1st in C-MTEB.

instruction: Can you help me find a detailed breakdown of the structure of the human brain?
description: inf-retriever-v1-pro is a specialized retrieval component of the INF-X-Retriever framework, designed to distill the core retrieval intent from complex, verbose, or reasoning-intensive queries. It is built upon the inf-retriever-v1 foundation and further trained to serve as the retriever within a RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) system. The model transforms raw user queries into concise, search-optimized queries for dense retrieval systems. In experiments, a single canonical query-writing prompt was applied across all datasets to ensure consistency and reproducibility. The task is defined as 'Given a web search query, retrieve relevant passages that answer the query.' This model is a key enabler for INF-X-Retriever's state-of-the-art performance, currently holding the No. 1 position on the (as of Dec 17, 2025). The BRIGHT (Benchmark for Reasoning-Intensive Grounded HT) is a rigorous text retrieval benchmark designed to evaluate the capability of retrieval models in handling questions that require intensive reasoning and cross-document synthesis. It is collected from real-world sources such as StackExchange, competitive programming platforms, and mathematical competitions, and comprises complex queries spanning diverse domains like mathematics, coding, biology, economics, and robotics. On the BRIGHT benchmark, INF-X-Retriever achieves state-of-the-art results (as of Dec 20, 2025). For short document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 68.3 on StackExchange, 55.3 on Coding, and 57.7 on Theorem-based categories. For detailed results across 12 datasets, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 63.4, with 79.8 in Bio., 70.9 in Earth., 69.9 in Econ., 73.3 in Psy., 57.7 in Rob., 64.3 in Stack., 61.9 in Sus., 56.1 in Leet., 54.5 in Pony, 51.9 in AoPS, 53.1 in TheoQ., and 67.9 in TheoT. For long document performance, INF-X-Retriever achieves an average of 54.6, with 73.2 in Bio., 59.6 in Earth., 69.3 in Econ., 12.1 in Pony, 74.3 in Psy., 55.9 in Rob., 27.8 in Stack., and 64.8 in Sus. The model is implemented using the transformers library version 4.51.0 and can be used with the SentenceTransformer or the Transformers library for encoding queries and documents and computing retrieval scores.

instruction: What are the limitations of depth estimation in low-light conditions, and how can I mitigate them?
description: DA3 Nested is a model that combines the any-view Giant model with the metric Large model for metric-scale visual geometry reconstruction. It is designed to perform a range of visual geometry tasks, including relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, 3D Gaussian generation, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. The model is based on a plain transformer architecture with a unified depth-ray representation, and it uses a vision transformer for visual geometry. It was developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. The model has 1.40B parameters and is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. It significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations on domain-specific images and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face Hub and can be accessed via a Python API or command line interface for inference and export in various formats such as glb, npz, ply, mini_npz, gs_ply, and gs_video.

instruction: Find the chemical entities in this excerpt from a medical case study.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used as a benchmark for evaluating named entity recognition models. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.98. The model is based on the e5-small-v2 architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: Can you draw a scene where a scientist is working in a lab filled with glowing beakers and machines?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a model designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts and to advance complex text rendering. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is a text-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is available in different quantized versions, with data types including INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). Two rank configurations are provided: r32 for faster inference and r128 for better quality but slower inference. The model includes standard inference speed versions for general use, as well as 4-step and 8-step distilled models fused with additional components using LoRA strength = 1.0. The 4-step distilled models are available in both r32 and r128 ranks for INT4 and NVFP4 data types, with the r128 versions offering better quality at the cost of slower inference. Similarly, the 8-step distilled models are available in the same configurations, with r128 versions providing improved quality but slower performance. The model is based on a diffusion architecture and is related to the SVDQuant method, as described in the paper 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models' presented at the Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025.

instruction: Can you detect and highlight all the vehicles in this traffic scene?
description: The Grounding DINO model was proposed by Shilong Liu, Zhaoyang Zeng, Tianhe Ren, Feng Li, Hao Zhang, Jie Yang, Chunyuan Li, Jianwei Yang, Hang Su, Jun Zhu, and Lei Zhang. Grounding DINO extends a closed-set object detection model with a text encoder, enabling open-set object detection. The model achieves remarkable results, such as 52.5 AP on COCO zero-shot. You can use the raw model for zero-shot object detection, which is the task of detecting things in an image out-of-the-box without labeled data.

instruction: Would you determine if this sequence is more likely to be intracellular or secreted?
description: SignalSeeker is a machine learning ensemble designed for predicting signal peptides in protein sequences. It integrates multiple algorithms, including Random Forest, Extra Trees, SVM, and Logistic Regression, with ProtBERT embeddings to achieve high accuracy in signal peptide detection. The model is intended to predict whether a protein sequence contains a signal peptide, assist in protein subcellular localization prediction, support research in protein secretion pathways, and aid in biotechnology applications requiring secreted proteins. The ensemble architecture includes feature extraction using ProtBERT embeddings of the N-terminal 50 amino acids, ensemble models with regularization, feature scaling via StandardScaler normalization, and a weighted ensemble decision logic with confidence assessment. The model was trained on 5000 mixed sequences from UniProt verified eukaryotic proteins and tested on 1000 mixed sequences from the same source, isolated from the training data. Performance metrics include a test AUC of 0.99433 for the best model, Logistic Regression (L2), with cross-validation AUCs ranging from 0.98711 to 0.99433 across models. The model is trained primarily on eukaryotic sequences and may exhibit reduced performance for prokaryotic proteins or highly divergent organisms. It requires sequences of at least 50 amino acids for optimal performance. The model is for research purposes only and not intended for clinical diagnosis. Users are advised to validate results experimentally and consider potential biases in the training data. SignalSeeker is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Can you generate a more vivid version of this faded photograph?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a high-performance image generation model. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is an image-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is quantized from an unspecified base model. The data type used is INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). The model supports different ranks for inference: r32 for faster inference, r128 for better quality but slower inference, and r256 for the highest quality (slowest inference). Standard inference speed models are available for general use. The model is based on an unspecified inference engine and quantization library. A paper titled 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models' by Li, Muyang, Lin, Yujun, Zhang, Zhekai, Cai, Tianle, Li, Xiuyu, Guo, Junxian, Xie, Enze, Meng, Chenlin, Zhu, Jun-Yan, and Han, Song, presented at The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025, is associated with the model.

instruction: Can you determine if 'He is a vegetarian' is entailed by 'He never eats meat'?
description: This model is ModernBERT multi-task fine-tuned on tasksource NLI tasks, including MNLI, ANLI, SICK, WANLI, doc-nli, LingNLI, FOLIO, FOL-NLI, LogicNLI, Label-NLI, and all datasets in the referenced table. It is equivalent to an 'instruct' version and was trained for 200k steps on an Nvidia A30 GPU. The model is very good at reasoning tasks, particularly outperforming llama 3.1 8B Instruct on ANLI and FOLIO, as well as long context reasoning, sentiment analysis, and zero-shot classification with new labels. The model is a single transformer with different classification heads on top, achieving high test accuracy across a wide range of NLI and related tasks. Further performance gains can be achieved by fine-tuning on a single task, such as SST, but this checkpoint is optimized for zero-shot classification and natural language inference, including contradiction, entailment, and neutral classification. The model supports zero-shot classification, natural language inference, and can serve as a backbone for further fine-tuning. It has stronger reasoning and fine-grained abilities than the base version of ModernBERT. The model is described in the paper 'tasksource: A Large Collection of NLP tasks with a Structured Dataset Preprocessing Framework' by Damien Sileo, presented at the 2024 Joint International Conference on Computational Linguistics, Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC-COLING 2024).

instruction: Is there any indication of a fight or assault in the background of this video?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 300 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 34 videos of abuse, 36 of arrest, 46 of arson, 36 of assault, 70 of burglary, 24 of explosion, 30 of fighting, 86 of road accidents, 98 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, and 62 of stealing. The dataset was split into 192 training videos, 48 validation videos, and 60 test videos. The model achieved a validation accuracy of 0.6250, precision of 0.6351, recall of 0.6250, and F1 score of 0.6114. On the test set, it achieved an accuracy of 0.6667, with equal precision, recall, and F1 score of 0.6667. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. Training was conducted for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and AdamW optimizer with betas (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon 1e-08. The model processes 16 frames per video clip at a resolution of 224x224 pixels and outputs binary classification results. It was trained using the HuggingFace Transformers framework with PyTorch 2.0.1+ and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. The model has several limitations, including potential generalization issues due to dataset scope, possible false positives with non-violent but intense activities, and environmental bias from lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It is intended for research, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes. Prohibited uses include unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, and privacy violations. The model was developed by a research team and is available for use under the HuggingFace Transformers framework.

instruction: How much space would I need to store the highest quality version of the i1 quant?
description: This is a vision model, and if applicable, mmproj files will be provided. The model is available in various quantized versions, with different types and sizes. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S (1.5 GB), i1-IQ1_M (1.5 GB), i1-IQ2_XXS (1.6 GB), i1-IQ2_XS (1.7 GB), i1-IQ2_S (1.8 GB), i1-IQ2_M (1.9 GB), i1-Q2_K_S (2.0 GB, very low quality), i1-Q2_K (2.0 GB, IQ3_XXS probably better), i1-IQ3_XXS (2.1 GB, lower quality), i1-IQ3_XS (2.3 GB), i1-IQ3_S (2.3 GB, beats Q3_K*), i1-Q3_K_S (2.3 GB, IQ3_XS probably better), i1-IQ3_M (2.4 GB), i1-Q3_K_M (2.5 GB, IQ3_S probably better), i1-Q3_K_L (2.6 GB, IQ3_M probably better), i1-IQ4_XS (2.7 GB), i1-IQ4_NL (2.8 GB, prefer IQ4_XS), i1-Q4_0 (2.8 GB, fast, low quality), i1-Q4_K_S (2.9 GB, optimal size/speed/quality), i1-Q4_K_M (3.0 GB, fast, recommended), i1-Q4_1 (3.1 GB), i1-Q5_K_S (3.3 GB), i1-Q5_K_M (3.4 GB), and i1-Q6_K (3.8 GB, practically like static Q6_K). The quants are sorted by size, not necessarily by quality, and IQ-quants are often preferable over similar-sized non-IQ quants. For a convenient overview and download list, users are directed to a specific page. If users are unsure how to use GGUF files, they are advised to refer to additional resources for details, including instructions on how to concatenate multi-part files. A graph by ikawrakow is available for comparing some lower-quality quant types, and Artefact2 has provided additional thoughts on the matter. Users are directed to a separate page for frequently asked questions and model request information. The model developer thanks their company for providing server access and workstation upgrades to support this work in their free time, and also thanks another individual for granting access to a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of many more imatrix quants at higher quality.

instruction: How would this paragraph sound when spoken naturally?
description: VibeVoice-Realtime is a lightweight real-time text-to-speech model that supports streaming text input and robust long-form speech generation. It is designed to enable real-time TTS services, narrate live data streams, and allow different LLMs to start producing speech from their first tokens before a full answer is generated. The model produces initial audible speech in approximately 300 milliseconds, depending on hardware. Although primarily built for English, it exhibits some level of multilingual capability and performs reasonably well in additional languages including German, French, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, and Spanish. The model uses an interleaved, windowed design that incrementally encodes incoming text chunks while continuing diffusion-based acoustic latent generation from prior context. It relies solely on an efficient acoustic tokenizer operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz. The model has a parameter size of 0.5B and is deployment-friendly. It supports only a single speaker and is currently intended for English speech only; other languages may produce unpredictable results. The model integrates a transformer-based LLM with a specialized acoustic tokenizer and a diffusion-based decoding head. The acoustic tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks and achieves 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. The diffusion head is a lightweight module with 4 layers and approximately 40M parameters, conditioned on LLM hidden states, and uses a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process with Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 8,192 tokens. The acoustic tokenizer is pre-trained, and during VibeVoice training, only the LLM and diffusion head parameters are trained using a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k to 8K). The model achieves satisfactory performance on short-sentence benchmarks and is focused on long-form speech generation. On the LibriSpeech test-clean set, it achieves a WER of 2.00% and a speaker similarity of 0.695. On the SEED test-en set, it achieves a WER of 2.05% and a speaker similarity of 0.633. The model is intended for research purposes exploring real-time highly realistic audio generation. It is not intended for use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations, including trade compliance laws, or for use in any other way that is prohibited by the MIT License. It is not intended for generating text transcripts or for use in scenarios such as voice impersonation, disinformation, real-time voice conversion, circumventing technical safeguards, unsupported languages, or generating background ambience, Foley, or music. The model may inherit any biases, errors, or omissions from its base model, Qwen2.5 0.5b. It may produce unexpected, biased, or inaccurate outputs. High-quality synthetic speech can be misused for impersonation, fraud, or disinformation. Users must ensure transcripts are reliable, check content accuracy, and avoid using generated content in misleading ways. The model does not support reading code, mathematical formulas, or uncommon symbols. It does not handle overlapping speech segments or non-speech audio. We do not recommend using VibeVoice in commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. We recommend disclosing to end users that they are listening to AI-generated content. The model is intended for research and development purposes only. To mitigate misuse, the acoustic tokenizer has been removed to prevent users from creating embeddings, an audible disclaimer is automatically embedded into every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added to generated audio for verification. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and being mindful of data privacy concerns.

instruction: How do I approach a problem that requires both mathematical and logical reasoning?
description: gpt-oss-120b-Distill-Phi-4-14B is a GRPO inference test model based on the microsoft/phi-4 (14B) base model. It is fine-tuned through a three-stage training pipeline—SFT (Cold Start) → RLHF (GRPO) → SFT—aiming to achieve excellent multi-step reasoning capabilities. The core methodology of this model is derived from three papers: DeepSeek-R1 (Nature, vol. 645), DeepSeekMath (arXiv:2402.03300v3), and Phi-4-reasoning (arXiv:2504.21318). Unlike the Microsoft paper, the reinforcement learning-guided reasoning part is moved to an earlier stage, using the SFT + GRPO + SFT method to generate explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT), attempting to induce reasoning abilities in the Phi-4 Instruct model through reinforcement learning and supervised learning. This model uses a distilled dataset from the open-source model gpt-oss-120b as its fine-tuning knowledge base, thereby inheriting its profound reasoning style and knowledge system. The model's training pipeline includes three stages: Foundational Supervised Fine-Tuning (Cold Start), Reinforcement Learning with GRPO, and Post-RL SFT (Alignment and Capability Consolidation). The GRPO algorithm is an advanced RLHF algorithm that estimates the advantage function by comparing the relative merits of a group of candidate outputs, achieving more stable policy gradient updates. The reward system includes multiple weighted rewards and penalty signals to shape the model's behavior, covering core objective rewards, quality and alignment rewards, behavioral regularization and penalties, and exploration and diversity incentives. The model's performance includes the ability to perform logical reasoning, mathematical problem-solving, and role-playing debates, with demonstrated capabilities in general chat, knowledge Q&A, adversarial reasoning, and mathematical reasoning. Limitations include limited training data and resources, potential language mixing in reasoning processes, and reduced expressive richness in broader open-domain conversations due to domain-specific training. The model is intended as a reference test model and is not a fully optimized reasoning model.

instruction: Can you analyze this short video clip to determine if it's authentic or artificially generated?
description: The SOTA Deepfake Detector - DFD is a spatiotemporal adaptation built on a frozen CLIP-ViT-Large backbone with lightweight spatiotemporal adapters inserted to learn temporal relationships across frames. The backbone parameters are frozen; only the adapters and final classification head are trainable. The model is designed to classify short video clips or frame sequences as either real or deepfake/manipulated. The input consists of 12 frames per video, each of size 384×384 pixels, and the model outputs logits for two classes: 0 = real, 1 = fake. The model uses a PyTorch framework with Hugging Face Transformers and was trained using BF16 mixed precision. The model has approximately 5,255,938 trainable parameters, which is about 1.7% of the total 308 million parameters. The model was trained on the DFD (Deep Fake Detection) dataset, which contains 3,431 videos, with 363 real videos (10.6%) and 3,068 fake videos (89.4%). A total of 41,172 frames were extracted from the videos. The training used a 90/10 stratified train/validation split and a WeightedRandomSampler to address class imbalance. The training configuration included a batch size of 16 (effective 32 with gradient accumulation), AdamW optimizer with weight decay of 0.05, a learning rate of 5e-6 with cosine decay and 10% warmup, and 12 training epochs. The loss function was cross-entropy with label smoothing of 0.1, and gradient clipping was applied with a max_grad_norm of 1.0. Training augmentations included random horizontal flip and brightness jitter. The model was trained on an NVIDIA L4 GPU with 24GB VRAM, and all random seeds were fixed for reproducibility. The best validation accuracy achieved was 84.88%, with real class detection ranging from approximately 47–55% and fake class detection ranging from approximately 64–93%. The training loss converged from 0.7097 to 0.6921 over 12 epochs. The model has several known limitations, including a highly imbalanced validation set (89% fake), a small number of real videos (363) that limits generalization to unseen real samples, and optimization for the DFD dataset, which may require fine-tuning for other deepfake types. The temporal context is limited to 12 frames (approximately 0.4–1s depending on FPS), so long-term artifacts may be missed. The model is intended for use in research on deepfake detection, benchmarking against other detectors, and integration into content-moderation pipelines with caution. It is not intended for use in medical, legal, or other high-stakes decisions without human review, or for use on domains/styles substantially different from the DFD dataset without further fine-tuning. The model is available on the Hugging Face Hub under the repository Arko007/deepfake-detector-dfd-sota and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model files include pytorch_model.bin (1.28GB) and config.json. Inference involves loading a checkpoint, initializing the model, preprocessing input frames using the CLIP image processor, and running a forward pass to obtain logits, which are then converted to softmax probabilities and a predicted class. The model expects input tensors of shape (batch_size, 12, 3, 384, 384) with dtype float32 or bf16 where supported. The model can be used with helper functions to extract frames from a video or folder, preprocess them, and perform inference. The model is not intended for production use without additional robustness, error handling, and optimization.

instruction: What are the key differences between the two images provided?
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. The models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, which include highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. Molmo2 models demonstrate state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models of similar size. The Molmo2-O-7B variant is based on a specific architecture and uses a specific vision backbone. It outperforms other open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science, and the Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2, including training code, evaluations, and intermediate checkpoints, will be made available at a later date to support open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. It is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Users should review the sources to determine if the model is appropriate for their use case. Evaluations show that Molmo2-O-7B achieves an average score of 59.7 on 15 academic benchmarks, compared to other models such as GPT-5 (70.6), Gemini 3 Pro (70.0), and others. The model supports tasks such as general video question answering and pointing video question answering, where it can generate text responses and extract pointing coordinates as a list of (frame_id, x, y) triplets from model output text.

instruction: Generate a detailed furniture plan for a double-sided bookcase with a total width of 200cm.
description: This is a 3D furniture generation model based on a Transformer architecture. It is designed to generate 3D furniture structural data based on text prompts such as 'wardrobe' or 'cabinet'. The model directly produces custom JSON structures containing panel size, position, rotation, and type information, which can be rendered directly in front-end frameworks such as Three.js. The model supports structured output in the form of JSON structures that can be directly parsed. It allows parametric control by supporting the input of furniture type, size, and other parameters. It achieves millimeter-level accuracy in coordinates through normalization training. The model is highly extensible and can be fine-tuned with a user's own furniture dataset. It is compatible with Three.js, enabling the generated furniture models to be rendered directly in the browser. The model is open-sourced on Hugging Face Model Hub and can be downloaded, tested, and fine-tuned. For collaboration or technical inquiries, contact chengyuhan1030@gmail.com.

instruction: Can you create a digital painting of a futuristic city at sunset with flying vehicles and glowing buildings?
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality. FLUX.2 [klein] 9B is a 9 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is the flagship small model, defining the Pareto frontier for quality versus latency across text-to-image, single-reference editing, and multi-reference generation. It matches or exceeds models five times its size in under half a second. It is built on a 9B flow model with an 8B Qwen3 text embedder, step-distilled to 4 inference steps. It is a distilled model for sub-second image generation with outstanding quality. It supports text-to-image and image-to-image multi-reference editing in a single unified model. It is suitable for real-time generation and integration into applications. It is ideal for creative exploration with excellent prompt adherence and output diversity. It is available for non-commercial use. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. The model fits in approximately 29GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and above. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation included multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision included a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks, and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license, and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models were released under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and may monitor use to detect misuse or abuse of models and services. A dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) is provided to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations.

instruction: Puoi analizzare un video e fornire un output con le coordinate degli occhi in ogni fotogramma?
description: Il modello implementa una rete neurale convoluzionale per la detection degli occhi basata su YOLOv12, ottimizzata per single-class detection, real-time processing, alta precisione e recall, e robusta performance in diverse condizioni di illuminazione. L'architettura utilizza YOLOv12n, una versione nano per performance ottimali, con input size configurabile a 640x640 pixel e output in forma di bounding boxes con confidence scores. Il modello è addestrato su un dataset custom di high-quality disponibile su HuggingFace, con una pipeline completa di training, validazione e test. Le metriche di performance includono una precisione superiore a 0.85, un recall superiore a 0.80, un F1 Score superiore a 0.82, un mAP@0.5 superiore a 0.80 e un mAP@0.5:0.95 superiore a 0.70. Il modello supporta predizioni su immagini, video e webcam in tempo reale, con configurazione flessibile tramite file YAML e monitoraggio avanzato con integrazione WandB. Il training include data augmentation con variazioni HSV, trasformazioni geometriche, mosaic augmentation e flipping orizzontale. Il modello è progettato per applicazioni come medical imaging, human-computer interaction, security systems, research applications e quality control. Tuttavia, presenta limitazioni in caso di posizioni non naturali degli occhi, ad esempio volto ruotato di 90°.

instruction: What emotions or moods are conveyed in this audio sample?
description: This is the fine-tuned version of the EAT-base model, further trained on the AS-2M dataset. Compared to the pre-trained model, this version provides enhanced audio representations and typically yields better performance in downstream audio understanding tasks such as classification and captioning. The model supports both frame-level (~50Hz) and utterance-level (CLS token) representations. It is designed for feature extraction and is intended for use in audio understanding tasks. The model is based on the EAT framework, which is described in the associated paper titled 'EAT: Self-supervised pre-training with efficient audio transformer' by Chen, Wenxi and Liang, Yuzhe and Ma, Ziyang and Zheng, Zhisheng and Chen, Xie, published as an arXiv preprint in 2024.

instruction: How can I find the most appropriate paragraph from a textbook to answer the question 'What is photosynthesis?'?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and was designed for semantic search. It has been trained on 215 million (question, answer) pairs from diverse sources. The model uses mean pooling to generate embeddings and produces normalized embeddings with a length of 1. It supports the use of dot-product, cosine-similarity, or euclidean distance as score functions. When loaded with sentence-transformers, dot-product is preferred as it is faster. The model is intended to encode queries and text paragraphs in a dense vector space to find relevant documents. It has a limit of 512 word pieces, and text longer than that will be truncated. The model was trained on input text up to 250 word pieces and may not work well for longer text. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the model using a contrastive learning objective on a concatenation of multiple datasets. The model was trained using mean-pooling, cosine-similarity as the similarity function, and a scale of 20. The training data includes duplicate question pairs from WikiAnswers, automatically generated (question, paragraph) pairs from Wikipedia, (title, body) and (title, answer) pairs from StackExchanges, triplets from Bing search engine, (query, answer) pairs from Google queries and featured snippets, (question, answer) pairs from Amazon product pages, Yahoo Answers, and other sources. The total number of training tuples is 214,988,242.

instruction: Can you turn this Telugu script into a spoken version that aligns with the intonation of the given reference?
description: IndicF5 is a high-quality, near-human polyglot Text-to-Speech (TTS) model trained on 1417 hours of high-quality speech data. It supports 11 Indian languages: Assamese, Bengali, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Malayalam, Marathi, Odia, Punjabi, Tamil, and Telugu. The model is designed to synthesize speech based on three inputs: the text to be spoken, a reference prompt audio that guides the model's prosody and speaker characteristics, and the transcript of the reference prompt audio. IndicF5 is intended for use with explicit permission for voice cloning, and unauthorized use is strictly prohibited. Users are responsible for any misuse of the model. The model is based on prior work by other researchers in the field of text-to-speech synthesis. If used in research or projects, it should be cited appropriately.

instruction: Which of these two summaries best captures the key points of the report on climate policy?
description: This is a model finetuned from Alibaba-NLP/gte-multilingual-reranker-base using the Sentence Transformers library. It is a Cross Encoder model designed to compute scores for pairs of texts, which can be used for text reranking and semantic search. The model has a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and produces one output label. It can be loaded and used with the Sentence Transformers library for inference, where it can calculate scores for text pairs or rank texts based on similarity to a single text. The model was trained on a dataset of 24,588 samples, with each sample containing two sentences and a label. The training used a non-default set of hyperparameters, including a batch size of 32 for both training and evaluation, 5 training epochs, and FP16 precision. During training, the model achieved a Spearman correlation of 0.8709 on the validation dataset. The training loss decreased over time, and the Spearman correlation on the validation set improved from 0.7581 at step 100 to 0.8709 at step 3200. The model was trained using PyTorch 2.8.0+cu128, Sentence Transformers 5.1.2, and Transformers 4.57.1. The training data included a variety of sentence pairs, with sentence lengths ranging from 3 to 998 characters for the first sentence and 73 to 352 characters for the second sentence. The labels ranged from 0.0 to 1.0, with a mean of 0.41. The model uses a linear learning rate scheduler with no warmup steps and a learning rate of 5e-05. The training process included logging of training loss and validation Spearman correlation at each step.

instruction: I want to change the text on a sign in a photo to a different message. Can you do that?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2509 is the monthly iteration of Qwen-Image-Edit, introduced in September. It is designed to support image editing tasks, with improvements over the previous version released in August. The model supports multi-image editing, allowing combinations such as 'person + person,' 'person + product,' and 'person + scene,' with optimal performance observed when using 1 to 3 input images. For single-image inputs, it significantly improves editing consistency, particularly in preserving facial identity for person editing, product identity for product editing, and text content, fonts, colors, and materials for text editing. The model also includes native support for ControlNet, enabling the use of depth maps, edge maps, keypoint maps, and other related features. It is built upon the Qwen-Image-Edit architecture and further trained via image concatenation to enable multi-image editing. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: Create an image of a cat with fur that looks soft and realistic under natural daylight.
description: Qwen-Image-2512 is a text-to-image foundational model, representing the December update of the Qwen-Image series. It is a GGUF quantized version of the model, using a methodology for state-of-the-art performance. Key improvements in Qwen-Image-2512 include enhanced human realism, finer natural detail, and improved text rendering. The model significantly reduces the 'AI-generated' look and substantially enhances overall image realism, especially for human subjects. It delivers more detailed rendering of landscapes, animal fur, and other natural elements. The model also improves the accuracy and quality of textual elements, achieving better layout and more faithful multimodal (text + image) composition. Over 10,000 rounds of blind model evaluations have been conducted, and the results show that Qwen-Image-2512 is currently the strongest open-source model and remains highly competitive among closed-source models. The model can be used with the diffusers library, and it supports generating images with various aspect ratios. It is designed to be used with prompts that describe the desired image, including detailed descriptions of subjects, environments, and visual styles. The model is capable of rendering complex scenes with high fidelity, including human portraits, landscapes, and technical or educational infographics. It is particularly effective in capturing fine details such as facial expressions, hair strands, and environmental textures. The model also supports generating images with specific lighting conditions, such as indoor illumination or natural daylight. It is capable of rendering realistic textures, including fur, fabric, and skin, and can produce images that resemble casual snapshots or professional photography. The model is suitable for a wide range of applications, including artistic creation, educational materials, and technical illustrations. It is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on devices with CUDA support for improved performance. The model is part of a series of updates to the Qwen-Image model, with each version introducing new features and improvements. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face and ModelScope platforms, and users are encouraged to try the latest version for the best results. The model is developed by a team of researchers and is available under an open-source license. If Qwen-Image-2512 is used in research, users are encouraged to cite the associated technical report.

instruction: Design a visual of a peaceful beach at sunrise with palm trees and a few boats in the distance.
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a model designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts and to advance complex text rendering. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is a text-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is available in different quantized versions, with data types including INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). Two rank configurations are provided: r32 for faster inference and r128 for better quality but slower inference. The model includes standard inference speed versions for general use, as well as 4-step and 8-step distilled models fused with additional components using LoRA strength = 1.0. The 4-step distilled models are available in both r32 and r128 ranks for INT4 and NVFP4 data types, with the r128 versions offering better quality at the cost of slower inference. Similarly, the 8-step distilled models are available in the same configurations, with r128 versions providing improved quality but slower performance. The model is based on a diffusion architecture and is related to the SVDQuant method, as described in the paper 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models' presented at the Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025.

instruction: How would you describe the key points of this article about cultural events in Athens?
description: GreekBART is a Greek sequence-to-sequence pretrained model designed for generative tasks such as abstractive summarization. It is based on a reconstruction approach, where the model learns to reconstruct a corrupted input sentence. The pretraining was conducted using a corpus of 76.9GB of Greek raw text. Unlike existing BERT-based Greek language models like GreekBERT, GreekBART includes both an encoder and a decoder that are pretrained, making it well-suited for generation tasks. The base version of GreekBART has 12 layers and 165 million parameters. In addition to the base model, it is fine-tuned for three specific tasks: generating an abstract from a Greek news article (`greekbart-news24-abstract`), generating a title from a Greek news article (`greekbart-news24-title`), and performing binary sentiment classification (`greekbart-sentiment-classification`). The model was trained and evaluated at École Polytechnique by Iakovos Evdaimon, Hadi Abdine, Christos Xypolopoulos, Stamatis Outsios, Michalis Vazirgiannis, and Giorgos Stamou.

instruction: Could you retrieve a document that includes a visual representation of the data pipeline architecture?
description: ColModernVBERT is the late-interaction version of ModernVBERT that is fine-tuned for visual document retrieval tasks and is the most performant model on this task. The ModernVBERT suite consists of compact 250M-parameter vision-language encoders that achieve state-of-the-art performance in this size class, matching the performance of models up to 10 times larger. ColModernVBERT is designed to support visual document retrieval tasks and is part of a family of models that includes BiModernVBERT, ModernVBERT-embed, and the base ModernVBERT model. The base ModernVBERT model is trained using a masked language modeling objective for modality alignment, while ModernVBERT-embed is further trained using contrastive learning without document specialization. BiModernVBERT is a bi-encoder version fine-tuned for visual document retrieval. ColModernVBERT matches the performance of models nearly 10 times larger on visual document benchmarks and provides an interesting inference speed on CPU compared to models of similar performance. The model is released under the MIT license. If used in academic work, it should be cited with the provided citation information.

instruction: Is there a way to generate code from a 3D scan of a toy for editing in Blender?
description: MeshCoder is a framework that converts 3D point clouds into editable Blender Python scripts, enabling programmatic reconstruction and editing of complex human-made objects. It addresses prior limitations by developing expressive APIs for modeling intricate geometries, building a large-scale dataset of 1 million object-code pairs across 41 categories, and training a multimodal LLM to generate accurate, part-segmented code from point clouds. The approach outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality, supports intuitive shape and topology editing via code modifications, and enhances 3D reasoning capabilities in LLMs.

instruction: How can I get a detailed pose estimation of this person for use in a biomechanics study?
description: YOLO26-X-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model is a native end-to-end architecture that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and results in faster, lighter, and easier deployment in real-world systems. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and enhances deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, and the removal of DFL to simplify inference and improve hardware compatibility. It achieves an mAP Pose of 71.6 (50-95, e2e) and 91.6 (50, e2e), with a CPU inference speed of 565.4 ms and a T4 TensorRT10 speed of 12.2 ms. The model has 57.6 million parameters and 201.7 billion FLOPs. It is optimized for edge computing, offering up to 43% faster CPU inference.

instruction: How do I optimize the generation process for faster output?
description: This repository contains quantized models for HunyuanVideo-1.5, optimized for use with the LightX2V framework. The quantized models are designed to significantly reduce memory usage while maintaining high-quality video generation performance. The available models include two Diffusion Transformer (DIT) models for 720p video generation: one for image-to-video (I2V) and one for text-to-video (T2V) tasks, as well as a quantized text encoder based on the Qwen2.5-VL LLM architecture. These models use FP8-E4M3 quantization with the SGL (SGLang) kernel scheme, which provides up to 50% reduction in VRAM usage, minimal quality degradation compared to full precision models, and faster inference through optimized kernels. The quantized models are intended to be used in conjunction with the original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights and require specific configurations to be applied before the generator is created. Performance benefits include lower VRAM requirements, enabling use on GPUs with less memory, faster inference due to optimized kernels, and preserved visual quality. The models support both T2V and I2V tasks with specific parameters such as frame count, guidance scale, and aspect ratio. The original HunyuanVideo-1.5 model weights are still required for operation. For best performance, SageAttention 2 is recommended as the attention mode. The models are released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Generate a list of 10 practical tips for maintaining mental health during a global pandemic.
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two models are available: gpt-oss-120b, intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters; and gpt-oss-20b, intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and require the harmony format to function correctly. The gpt-oss-120b model is covered by this model card. The models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them suitable for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, which is useful for debugging and increasing trust in outputs, though it is not intended for end users. They are fine-tunable for specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning and support agentic capabilities such as function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The models can be used with Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. They can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI. The larger model, gpt-oss-120b, can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node, while the smaller model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware. The models are suitable for web browsing, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks.

instruction: Can I use a short audio sample to create a custom voice for an interactive toy or device?
description: NeuTTS Air is a state-of-the-art, on-device, text-to-speech (TTS) language model that enables super-realistic voice synthesis and instant voice cloning. Built on a 0.5B LLM backbone, it delivers natural-sounding speech with real-time performance, making it suitable for embedded voice agents, assistants, toys, and compliance-safe applications. The model is optimized for on-device deployment in GGML format, allowing it to run efficiently on devices such as phones, laptops, and Raspberry Pis. It features a simple architecture combining a language model and a neural audio codec, which achieves high audio quality at low bitrates using a single codebook. NeuTTS Air supports voice cloning with as little as 3 seconds of reference audio and is designed to be secure, with watermarked outputs and optimized power consumption for mobile and embedded use. For best results, reference audio should be mono, 16-44 kHz, 3–15 seconds in length, clean, and contain natural, continuous speech. The model requires two inputs: a reference audio sample and a text string, which it uses to synthesize speech in the style of the reference. The model is available through the official website neuphonic.com, and users are warned to be cautious of unrelated websites. The model includes a disclaimer urging users not to use it for harmful purposes.

instruction: I need a set of snowflake images for a photo collage—can you generate a few unique ones?
description: This model is an unconditional DDPM U-Net (EMA) trained from scratch to generate images of snowflakes. It operates on latent representations produced by a pre-trained Variational Autoencoder (VAE), which is not part of this model. The model learns the visual structure of real snowflakes from macro photography, enabling the generation of novel, synthetic snowflake images that resemble real crystalline forms. The input to the model is Gaussian noise, and the output consists of snowflake images after VAE decoding, with a resolution of 512×512. The model is used in the open-source application Snowflakeizer for snowflake image generation. It was trained on a dataset of macro photos of real snowflakes by Alexey Kljatov, which is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY). The model weights are licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0). When using this model, proper attribution to the original dataset author is required. The model is intended for research in generative modeling, image synthesis experiments, artistic and creative applications, and educational purposes related to diffusion models. It has limitations, including being trained on a single-domain dataset of snowflake macro photography, and generated images may not correspond to physically accurate crystal growth processes. The model may also produce artifacts or unrealistic structures. The training data consists of non-sensitive macro photographs of natural objects, with no known privacy, biometric, or identity-related concerns. Generated images should not be presented as real scientific observations.

instruction: Is it possible to identify the water bodies in this satellite image using the segmentation results?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of facebook/mask2former-swin-base-ade-semantic for semantic segmentation of satellite and aerial imagery. It uses the Mask2Former architecture with a Swin Transformer backbone and is designed for semantic segmentation, specifically land cover classification. The model classifies pixels into nine land cover categories: Background, Bareland, Grass, Pavement, Road, Tree, Water, Cropland, and Building. It was trained for 17 epochs and achieved a best mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) of 0.5202 and a validation loss of 44.21. The model is intended for land cover classification from satellite and aerial imagery, with applications in urban planning, environmental monitoring, geographic information systems (GIS), and remote sensing research. However, it has several limitations: it exhibits geographic bias due to being primarily trained on imagery from specific regions in the OpenEarthMap dataset, performs best on imagery with resolution similar to the training data, may require fine-tuning for different satellite sensors or aerial platforms, and its performance may vary with seasonal or weather conditions.

instruction: I need a prediction for the next quarter’s sales using the data I've provided—can you assist?
description: TabPFN-2.5 is a transformer-based foundation model that uses in-context-learning to solve tabular prediction problems in a forward pass. It is designed for regression and classification tasks with up to 50,000 samples and 2000 features in structured tabular format. The model is not suitable for unstructured data such as text or images, and it has not been tested for use with more than 50,000 samples or 2000 features. The architecture consists of a transformer with TabPFNv2-like alternating attention and 18-24 layers. TabPFN-2.5 is trained purely on synthetic tabular tasks, while Real-TabPFN-2.5 is a version that has been continued pre-trained on real-world datasets. The model has been evaluated on proprietary benchmark collections, including TabArena and RealCause, and it achieves new state-of-the-art results in each. It is free from dataset leakage from the pretraining stage due to its training on synthetic data, but users should ensure that the labeled data is free of biases when applied to high-risk use cases. Performance can degrade when applied to more than 50,000 data points and/or 2000 features. The model weights are released under the tabpfn-2.5-license-v1.0, which allows for research and limited internal evaluation but prohibits use for commercial or production purposes. A Commercial Enterprise License is available for production use cases, providing access to a high-speed inference engine, dedicated support, integration tooling, and other internal models. The model was developed by Prior Labs and is available in version v1.0.

instruction: Identify the best materials that analyze the rise of e-commerce in emerging markets.
description: The mxbai-edge-colbert-v0-32m is a lightweight ColBERT model with 32 million parameters and a projection dimension of 64. It is built on top of ModernBERT, benefiting from its architectural efficiencies. Despite its small size, it is the best-performing 'edge-sized' retriever, outperforming ColBERTv2 and many models with over ten times more parameters. The model can create multi-vector representations for documents of up to 32,000 tokens and is fully compatible with the PyLate library. It is designed for end-to-end retrieval tasks and supports encoding documents and queries, indexing, and retrieving top-k matches. It is also well-suited for use as a re-ranker following a first-stage retrieval with more lightweight models. Evaluation results on the BEIR benchmark show that it achieves an average score of 0.521, with strong performance on tasks such as MS MARCO (0.450), SciFact (0.740), and TREC-COVID (0.775). On the LongEmbed benchmark, it achieves an average score of 0.849 for documents up to 32,000 tokens. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. If used, it should be cited using the associated tech report, and if its projection heads are specifically discussed, an additional report on projection variants should also be cited. Additionally, if PyLate is used in conjunction with the model, it should be cited separately.

instruction: I need an estimate of the water solubility for the chemical 'C(C)C(=O)O'. Can you help?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict aqueous solubility (log SW). This property affects environmental fate, bioavailability, and exposure potential. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model is trained on the SW dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What are the recommended versions for users with high-performance hardware?
description: This model provides various quantized versions of a neural network, optimized for different use cases and performance requirements. The available quantizations include IQ1_S, IQ1_M, IQ2_XXS, IQ2_XS, IQ2_S, IQ2_M, Q2_K_S, Q2_K, IQ3_XXS, IQ3_XS, Q3_K_S, IQ3_S, IQ3_M, Q3_K_M, Q3_K_L, IQ4_XS, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q4_K_S, Q4_K_M, Q4_1, Q5_K_S, Q5_K_M, and Q6_K. These quants vary in file size from 1.3 GB to 3.7 GB and are associated with different quality and performance characteristics. For example, the Q4_K_S quant is described as optimal in terms of size, speed, and quality, while the Q4_K_M is noted as fast and recommended. Some quants, such as IQ1_S and IQ1_M, are described as suitable only for desperate use cases, and others like Q2_K_S and IQ3_XXS are noted for their low quality. The model is intended for users who require lightweight and efficient inference, and it supports concatenation of multi-part files for use. The model quants are made available through a combination of company resources and private computing infrastructure provided by a collaborator.

instruction: Could you write down what’s being said in this Arabic language interview I recorded?
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Arabic. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe speech into text. Evaluation results on the Arabic test data of Common Voice show a Word Error Rate (WER) of 39.59% and a Character Error Rate (CER) of 18.18%. These results were obtained using a specific evaluation script. The model has been compared to other Arabic speech recognition models, with varying WER and CER values reported. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face Transformers library. It is important to note that the evaluation results may differ slightly depending on the specific evaluation script used. The model is intended for use in speech recognition tasks involving Arabic speech input.

instruction: Replace the sky in this landscape photo with a cloudy evening sky, ensuring the colors of the trees and mountains remain consistent.
description: LongCat-Image-Edit is the image editing version of Longcat-Image. It supports bilingual (Chinese-English) editing and achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source image editing models, delivering leading instruction-following and image quality with superior visual consistency. LongCat-Image-Edit supports various editing tasks, such as global editing, local editing, text modification, and reference-guided editing. It has strong semantic understanding capabilities and can perform precise editing according to instructions. The model has strong consistency preservation capabilities, specifically scrutinizing whether attributes in non-edited regions, such as layout, texture, color tone, and subject identity, remain invariant unless targeted by the instruction, which is well demonstrated in multi-turn editing. LongCat-Image-Edit achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in image editing tasks while significantly improving model inference efficiency, especially among open-source image editing models. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, you must enclose the target text within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: Can you determine the environmental distribution value for the compound with SMILES 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)C(=O)Cl'? It's part of a chemical safety evaluation.
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict fugacity-based environmental fate parameter A. This property is used in fugacity models for environmental distribution prediction. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on the AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model was trained on the FBA dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Can you match this query with the most relevant product description?
description: Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is a 47M parameter dense biencoder embedding model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 384 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. Compared to most other open-source models, this model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets.
