The r2 models show strong performance across standard and IBM-built information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, ClapNQ), code retrieval (COIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables), and on many enterprise use cases. These models use a bi-encoder architecture to generate high-quality embeddings from text inputs such as queries, passages, and documents, enabling seamless comparison through cosine similarity. Built using retrieval-oriented pretraining, contrastive finetuning, knowledge distillation, and model merging, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 is optimized to ensure strong alignment between query and passage embeddings. The latest granite embedding r2 release introduces two English embedding models, both based on the ModernBERT architecture: granite-embedding-english-r2 (149M parameters) with an output embedding size of 768, replacing granite-embedding-125m-english; and granite-embedding-small-english-r2 (47M parameters), a reduced-size model with 8192 context length support, fewer layers, and a smaller output embedding size (384), replacing granite-embedding-30m-english. The model is designed to produce fixed-length vector representations for a given text, which can be used for text similarity, retrieval, and search applications. For efficient decoding, these models use Flash Attention 2. Installing it is optional but can lead to faster inference. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and is very easy to use. It can also be used with the Huggingface Transformers library and PyTorch. The granite embedding r2 models show strong performance across diverse tasks, including MTEB Retrieval (i.e., BEIR), MTEB-v2, code retrieval (CoIR), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR, LongEmbed), conversational multi-turn (MTRAG), and table retrieval (NQTables, OTT-QA, AIT-QA, MultiHierTT, OpenWikiTables). The average speed to encode documents on a single H100 GPU using a sliding window with 512 context length chunks is also reported. Nearing encoding speed of 200 documents per second, granite-embedding-small-english-r2 demonstrates speed and efficiency while maintaining competitive performance. The model has 47 million parameters, an embedding size of 384, 12 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1536, uses the GeGLU activation function, has a vocabulary size of 50368, a maximum sequence length of 8192, and is based on the ModernBERT architecture. The granite embedding r2 models incorporate key enhancements from the ModernBERT architecture, including alternating attention lengths to accelerate processing, rotary position embeddings for extended sequence length, a newly trained tokenizer optimized with code and text data, Flash Attention 2.0 for improved efficiency, and streamlined parameters, eliminating unnecessary bias terms. The models are trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the popular MS-MARCO retrieval dataset is not used in the training corpus due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing critical information about the data, including content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing information, usage restrictions, how the data will be acquired, and an assessment of sensitive information (i.e., personal information). The models were trained using IBM's computing cluster, BlueVela Cluster, outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs. Granite-embedding-small-english-r2 leverages both permissively licensed open-source and select proprietary data for enhanced performance. The training data for the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is trained only for English texts and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: Can you detect any disease entities in this patient's medical history?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting disease entities from the NCBI dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the NCBI_DISEASE dataset, a curated collection of 793 PubMed abstracts with 6,892 disease mentions mapped to 790 unique disease concepts from MeSH and OMIM. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-Disease and I-Disease. It is intended for use in clinical and research applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the distilbert-base-multilingual-cased architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.86, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.87, and accuracy of 0.96. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: I found an old photo online. Can you estimate the person's age and determine their gender from the image?
description: A state-of-the-art Vision Transformer model for simultaneous age estimation and gender classification, achieving 94.3% gender accuracy and 4.5 years age MAE on the UTKFace dataset. The model is designed to predict age and gender from facial images and supports multiple input types, including file paths, URLs, PIL Images, and NumPy arrays. It features a dual-head design with age regression and gender classification, based on the ViT-Base architecture. The model was trained on the UTKFace dataset, which contains 23,687 images, using 15 epochs with the AdamW optimizer and a learning rate of 2e-5. It has 86.8 million parameters and achieves an inference speed of approximately 50 milliseconds per image on a CPU. The model provides detailed output, including predicted age, gender, confidence scores, and probability distributions for male and female classifications. It is optimized for adults aged 16 to 60 years, with reduced accuracy for children under 13 and seniors over 60. For best results, it is recommended to use clear, well-lit, front-facing images. The model includes helper functions to return proper age and gender values, and it is advised to avoid using the standard pipeline approach, which may return raw label outputs such as LABEL_0 or LABEL_1. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is available for commercial use with proper attribution.

instruction: Render a customer support message from English to Turkish for a local service team.
description: LMT-60 is a suite of Chinese-English-centric multilingual machine translation (MMT) models trained on 90B tokens, including both monolingual and bilingual data, covering 60 languages across 234 translation directions. The model achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among models with similar language coverage. Both the CPT and SFT versions of LMT-60 are available in four sizes: 0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, and 8B. The supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data are publicly released. The model supports translation tasks across a wide range of languages, including 13 high-resource languages, 18 medium-resource languages, and 29 low-resource languages. The high-resource languages include Arabic, English, Spanish, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Turkish, and Chinese. The medium-resource languages include Bulgarian, Bengali, Czech, Danish, Modern Greek, Persian, Finnish, Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Korean, Norwegian, Romanian, Slovak, Swedish, Thai, Ukrainian, and Vietnamese. The low-resource languages include Amharic, Azerbaijani, Tibetan, Modern Hebrew, Croatian, Armenian, Icelandic, Javanese, Georgian, Kazakh, Central Khmer, Kirghiz, Lao, Chinese Mongolian, Marathi, Malay, Burmese, Nepali, Pashto, Sinhala, Swahili, Tamil, Telugu, Tajik, Tagalog, Uighur, Urdu, Uzbek, and Yue Chinese. The model is designed to perform translation tasks and is available for use through the Hugging Face Transformers library. The model is described in a paper titled 'Beyond English: Toward Inclusive and Scalable Multilingual Machine Translation with LLMs,' authored by Yingfeng Luo, Ziqiang Xu, Yuxuan Ouyang, Murun Yang, Dingyang Lin, Kaiyan Chang, Tong Zheng, Bei Li, Peinan Feng, Quan Du, Tong Xiao, and Jingbo Zhu, and available on arXiv with the identifier 2511.07003.

instruction: I have a set of unlabeled images; can you extract meaningful features from them for use in downstream tasks?
description: VTP is a unified visual tokenizer pre-training framework designed to improve the quality of latent spaces for generative models. It integrates image-text contrastive, self-supervised, and reconstruction learning to train visual tokenizers from scratch. The framework addresses the 'pre-training scaling problem,' where traditional reconstruction-based training leads to latent spaces biased toward low-level information, which does not translate to better generation performance. VTP introduces a new paradigm where understanding high-level semantics becomes a key driver for improving generation quality. The model supports multiple tasks, including zero-shot classification, linear probing classification, image reconstruction, and image generation. It demonstrates strong performance, achieving 78.2% zero-shot accuracy, 0.36 rFID for reconstruction, and 2.81 FID for generation. VTP shows significant scaling properties, with generative performance improving effectively with increased compute, parameters, and data. Compared to conventional autoencoders, VTP achieves a 65.8% improvement in FID for downstream generation when trained with the same DiT specifications. The model is available for use as an autoencoder, a feature extractor for self-supervised learning, and a CLIP-like image-text encoder. The authors caution that traditional autoencoders cannot be scaled up for diffusion generative models and emphasize the importance of representation learning for parameter, data, and training scalability.

instruction: I need an illustration of a futuristic city with neon lights and flying vehicles, rendered in a cyberpunk style.
description: Qwen-Image is an image generation foundation model in the Qwen series that achieves significant advances in complex text rendering and precise image editing. It demonstrates strong general capabilities in both image generation and editing, with exceptional performance in text rendering, especially for Chinese. The model supports a wide range of artistic styles, from photorealistic scenes to impressionist paintings, anime aesthetics, and minimalist design. It enables advanced image editing operations such as style transfer, object insertion or removal, detail enhancement, text editing within images, and human pose manipulation. Qwen-Image also supports image understanding tasks, including object detection, semantic segmentation, depth and edge (Canny) estimation, novel view synthesis, and super-resolution. These capabilities are powered by deep visual comprehension, allowing the model to adapt fluidly to creative prompts and provide intuitive input with coherent output. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: What are the most recent findings in medical literature about the use of stem cells for spinal cord injury repair?
description: This is a model finetuned from EmbeddingGemma-300m on the Medical Instruction and Retrieval Dataset (MIRIAD), specifically the first 100,000 question-passage pairs. It maps sentences and documents to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and is designed for medical information retrieval, particularly for searching passages (up to 1,000 tokens) of scientific medical papers using detailed medical questions. The model is based on the Sentence Transformer architecture, with a maximum sequence length of 1,024 tokens and uses cosine similarity for measuring similarity. It was trained to showcase how the EmbeddingGemma model can be adapted for specific domains and tasks to achieve stronger performance. The model is not affiliated with Google. The architecture includes a Gemma3TextModel transformer, a pooling layer with mean token pooling, two dense layers with identity activation functions, and a normalization layer. The model is available through the Sentence Transformers library and can be used for encoding queries and documents to compute their similarity. It was evaluated on the MIRIAD dataset, achieving cosine_ndcg@10 scores of 0.9026 on the evaluation set and 0.8862 on the test set. The training was conducted using a batch size of 128, a learning rate of 2e-05, and a single epoch with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The training process consumed 0.828 kWh of energy and emitted 0.331 kg of CO2. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU and a 13th Gen Intel Core i7-13700K CPU with 31.78 GB of RAM. The training used Python 3.11.6, Sentence Transformers 5.2.0.dev0, Transformers 4.56.0.dev0, PyTorch 2.7.1+cu126, and other related libraries. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: What is the best way to identify all the vehicles in a high-resolution sky image?
description: This model is a Mask2Former-Large (Swin-L backbone) model fine-tuned on the DLR SkyScapes aerial imagery dataset for 20-class semantic segmentation. It is designed for high-resolution urban and peri-urban environments and produces detailed semantic masks for roads, buildings, vegetation, vehicles, and more. The architecture is based on the Mask2Former framework with a Transformer-based Swin-L backbone. The base checkpoint used is `facebook/mask2former-swin-large-ade-semantic`, and the model is trained for the task of semantic segmentation in the domain of high-resolution aerial imagery. It supports 20 semantic classes from the DLR SkyScapes dataset, which is a subset of the original 31 classes. The model uses the CrossEntropyLoss function with an ignore index of 255 and is preprocessed using the Hugging Face `AutoImageProcessor`. For full reproducibility, it is recommended to use `use_fast=False`. The model weights are stored in `model.safetensors`. The DLR SkyScapes dataset is a high-resolution aerial dataset created by the German Aerospace Center (DLR), providing high-quality pixel-level annotations for detailed scene understanding in urban environments. The dataset contains 16 images with a resolution of 5616 × 3744 pixels and a ground sampling distance of 13 cm per pixel. It covers an aerial area of approximately 5.69 km², including both urban and rural regions, with 70,346 annotated instances. The 20 semantic classes used in this model include low vegetation, paved road, non paved road, paved parking, non paved parking, bikeways, sidewalks, entrance/exit, danger area, lane markings, building, car, trailer, van, truck, long truck, bus, clutter, impervious surface, and tree. These classes correspond to the class IDs used during training. The intended applications of this model include urban mapping and GIS, HD map generation, scene understanding for robotics, autonomous driving (aerial-based priors), environmental monitoring, and remote sensing research. However, it is not suitable for safety-critical deployment without human supervision.

instruction: I need the content of this Russian speech file converted into written text for documentation.
description: GigaAM-v3 is a Conformer-based foundation model with 220–240M parameters, pretrained on diverse Russian speech data using the HuBERT-CTC objective. It is the third generation of the GigaAM family and provides state-of-the-art performance on Russian ASR across a wide range of domains. GigaAM-v3 includes the following model variants: `ssl` — self-supervised HuBERT–CTC encoder pre-trained on 700,000 hours of Russian speech; `ctc` — ASR model fine-tuned with a CTC decoder; `rnnt` — ASR model fine-tuned with an RNN-T decoder; `e2e_ctc` — end-to-end CTC model with punctuation and text normalization; and `e2e_rnnt` — end-to-end RNN-T model with punctuation and text normalization. GigaAM-v3 training incorporates new internal datasets: callcenter conversations, speech with background music, natural speech, and speech with atypical characteristics. The models perform on average 30% better on these new domains, while maintaining the same quality as previous GigaAM generations on public benchmarks. The Word Error Rate (%) for GigaAM-v3 and other existing models over diverse domains is as follows: Open Datasets — V3_CTC 3.0, V3_RNNT 2.6, T-One + LM 5.7, Whisper 12.0; Golos Farfield — V3_CTC 4.5, V3_RNNT 3.9, T-One + LM 12.2, Whisper 16.7; Natural Speech — V3_CTC 7.8, V3_RNNT 6.9, T-One + LM 14.5, Whisper 13.6; Disordered Speech — V3_CTC 20.6, V3_RNNT 19.2, T-One + LM 51.0, Whisper 59.3; Callcenter — V3_CTC 10.3, V3_RNNT 9.5, T-One + LM 13.5, Whisper 23.9; the average Word Error Rate is 9.2 for V3_CTC and 8.4 for V3_RNNT, compared to 19.4 for T-One + LM and 25.1 for Whisper. The end-to-end ASR models (`e2e_ctc` and `e2e_rnnt`) produce punctuated, normalized text directly. In end-to-end ASR comparisons of `e2e_ctc` and `e2e_rnnt` against Whisper-large-v3, using Gemini 2.5 Pro as an LLM-as-a-judge, GigaAM-v3 models win by an average margin of 70:30.

instruction: Visualize a dragon soaring over a medieval castle surrounded by misty mountains.
description: The Stable Diffusion v2-1-base is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model designed to generate and modify images based on text prompts. It is a latent diffusion model that combines an autoencoder with a diffusion model trained in the latent space of the autoencoder. The model uses a fixed, pretrained text encoder for processing text prompts. It was developed by Robin Rombach and Patrick Esser and is primarily intended for research purposes. The model supports tasks such as the safe deployment of generative models, understanding their limitations and biases, artistic creation, and educational or creative tool development. The model is not intended for misuse, including the generation of harmful, offensive, or discriminatory content, or the creation of mis- and disinformation. The model has several limitations, including the inability to achieve perfect photorealism, difficulties in rendering legible text, challenges with compositional tasks, and potential issues with generating faces and people. It was trained mainly on English captions and may not perform well with non-English prompts. The model was trained on a subset of the LAION-5B dataset, which contains adult, violent, and sexual content, and was partially filtered using LAION's NSFW detector. The model's training procedure involves encoding images into latent representations using an autoencoder with a downsampling factor of 8, and encoding text prompts using the OpenCLIP-ViT/H text encoder. The text encoder's output is fed into the UNet backbone of the latent diffusion model via cross-attention, with a reconstruction objective and the v-objective used for training. The model was trained using 32 x 8 x A100 GPUs, the AdamW optimizer, a batch size of 2048, and a learning rate that warmed up to 0.0001 over 10,000 steps. Evaluation results show relative improvements of the checkpoints using different classifier-free guidance scales and 50 steps of DDIM sampling. The model's environmental impact includes an estimated CO2 emission of 15,000 kg eq. based on 200,000 hours of A100 GPU usage on AWS in the US-east region.

instruction: There's a lot of grain and noise in this video. How can I reduce that and make it smoother?
description: SeedVR is a diffusion transformer model designed for generic video restoration. It addresses the limitations of conventional restoration models, which often perform poorly on real-world and AIGC (AI-generated content) video restoration due to limited generation ability. While recent diffusion-based models have improved performance by incorporating diffusion prior via ControlNet-like or adaptor-like architectures, these methods are constrained by the diffusion prior, leading to issues such as limited generation ability on small texts and faces, and fixed-resolution processing (e.g., 512 or 1024). These models also typically rely on patch-based sampling, which involves dividing the input video into overlapping spatial-temporal patches and fusing them using a Gaussian kernel at each diffusion step. The large overlap required for coherent output often results in slow inference speed, especially for long videos at high resolutions. SeedVR tackles the key challenge in diffusion-based restoration by enabling arbitrary-resolution restoration without relying on any pretrained diffusion prior. It introduces advanced video generation technologies suitable for video restoration and follows state-of-the-art video generation training pipelines. SeedVR is described as the largest-ever diffusion transformer model for generic video restoration and is intended to push the frontiers of advanced video restoration and inspire future research in large vision models for real-world video restoration. However, these are prototype models, and their performance may not perfectly align with the paper. The methods are sometimes not robust to heavy degradations and very large motions, and they share some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Additionally, due to their strong generation ability, the methods may overly generate details on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos, occasionally leading to oversharpened results.

instruction: Create an image of a desert with dunes shaped like waves and a sky filled with stars and constellations.
description: FLUX.2 [dev] is a 32 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating, editing, and combining images based on text instructions. It is designed to perform state-of-the-art open text-to-image generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing. The model supports character, object, and style reference without the need for additional training. It was trained using guidance distillation, which makes it more efficient. The model is available for personal, scientific, and commercial use under the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License. Developers and creatives can use a reference implementation and sampling code provided in a dedicated repository. For local deployment on consumer-grade graphics cards, such as an RTX 4090 or RTX 5090, users can follow instructions on the GitHub page. The model includes filters for NSFW and IP-infringing content at input and output, and these filters or manual review must be used under the terms of the license. Black Forest Labs has implemented multiple pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). These include filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM, targeted fine-tuning to suppress harmful content, and ongoing internal and external evaluations. The final FLUX.2 [dev] checkpoint demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and showed higher resilience than leading open-weight models. The model includes content provenance features such as pixel-layer watermarking and links to the Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity (C2PA) standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 Pro applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to output content. Use of the model is governed by the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License and other policies that prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Black Forest Labs is monitoring for patterns of violative use after release and may issue takedown requests or ban users who repeatedly violate policies. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains reporting relationships with organizations such as the Internet Watch Foundation and the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.

instruction: Can you help me identify whether this picture is AI-generated or taken by a person?
description: This is a fine-tuned model trained on 60,000 AI-generated and 60,000 human images. The model is designed to classify images as either AI-generated or human-created. It supports image classification tasks and is based on the Siglip architecture. During training, the model was trained for 5 epochs with a total of 51,652,280,821 GFLOPs. The training loss was 0.0799, and the training process took 2 hours, 39 minutes, and 49.46 seconds, achieving a training speed of 69.053 samples per second and 4.316 steps per second. On the test set, the model achieved an evaluation accuracy of 0.9923 with an evaluation loss of 0.0551. The evaluation process took 2 minutes and 35.78 seconds, with a speed of 212.533 samples per second and 6.644 steps per second. The final test accuracy is 0.9923, with a macro F1 score of 0.9923 and a weighted F1 score of 0.9923. Some users have reported overfitting issues. The model can be used for inference by loading the model and processor, preprocessing the input image, and running inference to obtain predicted labels and confidence scores.

instruction: Can you evaluate this video for signs of manipulation or artificial creation?
description: The SOTA Deepfake Detector - DFD is a spatiotemporal adaptation built on a frozen CLIP-ViT-Large backbone with lightweight spatiotemporal adapters inserted to learn temporal relationships across frames. The backbone parameters are frozen; only the adapters and final classification head are trainable. The model is designed to classify short video clips or frame sequences as either real or deepfake/manipulated. The input consists of 12 frames per video, each of size 384×384 pixels, and the model outputs logits for two classes: 0 = real, 1 = fake. The model uses a PyTorch framework with Hugging Face Transformers and was trained using BF16 mixed precision. The model has approximately 5,255,938 trainable parameters, which is about 1.7% of the total 308 million parameters. The model was trained on the DFD (Deep Fake Detection) dataset, which contains 3,431 videos, with 363 real videos (10.6%) and 3,068 fake videos (89.4%). A total of 41,172 frames were extracted from the videos. The training used a 90/10 stratified train/validation split and a WeightedRandomSampler to address class imbalance. The training configuration included a batch size of 16 (effective 32 with gradient accumulation), AdamW optimizer with weight decay of 0.05, a learning rate of 5e-6 with cosine decay and 10% warmup, and 12 training epochs. The loss function was cross-entropy with label smoothing of 0.1, and gradient clipping was applied with a max_grad_norm of 1.0. Training augmentations included random horizontal flip and brightness jitter. The model was trained on an NVIDIA L4 GPU with 24GB VRAM, and all random seeds were fixed for reproducibility. The best validation accuracy achieved was 84.88%, with real class detection ranging from approximately 47–55% and fake class detection ranging from approximately 64–93%. The training loss converged from 0.7097 to 0.6921 over 12 epochs. The model has several known limitations, including a highly imbalanced validation set (89% fake), a small number of real videos (363) that limits generalization to unseen real samples, and optimization for the DFD dataset, which may require fine-tuning for other deepfake types. The temporal context is limited to 12 frames (approximately 0.4–1s depending on FPS), so long-term artifacts may be missed. The model is intended for use in research on deepfake detection, benchmarking against other detectors, and integration into content-moderation pipelines with caution. It is not intended for use in medical, legal, or other high-stakes decisions without human review, or for use on domains/styles substantially different from the DFD dataset without further fine-tuning. The model is available on the Hugging Face Hub under the repository Arko007/deepfake-detector-dfd-sota and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model files include pytorch_model.bin (1.28GB) and config.json. Inference involves loading a checkpoint, initializing the model, preprocessing input frames using the CLIP image processor, and running a forward pass to obtain logits, which are then converted to softmax probabilities and a predicted class. The model expects input tensors of shape (batch_size, 12, 3, 384, 384) with dtype float32 or bf16 where supported. The model can be used with helper functions to extract frames from a video or folder, preprocess them, and perform inference. The model is not intended for production use without additional robustness, error handling, and optimization.

instruction: I want to see a video of a serene beach with crashing waves and a bright full moon.
description: This model is a GGUF conversion of the Wan2.2 T2V A14B model integrated with VACE scopes, intended for experimental use only. It is based on the Wan2.2 T2V A14B model, which is licensed under Apache 2.0 and permits commercial use of generated content. The model is provided in two versions: a high-noise model for initial denoising steps and a low-noise model for detail refinement. Both models are in FP16 precision within GGUF container format, not quantized. The base model combines Wan2.2 T2V A14B with VACE scopes, and the model uses a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture. The high-noise expert is used for early denoising, focusing on layout and motion, while the low-noise expert is used for refining textures and details. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). The model incorporates VACE (Video Aesthetic Control Embedding), which enhances cinematic-level aesthetics and allows fine control over lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone. The model supports text-to-video generation at 480p and 720p resolutions, with high-definition motion generation at 720p@24fps on consumer GPUs. The model files are approximately 34.7 GB each and are intended to be used with the ComfyUI-GGUF extension. The model has known limitations, including color shifting issues similar to the original lym00 model, and is not recommended for production environments without thorough testing. Commercial use is allowed under the Apache 2.0 license, but users are responsible for ensuring compliance with laws and ethical use. The model is not quantized, and the FP16 format provides maximum quality at the cost of larger file sizes. The model is experimental, and output quality may vary. The VACE team is reportedly working on a fix for the color shifting issue, but until then, the model should be avoided for applications requiring color accuracy.

instruction: Identify the types of fruits visible in the image.
description: The OWLv2 model, proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model designed to query an image with one or multiple text queries. It uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To enable detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads using an object detection objective and a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The model is intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model was released in June 2023.

instruction: Please create an image of a serene forest with a glowing lake at night.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date. FLUX.2 [klein] unifies generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. It is built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality and can run on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is a full-capacity foundation model, undistilled, preserving complete training signal for maximum flexibility. It is ideal for fine-tuning, LoRA training, research, and custom pipelines where control matters more than speed. It offers higher output diversity than the distilled models. This repository holds an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] Base 4B. The main repository of this model (full BF16 weights) can be found separately. The model has an exceptional speed and quality-to-size ratio and is ideal for local deployment and fine-tuning on limited hardware. It was trained without step or guidance distillation, making it more efficient and flexible. It has open weights for customization and fine-tuning to drive science, research, and empower artists to iterate with speed. Its outputs can be used for commercial purposes as described in the relevant documentation. A reference implementation of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base, as well as sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository. Developers and creatives looking to build on top of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base are encouraged to use this as a starting point. The model is also available in both FP8 and BF16 versions. The FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base model fits in approximately 13GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 3090/4070 and above. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of its models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in its model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user from generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user from generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation was conducted throughout the process, including multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision was made after safety fine-tuning and prior to release, a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints was conducted, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on the company's hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and provides a dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations. The model is licensed under the specified license. This project may contain trademarks or logos for projects, products, or services. Use of Black Forest Labs and FLUX trademarks or logos in modified versions of this project must not cause confusion or imply sponsorship or endorsement. Any use of third-party trademarks, intellectual property or logos is subject to those third-party's policies.

instruction: What part of the image corresponds to the 'calcified mass in the right kidney' mentioned in the radiology note?
description: Knowledge to Sight (K2Sight) is a novel framework designed for grounding abnormalities in medical images, where the goal is to localize clinical findings based on textual descriptions. Unlike generalist Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that often struggle with domain-specific medical terms, K2Sight introduces structured semantic supervision. It achieves this by decomposing clinical concepts into interpretable visual attributes like shape, density, and anatomical location, distilled from domain ontologies. This approach guides region-text alignment during training, enabling data-efficient training of compact models (0.23B and 2B parameters) using only 1.5% of the data required by state-of-the-art medical VLMs. Despite their small size and limited training data, K2Sight models achieve performance on par with or better than 7B+ medical VLMs, with up to 9.82% improvement in $mAP_{50}$. This model can be easily integrated and used for zero-shot abnormality grounding in medical images.

instruction: Is there a way to visualize the depth of this landscape from multiple perspectives?
description: DA3 Small is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation. It is an efficient foundation model that uses a unified depth-ray representation. The model is part of the Any-view Model series, has 0.08B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. The model is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a Vision Transformer for Visual Geometry with a plain transformer architecture. It is trained using public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to avoid complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: Can you determine the transformation between two photos of a scene for stitching?
description: LightGlue is a neural network designed to match two sets of local features by jointly finding correspondences and rejecting non-matchable points. It is a variant of the LightGlue model trained on DISK and is available under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license. The model requires the `kornia` library to be installed and is usable with the Transformers library. LightGlue was proposed by Philipp Lindenberger, Paul-Edouard Sarlin, and Marc Pollefeys. It is useful for tasks such as image matching, homography estimation, and pose estimation between images. The model is adaptive to the difficulty of the problem, with faster inference on image pairs that are easier to match. It is more efficient in terms of memory and computation, more accurate, and easier to train than the previous state-of-the-art model, SuperGlue. LightGlue can introspect the confidence of its predictions and adapts its depth and width during inference. It can stop at an early layer if all predictions are ready and discards non-matchable points early in the process. The model is developed by ETH Zurich - Computer Vision and Geometry Lab and is designed for feature matching and pose estimation tasks in computer vision. It can be applied to a variety of multiple-view geometry problems and can handle challenging real-world indoor and outdoor environments. However, it may not perform well on tasks that require different types of visual understanding, such as object detection or image classification. LightGlue is trained on large annotated datasets for pose estimation, including image pairs with ground truth correspondences and unmatched keypoints derived from ground truth poses and depth maps. It is first pre-trained with synthetic homographies sampled from 1 million images and then fine-tuned with the MegaDepth dataset, which includes 1 million crowd-sourced images of 196 tourism landmarks. The model is trained using a supervised setup and runs in real-time on a modern GPU, with a forward pass taking approximately 44 milliseconds (22 FPS) for an image pair. It has 13.7 million parameters and is suitable for integration into modern Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) or Structure-from-Motion (SfM) systems.

instruction: I want to know the key events in this video—can you summarize them for me?
description: Kwai Keye-VL is an 8-billion-parameter multimodal foundation model designed for leading-edge performance in short-video understanding while maintaining robust general-purpose vision-language abilities. It is engineered to bridge the gap between static image comprehension and dynamic, information-dense short-form videos, which are a dominant medium in today's digital landscape. The model is built on a massive, high-quality dataset exceeding 600 billion tokens with a strong emphasis on video, and an innovative training recipe. This recipe includes a four-stage pre-training process for solid vision-language alignment, followed by a meticulous two-phase post-training process. The first post-training stage enhances foundational capabilities like instruction following, while the second phase focuses on stimulating advanced reasoning. A key innovation in the second phase is the five-mode ``cold-start'' data mixture, which includes ``thinking'', ``non-thinking'', ``auto-think'', ``think with image'', and high-quality video data. This mixture teaches the model to decide when and how to reason. Subsequent reinforcement learning (RL) and alignment steps further enhance these reasoning capabilities and correct abnormal model behaviors, such as repetitive outputs. Kwai Keye-VL achieves state-of-the-art results on public video benchmarks and remains highly competitive on general image-based tasks. Additionally, it shows a significant advantage on the newly developed KC-MMBench, a benchmark tailored for real-world short-video scenarios. The model architecture is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and incorporates a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer then maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information, establishing a one-to-one correspondence between position encoding and absolute time to ensure precise perception of temporal changes in video information. The pre-training pipeline features a four-stage progressive strategy: Image-Text Matching, ViT-LLM Alignment, Multi-task Pre-training, and Annealing with model merging. The pre-training data is diverse, including image-text pairs, videos, pure text, and tasks such as fine-grained description, OCR, Q&A, and localization. The data is filtered using CLIP scores and VLM discriminators, and MinHASH is used for deduplication to prevent data leakage. High-quality internal datasets are specifically constructed, especially for detailed captions and Chinese OCR, to compensate for the shortcomings of open-source data. The training process includes four stages: Visual Pre-training, Cross-Modal Alignment, Multi-Task Pre-training, and Annealing Training. The post-training phase is designed into two phases with five stages, aiming to comprehensively enhance the model's performance, especially its reasoning ability in complex tasks. The first phase focuses on the model's basic performance and stability in non-reasoning scenarios, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Mixed Preference Optimization (MPO). The second phase is the highlight and major contribution of the training process, introducing a mix-mode Chain of Thought (CoT) and multi-thinking mode reinforcement learning (RL) mechanisms to significantly enhance the model's multimodal perception, reasoning, and think-with-image capabilities. The model is evaluated on a comprehensive suite of authoritative public video benchmarks, including Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU, where it significantly surpasses other top-tier models of a comparable size. It also demonstrates strong performance in evaluation sets that require complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: Can you identify and highlight all the objects in this image based on the description I provide?
description: This tiny model is intended for debugging purposes. It is randomly initialized using a configuration adapted from a source model. The model is designed to perform instance segmentation tasks, supporting inputs such as images, text prompts, and bounding box annotations. It includes a vision encoder, a text encoder, a geometry encoder, a DETR encoder, a DETR decoder, and a mask decoder. The vision encoder processes images through a vision transformer with a patch embedding layer, multiple transformer layers, and a feature pyramid network. The text encoder is based on a CLIP text model with projection layers. The geometry encoder processes box and label information with attention and pooling operations. The DETR encoder and decoder handle cross-modal interactions between vision and text, while the mask decoder generates segmentation masks. The model supports negative box annotations to exclude specific regions from segmentation. The architecture includes multiple attention mechanisms, layer normalization, and feed-forward networks. The model is randomly initialized and not trained on any specific dataset, and as such, it is not intended for production use or real-world applications.

instruction: How would it sound if a young woman whispered a secret with a hint of nervousness?
description: Maya1 is a state-of-the-art speech model for expressive voice generation, designed to capture real human emotion and precise voice design. It allows users to create any voice they can imagine, including specific age, gender, accent, and character traits, and to add emotion tags such as laugh, cry, whisper, rage, sigh, and gasp to make the output feel more realistic. The model streams audio instantly, runs on a single GPU, and has 3B parameters. It outperforms top proprietary models and is developed by Maya Research. Maya1 uses a 3B-parameter decoder-only transformer (Llama-style) to predict SNAC neural codec tokens, enabling real-time streaming at ~0.98 kbps. It is trained on an internet-scale English speech corpus and a curated dataset of studio recordings with human-verified voice descriptions, 20+ emotion tags, multi-accent English coverage, and character variations. The model supports natural language voice control, allowing users to describe voices in plain text without complex parameters or training data. It also supports inline emotion tags for expressive speech, enabling users to add emotions directly into the text. Maya1 is suitable for a variety of use cases, including game character voices, podcast and audiobook production, AI voice assistants, video content creation, customer service AI, and accessibility tools. It is open source under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing commercial use, deployment, and customization. The model can be fine-tuned on custom datasets for specialized voices and is compatible with vLLM for production-scale deployment. It generates 24 kHz audio output and is web audio compatible for browser playback. Maya1 is production-ready and was released in November 2025.

description: jina-reranker-v3-GGUF is a 0.6B parameter multilingual listwise reranker designed for efficient inference, using GGUF quantizations with llama.cpp. It is intended to rerank documents based on their relevance to a given query. The model supports reranking tasks and includes a rerank function that accepts a query, a list of documents, and optional parameters such as the number of top results to return and whether to include embeddings in the output. The model architecture includes quantized model weights in BF16 format and an MLP projector for additional processing. The model is implemented in Python and requires Python 3.8+, llama.cpp binaries, and specific dependencies such as numpy and safetensors. The model is available under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license, and commercial usage requires separate inquiries. The model is described in a research paper titled 'jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking' by Feng Wang, Yuqing Li, and Han Xiao, available on arXiv.

description: This model was trained from scratch on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 3.7004 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the ADAMW_TORCH optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a cosine learning rate scheduler, 10 training epochs, and native AMP for mixed precision training. description: sap-rpt-1-oss is a deep learning model designed for tabular in-context learning (ICL) that combines semantic understanding and alignment within a table-native ICL framework. The model is intended to perform classification and regression tasks on tabular data. It accepts input in the form of a pandas DataFrame or a NumPy array, with no preprocessing required. Column names and cell values are automatically embedded using a background large language model, and missing values are handled appropriately. The model is trained on large-scale real-world tabular data and is competitive with state-of-the-art models across a broad set of benchmarks, particularly excelling on the semantically rich CARTE benchmark. It employs specialized embeddings for different data modalities to better leverage the semantics and world knowledge in real-world data. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use a GPU with at least 80 GB of memory, a context size of 8192, and a bagging factor of 8. For lower resource requirements, the context size can be reduced to 2048 and the bagging factor to 1. The model checkpoints are available for download from Hugging Face and require login access. The model is licensed under the Apache Software License, version 2.0, and is intended for research purposes only. The model checkpoints inherit restrictions from the training data, which is a subset of another dataset. The model was previously known as ConTextTab, but the code and repository have been updated to reflect the new name, while the model checkpoint and functionality remain unchanged.

instruction: I want to convert this audio message into text so I can read it later. Can you do that for me?
description: NVIDIA NeMo Canary-Qwen-2.5B is an English speech recognition model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple English speech benchmarks. With 2.5 billion parameters and running at 418 RTFx, Canary-Qwen-2.5B supports automatic speech-to-text recognition (ASR) in English with punctuation and capitalization (PnC). The model works in two modes: as a transcription tool (ASR mode) and as an LLM (LLM mode). In ASR mode, the model is only capable of transcribing the speech into text, but does not retain any LLM-specific skills such as reasoning. In LLM mode, the model retains all of the original LLM capabilities, which can be used to post-process the transcript, e.g. summarize it or answer questions about it. In LLM mode, the model does not 'understand' the raw audio anymore - only its transcript. This model is ready for commercial use. Canary-Qwen is a Speech-Augmented Language Model (SALM) with FastConformer Encoder and Transformer Decoder. It is built using two base models: `nvidia/canary-1b-flash` and `Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B`, a linear projection, and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) applied to the LLM. The audio encoder computes audio representation that is mapped to the LLM embedding space via a linear projection, and concatenated with the embeddings of text tokens. The model is prompted with 'Transcribe the following: ', using Qwen's chat template. The model is intended for users requiring speech-to-text transcription capabilities for English speech, and/or transcript post-processing capabilities enabled by prompting the underlying LLMs. Typical use-cases: transcription, summarization, answering user questions about the transcript. The model was trained using the NVIDIA NeMo toolkit for a total of 90k steps on 32 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. LLM parameters were kept frozen. Speech encoder, projection, and LoRA parameters were trainable. The encoder's output frame rate is 80ms, or 12.5 tokens per second. The model was trained on approximately 1.3B tokens in total (this number includes the speech encoder output frames, text response tokens, prompt tokens, and chat template tokens). The tokenizer was inherited from `Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B`. The model can be trained using this and . The model is available for use in the NVIDIA NeMo toolkit, and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or for fine-tuning on another dataset. Input to Canary-Qwen-2.5B is a batch of prompts that include audio. Input Type(s): Audio, text prompt. Input Format(s): Audio: .wav or .flac files. Text prompt string for ASR mode: 'Transcribe the following: '. Input Parameters(s): Audio: Two-Dimensional (batch, audio-samples); Text: One-Dimensional (string). Other Properties Related to Input: 16000 Hz Mono-channel Audio, Pre-Processing Not Needed. Output Type(s): Text. Output Format: Text transcript as a sequence of token IDs or a string. Output Parameters: One-Dimensional text string. Other Properties Related to Output: May Need Inverse Text Normalization. Our AI models are designed and/or optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems. By leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware (e.g. GPU cores) and software frameworks (e.g., CUDA libraries), the model achieves faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. Runtime Engine(s): NeMo - 2.5.0 or higher. Supported Hardware Microarchitecture Compatibility: NVIDIA Ampere, NVIDIA Blackwell, NVIDIA Jetson, NVIDIA Hopper, NVIDIA Lovelace, NVIDIA Pascal, NVIDIA Turing, NVIDIA Volta. Preferred/Supported Operating System(s): Linux, Linux 4 Tegra, Windows. Model Version(s): Canary-Qwen-2.5B. Canary-Qwen-2.5B was trained on a total of 234K hrs of publicly available speech data. The datasets include conversations, videos from the web and audiobook recordings. The majority of the training data comes from the English portion of the Granary dataset: YouTube-Commons (109.5k hours), YODAS2 (77k hours), LibriLight (13.6k hours). In addition, the following datasets were used: Librispeech 960 hours, Fisher Corpus, Switchboard-1 Dataset, WSJ-0 and WSJ-1, National Speech Corpus (Part 1, Part 6), VCTK, VoxPopuli (EN), Europarl-ASR (EN), Multilingual Librispeech (MLS EN), Mozilla Common Voice (v11.0), Mozilla Common Voice (v7.0), Mozilla Common Voice (v4.0), AMI, FLEURS. AMI was oversampled during model training to constitute about 15% of the total data observed. This skewed the model towards predicting verbatim transcripts that include conversational speech disfluencies such as repetitions. The training transcripts contained punctuation and capitalization. The ASR performance is measured with word error rate (WER), and we process the groundtruth and predicted text with version 0.1.12. WER on: Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, RTFx 418, Mean 5.63, AMI 10.18, GigaSpeech 9.41, LS Clean 1.60, LS Other 3.10, Earnings22 10.42, SPGISpech 1.90, Tedlium 2.72, Voxpopuli 5.66. The ASR predictions were generated using greedy decoding. Number of characters per minute on 48 hrs eval set (`max_new_tokens=50` following `nvidia/canary-1b-flash` evaluation): Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, 138.1. WER on at different SNR (signal to noise ratio) levels of additive white noise: Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, SNR 10 2.41%, SNR 5 4.08%, SNR 0 9.83%, SNR -5 30.60%. As outlined in the paper 'Towards Measuring Fairness in AI: the Casual Conversations Dataset', we assessed the Canary-Qwen-2.5B model for fairness. The model was evaluated on the CasualConversations-v1 dataset with inference done on non-overlapping 40s chunks, and the results are reported as follows: Gender Bias: Gender Male, Female, N/A, Other. Num utterances 18471, 23378, 880, 18. % WER 16.71, 13.85, 17.71, 29.46. Age Bias: Age Group (18-30), (31-45), (46-85), (1-100). Num utterances 15058, 13984, 12810, 41852. % WER 15.73, 15.3, 14.14, 15.11. (Error rates for fairness evaluation are determined by normalizing both the reference and predicted text, similar to the methods used in the evaluations found at ). The model has the following limitations: Input length. The maximum audio duration in training was 40s, and the maximum token sequence length was 1024 tokens (including prompt, audio, and response). The model may technically be able to process longer sequences, but its accuracy may be degraded. Exclusively ASR oriented capabilities. The model is not expected to preserve any of the underlying LLM's capabilities into speech modality. English-only language support. The model was trained using English data only. It may be able to spuriously transcribe other languages as the underlying encoder was pretrained using German, French, and Spanish speech in addition to English, but it's unlikely to be reliable as a multilingual model. instruction: Show me a video of a digital clock changing from midnight to noon in a stylish, animated way.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) generation. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 is trained on significantly larger data, with 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The model includes a 5B version with a 16×16×4 compression ratio, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and is capable of running on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at 480P and 720P resolutions. It delivers outstanding video generation quality and surpasses leading commercial models on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.
