instruction: Is there a way to extract the person in this image for a transparent background?
description: Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation is a model designed for image matting tasks, specifically for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation. The model is intended to predict alpha matte outputs from input images, enabling the separation of foreground and background elements. It is based on the BiRefNet architecture, which incorporates bilateral reference mechanisms to enhance segmentation accuracy. The model is optimized for use in web environments and is provided in ONNX format to facilitate deployment with WebML technologies. The model has been developed by researchers from Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and the University of Trento. The model is available for use with the Transformers.js library, where it can be loaded, processed, and used to generate output masks from input images. The model's output is a mask that can be saved as an image file. The model is intended to be used as a temporary solution until WebML becomes more widely adopted. Users are encouraged to convert their models to ONNX format and structure their repositories similarly for web deployment.

instruction: Show me an anime-style video of a character cooking a meal in a cozy kitchen.
description: Wan2.2-Anime-Unlimited (v1.0 Stable Toy) is the first truly open-source, unrestricted anime video generation model. It is based on the Wan2.2-5B architecture and can run on 6GB VRAM. The model is uncensored and designed to generate anime-style video content. The v1.0 version is described as a 'Qualified Toy' and is fully stable, serving as a proof of concept that a 5B model, when fine-tuned with 531 high-quality polished clips, can produce impressive anime content. It specializes in character interactions and basic movements. The model is currently in a stable state and is intended to be a fun and usable tool for generating anime videos. The development roadmap includes future versions such as v2.x, which will focus on battle scenes and slice-of-life content with support for 8s and 10s video generation, and v3.0, which will expand the range of anime concepts and art styles. The v5.0 to v6.0 versions will introduce a new audio model, transforming the system into a multimodal audio-video model with sound alignment and unrestricted creativity. The model is being developed with the support of the open-source community and is intended to surpass the performance of the 27B base model in the anime domain. The model is currently being trained on 4,000+ high-quality videos for the v2.x series, and further development depends on community support and funding.

instruction: Visualize a library with floating books and magical lights swirling in the air.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family is the fastest image generation model to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture. It delivers state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Designed for real-time image generation without sacrificing quality, it runs on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is fully open under Apache 2.0 and can run on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090/4070. The model supports text-to-image, image editing, and multi-reference editing at quality that exceeds its size. It is suitable for local development, edge deployment, and production use. The model is available via the BFL API and can be used with the Diffusers Python library. Limitations include the inability to provide factual information, potential inaccuracies in rendered text, possible amplification of biases from training data, and the possibility of failing to generate output that matches prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate deceptive or harmful content, to generate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm individuals, to harass or abuse individuals or groups, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high-risk applications that adversely impact legal rights. Black Forest Labs is committed to responsible development and has implemented pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material and nonconsensual intimate imagery. These include filtering pre-training data for NSFW and CSAM content, targeted fine-tuning to inhibit harmful behaviors, and ongoing internal and external evaluations. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs and were released under an Apache 2.0 license. The model includes filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs, and implements pixel-layer watermarking and C2PA metadata for content provenance. Acceptable use is governed by policies including the FLUX Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy. Black Forest Labs maintains a safety email address and reporting relationships with organizations to address emerging risks and develop mitigations.

instruction: I need to hear how this French sentence sounds when spoken aloud—could you generate an audio version?
description: Kyutai TTS is a streaming text-to-speech model designed to generate audio as soon as the first few words of the input text are provided, rather than requiring the full text to produce audio. The model has 1.8B parameters, despite the name suggesting 1.6B. It is a hierarchical Transformer architecture that processes tokenized text and generates audio tokenized by Mimi. The frame rate is 12.5 Hz, with each audio frame represented by 32 tokens, though fewer tokens can be used during inference for faster generation. The backbone model consists of 1B parameters, and the depth transformer has 600M parameters with partial weight sharing. The audio is shifted by 16 steps (1.28 seconds) relative to the text, and the model uses an acoustic/semantic delay of 2. Kyutai TTS is a decoder-only model that uses a multistream architecture to model the text stream based on the speech stream, with the text stream shifted relative to the audio stream to allow prediction of text tokens from input audio. It supports English and French languages and is licensed under CC-BY 4.0. The model can perform streaming text-to-speech generation, including for dialogues, and supports voice conditioning through cross-attention pre-computed embeddings provided in the repository. It does not support Classifier Free Guidance (CFG) directly but was trained using CFG distillation to improve speed without requiring a doubled batch size. The model is easy to batch and can achieve a throughput of 75x generated audio per compute unit of time. It does not perform watermarking, as watermarking can be easily deactivated in open source models and existing TTS watermark systems are removed by encoding and decoding the audio with Mimi. Instead, voice cloning is restricted to the use of pre-computed voice embeddings. The model was trained for 750k steps with a batch size of 64 and a segment duration of 120 seconds, followed by 24k updates of CFG distillation. The pretraining data consisted of 2.5 million hours of publicly available audio, with synthetic transcripts generated using `whisper-medium`. Pretraining was conducted using 32 H100 Nvidia GPUs, and CFG distillation was performed on 8 H100 Nvidia GPUs. The model was developed by Kyutai and authored by Neil Zeghidour, Eugene Kharitonov, Manu Orsini, Václav Volhejn, Gabriel de Marmiesse, Edouard Grave, Patrick Perez, Laurent Mazaré, and Alexandre Défossez.

instruction: What object is the focus of this image taken in a garden?
description: MobileNet V1 is a pre-trained model on ImageNet-1k at a resolution of 192x192. It was introduced by Howard et al and first released by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide a model card. MobileNets are designed to be small, low-latency, and low-power models that can be adapted for various use cases by adjusting their parameters to meet resource constraints. They support tasks such as classification, detection, embeddings, and segmentation, similar to other large-scale models like Inception. MobileNets are optimized to run efficiently on mobile devices and balance trade-offs between latency, size, and accuracy while performing favorably compared to other models in the literature. The model can be used for image classification, predicting one of 1001 classes, which includes the 1000 ImageNet classes plus an additional 'background' class. The model and its feature extractor are currently supported in PyTorch.

instruction: Please extract the most relevant visual attributes from this image for analysis.
description: This is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed for image feature extraction and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer architecture with a patch size of 16. The model has 21.6 million parameters, 6.3 GMACs, and 17.0 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for these models. Some model sizes have variants with `qkvb` in the name that have the bias enabled (`qkv_bias=True`), but set to zero, to match the behavior of `transformers` and original models. The original models keep RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization. This results in some numerical differences, but the `timm` approach is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well, if not slightly better, for fine-tuning. To match the original model outputs, the `model.rope.periods` can be converted to `bfloat16` and then to `float32`. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 87.0% on IN-ReaL, 60.4% on IN-R, 50.9% on Obj.Net, 49.5% on Ox.-H, 47.0% on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7% on DAVIS, 56.3% on NAVI, and 50.4% on SPair. The model is also evaluated on satellite datasets such as SAT-493M, with performance metrics including 73.0% on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and a mean of 79.6%. For segmentation tasks, the model achieves 94.2% on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and a mean of 74.5%. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper, as well as the papers on Vision Transformers for image recognition at scale and PyTorch Image Models.

instruction: Please convert this English voice recording into a Spanish text summary.
description: Canary-1b-v2 is a 1-billion parameter model designed for high-quality speech transcription and translation across 25 European languages. It supports automatic speech recognition (ASR) for 25 languages, speech translation (AST) from English to 24 languages, and speech translation from 24 languages to English. The model is built on an encoder-decoder architecture with a FastConformer Encoder and a Transformer Decoder, featuring 32 encoder layers and 8 decoder layers, totaling 978 million parameters. It uses a unified SentencePiece Tokenizer with a vocabulary of 16,384 tokens optimized across all 25 languages. Canary-1b-v2 is trained using a 3-stage procedure on a multilingual speech recognition and translation dataset combining Nvidia's Granary dataset and NeMo ASR Set 3.0, with a total of 1.7 million training hours. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance among models of similar size, with comparable quality to models three times larger while being up to 10 times faster. It provides automatic punctuation and capitalization, accurate word-level and segment-level timestamps, and segment-level timestamps for translated outputs. Evaluation results show a Word Error Rate (WER) of 8.40% on Fleurs-25 Langs, 8.85% on CoVoST-13 Langs, and 7.27% on MLS-6 Langs for ASR. For speech translation, it achieves a COMET score of 79.30 on Fleurs-24 Langs and 77.48 on CoVoST-13 Langs for X→English, and 84.56 on Fleurs-24 Langs and 80.29 on CoVoST-5 Langs for English→X. The model is robust to noise, with a WER of 2.18% at 100 dB SNR and 19.38% at -5 dB SNR. It also performs well on long-form transcription, achieving a WER of 13.78% on Earnings-22 and 9.87% on This American Life. Canary-1b-v2 is released under a permissive CC BY 4.0 license and is designed to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems for faster training and inference. It is intended for use by developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. The model is available for use in the NeMo toolkit and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or fine-tuning on another dataset. It supports input in 16kHz audio formats such as .wav and .flac, and outputs text with punctuation and capitalization included. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell microarchitectures and requires at least 6GB RAM for model loading. Canary-1b-v2 is the first model from the NeMo team to leverage the full Nvidia Granary dataset, showcasing its multitask and multilingual capabilities. The model is verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is evaluated using metrics such as Word Error Rate (WER), BLEU score, and COMET score. Technical limitations include potential inaccuracies in transcripts and translations, which may vary based on source and target language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy may vary based on context. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model is not known to generate or reverse engineer personal data, and all datasets used in training have provenance. Data labeling complies with privacy laws, but data correction or removal is not possible with externally-sourced data. The model is not intended for life-critical applications and use is restricted by the license. Model and dataset restrictions include the application of the Principle of least privilege and adherence to dataset license constraints.

instruction: Are there any known compatibility issues with older versions of llama.cpp?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the gemma-3n-E4B-it model by Google, using the llama.cpp release b5760 for quantization. The original model is quantized using the imatrix option with a dataset provided. The quantizations are intended to be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. No chat template is specified, so a default prompt format is used, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The quantizations vary in quality and file size, with options ranging from Q8_0 (7.35GB, extremely high quality) to IQ3_XS (3.17GB, lower quality). Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for embed and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, particularly for the Q4_0 and IQ4_NL quantizations, which can improve performance on compatible hardware. The Q4_0 quantization has been deprecated in favor of the Q4_0 format with online repacking. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system (EPYC7702) show that the Q4_K_M quantization offers improved tokens per second compared to Q4_0 in most cases, while the Q4_0_8_8 quantization provides a performance boost for prompt processing and text generation. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, with recommendations for maximizing speed or quality based on RAM and VRAM availability. I-quants (e.g., IQ3_M) are newer and offer better performance for their size on cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD) systems, though they may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants (e.g., Q5_K_M). The model includes contributions from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, ZeroWw for inspiration on embed/output quantization, and LM Studio for sponsorship.

instruction: What are the key takeaways from this 10-minute English customer support call?
description: Voxtral-Small-24B-2507-FP8-dynamic is a model based on the VoxtralForConditionalGeneration architecture, designed to process audio-text inputs and generate text outputs. It is optimized through FP8 quantization for both weights and activations, reducing GPU memory requirements by approximately 50% and increasing matrix-multiply compute throughput by approximately 2x. The model is intended for audio understanding tasks, including transcription, question answering, summarization, and function calling based on spoken input. It supports a pure speech transcription mode with automatic language detection and transcribes audio in multiple languages, including English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Hindi, German, Dutch, and Italian. Voxtral can handle audios up to 30 minutes for transcription or 40 minutes for understanding, with a 32k token context length. It retains the text understanding capabilities of its language model backbone, Ministral-3B. The model was evaluated on the Fleurs transcription task, with performance measured by word error rate (WER) recovery. For English, the WER is 3.43% with 100.0% recovery; for French, 3.96% with 100.0% recovery; for Spanish, 2.84% with 100.1% recovery; for German, 3.36% with 100.1% recovery; for Italian, 2.54% with 99.7% recovery; for Portuguese, 3.57% with 100.0% recovery; and for Dutch, 5.29% with 100.1% recovery. The model was developed by Red Hat and released on 08/21/2025, with version 1.0. It was created by quantizing the MLP operators within transformer blocks of the language model using a symmetric static per-channel scheme for weights and a symmetric dynamic per-token scheme for activations. The model can be deployed using the vLLM framework and supports both transcription and audio instruct use cases.

instruction: Can you provide a detailed segmentation of the person’s lower body in this picture?
description: The FASHN Human Parser is a SegFormer-B4 model fine-tuned for human parsing with 18 semantic classes, optimized for fashion and virtual try-on applications. It segments human images into 18 semantic categories, including body parts such as face, hair, arms, hands, legs, feet, and torso; clothing items such as top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, and scarf; and accessories such as bag, hat, glasses, and jewelry. The model uses a SegFormer-B4 architecture, which includes an MIT-B4 encoder and an MLP decoder. It accepts input images of size 384 x 576 and produces 18-class semantic segmentation masks. The model was fine-tuned on a proprietary dataset curated and annotated by FASHN AI, specifically designed for virtual try-on applications. The 18-class label schema was developed to capture the semantic regions most relevant for clothing transfer and human body understanding in fashion contexts. The model is optimized for single-person images with clear visibility and performs best on fashion or e-commerce style photography. Input images are resized to 384x576, and very small subjects may lose detail. The label definitions include background, face, hair, top, dress, skirt, pants, belt, bag, hat, scarf, glasses, arms, hands, legs, feet, torso, and jewelry. For virtual try-on applications, the categories are mapped as follows: Tops (upper body) include top, dress, and scarf; Bottoms (lower body) include skirt, pants, and belt; and One-pieces (full body) include top, dress, scarf, skirt, pants, and belt. Labels typically preserved during virtual try-on include face, hair, jewelry, bag, glasses, and hat.

instruction: Can the RealRobot be integrated with other smart home devices?
description: The RealRobot_chatbot_llm is a highly optimized GGUF (quantized) version of a language model developed by fibonacciai. It is built on the Gemma 3n architecture and fine-tuned on a proprietary dataset from the RealRobot product catalog. The model is designed to serve as a proof-of-concept for a scalable business model that enables the rapid creation of accurate, cost-effective, and deployable specialized language models for any business based on their own product data. The model is trained exclusively on product data, eliminating the noise and inaccuracy of general-purpose models, and provides authoritative, relevant answers directly related to the RealRobot product line. The GGUF format allows for seamless integration into embedded systems, mobile applications, and local servers using tools like llama.cpp. The model is based on the Gemma 3n architecture, which is optimized for size and speed, with a model size of approximately 4 billion parameters in quantized form. It is fine-tuned from the gemma-3n-e2b-it-bnb-4bit base. The training data is derived from the RealRobot website product documentation and support materials, ensuring the chatbot can accurately answer customer questions about product features, usage, and troubleshooting. The model is intended for use in customer support and product-related inquiries. It is highly specialized and will have limited performance on general knowledge outside the RealRobot domain. Output should be verified by human oversight before use in critical processes. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Based on this image, can you infer the time of day and the location?
description: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that can operate PC/mobile GUIs, recognize elements, understand functions, and invoke tools to complete tasks. It supports visual coding by generating Draw.io/HTML/CSS/JS from images and videos. The model features advanced spatial perception, capable of judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with support for 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a long context and video understanding capability, with a native 256K context expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It has upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling it to recognize a wide range of subjects including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model also features expanded OCR, supporting 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare/ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. The model's text understanding is on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for deployment using the vLLM framework with a recommended context length of 32768. It is available for download and can be run with a vLLM startup command that specifies GPU memory utilization, tensor parallel size, and other parameters. The model file size is 17GB, last updated on 2025-10-04. The model can be used for chat with the Transformers library, requiring specific installation and setup steps. The model is described in technical reports and academic articles published in 2023, 2024, and 2025.

instruction: Show me a 3D version of a rare artifact from a fictional world, including its unique engravings and materials.
description: MV-RAG is a text-to-3D generation method that retrieves 2D reference images to guide a multiview diffusion model. By conditioning on both text and multiple real-world 2D images, MV-RAG improves realism and consistency for rare or out-of-distribution objects. The model is designed to produce 3D-consistent outputs by first retrieving relevant 2D images from a large in-the-wild 2D database and then conditioning a multiview diffusion model on these images to synthesize consistent and accurate multiview outputs. Training is achieved via a hybrid strategy that bridges structured multiview data and diverse 2D image collections. This involves training on multiview data using augmented conditioning views that simulate retrieval variance for view-specific reconstruction, alongside training on sets of retrieved real-world 2D images using a held-out view prediction objective, where the model predicts the held-out view from the other views to infer 3D consistency from 2D data. To facilitate a rigorous out-of-distribution evaluation, a new collection of challenging out-of-distribution prompts has been introduced. Experiments show that the approach significantly improves 3D consistency, photorealism, and text adherence for out-of-distribution or rare concepts, while maintaining competitive performance on standard benchmarks.

instruction: I want to create a 3D model of this futuristic cityscape — can you generate it from this image?
description: Stable Fast 3D (SF3D) is a large reconstruction model that takes a single image of an object and generates a textured UV-unwrapped 3D mesh asset. The model is a Transformer-based image-to-3D model designed to create a 3D model from a single image in under one second. The output asset is UV-unwrapped, textured, and has a relatively low polygon count. The model performs a delighting step, which facilitates easier use of the asset in downstream applications such as game engines or rendering work. It also predicts per-object material parameters, including roughness and metallic, which enhances reflective behaviors during rendering. The model expects an input size of 512x512 pixels. The model is trained using renders from a dataset available under the Open Data Commons Attribution License. An enhanced rendering method is used, which more closely replicates the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving the model's ability to generalize. Objects are filtered based on the review of licenses, and a subset is curated to meet training needs. Intended uses include the generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, applications in educational or creative tools, and research on reconstruction models, including understanding the limitations of these models. The model was not trained to produce factual or true representations of people or events, and generating such content is out of scope for the model's capabilities. Individuals or organizations generating annual revenue of US $1,000,000 (or local currency equivalent) or more must obtain an enterprise commercial license directly from Stability AI before commercially using SF3D or any derivative work of SF3D or its outputs. Community License allows free use for research, non-commercial, and commercial use by organizations and individuals generating annual revenue of US $1M or less. Developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on their specific use cases, as the model includes safety measures intended to reduce the risk of severe harms.

instruction: Produce a short video showing a dancer performing a contemporary routine in a modern art gallery.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 includes a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, with each expert having about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active parameters per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. Wan2.2 also features a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling efficient deployment. The TI2V-5B model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available, suitable for both industrial and academic applications. The model supports single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses, and is compatible with the Diffusers library. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions.

instruction: Can you detect and separate the text from the background in this image?
description: This tiny model is intended for debugging purposes. It is randomly initialized using a configuration adapted from a source model. The model is designed to perform instance segmentation tasks, supporting functions such as text-based object detection and exclusion of specific regions using input boxes. The architecture includes a vision encoder with a vision transformer backbone, a text encoder based on CLIP, a geometry encoder, a DETR encoder and decoder, and a mask decoder. The vision encoder processes images through a series of transformer layers and a feature pyramid network. The text encoder processes text inputs through a transformer-based architecture. The geometry encoder handles box and label embeddings, while the DETR components manage object detection and cross-attention between text and vision features. The mask decoder generates segmentation masks and includes components for pixel decoding and mask embedding. The model is configured with reduced hidden sizes, intermediate sizes, and attention heads for a lightweight version. It is explicitly noted that this model is not intended for production use and is provided for debugging and development purposes only.

instruction: Identify the main elements in this image and explain their relationship.
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. Molmo2 models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, a collection of datasets with highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. It has state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models with a similar size. Molmo2-4B is based on a vision backbone and outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science. The Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2 (training code, evaluations, intermediate checkpoints) will be made available at a later date, furthering the commitment to open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. The model is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Users should review the sources to determine if the model is appropriate for their use case. The Molmo2-4B model achieves an average score of 62.8 on 15 academic benchmarks.

instruction: How does the system handle outliers in income data, like unusually high or low values?
description: This model is a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) that uses Categorical Embeddings to classify income levels as either greater than 50K or less than or equal to 50K based on census data. It is a more complex and robust approach compared to models that use standard one-hot encoding. The architecture includes specialized Embedding layers for categorical features and has 13.1k parameters. It contains two hidden layers with dimensions 128 and 64, both using ReLU activation. A key feature of the model is its use of dense representations (embeddings) for categorical variables instead of one-hot encoding. The preprocessing pipeline includes Log-transformation for numerical stability and Standard Scaling. The model achieves an accuracy of 0.806 and an F1-Score of 0.768. While slightly more conservative in raw scores compared to base models, it demonstrates excellent generalization. The Embedding architecture is noted for its training stability, as the evaluation loss curves show that the model remains flatter and more controlled compared to base models, which tend to overfit more aggressively.

instruction: I want to generate an image of a person in a yoga pose—how can I get a detailed version from a skeleton outline?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into diffusers format and can be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model that supports additional input conditions. It is primarily used with openpose images, which represent human poses as a set of keypoints or skeletons. The model is trained to learn task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with small training datasets. The model supports a variety of conditions, including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, and image shuffling. Improvements in Openpose 1.1 include a more accurate implementation of OpenPose, especially for hands, and support for more inputs such as hand and face. The training dataset for the previous version had several issues, including duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality or blurry images with JPEG artifacts, and incorrect paired prompts due to data processing errors. These issues have been resolved in the new model, making it more reliable in many cases.

instruction: How does the setting change over the course of the video?
description: The SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT_RL from allenai is a 4B-parameter reinforcement learning (RL) post-trained vision-language model, further refined from the SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT base via RL on top of Qwen/Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. It serves as the core decision-maker in the SAGE (Smart Any-Horizon Agent) system for long video reasoning with improved performance, achieving a +4.1% improvement over SFT according to experiments. The model operates in two stages: Stage-1 analyzes initial frames and metadata to classify queries as single-turn or multi-turn, while Stage-2 iteratively invokes JSON-formatted tools such as web-search, transcribe-speech (ASR), ground-event (temporal localization), extract-video-parts (frames/subclips), and analyze for dynamic context updates until resolution. It is designed for extended video Q&A beyond fixed horizons, handling sports, narratives, and events. The model requires the SAGE GitHub runtime for tool parsing and execution and outputs action strings for robust, any-length video understanding. It is available under the Apache 2.0 license for research and educational use per Ai2 guidelines, with GGUF quantizations available.

instruction: Can you generate a CIFAR10-quality image of a frog and ensure it looks natural?
description: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) are a class of latent variable models inspired by considerations from nonequilibrium thermodynamics. These models are designed for high-quality image synthesis. The training process is based on a weighted variational bound derived from a novel connection between diffusion probabilistic models and denoising score matching with Langevin dynamics. The models naturally support a progressive lossy decompression scheme that can be interpreted as a generalization of autoregressive decoding. On the unconditional CIFAR10 dataset, the model achieves an Inception score of 9.46 and a state-of-the-art FID score of 3.17. On 256x256 LSUN, the sample quality is comparable to that of ProgressiveGAN. For inference, DDPM models can use discrete noise schedulers such as ddpm, ddim, or pndm. While the ddpm scheduler provides the highest quality, it is also the slowest. The ddim or pndm schedulers offer a better trade-off between quality and inference speed. The model can be loaded using the DDPMPipeline, DDIMPipeline, or PNDMPipeline from the diffusers library. A generated image can be saved as a file for later use.

instruction: Can you locate the part of the video where the scientist discusses renewable energy?
description: The SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT from allenai is a 4B-parameter vision-language model fine-tuned from Qwen/Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. It serves as the core decision-maker in the SAGE (Smart Any-Horizon Agent) system for long video reasoning. The model operates in two stages: Stage-1 analyzes initial sampled frames and metadata to classify queries as either single-turn (immediate answer) or multi-turn (tool-required). Stage-2 iteratively calls tools, processes observations, and refines context until resolution. The model generates JSON-formatted actions for specialized tools, including web-search for external knowledge, transcribe-speech for automatic speech recognition on timestamps, ground-event for temporal localization, extract-video-parts for high-resolution frames or subclips, and analyze for detailed visual breakdown. This enables robust handling of extended videos beyond fixed horizons through dynamic tool orchestration. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license for research and educational use, in accordance with Ai2 guidelines. It requires the SAGE GitHub runtime for tool execution and parsing and supports GGUF quantizations for efficient deployment. The model excels in video question-answering tasks across domains such as sports, narratives, and events.

instruction: What are the hardware requirements for running this system on a local machine?
description: Gemma 3n E4B IT is a fine-tuned version of the Gemma 3n model, produced using a modified abliteration engine. It has the best abliteration scores and can be used without a specialized system prompt for removing censorship. Use it with extreme caution and care. This repository contains static quants, while the quants have been moved to a separate repository and regenerated with a larger multilingual dataset. Vision support has been merged into llama.cpp, requiring a git pull and rebuild. Abliteration scores include 11 refusals out of 100 and a KL divergence of 0.1422. The model has 26 abliterated layers, 9 skipped layers, and modifications to 8 attn.o_proj matrices and 26 mlp.down_proj matrices. Gemma 3n models are a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. They are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and can handle multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, generating text outputs. These models were trained with data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters. The models support input in the form of text strings, images normalized to specific resolutions, audio data encoded at a specific rate, and a total input context of 32K tokens. Outputs include generated text in response to the input, with a total output length up to 32K tokens. The models were trained on a dataset of approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to exclude harmful and sensitive content. Gemma was trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. The models were evaluated on a range of benchmarks, showing strong performance in reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional categories. Ethics and safety evaluations were conducted, covering child safety, content safety, and representational harms. The models have limitations related to training data, context and task complexity, language ambiguity, factual accuracy, and common sense. Ethical considerations include bias and fairness, misinformation and misuse, transparency and accountability, and privacy. The models are intended for use in content creation, research, and education, with guidelines for responsible use provided.

instruction: What is the most prominent object in this video, and where is it located?
description: Molmo2 is a family of open vision-language models developed by the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) that support image, video, and multi-image understanding and grounding. The models are trained on publicly available third-party datasets, which include highly-curated image-text and video-text pairs. Molmo2 models demonstrate state-of-the-art performance among multimodal models of similar size. The Molmo2-O-7B variant is based on a specific architecture and uses a specific vision backbone. It outperforms other open weight and data models on short videos, counting, and captioning, and is competitive on long-videos. Ai2 is committed to open science, and the Molmo2 datasets are available. All other artifacts used in creating Molmo2, including training code, evaluations, and intermediate checkpoints, will be made available at a later date to support open-source AI development and reproducibility. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2’s policies. It is trained on third-party datasets that are subject to academic and non-commercial research use only. Users should review the sources to determine if the model is appropriate for their use case. Evaluations show that Molmo2-O-7B achieves an average score of 59.7 on 15 academic benchmarks, compared to other models such as GPT-5 (70.6), Gemini 3 Pro (70.0), and others. The model supports tasks such as general video question answering and pointing video question answering, where it can generate text responses and extract pointing coordinates as a list of (frame_id, x, y) triplets from model output text.

instruction: ສະຫຼຸບບົດຄວາມນີ້ໃຫ້ສັ້ນແລະໃຫ້ຄໍາຄຳຖາມທີ່ສາມາດເຮັດໃຫ້ຜູ້ຝຶກການສຶກສາສຳຫຼວດເນື້ອໃນໄດ້ດີຂຶ້ນ
description: This is a Lao language summarization model fine-tuned on a dataset using the base model Gemma 3-12B Instruct. The model is designed to generate concise summaries from Lao language text. It is specifically adapted to understand and generate responses in Lao language. It was trained using a curated dataset of over 5,000 high-quality Lao input-output pairs, primarily focused on AIDC (Artificial Intelligence and Digital Content) topics. The model supports summarization, question answering, and general chat tasks. The fine-tuning was performed using LoRA with PEFT (Unsloth). The training dataset consists of 10,000 Laos supervised samples, with a sequence length of 2048 and a batch size of 2 (with gradient accumulation). The model was trained for 3–5 epochs with early stopping enabled. The model is available in GGUF format, with F16 and Q8_0 versions available. The model is based on Google's Gemma 3-12B Instruct model and is suitable for Lao language generation. The model uses the Apache 2.0 license. The evaluation metric used is the BLEU score, which assesses the quality of generated summaries against reference summaries in the dataset.

instruction: Please list the disease entities present in this paragraph from a scientific article.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for disease entity recognition, specifically for disease entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC5CDR_DISEASE dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 5,818 annotated disease entities. The model supports the identification and classification of two entity types: B-DISEASE and I-DISEASE. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem and is validated on clinical benchmarks for production-ready reliability. Performance metrics for the model include an F1 score of 0.87, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.88, and accuracy of 0.97. The model is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: I want to visualize this product design in three dimensions—can you help?
description: TripoSR is a fast and feed-forward 3D generative model developed in collaboration between Stability AI and Tripo AI. The model incorporates a series of technical advancements over the LRM model in terms of both data curation as well as model and training improvements. We closely follow the network architecture for the model design. The model type is feed-forward 3D reconstruction from a single image, and it is available under the MIT license. We train TripoSR for 5 days on 22 GPU nodes each with 8 A100 40GB GPUs. We use renders from the dataset, utilizing our enhanced rendering method that more closely replicate the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving our model’s ability to generalize. We selected a carefully curated subset of the Objaverse dataset for the training data, which is available under the CC-BY license. The model should not be used to intentionally create or disseminate 3D models that people would foreseeably find disturbing, distressing, or offensive; or content that propagates historical or current stereotypes.

instruction: Can you determine the emotional tone of the text 'This movie had me laughing from start to finish.'?
description: The Emotion English DistilRoBERTa-base is a model designed to classify emotions in English text data. It predicts seven emotion classes based on Ekman's 6 basic emotions plus a neutral class: anger, disgust, fear, joy, neutral, sadness, and surprise. The model was trained on a balanced subset of six diverse English text datasets, including data from Twitter, Reddit, student self-reports, and TV dialogues. These datasets collectively provided 2,811 observations per emotion, totaling nearly 20,000 observations. The model was trained on 80% of this data and evaluated on the remaining 20%, achieving an evaluation accuracy of 66%, which is significantly higher than the random-chance baseline of 14%. The model is a fine-tuned checkpoint of a base model, and for a non-distilled version, users are directed to the model card of the original version. The model is intended for use in applications requiring emotion classification, such as analyzing sentiment in social media, news, or other text sources. It can be applied to single text examples or larger datasets, including .csv files, using Hugging Face's pipeline command. The model has been cited in scientific research, including studies on rumor detection, music-to-text synesthesia, and longitudinal analysis of news media sentiment. Users are encouraged to cite the model in their work, with a working paper and BibTeX citation provided for reference. The model's development was supported by Samuel Domdey and others, and users are invited to contact the model's creator for questions or feedback.
