description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities by leveraging large-scale data and model sizes. The model includes a video classification head and is pretrained on the Something-Something-V2 dataset. V-JEPA 2 is designed to support video classification tasks and is intended for use with the latest version of the transformers library. The model can be loaded using the AutoModelForVideoClassification and AutoVideoProcessor classes from the transformers library. During inference, video frames are sampled according to the model's configuration, preprocessed, and passed through the model to obtain classification logits. The model outputs probabilities for the top predicted classes, enabling interpretation of the video content. V-JEPA 2 is part of ongoing research in self-supervised video modeling and is intended to enable understanding, prediction, and planning in video data.

instruction: Can you determine the general theme or context of this image?
description: The convnext_large.dinov3_lvd1689m is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model. It is designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. The model is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and is distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. It has 196.2 million parameters, 34.4 GMACs, and 43.1 million activations. The model processes images of size 224 x 224. It supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. The model is based on the ConvNeXt architecture described in the paper 'A ConvNet for the 2020s' and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library. The model is associated with the DINOv3 research, as detailed in the arXiv preprint 'DINOv3'.

instruction: Can you provide a depth analysis of this image for augmented reality integration?
description: Depth Anything is a deep convolutional neural network model designed for estimating depth at each point in an image. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended to be used on Qualcomm® devices. The model has an input resolution of 518x518 and contains 24.7 million parameters, with a model size of 94.3 MB in float precision. The model is available as a checkpoint named DepthAnything_Small. Performance metrics across various devices include inference times ranging from 174.976 ms to 73.28 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 - 393 MB to 1 - 13 MB, depending on the device and runtime. The primary compute unit used is the NPU. The model can be run on a variety of Qualcomm® devices, including QCS8275, QCS8450, QCS8550, QCS9075, SA7255P, SA8255, SA8295P, SA8650, SA8775P, and Snapdragon® devices such as the Samsung Galaxy S24 and S25, as well as the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 QRD and Snapdragon X Elite CRD. The model can be executed using different runtimes, including TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. The model is part of the Qualcomm® AI Hub and can be deployed on Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch, and performance profiling and accuracy checks can be conducted on cloud-hosted devices. A demo is available to run the model on sample input data, and the model can also be executed on-device using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench. The model is intended for use in on-device AI applications and is supported by a community for collaboration and feedback.

instruction: Could you identify the key turning point in this multi-part video?
description: The SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT_RL from allenai is a 4B-parameter reinforcement learning (RL) post-trained vision-language model, further refined from the SAGE-MM-Qwen3-VL-4B-SFT base via RL on top of Qwen/Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. It serves as the core decision-maker in the SAGE (Smart Any-Horizon Agent) system for long video reasoning with improved performance, achieving a +4.1% improvement over SFT according to experiments. The model operates in two stages: Stage-1 analyzes initial frames and metadata to classify queries as single-turn or multi-turn, while Stage-2 iteratively invokes JSON-formatted tools such as web-search, transcribe-speech (ASR), ground-event (temporal localization), extract-video-parts (frames/subclips), and analyze for dynamic context updates until resolution. It is designed for extended video Q&A beyond fixed horizons, handling sports, narratives, and events. The model requires the SAGE GitHub runtime for tool parsing and execution and outputs action strings for robust, any-length video understanding. It is available under the Apache 2.0 license for research and educational use per Ai2 guidelines, with GGUF quantizations available.

instruction: Make an anime portrait with a rainbow-colored hair gradient and wide, curious eyes.
description: This is a fine-tuned diffusion model for generating high-quality anime faces using a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). It is based on Google's pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model and was fine-tuned on 7,000+ high-quality anime face images. The model is designed for unconditional image generation of 256×256 anime faces. It uses the Diffusers framework and is licensed under the MIT license. Training parameters include a learning rate of 2e-5, 15 epochs, a batch size of 4, and gradient accumulation steps of 2, resulting in approximately 26,250 training steps. The AdamW optimizer and Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss were used during training. The final training loss was approximately 0.0077. Inference speed ranges from 30 to 60 seconds per image, depending on the number of inference steps. It is recommended to use 100 inference steps for the best quality. The model can generate a wide diversity of anime faces with various hair colors and styles, eye colors and expressions, face shapes and features, and skin tones. However, the model is limited to 256×256 resolution and is specifically trained on anime faces, so it may not generate realistic or photorealistic faces. The diversity of generated faces is limited to the patterns present in the training data, and the clarity of face shapes depends on the number of inference steps used. The model was fine-tuned from the pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model using a low learning rate to preserve general face generation knowledge while adapting to anime-specific features such as large eyes and stylized features. The training data was sourced from the Anime Face Dataset (Kaggle), consisting of 7,000 images selected by file size. The images were preprocessed by resizing to 256×256, applying random horizontal flips with 50% probability, and normalizing to the range [-1, 1]. During training, the model adapted from photorealistic to anime style in the first 3 epochs, solidified anime features in epochs 4 to 8, and showed marginal improvements with a risk of overfitting in later epochs. Ethical considerations include the model's use for generating synthetic anime faces, and it should not be used to create misleading or deceptive content, generate non-consensual images of real people, or violate any local laws or regulations. If used in research or projects, credit should be given to the original DDPM paper, Google's pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model, and this fine-tuned adaptation. Potential future improvements include higher resolution, conditional generation for text-to-image anime faces, better diversity through larger training datasets, and improved training with advanced schedulers or techniques.

instruction: How can I improve the quality of a photo that looks oversharpened and unnatural?
description: The model is a work in progress, with checkpoints being uploaded incrementally. The current best version uses LoRA A: qwen-edit-enhance_64-v3_000001000 and LoRA B: qwen-edit-enhance_000004250. The upcoming V4 candidates are LoRA A: /1000 and LoRA B: /4750. LoRA A and LoRA B are intended to be applied sequentially. The model is used in a Comfy workflow and includes a demonstration available on 🤗. The model is designed to enhance image quality and generate a detailed textual description of the scene, with more descriptive prompts leading to better upscale effects. All images shown are generated using an 8-step Lighting LoRA. The ModelSamplingAuraFlow is required, and the shift parameter must remain below 0.3; for higher resolutions, such as image 3, it can be set as low as 0.02. Recommended samplers include LCM (best), Euler_Ancestral, and Euler. All schedulers are compatible and produce varying results in terms of smoothness. The model was trained on a filtered subset of Unsplash-Lite and UltraHR-100K datasets at 2.4MP resolution. The training style focuses on photography, with subjects including landscapes, architecture, interiors, portraits, plants, vehicles, abstract photos, man-made objects, and food. The model is trained to recover from low-resolution images up to 16x, oversharpened images, noise up to 50%, Gaussian blur with a radius up to 3px, JPEG artifacts with quality as low as 5%, motion blur up to 64px, pixelation up to 16x, and color banding up to 3 bits. It is also trained to recover images that have been upscaled by other models up to 16x. The model is not trained on 2D images, illustrations, artworks, text, graphics, or CGI.

instruction: How would you approach debugging and optimizing a complex software system?
description: INTELLECT-3 is a 106B (A12B) parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) reasoning model post-trained using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by large-scale reinforcement learning (RL). Training was conducted using environments built with a specific library, and all training and evaluation environments are available on the Environments Hub. The model, training frameworks, and environments are open-sourced under fully-permissive licenses, including MIT and Apache 2.0. INTELLECT-3 achieves best-in-class performance on math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks, including 98.1 on MATH-500, 90.8 on AIME24, 88.0 on AIME25, 69.3 on LCB, 74.4 on GPQA, 14.6 on HLE, and 81.9 on MMLU-Pro. The BF16 version of the model can be served on two H200 GPUs using the vLLM framework with specific configuration parameters, while the FP8 version can be served on a single H200 GPU with similar settings. The model is designed to support reasoning tasks and is part of the Prime Intellect Platform. For more information, refer to the technical report and blog.

instruction: Can you generate a video of a car driving through a rainy city at night with detailed lighting effects?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video generation models, incorporating several key innovations to enhance generation quality and model capability. It introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into the video diffusion model, which separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models. This design increases the overall model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. The A14B model series in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for the early stages of denoising, focusing on overall layout, and a low-noise expert for the later stages, refining video details. Each expert has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters, but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined to switch from the high-noise to the low-noise expert. Validation results show that the MoE architecture in Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality compared to other configurations. In addition to the MoE models, Wan2.2 also includes a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, making it one of the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a unified framework. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data, including detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, allowing for more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing its generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 open-sources a 5B model that supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It also supports character animation and replacement, where the model can generate a video of a character image that mimics the human motion in an input video (animation mode) or replace the character image with the input video (replacement mode). Wan2.2 has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and its inference code and model weights have been released. It also supports text-to-speech synthesis for the Speech-to-Video generation task. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating harmful or illegal content.

instruction: Detect all the books on the table in this image.
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, making it more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is primarily intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model is designed to enable researchers to explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection and to support interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training.

instruction: Transcribe the speech in this audio file, making sure to preserve punctuation and formatting.
description: GigaAM-v3 is a Conformer-based foundation model with 220–240M parameters, pretrained on diverse Russian speech data using the HuBERT-CTC objective. It is the third generation of the GigaAM family and provides state-of-the-art performance on Russian ASR across a wide range of domains. GigaAM-v3 includes the following model variants: `ssl` — self-supervised HuBERT–CTC encoder pre-trained on 700,000 hours of Russian speech; `ctc` — ASR model fine-tuned with a CTC decoder; `rnnt` — ASR model fine-tuned with an RNN-T decoder; `e2e_ctc` — end-to-end CTC model with punctuation and text normalization; and `e2e_rnnt` — end-to-end RNN-T model with punctuation and text normalization. GigaAM-v3 training incorporates new internal datasets: callcenter conversations, speech with background music, natural speech, and speech with atypical characteristics. The models perform on average 30% better on these new domains, while maintaining the same quality as previous GigaAM generations on public benchmarks. The Word Error Rate (%) for GigaAM-v3 and other existing models over diverse domains is as follows: Open Datasets — V3_CTC 3.0, V3_RNNT 2.6, T-One + LM 5.7, Whisper 12.0; Golos Farfield — V3_CTC 4.5, V3_RNNT 3.9, T-One + LM 12.2, Whisper 16.7; Natural Speech — V3_CTC 7.8, V3_RNNT 6.9, T-One + LM 14.5, Whisper 13.6; Disordered Speech — V3_CTC 20.6, V3_RNNT 19.2, T-One + LM 51.0, Whisper 59.3; Callcenter — V3_CTC 10.3, V3_RNNT 9.5, T-One + LM 13.5, Whisper 23.9; the average Word Error Rate is 9.2 for V3_CTC and 8.4 for V3_RNNT, compared to 19.4 for T-One + LM and 25.1 for Whisper. The end-to-end ASR models (`e2e_ctc` and `e2e_rnnt`) produce punctuated, normalized text directly. In end-to-end ASR comparisons of `e2e_ctc` and `e2e_rnnt` against Whisper-large-v3, using Gemini 2.5 Pro as an LLM-as-a-judge, GigaAM-v3 models win by an average margin of 70:30.

instruction: Can I process a very long text file into audio without it getting cut off?
description: Pocket TTS is a lightweight text-to-speech (TTS) application designed to run efficiently on CPUs. It eliminates the need for GPUs or web APIs by allowing audio generation through a simple pip install and function call. The model supports Python versions 3.10 through 3.14 and requires PyTorch 2.5 or higher, but does not require the GPU version of PyTorch. It is a CPU-based model with a small size of 100 million parameters. It supports audio streaming with low latency, approximately 200 milliseconds to produce the first audio chunk, and operates at about six times real-time speed on the CPU of a MacBook Air M4. It uses only two CPU cores and provides both a Python API and a command-line interface (CLI). The model supports voice cloning and can handle infinitely long text inputs. It is currently limited to English language support. Users can try the model through a web interface without installation, or use it via the CLI or as a Python library. The model can be run as a local server to generate audio via HTTP requests, keeping the model in memory between requests for faster performance. The model is not supported for GPU execution, as no speed improvements were observed compared to CPU execution due to the use of a batch size of 1 and a small model size. Contributions are welcome, and development instructions are available in the relevant documentation. The model must be used in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations, and its use must not involve illegal, harmful, deceptive, fraudulent, or unauthorized activities. Prohibited uses include voice impersonation or cloning without explicit and lawful consent, misinformation or disinformation, and the generation of unlawful, harmful, or privacy-invasive content.

instruction: Generate a landscape image of a mountain range at sunrise with vivid colors and sharp textures.
description: Qwen-Image-2512 is a text-to-image foundational model, representing the December update of the Qwen-Image series. It is a GGUF quantized version of the model, using a methodology for state-of-the-art performance. Key improvements in Qwen-Image-2512 include enhanced human realism, finer natural detail, and improved text rendering. The model significantly reduces the 'AI-generated' look and substantially enhances overall image realism, especially for human subjects. It delivers more detailed rendering of landscapes, animal fur, and other natural elements. The model also improves the accuracy and quality of textual elements, achieving better layout and more faithful multimodal (text + image) composition. Over 10,000 rounds of blind model evaluations have been conducted, and the results show that Qwen-Image-2512 is currently the strongest open-source model and remains highly competitive among closed-source models. The model can be used with the diffusers library, and it supports generating images with various aspect ratios. It is designed to be used with prompts that describe the desired image, including detailed descriptions of subjects, environments, and visual styles. The model is capable of rendering complex scenes with high fidelity, including human portraits, landscapes, and technical or educational infographics. It is particularly effective in capturing fine details such as facial expressions, hair strands, and environmental textures. The model also supports generating images with specific lighting conditions, such as indoor illumination or natural daylight. It is capable of rendering realistic textures, including fur, fabric, and skin, and can produce images that resemble casual snapshots or professional photography. The model is suitable for a wide range of applications, including artistic creation, educational materials, and technical illustrations. It is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on devices with CUDA support for improved performance. The model is part of a series of updates to the Qwen-Image model, with each version introducing new features and improvements. The model is available for use through the Hugging Face and ModelScope platforms, and users are encouraged to try the latest version for the best results. The model is developed by a team of researchers and is available under an open-source license. If Qwen-Image-2512 is used in research, users are encouraged to cite the associated technical report.

instruction: Based on its type and rarity, is this card likely to appreciate in value soon?
description: This model is a PyTorch-based Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) designed to analyze features of Pokémon cards to identify those with potential for a significant price increase. It is a tabular binary classification model that takes various card features—such as rarity, type, and historical price data—as input and outputs a single logit. A sigmoid function can be applied to this logit to obtain a probability score for the likelihood of a price rise. The model is implemented in PyTorch and is part of a custom dataset derived from the PokemonTCG/pokemon-tcg-data repository, augmented with pricing history. The model is trained to predict a 30% price increase over a 6-month period. It is important to note that the model is trained on historical data and may not accurately predict future trends, especially in a volatile market. Additionally, the model is not trained for other thresholds or timeframes, and the dataset may contain biases related to card popularity, set releases, or data collection artifacts. SHAP values were computed to provide model explainability, showing the average impact of each feature on the model's output magnitude.

instruction: Can you make a video where the depth of the background changes to create a floating illusion behind the subject's face?
description: This model is a ControlNet module designed for the Wan2.2 A14B text-to-video generation system, specifically for depth-based control. It follows the same approach as other ControlNet implementations for Wan2.2. The model is intended to be used with the ComfyUI framework and requires the latest version of the diffusers library. It supports inference tasks for generating video content based on textual prompts and depth control inputs. The architecture includes a custom WanControlnet component, a transformer model, and an autoencoder. Performance is demonstrated through inference examples using a CLI and code, where the model processes a video input and generates a controlled video output based on a detailed prompt. The model is evaluated using a prompt that describes a complex visual scene involving a close-up shot of a young woman's face with a bubblegum bubble containing a miniature aquarium. The model is trained on unspecified data and is available for use with specific model paths. Caveats include the requirement for specific software versions and hardware, such as CUDA-compatible devices, and the need for manual installation of dependencies. The model is provided under a GitHub repository, and users are directed to raise issues or seek professional support through the repository or via email.

instruction: Please render the image from a 45-degree angle to the left and a moderate distance away.
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Multiple-Angles-LoRA is a multi-angle camera control LoRA designed for the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It provides 96 precise camera positions, derived from 4 elevations, 8 azimuths, and 3 distances. The model was trained on over 3000 Gaussian Splatting renders, which are high-quality 3D-consistent images, to enable better spatial understanding and camera control. It supports low-angle camera positions as low as -30°, making it suitable for ground-level and low camera shots. The LoRA enhances the built-in viewpoint capabilities of Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 by offering exact control over camera position, with a large dataset ensuring maximum accuracy. The model is intended for use with the exact prompt format of [azimuth] [elevation] [distance], and it is recommended to use a LoRA strength between 0.8 and 1.0 for optimal results. The model was built by Lovis Odin at fal using synthetic 3D renders with precise camera control. For best results, clear input images with good lighting are recommended, and users are advised to follow the specified prompt order and experiment with low-angle shots, where the model excels.

instruction: Generate a cinematic scene of a futuristic city at night with flying cars and glowing buildings.
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 includes a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, with each expert having about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active parameters per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. Wan2.2 also features a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling efficient deployment. The TI2V-5B model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available, suitable for both industrial and academic applications. The model supports single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses, and is compatible with the Diffusers library. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions.

instruction: How can I create a short animation based on a still photo of a mountain landscape with a flowing river?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and an efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video (TI2V) system. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The high-noise expert handles early-stage layout, while the low-noise expert refines details in later stages. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defining the switch. The A14B model series, which uses this MoE design, has 27B total parameters but only 14B active parameters per step, maintaining low inference cost and GPU memory usage. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data with detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, enabling precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on 65.6% more images and 83.2% more videos, improving generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. The TI2V-5B model, built with the advanced Wan2.2-VAE, achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling 720P video generation at 24fps on consumer-grade GPUs like the RTX 4090. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model can run on a single GPU with at least 24GB VRAM and can be optimized for higher VRAM GPUs to improve speed. Wan2.2 is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their use complies with the license terms, including not generating harmful or illegal content. The model is open-sourced and supports both industrial and academic applications.

instruction: What is the main message or theme of this image, and how is it conveyed visually?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for deployment flexibility. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including object position, viewpoint, and occlusion judgment, with 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. It achieves text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct.

instruction: I’m looking for a visual chart in a financial report that shows revenue growth over the past five years. Can you help me find it?
description: ColQwen2.5 is a model based on a novel architecture and training strategy using Vision Language Models (VLMs) to efficiently index documents from their visual features. It generates multi-vector representations of text and images. The model takes dynamic image resolutions as input without resizing, which may change their aspect ratio, similar to ColPali. The maximum resolution is set to create at most 768 image patches, with experiments showing improvements with more patches at the cost of increased memory requirements. This version is trained with colpali-engine version 0.3.7 and uses the same data as the ColPali dataset described in the paper. The training dataset consists of 127,460 query-page pairs, including 63% from openly available academic datasets and 37% from a synthetic dataset made of web-crawled PDF pages augmented with VLM-generated pseudo-questions. The training set is entirely in English, allowing for the study of zero-shot generalization to non-English languages. No multi-page PDF document is used in both the training and evaluation sets to prevent contamination. A validation set of 2% of the samples is used for hyperparameter tuning. Multilingual data may be present in the pretraining corpus of the language model and in the multimodal training. All models are trained for one epoch on the training set using bfloat16 format, low-rank adapters with alpha=32 and r=32 on the transformer layers from the language model and the final projection layer, and the paged_adamw_8bit optimizer. Training is conducted on an 8 GPU setup with data parallelism, a learning rate of 5e-5 with linear decay and 2.5% warmup steps, and a batch size of 32. The model is intended for use with colpali-engine version 0.3.1 or higher and transformers version greater than 4.45.0. The model primarily focuses on PDF-type documents and high-resource languages, which may limit its generalization to other document types or less represented languages. It relies on multi-vector retrieval derived from the ColBERT late interaction mechanism, which may require engineering efforts to adapt to widely used vector retrieval frameworks that lack native multi-vector support. The vision language backbone model (Qwen2.5-VL) is licensed under the Qwen RESEARCH LICENSE AGREEMENT, while the adapters are under the MIT license.

instruction: Can you help me detect and track moving objects in a video using visual features?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. The models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN or logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or using a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, the iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and video classification. For example, the ViT-7B model achieves 90.4% on IN-ReaL, 91.1% on IN-R, 91.1% on Obj.Net, 72.8% on Ox.-H, 55.9% on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU, 79.7% on DAVIS, 64.4% on NAVI, and 58.7% on SPair. The ConvNeXt Base model achieves 88.5% on IN-ReaL, 89.2% on IN-R, 77.2% on Obj.Net, 78.2% on ADE20k, 56.2% on NYU, 61.3% on DAVIS, 46.3% on NAVI, and 0.420 on SPair. On satellite data, the ViT-L model achieves 73.0% on m-BEnet, 96.5% on m-brick-kiln, 94.1% on m-eurosat, 60.6% on m-forestnet, 96.0% on m-pv4ger, 57.4% on m-so2sat, and 79.6% on the mean classification task. The ViT-7B model achieves 74.0% on m-BEnet, 97.2% on m-brick-kiln, 94.8% on m-eurosat, 62.3% on m-forestnet, 96.1% on m-pv4ger, 62.1% on m-so2sat, and 81.1% on the mean classification task. On segmentation tasks, the ViT-L model achieves 94.2% on m-cashew, 75.6% on m-chesapeake, 61.8% on m-NeonTree, 83.7% on m-nz-cattle, 95.2% on m-pv4ger-seg, 36.8% on m-SA-crop, and 74.5% on the mean segmentation task. The ViT-7B model achieves 94.1% on m-cashew, 76.6% on m-chesapeake, 62.6% on m-NeonTree, 83.4% on m-nz-cattle, 95.5% on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6% on m-SA-crop, and 75.0% on the mean segmentation task. The model training used Nvidia H100 GPUs and required 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in 18t CO2eq of carbon emissions. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: What is the main idea of the text visible in this scanned page?
description: The H2OVL-Mississippi-800M is a compact yet powerful vision-language model developed by H2O.ai, featuring 0.8 billion parameters. Despite its relatively small size, it achieves state-of-the-art performance in text recognition, particularly excelling in the Text Recognition segment of OCRBench and outperforming larger models in this domain. The model is built upon the architecture of H2O-Danube language models, extending their capabilities by integrating vision and language tasks. It is trained on 19 million image-text pairs, with a focus on OCR, document comprehension, and the interpretation of charts, figures, and tables. The model is optimized for superior OCR performance and is suitable for OCR and document processing due to the balance it achieves between performance and efficiency. The model is designed to support a range of tasks, including text recognition, document comprehension, and the interpretation of visual data such as charts and tables. It can be used for both pure-text conversations and image-based queries. The model includes a disclaimer that acknowledges potential biases and limitations in the generated content, as well as ethical considerations for its use. Users are encouraged to use the model responsibly and to report any issues with the generated content.

instruction: How does the open model with a 128K context window improve performance on long documents?
description: Gemma is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models from Google, built from the same research and technology used to create the Gemini models. Gemma 3 models are multimodal, handling text and image input and generating text output, with open weights for both pre-trained variants and instruction-tuned variants. Gemma 3 has a large, 128K context window, multilingual support in over 140 languages, and is available in more sizes than previous versions. Gemma 3 models are well-suited for a variety of text generation and image understanding tasks, including question answering, summarization, and reasoning. Their relatively small size makes it possible to deploy them in environments with limited resources such as laptops, desktops or your own cloud infrastructure, democratizing access to state of the art AI models and helping foster innovation for everyone. The input includes text strings such as questions, prompts, or documents to be summarized, and images normalized to 896 x 896 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. The total input context is 128K tokens for the 4B, 12B, and 27B sizes, and 32K tokens for the 1B size. The output includes generated text in response to the input, such as an answer to a question, analysis of image content, or a summary of a document, with a total output context of 8192 tokens. These models were trained on a dataset of text data that includes a wide variety of sources. The 27B model was trained with 14 trillion tokens, the 12B model with 12 trillion tokens, the 4B model with 4 trillion tokens, and the 1B model with 2 trillion tokens. The training dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, and images. Web documents ensure the model is exposed to a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and vocabulary, including content in over 140 languages. Code helps the model learn the syntax and patterns of programming languages, improving its ability to generate code and understand code-related questions. Mathematics helps the model learn logical reasoning, symbolic representation, and to address mathematical queries. Images enable the model to perform image analysis and visual data extraction tasks. Rigorous CSAM filtering was applied at multiple stages in the data preparation process to ensure the exclusion of harmful and illegal content. Automated techniques were used to filter out certain personal information and other sensitive data from training sets. Additional methods were applied for content quality and safety in line with safety policies. Gemma was trained using Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) hardware (TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e). TPUs are specifically designed to handle the massive computations involved in training vision-language models (VLMs), offering performance, memory, scalability, and cost-effectiveness advantages. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways. JAX allows researchers to take advantage of the latest generation of hardware, including TPUs, for faster and more efficient training of large models. ML Pathways is Google's effort to build artificially intelligent systems capable of generalizing across multiple tasks, suitable for foundation models like these. These models were evaluated against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of text generation. In reasoning and factuality, the models showed performance across various benchmarks such as HellaSwag, BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, TriviaQA, Natural Questions, ARC-c, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BIG-Bench Hard, and DROP. In STEM and code, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MMLU, AGIEval, MATH, GSM8K, GPQA, MBPP, and HumanEval. In multilingual tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as MGSM, Global-MMLU-Lite, WMT24++, FloRes, XQuAD, ECLeKTic, and IndicGenBench. In multimodal tasks, the models were evaluated on benchmarks such as COCOcap, DocVQA, InfoVQA, MMMU, TextVQA, RealWorldQA, ReMI, AI2D, ChartQA, VQAv2, BLINK, OKVQA, TallyQA, SpatialSense VQA, and CountBenchQA. The evaluation methods included structured evaluations and internal red-teaming testing of relevant content policies. Red-teaming was conducted by different teams with different goals and human evaluation metrics. The models were evaluated against categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. For all areas of safety testing, the models showed major improvements in the categories of child safety, content safety, and representational harms relative to previous Gemma models. All testing was conducted without safety filters to evaluate the model capabilities and behaviors. For both text-to-text and image-to-text, and across all model sizes, the model produced minimal policy violations and showed significant improvements over previous Gemma models' performance with respect to ungrounded inferences. A limitation of the evaluations was that they included only English language prompts. These models have certain limitations that users should be aware of. The quality and diversity of the training data significantly influence the model's capabilities. Biases or gaps in the training data can lead to limitations in the model's responses. The scope of the training dataset determines the subject areas the model can handle effectively. Models are better at tasks that can be framed with clear prompts and instructions. Open-ended or highly complex tasks might be challenging. A model's performance can be influenced by the amount of context provided (longer context generally leads to better outputs, up to a certain point). Natural language is inherently complex. Models might struggle to grasp subtle nuances, sarcasm, or figurative language. Models generate responses based on information they learned from their training datasets, but they are not knowledge bases. They may generate incorrect or outdated factual statements. Models rely on statistical patterns in language. They might lack the ability to apply common sense reasoning in certain situations. The development of vision-language models (VLMs) raises several ethical concerns. VLMs trained on large-scale, real-world text and image data can reflect socio-cultural biases embedded in the training material. These models underwent careful scrutiny, input data pre-processing, and posterior evaluations. VLMs can be misused to generate text that is false, misleading, or harmful. Guidelines are provided for responsible use with the model. Transparency and accountability are emphasized, with details on the models' architecture, capabilities, limitations, and evaluation processes summarized in this model card. A responsibly developed open model offers the opportunity to share innovation by making VLM technology accessible to developers and researchers across the AI ecosystem. Risks identified include the perpetuation of biases, generation of harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. It is encouraged to perform continuous monitoring and the exploration of de-biasing techniques during model training, fine-tuning, and other use cases. Mechanisms and guidelines for content safety are essential. Developers are encouraged to exercise caution and implement appropriate content safety safeguards based on their specific product policies and application use cases. Technical limitations and developer and end-user education can help mitigate against malicious applications of VLMs. Educational resources and reporting mechanisms for users to flag misuse are provided. Prohibited uses of Gemma models are outlined in the Gemma Prohibited Use Policy. Models were trained on data filtered for removal of certain personal information and other sensitive data. Developers are encouraged to adhere to privacy regulations with privacy-preserving techniques. At the time of release, this family of models provides high-performance open vision-language model implementations designed from the ground up for responsible AI development compared to similarly sized models. Using the benchmark evaluation metrics described in this document, these models have shown to provide superior performance to other, comparably-sized open model alternatives.

instruction: Can you process a 1024x1024 image and return a set of tokens for downstream tasks?

The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for DINOv3 ViT-S/16. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for DINOv3 ConvNeXt Tiny. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and 79.6 mean for DINOv3 ViT-L/16. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Please locate and mark the animals in this wildlife photo.
description: YOLOv13 is a real-time object detection model designed to improve detection performance in complex scenarios by capturing global multi-to-multi high-order correlations. It introduces a Hypergraph-based Adaptive Correlation Enhancement (HyperACE) mechanism that adaptively exploits latent high-order correlations, overcoming the limitations of previous methods that were restricted to pairwise correlation modeling. YOLOv13 also features a Full-Pipeline Aggregation-and-Distribution (FullPAD) paradigm, which distributes correlation-enhanced features across the entire network to achieve fine-grained information flow and representation synergy. Additionally, the model uses depthwise separable convolutions to replace large-kernel convolutions, significantly reducing parameters and computational complexity without sacrificing performance. YOLOv13 includes four variants: Nano, Small, Large, and X-Large. Experimental results on the MS COCO benchmark show that YOLOv13-N improves mAP by 3.0% over YOLO11-N and by 1.5% over YOLOv12-N. The model is available in multiple formats and supports training, validation, prediction, and export to ONNX or TensorRT. The code and models are open-sourced and can be accessed at the provided URL.

instruction: This image contains a mix of Chinese and English text. Can you extract both?
description: PP-OCRv5_server_rec is one of the PP-OCRv5_rec models, which are the latest generation text line recognition models developed by the PaddleOCR team. It is designed to efficiently and accurately support the recognition of four major languages—Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, and Japanese—as well as complex text scenarios such as handwriting, vertical text, pinyin, and rare characters using a single model. The model achieves key accuracy metrics across various text recognition tasks, including 0.5807 for Handwritten Chinese, 0.5806 for Handwritten English, 0.9013 for Printed Chinese, 0.8679 for Printed English, 0.7472 for Traditional Chinese, 0.6039 for Ancient Text, 0.7372 for Japanese, 0.5946 for General Scenario, 0.8384 for Pinyin, 0.7435 for Rotation, 0.9314 for Distortion, 0.6397 for Artistic Text, and an overall average of 0.8401. These metrics are calculated by marking an entire line as incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong, ensuring higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks through the PaddleOCR framework, either as a standalone module or as part of an OCR pipeline that includes document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition modules. It supports integration into projects via Python code and command-line tools, and it can be used with GPU devices for model inference. The model is also compatible with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline for layout analysis, which includes layout detection, general OCR, document image preprocessing, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The default model used in the PP-StructureV3 pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, and users can also specify a local model file if needed.

instruction: Create a video of a person hiking with the sound of wind and distant birds chirping.
description: LTX-2 is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a diffusion-based audio-video foundation model that supports English. It includes several checkpoints, such as the full model in bf16, fp8, and nvfp4 quantizations, a distilled version of the full model, a LoRA version of the distilled model, and upscalers for spatial and temporal resolution enhancement. The model can be used for image-to-video generation and is supported in the Diffusers framework. It is accessible via online demos and can be run locally using the PyTorch codebase, which includes model definitions, pipelines, and training capabilities. The codebase requires Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA version 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. For local use, it is recommended to use the built-in LTXVideo nodes in the ComfyUI Manager. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify societal biases, may fail to generate videos that perfectly match prompts, prompt following is influenced by prompting style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and audio generated without speech may be of lower quality. The base model is fully trainable, and it is easy to reproduce the LoRAs and IC-LoRAs published with the model by following the provided instructions. Training for motion, style, or likeness (sound+appearance) can take less than an hour in many settings.

instruction: I need to transform this daytime photo into a nighttime scene. Can you help?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of a model capable of editing images based on text instructions. It is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. The model is developed by the Nunchaku Team and is categorized as an image-to-image model. Two quantized versions are available: one is SVDQuant quantized INT4 FLUX.1-Kontext-dev model for users with non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series), and the other is SVDQuant quantized NVFP4 FLUX.1-Kontext-dev model for users with Blackwell GPUs (50-series). The FLUX.1 [dev] Model is licensed by Black Forest Labs Inc. under the FLUX.1 [dev] Non-Commercial License. Copyright Black Forest Labs Inc. IN NO EVENT SHALL BLACK FOREST LABS INC. BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM, OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH USE OF THIS MODEL.

instruction: Is there a way to get a realistic image from a normal map, preserving the direction of surfaces?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model and is primarily associated with the English language. The license is adapted from the work of others in the area of responsible AI licensing. The model is conditioned on normalbae images, which are images containing surface normal information, typically represented as color-coded images. The preprocessor in Normal 1.1 is trained to estimate normal maps using a more correct protocol (NYU-V2's visualization method), making it more reasonable and physically correct compared to the previous version. This improvement allows the model to interpret real normal maps from rendering engines, provided the colors are correct (blue is front, red is left, green is top). In testing, the model has shown robust performance and can achieve results similar to the depth model. The previous Normal 1.0 model was not frequently used due to its limitations, but Normal 1.1 has significant improvements and potential for more frequent use.

instruction: Design a poster for a music festival with bold colors, musical notes, and a crowd of people dancing.
description: Qwen-Image is an image generation foundation model in the Qwen series that achieves significant advances in complex text rendering and precise image editing. It demonstrates strong general capabilities in both image generation and editing, with exceptional performance in text rendering, especially for Chinese. The model supports a wide range of artistic styles, from photorealistic scenes to impressionist paintings, anime aesthetics, and minimalist design. It enables advanced image editing operations such as style transfer, object insertion or removal, detail enhancement, text editing within images, and human pose manipulation. Qwen-Image also supports image understanding tasks, including object detection, semantic segmentation, depth and edge (Canny) estimation, novel view synthesis, and super-resolution. These capabilities are powered by deep visual comprehension, allowing the model to adapt fluidly to creative prompts and provide intuitive input with coherent output. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: Can you detect any truck movement in this footage?
description: The model is a variant that has been fine-tuned for multi-label video classification, specifically for camera motion classification on an internal dataset. It is designed to predict 18 different camera motion classes, including 'arc_left', 'arc_right', 'dolly_in', 'dolly_out', 'pan_left', 'pan_right', 'pedestal_down', 'pedestal_up', 'roll_left', 'roll_right', 'static', 'tilt_down', 'tilt_up', 'truck_left', 'truck_right', 'undefined', 'zoom_in', and 'zoom_out', as well as 3 shot type classes: 'pov', 'shake', and 'track'. The model is trained to associate the entire video with camera motion labels, rather than frame-level motions, meaning it outputs one or more labels for the whole video. If a camera motion is not present throughout the entire video, the model should predict 'undefined'. The model processes 16 frames per input clip, extracted uniformly from the video, regardless of its original duration. For videos longer than 2 seconds, it is recommended to split the video into non-overlapping 2-second segments, run inference on each segment independently, and then post-process the results. The model achieves an accuracy of 75% on an internal test dataset of 2-second videos, and approximately 84% when excluding the 'pov', 'shake', and 'track' classes.

instruction: What audio format is best suited for training models in environments with limited bandwidth?
description: NeuCodec is a lightweight neural audio codec designed for encoding speech at a bit-rate of 0.8 kbps. It is intended for use in training high-quality text-to-speech models and is optimized for real-world applications, including commercial use. The model uses Finite Scalar Quantisation (FSQ) to produce a single codebook, which is ideal for downstream modeling with Speech Language Models. It operates by taking 16kHz audio input and upscaling it to 24kHz using an upsampling decoder. The codec processes audio at 50 tokens per second with 16 bits per token, resulting in the 0.8kbps bit-rate. The FSQ encoding scheme provides bit-level error resistance, making it suitable for use in unreliable or noisy communication channels. NeuCodec was trained on a variety of datasets, including Emilia-YODAS, MLS, LibriTTS, Fleurs, CommonVoice, HUI, and an additional proprietary set. Publicly available data was covered by either the CC-BY-4.0 or CC0 license. The model is released under the Apache-2.0 license and is available for use in tools or products. Pre-encoded datasets are provided, including a version of Emilia-YODAS compressed from 1.7TB to 41GB using NeuCodec, which significantly reduces the compute requirements for training. The model is developed by Neuphonic and is available for installation via PyPI.

instruction: Could you describe the way the system learns to improve segmentation accuracy?
description: The VisionReasoner-7B model is part of the Seg-Zero framework, which is designed to demonstrate generalizability and derive explicit chain-of-thought reasoning through cognitive reinforcement for reasoning segmentation. The VisionReasoner-7B model uses a decoupled architecture that includes a reasoning model and a segmentation model. The reasoning model interprets user intentions, generates explicit reasoning chains, and produces positional prompts, which are then used by the segmentation model to generate precise pixel-level masks. The model is trained exclusively through reinforcement learning with GRPO and without the use of explicit reasoning data. This training approach enables Seg-Zero to achieve robust zero-shot generalization and to exhibit emergent test-time reasoning capabilities. Experimental results show that Seg-Zero-7B achieves a zero-shot performance of 57.5 on the ReasonSeg benchmark, which is an 18% improvement over the prior LISA-7B model. This improvement highlights the framework's ability to generalize across domains while presenting an explicit reasoning process.

instruction: Please find all the anatomical mentions in this excerpt from a medical textbook.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for anatomical entity recognition, focusing on identifying and extracting anatomical structures and body parts from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and trained on a curated ANATOMY dataset, making it suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-Anatomy and I-Anatomy. It is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. The model has achieved an F1 score of 0.88, precision of 0.88, recall of 0.89, and accuracy of 0.97. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Could you assist me in retrieving the best scientific evidence on the side effects of a specific antidepressant?
description: This is a model finetuned from EmbeddingGemma-300m on the Medical Instruction and Retrieval Dataset (MIRIAD), specifically the first 100,000 question-passage pairs. It maps sentences and documents to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and is designed for medical information retrieval, particularly for searching passages (up to 1,000 tokens) of scientific medical papers using detailed medical questions. The model is based on the Sentence Transformer architecture, with a maximum sequence length of 1,024 tokens and uses cosine similarity for measuring similarity. It was trained to showcase how the EmbeddingGemma model can be adapted for specific domains and tasks to achieve stronger performance. The model is not affiliated with Google. The architecture includes a Gemma3TextModel transformer, a pooling layer with mean token pooling, two dense layers with identity activation functions, and a normalization layer. The model is available through the Sentence Transformers library and can be used for encoding queries and documents to compute their similarity. It was evaluated on the MIRIAD dataset, achieving cosine_ndcg@10 scores of 0.9026 on the evaluation set and 0.8862 on the test set. The training was conducted using a batch size of 128, a learning rate of 2e-05, and a single epoch with a warmup ratio of 0.1. The training process consumed 0.828 kWh of energy and emitted 0.331 kg of CO2. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU and a 13th Gen Intel Core i7-13700K CPU with 31.78 GB of RAM. The training used Python 3.11.6, Sentence Transformers 5.2.0.dev0, Transformers 4.56.0.dev0, PyTorch 2.7.1+cu126, and other related libraries. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Can you help me identify all the legal entities mentioned in this Greek government document?
description: GEM-ELECTRA Legal is an improved ELECTRA-base model pre-trained from scratch on a 17GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. This second version includes refined training hyperparameters for enhanced performance and stability. It is designed to understand the complex vocabulary and context of the legal domain in Greece and the European Union. The model was developed as part of a research project and optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering within the legal field. The ELECTRA architecture enables more efficient pre-training compared to masked language models like BERT by using a generator-discriminator approach. The training data was compiled from various legal and governmental sources, including the FEK - Greek Government Gazette (11.0 GB), Greek Parliament Proceedings (2.9 GB), Political Reports of the Supreme Court (1.2 GB), Eur-Lex (Greek Content, 0.92 GB), Europarl (Greek Content, 0.38 GB), and Raptarchis Legal Dictionary (0.35 GB), totaling approximately 16.75 GB. The text was tokenized using a custom ByteLevelBPE tokenizer trained from scratch on the Greek legal corpus, with an uncased vocabulary of 50,264 tokens. The data was processed into fixed-size chunks of 512 tokens, respecting document boundaries to ensure contextual coherence. The model was pre-trained for 200,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs using BFloat16 mixed-precision. Key hyperparameters included a learning rate of 1e-4 with a linear warmup of 12,000 steps, an effective batch size of 3,840, AdamW optimizer with beta1=0.9, beta2=0.98, and epsilon=1e-6, weight decay of 0.01, max sequence length of 512, and equal loss weights for the generator and discriminator of 50.0. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.0056 and a final evaluation loss of 0.0054.

instruction: Can you help me draft a short story about a futuristic city powered entirely by renewable energy?
description: gemma-3n-E4B-it is a 6-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or safety of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or suitability for any purpose. Users are solely responsible for any damage or issues arising from the use of the model.

instruction: Is there a way to map human body points in a low-resolution image?
description: Ultralytics YOLO26 is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, engineered from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. This is the pose estimation variant optimized for human keypoint detection. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. YOLO26 is a native end-to-end model, producing predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS). By eliminating this post-processing step, inference becomes faster, lighter, and easier to deploy in real-world systems. The end-to-end design cuts out an entire stage of the pipeline, dramatically simplifying integration, reducing latency, and making deployment more robust across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon — inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training. This optimizer brings enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed for pose estimation, DFL Removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference, optimized for edge computing. The model has an input size of 640 pixels, an mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 70.4, an mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 90.5, a CPU Speed (ONNX) of 275.4 ms, a T4 TensorRT10 Speed of 6.5 ms, 25.9M parameters, and 91.3B FLOPs.

instruction: What are the performance benchmarks for the Q4_0 version on an AVX2 system?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the Voxtral-Small-24B-2507 model by MistralAI, using the llama.cpp release b6014 for quantization. The model is intended for use with llama.cpp or other llama.cpp-based projects. The quantizations are generated using the imatrix option and a dataset provided by the original model. The model supports various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The available quantizations include BF16, Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_1, Q4_K_L, Q4_K_M, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q3_K_XL, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, IQ3_M, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, Q2_K_L, IQ3_XXS, Q2_K, IQ2_M, IQ2_S, and IQ2_XS. Each quantization is described with its quality level and file size. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB. Some quantizations, such as Q4_0, support online repacking for improved performance on ARM and AVX hardware. The model includes information on performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system, showing speed improvements for certain quantizations. Users are advised to consider their hardware capabilities when selecting a quantization, balancing speed, quality, and memory usage. I-quants (IQX_X) are recommended for lower quantization levels when using cuBLAS or rocBLAS, while K-quants (QX_K_X) are generally suitable for most use cases. The model includes credits to contributors for dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: What is the significance of this lesion in the radiograph?
description: This repository contains a drop-in, Hugging Face–compatible checkpoint converted from an original model. It can be loaded using the same code as the original model, without requiring any additional conversion steps. The model is based on an architecture that includes an embedding layer (embed_tokens) and an output layer (lm_head), both of which may change in shape when the vocabulary size is expanded. However, the attention mechanism remains unaffected, as it operates on the hidden state dimensions, which stay consistent. Expanding the vocabulary increases the number of token embeddings but does not alter the attention calculation logic. The only change in the model is the addition of new token embeddings, which are initialized and used during the first forward pass. The output layer (lm_head) changes in shape but not in functional logic. The model supports tasks involving both image and text inputs, such as answering questions about medical images. It can be loaded using the LlavaForConditionalGeneration class from the transformers library and an AutoProcessor for input processing. Example inference involves constructing a prompt with an image and a text question, tokenizing the input, and generating a response using the model's generate method. The model is compatible with multi-GPU setups and uses the bfloat16 data type for efficient computation. It also supports the flash_attention_2 implementation for attention calculations.

instruction: I have a picture of a statue — can you turn it into a 3D model I can use in a creative project?
description: Stable Fast 3D (SF3D) is a large reconstruction model that takes a single image of an object and generates a textured UV-unwrapped 3D mesh asset. The model is a Transformer-based image-to-3D model designed to create a 3D model from a single image in under one second. The output asset is UV-unwrapped, textured, and has a relatively low polygon count. The model performs a delighting step, which facilitates easier use of the asset in downstream applications such as game engines or rendering work. It also predicts per-object material parameters, including roughness and metallic, which enhances reflective behaviors during rendering. The model expects an input size of 512x512 pixels. The model is trained using renders from a dataset available under the Open Data Commons Attribution License. An enhanced rendering method is used, which more closely replicates the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving the model's ability to generalize. Objects are filtered based on the review of licenses, and a subset is curated to meet training needs. Intended uses include the generation of artworks and use in design and other artistic processes, applications in educational or creative tools, and research on reconstruction models, including understanding the limitations of these models. The model was not trained to produce factual or true representations of people or events, and generating such content is out of scope for the model's capabilities. Individuals or organizations generating annual revenue of US $1,000,000 (or local currency equivalent) or more must obtain an enterprise commercial license directly from Stability AI before commercially using SF3D or any derivative work of SF3D or its outputs. Community License allows free use for research, non-commercial, and commercial use by organizations and individuals generating annual revenue of US $1M or less. Developers are encouraged to conduct their own testing and apply additional mitigations based on their specific use cases, as the model includes safety measures intended to reduce the risk of severe harms.

instruction: How would the scene look from the side if I take a step closer to the tree in this image?
description: SHARP is an approach to photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. Given a single photograph, SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of the depicted scene. This is done in less than a second on a standard GPU via a single feedforward pass through a neural network. The 3D Gaussian representation produced by SHARP can then be rendered in real time, yielding high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The representation is metric, with absolute scale, supporting metric camera movements. Experimental results demonstrate that SHARP delivers robust zero-shot generalization across datasets. It sets a new state of the art on multiple datasets, reducing LPIPS by 25–34% and DISTS by 21–43% versus the best prior model, while lowering the synthesis time by three orders of magnitude.

instruction: What are the best practices for integrating AI systems into a secure DevOps pipeline?
description: ELISARCyberAIEdge7B-LoRA-GGUF is a LoRA-finetuned, GGUF-quantized version of the Mistral-7B backbone, tailored for edge deployment in cybersecurity and blue-team AI scenarios. It is designed for contextualized cybersecurity risk assessment using Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Agentic AI capabilities. The model is based on Mistral-7B-v0.3 (FP16 / BF16) and includes a LoRA adapter called `sallani/ELISARCyberAIEdge7B`. It has been converted to GGUF format and optionally quantized to Q4_K_M (4-bit) for efficient inference on resource-constrained devices such as NVIDIA T4 and desktop GPUs. The resulting model file, `elisar_merged.gguf`, is approximately 160 MiB in size and can be deployed offline using frameworks or through minimal Torch-based inference. The model supports real-world use cases including threat modeling (Blue ELISAR), offensive use-case generation (Red ELISAR), and GRC compliance automation (GRC ELISAR). It is intended for tasks such as ISO/IEC 42001 and NIS2 risk evaluation, threat scenario generation, AI audit preparation and reporting, and secure AI system design. The model is developed by Dr. Sabri Sallani, an expert in Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity. Users are advised to use the instruction format `### Instruction:` / `### Response:` and to include relevant logs or code in prompts. The model is not intended as a replacement for certified analysts. The model is part of the ELISAR framework, which is described in a paper titled 'ELISAR: An Adaptive Framework for Cybersecurity Risk Assessment Powered by GenAI' to be published by Springer on July 10, 2025.

instruction: What are the most popular quantizations among users?
