description: This model provides various quantized versions of a neural network, optimized for different use cases and performance requirements. The available quantizations include IQ1_S, IQ1_M, IQ2_XXS, IQ2_XS, IQ2_S, IQ2_M, Q2_K_S, Q2_K, IQ3_XXS, IQ3_XS, Q3_K_S, IQ3_S, IQ3_M, Q3_K_M, Q3_K_L, IQ4_XS, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, Q4_K_S, Q4_K_M, Q4_1, Q5_K_S, Q5_K_M, and Q6_K. These quants vary in file size from 1.3 GB to 3.7 GB and are associated with different quality and performance characteristics. For example, the Q4_K_S quant is described as optimal in terms of size, speed, and quality, while the Q4_K_M is noted as fast and recommended. Some quants, such as IQ1_S and IQ1_M, are described as suitable only for desperate use cases, and others like Q2_K_S and IQ3_XXS are noted for their low quality. The model is intended for users who require lightweight and efficient inference, and it supports concatenation of multi-part files for use. The model quants are made available through a combination of company resources and private computing infrastructure provided by a collaborator.

instruction: What parts of this image are most relevant for semantic segmentation?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperform specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers applied on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the models outperform previous models on multiple benchmarks. For example, the ViT-7B model achieves 90.4 on IN-ReaL, 91.1 on IN-R, 91.1 on Obj.Net, 72.8 on Ox.-H, 55.9 on ADE20k, 0.309 on NYU, 79.7 on DAVIS, 64.4 on NAVI, and 58.7 on SPair. The ConvNeXt Large model achieves 88.9 on IN-ReaL, 89.4 on IN-R, 81.3 on Obj.Net, 82.4 on ADE20k, 59.3 on NYU, 65.2 on DAVIS, 47.8 on NAVI, and 0.403 on SPair. On satellite data, the ViT-7B model achieves 74.0 on m-BEnet, 97.2 on m-brick-kiln, 94.8 on m-eurosat, 62.3 on m-forestnet, 96.1 on m-pv4ger, 62.1 on m-so2sat, and 81.1 mean for classification tasks, and 94.1 on m-cashew, 76.6 on m-chesapeake, 62.6 on m-NeonTree, 83.4 on m-nz-cattle, 95.5 on m-pv4ger-seg, 37.6 on m-SA-crop, and 75.0 mean for segmentation tasks. The model architecture includes Vision Transformer models with varying parameters and ConvNeXt models with different sizes. The training of the ViT-7B model required 61,440 hours of compute time on Nvidia H100 GPUs, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The models were trained using PyTorch 2.7 on private infrastructure in the USA.

instruction: Can you help me break down a complex math problem into smaller steps to make it easier to solve?
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two open models are available: gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b. The gpt-oss-120b is intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters. The gpt-oss-20b is intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and should only be used with the harmony format, as they will not work correctly otherwise. The gpt-oss models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them ideal for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, facilitating easier debugging and increased trust in outputs, though this is not intended to be shown to end users. The models are fine-tunable, allowing for full customization to specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning. They support agentic capabilities, including function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The gpt-oss-20b model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware, while the gpt-oss-120b model can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node. The models support web browsing using built-in tools, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks. The reasoning level can be set in the system prompts to low, medium, or high, depending on the task requirements. The gpt-oss models can be used with various frameworks and tools, including Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. Model weights can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI.

instruction: How can I convert a single image of a statue into a 3D mesh with textures?
description: AIEXX GenAI Image to 3D is a professional, GPU-accelerated AI system for generating high-quality 3D models from text prompts or 2D images. It is built on cutting-edge deep learning technologies and delivers production-ready 3D assets in seconds. The system supports both text-to-3D generation and image-to-3D conversion. For text-to-3D, it uses natural language prompts to generate 3D models, integrating Stable Diffusion 1.5 and supporting advanced negative prompting. This process takes 20-60 seconds per model. For image-to-3D, it converts a single image into a full 3D mesh, with support for multi-view reconstruction and automated texture generation, taking 15-30 seconds to process. The system is optimized for NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPUs, using CUDA 12.8 and cuDNN for acceleration, and supports BF16 mixed precision training and Flash Attention v2. It is implemented in Python 3.11.9 with PyTorch 2.7.0 and includes TripoSR for 3D reconstruction. The system provides professional output in GLB/GLTF format with PBR material support and configurable mesh resolution, compatible with Blender, Unity, and Unreal. It supports local, offline processing with no compilation required, and includes a drag-and-drop interface. The system requires Windows 10/11 (64-bit), an NVIDIA RTX 5060 GPU with 8GB VRAM, 16GB minimum RAM (32GB recommended), and ~55GB of free storage. It offers three workflows: a basic TripoSR pipeline for quick prototypes, an intermediate prompt-to-3D pipeline using Stable Diffusion 1.5, and an advanced InstantMesh pipeline for high-quality output. Performance benchmarks indicate processing times of 15-30 seconds for image-to-3D, 25-40 seconds for prompt-to-3D with TripoSR, and 45-90 seconds for prompt-to-3D with InstantMesh. The system is built upon open-source projects including ComfyUI, Stable Diffusion, TripoSR, PyTorch, and torch_scatter. It is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: How can I create realistic images from different angles based on one picture?
description: This is a Core ML (.mlpackage) version of SHARP, a model for photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of a scene from a single photograph, which can then be rendered in real time to produce high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The model is metric, with absolute scale, and supports metric camera movements. The Core ML version is optimized for CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine inference on macOS and iOS. The model accepts a 3-channel RGB image in uint8 format with shape (1, 3, H, W) and a scalar disparity factor representing the ratio of focal length to image width. It outputs five tensors representing a 3D Gaussian splat representation: 3D positions, scale parameters, orientation quaternions, linear RGB colors, and opacity values. The total number of Gaussians is approximately 1,179,648 for the default model. The Core ML model has been validated against the original PyTorch implementation, with all outputs matching within 0.01% mean error. Quaternion angular errors are below 1° for 99% of Gaussians. Inference on an Apple M4 Max takes approximately 1.9 seconds. The model is designed to be used with the provided inference script, which includes features such as automatic model compilation and caching, decimation to reduce point cloud size while preserving visual fidelity, and compatibility with standard RGB input images. The model is not affiliated with the original research paper titled 'Sharp Monocular View Synthesis in Less Than a Second' by Lars Mescheder, Wei Dong, Shiwei Li, Xuyang Bai, Marcel Santos, Peiyun Hu, Bruno Lecouat, Mingmin Zhen, Amaël Delaunoy, Tian Fang, Yanghai Tsin, Stephan Richter, and Vladlen Koltun.

instruction: What actions are the people performing in this video?
description: Keye-VL-1.5 is a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) designed to address fundamental challenges in video comprehension. It incorporates a novel Slow-Fast video encoding strategy, a progressive four-stage pre-training methodology that extends context length up to 128K tokens, and a comprehensive post-training pipeline focused on reasoning enhancement and human preference alignment. The model demonstrates significant improvements in video understanding tasks and maintains competitive performance on general multimodal benchmarks. Keye-VL-1.5 is based on the Qwen3-8B language model and includes a vision encoder initialized from the open-source SigLIP. It supports SlowFast video encoding and native dynamic resolution, preserving the original aspect ratio of images by dividing each into a 14x14 patch sequence. A simple MLP layer maps and merges the visual tokens. The model uses 3D RoPE for unified processing of text, image, and video information. The post-training process includes a non-reasoning stage composed of SFT and MPO training, and a reasoning stage consisting of three key steps: CoT Cold Start, General RL, and Alignment RL. Keye-VL-1.5-8B establishes itself with powerful, state-of-the-art perceptual abilities that are competitive with leading models. It demonstrates exceptional proficiency in video understanding across authoritative public video benchmarks such as Video-MME, Video-MMMU, TempCompass, LongVideoBench, and MMVU. In evaluation sets requiring complex logical reasoning and mathematical problem-solving, such as WeMath, MathVerse, and LogicVista, Keye-VL-1.5-8B displays strong performance, highlighting its advanced capacity for logical deduction and solving complex quantitative problems.

instruction: I have a list of documents; how can I prioritize them based on their relevance to a specific topic?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B is a text reranking model part of the Qwen3 Embedding series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is a modified version of a foundational model, adapted as a sequence classification model. The Qwen3 Embedding series builds upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series, offering a comprehensive range of text embeddings and reranking models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B). This series inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of its foundational model. The Qwen3 Embedding series has achieved state-of-the-art performance in multiple text embedding and ranking tasks, including text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. The 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of June 5, 2025, score 70.58), while the reranking model excels in various text retrieval scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series offers a full spectrum of sizes (from 0.6B to 8B) for both embedding and reranking models, catering to diverse use cases that prioritize efficiency and effectiveness. Developers can combine these two modules seamlessly. The embedding model allows for flexible vector definitions across all dimensions, and both embedding and reranking models support user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The Qwen3 Embedding series supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B has 0.6B parameters, a context length of 32k, and supports 100+ languages. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B achieves a score of 65.80 on MTEB-R, 71.31 on CMTEB-R, 66.36 on MMTEB-R, 67.28 on MLDR, 73.42 on MTEB-Code, and 5.41 on FollowIR. Developers are advised to customize the input instructions according to their specific tasks and scenarios, as using instructions typically yields an improvement of 1% to 5% in performance. In multilingual contexts, it is recommended to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English.

instruction: Produce a surreal image of a desert with floating islands and a double sun in the sky.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family is the fastest image generation model to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture. It delivers state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Designed for real-time image generation without sacrificing quality, it runs on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is fully open under Apache 2.0 and can run on consumer GPUs like the RTX 3090/4070. The model supports text-to-image, image editing, and multi-reference editing at quality that exceeds its size. It is suitable for local development, edge deployment, and production use. The repository includes an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B, with the main repository containing full BF16 weights. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives. The model is available via the BFL API and in other formats. It can be used with the Diffusers Python library by installing or upgrading diffusers and using the Flux2KleinPipeline. The model is not intended to provide factual information and may produce inaccurate or distorted text. As a statistical model, it may represent or amplify biases from the training data and may fail to generate output that matches prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting or harming minors, to generate deceptive or harmful content, to generate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm individuals, to harass or abuse individuals or groups, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high-risk applications that adversely impact legal rights. Black Forest Labs is committed to responsible development and has implemented pre-training and post-training mitigations to prevent the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material and nonconsensual intimate imagery. Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with partnerships to filter known CSAM. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit harmful behaviors and suppress certain concepts. Ongoing evaluations were conducted with internal and external third-party assessments to identify and address risks. The final checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs and were released under Apache 2.0 for the 4B model and a non-commercial license for the 9B model. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content are included in the repository, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations. Content provenance features include pixel-layer watermarking and C2PA metadata to indicate AI-generated content. Policies govern acceptable use, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy. Black Forest Labs provides a dedicated email address for safety feedback and maintains relationships with organizations to address emerging risks.

instruction: Which passage aligns most closely with the user's inquiry about the impact of social media on mental health?
description: The granite-embedding-reranker-english-r2 is a 149M parameter dense cross-encoder model from the Granite Embeddings collection, designed to generate high-quality text embeddings. It produces embedding vectors of size 768 based on a context length of up to 8192 tokens. The model was trained using open-source relevance-pair datasets with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, as well as IBM-collected and generated datasets. It uses a cross-encoder architecture to compute relevance scores between queries and documents by jointly encoding their text, enabling precise reranking based on contextual alignment. The model is trained with ranking-specific loss functions such as pListMLE and model merging techniques to enhance performance. It shows strong performance on standard information retrieval benchmarks (BEIR, MIRACL), long-document search benchmarks (MLDR), and enterprise use cases. The model is based on the ModernBERT architecture, with an embedding size of 768, 22 layers, 12 attention heads, an intermediate size of 1152, a GeGLU activation function, a vocabulary size of 50368, and a maximum sequence length of 8192. It is designed to compute relevance scores for query-document pairs, making it well-suited for reranking tasks in information retrieval and search applications. The model is compatible with the SentenceTransformer library and can be used to jointly encode pairs of text to compute relevance scores. It is also compatible with the Huggingface Transformers library and can be used to encode pairs of text. The model can be used in conjunction with a retriever to produce an end-to-end search system. The model is trained using data from four key sources: unsupervised title-body paired data scraped from the web, publicly available paired data with permissive, enterprise-friendly licenses, IBM-internal paired data targeting specific technical domains, and IBM-generated synthetic data. Notably, the model does not use the MS-MARCO retrieval dataset due to its non-commercial license. The underlying encoder models use GneissWeb, an IBM-curated dataset composed exclusively of open, commercial-friendly sources. All data undergoes a data clearance process subject to technical, business, and governance review, capturing information about content description, ownership, intended use, data classification, licensing, usage restrictions, data acquisition, and an assessment of sensitive information. The model is trained using IBM's BlueVela Cluster, which is outfitted with NVIDIA H100 80gb GPUs. The data used to train the base language model was filtered to remove text containing hate, abuse, and profanity. The model is fine-tuned on English and has a context length of 8192 tokens (longer texts will be truncated to this size).

instruction: Can you describe the pattern of this rash and suggest possible treatments?
description: DermLIP is a vision-language model for dermatology, trained on the Derm1M dataset, which is the largest dermatological image-text corpus to date. The model is a pretrained vision-language model with a CLIP-style architecture. It includes a ViT-B16 vision encoder and a GPT2 text encoder, operating on images with a resolution of 224×224 pixels. The model was trained on 403,563 skin image-text pairs from the Derm1M dataset, which includes both dermoscopic and clinical images. The training objective was based on image-text contrastive loss, and the model was trained using an Nvidia H200 GPU with approximately 40GB memory usage, requiring about 5 hours of training time. DermLIP is intended for use in zero-shot classification, few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and concept annotation or explanation. The model is licensed under the cc-by-nc-nd-4.0 license.

instruction: Can you identify the type of lesion in this dermoscopic image?
description: DermLIP is a vision-language model for dermatology, trained on the Derm1M dataset, which is the largest dermatological image-text corpus to date. The model is a pretrained vision-language model with a CLIP-style architecture. It includes a ViT-B16 vision encoder and a GPT2 text encoder, operating on images with a resolution of 224×224 pixels. The model was trained on 403,563 skin image-text pairs from the Derm1M dataset, which includes both dermoscopic and clinical images. The training objective was based on image-text contrastive loss, and the model was trained using an Nvidia H200 GPU with approximately 40GB memory usage, requiring about 5 hours of training time. DermLIP is intended for use in zero-shot classification, few-shot learning, cross-modal retrieval, and concept annotation or explanation. The model is licensed under the cc-by-nc-nd-4.0 license.

instruction: 如何将用户输入的中文句子与数据库中的句子进行语义匹配？
description: This is a CoSENT (Cosine Sentence) model named shibing624/text2vec-base-chinese. It maps sentences to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for tasks like sentence embeddings, text matching, or semantic search. The model is trained using the CoSENT method based on the hfl/chinese-macbert-base architecture on the Chinese STS-B dataset. It achieves good performance on the Chinese STS-B test set. The model is available on the Hugging Face model hub and is recommended for general Chinese semantic matching tasks. The model outputs a vector that captures semantic information and can be used for information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated by default. The model architecture consists of a Transformer with a max sequence length of 128 and a Pooling layer that uses mean token pooling. The model is fine-tuned using a contrastive objective, where cosine similarity is computed for all possible sentence pairs in a batch, and a rank loss is applied by comparing true and false pairs. The training dataset is unspecified, and the maximum sequence length is set to 128. The best performance was achieved at epoch 5, and the sentence embedding dimension is 768. The model can be used with the text2vec library, HuggingFace Transformers, or the sentence-transformers library. It supports acceleration via ONNX and OpenVINO backends, with ONNX providing no performance loss on GPU and OpenVINO providing a 1.12x speedup on CPU. Quantization with OpenVINO (int8) results in a small performance hit on some tasks and a minor gain on others, with performance on CPU also improved. The model is intended for use as a sentence and short paragraph encoder.

instruction: Can you find the closest match in a database for the sentence 'I need help with my account setup'?
description: This is a model finetuned from distilbert/distilbert-base-uncased. It maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and can be used for semantic textual similarity, semantic search, paraphrase mining, text classification, clustering, and more. The model is a Sentence Transformer with a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens and an output dimensionality of 768 dimensions. The similarity function used is Cosine Similarity. The full model architecture includes a Transformer with a DistilBertModel architecture and a Pooling layer with a word embedding dimension of 768. The pooling configuration includes pooling_mode_mean_tokens set to True and all other pooling modes set to False. The model was trained using Python 3.12.8, Sentence Transformers 5.1.0, Transformers 4.55.4, PyTorch 2.8.0, Accelerate 1.5.2, Datasets 3.5.0, and Tokenizers 0.21.1.

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'notes' section provided for each quant type?
description: This is a vision model, and if applicable, mmproj files will be provided. Weighted imatrix quantizations are available. Static quantizations are also available for download. For users unfamiliar with GGUF files, guidance is available on how to use them, including instructions for concatenating multi-part files. A variety of quantized versions are provided, sorted by size but not necessarily by quality. IQ-quants are often considered preferable to similar-sized non-IQ quants. The available quant types include i1-IQ1_S, i1-IQ1_M, i1-IQ2_XXS, i1-IQ2_XS, i1-IQ2_S, i1-IQ2_M, i1-Q2_K_S, i1-IQ3_XXS, i1-Q2_K, i1-IQ3_XS, i1-IQ3_S, i1-Q3_K_S, i1-IQ3_M, i1-Q3_K_M, i1-Q3_K_L, i1-IQ4_XS, i1-IQ4_NL, i1-Q4_0, i1-Q4_K_S, i1-Q4_K_M, i1-Q4_1, i1-Q5_K_S, i1-Q5_K_M, and i1-Q6_K. Each quant type has a specified size in gigabytes and associated notes. For example, the i1-IQ1_S quant is 1.2 GB and is described as suitable for the desperate, while the i1-Q4_K_M quant is 2.6 GB and is described as fast and recommended. An imatrix file is also available for creating custom quants, and it is 0.1 GB in size. A graph by ikawrakow compares some lower-quality quant types, and Artefact2 has provided additional commentary on the topic. Users are directed to a resource for frequently asked questions and for requesting other models to be quantized. The model developer acknowledges their company for providing server access and workstation upgrades to support this work, as well as another individual for granting access to a private supercomputer, which enabled the production of higher-quality imatrix quants.

instruction: I have a voice memo; can you transcribe the words into text for me?
description: The LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B-8bit model is a multimodal audio model designed for a range of audio-related tasks, including Text-to-Speech (TTS), Speech-to-Text (ASR), and Speech-to-Speech (STS) conversion. It supports interleaved generation of text and audio in a single turn and enables real-time, token-by-token generation for low-latency applications. The model is based on the LFM2.5-Audio architecture and has been converted to MLX format using mlx-audio version 0.3.0. It features a 1.5B parameter Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) backbone, a Conformer-based audio encoder for processing input audio, a Depthformer-based audio decoder for generating audio codes, and an ISTFT-based neural detokenizer for reconstructing audio waveforms. The model also supports an alternative decoding method using the Mimi neural codec. It is capable of handling both text and audio inputs and outputs, with the ability to generate responses that include text, audio, or a combination of both. The model is trained to process audio at a 24kHz sample rate and provides configuration options for generation parameters such as max_new_tokens, temperature, top_k, and top_p for both text and audio outputs. The model includes a ChatState system for managing conversation turns and supports streaming generation for real-time audio playback. The model is available for use via the mlx-audio library, which can be installed using pip. The model's license follows the terms of the original LFM2.5-Audio model.

instruction: Create a caption that highlights the colors in this image of a rainbow over a lake.
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed for both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is pretrained on the COCO dataset using a base architecture with a Vision Transformer (ViT) base backbone. The model supports conditional and unconditional image captioning, allowing users to generate captions based on an image or a given text prompt. BLIP is designed to transfer flexibly to a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval, image captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). It achieves state-of-the-art results, with improvements of +2.7% in average recall@1 for image-text retrieval, +2.8% in CIDEr for image captioning, and +1.6% in VQA score. Additionally, BLIP demonstrates strong generalization ability when applied to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model utilizes noisy web data by bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. This release is intended for research purposes in support of an academic paper. The model, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model is released under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license.

instruction: Could you change the style of this photo to look like it was painted in a watercolor style?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit is an image editing model built upon the 20B Qwen-Image model. It extends Qwen-Image's text rendering capabilities to support image editing tasks, enabling precise text editing. The model simultaneously processes input images through Qwen2.5-VL for visual semantic control and the VAE Encoder for visual appearance control, allowing both semantic and appearance editing. Semantic editing includes tasks such as IP creation, object rotation, and style transfer, where the overall pixel content may change while maintaining semantic consistency. Appearance editing involves low-level modifications such as adding, removing, or modifying specific elements while keeping other regions unchanged. Qwen-Image-Edit supports bilingual (Chinese and English) text editing, allowing direct addition, deletion, and modification of text in images while preserving the original font, size, and style. Evaluations on multiple public benchmarks show that Qwen-Image-Edit achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in image editing tasks, making it a powerful foundation model for the field. The model is capable of tasks such as novel view synthesis, object rotation, style transfer, and detailed appearance modifications, including the addition of signboards with reflections and the removal of fine hair strands. It also supports chained editing approaches for progressive correction of errors in generated content, such as calligraphy artworks. Qwen-Image-Edit is licensed under Apache 2.0.

instruction: Evaluate how relevant this document page is to the question about customer satisfaction metrics.
description: The `llama-nemotron-rerank-vl-1b-v2` is a multimodal reranking model developed by NVIDIA for question-answering retrieval. It is designed to provide a logit score that represents the relevance of a document page to a given query. The model can process documents in the form of image, text, or a combination of both. The expected images are screenshots of document pages or slides, and the model supports images containing text, tables, charts, and infographics. The reranking model is a key component of a multimodal retrieval system, such as a vision RAG pipeline, where it helps improve overall accuracy by reranking top candidate documents retrieved by embedding models. The model is ready for commercial use and is suitable for users who want to build a multimodal question-and-answer application over a large corpus, leveraging the latest dense retrieval technologies. The model is based on the Eagle VLM architecture with a SigLIP 2 400M vision encoder and the Llama 3.2 1B language model. It is a cross-encoder model with approximately 1.7B parameters, fine-tuned for the ranking task. The final embedding is aggregated using a mean pooling strategy, and a binary classification head is used to produce the logit output. The model was fine-tuned exclusively on image data with a maximum of 4 input tiles and a maximum context length of 2048 tokens. For evaluation, it was tested on image-only, image+text, and text-only inputs with a maximum of 6 input tiles and a maximum context length of 10240 tokens. Inputs exceeding the maximum length are truncated. The model outputs a list of floats, where each value corresponds to a raw logit. Users can apply a Sigmoid activation function to convert the logits into probabilities. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference. The model requires transformers version 4.47.1 or higher and flash-attention 2.6.3 or higher. It is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, and Blackwell microarchitectures and is supported on Linux. The model was fine-tuned using publicly available image datasets and synthetic queries generated from proprietary models. The training data includes a mix of automated, human, and synthetic data collection and labeling methods. The model was evaluated on the ViDoRe V1, V2, and V3 benchmarks, as well as two internal visual retrieval datasets: DigitalCorpora-10k and Earnings V2. On these benchmarks, the model achieved an average Recall@5 improvement of approximately 7.2% for text modality, 6.9% for image modality, and 6% for image+text modality compared to the VLM embedding baseline. The model also demonstrated competitive performance on text retrieval benchmarks, comparable to NVIDIA's text-only reranking model. The model is designed to be used in conjunction with the `llama-nemotron-embed-vl-1b-v2` embedding model. The model is intended for use in document reranking for retrieval, where user queries can be text and documents can be text, document page images, charts, tables, and infographics. The model does not generate or reverse engineer personal data, and no personal data was used to create the model. The dataset is reviewed upon addition and as needed. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is intended for use by generative AI developers working with conversational AI models. The model has technical limitations, including a maximum sequence length of 10240 tokens. Longer text inputs should be truncated. The model does not always guarantee to provide a meaningful ranking of documents for a given query. The use of the model is governed by specific terms of use, and the post-processing scripts are licensed under a separate license. The model is built with Llama.

instruction: What are the top research papers on the application of neural networks in medical diagnostics?
description: The Diver Retriever 4B model is a reasoning-intensive model designed to tackle the challenge of reasonIR and rader. It is a text embedding model that supports bilingual (Chinese and English) processing with a context length of 40,000 tokens and contains 4 billion parameters. The model was trained using data from the fields of mathematics, coding, and healthcare, with careful attention to the difficulty level of the samples and the construction of unique negative samples for each field. As a result, the model performs very well on the Bright LeaderBoard and the Mteb-Medical Benchmark. Evaluation results show that the model achieves a BRIGHT score of 28.9, outperforming several other models in various categories including biology, earth science, economics, psychology, robotics, stack overflow, sustainability, leetcode, pony, AoPS, theoretical questions, and theoretical topics. The model can be used for inference tasks such as encoding queries and documents to compute cosine similarity, and it supports both Sentence Transformers and Transformers libraries for implementation. For finetuning, the model can be trained using the infonce loss with appropriate configuration and dependencies. The model is available for download from HuggingFace and ModelScope.

instruction: How can I switch between text and voice in a conversation without any lag?
description: LFM2-Audio-1.5B is the first end-to-end audio foundation model from Liquid AI. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, it enables seamless conversational interaction at 1.5 billion parameters, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. LFM2-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model that does not require separate ASR and TTS components. The model consists of a pretrained LFM2 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and a RQ-transformer generating discrete Mimi tokens as audio output. LFM2-Audio supports two distinct generation routines: interleaved generation, which enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities where audio generation latency is key, and sequential generation, which is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters in the language model component, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolutional and attention-based backbone, an audio tokenizer using 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. It is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. The model achieves a VoiceBench score of 56.78, with AlpacaEval 3.71, CommonEval 3.49, WildVoice 3.17, SD-QA 30.56, MMSU 31.95, OBQA 44.40, BBH 30.54, IFEval 98.85, and ADVBench 67.33. For ASR, it has a word error rate (WER) of 7.24, with Open 15.58, AMI 10.67, GigaSpeech 2.01, LibriSpeech-clean 4.39, LibriSpeech-other 3.56, and TED-LIUM 3.56. The model is suitable for multi-turn, multi-modal chat, where it can switch between audio and text modalities. It is available via the `liquid-audio` package and can be run using a Gradio demo interface. The model is intended for use with the LFM Open License v1.0, and the audio encoder is based on code licensed under other terms. The model is described in the LFM2 Technical Report, arXiv:2511.23404.

instruction: This video has some motion artifacts. How can I remove them and stabilize the footage?
description: SeedVR is a diffusion transformer model designed for generic video restoration. It addresses the limitations of conventional restoration models, which often perform poorly on real-world and AIGC (AI-generated content) video restoration due to limited generation ability. While recent diffusion-based models have improved performance by incorporating diffusion prior via ControlNet-like or adaptor-like architectures, these methods are constrained by the diffusion prior, leading to issues such as limited generation ability on small texts and faces, and fixed-resolution processing (e.g., 512 or 1024). These models also typically rely on patch-based sampling, which involves dividing the input video into overlapping spatial-temporal patches and fusing them using a Gaussian kernel at each diffusion step. The large overlap required for coherent output often results in slow inference speed, especially for long videos at high resolutions. SeedVR tackles the key challenge in diffusion-based restoration by enabling arbitrary-resolution restoration without relying on any pretrained diffusion prior. It introduces advanced video generation technologies suitable for video restoration and follows state-of-the-art video generation training pipelines. SeedVR is described as the largest-ever diffusion transformer model for generic video restoration and is intended to push the frontiers of advanced video restoration and inspire future research in large vision models for real-world video restoration. However, these are prototype models, and their performance may not perfectly align with the paper. The methods are sometimes not robust to heavy degradations and very large motions, and they share some failure cases with existing methods, such as failing to fully remove degradation or generating unpleasing details. Additionally, due to their strong generation ability, the methods may overly generate details on inputs with very light degradations, such as 720p AIGC videos, occasionally leading to oversharpened results.

instruction: What is the most prominent object in this photo of a forest?
description: The Vision Transformer (ViT) is a transformer encoder model, similar to BERT, that was pre-trained on a large collection of images in a supervised manner using the ImageNet-21k dataset, which contains 14 million images across 21,843 classes. The model processes images at a resolution of 224x224 pixels by dividing them into fixed-size patches of 16x16 pixels, which are linearly embedded. A [CLS] token is added to the beginning of the sequence for classification tasks, and absolute position embeddings are included before the sequence is fed into the Transformer encoder layers. The model does not include any fine-tuned heads, as these were set to zero by the researchers, but it does retain the pre-trained pooler, which can be used for downstream tasks such as image classification. Through pre-training, the model learns an internal representation of images that can be used to extract features for downstream tasks, such as training a standard classifier by placing a linear layer on top of the pre-trained encoder. The [CLS] token's last hidden state is typically used as a representation of the entire image. The model was pre-trained on TPUv3 hardware using 8 cores, with a batch size of 4096 and a learning rate warmup of 10,000 steps. During training, images were resized and normalized to 224x224 resolution with mean (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) and standard deviation (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) across the RGB channels. Evaluation results on image classification benchmarks are detailed in the original paper, with the best fine-tuning results achieved at a higher resolution of 384x384. Larger model sizes are associated with better performance. The model can be used for image classification, and fine-tuned versions for specific tasks are available. The model was introduced in a paper by Dosovitskiy et al. and the weights were converted from JAX to PyTorch by Ross Wightman. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original team did not provide one.

instruction: How do I evaluate the quality of the reconstructed audio after decoding?
description: X-Codec2 is a neural audio codec designed to improve speech synthesis and general audio generation for large language model (LLM) pipelines. It extends the original X-Codec by refining how semantic and acoustic information is integrated and tokenized, enabling efficient and high-fidelity audio representation. The architecture of X-Codec2 is based on X-Codec with several major differences. It uses a unified semantic-acoustic tokenization approach, fusing outputs from a semantic encoder (e.g., Wav2Vec2-BERT) and an acoustic encoder into a single embedding, capturing both high-level meaning (e.g., text content, emotion) and low-level audio details (e.g., timbre). It employs a single-stage vector quantization (VQ) using a single-layer Feature-Space Quantization (FSQ), which provides stability and compatibility with causal, autoregressive LLMs, in contrast to the multi-layer residual VQ used in most approaches. X-Codec2 includes semantic supervision during training, adding a semantic reconstruction loss to ensure that the discrete tokens preserve meaningful linguistic and emotional information, which is crucial for TTS tasks. Its 1D token structure is designed to align with the autoregressive modeling in LLMs like LLaMA, improving training efficiency and downstream compatibility. The model supports encoding and decoding of audio, with the ability to extract audio codes and reconstruct audio values. It is intended for use in LLM pipelines and is compatible with the Transformers library, though it is not yet merged into the main branch. Users can install it from source and use it with Python code to load the model, process audio data, and perform encoding and decoding operations. The model was contributed by external contributors, and the original code and checkpoints are available for reference.

instruction: Can you upscale this image of a dish and make the food look more appetizing?
description: The model is a work in progress, with checkpoints being uploaded incrementally. The current best version uses LoRA A: qwen-edit-enhance_64-v3_000001000 and LoRA B: qwen-edit-enhance_000004250. The upcoming V4 candidates are LoRA A: /1000 and LoRA B: /4750. LoRA A and LoRA B are intended to be applied sequentially. The model is used in a Comfy workflow and includes a demonstration available on 🤗. The model is designed to enhance image quality and generate a detailed textual description of the scene, with more descriptive prompts leading to better upscale effects. All images shown are generated using an 8-step Lighting LoRA. The ModelSamplingAuraFlow is required, and the shift parameter must remain below 0.3; for higher resolutions, such as image 3, it can be set as low as 0.02. Recommended samplers include LCM (best), Euler_Ancestral, and Euler. All schedulers are compatible and produce varying results in terms of smoothness. The model was trained on a filtered subset of Unsplash-Lite and UltraHR-100K datasets at 2.4MP resolution. The training style focuses on photography, with subjects including landscapes, architecture, interiors, portraits, plants, vehicles, abstract photos, man-made objects, and food. The model is trained to recover from low-resolution images up to 16x, oversharpened images, noise up to 50%, Gaussian blur with a radius up to 3px, JPEG artifacts with quality as low as 5%, motion blur up to 64px, pixelation up to 16x, and color banding up to 3 bits. It is also trained to recover images that have been upscaled by other models up to 16x. The model is not trained on 2D images, illustrations, artworks, text, graphics, or CGI.

instruction: Would it be possible to get a smooth, natural-sounding 44.1kHz audio version of this dialogue?
description: Anime-XCodec2-44.1kHz-v2 is a fine-tuned variant of NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2, designed to produce 44.1kHz high-fidelity Japanese speech, particularly in anime and game-style voices. It incorporates upsampling layers and RMS loss, inspired by Inworld TTS-1, and was trained on approximately 22,000 hours of Japanese speech. This version includes updates to the upsampler parameters, loss configurations, and a fix for a RoPE bug from the original XCodec2. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, ensuring speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for systems already using XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model accepts 16kHz input and outputs 44.1kHz audio, and can be used for decoding XCodec2 speech tokens into high-fidelity Japanese speech, upgrading existing pipelines to 44.1kHz output, or as a form of audio super-resolution, though the latter use is untested. The model modifies the original XCodec2 architecture and requires a custom xcodec2 library version (v0.1.7 or later) for inference, as older versions will not work. The model is optimized for Japanese and anime/game-style voices, and may perform poorly on other languages or domains. It is based on NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2 (16kHz), with modifications including the integration of an UpSamplerBlock into the decoder, the adoption of RMS Loss in addition to original losses, and the freezing of the encoder and codebook. Key updates in v2 include a RoPE bug fix, changes to upsampler settings, a switch in the perceptual loss model, and adjustments to the spectral discriminator for 44.1kHz audio. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, inherited from XCodec2 and Anime-XCodec2. It acknowledges HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 as the original model, Inworld AI for upsampler architecture and RMS Loss, imprt for the kushinada-hubert-large model used in perceptual loss, and the community for Japanese speech resources.

instruction: Can you help me find the most relevant documents for a legal case by improving the search results?
description: The zeroentropy/zerank-1-small is a 1.7B parameter reranker model, serving as a smaller version of the flagship model. Despite being over 2x smaller, it maintains nearly the same level of performance, outperforming other popular rerankers and showing significant accuracy improvements over traditional vector search. The model is designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems and is intended for use in search engines. It is released under the open-source Apache 2.0 license to support the open-source community and advance the capabilities of open-source models. The model can be used for reranking tasks and is compatible with the sentence_transformers library as a CrossEncoder. Evaluations show that the model achieves high NDCG@10 scores across various tasks, including Code, Conversational, Finance, Legal, Medical, and STEM. For example, in the Code task, it achieves an NDCG@10 score of 0.730, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.678 and other rerankers. In the Conversational task, it achieves 0.556, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.250. In the Finance task, it achieves 0.861, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.839. In the Legal task, it achieves 0.817, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.703. In the Medical task, it achieves 0.773, outperforming the embedding baseline of 0.619. In the STEM task, it achieves 0.680, surpassing the embedding baseline of 0.401. The model also outperforms other rerankers such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and Salesforce/Llama-rank-v1 in these tasks. The model can be used in conjunction with an initial retriever, such as OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small, to rerank the top 100 candidate documents. The model can be used for inference via ZeroEntropy's endpoint.

instruction: I need help assigning a label to each of these data rows—can you provide a classification?
description: Mitra classifier is a tabular foundation model that is pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random classifiers. It is based on a 12-layer Transformer with 72 million parameters, pre-trained by incorporating an in-context learning paradigm. The model is designed for use in tabular data classification tasks and supports both inference and fine-tuning. For inference, the model can be used without fine-tuning, and for fine-tuning, a specified number of training steps can be applied. The model is implemented within the AutoGluon.tabular framework and can be trained and evaluated using standard machine learning workflows, including train/test splits and performance evaluation. The model is licensed under the Apache-2.0 License. The model's development and methodology are described in the research paper titled 'Mitra: Mixed synthetic priors for enhancing tabular foundation models' by Zhang et al., published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: Can you convert this Thai paragraph into German while preserving the tone?
description: The Hunyuan Translation Model includes a translation model, Hunyuan-MT-7B, and an ensemble model, Hunyuan-MT-Chimera. The translation model is used to translate source text into the target language, while the ensemble model integrates multiple translation outputs to produce a higher-quality result. It primarily supports mutual translation among 33 languages, including five ethnic minority languages in China. In the WMT25 competition, the model achieved first place in 30 out of the 31 language categories it participated in. Hunyuan-MT-7B achieves industry-leading performance among models of comparable scale. Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B is the industry’s first open-source translation ensemble model, elevating translation quality to a new level. A comprehensive training framework for translation models has been proposed, spanning from pretrain → cross-lingual pretraining (CPT) → supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → translation enhancement → ensemble refinement, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results for models of similar size. The model supports translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple non-Chinese languages. Specific supported languages include Chinese, English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, Uyghur, and Cantonese. The model can be used with the transformers library, and specific instructions are provided for loading and applying the model, including adjustments for fp8 quantized versions. The model is available for download on Hugging Face, and users are encouraged to use version v4.56.0 of the transformers library. For inference, a set of recommended parameters is provided, including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The model does not have a default system_prompt. The model was open-sourced in September 2025.

instruction: Can you recognize the lab activity shown in this 16-frame video?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of VideoMAE-v2 vit_base_patch16_224 for video classification on the FineBio dataset. The base model is VideoMAE-v2 (vit_base_patch16_224), and it has been fine-tuned on the FineBio dataset for a total of 32 classes. The model processes video clips consisting of 16 frames, each with a resolution of 224x224 pixels. The training configuration includes the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001, a batch size of 8 (effective: 24), and 50 training epochs. Additional training parameters include a weight decay of 0.05, a layer decay of 0.75, a drop path rate of 0.1, and 10 warmup epochs. The dataset used is a custom video dataset for laboratory action recognition, which contains 32 classes, but the sizes of the training, validation, and test sets are not provided. The best validation accuracy is not available. The model is intended for video classification tasks and is based on the VideoMAE-v2 architecture. Users are advised to cite the associated research paper if they use this model. For any questions or issues, users should open an issue in the model repository.

instruction: Identify the protein entities in this paragraph about molecular signaling pathways.
description: This model is a state-of-the-art fine-tuned transformer designed to deliver enterprise-grade accuracy for biomedical entity recognition. It is specialized for identifying and extracting various biomedical entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is production-ready and suitable for clinical and research applications. The model can identify and classify biomedical entities including B-protein, B-protein_complex, B-protein_enum, B-protein_familiy_or_group, B-protein_variant, I-protein, I-protein_complex, I-protein_enum, I-protein_familiy_or_group, and I-protein_variant. It is trained on the FSU dataset, a biomedical NER dataset focused on protein interactions and molecular biology entities for systems biology research. The dataset includes annotations for proteins, protein complexes, protein families, protein variants, and molecular interaction entities relevant to systems biology and biochemistry research. The model is based on the deberta-v3-small architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling, and validated through cross-validation on a held-out test set. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.97. It is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: Could you provide a depth map for this nature scene?
description: DPT-Large, also known as MiDaS 3.0, is a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT) model trained on 1.4 million images for monocular depth estimation. It was introduced in the paper by Ranftl et al. (2021) and first released in March 2022. DPT uses the Vision Transformer (ViT) as a backbone and adds a neck and head on top for monocular depth estimation. The model is designed for zero-shot monocular depth estimation and can be used by anyone performing such tasks. It is typically intended to be fine-tuned for specific applications and should not be used to intentionally create hostile or alienating environments for people. The model was trained on a dataset called MIX 6, which contains around 1.4 million images, and was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. During training, images were resized such that the longer side was 384 pixels, and random square crops of size 384 were used, with random horizontal flips for data augmentation. The model was evaluated on several benchmarks, including DIW WHDR, ETH3D AbsRel, Sintel AbsRel, KITTI δ>1.25, NYU δ>1.25, and TUM δ>1.25, achieving results of 10.82, 0.089, 0.270, 8.46, 8.32, and 9.97, respectively. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 license. The training and inference were conducted on an Intel Xeon Platinum 8280 CPU with 8 physical cores and an NVIDIA RTX 2080 GPU. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The extent of the risks involved by using the model remains unknown, and no additional risk mitigation strategies were considered during model development. The model is not intended to inform decisions central to human life or flourishing.

instruction: Can you generate a voice recording of this article summary so I can listen to it during my walk?
description: Kyutai TTS is a streaming text-to-speech (TTS) model designed to generate audio as soon as the first few words of the input text are provided, rather than requiring the full text to produce audio. It is trained on a mix of public TTS datasets to enable fair comparisons with other methods. The model architecture is a hierarchical Transformer that processes tokenized text and generates audio tokenized by Mimi. The frame rate is 12.5 Hz, with each audio frame represented by 16 audio tokens, and the model cannot use fewer tokens during inference. The backbone model has 300M parameters, while the depth transformer has 450M parameters and uses partial weight sharing. The audio is shifted by 16 steps (1.28 seconds) relative to the text, and the model uses an acoustic/semantic delay of 2. Kyutai TTS is a decoder-only model for streaming speech-to-text, leveraging a multistream architecture to model the text stream based on the speech stream, with the text stream shifted relative to the audio stream to allow the model to predict text tokens based on the input audio. It supports streaming TTS generation and allows for voice cloning through prefixing, achieving a speaker similarity score of 74.9%, which is in line with some existing baselines like CSM. The model does not perform watermarking because watermarking can be easily deactivated for open source models, and early experiments show that all watermark systems used by existing TTS models are removed by simply encoding and decoding the audio with Mimi. The model is provided primarily for scientific comparisons on public benchmarks. It is developed by Kyutai, is a streaming text-to-speech model, supports English, and is licensed under CC-BY 4.0.

instruction: Which quantization is most suitable for a low-power device with limited computational resources?
description: This model card describes imatrix quantizations of the LLaMA-Mesh model, using the llama.cpp release b4132 for quantization. The model is available in various quantization formats, each with different file sizes, quality levels, and performance characteristics. The quantizations are generated using the imatrix option with a dataset provided by an unspecified source. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports a range of quant types, including f16, Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_K_L, Q4_K_M, Q3_K_XL, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, Q4_0_8_8, Q4_0_4_8, Q4_0_4_4, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, IQ3_M, Q2_K_L, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, Q2_K, and IQ2_M. Each quant type is described with its file size, quality level, and specific recommendations or caveats. For example, Q6_K and Q6_K_L are described as very high quality and recommended, while Q4_0_8_8 is optimized for ARM and AVX inference and should not be used on Mac. The Q4_0_4_4 quant is optimized for ARM inference and should not be used with GPUs. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for targeting specific files and handling large models split into multiple files. The Q4_0_X_X quantizations are not suitable for Metal (Apple) or GPU offloading and are intended for ARM chips and certain AVX2/AVX512 CPUs. On AVX2 systems, the Q4_0_8_8 quant offers improved performance for prompt processing and text generation compared to the standard Q4_0 quant. When choosing a quantization, users should consider their available RAM and VRAM, as well as their preference for speed versus quality. K-quants, such as Q5_K_M, are recommended for general use, while I-quants, such as IQ3_M, offer better performance for their size but may be slower on CPU and Apple Metal. I-quants are not compatible with Vulcan on AMD hardware. The model includes credits to kalomaze and Dampf for assistance in creating the imatrix calibration dataset and to ZeroWw for inspiration in experimenting with embedding and output weights.

instruction: What are the best practices for upscaling a video for VR playback with high frame consistency?
description: ComfyUI-SeedVR2_VideoUpscaler is a high-quality video and image upscaling model designed for use with ComfyUI. It supports both ComfyUI integration and standalone CLI operation, enabling users to upscale images and videos with diffusion-based techniques. The model is available in multiple variants, including 3B and 7B parameter models, with options for FP16, FP8, and GGUF quantization to accommodate different VRAM requirements. It features temporal consistency to maintain coherence across video frames, multi-format support for RGB and RGBA, and any video length compatibility. The model includes memory optimization techniques such as BlockSwap technology, VAE tiling, and intelligent offloading to reduce VRAM usage. Performance features include torch.compile integration for speed improvements, multi-GPU CLI support, and model caching for faster batch processing. Quality control options include advanced color correction methods and noise injection controls. The model can be installed via ComfyUI Manager or manually, with models automatically downloaded from HuggingFace. It supports a modular node architecture in ComfyUI, including dedicated nodes for DiT model, VAE model, torch.compile settings, and the main upscaler. The standalone CLI provides batch processing capabilities, multi-GPU support, and sophisticated optimization options. The model has specific limitations, including a required batch_size following the 4n+1 formula and potential VRAM bottlenecks during VAE encoding/decoding. Best practices include starting with debug mode enabled, using appropriate optimization settings for VRAM constraints, and testing with short clips before processing long videos.

instruction: I need to know the positions of all titles in this document.
description: This repository hosts three YOLOv11 models (nano, small, and medium) fine-tuned for high-performance Document Layout Analysis. The models are designed to accurately detect and classify key layout elements in a document, such as text, tables, figures, and titles. This is a fundamental task for document understanding and information extraction pipelines. The models are trained on the DocLayNet dataset to recognize 11 distinct layout types: Text, Title, Section-header, Table, Picture, Caption, List-item, Formula, Page-header, Page-footer, and Footnote. The recommended model is the `yolo11n` (nano) model, which offers an exceptional balance of speed and accuracy, making it ideal for production environments. The nano, small, and medium variants allow users to choose the best model for their use case. The nano model provides the best trade-off between speed and accuracy, while the small model is slightly more accurate, and the medium model provides the highest accuracy with a corresponding increase in computational cost. Performance gains are marginal when moving from the small to the medium model, making the nano and small variants the most practical choices. A deeper analysis revealed that the `train4` nano model stands out for its superior localization quality. It delivered significantly more accurate bounding boxes, with a +9.0% precision improvement for the `title` class and strong gains for `section-header` and `table`. It also achieved a +2.4% mAP50 and +2.05% mAP50-95 improvement for the difficult `footnote` class. The models were trained at 1280x1280 resolution, as initial tests at the default 640x640 resulted in a significant performance drop, especially for smaller elements like `footnote` and `caption`.

instruction: What objects in this image are related to cooking?
description: OWL-ViT, short for Vision Transformer for Open-World Localization, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model that allows querying an image with one or multiple text queries. It was proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, Austin Stone, Maxim Neumann, Dirk Weissenborn, Alexey Dosovitskiy, Aravindh Mahendran, Anurag Arnab, Mostafa Dehghani, Zhuoran Shen, Xiao Wang, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Kipf, and Neil Houlsby. OWL-ViT uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, combining a ViT-like Transformer for visual features and a causal language model for text features. To enable detection, the final token pooling layer of the vision model is removed, and a lightweight classification and box head is attached to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned end-to-end with the classification and box heads on standard detection datasets using a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-B/16 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads with an object detection objective. The CLIP backbone of the model was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including crawling a handful of websites and using commonly-used pre-existing image datasets. A large portion of the data comes from crawling the internet, making it more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model is intended as a research output for research communities. It is primarily intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The model is designed to enable researchers to explore zero-shot, text-conditioned object detection and to support interdisciplinary studies of the potential impact of such models, especially in areas that commonly require identifying objects whose label is unavailable during training.

instruction: Can you detect the pose of the individual in this low-resolution image and provide the joint positions?
description: YOLO26-X-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model is a native end-to-end architecture that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and results in faster, lighter, and easier deployment in real-world systems. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and enhances deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, and the removal of DFL to simplify inference and improve hardware compatibility. It achieves an mAP Pose of 71.6 (50-95, e2e) and 91.6 (50, e2e), with a CPU inference speed of 565.4 ms and a T4 TensorRT10 speed of 12.2 ms. The model has 57.6 million parameters and 201.7 billion FLOPs. It is optimized for edge computing, offering up to 43% faster CPU inference.

instruction: Can I get the final text of a speech after processing it in real time?
description: T-one is a high-performance streaming automatic speech recognition (ASR) pipeline for the Russian language, specialized for the telephony domain. It provides a complete low-latency solution for real-time transcription, featuring a pretrained streaming Conformer-based acoustic model, a custom phrase boundary detector, and a decoder. Developed by T-Software DC, T-one is a practical, low-latency, high-throughput ASR solution with modular components. It includes a full suite of tools for inference, fine-tuning, and deployment, making it a ready-to-use solution for production environments. The model is based on a 71M parameter acoustic model using the Conformer architecture, with innovations such as SwiGLU activation, SiLU (Swish) activations, RMSNorm, RoPE embeddings, a U-Net structure, attention score reuse, and efficient state management. It processes audio in 300 ms chunks and supports both greedy decoding and a KenLM-based CTC beam search decoder. T-one is primarily intended for use with telephone-channel audio but is robust across different domains due to its training on heterogeneous data. It supports streaming inference, allowing it to process long audio files in real time. The primary use case is streaming speech recognition of calls, where the model processes small audio chunks incrementally and returns finalized text and word-level timestamps in real time. T-one can be easily fine-tuned for specific domains using the 🤗 ecosystem. The model was trained on over 80,000 hours of Russian speech, with a significant portion pseudo-labeled using a robust ROVER model ensemble. Training was conducted for 7 days on 8 A100 GPUs using the NVIDIA NeMo framework with AdamW optimizer, cosine annealing with warmup, 16-bit mixed precision, and semi-sorted batching. T-one demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, especially on its target domain of telephony, while remaining competitive on general-purpose benchmarks. It provides a ready-to-use pipeline with examples for offline and streaming speech recognition inference, a demo for launching a local speech recognition service via Docker, and examples for deploying with Triton Inference Server for high-throughput scenarios. The model is fully open source, with all model and pipeline code available, and is released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: How can I convert high-quality audio into a more compact format suitable for mobile devices?
description: Distill-NeuCodec is a version of NeuCodec that features a compatible, distilled encoder. The distilled encoder is 10 times smaller in parameter count and uses approximately 7.5 times fewer MACs at inference time. The distilled model includes the following adjustments: the notoriously slow acoustic encoder is replaced with a new acoustic encoder, reducing the parameter count from 70 million to 36 million; the semantic encoder is replaced, reducing the parameter count from 600 million to 21 million. The model is developed by Neuphonic and is categorized as a Neural Audio Codec. It is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. The model was trained using the same data as the full model, with an additional distillation loss that computes the mean squared error between the outputs of the distilled and original encoders.

instruction: Could you identify key legal terms in this legislation draft?
description: LEGAL-BERT is a family of BERT models designed for the legal domain, intended to support legal NLP research, computational law, and legal technology applications. The models were pre-trained on 12 GB of diverse English legal text from various fields, including legislation, court cases, and contracts, collected from publicly available resources. Different sub-domain variants, such as CONTRACTS-BERT-BASE, EURLEX-BERT-BASE, and ECHR-BERT-BASE, as well as the general LEGAL-BERT-BASE, perform better than using BERT out of the box for domain-specific tasks. LEGAL-BERT-SMALL is a lightweight version of BERT-BASE, pre-trained from scratch on legal data, which is 33% the size of BERT-BASE. It achieves comparable performance to larger models while being approximately four times faster and having a smaller environmental footprint. The pre-training corpora include 116,062 documents of EU legislation from EURLEX, 61,826 documents of UK legislation, 19,867 cases from the European Court of Justice, 12,554 cases from the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR), 164,141 cases from various US courts, and 76,366 US contracts from EDGAR. LEGAL-BERT-BASE, also known as LEGAL-BERT-SC, was trained from scratch using a newly created vocabulary by a sentence-piece tokenizer trained on the same legal corpora. LEGAL-BERT-FP models, which rely on the original BERT-BASE checkpoint, have been released in Archive.org and are considered secondary. The models were trained using the official Google BERT code, with a setup of 1 million training steps, batches of 256 sequences of length 512, and an initial learning rate of 1e-4. Training was conducted on a single Google Cloud TPU v3-8. LEGAL-BERT variants have been evaluated on downstream tasks and are described in the paper 'LEGAL-BERT: The Muppets straight out of Law School' by Chalkidis et al. (2020).

instruction: Describe the spatial arrangement of objects in this 3D rendering and how they interact.
description: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that can operate PC/mobile GUIs, recognize elements, understand functions, and invoke tools to complete tasks. It supports visual coding by generating Draw.io/HTML/CSS/JS from images and videos. The model features advanced spatial perception, capable of judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with support for 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a long context and video understanding capability, with a native 256K context expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It has upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling it to recognize a wide range of subjects including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model also features expanded OCR, supporting 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare/ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing.
