The model's text understanding is on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for deployment using the vLLM framework with a recommended context length of 32768. It is available for download and can be run with a vLLM startup command that specifies GPU memory utilization, tensor parallel size, and other parameters. The model file size is 17GB, last updated on 2025-10-04. The model can be used for chat with the Transformers library, requiring specific installation and setup steps. The model is described in technical reports and academic articles published in 2023, 2024, and 2025.

instruction: What kind of trees or plants are visible in this outdoor scene?
description: The OWLv2 model, proposed by Matthias Minderer, Alexey Gritsenko, and Neil Houlsby, is a zero-shot text-conditioned object detection model designed to query an image with one or multiple text queries. It uses CLIP as its multi-modal backbone, with a ViT-like Transformer to extract visual features and a causal language model to extract text features. To enable detection, OWL-ViT removes the final token pooling layer of the vision model and attaches a lightweight classification and box head to each transformer output token. Open-vocabulary classification is achieved by replacing the fixed classification layer weights with class-name embeddings from the text model. The CLIP backbone is trained from scratch and fine-tuned together with the box and class prediction heads using an object detection objective and a bipartite matching loss. The model uses a CLIP backbone with a ViT-L/14 Transformer architecture as an image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as a text encoder. These encoders are trained to maximize the similarity of (image, text) pairs via a contrastive loss. The model is intended for use by AI researchers to better understand robustness, generalization, and other capabilities, biases, and constraints of computer vision models. The CLIP backbone was trained on publicly available image-caption data, including data crawled from the internet and pre-existing image datasets. The prediction heads of OWL-ViT, along with the CLIP backbone, are fine-tuned on publicly available object detection datasets. The model was released in June 2023.

instruction: What is the best match for this image in a database of product photos?
description: The Perception Encoder (PE) is a state-of-the-art encoder for image and video understanding, trained using a simple vision-language learning approach. It was introduced by Meta and is part of a family of large-scale vision encoder models with strong performance on a wide range of vision tasks. PE leverages a robust contrastive pretraining recipe and is finetuned on synthetically aligned videos, enabling it to outperform existing models on classification and retrieval tasks. It also produces strong, general features that scale well for downstream applications. PE core is the base model, trained with a robust image pretraining schedule and finetuned on data generated by a synthetic video data engine. PE core currently comes in three sizes: PE core G is the main checkpoint, with L and B models distilled from it. The L and B models include a class token for global aggregation in addition to an attention pooling block with 8 heads on top of the vision tower. PE core achieves strong results on zero-shot image classification and retrieval, as well as zero-shot video classification and retrieval. It performs particularly well on challenging benchmarks such as ObjectNet and ImageNet-A. The model is designed for image and text feature extraction and supports tasks such as classification and retrieval. It is based on a contrastive pretraining approach and is compatible with the OpenCLIP framework. The model is available for use through the OpenCLIP library and can be accessed via the provided GitHub repositories. The model is described in detail in the associated technical report and research papers.

instruction: I’m creating an audio guide and need a narration. Can you generate a voice recording from this description?
description: Kyutai TTS is a streaming text-to-speech (TTS) model designed to generate audio as soon as the first few words of the input text are provided, rather than requiring the full text to produce audio. It is trained on a mix of public TTS datasets to enable fair comparisons with other methods. The model architecture is a hierarchical Transformer that processes tokenized text and generates audio tokenized by Mimi. The frame rate is 12.5 Hz, with each audio frame represented by 16 audio tokens, and the model cannot use fewer tokens during inference. The backbone model has 300M parameters, while the depth transformer has 450M parameters and uses partial weight sharing. The audio is shifted by 16 steps (1.28 seconds) relative to the text, and the model uses an acoustic/semantic delay of 2. Kyutai TTS is a decoder-only model for streaming speech-to-text, leveraging a multistream architecture to model the text stream based on the speech stream, with the text stream shifted relative to the audio stream to allow the model to predict text tokens based on the input audio. It supports streaming TTS generation and allows for voice cloning through prefixing, achieving a speaker similarity score of 74.9%, which is in line with some existing baselines like CSM. The model does not perform watermarking because watermarking can be easily deactivated for open source models, and early experiments show that all watermark systems used by existing TTS models are removed by simply encoding and decoding the audio with Mimi. The model is provided primarily for scientific comparisons on public benchmarks. It is developed by Kyutai, is a streaming text-to-speech model, supports English, and is licensed under CC-BY 4.0.

instruction: Create a face that blends common facial traits with a touch of individuality.
description: This model generates realistic human faces at 128×128 resolution using a StyleGAN1 architecture trained with StyleGAN2 regularization techniques. The model is based on the FFHQ (Flickr-Faces-HQ) dataset, which contains 65,000 images at 128×128 resolution. The architecture includes features such as style mixing, noise injection, and the truncation trick. The model uses a latent dimension of 512 and is trained using Lazy R1 regularization and Path Length Regularization. Training details include the use of the Adam optimizer with β₁=0.0 and β₂=0.99, learning rates of 0.0002 for both the generator and discriminator, and a batch size of 32. Regularization is applied every 16 iterations with R1 gamma set to 5.0 and Path Length Regularization set to 1.0. The model supports tasks such as generating single or multiple faces, applying the truncation trick to balance quality and diversity, and performing style mixing to combine features from different latent codes. The model is a hybrid approach, combining the StyleGAN1 architecture with StyleGAN2 regularization techniques to achieve improved training stability. The resolution is limited to 128×128. The model is available under the MIT License.

instruction: What are some ethical concerns surrounding the use of AI in healthcare?
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. The raw parameter count of the model is 8B, but the architecture design allows the model to be run with a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 4B model by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator. The models use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the E4B model. One sub-model is provided, or a spectrum of custom-sized models can be accessed. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens, subtracting the request input tokens. Input images are normalized to 256x256, 512x512, or 768x768 resolution and encoded to 256 tokens each. Audio data is encoded to 6.25 tokens per second from a single channel. The models were trained on a dataset totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous filtering methods were applied to the training data, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. The models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware. Training was done using JAX and ML Pathways, which allow for efficient training of large models. The models were evaluated at full precision (float32) against a large collection of different datasets and metrics to cover different aspects of content generation. Evaluation results show performance improvements over previous Gemma models in categories such as child safety, content safety, and representational harms. The models have certain limitations, including potential biases or gaps in the training data, challenges with open-ended or highly complex tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and the possibility of generating incorrect or outdated factual statements. Ethical considerations include the risk of perpetuating biases, generating harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to implement appropriate content safety safeguards and adhere to privacy regulations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, and education. They provide superior performance compared to other, similarly sized open model alternatives.

instruction: I have a list of code examples. How can I determine which ones are most relevant to a specific programming problem?
description: ZeroEntropy has developed zerank-1, a state-of-the-art reranker designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems in search engines. This model outperforms closed-source competitors such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and Salesforce/LlamaRank-v1 across various domains, including finance, legal, code, STEM, medical, and conversational data. The model is part of an innovative multi-stage pipeline that models query-document relevance scores as adjusted values. The model is currently released under a non-commercial license, and commercial license inquiries should be directed to founders@zeroentropy.dev. A smaller version of the model is fully open-sourced under an Apache 2.0 License. Evaluations using NDCG@10 scores show that zerank-1 achieves strong performance across multiple tasks, including Code, Conversational, Finance, Legal, Medical, and STEM. For example, in the Finance domain, zerank-1 achieves an NDCG@10 score of 0.894, outperforming other models. In the STEM domain, it achieves a score of 0.694. The model is implemented as a CrossEncoder and can be used with the sentence_transformers library. It can also be accessed via ZeroEntropy's inference endpoint.

instruction: I have a French podcast snippet. Can you create a French transcript from it?
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a timestamp prediction token. The task token can be set to either transcribe or translate. If the tokens are forced, the model is made to predict each token at each position, allowing control over the output language and task. If un-forced, the model predicts the output language and task automatically. Whisper can be used for English to English transcription, French to French transcription, and French to English translation by setting the appropriate context tokens. The model is evaluated using datasets such as LibriSpeech, and performance is measured using metrics like word error rate (WER). Whisper Tiny, for example, achieves a WER of approximately 7.55% on the LibriSpeech test clean dataset. The model is designed to work on audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration, but can be extended to handle longer audio through a chunking algorithm. Whisper can also predict sequence-level timestamps by enabling the return_timestamps option. While the pre-trained model generalizes well, its performance can be improved through fine-tuning, especially for certain languages and tasks. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks and show strong results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities if fine-tuned for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. Users are advised to perform thorough evaluations before deployment. The models are trained on 680,000 hours of audio data collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, due to training on noisy, weakly supervised data, the models may generate hallucinations—texts that are not actually spoken in the audio. Performance is uneven across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The models may also exhibit higher word error rates for speakers of different genders, races, or ages. The sequence-to-sequence architecture makes the model prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be partially mitigated by beam search and temperature scheduling. The models are not recommended for use in high-risk domains or for subjective classification tasks. The broader implications of Whisper include potential use in accessibility tools and real-time speech recognition applications. However, the release of Whisper also raises concerns about dual use, including the potential for surveillance technologies. The models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns. The cost of transcription is not expected to be a limiting factor in scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: Which of these images is most closely related to the phrase 'a person reading a book'?
description: This is a CLIP model with an extra text depth and a vision MLP head, trained on the LAION-2B English subset. The model uses the ConvNeXt-Large architecture as the image tower, with a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) head in the vision tower instead of a single projection. The text tower has the same width but includes four additional layers, resulting in a depth of 16 and an embedding dimension of 768. The 320x320 resolution model is a weight-averaged combination (model soup) of three fine-tunes from the original 256x256 training run. Each fine-tune used a different learning rate (1e-4, 6e-5, 5e-5) and a different number of additional samples (3.2B, 2B, 2.5B). At 320x320 resolution, the ConvNext-Large-D model is more efficient than the L/14 model at 336x336 resolution, with 2.5x fewer GMACs, 2.8x fewer activations, and 1.22x fewer parameters. The model achieves between 75.9% and 76.9% top-1 zero-shot accuracy on ImageNet-1k. It supports tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, image classification fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. The model is intended for research purposes and is not recommended for deployment in any use case without thorough in-domain testing. It is not suitable for use in surveillance or facial recognition due to the lack of testing norms and checks for fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on languages other than English and is therefore limited to English language use cases. The model was trained using the LAION-2B dataset, which is a 2 billion sample English subset of LAION-5B. This dataset is uncurated and may contain disturbing content. It is recommended to use a safety filter to reduce the risk of encountering harmful content. The model was trained by Ross Wightman on a cluster using a global batch size of 131072 for 10-16 checkpoint intervals, with a total of approximately 2-3 billion samples seen over the fine-tune. The training procedure involved a slurm script on 64 8-GPU (A100 40GB) nodes with specific training parameters. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB+ datasets for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval.

instruction: Can you help me identify each person in this image and map their body parts accurately?
description: The BBox-Mask-Pose (BMP) method integrates detection, pose estimation, and segmentation into a self-improving loop by conditioning these tasks on each other. This approach enhances all three tasks simultaneously. Using segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes improves performance in crowded scenarios, making top-down methods competitive with bottom-up approaches. The method includes MaskPose, a pose estimation model conditioned by segmentation masks instead of bounding boxes, which boosts performance in dense scenes without adding parameters. The BBox-MaskPose (BMP) method links bounding boxes, segmentation masks, and poses to simultaneously address multi-body detection, segmentation, and pose estimation. A fine-tuned RTMDet is adapted for iterative detection, ignoring 'holes'. The system supports multi-dataset training of ViTPose, previously implemented in the official ViTPose repository but absent in MMPose. The ViTPose-b multi-dataset model uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. The model was trained on multiple datasets and performs better in multi-body and crowded scenarios than COCO-trained ViTPose. The MaskPose-b model also uses a ViT-b backbone with a multi-layer decoder, takes RGB images (192x256) and estimated instance segmentation as input, and outputs keypoint coordinates (48x64 heatmap for each keypoint, 21 keypoints). It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMPose framework. It was trained for 210 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 5e-5 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It uses training data including SAM-estimated instance masks. Compared to ViTPose, MaskPose takes instance segmentation as input and is better at distinguishing instances in multi-body scenes without computational overhead. The fine-tuned RTMDet-l model uses a CSPNeXt-P5 backbone, CSPNeXtPAFPN neck, and RTMDetInsSepBN head. It takes RGB images as input and outputs detected instances, including bounding boxes, instance masks, and class for each. It is a vision model not dependent on language, licensed under GPL-3.0, and implemented in the MMDetection framework. It was trained for 10 epochs with a batch size of 16 and a learning rate of 2e-2 using 4x NVIDIA A-100 hardware. It was trained on data with randomly masked-out instances and is designed for iterative detection, especially effective in multi-body scenes where background would not be detected otherwise.

instruction: Can you estimate the pose of individuals in a crowded scene?
description: Ultralytics YOLO26 is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, engineered from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. This is the pose estimation variant optimized for human keypoint detection. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. YOLO26 is a native end-to-end model, producing predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS). By eliminating this post-processing step, inference becomes faster, lighter, and easier to deploy in real-world systems. The end-to-end design cuts out an entire stage of the pipeline, dramatically simplifying integration, reducing latency, and making deployment more robust across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon — inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training. This optimizer brings enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed for pose estimation, DFL Removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference, optimized for edge computing. The model has an input size of 640 pixels, an mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 70.4, an mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 90.5, a CPU Speed (ONNX) of 275.4 ms, a T4 TensorRT10 Speed of 6.5 ms, 25.9M parameters, and 91.3B FLOPs.

instruction: I want to track a person's arm movement in real time—can you help with that?
description: The Person Pose Detection 256x480 model is a lightweight quantized tflite model developed by Synaptics for the SR100 processor in the Synaptics Astra™ SR MCU Series. It is designed to generate bounding boxes and identify 17 keypoints for each person, corresponding to various body parts. Each keypoint is accompanied by a confidence score that indicates the reliability of the detection, enabling accurate estimation of the person's pose. The model is optimized to fit in Flash and is also designed to fit in SRAM. It accepts an input size of 256x480 and outputs, for each detected person, bounding box coordinates, 17 keypoint locations, and confidence scores. The model is a single-class model, specifically designed for pose estimation of people. It is fully quantized in INT8 and is intended for real-time person pose estimation on embedded, resource-constrained edge devices. Typical use cases include human activity recognition and gesture control, fitness and wellness tracking, smart home automation based on body posture, and interactive applications and user interfaces. The model can be optimized for the Astra™ SR100 MCU using the Astra™ Machina Micro Dev Kit. Input images must be resized and quantized to match model requirements. The model is distributed under a license that allows flexible use, modification, and distribution. Evaluation and testing can be performed in a hosted environment optimized for the Synaptics SR110 MCU, which provides a seamless sandbox for model evaluation using hardware-specific quantization and runtime settings.

instruction: I want to analyze body movements in this image. Can you detect the key points?
description: YOLO26-M-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model operates with an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 68.8 for pose estimation across the 50-95 confidence range and 89.6 for the 50 confidence threshold. On CPU using ONNX, inference takes 218.0 milliseconds, while on T4 TensorRT10, it runs at 5.0 milliseconds. The model contains 21.5 million parameters and 73.1 billion FLOPs. YOLO26 is built on three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that eliminates the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), resulting in faster and lighter inference. The end-to-end design reduces pipeline complexity, lowers latency, and improves deployment robustness. The model introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. YOLO26-M-pose includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing.

instruction: Using customer data, how can I estimate the likelihood of a user churning within the next month?
description: Mitra regressor is a tabular foundation model that is pre-trained on purely synthetic datasets sampled from a mix of random regressors. It is based on a 12-layer Transformer with 72 million parameters, pre-trained by incorporating an in-context learning paradigm. The model is designed for regression tasks and supports inference and training on tabular data. To use the model, users can install AutoGluon and follow a workflow that includes loading a dataset, splitting it into training and test sets, converting it into a TabularDataset, and creating a TabularPredictor configured for regression. The model can be trained on a subset of the training data, and its performance can be evaluated using a leaderboard on the test data. The model is available under the Apache-2.0 License. The development of Mitra is described in the paper titled 'Mitra: Mixed synthetic priors for enhancing tabular foundation models,' published as an arXiv preprint in 2025.

instruction: Can you turn this speech into an audio file for a public address system?
description: VibeVoice-Realtime is a lightweight real-time text-to-speech model that supports streaming text input and robust long-form speech generation. It is designed to enable real-time TTS services, narrate live data streams, and allow different LLMs to start producing speech from their first tokens before a full answer is generated. The model produces initial audible speech in approximately 300 milliseconds, depending on hardware. Although primarily built for English, it exhibits some level of multilingual capability and performs reasonably well in additional languages including German, French, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, and Spanish. The model uses an interleaved, windowed design that incrementally encodes incoming text chunks while continuing diffusion-based acoustic latent generation from prior context. It relies solely on an efficient acoustic tokenizer operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz. The model has a parameter size of 0.5B and is deployment-friendly. It supports only a single speaker and is currently intended for English speech only; other languages may produce unpredictable results. The model integrates a transformer-based LLM with a specialized acoustic tokenizer and a diffusion-based decoding head. The acoustic tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks and achieves 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. The diffusion head is a lightweight module with 4 layers and approximately 40M parameters, conditioned on LLM hidden states, and uses a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process with Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 8,192 tokens. The acoustic tokenizer is pre-trained, and during VibeVoice training, only the LLM and diffusion head parameters are trained using a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k to 8K). The model achieves satisfactory performance on short-sentence benchmarks and is focused on long-form speech generation. On the LibriSpeech test-clean set, it achieves a WER of 2.00% and a speaker similarity of 0.695. On the SEED test-en set, it achieves a WER of 2.05% and a speaker similarity of 0.633. The model is intended for research purposes exploring real-time highly realistic audio generation. It is not intended for use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations, including trade compliance laws, or for use in any other way that is prohibited by the MIT License. It is not intended for generating text transcripts or for use in scenarios such as voice impersonation, disinformation, real-time voice conversion, circumventing technical safeguards, unsupported languages, or generating background ambience, Foley, or music. The model may inherit any biases, errors, or omissions from its base model, Qwen2.5 0.5b. It may produce unexpected, biased, or inaccurate outputs. High-quality synthetic speech can be misused for impersonation, fraud, or disinformation. Users must ensure transcripts are reliable, check content accuracy, and avoid using generated content in misleading ways. The model does not support reading code, mathematical formulas, or uncommon symbols. It does not handle overlapping speech segments or non-speech audio. We do not recommend using VibeVoice in commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. We recommend disclosing to end users that they are listening to AI-generated content. The model is intended for research and development purposes only. To mitigate misuse, the acoustic tokenizer has been removed to prevent users from creating embeddings, an audible disclaimer is automatically embedded into every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added to generated audio for verification. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and being mindful of data privacy concerns.

instruction: Could you provide a visual representation of this image suitable for depth estimation tasks?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN classifiers on the class token, logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation using linear layers, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) consists of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) consists of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, image retrieval, 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification. Evaluation results show that the ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on web data achieve high scores on IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair. The ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data also achieve strong performance on similar tasks. The ViT backbones pretrained or distilled on satellite data achieve high scores on GEO-Bench classification and segmentation tasks. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time and an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Can you provide a depth estimation for this image to help with a 3D reconstruction project?
description: DA3MONO-LARGE is a monocular depth estimation model designed for high-quality relative depth prediction. It directly predicts depth, which results in superior geometric accuracy compared to disparity-based models like Depth Anything 2. The model is part of the Depth Anything 3 series and is based on a plain transformer architecture with a unified depth-ray representation. It supports tasks such as relative depth estimation and sky segmentation. The model has 0.35B parameters and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It was developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. The model outperforms Depth Anything 2 in monocular depth estimation and VGGT in multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images, and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: I need this email translated from German to Italian for a business client—can you help?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes two models: HY-MT1.5-1.8B, a 1.8B parameter translation model, and HY-MT1.5-7B, a 7B parameter translation model. Both models support mutual translation across 33 languages and include 5 ethnic and dialect variations. HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios. It supports terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. HY-MT1.5-1.8B, despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, delivers comparable translation performance, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and supports real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. It supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios compared to its September open-source version. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. The models are available in multiple quantization formats, including FP8 and Int4. The models support a wide range of languages, including Chinese, English, French, Portuguese, Spanish, Japanese, Turkish, Russian, Arabic, Korean, Thai, Italian, German, Vietnamese, Malay, Indonesian, Filipino, Hindi, Traditional Chinese, Polish, Czech, Dutch, Khmer, Burmese, Persian, Gujarati, Urdu, Telugu, Marathi, Hebrew, Bengali, Tamil, Ukrainian, Tibetan, Kazakh, Mongolian, and Uyghur. The models can be used with the transformers library, and specific parameters are recommended for inference. The models do not have a default system prompt. The HY-MT1.5-1.8B and HY-MT1.5-7B models were open-sourced on Hugging Face in December 2025 and September 2025, respectively. The technical report provides more experimental results and analysis.

instruction: Could you convert this technical blog post from English to Sindhi, making sure the code comments are translated appropriately?
description: Sarvam-Translate is an advanced translation model developed by Sarvam AI in collaboration with AI4Bharat. It is specifically designed for comprehensive, document-level translation across the 22 official Indian languages, built on the Gemma3-4B-IT architecture. The model addresses modern translation needs by handling long-context inputs, diverse content types, and various formats, moving beyond isolated sentences. Sarvam-Translate aims to provide high-quality, contextually aware translations for Indian languages, which have traditionally lagged behind high-resource languages in LLM performance. It supports a wide range of input formats, including markdown, digitized content with OCR errors, documents with embedded math and chemistry equations, and code files, translating only comments. The model is engineered to respect different contexts, formats, styles (formal/informal), and ensure inclusivity, such as appropriate gender attribution. The maximum context length supported is 8k tokens. The 22 supported Indian languages include Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, Gujarati, English, Hindi, Kannada, Kashmiri, Konkani, Maithili, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Santali, Sindhi, Tamil, Telugu, and Urdu.

instruction: How can I identify the most relevant section of a user manual that answers the question 'How do I reset my router?'?
description: This is a model that maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space and was designed for semantic search. It has been trained on 215 million (question, answer) pairs from diverse sources. The model uses mean pooling to generate embeddings and produces normalized embeddings with a length of 1. It supports the use of dot-product, cosine-similarity, or euclidean distance as score functions. When loaded with sentence-transformers, dot-product is preferred as it is faster. The model is intended to encode queries and text paragraphs in a dense vector space to find relevant documents. It has a limit of 512 word pieces, and text longer than that will be truncated. The model was trained on input text up to 250 word pieces and may not work well for longer text. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the model using a contrastive learning objective on a concatenation of multiple datasets. The model was trained using mean-pooling, cosine-similarity as the similarity function, and a scale of 20. The training data includes duplicate question pairs from WikiAnswers, automatically generated (question, paragraph) pairs from Wikipedia, (title, body) and (title, answer) pairs from StackExchanges, triplets from Bing search engine, (query, answer) pairs from Google queries and featured snippets, (question, answer) pairs from Amazon product pages, Yahoo Answers, and other sources. The total number of training tuples is 214,988,242.

instruction: I want to replace the character in a sample video with my own photo. Is that possible?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to the foundational video generation models, incorporating several key innovations to enhance generation quality and model capability. It introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture into the video diffusion model, which separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models. This design increases the overall model capacity while maintaining the same computational cost. The A14B model series in Wan2.2 uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for the early stages of denoising, focusing on overall layout, and a low-noise expert for the later stages, refining video details. Each expert has about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters, but only 14B active parameters per step, keeping inference computation and GPU memory nearly unchanged. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a threshold step defined to switch from the high-noise to the low-noise expert. Validation results show that the MoE architecture in Wan2.2 achieves the lowest validation loss, indicating superior convergence and video quality compared to other configurations. In addition to the MoE models, Wan2.2 also includes a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE that achieves a compression ratio of 16×16×4, increasing the overall compression rate to 64 while maintaining high-quality video reconstruction. With an additional patchification layer, the total compression ratio of TI2V-5B reaches 4×32×32. TI2V-5B can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, making it one of the fastest 720P@24fps video generation models. It supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks within a unified framework. Wan2.2 also incorporates meticulously curated aesthetic data, including detailed labels for lighting, composition, contrast, and color tone, allowing for more precise and controllable cinematic style generation. Compared to Wan2.1, Wan2.2 is trained on a significantly larger dataset, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos, enhancing its generalization across multiple dimensions such as motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among all open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 open-sources a 5B model that supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps and can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090. It also supports character animation and replacement, where the model can generate a video of a character image that mimics the human motion in an input video (animation mode) or replace the character image with the input video (replacement mode). Wan2.2 has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and its inference code and model weights have been released. It also supports text-to-speech synthesis for the Speech-to-Video generation task. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license provisions, including not generating harmful or illegal content.

instruction: Add a small logo in the top right corner of this product photo without changing the background or product colors.
description: LongCat-Image-Edit is the image editing version of Longcat-Image. It supports bilingual (Chinese-English) editing and achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source image editing models, delivering leading instruction-following and image quality with superior visual consistency. LongCat-Image-Edit supports various editing tasks, such as global editing, local editing, text modification, and reference-guided editing. It has strong semantic understanding capabilities and can perform precise editing according to instructions. The model has strong consistency preservation capabilities, specifically scrutinizing whether attributes in non-edited regions, such as layout, texture, color tone, and subject identity, remain invariant unless targeted by the instruction, which is well demonstrated in multi-turn editing. LongCat-Image-Edit achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in image editing tasks while significantly improving model inference efficiency, especially among open-source image editing models. For both Text-to-Image and Image Editing tasks involving text generation, you must enclose the target text within single or double quotation marks (both English '...' / "..." and Chinese ‘...’ / “...” styles are supported). The model utilizes a specialized character-level encoding strategy specifically for quoted content. Failure to use explicit quotation marks prevents this mechanism from triggering, which will severely compromise the text rendering capability.

instruction: Can you give me the environmental fate prediction for the SMILES 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)C(=O)Nc2ccc(cc2)OC'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict fugacity-based environmental fate parameter C. This property is used in fugacity models for environmental distribution prediction. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 64, two layers, two timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.2, a learning rate of 0.001, a weight decay of 1e-05, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the FBC dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What kind of textures and colors are most prominent in this picture?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN or logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or using a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, the iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for the ViT-S/16 model. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for the ConvNeXt Tiny model. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and a mean of 79.6 for the ViT-L/16 model. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Could you outline the major events of World War II in chronological order?
description: Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 is an updated version of the Qwen3-4B non-thinking mode, featuring significant improvements in general capabilities such as instruction following, logical reasoning, text comprehension, mathematics, science, coding, and tool usage. It demonstrates substantial gains in long-tail knowledge coverage across multiple languages and is better aligned with user preferences in subjective and open-ended tasks, enabling more helpful responses and higher-quality text generation. The model supports 256K long-context understanding and has 4.0B parameters, with 3.6B non-embedding parameters, 36 layers, and 32 attention heads for Q and 8 for KV. It has a context length of 262,144 tokens natively. This model is designed for non-thinking mode only and does not generate code blocks in its output. It is reported to outperform previous versions and other models on a range of benchmarks, including MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux, GPQA, SuperGPQA, AIME25, HMMT25, ZebraLogic, LiveBench, LiveCodeBench, MultiPL-E, Aider-Polyglot, IFEval, Arena-Hard v2, Creative Writing v3, WritingBench, BFCL-v3, TAU1-Retail, TAU1-Airline, TAU2-Retail, TAU2-Airline, TAU2-Telecom, MultiIF, MMLU-ProX, INCLUDE, and PolyMATH. The model is compatible with Hugging Face transformers and can be used with vLLM or SGLang for creating an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use sampling parameters such as Temperature=0.7, TopP=0.8, TopK=20, and MinP=0, and to set an output length of 16,384 tokens. The model supports agentic use through Qwen-Agent, which encapsulates tool-calling templates and parsers to reduce coding complexity. Users can define available tools using an MCP configuration file or integrate other tools manually. The model is supported by various local applications, including Ollama, LMStudio, MLX-LM, llama.cpp, and KTransformers. If out-of-memory issues occur, reducing the context length to 32,768 is advised. For benchmarking, it is recommended to standardize output formats using specific prompts for math problems and multiple-choice questions.

instruction: The image I have is a bit warped. Can you help make it look more like a normal document?
description: UVDoc is a model designed to perform geometric transformations on images to correct document distortion, inclination, and perspective deformation, thereby improving the accuracy of subsequent text recognition. The model achieved a Character Error Rate (CER) of 0.179 on the DocuNet benchmark dataset. UVDoc is intended to be used as part of the TextImageUnwarping module within the PaddleOCR framework. It can be integrated into projects for document image correction and is compatible with PaddlePaddle versions 3.0.0 for CUDA 11.8, CUDA 12.6, and CPU environments. The model supports inference through command-line tools and Python code, allowing users to process images and save results in various formats, including images and JSON. UVDoc can also be used in conjunction with the PP-StructureV3 pipeline, which includes layout detection, OCR, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition modules. The pipeline can be enabled with the use_doc_unwarping parameter to enhance document processing in real-world scenarios.

instruction: Identify any electronic devices in this image of a modern office desk.
description: YOLOWorld is an SDK that enables efficient open-vocabulary object detection using YOLO-Worldv2 Large. It is optimized for Axera’s NPU-based SoC platforms, including the AX650 Series, AX630C Series, and AX8850 Series, as well as Axera's dedicated AI accelerator. The model supports inference on both host systems and M.2 accelerator cards, such as those used with Raspberry PI 5. It includes two main models: one for object detection (yolo_u16_ax650.axmodel and yolo_u16_ax630c.axmodel) and one for text encoding (clip_b1_u16_ax650.axmodel and clip_b1_u16_ax630c.axmodel). On the AX650 platform, the object detection model has an input shape of 1 x 640 x 640 x 3 and a latency of 9.522 ms, with a CMM usage of 21 MB, while the text encoder has an input shape of 1 x 77 and a latency of 2.997 ms, with a CMM usage of 137 MB. On the AX630C platform, the object detection model has a latency of 43.450 ms and a CMM usage of 31 MB, and the text encoder has a latency of 10.703 ms and a CMM usage of 134 MB. The model is designed to accept text inputs for object detection, such as 'person', 'dog', 'car', 'horse', 'man', 'shoes', 'ball', and others, and it processes images for inference. The SDK includes a Python interface with example scripts for running inference, and it requires specific dependencies installed via pip. The model is deployed using precompiled shared libraries and model files, and it supports both host and AXCL device types for execution. The system outputs detection results in multiple feature maps with varying spatial resolutions. The model is intended for use with Axera hardware and may not be compatible with other platforms.

instruction: Render a cozy cabin in the woods with a chimney smoke and surrounding trees, including the depth map for 3D modeling.
description: The LDM3D model was proposed in a 2023 paper authored by Gabriela Ben Melech Stan, Diana Wofk, Scottie Fox, Alex Redden, Will Saxton, Jean Yu, Estelle Aflalo, Shao-Yen Tseng, Fabio Nonato, Matthias Muller, and Vasudev Lal. LDM3D is a Latent Diffusion Model for 3D that generates both image and depth map data from a given text prompt, allowing users to generate RGBD images from text prompts. The model is fine-tuned on a dataset of tuples containing an RGB image, depth map, and caption, and validated through extensive experiments. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset constructed from a subset of the LAION-400M dataset, a large-scale image-caption dataset that contains over 400 million image-caption pairs. The fine-tuning process comprises two stages. In the first stage, an autoencoder is trained to generate a lower-dimensional, perceptually equivalent data representation. Subsequently, the diffusion model is fine-tuned using the frozen autoencoder. The model can be used to generate an RGB image and depth map given a text prompt. The LDM3D model is on par with the Stable Diffusion models with the same number of parameters (1.06B). On the 512 x 512-sized MS-COCO dataset with 50 DDIM steps, the model achieved a FID score of 27.82, an IS score of 28.79 ± 0.49, and a CLIP similarity score of 26.61 ± 2.92. In depth evaluation, LDM3D achieved an AbsRel of 0.0911 and an RMSE of 0.334, compared to DPT-Large, which achieved an AbsRel of 0.0779 and an RMSE of 0.297. The model has some limitations and biases, particularly in depth map generation, where the ground truth is produced using DPT-large, and other limitations and biases from that model are applicable. Users should be made aware of the risks, biases, and limitations of the model. The model is available for use with PyTorch on both CPU and GPU architectures. The license on this model does not constitute legal advice. Users are advised to consult an attorney before using this model for commercial purposes.

instruction: I need to find and track a specific object in this video using a box prompt. How can I proceed?
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images, taking text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returning segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It also supports semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. For video, SAM 3 Video performs promptable concept segmentation (PCS) on videos, taking text as prompts and detecting and tracking all matching object instances across video frames. It supports both pre-loaded and streaming video inference. SAM 3 Tracker performs promptable visual segmentation (PVS) on images, taking interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It is an updated version of SAM2 that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. SAM 3 Tracker Video performs promptable visual segmentation (PVS) on videos, taking interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to track a specific object instance per prompt across video frames. It is an updated version of SAM2 Video that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. For streaming inference, it is noted that this mode disables hotstart heuristics that remove unmatched and duplicate objects, which may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks compared to pre-loaded video inference. For best results, pre-loaded video inference is recommended when all frames are available.

instruction: How can I generate a clean background mask for this image?
description: BiRefNet is a model designed for high-resolution dichotomous image segmentation (DIS). It is trained on the DIS-TR dataset and validated on DIS-TE and DIS-VD datasets. The model has achieved state-of-the-art performance on three tasks: DIS, HRSOD, and COD. BiRefNet is based on a bilateral reference approach, which is intended to improve segmentation accuracy and resolution. The model supports inference on single images and can be used with adjustable resolutions. It is implemented in Python and can be loaded using the HuggingFace Transformers library or from local GitHub code. The model weights are available on HuggingFace, and the model can be used for inference with or without GPU acceleration. The model includes a preprocessing pipeline that resizes images, normalizes pixel values, and applies a sigmoid function to the final predictions. The model outputs a segmentation mask that can be visualized or used for further processing. The model is described in the paper 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation,' published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The authors of the model are Peng Zheng, Dehong Gao, Deng-Ping Fan, Li Liu, Jorma Laaksonen, Wanli Ouyang, and Nicu Sebe, affiliated with Nankai University, Northwestern Polytechnical University, National University of Defense Technology, Aalto University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and University of Trento, respectively. The model is supported by GPU resources provided by @fal and deployment assistance from @not-lain.

instruction: How can I transform a normal map into a full image that looks like it was captured in real life?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is intended to be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model and is primarily associated with the English language. The license is adapted from the work of others in the area of responsible AI licensing. The model is conditioned on normalbae images, which are images containing surface normal information, typically represented as color-coded images. The preprocessor in Normal 1.1 is trained to estimate normal maps using a more correct protocol (NYU-V2's visualization method), making it more reasonable and physically correct compared to the previous version. This improvement allows the model to interpret real normal maps from rendering engines, provided the colors are correct (blue is front, red is left, green is top). In testing, the model has shown robust performance and can achieve results similar to the depth model. The previous Normal 1.0 model was not frequently used due to its limitations, but Normal 1.1 has significant improvements and potential for more frequent use.

instruction: Could you describe the structure of a typical business report?
description: GLM-4.6V-Flash is an 8-bit quantized version of the GLM-4.6V-Flash model, optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is designed to support various tasks and functions as part of the Community Model Program. The model is created and provided by third parties, and its performance or evaluation results are not specified in the description. instruction: Can you create an image of a peaceful lake surrounded by autumn leaves and a small boat on the water?
description: Quantized GGUF versions of the model by Tongyi-Mai. The available models include Z-Image Turbo GGUF and Qwen3-4B (Text Encoder). The model can be used with tools provided by city96. The model is designed for image generation tasks and supports detailed text-to-image synthesis. It utilizes a transformer-based architecture and can be loaded using the Diffusers library with specific quantization configurations. The model can be run on CUDA-enabled devices with options for attention backend selection, model compilation, and CPU offloading to optimize performance and memory usage. The model is evaluated for efficiency and inference speed, particularly with the Turbo variant requiring zero guidance and a reduced number of inference steps. Users are advised to consult the original model card for detailed information. The repository follows the same license as the original model.

instruction: I need to convert a 22.05 kHz mono audio file into a smaller format. Can you assist with the process?
description: NanoCodec for Apple Silicon is an MLX implementation of a lightweight neural audio codec. The model is designed for audio encoding and decoding tasks. It supports input and output in .wav file formats, with 22050 Hz mono-channel audio as the standard for both. The architecture consists of a fully convolutional generator neural network and three discriminators. The generator includes an encoder, followed by vector quantization, and a decoder. The model operates at a sample rate of 22.05 kHz and is built using the MLX framework. It contains 105 million parameters. The model is intended to be used for encoding and decoding audio signals, preserving the audio quality while reducing the data size. Users are required to comply with the Apache License 2.0 for the code and the original NVIDIA NeMo NanoCodec license for the model weights and architecture. When using this project, both licenses must be adhered to. The original NVIDIA NeMo NanoCodec model weights and architecture are developed by NVIDIA and are subject to their respective licensing terms.

instruction: I need to rephrase a set of user questions to improve matching with existing knowledge base entries. How can I generate semantic equivalents?
description: The Qwen3-Embedding-4B is a text embedding model from the Qwen3 Embedding model series, designed for text embedding and ranking tasks. It is part of a comprehensive range of models in various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B) that provide text embeddings and reranking capabilities. The model inherits the multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills of the Qwen3 series. It supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and offers robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The model has 4 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and an embedding dimension of up to 2560, with support for user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 2560. It is instruction-aware, allowing users to customize input instructions for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios, which can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. The model is evaluated on multiple benchmarks, achieving a score of 69.45 on the MTEB multilingual leaderboard (as of May 24, 2025), and it supports flexible vector definitions and seamless integration with other modules. Developers are advised to write instructions in English for optimal performance in multilingual contexts. The model can be used with frameworks such as Sentence Transformers, Transformers, and vLLM, and it is compatible with deployment via Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on both GPU and CPU devices.

instruction: Generate a short video showing a sunrise over the mountains, with the colors gradually changing from dark blue to golden orange.
description: HunyuanVideo-1.5 is a lightweight video generation model with 8.3 billion parameters, designed to deliver state-of-the-art visual quality and motion coherence while enabling efficient inference on consumer-grade GPUs. It supports both text-to-video (T2V) and image-to-video (I2V) generation across multiple durations and resolutions. The model integrates an advanced DiT (Diffusion Transformer) architecture with a 3D causal VAE, achieving compression ratios of 16× in spatial dimensions and 4× along the temporal axis. A key innovation is the SSTA (Selective and Sliding Tile Attention) mechanism, which prunes redundant spatiotemporal key-value blocks, significantly reducing computational overhead for long video sequences and accelerating inference. HunyuanVideo-1.5 also features a video super-resolution network that enhances sharpness and corrects distortions, allowing outputs to be upscaled to 1080p. The model is trained using a multi-stage, progressive strategy that refines motion coherence, aesthetic quality, and human preference alignment. It supports various inference optimizations, including step-distilled models for faster generation (e.g., 8 or 12 steps for 480p I2V), sparse attention, and caching techniques like deepcache, teacache, and taylorcache. The model is available on Hugging Face Diffusers and supports integration with tools like ComfyUI and LightX2V. It is trained using the Muon optimizer, which accelerates convergence and improves training stability. Evaluation results show that HunyuanVideo-1.5 achieves professional-grade content generation and sets a new state-of-the-art among open-source models. The model is accessible for developers and creators, with code, weights, and training scripts open-sourced to lower the cost of video creation and research.

instruction: I need a voice that sounds like it's being mumbled for a specific scene—can you handle that?
description: Dia is a 1.6B parameter text-to-speech model developed by Nari Labs. It directly generates highly realistic dialogue from a transcript and allows conditioning the output on audio, enabling control over emotion and tone. The model can also produce nonverbal communications such as laughter, coughing, and clearing the throat. To support research, the model provides access to pretrained checkpoints and inference code. The model weights are hosted on a platform, and currently, the model only supports English generation. A comparison of the model to other models is also provided. The model was not fine-tuned on a specific voice, so different voices are generated with each run. Speaker consistency can be maintained by adding an audio prompt or fixing the seed. The model supports dialogue generation using [S1] and [S2] tags, and it can recognize non-verbal tags like (laughs), (clears throat), (sighs), (gasps), (coughs), (singing), (sings), (mumbles), (beep), (groans), (sniffs), (claps), (screams), (inhales), (exhales), (applause), (burps), (humming), (sneezes), (chuckle), and (whistles). Voice cloning is supported, and more information is available. The model can be used as a Python library or as part of the transformers library. It has been tested on GPUs with PyTorch 2.0+ and CUDA 12.6, and CPU support is planned. On enterprise GPUs, the model can generate audio in real-time, while on older GPUs, inference is slower. On an A4000 GPU, the model generates approximately 40 tokens per second, with 86 tokens corresponding to one second of audio. The full version requires around 10GB of VRAM, and a quantized version is planned for the future. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. It is intended for research and educational use, and the following uses are strictly forbidden: identity misuse, deceptive content, and illegal or malicious use. The developers are not responsible for any misuse and oppose unethical usage. Future work includes Docker support, inference speed optimization, and memory-efficient quantization. The project is maintained by a small team and welcomes contributions. The developers acknowledge the support of computation resources, inspiration from other works, and the HuggingFace ZeroGPU Grant. The name 'Nari' is a Korean word for lily, and Jason Y. is thanked for assistance with data filtering.

instruction: What's the best way to track body movements in real-time with minimal hardware?
description: YOLO26-M-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model operates with an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 68.8 for pose estimation across the 50-95 confidence range and 89.6 for the 50 confidence threshold. On CPU using ONNX, inference takes 218.0 milliseconds, while on T4 TensorRT10, it runs at 5.0 milliseconds. The model contains 21.5 million parameters and 73.1 billion FLOPs. YOLO26 is built on three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that eliminates the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), resulting in faster and lighter inference. The end-to-end design reduces pipeline complexity, lowers latency, and improves deployment robustness. The model introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon, inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. YOLO26-M-pose includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing.

instruction: Can I explore this mountain landscape from a viewpoint just to the left of where the camera is now?
description: SHARP is an approach to photorealistic view synthesis from a single image. Given a single photograph, SHARP regresses the parameters of a 3D Gaussian representation of the depicted scene. This is done in less than a second on a standard GPU via a single feedforward pass through a neural network. The 3D Gaussian representation produced by SHARP can then be rendered in real time, yielding high-resolution photorealistic images for nearby views. The representation is metric, with absolute scale, supporting metric camera movements. Experimental results demonstrate that SHARP delivers robust zero-shot generalization across datasets. It sets a new state of the art on multiple datasets, reducing LPIPS by 25–34% and DISTS by 21–43% versus the best prior model, while lowering the synthesis time by three orders of magnitude.

instruction: Please detect any organism names mentioned in this adverse event report.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying species names from the Species-800 dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated SPECIES800 dataset, which contains 800 manually annotated abstracts for organism mentions. The model supports the classification of two entity types: `B-SPECIES` and `I-SPECIES`. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.82, precision of 0.81, recall of 0.82, and accuracy of 0.96. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the Bio_Discharge_Summary_BERT architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: How do I get the model to highlight every bird in a forest scene using a short text phrase?
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images and videos, as well as promptable visual segmentation (PVS) for images and videos.
