For image PCS, the model takes text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returns segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It also provides semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. For video PCS, the model detects and tracks all matching object instances across video frames using text prompts. It supports both pre-loaded and streaming video inference, with the latter being suitable for real-time applications. However, streaming inference may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks compared to pre-loaded inference. For image PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It is an updated version of SAM2 that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. For video PVS, the model tracks a specific object instance per prompt across video frames using interactive visual prompts. It is an updated version of SAM2 Video that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. The model supports batched inference with text prompts and mixed prompts for different images in the same batch. It also supports multi-object video tracking, allowing multiple objects to be tracked simultaneously across video frames. The model can be used with the 🤗 Transformers library and provides various helper functions for post-processing results, such as overlaying masks on images.

instruction: Can you upscale this video to make it suitable for a high-definition monitor?
description: The LTX Video Spatial Upscaler 0.9.8 is a component model designed to work in conjunction with the LTX-Video generation models. It is a diffusion-based model that enhances the spatial resolution of videos by being specifically trained to upscale the latent representations of videos generated by LTX Video models. LTX-Video is the first DiT-based video generation model capable of generating high-quality videos in real-time, producing 30 FPS videos at a 1216×704 resolution faster than they can be watched. It is trained on a large-scale dataset of diverse videos and can generate high-resolution videos with realistic and varied content for both text-to-video and image+text-to-video use cases. The LTX Video Spatial Upscaler is compatible with and can be used to improve the output quality of videos generated by both Lightricks/LTX-Video-0.9.8-dev and Lightricks/LTX-Video-0.9.8-distilled. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a Latent Diffusion Video Spatial Upscaler. It takes latent video frames from an LTX Video model as input and outputs higher-resolution latent video frames. It is compatible with Lightricks/LTX-Video-0.9.7-dev and Lightricks/LTX-Video-0.9.8-distilled. The model works best on resolutions under 720 x 1280 and number of frames below 257. It works on resolutions that are divisible by 32 and number of frames that are divisible by 8 + 1. In case the resolution or number of frames are not divisible by 32 or 8 + 1, the input will be padded with -1 and then cropped to the desired resolution and number of frames. Prompts should be in English and as elaborate as possible. The model is accessible via online demos and can be used with ComfyUI by following dedicated instructions. It can also be run locally with Python 3.10.5, CUDA version 12.2, and PyTorch >= 2.1.2. The LTX Video Spatial Upscaler is used via the LTXLatentUpsamplePipeline in the diffusers library and is intended to be part of a multi-stage generation process. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. As a statistical model, this checkpoint might amplify existing societal biases. The model may fail to generate videos that match the prompts perfectly. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting-style.

instruction: Can you generate a summary of this news article in English and its equivalent in Arabic?
description: mmBERT is a modern multilingual encoder that significantly outperforms previous generation models like XLM-R on classification, embedding, and retrieval tasks. Built on the ModernBERT architecture with novel multilingual training innovations, mmBERT demonstrates that low-resource languages can be effectively learned during the decay phase of training. It is also significantly faster than any previous multilingual encoder. The model uses bidirectional attention with masked language modeling objectives, optimized specifically for multilingual understanding and cross-lingual transfer. Key features include massive language coverage, with training on over 1800 languages using a progressive inclusion strategy. The model is built on the ModernBERT foundation with Flash Attention 2 and unpadding techniques. It introduces novel training methods such as inverse mask scheduling and temperature sampling. The training data is publicly available, consisting of over 3T tokens. The model demonstrates effective learning of low-resource languages in the final training phase. The training data is composed of 2.3T tokens in the first pre-training phase with 60 languages, followed by an extension phase and a final pre-training phase. The mid-training phase includes 600B tokens across 110 languages, and the decay phase includes 100B tokens across 1833 languages. Data sources include Filtered DCLM, FineWeb2, FineWeb2-HQ, code repositories, academic papers, Wikipedia, and community discussions. The model family includes mmBERT-small and mmBERT-base, with varying parameter counts and language coverage. mmBERT supports tasks such as masked language modeling, cross-lingual embeddings, dense retrieval, cross-lingual classification, and multilingual reranking. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be fine-tuned for specific downstream tasks. The model is publicly available for download and use under an open license. If used in research, it should be cited with the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: What are the chances that a new major cryptocurrency will surpass Bitcoin in market value by 2027?
description: OpenForecaster-8B is a specialized language model for open-ended forecasting and predicting future events. It is post-trained from Qwen3-8B using reinforcement learning. The model is trained to make calibrated predictions on open-ended questions about future events, including providing calibrated confidence estimates when asked, reasoning about uncertainty and future scenarios, and leveraging retrieved information in context to improve predictions. OpenForecaster-8B was trained on the OpenForesight dataset, which contains over 52,000 forecasting questions generated from global news events, using GRPO to optimize a joint reward function combining accuracy and brier score. The model's knowledge cutoff is at best until April 2025, with the base model's cutoff being around June 2024, meaning it has no knowledge of events occurring after that date. Therefore, if asked about events in 2026 or later without recent developments or relevant context, the model's answers may not be helpful or up to date. OpenForecaster-8B achieves competitive performance with larger models like DeepSeek-v3 and Qwen3-235B-A22B on forecasting benchmarks, with key improvements in accuracy, calibration of confidence estimates, and consistency in predictions. Performance on a benchmark in July-August 2025 on non-numeric questions (86 questions) showed that OpenForecaster-8B has much higher accuracy than 100B+ models, with a comparison limited to models released before April 2025 for a fair, equal knowledge cutoff. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Can you estimate the depth of objects in this image from different angles?
description: DA3 Giant is a model for multi-view depth estimation, camera pose estimation, and 3D Gaussian estimation. It is the flagship foundation model that uses a unified depth-ray representation. The model is part of the Any-view Model series and contains 1.15 billion parameters. It is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, and 3D Gaussian estimation. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture for visual geometry. The model uses a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to avoid complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is intended for non-commercial use only due to its CC BY-NC 4.0 license.

instruction: I want to digitize this Chinese document. Can you pull out the text for me?
description: POINTS-Reader is a vision-language model designed for end-to-end document conversion. It is a streamlined model that follows the structure of POINTS1.5, with the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct replaced by Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct. The input to POINTS-Reader consists of a fixed prompt and a document image, and the output is a string of text extracted from the document image, delivered directly to the user without any post-processing. The model supports extraction from both Chinese and English documents, achieving scores of 0.133 for English and 0.212 for Chinese on OmniDocBench. It is optimized for high throughput by using a ViT with a moderate number of parameters (600M NaViT) and supports deployment with SGLang. The model's development includes a two-stage data augmentation strategy: the first stage uses automated data to provide basic document extraction capabilities, and the second stage employs continuous self-evolution to improve the quality of generated data. POINTS-Reader has been accepted for presentation at the EMNLP 2025 Main Conference and is available on Hugging Face Spaces. It can struggle with complex layouts, such as newspapers, and may produce repeated or missing content. It also has difficulty handling handwritten inputs, such as receipts or notes, which can lead to recognition errors or omissions. Currently, it supports only English and Chinese, limiting its effectiveness on other languages.

instruction: Could you provide a prediction for the molecule represented by 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)C=O'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict plasma level volume or protein binding parameter, which may relate to pharmacokinetics or blood partitioning behavior. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the PLV dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: What chemical compounds are present in this part of the research on disease mechanisms?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used as a benchmark for evaluating named entity recognition models. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.98. The model is based on the e5-small-v2 architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: What does the scene look like when captured from a slightly elevated back angle?
description: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511-Multiple-Angles-LoRA is a multi-angle camera control LoRA designed for the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model. It provides 96 precise camera positions, derived from 4 elevations, 8 azimuths, and 3 distances. The model was trained on over 3000 Gaussian Splatting renders, which are high-quality 3D-consistent images, to enable better spatial understanding and camera control. It supports low-angle camera positions as low as -30°, making it suitable for ground-level and low camera shots. The LoRA enhances the built-in viewpoint capabilities of Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 by offering exact control over camera position, with a large dataset ensuring maximum accuracy. The model is intended for use with the exact prompt format of [azimuth] [elevation] [distance], and it is recommended to use a LoRA strength between 0.8 and 1.0 for optimal results. The model was built by Lovis Odin at fal using synthetic 3D renders with precise camera control. For best results, clear input images with good lighting are recommended, and users are advised to follow the specified prompt order and experiment with low-angle shots, where the model excels.

instruction: What are the system requirements to run the speech recognition pipeline efficiently?
description: T-one is a high-performance streaming automatic speech recognition (ASR) pipeline for the Russian language, specialized for the telephony domain. It provides a complete low-latency solution for real-time transcription, featuring a pretrained streaming Conformer-based acoustic model, a custom phrase boundary detector, and a decoder. Developed by T-Software DC, T-one is a practical, low-latency, high-throughput ASR solution with modular components. It includes a full suite of tools for inference, fine-tuning, and deployment, making it a ready-to-use solution for production environments. The model is based on a 71M parameter acoustic model using the Conformer architecture, with innovations such as SwiGLU activation, SiLU (Swish) activations, RMSNorm, RoPE embeddings, a U-Net structure, attention score reuse, and efficient state management. It processes audio in 300 ms chunks and supports both greedy decoding and a KenLM-based CTC beam search decoder. T-one is primarily intended for use with telephone-channel audio but is robust across different domains due to its training on heterogeneous data. It supports streaming inference, allowing it to process long audio files in real time. The primary use case is streaming speech recognition of calls, where the model processes small audio chunks incrementally and returns finalized text and word-level timestamps in real time. T-one can be easily fine-tuned for specific domains using the 🤗 ecosystem. The model was trained on over 80,000 hours of Russian speech, with a significant portion pseudo-labeled using a robust ROVER model ensemble. Training was conducted for 7 days on 8 A100 GPUs using the NVIDIA NeMo framework with AdamW optimizer, cosine annealing with warmup, 16-bit mixed precision, and semi-sorted batching. T-one demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, especially on its target domain of telephony, while remaining competitive on general-purpose benchmarks. It provides a ready-to-use pipeline with examples for offline and streaming speech recognition inference, a demo for launching a local speech recognition service via Docker, and examples for deploying with Triton Inference Server for high-throughput scenarios. The model is fully open source, with all model and pipeline code available, and is released under the Apache 2.0 License.

instruction: Can you help me create a 3D model of a custom-designed lamp from the images I have?
description: Hunyuan3D-2mv is a model finetuned to support multiview controlled shape generation. It is designed to create and manipulate 3D assets based on input images from multiple views, such as front, left, and back. The model uses a diffusion-based approach and is intended for high-resolution textured 3D asset generation. It is part of the Hunyuan3D 2.0 system, which scales diffusion models for this purpose. The model is described as a tool for realizing the imagination of creating and manipulating 3D assets. It is implemented using a pipeline that accepts multiple image inputs and generates a 3D mesh as output. The model is reported to be part of research detailed in an academic paper titled 'Hunyuan3D 2.0: Scaling Diffusion Models for High Resolution Textured 3D Assets Generation' and is associated with the Tencent Hunyuan3D Team.

instruction: Can you help me remove the background from this product photo so I can place it on a different background?
description: RMBG v2.0 is a state-of-the-art background removal model that significantly improves upon RMBG v1.4. It is designed to effectively separate foreground from background in a range of categories and image types. The model has been trained on a carefully selected dataset that includes general stock images, e-commerce, gaming, and advertising content, making it suitable for commercial use cases and enterprise content creation at scale. The accuracy, efficiency, and versatility of the model currently rival leading source-available models. It is ideal for applications where content safety, legally licensed datasets, and bias mitigation are paramount. Developed by BRIA AI, RMBG v2.0 is available as a source-available model for non-commercial use. Bria RMBG2.0 is available as source-code and weights, ComfyUI nodes, or API endpoints. Commercial use is subject to a commercial agreement with BRIA. The model is released under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license for non-commercial use. BRIA RMBG-2.0 is a dichotomous image segmentation model trained exclusively on a professional-grade dataset. The model output includes a single-channel 8-bit grayscale alpha matte, where each pixel value indicates the opacity level of the corresponding pixel in the original image. This non-binary output approach offers developers the flexibility to define custom thresholds for foreground-background separation, catering to varied use cases and enhancing integration into complex pipelines. The Bria-RMBG model was trained with over 15,000 high-quality, high-resolution, manually labeled (pixel-wise accuracy), fully licensed images. The training data includes balanced gender, balanced ethnicity, and people with different types of disabilities. The distribution of images is as follows: 45.11% objects only, 25.24% people with objects/animals, 17.35% people only, 8.52% people/objects/animals with text, 2.52% text only, and 1.89% animals only. The distribution of image types is 87.70% photorealistic and 12.30% non-photorealistic. The distribution of background types is 52.05% non-solid background and 47.95% solid background. The distribution of foreground content is 51.42% single main foreground object and 48.58% multiple objects in the foreground. RMBG-2.0 is developed on an enhanced architecture with a proprietary dataset and training scheme, which significantly improves the model’s accuracy and effectiveness for background-removal tasks. If you use this model in your research, please cite the article titled 'Bilateral Reference for High-Resolution Dichotomous Image Segmentation' by Zheng, Peng and Gao, Dehong and Fan, Deng-Ping and Liu, Li and Laaksonen, Jorma and Ouyang, Wanli and Sebe, Nicu, published in CAAI Artificial Intelligence Research in 2024. The model requires the following libraries: torch, torchvision, pillow, kornia, and transformers. To use the model, load the model from the 'briaai/RMBG-2.0' repository, preprocess the input image by resizing it to 1024x1024, normalizing it, and converting it to a tensor. Perform inference using the model, apply a sigmoid function to the output, and convert the result to a PIL image. Resize the mask to the original image size and apply it as an alpha channel to the image. Save the resulting image with the background removed.

instruction: I want to hear how this poem sounds when read aloud. Can you help?
description: SpeechT5 is a model fine-tuned for speech synthesis (text-to-speech) on the LibriTTS dataset. It is based on the T5 (Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer) framework and is designed to explore encoder-decoder pre-training for self-supervised speech and text representation learning. The SpeechT5 framework includes a shared encoder-decoder network and six modal-specific (speech/text) pre/post-nets. Input speech or text is processed through the pre-nets, the shared encoder-decoder network models the sequence-to-sequence transformation, and the post-nets generate the output in the speech or text modality based on the decoder's output. The model leverages large-scale unlabeled speech and text data to learn a unified-modal representation, aiming to improve modeling capabilities for both modalities. A cross-modal vector quantization approach is used to align textual and speech information into a unified semantic space by randomly mixing up speech/text states with latent units as the interface between encoder and decoder. Extensive evaluations have demonstrated the effectiveness of the SpeechT5 framework on a wide range of spoken language processing tasks, including automatic speech recognition, speech synthesis, speech translation, voice conversion, speech enhancement, and speaker identification. The model was developed by Junyi Ao, Rui Wang, Long Zhou, Chengyi Wang, Shuo Ren, Yu Wu, Shujie Liu, Tom Ko, Qing Li, Yu Zhang, Zhihua Wei, Yao Qian, Jinyu Li, and Furu Wei. It is intended for use in speech synthesis and related spoken language processing tasks. Users should be aware of the model's potential risks, biases, and limitations, though further information is needed for detailed recommendations.

instruction: Based on this image, what kind of environment do you think it was taken in?
description: This is a CLIP model with an extra text depth and a vision MLP head, trained on the LAION-2B English subset. The model uses the ConvNeXt-Large architecture as the image tower, with a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) head in the vision tower instead of a single projection. The text tower has the same width but includes four additional layers, resulting in a depth of 16 and an embedding dimension of 768. The 320x320 resolution model is a weight-averaged combination (model soup) of three fine-tunes from the original 256x256 training run. Each fine-tune used a different learning rate (1e-4, 6e-5, 5e-5) and a different number of additional samples (3.2B, 2B, 2.5B). At 320x320 resolution, the ConvNext-Large-D model is more efficient than the L/14 model at 336x336 resolution, with 2.5x fewer GMACs, 2.8x fewer activations, and 1.22x fewer parameters. The model achieves between 75.9% and 76.9% top-1 zero-shot accuracy on ImageNet-1k. It supports tasks such as zero-shot image classification, image and text retrieval, image classification fine-tuning, linear probe image classification, and image generation guiding and conditioning. The model is intended for research purposes and is not recommended for deployment in any use case without thorough in-domain testing. It is not suitable for use in surveillance or facial recognition due to the lack of testing norms and checks for fair use. The model has not been trained or evaluated on languages other than English and is therefore limited to English language use cases. The model was trained using the LAION-2B dataset, which is a 2 billion sample English subset of LAION-5B. This dataset is uncurated and may contain disturbing content. It is recommended to use a safety filter to reduce the risk of encountering harmful content. The model was trained by Ross Wightman on a cluster using a global batch size of 131072 for 10-16 checkpoint intervals, with a total of approximately 2-3 billion samples seen over the fine-tune. The training procedure involved a slurm script on 64 8-GPU (A100 40GB) nodes with specific training parameters. The model was evaluated using a combination of VTAB+ datasets for classification and COCO and Flickr for retrieval.

instruction: Generate a concise version of this academic thesis, with each point clearly linked to the original text for verification.
description: sui-1 (Summarization with Unique Identifiers) is a specialized model for high-quality summarization of very long texts with built-in source grounding. Every claim in the summary can be traced back to its source sentence, enabling verification and reducing hallucination risk. The model handles up to 128k tokens natively, with a two-step iterative approach for documents up to 2 million tokens. The FP8 variant runs on a single A100 40GB or A6000 48GB GPU, and the iterative approach enables deployment on even more modest hardware. sui-1 significantly outperforms all tested open-weight baselines, including models with 3x more parameters. It is fine-tuned for English, German, Spanish, French, and Italian, and inherits 20+ additional languages from Mistral Small 3.2. The model is built using a sophisticated data generation pipeline that produced 22,000+ training examples from parliamentary documents, web sources, and Wikipedia using chain-of-thought reasoning with multi-stage verification. It features a built-in citation mechanism that links each claim to its source sentence for full traceability. The model follows a three-phase approach: a planning phase that analyzes the input and plans the summary structure, a reference selection phase that identifies the most important sentences to cite, and a grounded generation phase that produces a summary with inline citations to source sentences. Citations use XML tags assigned during preprocessing, enabling deterministic verification of each claim. The input text must be preprocessed with XML sentence tags, each sentence wrapped in a unique 8-character hexadecimal tag. The model outputs a JSON object with three keys: 'structure', 'xml_tags', and 'summary'. The 'structure' key contains internal reasoning about the content and planned summary structure, the 'xml_tags' key is a list of XML tags cited in the summary, and the 'summary' key is the actual summary with inline XML tag references. The model supports a 128k token context window natively and an iterative approach for longer documents. For documents exceeding the context limit, a two-step iterative approach is used that preserves grounding quality. The first step involves creating partial summaries of document chunks, and the second step merges these into a final summary. The iterative approach maintains source grounding through careful XML tag propagation, ensuring that every claim in the final summary traces back to a specific source sentence. The model provides two variants: bfloat16 (full precision, ~48GB weights) and FP8 (quantized, ~24GB weights). The FP8 version preserves high quality, scoring 81.05% overall on the benchmark, nearly identical to bfloat16. The bfloat16 variant requires ~55GB VRAM for 8k context, scaling to ~76GB for 128k. The FP8 variant requires ~38GB for 8k and ~50GB for 128k. The model is evaluated using an LLM-as-a-Judge methodology, where a strong judge model evaluates summary quality across multiple criteria, including factual accuracy, coverage and completeness, specificity and informativeness, format compliance, and custom instruction adherence. The evaluation was conducted on 100 diverse test samples covering multiple languages and document types, with scoring using binary pass/fail per criterion, aggregated to success rates. Grounding quality is validated using structural checks for tag uniqueness, tag validity, and tag usage. The model is a contribution to the open-source community and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It has limitations, including the requirement for preprocessing of input text with XML tags, a maximum single-pass context of 128k tokens, and occasional JSON output parsing failures, for which retry logic is recommended in production use.

instruction: How do I configure an object detector to suppress predictions for objects it hasn't been trained on?
description: This repository contains models from the paper 'Mitigating Hallucinations in Object Detection Models: A Revisit to Out-of-Distribution Detection'. It provides YOLOv10 and Faster R-CNN models, including standard baseline versions and fine-tuned models designed to reduce false positive detections on out-of-distribution (OoD) data. The models are trained to address hallucinations, where object detectors produce high-confidence false predictions when encountering novel objects not seen during training. The approach involves identifying flaws in existing OoD benchmarks and introducing a fine-tuning strategy that teaches detectors to be less overconfident. This is achieved by training on 'proximal OoD' samples—objects semantically similar to training classes but not part of them. These samples are treated as background, helping the model suppress predictions for unfamiliar objects. The result is a model that is more conservative and less likely to hallucinate, with minimal impact on performance on the original task. The models are available for the BDD 100K dataset, used in autonomous driving, and the Pascal VOC dataset for general object detection. Both vanilla and fine-tuned versions are provided for each architecture. The fine-tuned models show significant reductions in hallucination errors, with an 88% reduction on the BDD-100K benchmark when combined with an OoD filter. For example, the YOLOv10 model on BDD-100K reduces hallucination counts from 708 in the vanilla version to 145 in the fine-tuned version, and to 78 when combined with a KNN filter. Similarly, the Faster R-CNN model on BDD-100K reduces hallucination counts from 2,595 in the vanilla version to 395 in the fine-tuned version, and to 270 with the KNN filter. The models are available in different configurations, including real-time YOLOv10 and high-accuracy Faster R-CNN. The repository also includes corrected benchmark datasets, 'Near-OoD' and 'Far-OoD', which address flaws in previous benchmarks. The models are released under the MIT License. If used in research, the models and methodology should be cited according to the provided references.

instruction: How can I determine if a person is standing straight in a given image?
description: RTMPose-Body2d is a machine learning model designed for human pose estimation, detecting the location and confidence of 133 joints. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is part of the RTMPose series. The model has an input resolution of 256x192, contains 17.9 million parameters, and has a model size of 68.5 MB in float precision and 18.2 MB in w8a16 precision. It is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices, including various Snapdragon® and QCS chipsets, using different runtimes such as TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference times ranging from 0.75 ms to 176.198 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 MB to 189 MB, depending on the device and precision. The model is primarily executed on the NPU, with some CPU-based execution for specific configurations. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch's JIT tracing and Qualcomm® AI Hub APIs. It supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and deployment to Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN shared libraries. The model is available for use with an API token through the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and additional details on performance across devices can be found on the Qualcomm® AI Hub website. The original implementation of RTMPose-Body2d is licensed separately.

instruction: Illustrate a slide for a presentation about renewable energy, including images of wind turbines and solar panels with explanatory text.
description: Qwen-Image-2512 is the December update of Qwen-Image’s text-to-image foundational model. It is designed to generate high-quality images from text prompts, with a focus on enhancing realism, detail, and text integration. Compared to the base Qwen-Image model released in August, Qwen-Image-2512 features enhanced human realism, finer natural detail, and improved text rendering. The model significantly reduces the “AI-generated” look and substantially enhances overall image realism, especially for human subjects. It delivers more detailed rendering of landscapes, animal fur, and other natural elements. The model also improves the accuracy and quality of textual elements, achieving better layout and more faithful multimodal (text + image) composition. Over 10,000 rounds of blind model evaluations have been conducted, and the results show that Qwen-Image-2512 is currently the strongest open-source model and remains highly competitive among closed-source models. The model supports a variety of tasks, including generating images with specific aspect ratios, rendering detailed human portraits, landscapes, and complex infographics. It is capable of accurately capturing age cues, facial expressions, and environmental context, as well as rendering fine details such as hair strands, fur, and water flow. The model also supports the generation of text-rich images, including PPT slides, infographics, and educational posters, with improved accuracy, layout, and multimodal integration. The model is implemented using the diffusers library and can be loaded and used with Python code. It is available for use on platforms such as Hugging Face and ModelScope. The model is based on a diffusion pipeline and can be run on CUDA-enabled devices for faster inference. The model is suitable for a wide range of applications, including but not limited to, content creation, design, and educational materials. However, the model may still have limitations in certain scenarios, and users are encouraged to test and evaluate its performance for their specific use cases.

instruction: Can you detect and track a specific 'red balloon' in this video using a mask I provide?
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images, taking text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returning segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It also supports semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. For video, SAM 3 Video performs promptable concept segmentation (PCS) on videos, taking text as prompts and detecting and tracking all matching object instances across video frames. It supports both pre-loaded and streaming video inference. SAM 3 Tracker performs promptable visual segmentation (PVS) on images, taking interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It is an updated version of SAM2 that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. SAM 3 Tracker Video performs promptable visual segmentation (PVS) on videos, taking interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to track a specific object instance per prompt across video frames. It is an updated version of SAM2 Video that maintains the same API while providing improved performance. For streaming inference, it is noted that this mode disables hotstart heuristics that remove unmatched and duplicate objects, which may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks compared to pre-loaded video inference. For best results, pre-loaded video inference is recommended when all frames are available.

instruction: Can you process these tokens and output them as an anime-style Japanese voice for a game demo?
description: Anime‑XCodec2 is a fine‑tuned variant of HKUSTAudio/xcodec2, trained on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese anime/game-style voices. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, so speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for downstream systems that already work with XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model is a neural speech codec or speech tokenizer model based on XCodec2 with a decoder fine-tuned for Japanese speech, particularly anime/game-style voices. The training scope involved decoder-only fine-tuning on approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese data, while the encoder and codebook are frozen. Because the encoder and codebook are unchanged, speech tokens produced at encode time are identical with the original XCodec2. Any downstream model expecting XCodec2 codes can use Anime‑XCodec2 as a drop-in decoder. The model operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz. The intended use of the model is to decode XCodec2 speech tokens, such as from Llasa or other autoregressive token generators trained on XCodec2 codes, into Japanese speech with improved naturalness for anime/game-style voices. It is also intended for the reconstruction of Japanese speech from XCodec2 tokens when analyzing or building Japanese-focused speech pipelines. The model is optimized for Japanese, and performance on other languages may degrade compared to the baseline XCodec2. It operates at a sampling rate of 16 kHz, and inputs must be resampled to 16 kHz before encoding; decoding assumes 16 kHz. The model is tuned toward anime/game-style voices, and out-of-domain speech may not benefit. The training data consists of approximately 25,000 hours of Japanese speech, with a focus on anime/game-style voices, including acting and character voices. Data preparation included resampling to 16 kHz and standard loudness/peak checks where appropriate. The training procedure involved fine-tuning the components `generator.backbone`, `generator.head`, and `fc_post_a`, while all other non-listed components were frozen. The goal of the training was to preserve token compatibility with HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 while improving reconstruction quality for Japanese anime/game-style speech. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, the same as the original XCodec2 license. The model acknowledges the original HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 and thanks contributors and the community around Japanese speech resources.

instruction: Generate a modern apartment interior with sleek furniture and natural lighting.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date. FLUX.2 [klein] unifies generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. It is built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality and can run on consumer hardware with as little as 13GB VRAM. FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base is a 4 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is a full-capacity foundation model, undistilled, preserving complete training signal for maximum flexibility. It is ideal for fine-tuning, LoRA training, research, and custom pipelines where control matters more than speed. It offers higher output diversity than the distilled models. This repository holds an FP8 version of FLUX.2 [klein] Base 4B. The main repository of this model (full BF16 weights) can be found separately. The model has an exceptional speed and quality-to-size ratio and is ideal for local deployment and fine-tuning on limited hardware. It was trained without step or guidance distillation, making it more efficient and flexible. It has open weights for customization and fine-tuning to drive science, research, and empower artists to iterate with speed. Its outputs can be used for commercial purposes as described in the relevant documentation. A reference implementation of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base, as well as sampling code, is provided in a dedicated repository. Developers and creatives looking to build on top of FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base are encouraged to use this as a starting point. The model is also available in both FP8 and BF16 versions. The FLUX.2 [klein] 4B Base model fits in approximately 13GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 3090/4070 and above. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. Nothing contained in this Model Card should be interpreted as or deemed a restriction or modification to the license the model is released under. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of its models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in its model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user from generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user from generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation was conducted throughout the process, including multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision was made after safety fine-tuning and prior to release, a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints was conducted, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on the company's hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and provides a dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations. The model is licensed under the specified license. This project may contain trademarks or logos for projects, products, or services. Use of Black Forest Labs and FLUX trademarks or logos in modified versions of this project must not cause confusion or imply sponsorship or endorsement. Any use of third-party trademarks, intellectual property or logos is subject to those third-party's policies.

instruction: Could you look for a signature in this image and let me know if you find one?
description: This model is a Conditional-DETR with a ResNet-50 backbone, fine-tuned for the task of detecting handwritten signatures in document images. It was selected as the best-performing model among various object detection architectures based on its highest mAP@0.5 score of 93.65% in a comprehensive evaluation. The model was trained on a dataset consisting of 2,819 document images, with 1,980 images used for training (70%), 420 for validation (15%), and 419 for testing (15%). The dataset was processed to a resolution of 640x640 pixels in COCO JSON format. During the training process, multiple models were evaluated for precision, recall, and inference time, with Conditional-DETR ResNet-50 achieving the best mAP@0.5 score. Hyperparameter tuning was performed on the YOLOv8s model using Optuna, with parameters including dropout, learning rate, box and class loss coefficients, and optimizer selection. After tuning, the best model achieved a precision of 94.74%, recall of 89.72%, mAP@50 of 94.50%, and mAP@50-95 of 67.35%. Inference times were 171.56 ms on CPU (ONNX Runtime) and 7.657 ms on GPU (TensorRT). The model can be used for inference via the Hugging Face Transformers library, and it supports post-processing for object detection results. A live demo is available for real-time inference, and the model can be deployed using the NVIDIA Triton Inference Server. The model was developed using Python 3.10.12, PyTorch 2.5.1, and related libraries, and trained on a Google Cloud Platform n1-standard-8 instance with an NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU. The model weights, code, and training materials are licensed under the Apache 2.0 license.

instruction: Can you remove the background from this photo of a person with long hair?
description: BEN (Background Erase Network) is a model designed for foreground segmentation using a Confidence Guided Matting (CGM) pipeline. The architecture includes a refiner network that processes pixels where the base model has lower confidence, leading to more accurate matting results. BEN2, an enhanced version, is publicly available and was trained on the DIS5k dataset and a proprietary 22K segmentation dataset. It supports tasks such as hair matting, 4K processing, object segmentation, and edge refinement. The base model is available for free commercial use on HuggingFace. The model generates both a binary mask and a foreground image from input images and supports CUDA for GPU acceleration. Performance metrics for the BEN_Base model on the Disk 5k evaluation include a MAE of 0.0309, DICE of 0.8806, IOU of 0.8371, BER of 0.0516, and ACC of 0.9718. The BEN_Base combined with the commercial refiner model achieves a MAE of 0.0270, DICE of 0.8989, IOU of 0.8506, BER of 0.0496, and ACC of 0.9740. The model is implemented in Python and can be integrated using a simple API. Users are required to clone the repository and install the dependencies to use the model.

instruction: What emotion is being expressed in this short voice recording?
description: SEW-D-tiny is a base model pretrained on 16kHz sampled speech audio. It is designed to be fine-tuned on downstream tasks such as Automatic Speech Recognition, Speaker Identification, Intent Classification, and Emotion Recognition. The model is part of the SEW (Squeezed and Efficient Wav2vec) architecture, which is optimized for performance and efficiency in pre-trained models for automatic speech recognition. SEW-D-tiny demonstrates significant improvements in both performance and efficiency compared to wav2vec 2.0. For example, under the 100h-960h semi-supervised setup on LibriSpeech, SEW achieves a 1.9x inference speedup compared to wav2vec 2.0, with a 13.5% relative reduction in word error rate. With a similar inference time, SEW reduces word error rate by 25-50% across different model sizes. When using the model, it is important to ensure that the speech input is also sampled at 16kHz. Evaluation results on LibriSpeech's 'clean' and 'other' test data show a word error rate (WER) of 10.47 and 22.73, respectively.

instruction: Could you process this image of a historical document and identify all the small, dense text passages?
description: olmOCR-2-7B-1025 is a full BF16 version of a model fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct using a specific dataset. It has been further fine-tuned using GRPO RL training to enhance its performance on math equations, tables, and other challenging OCR cases. The model is designed to process document images, particularly those with complex layouts, and extract structured metadata. It is recommended to use the FP8 version for most practical applications, except for further fine-tuning. The model is best used via a dedicated toolkit that includes an efficient inference setup using VLLM, capable of handling millions of documents at scale. The model expects a single document image as input, with the longest dimension rendered to 1288 pixels. The prompt must include additional metadata from the document, which can be generated using the provided methods. The model scores 82.3 ± 1.1 on the olmOCR-Bench, with specific performance metrics including 82.9 on ArXiv, 82.1 on Old Scans Math, 84.3 on Tables, 48.3 on Old Scans, 95.7 on Headers and Footers, 84.3 on Multi column, 81.4 on Long tiny text, and 99.7 on Base. The FP8 version of the model scores 82.4 ± 1.1 on the same benchmark, with slightly improved performance on most categories. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2's policies.

instruction: Generate a unique anime face featuring a bold red headband and a gentle smile.
description: This is a fine-tuned diffusion model for generating high-quality anime faces using a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). It is based on Google's pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model and was fine-tuned on 7,000+ high-quality anime face images. The model is designed for unconditional image generation of 256×256 anime faces. It uses the Diffusers framework and is licensed under the MIT license. Training parameters include a learning rate of 2e-5, 15 epochs, a batch size of 4, and gradient accumulation steps of 2, resulting in approximately 26,250 training steps. The AdamW optimizer and Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss were used during training. The final training loss was approximately 0.0077. Inference speed ranges from 30 to 60 seconds per image, depending on the number of inference steps. It is recommended to use 100 inference steps for the best quality. The model can generate a wide diversity of anime faces with various hair colors and styles, eye colors and expressions, face shapes and features, and skin tones. However, the model is limited to 256×256 resolution and is specifically trained on anime faces, so it may not generate realistic or photorealistic faces. The diversity of generated faces is limited to the patterns present in the training data, and the clarity of face shapes depends on the number of inference steps used. The model was fine-tuned from the pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model using a low learning rate to preserve general face generation knowledge while adapting to anime-specific features such as large eyes and stylized features. The training data was sourced from the Anime Face Dataset (Kaggle), consisting of 7,000 images selected by file size. The images were preprocessed by resizing to 256×256, applying random horizontal flips with 50% probability, and normalizing to the range [-1, 1]. During training, the model adapted from photorealistic to anime style in the first 3 epochs, solidified anime features in epochs 4 to 8, and showed marginal improvements with a risk of overfitting in later epochs. Ethical considerations include the model's use for generating synthetic anime faces, and it should not be used to create misleading or deceptive content, generate non-consensual images of real people, or violate any local laws or regulations. If used in research or projects, credit should be given to the original DDPM paper, Google's pre-trained `ddpm-celebahq-256` model, and this fine-tuned adaptation. Potential future improvements include higher resolution, conditional generation for text-to-image anime faces, better diversity through larger training datasets, and improved training with advanced schedulers or techniques.

instruction: Could you create a representation of this image in a numerical format for further analysis?
description: This is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model, designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. It is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model has 27.8 million parameters, 4.5 GMACs, and 13.4 million activations. It processes images of size 224 x 224. The model supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, it can be used to generate top 5 probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, it outputs feature maps with varying shapes, such as [1, 96, 56, 56], [1, 192, 28, 28], [1, 384, 14, 14], and [1, 768, 7, 7]. For image embeddings, it can produce a tensor of shape (batch_size, num_features) by removing the classifier or using forward features and forward head methods. The model is based on the research described in the papers 'DINOv3', 'A ConvNet for the 2020s', and 'PyTorch Image Models'.

instruction: What type of genre does this song belong to, and what are the defining characteristics?
description: Music Flamingo (MF) is a fully open, state-of-the-art Large Audio-Language Model (LALM) designed to advance music (including song) understanding in foundational audio models. It brings together innovations in deep music understanding across songs and instrumentals, rich, theory-aware captions and question answering (harmony, structure, timbre, lyrics, cultural context), reasoning-centric training using chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning with custom rewards for step-by-step reasoning, and long-form song reasoning over full-length, multicultural audio (extended context). Extensive evaluations confirm Music Flamingo's effectiveness, setting new benchmarks on over 10+ public music understanding and reasoning tasks. The model is intended for non-commercial research purposes only. Music Flamingo is supported in HuggingFace Transformers and processes audio in 30-second windows with a 10-minute total cap per sample. Longer inputs are truncated. It supports single-turn audio and text instruction, batch multiple conversations, text-only and audio-only prompts, and training or fine-tuning. The model is based on a Transformer architecture with a decoder-only LLM backbone (Qwen2.5-7B), an AF-Whisper unified audio encoder from Audio Flamingo 3, and an MLP-based audio adaptor. It has 8 billion parameters. The model accepts music (song or instrumental) and text as input, with audio in WAV/MP3/FLAC formats and text in UTF-8. The maximum audio length is 20 minutes, and the maximum text length is 24,000 tokens. The output is text (and optional speech) in UTF-8 string format, with a maximum text length of 2048 tokens. The model is designed and optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems (A100/H100), leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference. It is compatible with PyTorch and HuggingFace Transformers and supports NVIDIA Ampere (A100) and Hopper (H100) hardware on Linux. The model was trained entirely on music data collected from various sources, with training data size ranging from 10,000 to 1 million hours. The data collection method for training data is human, with labeling methods including both human and automated. The model is evaluated on test splits of datasets with human data collection and labeling methods. The model version is v1.0. Ethical considerations include the belief in Trustworthy AI and the responsibility of developers to ensure the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. The model is built with Audio Flamingo 3, Qwen, NVILA, and the open audio-ML community.

instruction: Determine the relationship between this image and the diagnosis 'basal cell carcinoma on the nasal tip'?
description: MedSigLIP is a variant of SigLIP (Sigmoid Loss for Language Image Pre-training) designed to encode medical images and text into a common embedding space. It contains a 400M parameter vision encoder and a 400M parameter text encoder, supporting 448x448 image resolution with up to 64 text tokens. MedSigLIP was trained on a combination of de-identified medical image and text pairs, including chest X-rays, dermatology images, ophthalmology images, histopathology slides, and slices of CT and MRI volumes, along with associated descriptions or reports. This training data was combined with natural (non-medical) image and text pairs to retain the model's ability to parse natural images. MedSigLIP is recommended for medical image interpretation applications that do not require text generation, such as data-efficient classification, zero-shot classification, and semantic image retrieval. For medical applications that require text generation, another model is recommended. MedSigLIP accepts images and text as inputs, producing image embeddings, text embeddings, and similarity scores between the image and text. It was evaluated across a range of medical image modalities, including chest X-ray, pathology, dermatology, and ophthalmology. Key performance metrics include zero-shot AUCs for chest X-ray findings, where MedSigLIP outperformed ELIXR in several categories, and AUCs for dermatology, ophthalmology, and pathology findings, where MedSigLIP showed competitive performance compared to existing HAI-DEF embedding models. MedSigLIP is based on SigLIP-400M and uses the same encoder that powers image interpretation in a generative model. It is intended for use by software developers and researchers to facilitate the creation of healthcare applications involving medical images and text. MedSigLIP is not a finished product and requires adaptation and validation for specific use cases. Developers should consider potential biases in validation data and data contamination concerns when evaluating the model's generalization capabilities. MedSigLIP was trained on diverse public datasets, including MIMIC-CXR, Slake-VQA, PAD-UFES-20, SCIN, TCGA, CAMELYON, PMC-OA, and Mendeley Digital Knee X-Ray, as well as de-identified, licensed datasets or datasets collected internally at Google from consented participants. The model was created on July 9, 2025, and is version 1.0.0. When using this model, please cite the MedGemma Technical Report by Sellergren et al. (2025).

instruction: What's the likelihood of a wide receiver being classified as elite if they have 100 targets and 80 receptions?
description: This model is a binary classifier developed as part of a class assignment on designing and deploying AI/ML systems. It uses AutoML (AutoGluon Tabular) to predict whether a football receiver is classified as 'Elite' (0/1) based on their statistical features. The model was trained on a dataset containing the following features: 'Tgt', 'Rec', 'Yds', 'YBC_per_R', 'YAC_per_R', 'ADOT', 'Drop_pct', and 'Rat'. The dataset was split using a stratified 80/20 train/test split on the original data. Preprocessing steps included dropping identifier columns (e.g., 'Player'), coercing data to numeric types, and removing rows with missing values. The model was trained using the 'best_quality' preset in AutoGluon Tabular with a time budget of 300 seconds and a random seed of 42. The evaluation metric used was F1 score for binary classification. Training was performed on a Colab CPU runtime with 2 vCPUs and approximately 12 GB of RAM. AutoGluon explored LightGBM, XGBoost, and ensembling methods, with auto-stacking and bagging enabled under the 'best_quality' preset. Hyperparameter tuning was handled automatically by AutoGluon. On the held-out test set, the model achieved an accuracy of 0.8333 and an F1 score of 0.8. The model has several limitations: correlations in the data do not imply causation, and the labels may reflect selection bias. Performance may decrease for out-of-distribution players or contexts. The model is intended for coursework purposes and should not be used for real personnel decisions. Generative AI tools were used to help structure code and documentation, but the model training and results are real.

instruction: Could you provide a breakdown of speaker activity in this 4-person call?
description: This model is a streaming version of the Sortformer diarizer, designed for speaker diarization in conversations. It is a novel end-to-end neural model trained with unconventional objectives compared to existing end-to-end diarization models. The model employs an Arrival-Order Speaker Cache (AOSC) to store frame-level acoustic embeddings of previously observed speakers. Sortformer resolves the permutation problem in diarization by following the arrival-time order of the speech segments from each speaker. The model can be used to recognize speakers in conversations. The streaming version of Sortformer employs a pre-encode layer in the Fast-Conformer to generate a speaker-cache, which is filtered at each step to retain only high-quality speaker cache vectors. Aside from the speaker-cache management part, the streaming Sortformer follows the architecture of the offline version of Sortformer. The offline version consists of an L-size (17 layers) encoder based on a Fast-Conformer, followed by an 18-layer Transformer encoder with a hidden size of 192, and two feedforward layers with 4 sigmoid outputs for each frame input at the top layer. The model is available for use in the NeMo Framework and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or for fine-tuning on another dataset. The model accepts single-channel (mono) audio sampled at 16,000 Hz. The input tensor is a Ns x 1 matrix for each audio clip, where Ns is the number of samples in the time-series signal. The output of the model is an T x S matrix, where S is the maximum number of speakers (in this model, S = 4), and T is the total number of frames, including zero-padding. Each frame corresponds to a segment of 0.08 seconds of audio. Each element of the T x S matrix represents the speaker activity probability in the [0, 1] range. The model operates in a streaming mode (online mode) and can detect a maximum of 4 speakers; performance degrades on recordings with 5 and more speakers. While the model is designed for long-form audio and can handle recordings that are several hours long, performance may degrade on very long recordings. The model was trained on publicly available speech datasets, primarily in English, and performance may degrade on non-English speech and on out-of-domain data, such as recordings in noisy conditions. The model was trained on a combination of 2445 hours of real conversations and 5150 hours of simulated audio mixtures generated by a specific method. The datasets include Fisher English (LDC), AMI Meeting Corpus, VoxConverse-v0.3, ICSI, AISHELL-4, Third DIHARD Challenge Development (LDC), 2000 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation, split1 (LDC), DiPCo, AliMeeting, 2004-2010 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation (LDC), and Librispeech. The model was trained on 8 nodes of 8×NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs using 90-second long training samples and a batch size of 4. The model can be trained using specific tools and scripts. Inference can be performed with post-processing algorithms using inference tools. The model's performance is evaluated using the Diarization Error Rate (DER) on various datasets, including DIHARD III Eval, CALLHOME-part2, and CH109. Evaluation results show varying DER values depending on the latency and whether post-processing is applied. The model is deployed via NVIDIA RIVA ASR, an accelerated speech AI SDK deployable on-prem, in all clouds, multi-cloud, hybrid, on edge, and embedded. Riva provides world-class out-of-the-box accuracy for the most common languages, best-in-class accuracy with run-time word boosting, and customization of acoustic model, language model, and inverse text normalization. It also provides streaming speech recognition, Kubernetes compatible scaling, and enterprise-grade support. The model is licensed under the CC-BY-4.0 license.

instruction: Can you identify the language used in the text within this image?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It features long context and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. Enhanced multimodal reasoning is demonstrated in STEM and Math tasks, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. The model has upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that enables recognition of a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. The model also supports long-document structure parsing and text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct.

instruction: I recorded a song in Arabic—can you write out the lyrics from the audio?
description: This is a fine-tuned XLSR-53 large model for speech recognition in Arabic. It was fine-tuned using the train and validation splits of unspecified datasets. When using this model, the speech input must be sampled at 16kHz. The model was fine-tuned with the support of GPU credits provided by an unspecified source. The model can be used directly for speech recognition without a language model. It is based on the Wav2Vec2 architecture and is designed to transcribe speech into text. Evaluation results on the Arabic test data of Common Voice show a Word Error Rate (WER) of 39.59% and a Character Error Rate (CER) of 18.18%. These results were obtained using a specific evaluation script. The model has been compared to other Arabic speech recognition models, with varying WER and CER values reported. The model can be accessed and used via the Hugging Face Transformers library. It is important to note that the evaluation results may differ slightly depending on the specific evaluation script used. The model is intended for use in speech recognition tasks involving Arabic speech input.

instruction: Could you describe the key visual elements in this product packaging image?
description: Llama Nemotron Nano VL is a document intelligence vision language model (VLM) designed to query and summarize images from the physical or virtual world. It is deployable in the data center, cloud, and at the edge, including Jetson Orin and laptops, using AWQ 4bit quantization through the TinyChat framework. The model was trained on commercial images for all three stages of training and supports single image inference. Key findings include the necessity of interleaved image-text data over image-text pairs, the importance of unfreezing the language model during interleaved pre-training for in-context learning, and the benefit of re-blending text-only instruction data to enhance both VLM and text-only performance. The model uses a Transformer architecture with a vision encoder and a Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct language encoder. It accepts image and text inputs, with images in RGB format and text in string format. Input images are constrained by a 12-tile layout, with each tile being 512 × 512 pixels, supporting various aspect ratios. The model processes 2D images and 1D text, with a total input and output token limit of 16K. Output is text in string format. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA hardware and software for faster training and inference. It is compatible with the TensorRT-LLM runtime engine and the H100 SXM 80GB microarchitecture, and supports Linux operating systems. The model was trained and evaluated using the NV-Pretraining and NV-CosmosNemotron-SFT datasets, which include a mix of internal and public datasets, as well as synthetic data for specific tasks. Evaluation benchmarks include scores on MMMU Val (48.2%), AI2D (85.0%), ChartQA (86.3%), InfoVQA Val (77.4%), OCRBench (839), OCRBenchV2 English (60.1%), OCRBenchV2 Chinese (37.9%), DocVQA val (91.2%), and VideoMME (54.7%). The model supports use cases such as image summarization, text-image analysis, optical character recognition, interactive Q&A on images, and text chain-of-thought reasoning. It is intended for AI foundry enterprise customers and is available in the Llama-3.1-Nemotron-Nano-VL-8B-V1 version. The model is governed by the Llama 3.1 Community Model License and is built with Llama. Ethical considerations include the responsibility of users to ensure safe integration, including the implementation of guardrails and other safety mechanisms, as outputs may contain political content, misleading information, content security issues, or unwanted bias. Users are responsible for model inputs and outputs and should work with their internal model teams to ensure compliance with industry and use case requirements.

instruction: What is the total number of electricity connections in the city of Ahmedabad according to the data?
description: The model is a M2M100ForConditionalGeneration model fine-tuned for the task of table question answering in low-resource Indic languages, specifically Hindi and Bengali. It is designed to answer questions over structured tabular data, returning either individual cells or entire tables as output. The model supports tasks involving structured information retrieval and processing in languages with limited annotated data and neural models. It is based on the M2M100 architecture, which is a multilingual machine translation model. The model has been trained on large-scale datasets generated through a fully automatic data generation process, which is particularly useful for low-resource languages with limited budgets. Evaluation results show that models trained on these datasets outperform state-of-the-art large language models. The model is intended for use in environments where Indic language support is required, and it includes functionality for processing and linearizing tables, translating between English and Hindi digits, and generating input-output sequences for training and inference. The model is part of a broader research effort focused on scalable data generation and evaluation procedures for low-resource table question answering. The work introduces the first tableQA datasets and models for Hindi and Bengali, and the proposed data generation method can be applied to any low-resource language with a web presence. The model and associated datasets, along with code, are publicly available for research and development purposes.
domain: Natural Language Processing Table Question Answering

instruction: Can you generate a scene of a futuristic spaceship interior with realistic material and lighting effects?
description: OmniX is a family of flow matching generative models designed for unified panoramic perception, generation, and completion. It enables the creation of graphics-ready 3D scenes suitable for physically based rendering, relighting, and simulation. OmniX repurposes 2D generative models for panoramic perception of geometry, textures, and PBR materials, offering a versatile and unified framework. It is based on a lightweight and efficient cross-modal adapter structure that reuses 2D generative priors for a broad range of panoramic vision tasks. OmniX supports tasks such as panoramic image generation from text or image prompts, panoramic perception of geometric, intrinsic, and semantic properties, and the combination of generation and perception to produce panoramic images with multiple property annotations. The model is evaluated using a large-scale synthetic panorama dataset containing high-quality multimodal panoramas from diverse indoor and outdoor scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of OmniX in panoramic visual perception and graphics-ready 3D scene generation. The model is still under development for certain functionalities, such as graphics-ready scene reconstruction and generation, which are noted as being in progress.

instruction: Can you summarize the content of this video and highlight the main events that took place?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that operates PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It supports Visual Coding Boost, generating Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. Advanced Spatial Perception allows the model to judge object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, providing stronger 2D grounding and enabling 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. The model supports Long Context and Video Understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. Enhanced Multimodal Reasoning excels in STEM and Math, providing causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers.
