Upgraded Visual Recognition includes broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling the model to recognize a wide range of subjects such as celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE, which enhances long-horizon video reasoning through full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to provide precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and supports generation hyperparameters for both vision-language and text tasks. The model is intended for deployment in a variety of settings, with options for fine-tuning and customization. The model is part of a series of vision-language models, with prior versions including Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen2-VL, and Qwen-VL, each with their own technical reports and research contributions.

instruction: How would you describe the scene in this image in simple terms?
description: DINOv3 is a family of vision foundation models that outperforms specialized state-of-the-art models across a wide range of settings without requiring fine-tuning. These models produce high-quality dense features that achieve outstanding performance on various vision tasks, significantly surpassing previous self- and weakly-supervised foundation models. DINOv3 includes Vision Transformer (ViT) and ConvNeXt models trained according to the method described in the DINOv3 paper. A total of 12 models are provided: 10 models pretrained on web data (LVD-1689M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch, 5 ViT-S/S+/B/L/H+ models distilled from the ViT-7B, and 4 ConvNeXt-{T/S/B/L} models distilled from the ViT-7B; and 2 models pretrained on satellite data (SAT-493M dataset), including 1 ViT-7B trained from scratch and 1 ViT-L distilled from the ViT-7B. Each Transformer-based model takes an image as input and returns a class token, patch tokens, and register tokens. The models follow a ViT architecture with a patch size of 16. For a 224x224 image, this results in 1 class token + 4 register tokens + 196 patch tokens = 201 tokens. The models can accept larger images provided the image shapes are multiples of the patch size (16). If this condition is not met, the model will crop to the closest smaller multiple of the patch size. The models are vision backbones that provide multi-purpose features for downstream tasks. They can be used without fine-tuning, with downstream classifiers as simple as linear layers, to obtain competitive results on image classification using k-NN or logistic regression classifiers on the class token, or using a linear layer applied on the class token and the average of the patch tokens. They can also be used for image retrieval using nearest neighbors, geometric and semantic 3D keypoint correspondences, depth estimation, semantic segmentation, unsupervised object discovery, video segmentation tracking, and video classification using a small 4-layer attentive probe. While fine-tuning the models can yield some gains, it is recommended to keep this option as a last resort, as the frozen features are expected to provide good performance out-of-the-box. Compared to DINOv2 and SEERv2, DINOv3 delivers somewhat consistent performance across income categories on geographical fairness and diversity, although with a notable performance drop in the low-income bucket compared to the highest-income bucket. DINOv3 also achieves relatively good scores across different regions, improving over its predecessor DINOv2, but a relative difference is still observed between Europe and Africa. Fine-tuning is expected to increase the biases in the features produced by the model as they will be tuned to the fine-tuning labels. The web dataset (LVD-1689M) is a curated dataset of 1,689 million images extracted from a large data pool of 17 billion web images collected from public posts on Instagram. The satellite dataset (SAT-493M) is a dataset of 493 million 512x512 images sampled randomly from Maxar RGB ortho-rectified imagery at 0.6 meter resolution. The training objective includes the DINO self-distillation loss with multi-crop, the iBOT masked-image modeling loss, KoLeo regularization on [CLS] tokens, and Gram anchoring. The training regime uses PyTorch FSDP2 with bf16 and fp8 matrix multiplications. Distillation follows the standard DINOv3 pretraining procedure, except the teacher is a frozen pretrained ViT-7B. The models achieve high performance on a variety of tasks, including image classification, semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and video classification. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on web data, the models achieve results such as 87.0 on IN-ReaL, 60.4 on IN-R, 50.9 on Obj.Net, 49.5 on Ox.-H, 47.0 on ADE20k, 0.403 on NYU, 72.7 on DAVIS, 56.3 on NAVI, and 50.4 on SPair for the ViT-S/16 model. For ConvNeXt backbones distilled on web data, the models achieve results such as 86.6 on IN-ReaL @256px, 87.7 on IN-ReaL @512px, 73.7 on Obj.Net @256px, 74.1 on Obj.Net @512px, 52.6 on ADE20k @256px, 58.7 on ADE20k @512px, 42.7 on NYU @256px, and 0.448 on NYU @512px for the ConvNeXt Tiny model. For ViT backbones pretrained (or distilled) on satellite data, the models achieve results such as 73.0 on m-BEnet, 96.5 on m-brick-kiln, 94.1 on m-eurosat, 60.6 on m-forestnet, 96.0 on m-pv4ger, 57.4 on m-so2sat, and a mean of 79.6 for the ViT-L/16 model. The models were trained using Nvidia H100 GPUs on private infrastructure in the USA, with 61,440 hours of compute time for the ViT-7B model, resulting in an estimated carbon emission of 18t CO2eq. The Vision Transformer models include ViT-S (21M parameters), ViT-S+ (29M parameters), ViT-B (86M parameters), ViT-L (300M parameters), ViT-H+ (840M parameters), and ViT-7B (6716M parameters), all with a patch size of 16 and varying embedding dimensions, register tokens, heads, and FFN types. The ConvNeXt models include ConvNeXt Tiny (29M parameters), ConvNeXt Small (50M parameters), ConvNeXt Base (89M parameters), and ConvNeXt Large (198M parameters). The software used for training is PyTorch 2.7.

instruction: Can you extract the names of all chemical substances mentioned in this medical research paper?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition, specifically for identifying chemical entities from the BC5CDR dataset. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the curated BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, which contains 1,500 PubMed abstracts with 4,409 annotated chemical entities. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It is intended for use in applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the DeBERTa-v3-large architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It has been validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.96, precision of 0.95, recall of 0.97, and accuracy of 0.99. It is ranked among the top-performing models on the BC5CDR_CHEM dataset, with an F1 score of 0.9614. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. It is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and supporting biomedical NLP research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: What are the chemical substances listed in this clinical trial summary?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.92, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.93, and accuracy of 0.98. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is suitable for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited as described in the provided citation.

instruction: How can I retrieve details from a video based on a specific question?
description: TomoroAI/tomoro-colqwen3-embed-4b is a state-of-the-art multimodal embedding model that maps text queries, visual documents (images, PDFs), or short videos into aligned multi-vector embeddings. It is built by merging components with a full vision stack, inheriting robust text retrieval capabilities. The model has been fine-tuned on a curated mixture of data sources and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across ViDoRe V1-V3 in both English and multilingual settings. It offers a significantly reduced embedding footprint compared to other full-dimension Colpali model alternatives. The architecture is based on Qwen3-VL 4B (encoder-only variant) with a 320-dimension projection head. It uses a ColPali-style late interaction method with MaxSim scoring. The model supports up to 1,280 visual tokens per page or 5,120 visual tokens per video, with text prompts constrained by the base context window of 32k. It is capable of handling complex layouts, such as chart-rich PDFs and domain-specific documents, and supports end-to-end retrieval of unseen multimodal documents without requiring OCR or an intermediate vision LLM for summaries. It demonstrates strong performance on non-English document inputs and inherits strong text retrieval performance from the Qwen3-Embedding-8B model. The model generalizes to video retrieval but is not fine-tuned on large-scale video retrieval datasets, and improvements are planned. It is larger than single-vector baselines despite the smaller token dimension. The model is not currently fine-tuned with diverse system instructions, and future improvements are intended through synthetic datasets. It is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, with upstream Qwen checkpoints retaining their community licenses. The training data includes ViDoRe/MTEB corpora and synthetic VisRAG assets. The model is applied to enhance Tomoro’s customized multimodal agentic RAG pipelines, enabling autonomous agents to parse, reason over, and retrieve from large-scale enterprise internal documentation. It supports Tomoro AI's mission to deliver high-quality enterprise multimodal solutions across high-stakes industries.

instruction: Can you provide an estimate of the environmental persistence of a compound with the SMILES 'CC(=O)C1=CC=CC=C1'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict the reaction rate constant with hydroxyl radicals (log kAOH). This property is related to atmospheric degradation and environmental persistence. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It processes SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 128, three layers, three timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and 50 training epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the kAOH dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Can you generate a spoken version of a technical document for accessibility purposes?
description: LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B is an updated end-to-end audio foundation model. Key improvements include a custom, LFM-based audio detokenizer, `llama.cpp` compatible GGUFs for CPU inference, and better ASR and TTS performance. LFM2.5-Audio is an end-to-end multimodal speech and text language model, and as such does not require separate ASR and TTS components. Designed with low latency and real-time conversation in mind, at only 1.5 billion parameters LFM2.5-Audio enables seamless conversational interaction, achieving capabilities on par with much larger models. Our model consists of a pretrained LFM2.5 model as its multimodal backbone, along with a FastConformer-based audio encoder to handle continuous audio inputs, and an RQ-transformer generating discrete tokens coupled with a lightweight audio detokenizer for audio output. LFM2.5-Audio supports two distinct generation routines, each suitable for a set of tasks. Interleaved generation enables real-time speech-to-speech conversational chatbot capabilities, where audio generation latency is key. Sequential generation is suited for non-conversational tasks such as ASR or TTS, and allows the model to switch generated modality on the fly. The model has 1.2 billion parameters for the language model only, an audio encoder with 115 million parameters, a hybrid convolution and attention-based backbone, an audio detokenizer compatible with 8 codebooks, a context length of 32,768 tokens, a vocabulary size of 65,536 for text and 2049*8 for audio, and uses bfloat16 precision. The model is licensed under the LFM Open License v1.0 and supports English. Performance on VoiceBench (audio input) includes scores of 3.76 on AlpacaEval, 3.53 on CommonEval, 3.15 on WildVoice, 30.92 on SD-QA, 33.04 on MMSU, 44.40 on OBQA, 49.54 on BBH, 28.48 on IFEval, 99.04 on Overall. For ASR, the model has a word error rate (WER) average of 7.53, with 15.63 on AMI, 14.56 on Earnings22, 10.47 on GigaSpeech, 1.95 on LibriSpeech-clean, 4.30 on LibriSpeech-other, 2.76 on SPGISpeech, 3.47 on TED-LIUM, and 7.13 on VoxPopuli. The model is intended for use with the `liquid-audio` package, which provides a lower-level interface for custom use cases, including multi-turn, multi-modal chat. The model is suitable for tasks such as multi-turn chat, ASR, and TTS. The model has some caveats, including that it is currently limited to English and that performance may vary depending on the specific use case and deployment environment.

instruction: Could you process this image to detect the pose of each visible person?
description: This model combines an object detector with a custom PyTorch keypoint head and is trained on a 500-person subset of the COCO dataset. The system detects people using DETR and then predicts 17 COCO-style keypoints using heatmap regression. The model is designed to perform keypoint estimation in a top-down approach, where person bounding boxes are first detected, and then individual keypoint heatmaps are generated for each detected person. The architecture processes an input image through DETR to detect person bounding boxes, crops and resizes the detected regions, and feeds them into a CNN keypoint head to produce 17 heatmaps corresponding to the COCO keypoints. The model is trained on 500 images from the COCO val2017 dataset that contain visible persons, with 17 annotated keypoints per person. The model files include trained PyTorch weights, a Colab notebook for training and inference, a configuration file, and a project description. For inference, the model can be loaded using PyTorch, and the DETR object detector can be used to extract person bounding boxes from an input image, which are then used to generate keypoint heatmaps. The model supports optional fine-tuning on custom datasets by converting the data to COCO format and using the provided notebook. The model is based on the DETR architecture for object detection and a custom CNN for keypoint estimation. The system is intended for use in applications requiring human pose estimation from images.

instruction: Is there a way to infer how far each object is from the camera in this sequence of images?
description: DA3 Large is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation, designed as a foundation model with a unified depth-ray representation. It is part of the Any-view Model series, contains 0.35 billion parameters, and is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a Vision Transformer for Visual Geometry with a plain transformer architecture. The model is trained using public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows for a simplified approach without complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: I want to generate a depth map that reflects real-world scale—can you assist with that?
description: The DA3 Metric Large model is specialized for metric depth estimation in monocular settings, ideal for applications requiring real-world scale. It provides canonical metric depth, where multiplying by focal length gives metric depth. The model is part of the Monocular Metric Depth series, has 0.35B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It supports relative depth estimation, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. The model is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer for visual geometry. Its architecture uses a plain transformer with a unified depth-ray representation. The model is trained on public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is sufficient as a backbone without architectural specialization. A singular depth-ray representation eliminates the need for complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on certain domain-specific images. Performance may vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: What is the speaker trying to convey in this clip, and how does the image support that?
description: Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The languages that each modal supports are the following: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model is intended for broad multilingual and multimodal commercial and research use. The model provides uses for general purpose AI systems and applications which require memory/compute constrained environments, latency bound scenarios, strong reasoning (especially math and logic), function and tool calling, general image understanding, optical character recognition, chart and table understanding, multiple image comparison, multi-image or video clip summarization, speech recognition, speech translation, speech QA, and audio understanding. The model is designed to accelerate research on language and multimodal models, for use as a building block for generative AI powered features. The model is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. This release of Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is based on valuable user feedback from the Phi-3 series. Previously, users could use a speech recognition model to talk to the Mini and Vision models. To achieve this, users needed to use a pipeline of two models: one model to transcribe the audio to text, and another model for the language or vision tasks. This pipeline means that the core model was not provided the full breadth of input information – e.g. cannot directly observe multiple speakers, background noises, jointly align speech, vision, language information at the same time on the same representation space. With Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, a single new open model has been trained across text, vision, and audio, meaning that all inputs and outputs are processed by the same neural network. The model employed new architecture, larger vocabulary for efficiency, multilingual, and multimodal support, and better post-training techniques were used for instruction following and function calling, as well as additional data leading to substantial gains on key multimodal capabilities. It is anticipated that Phi-4-multimodal-instruct will greatly benefit app developers and various use cases. The enthusiastic support for the Phi-4 series is greatly appreciated. Feedback on Phi-4 is welcomed and crucial to the model's evolution and improvement. The Phi-4-multimodal-instruct was observed as having strong automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation (ST) performance, surpassing expert ASR model WhisperV3 and ST models SeamlessM4T-v2-Large. It ranked number 1 on the leaderboard with word error rate 6.14% in comparison with the current best model 6.5% as of March 04, 2025. It is the first open-sourced model that can perform speech summarization, and the performance is close to GPT4o. It has a gap with close models, e.g. Gemini-1.5-Flash and GPT-4o-realtime-preview, on speech QA task. Work is being undertaken to improve this capability in the next iterations. Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is capable of processing both image and audio together, and achieves much stronger performance on multiple benchmarks compared to other existing state-of-the-art omni models that can enable audio and visual signal as input. The model has 5.6B parameters and is a multimodal transformer model. The model has the pretrained Phi-4-Mini-Instruct as the backbone language model, and the advanced encoders and adapters of vision and speech. The model's inputs are text, image, and audio. It is best suited for prompts using the chat format. The model's context length is 128K tokens. The model was trained on 512 A100-80G GPUs for 28 days. The training data includes 5 trillion text tokens, 2.3M speech hours, and 1.1T image-text tokens. The model's outputs are generated text in response to the input. The model was trained between December 2024 and January 2025. The model is a static model trained on offline datasets with the cutoff date of June 2024 for publicly available data. The model supports the following languages: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model's release date is February 2025. The model's training data includes a wide variety of sources, totaling 5 trillion text tokens, and is a combination of publicly available documents filtered for quality, selected high-quality educational data, and code; newly created synthetic, “textbook-like” data for the purpose of teaching math, coding, common sense reasoning, general knowledge of the world (e.g., science, daily activities, theory of mind, etc.); high quality human labeled data in chat format; selected high-quality image-text interleave data; synthetic and publicly available image, multi-image, and video data; anonymized in-house speech-text pair data with strong/weak transcriptions; selected high-quality publicly available and anonymized in-house speech data with task-specific supervisions; selected synthetic speech data; and synthetic vision-speech data. Focus was placed on the quality of data that could potentially improve the reasoning ability for the model, and the publicly available documents were filtered to contain a preferred level of knowledge. As an example, the result of a game in premier league on a particular day might be good training data for large foundation models, but such information was removed for the Phi-4-multimodal-instruct to leave more model capacity for reasoning for the model's small size. The data collection process involved sourcing information from publicly available documents, with a focus on filtering out undesirable documents and images. To safeguard privacy, image and text data sources were filtered to remove or scrub potentially personal data from the training data. The decontamination process involved normalizing and tokenizing the dataset, then generating and comparing n-grams between the target dataset and benchmark datasets. Samples with matching n-grams above a threshold were flagged as contaminated and removed from the dataset. A detailed contamination report was generated, summarizing the matched text, matching ratio, and filtered results for further analysis. Like other language models, the Phi family of models can potentially behave in ways that are unfair, unreliable, or offensive. Some of the limiting behaviors to be aware of include: Quality of Service: The Phi models are trained primarily on English language content across text, speech, and visual inputs, with some additional multilingual coverage. Performance may vary significantly across different modalities and languages: Text: Languages other than English will experience reduced performance, with varying levels of degradation across different non-English languages. English language varieties with less representation in the training data may perform worse than standard American English. Speech: Speech recognition and processing shows similar language-based performance patterns, with optimal performance for standard American English accents and pronunciations. Other English accents, dialects, and non-English languages may experience lower recognition accuracy and response quality. Background noise, audio quality, and speaking speed can further impact performance. Vision: Visual processing capabilities may be influenced by cultural and geographical biases in the training data. The model may show reduced performance when analyzing images containing text in non-English languages or visual elements more commonly found in non-Western contexts. Image quality, lighting conditions, and composition can also affect processing accuracy. Multilingual performance and safety gaps: We believe it is important to make language models more widely available across different languages, but the Phi 4 models still exhibit challenges common across multilingual releases. As with any deployment of LLMs, developers will be better positioned to test for performance or safety gaps for their linguistic and cultural context and customize the model with additional fine-tuning and appropriate safeguards. Representation of Harms & Perpetuation of Stereotypes: These models can over- or under-represent groups of people, erase representation of some groups, or reinforce demeaning or negative stereotypes. Despite safety post-training, these limitations may still be present due to differing levels of representation of different groups, cultural contexts, or prevalence of examples of negative stereotypes in training data that reflect real-world patterns and societal biases. Inappropriate or Offensive Content: These models may produce other types of inappropriate or offensive content, which may make it inappropriate to deploy for sensitive contexts without additional mitigations that are specific to the case. Information Reliability: Language models can generate nonsensical content or fabricate content that might sound reasonable but is inaccurate or outdated. Limited Scope for Code: The majority of Phi 4 training data is based in Python and uses common packages such as

instruction: Can you create a 3D animation of a person doing a slow, controlled squat with their arms extended forward?
description: HY-Motion 1.0 is a series of text-to-3D human motion generation models based on Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and Flow Matching. It enables developers to generate skeleton-based 3D character animations from simple text prompts, which can be directly integrated into various 3D animation pipelines. This model series is the first to scale DiT-based text-to-motion models to the billion-parameter level, achieving significant improvements in instruction-following capabilities and motion quality over existing open-source models. The HY-Motion 1.0 series includes two models: HY-Motion-1.0, a standard text-to-motion model with 1.0 billion parameters, and HY-Motion-1.0-Lite, a lightweight version with 0.46 billion parameters. Both models require a minimum of 24GB GPU VRAM for operation. The models are trained using a three-stage process: large-scale pre-training on over 3,000 hours of diverse motion data to learn a broad motion prior, high-quality fine-tuning on 400 hours of curated, high-quality 3D motion data to enhance motion detail and smoothness, and reinforcement learning using human feedback and reward models to refine instruction-following and motion naturalness. HY-Motion 1.0 supports macOS, Windows, and Linux. It provides a command-line interface for batch inference and a Gradio-based web interface for interactive visualization. Users are advised to use English prompts under 60 words, focusing on action descriptions or detailed movements of the limbs and torso. The model does not support non-humanoid characters, subjective or visual attributes, environment or camera descriptions, multi-person interactions, or special modes such as seamless loops or in-place animations. Example prompts include descriptions of specific physical actions like squats, climbing, standing up, and walking. The model is evaluated for its state-of-the-art performance in instruction-following and motion quality, with the billion-scale models outperforming comparable open-source models. Users should note that the model requires specific settings to reduce GPU VRAM usage, such as limiting the number of seeds, using short text prompts, and generating motions under 5 seconds in length.

instruction: Could you locate the signature area in this image of a signed agreement?
description: YOLOS is a Vision Transformer (ViT) trained using the DETR loss. A base-sized YOLOS model achieves 42 AP on COCO validation 2017, similar to DETR and more complex frameworks such as Faster R-CNN. This model is a finetuned version of YOLOS, specifically adapted for detecting handwritten signatures in document images. It is intended for use in scanned documents, contracts, or forms. The model was finetuned using a dataset built from two public datasets, unified and processed by an external contributor. The processed dataset contains 1,980 training images, 420 validation images, and 419 testing images, all in COCO JSON format with a resolution of 640x640 pixels. Performance metrics on the testing set include an mAP50 of 0.856 and an mAP50-95 of 0.395. Inference times are 0.262 seconds on CPU and 0.014 seconds on GPU, measured on a laptop with an Intel Core i7-9750H CPU and an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 GPU. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 license, which allows for permissive use, including commercial applications, without the requirement to share modified code. The model must not be used for fraudulent purposes, such as forging signatures or any illegal activity. It is not suitable for detecting non-signature elements in documents and should not be solely relied on for high-stakes decisions without human validation. The model may exhibit bias if the training data lacks diversity in signature styles or cultural context. False positives and negatives can occur, which may impact document validation. Performance may degrade on poor-quality images or in challenging visual conditions such as noise or lighting. To improve performance, it is recommended to fine-tune the model with diverse and representative samples, include human oversight for critical use cases, use clean, high-resolution images, and apply preprocessing if needed. The model is designed for legitimate signature detection in documents and should be used in accordance with legal and ethical standards, ensuring privacy and responsible deployment.

instruction: Please find a document that includes a table comparing the performance of different machine learning algorithms.
description: The nvidia/llama-nemoretriever-colembed-1b-v1 is a late interaction embedding model fine-tuned for query-document retrieval. It accepts text queries and documents in the form of text or page images as input and outputs ColBERT-style multi-vector numerical representations for both. It is the smaller version of a model that achieved 1st place on ViDoRe V1 (nDCG@5), ViDoRe V2 (nDCG@5), and MTEB VisualDocumentRetrieval (Rank Borda) as of June 27, 2025. The nvidia/llama-nemoretriever-colembed-1b-v1 achieves 2nd place on these benchmarks. The model is based on a transformer architecture and is built on top of a vision-language model derived from google/siglip2-giant-opt-patch16-384 and meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B. It is intended for researchers exploring applications that require understanding or retrieving information across both text and image modalities, particularly in multimodal RAG systems where queries are in text format and documents are images such as pages, text, charts, tables, or infographics. Potential applications include multimedia search engines, cross-modal retrieval systems, and conversational AI with rich input understanding. The model has a maximum context length of 8192 tokens for both text queries and documents, with longer texts needing to be chunked or truncated. For images, the model scales them into multiple tiles of 512x512 and requires them to be in Python PIL format. The model outputs embedding vectors of a specified dimension for each input token. It is designed and optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. The model was trained on 12.74 million QA pairs across 23 datasets and evaluated on multiple benchmarks for Visual Document Retrieval, including ViDoRe V1, ViDoRe V2, and MTEB Visual Document Retrieval. On these benchmarks, the model achieved an average NDCG@5 score of 0.9050 on ViDoRe V1 (06/27/2025), 0.6209 on ViDoRe V2 (06/27/2025), and 0.8238 on MTEB Visual Document Retrieval. The model is for non-commercial/research use only and is governed by specific terms of use. It is built with Meta Llama 3 and improved using Qwen. The model requires transformers version 4.49.0 and flash attention for usage. It is supported on Linux operating systems and compatible with A100 40GB, A100 80GB, and H100 80GB hardware. NVIDIA encourages users to ensure the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and to address any potential misuse. Security vulnerabilities or concerns should be reported to NVIDIA.

instruction: Could you extract the content from this image of a document with a complex layout, including headers and footers?
description: olmOCR-2-7B-1025-FP8 is a quantized version of the olmOCR model, fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct using a specific dataset. It has been further fine-tuned using GRPO RL training to improve its performance on math equations, tables, and other challenging OCR cases. The model is designed to process document images and extract relevant text and metadata. It is intended to be used via the olmOCR toolkit, which provides an efficient inference setup using VLLM to handle millions of documents at scale. The model expects a single document image as input, with the longest dimension rendered to 1288 pixels. The prompt must include additional metadata from the document, which can be generated using the methods provided by the olmOCR toolkit. The model scores 83.0 on ArXiv, 82.3 on Old Scans Math, 84.9 on Tables, 47.7 on Old Scans, 96.1 on Headers and Footers, 83.7 on Multi column, 81.9 on Long tiny text, and 99.7 on Base, with an overall score of 82.4 ± 1.1 on the olmOCR-Bench when used with the olmOCR pipeline v0.4.0. This model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2's policies.

instruction: How do I make this character adopt the same posture as the one in the provided photo?
description: AnyPose LoRAs are designed to enable pose transfer between images without the need for control nets or openpose. The model allows users to copy the pose from a reference image onto another image by using a detailed prompt. It is optimized for fast inference and works with a single reference image as a pose guide. The model supports both simple and complex poses, including yoga poses, and is intended to be used with Qwen Image Edit 2511. It uses a 4-step lightning LoRA setup with both base and helper weights set to 0.7 for optimal performance. The model is trained primarily on 3D poses generated via Blender, which allows for precise control over character positioning and movement. However, it has limitations when applied to 2D or flat art styles such as cel-shaded or pixel art, where performance may be poor. It also struggles with highly technical poses and images containing more than one character. The model may sometimes replace the background of the reference image with the background of the target image, which can be corrected by explicitly prompting for the desired background. Users are advised to provide full-body images with visible environments to improve consistency. The model may hallucinate or incorrectly fill in missing areas, but these issues can often be addressed with additional prompting. A version 2 of the model is planned to address these limitations and improve support for non-humanoid characters and overall consistency.

instruction: Can you create a video of a chef preparing a gourmet dish in a professional kitchen?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. The A14B model series uses a two-expert design: a high-noise expert for early-stage layout and a low-noise expert for later-stage detail refinement, with each expert having approximately 14B parameters and a total of 27B parameters. The transition between experts is determined by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with a defined threshold step for switching. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also introduces a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, and supports both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps. This model can run on consumer-grade graphics cards like the 4090 and is one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The T2V-A14B model supports generating 5-second videos at both 480P and 720P resolutions and delivers outstanding video generation quality. On the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, the model surpasses leading commercial models across most key evaluation dimensions. The model is available for download via Hugging Face and ModelScope, and supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU inference using FSDP and DeepSpeed Ulysses. Prompt extension is available via Dashscope API or local Qwen models to enhance video quality. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with the license terms and applicable laws.

instruction: What is the estimated distance between the two trees in the photo?
description: SpaceThinker-Qwen2.5VL-3B is a multimodal vision-language model (VLM) designed to enhance spatial reasoning through test-time compute. It is a fine-tuned version of UCSC-VLAA/VLAA-Thinker-Qwen2.5VL-3B, using synthetic reasoning traces generated by a 3D scene reconstruction pipeline involving Molmo, VGGT, and SAM2. The model has 3.75B parameters in FP16 format and is based on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B architecture. It was fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with a rank of 128 and an alpha of 256, targeting modules q_proj and v_proj. The training used AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 2e-5, a batch size of 1, and 3 epochs. The model is trained on a dataset of over 12K synthetic spatial reasoning traces, covering question types such as distances (with units), spatial relations (e.g., left-of, above, inside), and object properties. It is designed to support tasks involving spatial reasoning, including distance estimation, object relations, and scene interpretation. The model demonstrates strong performance on benchmarks such as SpatialScore, SpaCE-10, and QSpatial++, particularly in the SpatialScore-Hard split, where it achieves an overall score of 19.47. It also performs well in the SpaCE-10 benchmark, achieving an overall score of 32.72. On the QSpatial++ benchmark, it achieves a success rate of 55% and a sample completion rate of 99 out of 100. The model is intended for applications such as robotics and drones, where planning and navigation in 3D space are required. However, it has limitations, including potential performance degradation in cluttered environments or under certain camera perspectives, and it was trained on synthetic data over an internet image dataset. Multimodal biases from the base model may persist, and the model is not intended for safety-critical or legal decision-making. Users are advised to evaluate outputs critically and consider domain-specific fine-tuning for improved safety and performance. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0.

instruction: Based on the molecular formula 'C1CCCCC1', can you predict its plasma-related property?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict plasma level volume or protein binding parameter, which may relate to pharmacokinetics or blood partitioning behavior. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. It is designed for a regression task, producing a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and features an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model has 128 hidden channels, 3 layers, 3 timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.1, a learning rate of 0.0005, a weight decay of 0.0001, a batch size of 32, and is trained for 50 epochs with a patience of 10. The model was trained on the PLV dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Could you create a voice recording of a fictional courtroom scene with distinct voices for the judge, lawyer, and witness?
description: VibeVoice is a novel open-source text-to-speech model designed to generate expressive, long-form, multi-speaker conversational audio, such as podcasts, from text. It addresses challenges in traditional TTS systems, particularly in scalability, speaker consistency, and natural turn-taking. A core innovation is the use of continuous speech tokenizers (Acoustic and Semantic) operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz, which preserve audio fidelity while improving computational efficiency for long sequences. VibeVoice employs a next-token diffusion framework, combining a Large Language Model (LLM) to understand textual context and dialogue flow with a diffusion head to generate high-fidelity acoustic details. The model can synthesize speech up to 90 minutes long with up to 4 distinct speakers, surpassing the typical 1-2 speaker limits of many prior models. The LLM used is Qwen2.5. The Acoustic Tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks, achieving 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. Encoder and decoder components are approximately 340M parameters each. The Semantic Tokenizer mirrors the Acoustic Tokenizer's architecture (without VAE components) and is trained with an ASR proxy task. The Diffusion Head is a lightweight module (4 layers, ~600M parameters) conditioned on LLM hidden states, predicting acoustic VAE features using a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process. It uses Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 32,768 tokens. Training involves separate pre-training of the Acoustic and Semantic tokenizers, followed by training of the LLM and diffusion head with a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k -> 16K -> 32K). The model is intended for research purposes only and is not recommended for commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. It is limited to English and Chinese data, and outputs in other languages may be unintelligible or offensive. The model does not handle background noise, music, or other sound effects, and does not generate overlapping speech segments in conversations. Potential risks include misuse for deepfakes, disinformation, or voice impersonation. To mitigate these risks, an audible disclaimer is embedded in every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added for verification. Inference requests are logged (hashed) for abuse pattern detection and publishing aggregated statistics quarterly. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and ethically, including securing appropriate rights and anonymizing data prior to use with VibeVoice. The model is developed by members of Microsoft Research, and feedback or reports of issues can be sent to VibeVoice@microsoft.com.

instruction: Can you provide a depth map for this complex urban scene?
description: DA3 Nested is a model that combines the any-view Giant model with the metric Large model for metric-scale visual geometry reconstruction. It is the recommended model that integrates all available capabilities. The model belongs to the Nested series and contains 1.40B parameters. It is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0, which restricts its use to non-commercial purposes. The model supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, pose conditioning, 3D Gaussians, metric depth estimation, and sky segmentation. It is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer architecture with a plain transformer backbone and a unified depth-ray representation. The model is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. It significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images, and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity. The model is intended for non-commercial use only due to its license.

instruction: Can you create an image of a futuristic cityscape with flying vehicles and glowing towers?
description: This is a GGUF quantized version of the FLUX.2 [dev] model, which uses a methodology for state-of-the-art performance, with important layers upcasted to higher precision. FLUX.2 [dev] is a 32 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating, editing, and combining images based on text instructions. It is designed for open text-to-image generation, single-reference editing, and multi-reference editing, allowing character, object, and style reference without the need for additional training. The model is trained using guidance distillation, making it more efficient. It has open weights to support scientific research and empower artists to develop innovative workflows. Generated outputs can be used for personal, scientific, and commercial purposes as described in the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License. A reference implementation and sampling code are provided for developers and creatives to build upon the model. FLUX.2 [dev] is available in both quantized and non-quantized versions. For local deployment on consumer-grade graphics cards, such as an RTX 4090 or RTX 5090, instructions are available on the GitHub page. Black Forest Labs has evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in the model, including the generation of unlawful content such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for NSFW and known CSAM content, with collaboration from the Internet Watch Foundation. Post-training mitigation involved targeted fine-tuning to inhibit certain behaviors and suppress concepts that could lead to synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluations, including internal and external third-party testing, were conducted to identify and address residual risks. The final FLUX.2 [dev] checkpoint demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks and showed higher resilience than leading open-weight models. The model includes filters for NSFW and IP-infringing content at input and output, and developers must use these filters or manual review processes under the terms of the FLUX.2 [dev] Non-Commercial License. Inference filters are applied to the API for FLUX.2 Pro, using both in-house and third-party filters to prevent CSAM and NCII outputs. The model also includes content provenance features, such as pixel-layer watermarking and links to the C2PA standard for metadata. The FLUX.2 Pro API applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to output content. Use of the model and access to the API are governed by the FLUX [dev] Non-Commercial License, Developer Terms of Service, and Usage Policy, which prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. The model is monitored for patterns of violative use after release, and takedown requests are issued to websites or businesses that misuse the model. Users or developers who repeatedly violate policies may be banned. A dedicated email address is provided for community feedback, and the company maintains reporting relationships with organizations such as the Internet Watch Foundation and the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children.

instruction: Can you retrieve the most relevant paragraph in Persian related to education reform?
description: The Multilingual-E5-base model is a multilingual text embedding model with 12 layers and an embedding size of 768. It is designed to encode queries and passages for tasks such as passage retrieval, semantic similarity, and bitext mining. The model supports 100 languages, including English, Chinese, and others, but performance may degrade for low-resource languages. It is initialized from a multilingual foundation and trained on a mixture of multilingual datasets. The first stage of training involves contrastive pre-training with weak supervision using a large number of text pairs from various sources, including titles, page content, news content, translation pairs, and more. The second stage involves supervised fine-tuning on labeled datasets, primarily in English and Chinese. The model is evaluated on benchmark tasks and achieves an average MRR@10 score of 65.9, outperforming baselines such as BM25 and mDPR. It is also evaluated on the MTEB benchmark. The model requires input texts to be prefixed with 'query: ' or 'passage: ' to maintain performance, as this is how it was trained. Cosine similarity scores typically range from 0.7 to 1.0 due to the use of a low temperature in the InfoNCE contrastive loss, but the relative order of scores is what matters for tasks like text retrieval. Long texts are truncated to a maximum of 512 tokens.

instruction: Design a scene of a young inventor in a workshop surrounded by robots and mechanical contraptions.
description: HunyuanImage-3.0 is a native multimodal model that unifies multimodal understanding and generation within an autoregressive framework. It is designed for image generation and supports tasks such as text-to-image generation, prompt self-rewrite, and multi-turn interaction. The model employs a unified autoregressive architecture, moving beyond traditional DiT-based designs, and is the largest open-source image generation Mixture of Experts (MoE) model to date, with 64 experts and a total of 80 billion parameters, of which 13 billion are activated per token. It demonstrates superior image generation performance, achieving a balance between semantic accuracy and visual quality through advanced dataset curation and reinforcement learning post-training. The model also exhibits intelligent world-knowledge reasoning, allowing it to interpret user intent and elaborate on sparse prompts to produce detailed and contextually appropriate visual outputs. Evaluation results include structured semantic alignment scores (SSAE) and human evaluations (GSB), with HunyuanImage-3.0 showing strong performance in both. The model requires a Linux operating system, NVIDIA GPU with CUDA support, and at least 3×80GB GPU memory for optimal performance. It is available for download and use with specific dependencies and installation instructions provided. Performance optimizations such as FlashAttention and FlashInfer can significantly speed up inference. The model is currently available in a pretrain checkpoint and an Instruct checkpoint, with the latter supporting prompt rewriting and reasoning. Users are advised to consult the official documentation for best practices in prompt writing and system configuration.

instruction: What is the main message or theme of this comic strip, and how is it conveyed visually?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. It also provides improved long-document structure parsing and text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct.

instruction: What is the best way to match features in two images with partial occlusions?
description: LightGlue is a neural network designed to match two sets of local features by jointly finding correspondences and rejecting non-matchable points. It is a variant of the LightGlue model trained on DISK and is available under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license. The model requires the `kornia` library to be installed and is usable with the Transformers library. LightGlue was proposed by Philipp Lindenberger, Paul-Edouard Sarlin, and Marc Pollefeys. It is useful for tasks such as image matching, homography estimation, and pose estimation between images. The model is adaptive to the difficulty of the problem, with faster inference on image pairs that are easier to match. It is more efficient in terms of memory and computation, more accurate, and easier to train than the previous state-of-the-art model, SuperGlue. LightGlue can introspect the confidence of its predictions and adapts its depth and width during inference. It can stop at an early layer if all predictions are ready and discards non-matchable points early in the process. The model is developed by ETH Zurich - Computer Vision and Geometry Lab and is designed for feature matching and pose estimation tasks in computer vision. It can be applied to a variety of multiple-view geometry problems and can handle challenging real-world indoor and outdoor environments. However, it may not perform well on tasks that require different types of visual understanding, such as object detection or image classification. LightGlue is trained on large annotated datasets for pose estimation, including image pairs with ground truth correspondences and unmatched keypoints derived from ground truth poses and depth maps. It is first pre-trained with synthetic homographies sampled from 1 million images and then fine-tuned with the MegaDepth dataset, which includes 1 million crowd-sourced images of 196 tourism landmarks. The model is trained using a supervised setup and runs in real-time on a modern GPU, with a forward pass taking approximately 44 milliseconds (22 FPS) for an image pair. It has 13.7 million parameters and is suitable for integration into modern Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) or Structure-from-Motion (SfM) systems.

instruction: How should an organization prepare for a regulatory audit under NIS2 directives?
description: ELISARCyberAIEdge7B-LoRA-GGUF is a LoRA-finetuned, GGUF-quantized version of the Mistral-7B backbone, tailored for edge deployment in cybersecurity and blue-team AI scenarios. It is designed for contextualized cybersecurity risk assessment using Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Agentic AI capabilities. The model is based on Mistral-7B-v0.3 (FP16 / BF16) and includes a LoRA adapter called `sallani/ELISARCyberAIEdge7B`. It has been converted to GGUF format and optionally quantized to Q4_K_M (4-bit) for efficient inference on resource-constrained devices such as NVIDIA T4 and desktop GPUs. The resulting model file, `elisar_merged.gguf`, is approximately 160 MiB in size and can be deployed offline using frameworks or through minimal Torch-based inference. The model supports real-world use cases including threat modeling (Blue ELISAR), offensive use-case generation (Red ELISAR), and GRC compliance automation (GRC ELISAR). It is intended for tasks such as ISO/IEC 42001 and NIS2 risk evaluation, threat scenario generation, AI audit preparation and reporting, and secure AI system design. The model is developed by Dr. Sabri Sallani, an expert in Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity. Users are advised to use the instruction format `### Instruction:` / `### Response:` and to include relevant logs or code in prompts. The model is not intended as a replacement for certified analysts. The model is part of the ELISAR framework, which is described in a paper titled 'ELISAR: An Adaptive Framework for Cybersecurity Risk Assessment Powered by GenAI' to be published by Springer on July 10, 2025.

instruction: I need to identify the region in the photo that shows 'a blue sky with scattered clouds'. Can you assist?
description: Talk2DINO is a hybrid model that combines the spatial accuracy of DINOv2 with the language understanding capabilities of CLIP to address the task of Open-Vocabulary Segmentation (OVS). OVS involves segmenting images based on free-form textual concepts without predefined training classes. While CLIP can generate segmentation masks using Vision Transformers, it struggles with spatial localization due to global alignment of image and text features. On the other hand, self-supervised models like DINO excel in fine-grained visual encoding but lack language integration. Talk2DINO bridges this gap by aligning CLIP's textual embeddings with DINOv2's patch-level features through a learned mapping function, without fine-tuning the underlying backbones. During training, the model uses DINOv2's attention maps to selectively align local visual patches with textual embeddings. This approach enhances the segmentation process by producing more natural and less noisy segmentations and effectively distinguishing foreground objects from the background. Experimental results show that Talk2DINO achieves state-of-the-art performance on several unsupervised OVS benchmarks.

instruction: Please determine the primary subject in this picture and provide its classification.
description: LSE-DINOv2 is a DINOv2 Vision Transformer enhanced with a Deep Equilibrium Model (DEM) based local scale adaptation mechanism to improve scale equivariance, ensuring local scale consistency. The model learns to adaptively scale different image regions based on their content using fixed-point iteration to find optimal deformation parameters. This adaptation is applied at multiple transformer layers, and the model is fine-tuned on the ImageNet-1K dataset for image classification. The base architecture is a ViT-Base model with a 14×14 patch size, 224×224 image size, 768 embedding dimension, 12 transformer blocks, 12 attention heads, and 4 register tokens. The DEM adapter includes 3 phi layers, local scaling grids of [16x16, 8x8, 8x8], 2 DEQ layers, CNN channels of [3, 64, 128], 5 DEQ iterations, and is applied at the input, Layer 4, and Layer 8. The model was trained on ImageNet-1K for 20 epochs with a batch size of 200, using the AdamW optimizer with a backbone learning rate of 5e-5 and a DEM learning rate of 1e-5. The model introduces some computational overhead due to DEQ iterations and requires higher memory usage compared to standard DINOv2. It also depends on the `timm` library and optionally the `torchdeq` library. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 license and is described in the paper titled 'Local Scale Equivariance with Latent Deep Equilibrium Canonicalizer' by Rahman et al., published in the Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision in 2025.

instruction: Can you change the background to a vibrant forest and adjust the lighting to look like golden hour?
description: Lucy Edit 1.1 Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of edits, such as clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. Built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, it inherits a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, making it easier to adapt existing scripts and workflows. The model preserves motion and composition perfectly, allowing for precise edits. It offers improved edit reliability compared to common inference time methods. It supports wardrobe and accessory changes, such as changing outfits and adding items like glasses, earrings, and hats. It can also perform character changes, replacing characters with monsters, animals, or known characters. It supports scene replacements, such as transforming a scene into a 2D cartoon. It accepts pure text instructions without requiring finetuning or masks for common edits. It can perform global edits to the background and style, maintaining subject identity. It provides seamless blending with cleaner boundaries and more coherent lighting across edits. It supports the insertion of general objects with improved spatial grounding. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of Wan2.2 5B. Version 1.1 includes better overall quality, higher fidelity details, stronger prompt adherence, and improved identity preservation. It offers more reliable global edits with better subject preservation and control. It provides better blending and seamless edits with cleaner boundaries, fewer artifacts, and improved color and lighting consistency across frames. It supports the addition of general objects, including wearable, handheld, and general scene objects, with improved spatial grounding, occlusion, and shadows. Lucy Edit 1.1 is designed for precise, realistic, and identity-preserving video edits. Prompts with 20–30 descriptive words work best, and using the right trigger words helps the model understand the intent. Supported edit types include clothing changes, human or character replacement, object replacement, color changes, adding objects, and global transformations. The model excels at identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and prompt adherence. Longer prompts describing style, appearance, and context improve results. Frame counts of 81 produce better temporal consistency than shorter clips. Version 1.1 improves boundary blending and temporal coherence across edits. The model is available via a hosted API and is compatible with the Diffusers pipeline. Future integrations include ComfyUI custom nodes and local inference nodes. The model is based on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges contributions from the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face.

instruction: Can you turn this short story into a spoken version that sounds natural and expressive?
description: GLM-TTS is a high-quality text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis system based on large language models, supporting zero-shot voice cloning and streaming inference. The system uses a two-stage architecture that combines an LLM for speech token generation and a Flow Matching model for waveform synthesis. By introducing a Multi-Reward Reinforcement Learning framework, GLM-TTS significantly improves the expressiveness of generated speech, achieving more natural emotional control compared to traditional TTS systems. Key features include the ability to clone any speaker's voice with just 3-10 seconds of prompt audio, the use of a multi-reward reinforcement learning framework (GRPO) to optimize prosody and emotion, high-quality speech synthesis comparable to commercial systems with reduced Character Error Rate (CER), support for 'Hybrid Phoneme + Text' input for precise pronunciation control, real-time audio generation suitable for interactive applications, and optimization for Chinese and English mixed text. The system follows a two-stage design: Stage 1 uses a Llama-based model to convert input text into speech token sequences, and Stage 2 uses a Flow model to convert token sequences into high-quality mel-spectrograms, which are then turned into waveforms by a vocoder. To address flat emotional expression, GLM-TTS uses a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm with multiple reward functions (Similarity, CER, Emotion, Laughter) to align the LLM's generation strategy. Evaluated on seed-tts-eval, GLM-TTS_RL achieves the lowest Character Error Rate (CER) while maintaining high speaker similarity. The model GLM-TTS_RL achieves a CER of 0.89 and a SIM score of 76.4, outperforming other models such as Seed-TTS (CER 1.12, SIM 79.6), CosyVoice2 (CER 1.38, SIM 75.7), and F5-TTS (CER 1.53, SIM 76.0). GLM-TTS is open-source, while Seed-TTS is not. The system is designed to be used for real-time applications and supports phoneme-level control for precise pronunciation. It is optimized for both Chinese and English mixed text and is intended for use in interactive applications requiring streaming inference.

instruction: How can someone start learning about artificial intelligence in a simple way?
description: GLM-4.6V-Flash is a 6-bit quantized version of the GLM-4.6V-Flash model, optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or appropriateness of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or availability. Users are solely responsible for any damage resulting from their use of the model.

instruction: I’m trying to determine if this voice was generated using software. Could you help with that?
description: This is a fine-tuned Wav2Vec2 transformer model for binary audio classification, designed to distinguish between real human speech and AI-generated speech from text-to-speech and voice cloning software. The model is trained to detect audio generated by platforms including ElevenLabs, Amazon Polly, Hexgrad Kokoro, Hume AI, Speechify, and Luvvoice. It is based on a pre-trained Wav2Vec2-XLSR model that was already fine-tuned for deepfake audio detection, and it uses transfer learning with selective layer freezing. The feature extractor and bottom 12 transformer encoder layers are frozen, while the top 12 transformer encoder layers and a classification head are trained. The model has approximately 160 million trainable parameters. It is optimized for 2.5-13 second audio clips, resampled to 16kHz and converted to mono. The model outputs logits for two classes: real (human) audio and fake (AI-generated) audio, which can be converted to probabilities using softmax. The model achieves high performance on the validation set, with an accuracy of 97.9%, a ROC-AUC of 0.998, and a balanced accuracy of 97.9%. However, it is important to note that the high performance is due to the base model's existing deepfake detection capabilities and the similarity of the training data to the validation set. The model may not generalize well to novel TTS engines, advanced voice cloning systems, real-time voice manipulation, or low-quality recordings. The model is intended for use on audio files in WAV, MP3, or FLAC formats, and it is recommended to test it on TTS engines not included in the training data for true generalization assessment.

instruction: Can you process multiple people in a single image and provide joint positions for each?
description: RTMPose-Body2d is a machine learning model designed for human pose estimation, detecting the location and confidence of 133 joints. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is part of the RTMPose series. The model has an input resolution of 256x192, contains 17.9 million parameters, and has a model size of 68.5 MB in float precision and 18.2 MB in w8a16 precision. It is intended to run on Qualcomm® devices, including various Snapdragon® and QCS chipsets, using different runtimes such as TFLITE, QNN_DLC, and ONNX. Performance metrics include inference times ranging from 0.75 ms to 176.198 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 0 MB to 189 MB, depending on the device and precision. The model is primarily executed on the NPU, with some CPU-based execution for specific configurations. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment using PyTorch's JIT tracing and Qualcomm® AI Hub APIs. It supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and deployment to Android using TensorFlow Lite or QNN shared libraries. The model is available for use with an API token through the Qualcomm® AI Hub, and additional details on performance across devices can be found on the Qualcomm® AI Hub website. The original implementation of RTMPose-Body2d is licensed separately.

instruction: Can you explain the concept of selective parameter activation in simple terms?
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, which is lower than the total number of parameters they contain. The models are available in two sizes, with the raw parameter count of this model being 6B, but the architecture design allows it to be run with a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 2B model by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator. They use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the model. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens, subtracting the request input tokens. The training dataset includes a wide variety of sources totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The dataset includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio. Rigorous data filtering methods were applied, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional methods based on content quality and safety. The models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. Evaluation results show that the models perform well across a range of benchmarks, including reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional benchmarks. The models were evaluated for safety and ethics, including child safety, content safety, and representational harms. They showed safe levels of performance relative to previous Gemma models, with minimal policy violations and significant improvements in high severity violations. The models have certain limitations, including potential biases in training data, challenges with complex tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and potential factual inaccuracies. Ethical considerations include the risk of perpetuating biases, generating harmful content, misuse for malicious purposes, and privacy violations. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, and education. They provide high-performance open generative model implementations designed for responsible AI development.

instruction: Is there a way to detect if a player is currently holding a piece in this image?
description: This repository contains fine-tuned models for a Chess Move Tracking pipeline. The first model, yolo11s_pose_chessboard.pt, is based on the YOLO11s-pose architecture and is designed for keypoint detection, specifically for localizing the four corners of a chessboard (a1, h1, a8, h8). It operates on raw video frames, which may be rotated or occluded, and identifies one class, the chessboard. The second model, yolo11m_pieces.pt, is based on the YOLO11m architecture and is designed for object detection, identifying 13 classes: Hand, bB, bK, bN, bP, bQ, bR, wB, wK, wN, wP, wQ, and wR. It operates on warped top-down chessboard images of size 640x640. These models are intended to be used with the ultralytics library, which must be installed via pip. The chessboard corner localization model is used to detect the four semantic corners of the chessboard to assist with perspective warping. The chess pieces detection model is used to detect the 12 chess pieces and the hand on a warped top-down view of the board.

instruction: What are the most striking features of this image and why?
