description: Next 12B is a 12-billion parameter multimodal Vision-Language Model (VLM) based on Gemma 3, fine-tuned to deliver exceptional performance in both text and image understanding. It is designed for superior understanding and generation of text and image descriptions, advanced reasoning and context-aware multimodal outputs, professional-grade Turkish support with extensive multilingual capabilities, and enterprise-ready deployment with optimized quantization options. The model is ideal for enterprises, researchers, and organizations who need a state-of-the-art multimodal AI capable of complex visual understanding, advanced reasoning, and creative generation. Next 12B approaches frontier model performance while maintaining efficiency, with 91.8% on MMLU benchmark, 81.2% on MATH benchmark, 94.3% on GSM8K, and 78.4% on MMLU-Pro. It supports multiple quantization formats including Q8_0, Q4_K_M, F16, and F32 for flexible deployment options. The model is built on a Transformer-based architecture with a causal LLM and an enhanced vision encoder, and it is fine-tuned using advanced instruction and multimodal fine-tuning (SFT) on curated Turkish and multilingual datasets. It supports both text and image modalities and is suitable for use cases such as advanced image captioning, multimodal question answering, text generation, complex reasoning, creative storytelling, and enterprise applications. The model is open-source and licensed under the MIT License, allowing free use, modification, and distribution for commercial and non-commercial purposes.

instruction: Please rank these articles by how closely they match the query about artificial intelligence advancements.
description: jina-reranker-v3-GGUF is a 0.6B parameter multilingual listwise reranker designed for efficient inference, using GGUF quantizations with llama.cpp. It is intended to rerank documents based on their relevance to a given query. The model supports reranking tasks and includes a rerank function that accepts a query, a list of documents, and optional parameters such as the number of top results to return and whether to include embeddings in the output. The model architecture includes quantized model weights in BF16 format and an MLP projector for additional processing. The model is implemented in Python and requires Python 3.8+, llama.cpp binaries, and specific dependencies such as numpy and safetensors. The model is available under a CC BY-NC 4.0 license, and commercial usage requires separate inquiries. The model is described in a research paper titled 'jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking' by Feng Wang, Yuqing Li, and Han Xiao, available on arXiv.

instruction: Please create a Japanese audio version of this dialogue that feels like it's from a modern visual novel.
description: Anime-XCodec2-44.1kHz-v2 is a fine-tuned variant of NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2, designed to produce 44.1kHz high-fidelity Japanese speech, particularly in anime and game-style voices. It incorporates upsampling layers and RMS loss, inspired by Inworld TTS-1, and was trained on approximately 22,000 hours of Japanese speech. This version includes updates to the upsampler parameters, loss configurations, and a fix for a RoPE bug from the original XCodec2. Only the decoder was updated; the encoder and codebook remain frozen, ensuring speech tokens are identical to the original XCodec2. This makes the model a drop-in decoder for systems already using XCodec2 tokens, such as Llasa. The model accepts 16kHz input and outputs 44.1kHz audio, and can be used for decoding XCodec2 speech tokens into high-fidelity Japanese speech, upgrading existing pipelines to 44.1kHz output, or as a form of audio super-resolution, though the latter use is untested. The model modifies the original XCodec2 architecture and requires a custom xcodec2 library version (v0.1.7 or later) for inference, as older versions will not work. The model is optimized for Japanese and anime/game-style voices, and may perform poorly on other languages or domains. It is based on NandemoGHS/Anime-XCodec2 (16kHz), with modifications including the integration of an UpSamplerBlock into the decoder, the adoption of RMS Loss in addition to original losses, and the freezing of the encoder and codebook. Key updates in v2 include a RoPE bug fix, changes to upsampler settings, a switch in the perceptual loss model, and adjustments to the spectral discriminator for 44.1kHz audio. The model is licensed under CC-BY-NC 4.0, inherited from XCodec2 and Anime-XCodec2. It acknowledges HKUSTAudio/xcodec2 as the original model, Inworld AI for upsampler architecture and RMS Loss, imprt for the kushinada-hubert-large model used in perceptual loss, and the community for Japanese speech resources.

instruction: Illustrate a quiet forest path covered in fallen leaves during the early morning mist.
description: Z-Image is a powerful and highly efficient image generation model with 6B parameters. There are three variants: Z-Image-Turbo, Z-Image-Base, and Z-Image-Edit. Z-Image-Turbo is a distilled version that matches or exceeds leading competitors with only 8 NFEs (Number of Function Evaluations). It offers sub-second inference latency on enterprise-grade H800 GPUs and fits within 16G VRAM consumer devices. It excels in photorealistic image generation, bilingual text rendering (English and Chinese), and robust instruction adherence. Z-Image-Base is the non-distilled foundation model, intended for community-driven fine-tuning and custom development. Z-Image-Edit is a variant fine-tuned for image editing tasks, supporting creative image-to-image generation with strong instruction-following capabilities, allowing for precise edits based on natural language prompts. The model uses a Scalable Single-Stream DiT (S3-DiT) architecture, where text, visual semantic tokens, and image VAE tokens are concatenated at the sequence level to serve as a unified input stream, maximizing parameter efficiency compared to dual-stream approaches. According to the Elo-based Human Preference Evaluation, Z-Image-Turbo shows highly competitive performance against other leading models and achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source models. The model is supported by the Decoupled-DMD algorithm, a core few-step distillation method that enables the 8-step Z-Image model. Decoupled-DMD is based on the insight that the success of existing DMD methods results from two independent mechanisms: CFG Augmentation, which drives the distillation process, and Distribution Matching, which ensures the stability and quality of the generated output. By decoupling these mechanisms, the model's distillation process is optimized for performance. Building on Decoupled-DMD, the model also incorporates DMDR, which fuses DMD with Reinforcement Learning to further improve semantic alignment, aesthetic quality, and structural coherence, while producing images with richer high-frequency details. DMDR integrates RL and DMD during the post-training of few-step models, demonstrating that RL unlocks the performance of DMD while DMD effectively regularizes RL.

instruction: What kind of scene is depicted in this photograph?
description: The convnext_small.dinov3_lvd1689m is a DINOv3 ConvNeXt image feature model. It is designed for image classification and as a feature backbone. The model is pretrained on the LVD-1689M dataset using the self-supervised DINOv3 method and was distilled from a DINOv3 ViT-7B model. It has 49.5 million parameters, 8.7 GMACs, and 21.6 million activations. The model processes images of size 224 x 224. It supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. For image classification, the model can be used to generate output probabilities and class indices. For feature map extraction, the model can produce feature maps with varying spatial dimensions. For image embeddings, the model can generate embeddings either by removing the classifier or using forward features and head functions. The model is based on the ConvNeXt architecture described in the paper 'A ConvNet for the 2020s' and the DINOv3 method described in the paper 'DINOv3'. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library.

instruction: List all the gene names found in this abstract about cancer treatment.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene and protein entity recognition in biomedical texts. It is optimized for high-precision identification of gene and protein mentions in clinical documents, research papers, and healthcare records. The model supports tasks such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is trained on the BC2GM dataset, a curated corpus of MEDLINE abstracts with manually annotated gene and protein mentions. The model uses a ModernBERT-large architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. It achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.96. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is production-ready for integration into clinical and research workflows. It supports the entity types B-GENE/PROTEIN and I-GENE/PROTEIN. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is available for use in applications such as clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is compatible with Hugging Face's pipeline API and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. The model is trained using the AdamW optimizer with learning rate scheduling and validated using cross-validation on a held-out test set. It is designed for efficient batch processing and can be tuned for performance based on hardware capabilities, including CPU and GPU configurations.

instruction: Detect and highlight any visible 'wildlife' in the given image.
description: LLMDet (large variant) was proposed by Shenghao Fu, Qize Yang, Qijie Mo, Junkai Yan, Xihan Wei, Jingke Meng, Xiaohua Xie, and Wei-Shi Zheng. LLMDet improves upon previous models by co-training with a large language model. The model is designed for zero-shot object detection and can be used for this task without prior training on specific object categories. The checkpoints for LLMDet are available for inference only and do not include the large language model used during training. The inference process is identical to that of other similar models. The model was trained on a combination of datasets including O365, GoldG, GRIT, V3Det, O365V2, and OpenImageV6, along with GroundingCap-1M. Evaluation results on LVIS show that the large variant of LLMDet achieves a MiniVal APr of 51.1, MiniVal APc of 45.1, MiniVal APf of 46.1, and MiniVal AP of 56.6. On Val1.0, it achieves an APr of 42.0, APc of 31.6, APf of 38.8, and AP of 50.2. The model is intended for use in open-vocabulary object detection tasks and supports inference with custom text labels provided as input. Users are advised that the checkpoints provided are for inference only and do not include the training components.

instruction: Please show me all the books on this table using the phrase 'a rectangular object with a cover'.
description: SAM 3 is a unified foundation model for promptable segmentation in images and videos. It can detect, segment, and track objects using text or visual prompts such as points, boxes, and masks. Compared to its predecessor, SAM 3 introduces the ability to exhaustively segment all instances of an open-vocabulary concept specified by a short text phrase or exemplars. Unlike prior work, SAM 3 can handle a vastly larger set of open-vocabulary prompts. It achieves 75-80% of human performance on a new benchmark that contains 270K unique concepts, over 50 times more than existing benchmarks. SAM 3 supports promptable concept segmentation (PCS) for images and videos, as well as promptable visual segmentation (PVS) for images and videos. For image PCS, the model takes text and/or image exemplars as prompts and returns segmentation masks for all matching object instances in the image. It supports text-only prompts, single or multiple bounding box prompts (positive and negative), and combined prompts (text + negative box). It also supports batched inference with text prompts and batched mixed prompts. For video PCS, the model takes text as prompts and detects and tracks all matching object instances across video frames. It supports pre-loaded video inference and streaming video inference. For image PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to segment a specific object instance per prompt. It supports single point clicks, multiple points for refinement, bounding box input, and multiple objects segmentation. It also supports batch inference. For video PVS, the model takes interactive visual prompts (points, boxes, masks) to track a specific object instance per prompt across video frames. It supports basic video tracking, multi-object video tracking, and streaming video inference. SAM 3 also provides semantic segmentation alongside instance masks. The model is an updated version of SAM2 and SAM2 Video, maintaining the same API while providing improved performance, making it a drop-in replacement for SAM2 workflows. It is available through the Transformers library and supports both image and video processing. For best results, pre-loaded video inference is recommended when all frames are available, as streaming inference may result in more false positive detections and duplicate object tracks due to the disabling of hotstart heuristics.

instruction: Can you translate this legal notice from English into Mandarin for a local client?
description: Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B-fp8 is a translation model developed by Tencent. The Hunyuan Translation Model includes a translation model, Hunyuan-MT-7B, and an ensemble model, Hunyuan-MT-Chimera. The translation model is used to translate source text into the target language, while the ensemble model integrates multiple translation outputs to produce a higher-quality result. It primarily supports mutual translation among 33 languages, including five ethnic minority languages in China. In the WMT25 competition, the model achieved first place in 30 out of the 31 language categories it participated in. Hunyuan-MT-7B achieves industry-leading performance among models of comparable scale. Hunyuan-MT-Chimera-7B is the industry’s first open-source translation ensemble model, elevating translation quality to a new level. A comprehensive training framework for translation models has been proposed, spanning from pretrain → cross-lingual pretraining (CPT) → supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → translation enhancement → ensemble refinement, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results for models of similar size. The model supports translation between Chinese and other languages, as well as among multiple other language pairs. The model is available in both standard and fp8 quantized versions. The model can be used with the transformers library, and specific parameters are recommended for inference, including top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, and temperature. The model does not have a default system_prompt. The model is open-sourced on Hugging Face.

instruction: I need to identify people and objects in a crowded street scene—can you help?
description: YOLOv13 is a real-time object detection model designed to improve detection performance in complex scenarios by capturing global multi-to-multi high-order correlations. It introduces a Hypergraph-based Adaptive Correlation Enhancement (HyperACE) mechanism that adaptively exploits latent high-order correlations, overcoming the limitations of previous methods that were restricted to pairwise correlation modeling. YOLOv13 also features a Full-Pipeline Aggregation-and-Distribution (FullPAD) paradigm, which distributes correlation-enhanced features across the entire network to achieve fine-grained information flow and representation synergy. Additionally, the model uses depthwise separable convolutions to replace large-kernel convolutions, significantly reducing parameters and computational complexity without sacrificing performance. YOLOv13 includes four variants: Nano, Small, Large, and X-Large. Experimental results on the MS COCO benchmark show that YOLOv13-N improves mAP by 3.0% over YOLO11-N and by 1.5% over YOLOv12-N. The model is available in multiple formats and supports training, validation, prediction, and export to ONNX or TensorRT. The code and models are open-sourced and can be accessed at the provided URL.

instruction: I’d like the camera to pan left to reveal the hidden path behind the rocks.
description: This model is trained on ModelScope without the use of code. It is designed to support camera control functions, including moving the camera up, down, left, and right, rotating it left and right, looking down or up, and switching to a wide-angle or close-up shot. The model does not require trigger words for these operations. It is based on the Qwen-Edit-2509 model, and a LoRa version is available for use. The model has been updated to address an instability and consistency issue by increasing the number of training iterations. Users are encouraged to try various camera control commands, as there are many possibilities. The model's performance is described as very amazing, and it is considered more powerful and intelligent compared to the original model, which already had certain capabilities. Users are invited to join a Discord group for communication and cooperation. If the resources are helpful or used for business purposes, users are encouraged to support the content creator through a PayPal donation.

instruction: Does the sentence 'The movie was released in 2020' support the hypothesis 'It is a recent film'?
description: This multilingual model is designed to perform natural language inference (NLI) on 100 languages, making it suitable for multilingual zero-shot classification. It is based on the mDeBERTa-v3-base model, which was pre-trained by Microsoft on 100 languages. The model was fine-tuned on the multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset and the XNLI validation dataset. The multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset contains 2,730,000 NLI hypothesis-premise pairs in 26 languages spoken by more than 4 billion people, with 105,000 text pairs per language. The dataset was created using the latest open-source machine translation models and is based on English datasets. The languages included are: ar, bn, de, es, fa, fr, he, hi, id, it, ja, ko, mr, nl, pl, ps, pt, ru, sv, sw, ta, tr, uk, ur, vi, zh. An additional 105,000 text pairs for English were added, bringing the total to 27 languages. The dataset also includes a random set of 10% of the hypothesis-premise pairs where an English hypothesis is paired with a premise in another language, and vice versa. This mix of languages enables users to formulate a hypothesis in English for a target text in another language. The XNLI validation set consists of 2490 professionally translated texts from English to 14 other languages, totaling 37,350 texts. The XNLI training set was excluded due to quality issues with the 2018 machine translations. For evaluation, three languages—bg, el, th—were excluded from the XNLI training data and included only in the test data to assess the model's performance on languages not seen during NLI fine-tuning but pre-trained on. The total training dataset had 3,287,280 hypothesis-premise pairs. The model was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: 3 training epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a per-device train batch size of 32, gradient accumulation steps of 2, a warmup ratio of 0.06, a weight decay of 0.01, and fp16 disabled. The model was evaluated on the XNLI test set in 15 languages (5010 texts per language, 75150 in total) and the English test sets of MNLI, ANLI, FEVER, LING, and WANLI. The model's performance on the XNLI test set in 15 languages achieved an average accuracy of 0.822, with individual language accuracies ranging from 0.744 to 0.871. On English datasets, the model achieved an average accuracy of 0.794. The model's speed varied by language, with the highest speed of 1887 texts per second on a ZH language and the lowest of 374 texts per second on the FEVER test set. The model is capable of cross-lingual transfer, meaning it can perform NLI on languages it has not seen during NLI fine-tuning, but performance is likely lower than for languages seen during fine-tuning. The performance on the languages bg, el, and th is an indicator of this cross-lingual transfer. The model has limitations due to the use of machine-translated data, which may reduce data quality for complex tasks like NLI. Users are advised to consult the original DeBERTa-V3 paper and literature on NLI datasets for potential biases. The model may have issues with older versions of the Hugging Face Transformers library, and using Transformers==4.13 or higher is recommended. The model currently does not support FP16.

instruction: Can you add facial expressions to a pose-based image while keeping the body pose accurate?
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor to an earlier version and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into diffusers format and can be used in combination with Stable Diffusion. The model is a diffusion-based text-to-image generation model that supports additional input conditions. It is primarily used with openpose images, which represent human poses as a set of keypoints or skeletons. The model is trained to learn task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with small training datasets. The model supports a variety of conditions, including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, and image shuffling. Improvements in Openpose 1.1 include a more accurate implementation of OpenPose, especially for hands, and support for more inputs such as hand and face. The training dataset for the previous version had several issues, including duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality or blurry images with JPEG artifacts, and incorrect paired prompts due to data processing errors. These issues have been resolved in the new model, making it more reliable in many cases.

instruction: I need this customer support guide translated from English to Ukrainian for a new branch.
description: Seed-X is a series of open-source multilingual translation language models, including an instruction model, a reinforcement learning model, and a reward model. It is designed to push the boundaries of translation capabilities within a 7 billion parameter scale. Seed-X is intended as an accessible, off-the-shelf tool to support the community in advancing translation research and applications. It exhibits state-of-the-art translation capabilities, on par with or outperforming ultra-large models like Gemini-2.5, Claude-3.5, and GPT-4, as validated by human evaluations and automatic metrics. With a compact 7B parameter count and mistral architecture, Seed-X offers outstanding translation performance in a lightweight and efficient package, ideal for deployment and inference. It excels on a highly challenging translation test set spanning diverse domains, including the internet, science and technology, office dialogues, e-commerce, biomedicine, finance, law, literature, and entertainment. The Seed-X-PPO model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It supports multilingual translation among 28 languages, including Arabic, Czech, Danish, German, English, Spanish, Finnish, French, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Dutch, Norwegian Bokmal, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, and Chinese. The model is specialized in multilingual translation and is not expected to support other tasks. The language tags at the end of the prompt are necessary for translation, as used in PPO training. The model does not have a chat template, so users should avoid prompting it in a multi-round conversation format. The model is evaluated on a diverse set of translation benchmarks, including FLORES-200, WMT-25, and a publicly released benchmark, accompanied by human evaluations. The model is licensed under OpenMDW. Users are recommended to use the Seed-X-PPO model, as its translation performance is superior to Seed-X-Instruct. Unofficial quantized versions for local deployment are not recommended, as the team plans to release an official quantized model and develop a demo on Hugging Face Space.

instruction: Can you turn this paragraph into a natural-sounding voice recording?
description: Soprano is an ultra-lightweight, on-device text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for expressive, high-fidelity speech synthesis at unprecedented speed. It supports up to 2000x real-time generation on GPU and 20x real-time on CPU. The model enables lossless streaming and is designed for use in various environments, including WebUI, CLI, Python scripts, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Soprano allows for basic inference, batched inference, and streaming inference with customizable sampling parameters such as temperature, top_p, and repetition_penalty. It can be configured with different cache sizes and decoder batch sizes to optimize inference speed, though this may increase memory usage. The model is currently English-only and does not support voice cloning. It was trained on 1,000 hours of audio, which is approximately 100 times less than other TTS models, and as a result, mispronunciation of uncommon words may occur. This limitation is expected to improve with additional training data. Users are advised that Soprano is now outdated and should use a newer version instead. On Windows with CUDA, users must reinstall PyTorch explicitly with the correct CUDA wheel after installing Soprano to ensure CUDA support. If Soprano produces unsatisfactory results, users can regenerate the output for a new generation or adjust sampling settings for more varied results. Improper grammar, such as the lack of contractions or multiple spaces, should be avoided for optimal performance.

instruction: Can you produce a wardrobe with two doors and a built-in lighting system?
description: This is a 3D furniture generation model based on a Transformer architecture. It is designed to generate 3D furniture structural data based on text prompts such as 'wardrobe' or 'cabinet'. The model directly produces custom JSON structures containing panel size, position, rotation, and type information, which can be rendered directly in front-end frameworks such as Three.js. The model supports structured output in the form of JSON structures that can be directly parsed. It allows parametric control by supporting the input of furniture type, size, and other parameters. It achieves millimeter-level accuracy in coordinates through normalization training. The model is highly extensible and can be fine-tuned with a user's own furniture dataset. It is compatible with Three.js, enabling the generated furniture models to be rendered directly in the browser. The model is open-sourced on Hugging Face Model Hub and can be downloaded, tested, and fine-tuned. For collaboration or technical inquiries, contact chengyuhan1030@gmail.com.

instruction: Create a 3D object of a rare animal species that’s not commonly found in nature.
description: MV-RAG is a text-to-3D generation method that retrieves 2D reference images to guide a multiview diffusion model. By conditioning on both text and multiple real-world 2D images, MV-RAG improves realism and consistency for rare or out-of-distribution objects. The model is designed to produce 3D-consistent outputs by first retrieving relevant 2D images from a large in-the-wild 2D database and then conditioning a multiview diffusion model on these images to synthesize consistent and accurate multiview outputs. Training is achieved via a hybrid strategy that bridges structured multiview data and diverse 2D image collections. This involves training on multiview data using augmented conditioning views that simulate retrieval variance for view-specific reconstruction, alongside training on sets of retrieved real-world 2D images using a held-out view prediction objective, where the model predicts the held-out view from the other views to infer 3D consistency from 2D data. To facilitate a rigorous out-of-distribution evaluation, a new collection of challenging out-of-distribution prompts has been introduced. Experiments show that the approach significantly improves 3D consistency, photorealism, and text adherence for out-of-distribution or rare concepts, while maintaining competitive performance on standard benchmarks.

instruction: Write a dialogue between two characters discussing their future goals.
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. This model introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, supported by specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model supports over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. The instruction-tuned 14B Qwen2.5 model is a causal language model with 14.7B parameters, of which 13.1B are non-embedding parameters. It has 48 layers and uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. The model has 40 attention heads for queries and 8 for key-value pairs. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model is trained through pretraining and post-training stages. The code for Qwen2.5 is compatible with the latest Hugging Face `transformers` library, and using versions below 4.37.0 may result in a KeyError. To process long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, a technique called YaRN is used to enhance model length extrapolation. This can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the `config.json` file. However, YaRN is currently only supported with static scaling in vLLM, which may impact performance on shorter texts. The model's evaluation results are detailed in a separate document, along with information on GPU memory requirements and throughput. The model is designed to be used with the Hugging Face Transformers library and can be loaded and used with the `AutoModelForCausalLM` and `AutoTokenizer` classes. The model supports deployment with vLLM for optimized performance. The model includes a chat template for generating responses based on system and user messages. The model is intended for use in a variety of natural language processing tasks, including but not limited to text generation, instruction following, and structured data understanding. The model is part of a larger series of foundation models and is described in a technical report and a preprint publication.

instruction: Could you translate this poem from Spanish into French while preserving its rhythm and style?
description: Hunyuan Translation Model Version 1.5 includes a 1.8B translation model, HY-MT1.5-1.8B, and a 7B translation model, HY-MT1.5-7B. Both models focus on supporting mutual translation across 33 languages and incorporating 5 ethnic and dialect variations. Among them, HY-MT1.5-7B is an upgraded version of the WMT25 championship model, optimized for explanatory translation and mixed-language scenarios, with newly added support for terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation. Despite having less than one-third the parameters of HY-MT1.5-7B, HY-MT1.5-1.8B delivers translation performance comparable to its larger counterpart, achieving both high speed and high quality. After quantization, the 1.8B model can be deployed on edge devices and support real-time translation scenarios, making it widely applicable. HY-MT1.5-1.8B achieves the industry-leading performance among models of the same size, surpassing most commercial translation APIs. HY-MT1.5-1.8B supports deployment on edge devices and real-time translation scenarios, offering broad applicability. HY-MT1.5-7B, compared to its September open-source version, has been optimized for annotated and mixed-language scenarios. Both models support terminology intervention, contextual translation, and formatted translation.

instruction: Помогите получить точную расшифровку аудио, сохраняя все элементы пунктуации.
description: Borealis - это первая аудио-модель с поддержкой ASR для русского языка. Модель обучалась на примерно 7000 часах аудио на русском языке. Важной особенностью модели является поддержка пунктуации в распознанных аудио. Архитектура модели во многом вдохновлена другими моделями, но имеет отличия в некоторых аспектах. Модель демонстрирует следующие метрики: средний WER 6.33%, RuLS 6.39%, CV 22.0 2.67%, Books 5.28%, Speak 1.95%, Sova 15.37%. Модель предназначена для распознавания речи и поддерживает генерацию текста с сохранением пунктуации.

instruction: I need a system that can automatically tag images with relevant keywords—can you handle that?
description: VTP is a unified visual tokenizer pre-training framework designed to improve the quality of latent spaces for generative models. It integrates image-text contrastive, self-supervised, and reconstruction learning to train visual tokenizers from scratch. The framework addresses the 'pre-training scaling problem,' where traditional reconstruction-based training leads to latent spaces biased toward low-level information, which does not translate to better generation performance. VTP introduces a new paradigm where understanding high-level semantics becomes a key driver for improving generation quality. The model supports multiple tasks, including zero-shot classification, linear probing classification, image reconstruction, and image generation. It demonstrates strong performance, achieving 78.2% zero-shot accuracy, 0.36 rFID for reconstruction, and 2.81 FID for generation. VTP shows significant scaling properties, with generative performance improving effectively with increased compute, parameters, and data. Compared to conventional autoencoders, VTP achieves a 65.8% improvement in FID for downstream generation when trained with the same DiT specifications. The model is available for use as an autoencoder, a feature extractor for self-supervised learning, and a CLIP-like image-text encoder. The authors caution that traditional autoencoders cannot be scaled up for diffusion generative models and emphasize the importance of representation learning for parameter, data, and training scalability.

instruction: Could you analyze this voice clip and tell me if the speaker is likely male or female?
description: Common-Voice-Gender-Detection is a fine-tuned version of `facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h` for binary audio classification, specifically trained to detect speaker gender as female or male. This model uses the `Wav2Vec2ForSequenceClassification` architecture for efficient and accurate voice-based gender classification. The model achieves a precision of 0.9705 for female and 0.9943 for male, with a recall of 0.9916 for female and 0.9799 for male. The F1-scores are 0.9809 for female and 0.9870 for male, with an overall accuracy of 0.9846 across 6545 samples. The model is intended for use in speech analytics, conversational AI personalization, voice dataset curation, research applications, and multimedia content tagging. It is designed to assist in analyzing speaker demographics in call centers or customer service recordings, adjust tone or dialogue in voice assistants based on gender detection, automatically tag or filter voice datasets by speaker gender, enable linguistic and acoustic research involving gender-specific speech patterns, and automate metadata generation for gender identification in podcasts, interviews, or video content.

instruction: Please assess how closely this image of a research paper matches the question on neural network optimization techniques.
description: The `llama-nemotron-rerank-vl-1b-v2` is a multimodal reranking model developed by NVIDIA for question-answering retrieval. It is designed to provide a logit score that represents the relevance of a document page to a given query. The model can process documents in the form of image, text, or a combination of both. The expected images are screenshots of document pages or slides, and the model supports images containing text, tables, charts, and infographics. The reranking model is a key component of a multimodal retrieval system, such as a vision RAG pipeline, where it helps improve overall accuracy by reranking top candidate documents retrieved by embedding models. The model is ready for commercial use and is suitable for users who want to build a multimodal question-and-answer application over a large corpus, leveraging the latest dense retrieval technologies. The model is based on the Eagle VLM architecture with a SigLIP 2 400M vision encoder and the Llama 3.2 1B language model. It is a cross-encoder model with approximately 1.7B parameters, fine-tuned for the ranking task. The final embedding is aggregated using a mean pooling strategy, and a binary classification head is used to produce the logit output. The model was fine-tuned exclusively on image data with a maximum of 4 input tiles and a maximum context length of 2048 tokens. For evaluation, it was tested on image-only, image+text, and text-only inputs with a maximum of 6 input tiles and a maximum context length of 10240 tokens. Inputs exceeding the maximum length are truncated. The model outputs a list of floats, where each value corresponds to a raw logit. Users can apply a Sigmoid activation function to convert the logits into probabilities. The model is optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware and software frameworks for faster training and inference. The model requires transformers version 4.47.1 or higher and flash-attention 2.6.3 or higher. It is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, and Blackwell microarchitectures and is supported on Linux. The model was fine-tuned using publicly available image datasets and synthetic queries generated from proprietary models. The training data includes a mix of automated, human, and synthetic data collection and labeling methods. The model was evaluated on the ViDoRe V1, V2, and V3 benchmarks, as well as two internal visual retrieval datasets: DigitalCorpora-10k and Earnings V2. On these benchmarks, the model achieved an average Recall@5 improvement of approximately 7.2% for text modality, 6.9% for image modality, and 6% for image+text modality compared to the VLM embedding baseline. The model also demonstrated competitive performance on text retrieval benchmarks, comparable to NVIDIA's text-only reranking model. The model is designed to be used in conjunction with the `llama-nemotron-embed-vl-1b-v2` embedding model. The model is intended for use in document reranking for retrieval, where user queries can be text and documents can be text, document page images, charts, tables, and infographics. The model does not generate or reverse engineer personal data, and no personal data was used to create the model. The dataset is reviewed upon addition and as needed. The model has been verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is intended for use by generative AI developers working with conversational AI models. The model has technical limitations, including a maximum sequence length of 10240 tokens. Longer text inputs should be truncated. The model does not always guarantee to provide a meaningful ranking of documents for a given query. The use of the model is governed by specific terms of use, and the post-processing scripts are licensed under a separate license. The model is built with Llama.

instruction: Create a time-lapse of a flower blooming in a garden, with soft background music that mimics birds chirping and a gentle breeze.
description: LTX-2 is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is based on a diffusion approach, supporting English language inputs. LTX-2 is intended for tasks involving the generation of audio-visual content, including image-to-video generation. The model is available in multiple versions, including a full model, quantized variants (fp8, nvfp4), a distilled version, and LoRA versions. Additional components include spatial and temporal upscalers for increasing resolution and frame rate in multi-stage pipelines. The model can be used locally with a direct use license, and is supported in the PyTorch framework and the Diffusers library. The codebase is compatible with Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. The base model is fully trainable, and training for specific aspects such as motion, style, or likeness can be completed in under an hour in many settings. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify societal biases, may not perfectly match prompts, is influenced by prompting style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and may produce lower quality audio when no speech is present.

instruction: Can you recognize any cell types in this excerpt from a dermatology article?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for biomedical entity recognition, specifically for identifying proteins, DNA, RNA, cell lines, and cell types. It is optimized for high precision and is trained on the JNLPBA dataset, a curated corpus for biomedical named entity recognition. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem, enabling easy integration into production environments. It supports the classification of biomedical entities such as B-DNA, B-RNA, B-cell_line, B-cell_type, B-protein, I-DNA, I-RNA, I-cell_line, I-cell_type, and I-protein. The model is suitable for applications including drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It achieves an F1 score of 0.80, with a precision of 0.76, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.93. The JNLPBA dataset, derived from the GENIA corpus, contains annotations for five entity types and is widely used as a benchmark for evaluating biomedical NER systems. The model is based on the roberta-base architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It performs token classification for named entity recognition, taking tokenized biomedical text as input and producing BIO-tagged entity predictions as output. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: What would you say if this image were shown to someone who can't see it?
description: BLIP-2 is a model that consists of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model with 2.7 billion parameters. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. The model can be used for conditional text generation given an image and optional text. BLIP-2 uses an off-the-shelf OPT model as its language model, which inherits the same risks and limitations as described in Meta's model card, including potential biases, safety issues, and generation quality concerns. The model is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may result in the model generating inappropriate content or replicating biases present in the training data. It has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without careful evaluation of its safety and fairness in the intended context. This release is for research purposes only and is not designed or evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model's memory requirements vary depending on the precision used, with options including float32, float16/bfloat16, int8, and int4. The model can be run on CPU or GPU, with different configurations for full, half, and 8-bit precision.

instruction: What are the benefits of using a pre-processed setup for faster inference later?
description: This is a training adapter designed for fine-tuning, originally intended for use with a specific trainer but potentially compatible with others. It can also be used as a general de-distillation LoRA for inference to remove the 'Turbo' component from 'Z-Image-Turbo'. Training directly on a step distilled model can cause the distillation to break down quickly, leading to unpredictable loss of the step distillation and reduced speed. This adapter significantly slows down the distillation breakdown, allowing for short training runs while preserving the speed benefits of step distillation. However, it is important to note that this is a workaround rather than a complete solution. The distillation will still degrade over time, and long training runs may result in the distillation breaking down to the point where artifacts are produced when the adapter is removed. The adapter was created by generating thousands of images at various sizes and aspect ratios, then training a LoRA on those images at a low learning rate (1e-5). This process allowed the distillation to break down while preserving the model's existing knowledge. When using this adapter, the distillation is already broken down, so any LoRA trained on top of it will not cause further distillation breakdown. This means the new LoRA will only learn the subject being trained. During inference, the adapter is removed, leaving the new information on the distilled model, which allows the new information to run at the faster distilled speeds.

instruction: How well does this article match the query about the history of the internet?
description: The Qwen3-Reranker-4B is a text reranking model designed for text retrieval tasks. It is part of the Qwen3 Embedding model series, which is specifically developed for text embedding and ranking. The model is built upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series and inherits their multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills. The Qwen3 Embedding series includes various sizes of embedding and reranking models (0.6B, 4B, and 8B), offering a comprehensive range of options for different use cases. The Qwen3-Reranker-4B has 4 billion parameters and supports a context length of 32,000 tokens. It is instruction-aware, meaning it can accept user-defined instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. The model supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and provides robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. Evaluation results show that the Qwen3-Reranker-4B achieves a score of 69.76 on the MTEB-R benchmark, 75.94 on the CMTEB-R benchmark, 72.74 on the MMTEB-R benchmark, 69.97 on the MLDR benchmark, 81.20 on the MTEB-Code benchmark, and 14.84 on the FollowIR benchmark. These results are based on top-100 candidates retrieved by a dense embedding model. The model is recommended to be used with customized instructions, as this can improve performance by 1% to 5% in most retrieval scenarios. Developers are advised to write instructions in English, as most training instructions were originally in English. The model can be used with the Transformers library version 4.51.0 or higher, or with the vLLM library version 0.8.5 or higher. It is recommended to enable flash_attention_2 for better acceleration and memory saving. The model processes input pairs consisting of a query and a document, and outputs a score indicating the relevance of the document to the query. The model is evaluated on a range of benchmarks, including MTEB, CMTEB, MMTEB, and MTEB-Code, and demonstrates strong performance across these tasks.

instruction: What are the long-term effects of social media on human relationships?
description: The gpt-oss series is designed for powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases. Two models are available: gpt-oss-120b, intended for production, general-purpose, and high reasoning use cases that fit into a single 80GB GPU (like NVIDIA H100 or AMD MI300X), with 117B parameters and 5.1B active parameters; and gpt-oss-20b, intended for lower latency, local, or specialized use cases, with 21B parameters and 3.6B active parameters. Both models were trained on OpenAI's data and require the harmony format to function correctly. The gpt-oss-120b model is covered by this model card. The models are released under a permissive Apache 2.0 license, allowing for free use without copyleft restrictions or patent risk, making them suitable for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment. Users can adjust the reasoning effort (low, medium, high) based on their specific use case and latency needs. The models provide full access to the reasoning process, which is useful for debugging and increasing trust in outputs, though it is not intended for end users. They are fine-tunable for specific use cases through parameter fine-tuning and support agentic capabilities such as function calling and structured outputs. The models were post-trained with MXFP4 quantization of the MoE weights, enabling gpt-oss-120b to run on a single 80GB GPU and gpt-oss-20b to run within 16GB of memory. All evaluations were performed using the same MXFP4 quantization. The models can be used with Transformers, vLLM, PyTorch / Triton, Ollama, and LM Studio. They can be downloaded directly from Hugging Face CLI. The larger model, gpt-oss-120b, can be fine-tuned on a single H100 node, while the smaller model can be fine-tuned on consumer hardware. The models are suitable for web browsing, function calling with defined schemas, and agentic operations like browser tasks.

instruction: Can you locate and highlight every item of clothing in this picture?
description: The Grounding DINO model was proposed by Shilong Liu, Zhaoyang Zeng, Tianhe Ren, Feng Li, Hao Zhang, Jie Yang, Chunyuan Li, Jianwei Yang, Hang Su, Jun Zhu, and Lei Zhang. Grounding DINO extends a closed-set object detection model with a text encoder, enabling open-set object detection. The model achieves remarkable results, such as 52.5 AP on COCO zero-shot. You can use the raw model for zero-shot object detection, which is the task of detecting things in an image out-of-the-box without labeled data.

instruction: Can you identify the location of this famous landmark and provide some historical context?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including judging object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, with capabilities for 2D and 3D grounding. It handles long contexts and video understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling full recall and second-level indexing for books and hours-long video. The model excels in multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that enables recognition of a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon. It provides enhanced text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for deployment in a variety of settings, with performance optimized for both text and vision tasks. It is intended for use in applications requiring visual and textual understanding, including but not limited to agent interaction, coding from visual input, and spatial reasoning. The model is available as Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, and its code is compatible with the latest Hugging Face Transformers library. The model supports a range of generation hyperparameters for both vision-language and text-only tasks, including settings for sampling strategies, repetition penalties, and output length. The model is described in technical reports and academic publications available on arXiv.

instruction: Can you create a written summary of this English audio in Italian with timestamps?
description: Canary-1b-v2 is a 1-billion parameter model designed for high-quality speech transcription and translation across 25 European languages. It supports automatic speech recognition (ASR) for 25 languages, speech translation (AST) from English to 24 languages, and speech translation from 24 languages to English. The model is built on an encoder-decoder architecture with a FastConformer Encoder and a Transformer Decoder, featuring 32 encoder layers and 8 decoder layers, totaling 978 million parameters. It uses a unified SentencePiece Tokenizer with a vocabulary of 16,384 tokens optimized across all 25 languages. Canary-1b-v2 is trained using a 3-stage procedure on a multilingual speech recognition and translation dataset combining Nvidia's Granary dataset and NeMo ASR Set 3.0, with a total of 1.7 million training hours. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance among models of similar size, with comparable quality to models three times larger while being up to 10 times faster. It provides automatic punctuation and capitalization, accurate word-level and segment-level timestamps, and segment-level timestamps for translated outputs. Evaluation results show a Word Error Rate (WER) of 8.40% on Fleurs-25 Langs, 8.85% on CoVoST-13 Langs, and 7.27% on MLS-6 Langs for ASR. For speech translation, it achieves a COMET score of 79.30 on Fleurs-24 Langs and 77.48 on CoVoST-13 Langs for X→English, and 84.56 on Fleurs-24 Langs and 80.29 on CoVoST-5 Langs for English→X. The model is robust to noise, with a WER of 2.18% at 100 dB SNR and 19.38% at -5 dB SNR. It also performs well on long-form transcription, achieving a WER of 13.78% on Earnings-22 and 9.87% on This American Life. Canary-1b-v2 is released under a permissive CC BY 4.0 license and is designed to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems for faster training and inference. It is intended for use by developers, researchers, academics, and industries building applications that require speech-to-text capabilities, including conversational AI, voice assistants, transcription services, subtitle generation, and voice analytics platforms. The model is available for use in the NeMo toolkit and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or fine-tuning on another dataset. It supports input in 16kHz audio formats such as .wav and .flac, and outputs text with punctuation and capitalization included. The model is compatible with NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell microarchitectures and requires at least 6GB RAM for model loading. Canary-1b-v2 is the first model from the NeMo team to leverage the full Nvidia Granary dataset, showcasing its multitask and multilingual capabilities. The model is verified to meet prescribed NVIDIA quality standards and is evaluated using metrics such as Word Error Rate (WER), BLEU score, and COMET score. Technical limitations include potential inaccuracies in transcripts and translations, which may vary based on source and target language and input audio characteristics. The model is not recommended for word-for-word or incomplete sentences as accuracy may vary based on context. Ethical considerations include ensuring the model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addressing unforeseen product misuse. The model is not known to generate or reverse engineer personal data, and all datasets used in training have provenance. Data labeling complies with privacy laws, but data correction or removal is not possible with externally-sourced data. The model is not intended for life-critical applications and use is restricted by the license. Model and dataset restrictions include the application of the Principle of least privilege and adherence to dataset license constraints.

instruction: Could you describe the fine details of the object in the foreground of this photo?
description: The VLM-FO1_Qwen2.5-VL-3B-v01 model is an implementation of the VLM-FO1 framework built on the Qwen2.5-VL-3B base model. VLM-FO1 is a novel plug-and-play framework designed to bridge the gap between the high-level reasoning of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and the need for fine-grained visual perception. It endows pre-trained VLMs with superior fine-grained perception without compromising their inherent high-level reasoning and general understanding capabilities. The framework operates as a plug-and-play module that can be integrated with any existing VLM, preserving its original weights and capabilities. VLM-FO1 excels at a wide range of fine-grained perception tasks, including Object Grounding, Region Generative Understanding, and Visual Region Reasoning. The framework is designed as a set of enhancement modules that can be seamlessly integrated with any pre-trained VLM. A novel Dual-Vision Encoder architecture, called the Hybrid Region Encoder (HFRE), fuses semantic-rich features with perception-enhanced features, creating powerful region tokens that capture both high-level meaning and fine-grained spatial detail. VLM-FO1 achieves state-of-the-art results across a diverse suite of benchmarks. A two-stage training strategy ensures that fine-grained perception is gained without causing catastrophic forgetting of the base model's powerful general visual understanding abilities.

instruction: How can I ensure this document is correctly oriented before I start processing it?
description: The Document Image Orientation Classification Module is designed to identify the orientation of document images and correct them through post-processing. It is used to determine the orientation of documents or IDs containing text regions before OCR processing, which helps improve OCR accuracy. The model is based on PP-LCNet_x1_0 and classifies document images into four orientation categories: 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270°. It achieves an average recognition accuracy of 99.06% and has a model storage size of 7 MB. The model can be used as part of a larger pipeline that includes document orientation classification and geometric distortion correction. The pipeline supports document preprocessing for OCR, digital document management, and scenarios requiring improved document image quality. It offers flexible deployment options across multiple hardware platforms and supports secondary development through fine-tuning on custom datasets. The model can be integrated into projects using the PaddleOCR library and is compatible with both command-line and programmatic usage. The model is optional within the pipeline and can be enabled or disabled as needed.

instruction: What patterns or objects can you identify in this image using feature extraction?
description: This is a DINOv3 Vision Transformer (ViT) model image feature encoder, distilled on the LVD-1689M dataset from the DINOv3 ViT-7B model. The model is designed for image feature extraction and supports tasks such as image classification, feature map extraction, and image embeddings. It is based on a Vision Transformer architecture with a patch size of 16x16. The model has 303.1 million parameters, 82.4 GMACs, and 90.6 million activations, and processes images of size 256 x 256. The original model weights had all QKV projection biases set to zero, and for the `timm` implementation, the QKV bias is disabled (`qkv_bias=False`) for most models, except for variants with `qkvb` in the name, which have the bias enabled but set to zero. The original models store RoPE periods as a persistent `bfloat16` buffer, while `timm` generates `float32` periods at initialization, which may result in some numerical differences but is less problematic on devices without bfloat16 support and appears to work as well or slightly better for fine-tuning. The model is evaluated on various benchmarks, including IN-ReaL, IN-R, Obj.Net, Ox.-H, ADE20k, NYU, DAVIS, NAVI, and SPair, with performance metrics such as 90.2% on IN-ReaL and 0.352 on NYU for the ViT-L/16 variant. It is also evaluated on satellite-related tasks such as classification and segmentation, with performance metrics such as 79.6% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench classification and 74.5% mean accuracy on GEO-Bench segmentation for the ViT-L/16 variant. The model is associated with the DINOv3 paper and the Vision Transformer paper, and is implemented using the PyTorch Image Models library.

instruction: What does the largest slice in this pie chart indicate about the data distribution?
description: This model is a specialized version of Qwen3-VL-2B-Thinking, fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on the ChartQA dataset for advanced chart understanding, data extraction, and visual reasoning. It is designed to perform 'System 2' thinking, generating step-by-step reasoning traces before providing the final answer, which makes the decision process regarding chart interpretation transparent and verifiable. The model uses tags to articulate the visual analysis process, such as identifying axes and comparing bars, before concluding. It is optimized for bar charts, line graphs, and pie charts found in the ChartQA dataset. The model is fine-tuned with LoRA, involving approximately 2% trainable parameters, while retaining the base model's general capabilities. It is trained to return answers in a strict JSON format {\

instruction: What is the main sentiment in this review: 'The product is overpriced and underwhelming'?
description: This is an ONNX-optimized version of a RoBERTa-base model for efficient inference. The repository contains a regular ONNX exported model and an INT8 dynamically quantized model for faster inference with minimal accuracy loss. The model is optimized for zero-shot classification tasks across multiple languages. The quantized model provides faster inference, with a speedup of approximately 2-3x compared to PyTorch, a smaller size due to INT8 quantization, and maintained accuracy with less than 1% loss compared to the original model. The base model is RoBERTa-base, and it is designed for zero-shot classification or natural language inference tasks. The model was converted from PyTorch to ONNX format and uses dynamic quantization with INT8 weights. It is implemented using ONNX Runtime with Optimum. The model is licensed under the MIT License. If used, it should be cited as part of the original RoBERTa Base Zero-Shot Classification model by Laurer, Atteveldt, Casas, and Welbers in 2022. This ONNX optimization was created for efficient deployment in production environments, with special thanks to the original model authors and the Hugging Face Optimum team.

instruction: This English user guide must be translated into Chinese for our support team—can you do that?
description: Tri-1.8B Translation is a lightweight multilingual translation model developed by Trillion Labs. It is trained through pretraining and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and was distilled from the larger Tri-21B model to maintain strong translation quality while being a much smaller, deployment-friendly model with approximately 1.8B parameters. The model supports all translation directions among English, Korean, Japanese, and Chinese, enabling fully bidirectional translation between these languages. It is designed to be compact and efficient, suitable for serving on a single GPU. The model works with a simple task instruction and language tag format. It is research-ready and can be used for domain-specific fine-tuning or with lightweight adapters. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0 for model weights and code, with a note to verify data licenses for specific use cases. The development of the model was influenced by the Seed-X project from the ByteDance Seed team, particularly in terms of prompt template and training design.

instruction: I have a simple sketch of a mountain—can you generate a realistic image of it with natural elements?
description: Anything2Real 2601 A is a version of the Anything2Real family of LoRA models, designed to transform non-photorealistic images such as illustrations, anime, cartoons, and paintings into convincing photographs while preserving the original composition and content. It is based on Anything2Real 2601 and incorporates adjustments to the layer weights of anything2real alpha to achieve a more balanced performance between maintaining the original image and generating a realistic environment. Anything2Real 2601 A addresses weaknesses in previous versions, such as the limited effectiveness of Anything2Real 2601 in transforming eyes and the limited color performance of anything2real alpha. The model is built on the Qwen Edit 2511 base model and is recommended to be used with a strength of 1 to 0.75. A prompt template is suggested: 'change the picture 1 to realistic photograph, [description of your image]'. Including detailed descriptions in the prompt helps the model better understand the content and produce superior transformations, although it can still function without them. The model is used in a workflow involving 21 nodes, including CLIPLoader, ConditioningZeroOut, ImageScaleBy, KSampler, LoadImage, LoraLoaderModelOnly, and specialized Qwen Edit nodes, and is powered by an RTX 4090 Series GPU. Realism is subjective, and it is recommended to modulate strength or switch base models rather than increasing the LoRA weight if realism is insufficient. If realism remains inadequate, it is suggested to blend with an additional photorealistic LoRA and adjust to taste. The model was created to bridge the gap between artistic creations and photorealistic results without requiring users to start from scratch. It is intended for artists who want to see their characters in realistic form or for anyone interested in transforming their favorite art styles into photographs. Users are encouraged to share their results and suggestions for future improvements. For best results, it is recommended to follow the workflow demonstrated in the RunningHub example. Users can reach out with questions or collaboration ideas via Twitter, email, QQ Group, WeChat, or CivitAI.

instruction: Can I use the huggingface-cli to download all available quantizations at once?
description: This model is a quantized version of the Huihui-gemma-3n-E4B-it-abliterated model, processed using the imatrix quantization method with a dataset provided by an unspecified source. The quantizations were performed using the llama.cpp release b5856. The model is intended to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format uses a default template since no chat template was specified, which may not be accurate and should be verified against the original model card. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations, such as Q3_K_XL and Q4_K_L, use Q8_0 for the embedding and output weights instead of the default quantization. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. The Q4_0 quantization format is compatible with online repacking, and newer IQ4_NL and IQ4_XS formats offer similar benefits for ARM CPUs. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for both single-file and multi-file downloads. Users are advised to consider their hardware limitations, such as RAM and VRAM availability, when selecting a quantization type. For maximum quality, users should aim for a quant with a file size 1-2GB smaller than the total available memory. K-quant formats, such as Q5_K_M, are recommended for general use, while I-quant formats, such as IQ3_M, may offer better performance for specific hardware like Nvidia or AMD GPUs. The model includes a variety of quantization options, ranging from high-quality Q6_K_L and Q5_K_L to lower-quality but more memory-efficient options like Q3_K_S and Q2_K. Performance benchmarks on an AVX2 system show that Q4_K_M and Q4_0_8_8 formats offer improvements in prompt processing and text generation compared to the standard Q4_0 format. The model was developed with assistance from kalomaze and Dampf for the imatrix calibration dataset, and inspired by ZeroWw's work on embedding and output weights. Support for the model's development was provided by LM Studio.

instruction: What are the main points in this transcript of a meeting?
description: This model is a set of imatrix quantizations of the INTELLECT-3 model by PrimeIntellect, using the llama.cpp release b7127. The quantizations are based on a dataset from a combined source, including a subset of combined_all_small.parquet from Ed Addario. The model can be run directly with llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The prompt format includes a system prompt, user input, and assistant response. The model is available in various quantization types, each with different file sizes and quality levels. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports online repacking for ARM and AVX CPU inference, which can improve performance on specific hardware. For models larger than 50GB, the files are split and can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool. The Q4_0 quantization format has been deprecated in favor of online repacking. The I-quants (IQX_X) are newer and offer better performance for their size, especially when using cuBLAS (Nvidia) or rocBLAS (AMD), but may be slower on CPU compared to K-quants. The choice of quantization depends on available hardware resources, desired speed, and quality. The model has been benchmarked on an AVX2 system, showing performance improvements for certain quantizations. Credits are given to several contributors for assistance with dataset creation, inspiration, and sponsorship.

instruction: I’m working on a project involving code documentation—can you help me identify the most useful resources?
description: ZeroEntropy has developed zerank-2, a state-of-the-art reranker designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems in search engines. This model outperforms closed-source competitors such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and gemini-2.5-flash across various domains, including finance, legal, code, STEM, medical, and conversational data. The model uses an innovative multi-stage pipeline that models query-document relevance scores as adjusted. The model is currently released under a non-commercial license, and commercial license inquiries can be directed to founders@zeroentropy.dev. For evaluation purposes, OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small is used as an initial retriever for the Top 100 candidate documents. Across multiple domains, zerank-2 achieves NDCG@10 scores of 0.6346 in the Web domain, 0.6140 in Conversational, 0.6521 in STEM & Logic, 0.6528 in Code, 0.6644 in Legal, 0.7217 in Biomedical, and 0.7600 in Finance, compared to an average score of 0.6714. These scores outperform or are competitive with other models such as zerank-1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Cohere rerank-3.5. The model can be used via the sentence_transformers library as a CrossEncoder and is also available for inference through ZeroEntropy's endpoint.

instruction: What does the speaker in this audio mean when they say, 'I’ll be there soon'?
description: Ultravox is a multimodal Speech LLM that can consume both speech and text as input, such as a text system prompt and a voice user message. The input to the model is given as a text prompt with a special pseudo-token, and the model processor replaces this token with embeddings derived from the input audio. Using the merged embeddings as input, the model generates output text as usual. In the v0.6 series, Ultravox models are trained on expanded Hindi speech data, resulting in significantly improved speech understanding performance on Hindi and modest degradation on other languages. Additionally, the v0.6 models are trained on noise datasets for improved noise robustness and the ability to output a special string '((noise))' if the input audio is too noisy or doesn't contain clear speech. In a future revision of Ultravox, the plan is to expand the token vocabulary to support the generation of semantic and acoustic audio tokens, which can then be fed to a vocoder to produce voice output. No preference tuning has been applied to this revision of the model. Ultravox is built around a pretrained LLM (Llama, Gemma, Qwen, etc) and a speech encoder backbone. The multi-modal adapter is trained, the Whisper encoder is fine-tuned, and the LLM is kept frozen. The model uses a knowledge-distillation loss where Ultravox is trying to match the logits of the text-based LLM backbone. The training dataset is a mix of ASR datasets, extended with continuations generated by Llama 3.1 8B, speech translation datasets, and noise datasets. The training procedure involves supervised speech instruction finetuning via knowledge-distillation. The training regime uses BF16 mixed precision training on 8x H100 GPUs. The current version of Ultravox, when invoked with audio content, has a time-to-first-token (TTFT) of approximately 150ms, and a tokens-per-second rate of ~50-100 when using an A100-40GB GPU, all using a text-based LLM (Llama, Gemma, or Qwen) backbone. Evaluations are conducted on covost2 (speech translation measured in BLEU), fleurs and ultravox_calls (speech recognition measured in WER), big bench audio (audio reasoning measured in accuracy), as well as musan and ultravox_unintelligible (noise/unintelligible speech detection measured in recall). The model is developed by Fixie.ai and is licensed under the MIT license. It can be used as a voice agent, for speech-to-speech translation, and for the analysis of spoken audio.

instruction: Can you analyze the current technical signals to determine if the market is in a RISK_ON or RISK_OFF state?
description: This is a production-ready XGBoost model for predicting market regimes (RISK_ON/RISK_OFF) in Indian financial markets, specifically for the NIFTY 50 index. It is part of a complete MLOps pipeline that includes experiment tracking, data versioning, and automated deployment. The model performs binary classification to predict whether the market is in a RISK_ON or RISK_OFF regime. RISK_ON indicates favorable market conditions with lower volatility and bullish momentum, suitable for aggressive trading, while RISK_OFF indicates cautious conditions with higher volatility, where defensive positioning is advised. The model uses four key technical indicators as features: India VIX (a volatility index), RSI (14-day) for momentum, 50-Day MA for short-term trend, and 200-Day MA for long-term trend. It is trained using XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) with the binary logistic objective function and logloss as the evaluation metric. The model is evaluated using both ML metrics and finance metrics, with production thresholds requiring an F1 score of at least 0.65 and a Sharpe Ratio of at least 0.5. The model is integrated into an MLOps system that includes MLflow for experiment tracking and model registry, DVC for data version control, GitHub Actions for automated CI/CD pipeline, FastAPI for REST API serving, Docker for containerized deployment, and Hugging Face Spaces for cloud deployment. The training pipeline includes data preprocessing, feature engineering, train/test split with stratification, XGBoost training with hyperparameters, model evaluation, backtesting on historical data, and model registration in MLflow. The model is retrained weekly via GitHub Actions, with automatic promotion to production if performance thresholds are met. The model is version-controlled in MLflow Model Registry under the name market_regime_classifier, version 1, in the Production stage, with run ID 43544e1ed8eb4c2a9268948b6795bdf5. The dataset used is synthetic market data for NIFTY 50, with an 80/20 train/test split. The model can be used via MLflow or a REST API endpoint. This project demonstrates MLOps pipeline architecture, experiment tracking, model registry management, data versioning, CI/CD automation, model serving, financial metrics and backtesting, Docker containerization, and cloud deployment. It is licensed under the MIT License for free use in learning and projects.

instruction: What is the top prediction for the content of this video?
