description: V-JEPA 2 is a frontier video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta. It extends the pretraining objectives of previous models, resulting in state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities by leveraging large-scale data and model sizes. The model includes a video classification head and is pretrained on the Something-Something-V2 dataset. V-JEPA 2 is designed to support video classification tasks and is intended for use with the latest version of the transformers library. The model can be loaded using the AutoModelForVideoClassification and AutoVideoProcessor classes from the transformers library. During inference, video frames are sampled according to the model's configuration, preprocessed, and passed through the model to obtain classification logits. The model outputs probabilities for the top predicted classes, enabling interpretation of the video content. V-JEPA 2 is part of ongoing research in self-supervised video modeling and is intended to enable understanding, prediction, and planning in video data.

instruction: How can I apply this technique to autonomous vehicle navigation in real-world conditions?
description: STream3R presents a novel approach to 3D reconstruction by reformulating pointmap prediction as a decoder-only Transformer problem. It introduces a streaming framework that processes image sequences efficiently using causal attention, inspired by advances in modern language modeling. By learning geometric priors from large-scale 3D datasets, STream3R generalizes well to diverse and challenging scenarios, including dynamic scenes where traditional methods often fail. The model reformulates dense 3D reconstruction into a sequential registration task with causal attention. It supports multiple attention mechanisms, including FlashAttention, KV Cache, Causal Attention, Sliding Window Attention, and Full Attention. STream3R is inherently compatible with LLM-style training infrastructure, enabling efficient large-scale pretraining and fine-tuning for various downstream 3D tasks. Extensive experiments show that the method consistently outperforms prior work across both static and dynamic scene benchmarks. The model can be used in real-time or low-latency applications, such as real-time robotics, autonomous navigation, online 3D understanding, and SLAM. The model supports inference modes such as causal, window (with a default window size of 5), and full attention. A KV cache version is available to enable streaming input using StreamSession, which processes sequential input one by one. On the NRGBD dataset, STream3R achieves an accuracy mean of 0.057, an accuracy median of 0.014, a completeness mean of 0.028, a completeness median of 0.013, a non-completeness mean of 0.910, and a non-completeness median of 0.993, outperforming other methods. The peak GPU memory usage (VRAM) and runtime for the model are reported for different numbers of frames. For causal attention, VRAM usage increases with the number of frames, reaching 45.41 GB for 200 frames, while runtime increases to 1.1703 seconds. For sliding window attention, VRAM usage remains constant at 6.53 GB, and runtime stabilizes at around 0.1463 seconds for 200 frames. The model is trained on preprocessed datasets following standard configurations. Evaluation and training code are not yet released, and a metric-scale version is also pending. The project is licensed under an unspecified license, and redistribution and use should follow this license. If you use the code or paper, you are encouraged to cite the provided BibTeX entry.

instruction: I need to understand if the actions in this clip are aggressive or not. Can you assess it?
description: This model is a fine-tuned version of the VideoMAE architecture for binary violence detection in video content. It classifies videos into two categories: Violent Crime (1) and Non-Violent Incident (0). The model was trained on a curated subset of the UCF Crime dataset with event-based categorization, consisting of 600 videos, equally split between violent and non-violent categories. The event distribution includes 28 videos of abuse, 18 of arrest, 16 of arson, 62 of assault, 120 of burglary, 54 of explosion, 48 of fighting, 58 of road accidents, 184 of robbery, 36 of shoplifting, 46 of stealing, and 72 of vandalism. The dataset was split into 384 training videos, 96 validation videos, and 120 test videos. The model uses a VideoMAE Base architecture with a fine-tuning approach for event-based binary classification. It processes 16 frames per video clip at an input resolution of 224x224 pixels per frame. The model was trained for 15 epochs with a learning rate of 5e-05, a batch size of 2, and the AdamW optimizer. On the validation set, it achieved a loss of 1.4159, accuracy of 0.7188, precision of 0.7207, recall of 0.7188, and F1 score of 0.7190. On the test set, it achieved a loss of 1.7586, accuracy of 0.6833, precision of 0.6963, recall of 0.6833, and F1 score of 0.6802. The model was trained in 69.5 minutes and achieved a best accuracy of 0.7188. The model is intended for use in research and development in video analysis, security system enhancement with human oversight, and educational purposes in computer vision and AI safety. It is not suitable for unauthorized surveillance, discriminatory profiling, automated punishment, or privacy violations. The model may have limitations in generalizing to all types of violence, may miss context in longer sequences due to its 16-frame clip approach, and may be affected by environmental factors such as lighting, camera angles, and video quality. It may also misclassify intense but non-violent activities. The model was developed using the HuggingFace Transformers framework and is compatible with Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, or CPU. It is based on the MCG-NJU/videomae-base model and is available for use with the HuggingFace Transformers library. The model was trained on 2025-06-02 11:52:12 UTC and is intended for use by researchers, security professionals, and developers.

instruction: How can I get a sense of the 3D structure from a single 2D image like this one?
description: DA3 Small is a model for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation. It is an efficient foundation model that uses a unified depth-ray representation. The model is part of the Any-view Model series, has 0.08B parameters, and is licensed under Apache 2.0. It supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. The model is developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a Vision Transformer for Visual Geometry with a plain transformer architecture. It is trained using public academic datasets only. A single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, is used as the backbone without architectural specialization. The depth-ray representation allows the model to avoid complex multi-task learning. The model significantly outperforms Depth Anything 2 for monocular depth estimation and VGGT for multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it is trained on academic datasets and may have limitations on domain-specific images. Performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: I need to replace the lighting in this scene with a warm golden glow—can you do that seamlessly?
description: Lucy Edit 1.1 Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of edits, including clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the video. Built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, it inherits a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, making it easier to adapt existing scripts and workflows. The model preserves motion and composition perfectly, allowing for precise edits. It offers improved edit reliability compared to common inference time methods. It supports changing wardrobes and accessories, such as adding glasses, earrings, or hats. It can replace characters with monsters, animals, or known characters, for example, replacing a person with a polar bear. It can also transform scenes, such as turning a scene into a 2D cartoon. It uses pure text instructions without requiring finetuning or masks for common edits. It supports global edits to the background and style, performing scene-wide changes while preserving subjects. It provides seamless blending with cleaner boundaries and more coherent lighting across edits. It can insert general objects with improved spatial grounding. The model has approximately 5B parameters and is built on top of Wan2.2 5B. Version 1.1 includes better overall quality, higher fidelity details, stronger prompt adherence, and improved identity preservation. It offers more reliable global edits with better subject preservation and control. It provides better blending and seamless edits with cleaner boundaries, fewer artifacts, and improved color and lighting consistency across frames. It supports the addition of general objects with more reliable insertion of wearable, handheld, and general scene objects with improved spatial grounding, occlusion, and shadows. Lucy Edit 1.1 is designed for precise, realistic, and identity-preserving video edits. Prompts with 20–30 descriptive words work best, and using the right trigger words helps the model understand the intent. Supported edit types include clothing changes, human or character replacement, object replacement, color changes, adding objects, and global transformations. The model excels at identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and prompt adherence. Longer prompts describing style, appearance, and context improve results. Frame counts of 81 produce better temporal consistency than shorter clips. Version 1.1 improves boundary blending and temporal coherence across edits. The model is available as a hosted API with 5000 free credits. A Diffusers pipeline (LucyEditPipeline) is available, and ComfyUI integration is coming soon. The roadmap includes a public batch API, Diffusers pipeline, remote ComfyUI custom nodes, and a technical report. The model is based on the Wan2.2 (5B) foundation and acknowledges the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face.

instruction: Please identify and transcribe the English text in this photo of a signboard.
description: en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is a text line recognition model developed by the PaddleOCR team as part of the PP-OCRv5_rec series. It is designed to efficiently and accurately recognize English text. The model achieves an accuracy of 85.3 percent, where a line is considered incorrect if any character, including punctuation, is wrong. This strict evaluation ensures higher accuracy in practical applications. The model can be used for text recognition tasks and is part of a larger OCR pipeline that includes modules for document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition. The en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec model is intended to be used in conjunction with other models in the pipeline to address complex real-world OCR challenges. The model supports integration into projects through the PaddleOCR library, which requires installation of PaddlePaddle and PaddleOCR. The model can be run using a command-line interface or integrated into Python code for inference. The default model in the pipeline is PP-OCRv5_server_rec, so specifying en_PP-OCRv5_mobile_rec is necessary for use in the pipeline. The model can also be used with a local model file if provided.

instruction: Please locate all the chemical terms in this section of a biomedical review.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.93, precision of 0.92, recall of 0.94, and accuracy of 0.99. The model is based on the ModernBERT-large architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is suitable for use in clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. If used in research or applications, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: This email is full of abbreviations. Can you still make a coherent summary of what the sender is saying?
description: This is a fine-tuned T5-small model specialized for email summarization. The model can generate both brief (one-line) and detailed (comprehensive) summaries of emails, and is robust to messy, informal inputs with typos and abbreviations. The base model is T5-small, and it was fine-tuned by the Wordcab Team. It is a T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) model designed for English language processing and is released under the Apache 2.0 License. The model supports dual-mode summarization through the use of `summarize_brief:` and `summarize_full:` prefixes. It is optimized for fast inference and real-world email processing. The intended uses include summarizing professional and casual emails, email inbox management and prioritization, meeting invitation processing, customer support email triage, and newsletter summarization. Limitations include a maximum input length of 512 tokens (~2500 characters), support for English language only, potential difficulty in capturing nuanced context in very long email threads, and unsuitability for legal or medical document summarization. The model was fine-tuned on a dataset containing 364,000 email-summary pairs, including professional business emails, casual informal communications, meeting invitations and updates, technical discussions, and customer service interactions. The training data was augmented with subtle typos and misspellings in 50% of examples, common abbreviations, and various formatting styles. Training was conducted for one epoch with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 3e-4, using a validation split of 1%. The model was trained on an NVIDIA GPU with mixed precision (fp16) using the HuggingFace Transformers 4.36.0 framework. Emails were formatted as `Subject: [subject]. Body: [content]`, and two training examples were created per email (brief and full summaries). Data augmentation was applied to 50% of examples. The model achieved a ROUGE-L score of 0.42, an average inference time of 0.63 seconds for brief summaries and 0.71 seconds for full summaries on a T4 GPU, and a coherence score of 80% on messy inputs. It successfully differentiates between brief and full summaries, with a 2.5x length difference, and is robust in handling informal text and typos. The model is based on the T5 architecture, which includes an encoder and decoder with multiple T5 blocks, embedding layers, and a linear language model head. The model is released under the Apache 2.0 License and is based on the T5 model. It was fine-tuned on a dataset and trained using HuggingFace Transformers. Special thanks are given to the open-source community.

instruction: How would you turn this paragraph about a forest into a moving visual scene?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The high-noise expert handles early-stage layout, while the low-noise expert refines details in later stages. Each expert has approximately 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also introduces a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling 720P@24fps video generation on consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. This model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks and is among the fastest available for 720P@24fps generation. The T2V-A14B model supports 5-second video generation at 480P and 720P resolutions. On the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, the model outperforms leading commercial models across most key evaluation dimensions. The model is available in multiple versions, including T2V-A14B, I2V-A14B, and TI2V-5B, and can be run using ComfyUI or Diffusers integrations. Computational efficiency tests show performance and memory usage across different GPU configurations. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with legal and ethical standards.

instruction: Produce a 3D rendering of a fractured skull to assist in a trauma surgery planning session.
description: Shap-MeD is a text-to-3D object generative model specialized in the biomedical domain. It is designed to assist in the 3D modeling of medical objects, aiming to reduce development time. 3D modeling in medicine has various applications, including surgical procedure simulation and planning, the design of personalized prosthetic implants, medical education, the creation of anatomical models, and the development of research prototypes. Shap-MeD is based on Shap-e, an open-source text-to-3D generative model developed by OpenAI, and is fine-tuned using a dataset of biomedical objects. The model achieved a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.089 in latent generation on the evaluation set, compared to Shap-e's MSE of 0.147. A qualitative evaluation was also conducted, comparing Shap-MeD with other models in the generation of biomedical objects. The results indicate that Shap-MeD demonstrates higher structural accuracy in biomedical object generation.

instruction: Generate a short video clip that brings this static scene to life with natural movement.
description: Wan2.1 is a comprehensive and open suite of video foundation models designed to push the boundaries of video generation. It consistently outperforms existing open-source models and state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple benchmarks. The T2V-1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with most consumer-grade GPUs. It can generate a 5-second 480P video on an RTX 4090 in about 4 minutes, with performance comparable to some closed-source models. Wan2.1 supports multiple tasks, including Text-to-Video, Image-to-Video, Video Editing, Text-to-Image, and Video-to-Audio. It is the first video model capable of generating both Chinese and English text, enhancing its practical applications. The model features a powerful video VAE called Wan-VAE, which delivers exceptional efficiency and performance, encoding and decoding 1080P videos of any length while preserving temporal information. The repository includes the I2V-14B model, which is capable of generating 480P videos with fast generation and excellent quality. Wan2.1 is built on the diffusion transformer paradigm, incorporating a novel 3D causal VAE architecture, scalable training strategies, large-scale data construction, and automated evaluation metrics. The model uses a Flow Matching framework within the Diffusion Transformer paradigm, with a T5 Encoder to encode multilingual text input and cross-attention in each transformer block to embed the text. An MLP with a Linear layer and a SiLU layer processes the input time embeddings and predicts six modulation parameters individually. The model is trained on a curated and deduplicated dataset of image and video data, processed through a four-step data cleaning process focusing on fundamental dimensions, visual quality, and motion quality. Manual evaluations indicate that Wan2.1 outperforms both closed-source and open-source models. The model is available under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards.

instruction: Explain the concept of financial leverage and its impact on corporate profitability.
description: LLM Pro Finance is a multilingual AI model designed for finance, economics, and business professionals. It is trained on a vast corpus of high-quality financial and economic data, including market reports, regulatory filings, central bank publications, and academic research. The model is fluent in English, French, and German, with a nuanced understanding of financial terminology across these languages. It provides well-structured, factually grounded explanations, summaries, and analyses tailored to professional standards. The model is built with a focus on reliability, accuracy, and transparency, using low-carbon computing infrastructure. It supports a range of use cases, including financial analysis, regulatory compliance, investment research, economic forecasting, and client communication. The training data is composed of 54.4% financial content, 19.8% translation tasks, 15.6% general knowledge, 8.0% retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and 2.2% reasoning, math, and code. This composition ensures strong domain expertise in banking, investment, and economic reasoning, while retaining versatility for general and multilingual use cases. The model is based on Llama 3 and is fine-tuned for domain-specific performance. It was trained in a low-carbon datacenter powered by renewable energy. Rigorous red teaming exercises were conducted to ensure the model maintains high standards of safety and ethical integrity. These exercises confirmed that the model maintains a significantly low toxicity score, consistently avoids biased or stereotypical language, and resists generating misinformation, prioritizing factual accuracy and contextual relevance in its responses.

instruction: I'm working on a project where I need to prioritize documents based on their relevance to a specific topic. Can you help me with that?
description: ZeroEntropy has developed zerank-1, a state-of-the-art reranker designed to improve the accuracy of retrieval systems in search engines. This model outperforms closed-source competitors such as cohere-rerank-v3.5 and Salesforce/LlamaRank-v1 across various domains, including finance, legal, code, STEM, medical, and conversational data. The model is part of an innovative multi-stage pipeline that models query-document relevance scores as adjusted values. The model is currently released under a non-commercial license, and commercial license inquiries should be directed to founders@zeroentropy.dev. A smaller version of the model is fully open-sourced under an Apache 2.0 License. Evaluations using NDCG@10 scores show that zerank-1 achieves strong performance across multiple tasks, including Code, Conversational, Finance, Legal, Medical, and STEM. For example, in the Finance domain, zerank-1 achieves an NDCG@10 score of 0.894, outperforming other models. In the STEM domain, it achieves a score of 0.694. The model is implemented as a CrossEncoder and can be used with the sentence_transformers library. It can also be accessed via ZeroEntropy's inference endpoint.

instruction: Explain the steps involved in solving a quadratic equation with an example.
description: Qwen2.5 is the latest series of Qwen large language models. The Qwen2.5 series includes a range of base language models and instruction-tuned language models with parameter sizes ranging from 0.5 to 72 billion. This model introduces several improvements over Qwen2, including significantly more knowledge and enhanced capabilities in coding and mathematics, achieved through specialized expert models in these domains. It also shows significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data such as tables, and generating structured outputs, especially JSON. The model is more resilient to the diversity of system prompts, which enhances role-play implementation and condition-setting for chatbots. It supports long-context processing up to 128K tokens and can generate up to 8K tokens. The model supports over 29 languages, including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, and Arabic. The instruction-tuned 7B Qwen2.5 model is a causal language model that has undergone pretraining and post-training. It uses a transformer architecture with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. It has 7.61 billion parameters, with 6.53 billion non-embedding parameters. The model has 28 layers and uses 28 attention heads for queries and 4 for keys and values. It supports a full context length of 131,072 tokens and can generate up to 8192 tokens. The model requires the latest version of the Hugging Face `transformers` library for compatibility. Older versions of `transformers` (before 4.37.0) may result in a KeyError. The model can be loaded and used with the `AutoModelForCausalLM` and `AutoTokenizer` classes from the `transformers` library. For processing long texts beyond 32,768 tokens, a technique called YaRN is used to enhance model length extrapolation. This can be enabled by adding a specific configuration to the `config.json` file. For deployment, vLLM is recommended, although it currently only supports static YARN, which may impact performance on shorter texts. The model's evaluation results are detailed in a separate document, and information on GPU memory requirements and throughput is also available. If the model is used, it should be cited using the provided references.

instruction: How do I add a subtle vignette effect to the corners of this video?
description: Ditto is a framework designed to address the scarcity of high-quality training data for instruction-based video editing. It introduces a novel data generation pipeline that combines the creative diversity of a leading image editor with an in-context video generator. The framework employs an efficient, distilled model architecture augmented by a temporal enhancer to reduce computational overhead and improve temporal coherence. An intelligent agent drives the entire pipeline, crafting diverse instructions and filtering output to ensure quality control at scale. Using this framework, the Ditto-1M dataset was created, consisting of one million high-fidelity video editing examples. The Editto model was trained on this dataset using a curriculum learning strategy, demonstrating superior instruction-following ability and establishing a new state-of-the-art in instruction-based video editing. Editto is a model designed for video editing based on textual instructions. It supports tasks such as applying editing instructions to input videos to generate output videos. The model can be used with the DiffSynth framework or the ComfyUI interface. When using ComfyUI, it is noted that due to the use of quantized and distilled models, there may be some quality degradation compared to full-precision models. The model requires specific model weights and dependencies to be downloaded and set up in designated directories. The model is licensed under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 and is provided for academic research purposes only.

instruction: Can you identify and label all the objects in this photo of a busy street scene?
description: The DETR (DEtection TRansformer) model is an encoder-decoder transformer with a convolutional backbone, specifically a ResNet-50 backbone. It is designed for end-to-end object detection and was trained on the COCO 2017 dataset, which includes 118,000 annotated images for training and 5,000 for validation. The model was introduced in a paper by Carion et al. and features two heads on top of the decoder outputs: a linear layer for class labels and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for bounding boxes. Object detection is performed using object queries, with 100 queries for the COCO dataset. The model is trained using a bipartite matching loss, which compares predicted classes and bounding boxes to ground truth annotations. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create a one-to-one mapping between queries and annotations, followed by optimization using cross-entropy for classes and a combination of L1 and generalized IoU loss for bounding boxes. The model achieves an average precision (AP) of 42.0 on the COCO 2017 validation set. It is intended for object detection tasks and supports PyTorch. The model can be used with an image processor and object detection pipeline to extract and interpret detection results. The training process involved 300 epochs on 16 V100 GPUs, with a batch size of 64. Images were preprocessed by resizing and normalizing with ImageNet mean and standard deviation values. The model card was written by the Hugging Face team, as the original DETR team did not provide one.

instruction: Can you help me find the most relevant documents in a multilingual dataset?
description: The Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B is a text embedding model from the Qwen3 Embedding series, designed for tasks such as text retrieval, code retrieval, text classification, text clustering, and bitext mining. It is part of a series that includes models of various sizes (0.6B, 4B, and 8B) and provides both embedding and reranking capabilities. The model is built upon the dense foundational models of the Qwen3 series and inherits their multilingual capabilities, long-text understanding, and reasoning skills. It supports over 100 languages, including various programming languages, and offers robust multilingual, cross-lingual, and code retrieval capabilities. The Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B has 0.6 billion parameters, a context length of 32,000 tokens, and supports embedding dimensions up to 1024, with user-defined output dimensions ranging from 32 to 1024. The model is instruction-aware, meaning it supports customizing input instructions to enhance performance for specific tasks, languages, or scenarios. Evaluation results show that the 8B size embedding model ranks No.1 in the MTEB multilingual leaderboard with a score of 70.58 (as of June 5, 2025), and the Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B achieves a mean score of 64.33 on the MTEB multilingual benchmark. Developers are advised to use tailored instructions for their tasks and to write instructions in English for optimal performance, as most training instructions were in English. The model can be used with frameworks such as Sentence Transformers, Transformers, and vLLM, and supports deployment via Text Embeddings Inference (TEI) on both GPU and CPU devices. The model is available for use with specific versions of libraries and requires attention to compatibility with older versions of Transformers. The Qwen3 Embedding series is described as offering exceptional versatility and comprehensive flexibility, with a full spectrum of sizes and features to cater to diverse use cases.

instruction: Could you help me create a short animation based on this image, preserving the first frame's details?
description: SteadyDancer is a model designed for harmonized and coherent human image animation with first-frame preservation. It is intended to animate human images while maintaining the structure and appearance of the first frame. The model is associated with the Multimedia Computing Group at Nanjing University and the Platform and Content Group (PCG) at Tencent. This repository provides checkpoints for the model described in the paper titled 'SteadyDancer: Harmonized and Coherent Human Image Animation with First-Frame Preservation.' The model offers lower computational cost compared to alternatives. For users encountering a 'RuntimeError: expected scalar type Float but found BFloat16' issue, it is recommended to pull the latest code of Kijai ComfyUI. For each quantized version of the model, two sets of weights are provided: one derived from fp16 format and another from fp32 format. Both sets of weights are available and yield largely consistent results. The team is currently working to determine the final version of the model to be retained.

instruction: How can I make the background of a fast-paced sports video appear more naturally lit?
description: TC-Light is a one-shot model designed to manipulate the illumination distribution of video and achieve realistic world transfer. It is particularly suitable for high-dynamic videos, such as those with motion-rich actions and frequent switching of foreground and background objects. The model is distinguished by its outstanding temporal consistency on highly dynamic scenarios and superior computational efficiency, enabling long video processing, such as 300 frames at a resolution of 1280x720 on a 40G A100 GPU. These features make it valuable for sim2real and real2real augmentation for embodied agents or for preparing video pairs to train stronger video relighting models. TC-Light is a novel generative renderer that addresses the limitations of existing models, which are often constrained by the domain of training data or face bottlenecks in temporal consistency and computational efficiency, especially with complex dynamics and long durations. The model operates in two stages: the first stage optimizes appearance embedding to align global illumination, and the second stage optimizes the Unique Video Tensor (UVT), a canonical video representation, to align fine-grained texture and lighting. To evaluate performance, a long and highly dynamic video benchmark was established. Extensive experiments show that TC-Light enables physically plausible re-rendering results with superior temporal coherence and low computational cost. The model tokenizes input latents in the xy plane and yt plane separately, and the predicted noises are combined for denoising. The output undergoes the two-stage optimization process. The model works better on video resolutions over 512x512, as this is the minimum resolution used to train the related IC-Light model. Higher resolutions help maintain the consistency of image intrinsic properties. It performs relatively better on realistic scenes than on synthetic ones, in terms of both temporal consistency and physical plausibility. However, it struggles to drastically change the illumination of night scenarios or hard shadows, as demonstrated in IC-Light.

instruction: Can I use a bounding box to isolate a specific region in this photo?
description: EdgeTAM is an on-device executable variant of the SAM 2 model designed for promptable segmentation and tracking in videos. It runs 22× faster than SAM 2 and achieves 16 FPS on iPhone 15 Pro Max without quantization. EdgeTAM supports automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image. It can be used for basic image segmentation through single point clicks, multiple points for refinement, and bounding box inputs. The model allows for segmentation of multiple objects simultaneously. It supports batch inference for multiple images and multiple objects per image, including complex batch scenarios with multiple points per object. EdgeTAM can use masks from previous predictions as input to refine segmentation. A key strength of EdgeTAM is its ability to track objects across video frames. It supports basic video tracking, multi-object video tracking, and refining video segmentation through additional clicks on any frame. For real-time applications, EdgeTAM supports streaming video inference, processing video frames as they arrive. It also supports video batch processing for multiple objects, allowing multiple objects to be tracked simultaneously in video by adding them all at once.

instruction: Can you analyze a short video clip and extract key visual features from it?
description: The VideoMAEv2-Base model is a base-sized model pre-trained for 800 epochs in a self-supervised manner on the UnlabeldHybrid-1M dataset. It was introduced in the paper 'VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking' by Wang et al. and first released in 2023. The model is intended for use in video feature extraction. It is designed to process video data and extract meaningful features from it. The model can be used with the Transformers library by loading the appropriate configuration, image processor, and model from the 'OpenGVLab/VideoMAEv2-Base' repository. The video input is expected to be in the form of a list of numpy arrays with dimensions (16, 3, 224, 24), representing 16 frames of 224x224 RGB images. The input is processed using the VideoMAEImageProcessor, and the model outputs are obtained by passing the processed inputs through the model in a no-grad context. The model is described in the following BibTeX entries: @InProceedings{wang2023videomaev2, author = {Wang, Limin and Huang, Bingkun and Zhao, Zhiyu and Tong, Zhan and He, Yinan and Wang, Yi and Wang, Yali and Qiao, Yu}, title = {VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders With Dual Masking}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)}, month = {June}, year = {2023}, pages = {14549-14560}} and @misc{videomaev2, title={VideoMAE V2: Scaling Video Masked Autoencoders with Dual Masking}, author={Limin Wang and Bingkun Huang and Zhiyu Zhao and Zhan Tong and Yinan He and Yi Wang and Yali Wang and Yu Qiao}, year={2023}, eprint={2303.16727}, archivePrefix={arXiv}, primaryClass={cs.CV}}.

instruction: What is the predicted C value for the chemical with the SMILES 'C1=CC=C(C=C1)C(=O)N'?
description: This is an AttentiveFP (Attention-based Fingerprint) Graph Neural Network model trained to predict fugacity-based environmental fate parameter C. This property is used in fugacity models for environmental distribution prediction. The model takes SMILES strings as input and uses graph neural networks to predict molecular properties directly from the molecular structure. The model type is AttentiveFP (Graph Neural Network), and it performs a regression task. It accepts SMILES strings as input and produces a continuous numerical value as output. The model is implemented using the PyTorch Geometric framework and is based on an AttentiveFP architecture with enhanced atom and bond features. The model's hyperparameters include a hidden channel size of 64, two layers, two timesteps, a dropout rate of 0.2, a learning rate of 0.001, a weight decay of 1e-05, a batch size of 32, 50 epochs, and a patience of 10. The model was trained on the FBC dataset from the Pyrosage project, which focuses on molecular toxicity and environmental property prediction. The model is trained on specific chemical datasets and may not generalize to all molecular types. Performance may vary for molecules significantly different from the training distribution. The model requires proper SMILES string format for input.

instruction: Show me a surreal landscape with floating mountains and waterfalls flowing upwards.
description: The FLUX.2 [klein] model family are the fastest image models to date, unifying generation and editing in a single compact architecture, delivering state-of-the-art quality with end-to-end inference in as low as under a second. Built for applications that require real-time image generation without sacrificing quality. FLUX.2 [klein] 9B is a 9 billion parameter rectified flow transformer capable of generating images from text descriptions and supports multi-reference editing capabilities. It is the flagship small model, defining the Pareto frontier for quality versus latency across text-to-image, single-reference editing, and multi-reference generation. It matches or exceeds models five times its size in under half a second. It is built on a 9B flow model with an 8B Qwen3 text embedder, step-distilled to 4 inference steps. It is a distilled model for sub-second image generation with outstanding quality. It supports text-to-image and image-to-image multi-reference editing in a single unified model. It is suitable for real-time generation and integration into applications. It is ideal for creative exploration with excellent prompt adherence and output diversity. It is available for non-commercial use. The model is not intended or able to provide factual information. While the model can output text, the text rendered may be inaccurate or subject to distortion. As a statistical model, this checkpoint may represent or amplify biases observed in the training data. The model may fail to generate output that matches the prompts. Prompt following is heavily influenced by the prompting style. The model and its derivatives may not be used in any way that violates applicable law, for the purpose of exploiting, harming or attempting to exploit or harm minors in any way, including but not limited to the solicitation, creation, acquisition, or dissemination of child exploitative content, to generate or disseminate deceptive, fraudulent, misleading or otherwise harmful content, to generate or disseminate personal identifiable information that can be used to harm an individual, to harass, abuse, threaten, stalk, or bully individuals or groups of individuals, to create non-consensual intimate imagery or illegal pornographic content, or for fully automated decision making or high risk applications that adversely impact an individual's legal rights or otherwise create or modify a binding, enforceable obligation. The model fits in approximately 29GB VRAM and is accessible on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and above. Black Forest Labs is committed to the responsible development and deployment of models. Prior to releasing the FLUX.2 family of models, the company evaluated and mitigated a number of risks in model checkpoints and hosted services, including the generation of unlawful content, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate imagery (NCII). Pre-training mitigation included filtering pre-training data for multiple categories of 'not safe for work' (NSFW) and known CSAM to help prevent a user generating unlawful content in response to text prompts or uploaded images. The company partnered with an independent nonprofit organization dedicated to preventing online abuse to filter known CSAM from the training data. Post-training mitigation included multiple rounds of targeted fine-tuning to provide additional mitigation against potential abuse, including both text-to-image (T2I) and image-to-image (I2I) attacks. By inhibiting certain behaviors and suppressing certain concepts in the trained model, these techniques can help to prevent a user generating synthetic CSAM or NCII from a text prompt, or transforming an uploaded image into synthetic CSAM or NCII. Ongoing evaluation included multiple internal and external third-party evaluations of model checkpoints to identify further opportunities for mitigation. External third-party evaluations focused on eliciting CSAM and NCII through adversarial testing with (i) text-only prompts, (ii) a single uploaded reference image with text prompts, and (iii) multiple uploaded reference images with text prompts. Based on this feedback, further safety fine-tuning was conducted to produce the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] models. The release decision included a final third-party evaluation of the proposed release checkpoints, focused on T2I and I2I generation of synthetic CSAM and NCII, including a comparison with other open-weight T2I and I2I models. The final FLUX.2 [klein] checkpoints demonstrated high resilience against violative inputs in complex generation and editing tasks, and demonstrated higher resilience than leading open-weight models across these risk categories. Based on these findings, the open-weight FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models were approved for release under an Apache 2.0 license, and the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models were released under a non-commercial license to support third-party research and development. Inference filters for NSFW and protected content in inputs and outputs are included in the repository for the FLUX.2 [klein] models. Filters or manual review must be used with the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models under the terms of the FLUX Non-Commercial License, and deployers are encouraged to implement these mitigations when using the FLUX.2 [klein] 4B models. Where these features are implemented on hosted services, multiple filters may be applied to intercept text prompts, uploaded images, and output images. Both in-house and third-party filters are used to mitigate against harmful outputs, such as CSAM and NCII outputs, including filters provided by external organizations. Content provenance features can help users and platforms better identify, label, and interpret AI-generated content online. The inference code for FLUX.2 [klein] implements an example of pixel-layer watermarking. Additionally, the repository includes links to a standard for metadata. The API for FLUX.2 [klein] applies cryptographically-signed C2PA metadata to downloaded output content to indicate that images were produced with the model. Acceptable use of the models and access to the API are governed by policies set out in applicable documentation, including the FLUX Non-Commercial License (for non-commercial open-weight users), Developer Terms of Service, Self-Hosted Commercial License Terms, and Usage Policy (for commercial open-weight model users), and Developer Terms of Service, FLUX API Service Terms, and Usage Policy (for API users). These prohibit the generation of unlawful content or the use of generated content for unlawful, defamatory, or abusive purposes. Developers and users must consent to these conditions to access the FLUX.2 [klein] 9B models on Hugging Face. Black Forest Labs takes model safety seriously and may monitor use to detect misuse or abuse of models and services. A dedicated email address (safety@blackforestlabs.ai) is provided to solicit feedback from the community. The company maintains a reporting relationship with organizations such as the and the and welcomes ongoing engagement with authorities, developers, and researchers to share intelligence about emerging risks and develop effective mitigations.

instruction: Can you generate a flowchart from this image using Draw.io format?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for deployment flexibility. Key enhancements include the ability to operate PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It can generate Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. The model supports advanced spatial perception, including object position, viewpoint, and occlusion judgment, with 2D and 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. It has a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. The model excels in enhanced multimodal reasoning, particularly in STEM and Math, with causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. It features upgraded visual recognition with broader, higher-quality pretraining that can recognize a wide range of subjects, including celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. The model supports expanded OCR in 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. It achieves text understanding comparable to pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE for full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings, enhancing long-horizon video reasoning. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. This is the weight repository for Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct.

instruction: I want to create an audiobook — can you generate a voice that sounds engaging and expressive?
description: VibeVoice is a novel open-source text-to-speech (TTS) model designed to generate expressive, long-form, multi-speaker conversational audio, such as podcasts, from text. It addresses key limitations in traditional TTS systems, including scalability, speaker consistency, and natural turn-taking. A core innovation is the use of continuous speech tokenizers—Acoustic and Semantic—operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz. These tokenizers preserve audio fidelity while improving computational efficiency for long sequences. VibeVoice employs a next-token diffusion framework, combining a Large Language Model (LLM) to understand textual context and dialogue flow with a diffusion head to generate high-fidelity acoustic details. The model can synthesize speech up to 90 minutes long with up to 4 distinct speakers, surpassing the typical 1-2 speaker limits of many prior models. The LLM is integrated with specialized acoustic and semantic tokenizers and a diffusion-based decoding head. The Acoustic Tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks, achieving 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. Encoder and decoder components are approximately 340M parameters each. The Semantic Tokenizer mirrors the Acoustic Tokenizer's architecture (without VAE components) and is trained with an ASR proxy task. The Diffusion Head is a lightweight module with 4 layers and ~123M parameters, conditioned on LLM hidden states, and uses a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process with Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 65,536 tokens. Training involves separate pre-training of the Acoustic and Semantic tokenizers, followed by training of the LLM and diffusion head with a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k -> 16K -> 32K -> 64K). The LLM used is Qwen2.5, which typically uses its own text tokenizer. Audio is tokenized via the acoustic and semantic tokenizers. The VibeVoice-1.5B model has a context length of 64K and can generate up to ~90 minutes of audio. The model is intended for research purposes only and is not recommended for commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. It is limited to English and Chinese data, and outputs in other languages may be unintelligible or offensive. The model does not handle background noise, music, or other sound effects and does not generate overlapping speech segments in conversations. It may inherit biases, errors, or omissions from its base model (Qwen2.5 1.5b). High-quality synthetic speech can be misused for impersonation, fraud, or disinformation. Users are expected to use the model lawfully and to disclose the use of AI when sharing AI-generated content. An audible disclaimer is embedded in every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added for provenance verification. Inference requests are logged (hashed) for abuse pattern detection and to publish aggregated statistics quarterly. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and ethically, including securing appropriate rights and anonymizing data prior to use. The model is developed by members of Microsoft Research, and feedback or reports of issues can be sent to VibeVoice@microsoft.com.

instruction: I want to know the relative distances of objects in this nature photograph. Can you help?
description: DA3MONO-LARGE is a monocular depth estimation model designed for high-quality relative depth prediction. It directly predicts depth, which results in superior geometric accuracy compared to disparity-based models like Depth Anything 2. The model is part of the Depth Anything 3 series and is based on a plain transformer architecture with a unified depth-ray representation. It supports tasks such as relative depth estimation and sky segmentation. The model has 0.35B parameters and is released under the Apache 2.0 license. It was developed by the ByteDance Seed Team and is trained exclusively on public academic datasets. The model outperforms Depth Anything 2 in monocular depth estimation and VGGT in multi-view depth estimation and pose estimation. However, it may have limitations when applied to domain-specific images, and its performance can vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: What species names are included in this excerpt from a medical journal?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for species entity recognition, specifically for identifying and extracting species and organism names from biomedical texts. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the LINNAEUS dataset, a specialized biomedical named entity recognition (NER) dataset focused on species name identification and organism recognition in scientific literature. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. It supports the classification of two entity types: B-SPECIES and I-SPECIES. The model is based on the ModernBERT-base architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.89, recall of 0.81, and accuracy of 0.99. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and healthcare analytics contexts. The model is part of the OpenMed NER project, which provides open-source, domain-adapted state-of-the-art transformers for biomedical NER across multiple datasets.

instruction: Imagine a world where people can speak to animals—what kind of conflicts might arise?
description: The gemma-3n-E4B-it is an 8-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or safety of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or suitability for any purpose. Users are solely responsible for any damage or issues arising from the use of the model.

instruction: What are the recommended quantizations for a system with limited VRAM?
description: This is a set of imatrix quantizations of the Voxtral-Mini-3B-2507 model by mistralai, using the llama.cpp release b6014 for quantization. The model is designed to be run using llama.cpp or any other llama.cpp-based project. The quantizations vary in file size, quality, and performance characteristics, with options ranging from full BF16 weights to highly compressed formats such as IQ2_M. Some quantizations use Q8_0 for embedding and output weights to maintain higher quality. The model supports a variety of quantization types, including Q8_0, Q6_K_L, Q6_K, Q5_K_L, Q5_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q4_K_L, Q4_1, Q3_K_XL, Q4_K_M, Q4_K_S, Q4_0, IQ4_NL, IQ4_XS, Q3_K_L, Q3_K_M, Q2_K_L, IQ3_M, Q3_K_S, IQ3_XS, IQ3_XXS, Q2_K, and IQ2_M. Each quantization is described with its quality level, file size, and whether it is recommended for use. Some quantizations, such as Q4_0, support online repacking for improved performance on ARM and AVX hardware. The model can be downloaded using the huggingface-cli tool, with instructions provided for downloading specific files or multiple files if the model exceeds 50GB in size. Performance benchmarks are available for an AVX2 system, showing the tokens per second for various quantizations and tasks. The model can be run on CPU or GPU, with recommendations for choosing a quantization based on available RAM and VRAM. I-quants (IQX_X) are newer and offer better performance for their size when used with cuBLAS or rocBLAS, but may be slower than K-quants on CPU. The model includes credits to contributors for assistance with the imatrix calibration dataset, inspiration for embedding/output experiments, and sponsorship support.

instruction: Generate a spoken version of this email so I can review it by listening.
description: SpeechT5 is a model fine-tuned for speech synthesis (text-to-speech) on the LibriTTS dataset. It is based on the T5 (Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer) framework and is designed to explore encoder-decoder pre-training for self-supervised speech and text representation learning. The SpeechT5 framework includes a shared encoder-decoder network and six modal-specific (speech/text) pre/post-nets. Input speech or text is processed through the pre-nets, the shared encoder-decoder network models the sequence-to-sequence transformation, and the post-nets generate the output in the speech or text modality based on the decoder's output. The model leverages large-scale unlabeled speech and text data to learn a unified-modal representation, aiming to improve modeling capabilities for both modalities. A cross-modal vector quantization approach is used to align textual and speech information into a unified semantic space by randomly mixing up speech/text states with latent units as the interface between encoder and decoder. Extensive evaluations have demonstrated the effectiveness of the SpeechT5 framework on a wide range of spoken language processing tasks, including automatic speech recognition, speech synthesis, speech translation, voice conversion, speech enhancement, and speaker identification. The model was developed by Junyi Ao, Rui Wang, Long Zhou, Chengyi Wang, Shuo Ren, Yu Wu, Shujie Liu, Tom Ko, Qing Li, Yu Zhang, Zhihua Wei, Yao Qian, Jinyu Li, and Furu Wei. It is intended for use in speech synthesis and related spoken language processing tasks. Users should be aware of the model's potential risks, biases, and limitations, though further information is needed for detailed recommendations.

instruction: Extract the names of all proteins or gene products in this text about cancer signaling.
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for high-precision biomedical entity recognition in the domain of cancer genetics. It is optimized for identifying and extracting cancer-related genetic entities from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports a wide range of biomedical entity types, including but not limited to amino acids, anatomical systems, cancers, cells, cellular components, developing anatomical structures, genes or gene products, immaterial anatomical entities, multi-tissue structures, organs, organisms, organism subdivisions, organism substances, pathological formations, simple chemicals, and tissues. Both B- and I- tags are supported for these entity types. The model is trained on the BIONLP2013_CG dataset, a specialized corpus for cancer genetics entities and gene regulation in oncology research. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model achieves an F1 score of 0.88, precision of 0.87, recall of 0.88, and accuracy of 0.94. It is suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model is based on the bge-m3 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is intended for use in clinical, research, and academic settings. If used in research, it should be cited according to the provided citation information.

instruction: How can a system understand the motion of a car turning a corner from a video feed?
description: KAIROS-MM-Qwen2.5-VL-7B-SFT is a multimodal reasoning model based on Qwen2.5-VL, designed to enable robots and vision AI agents to reason about the real world using physics understanding, prior knowledge, and common sense. The model understands space, time, and fundamental physical principles, and is well suited for planning, decision making, and long horizon video reasoning in embodied and agentic systems. It serves as a planning and reasoning backbone for embodied agents, allowing them to infer what actions to take next based on visual observations, temporal context, and physical constraints.

instruction: Please produce a depth analysis for this photo of a kitchen to understand object distances.
description: This is a model card for the `marigold-depth-v1-1` model for monocular depth estimation from a single image. The model is fine-tuned from the `stable-diffusion-2` as described in the papers titled 'Repurposing Diffusion-Based Image Generators for Monocular Depth Estimation' and 'Marigold: Affordable Adaptation of Diffusion-Based Image Generators for Image Analysis'. The model can be used to generate an estimated depth map of an input image. Even though any resolution can be processed, the model inherits the base diffusion model's effective resolution of roughly 768 pixels. This means that for optimal predictions, any larger input image should be resized to make the longer side 768 pixels before feeding it into the model. This model was designed for usage with the DDIM scheduler and between 1 and 50 denoising steps. The predicted values are between 0 and 1, interpolating between the near and far planes of the model's choice. An uncertainty map is produced only when multiple predictions are ensembled with ensemble size larger than 2.

instruction: Replace the person in the video with a robot that moves in the same way.
description: Lucy Edit Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of editing tasks, including clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. The model is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, which includes a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, allowing for easy adaptation of existing scripts and workflows. It is designed to preserve motion and composition perfectly, enabling precise edits. Compared to common inference-time methods, Lucy Edit Dev offers more reliable editing results. It supports changes to wardrobe and accessories, such as adding glasses, earrings, or hats. It can also perform character changes, such as replacing a person with a monster, animal, or known character. Additionally, it supports scene transformations, such as changing a real-world scene into a 2D cartoon. The model operates using pure text instructions, requiring no finetuning or masks for common edits. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of the Wan2.2 5B architecture. Lucy Edit Dev is the first open-source instruction-guided video editing model. It is designed to be used with descriptive text prompts, with best results achieved using prompts of around 20–30 words. Trigger words such as 'change,' 'add,' 'replace,' and 'transform to' help the model understand the user's intent. The model excels at clothing changes, human or character replacement, and object replacement, with strong results for structure-preserving swaps. Color changes have mixed reliability and work best with precise descriptions. Adding objects is most effective for wearable or handheld props. Global transformations can alter the environment or style but may also affect the subject's identity. Lucy Edit Dev is particularly strong in identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and adherence to prompts. Longer, detailed prompts improve results, and 81-frame generations provide better temporal consistency than shorter clips. The model is available for use with the diffusers library and is integrated with the LucyEditPipeline. It is also available via a hosted API. Lucy Edit Dev is built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges the contributions of the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face. The model was first released on 2025-09-18, with a diffusers integration merged on 2025-09-16. Future plans include local inference ComfyUI nodes, LoRA and fine-tuning scripts, and additional technical documentation.

instruction: I need to remove some distracting elements from this background. Can you assist?
description: This repository contains Nunchaku-quantized versions of an image-editing model based on advances in complex text rendering. The model is optimized for efficient inference while maintaining minimal loss in performance. It is an image-to-image model developed by the Nunchaku Team and is licensed under the Apache-2.0 license. The model is quantized from an unspecified base model. The data type for the model is INT4 for non-Blackwell GPUs (pre-50-series) and NVFP4 for Blackwell GPUs (50-series). Two rank configurations are available: r32 for faster inference and r128 for better quality but slower inference. Standard inference speed models are provided for general use. Additionally, 4-step distilled models are available, fused with unspecified components using LoRA strength = 1.0. These include models with data types INT4 and NVFP4, and ranks r32 and r128. Similarly, 8-step distilled models are available, also fused with unspecified components using LoRA strength = 1.0. The model sources include an unspecified inference engine, quantization library, paper, and demo. The model is described in the paper titled 'SVDQuant: Absorbing Outliers by Low-Rank Components for 4-Bit Diffusion Models,' presented at The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations in 2025.

instruction: Based on this image, can you estimate the probability of a poor prognosis?
description: Digepath is a self-supervised foundation model for intelligent gastrointestinal pathology image analysis. The model is a Vision Transformer Large/16 with DINO-V2 self-supervised pre-training on 353 million multi-scale images from 210,043 H&E-stained gastrointestinal related slides. Gastrointestinal (GI) diseases represent a clinically significant burden, necessitating precise diagnostic approaches to optimize patient outcomes. Conventional histopathological diagnosis suffers from limited reproducibility and diagnostic variability. To overcome these limitations, Digepath was developed as a specialized foundation model for GI pathology. The framework introduces a dual-phase iterative optimization strategy combining pretraining with fine-screening, specifically designed to address the detection of sparsely distributed lesion areas in whole-slide images. Digepath was initially pretrained on a large-scale dataset comprising over 353 million multi-scale images derived from 210,043 H&E-stained slides of GI diseases. It was subsequently fine-tuned on 471,443 carefully selected regions of interest (ROIs) in the second stage. It attains state-of-the-art performance on 32 out of 33 tasks related to GI pathology, including pathological diagnosis, protein expression status prediction, gene mutation prediction, and prognosis evaluation. Digepath demonstrates broad applicability across diverse clinical tasks, highlighting its potential for reliable deployment in real-world pathology workflows.

instruction: Generate a 5-second video from this prompt: 'A child flying a kite in a meadow with butterflies around.'
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 includes a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, each with about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also features a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and running on consumer-grade graphics cards like 4090. The model supports both 480P and 720P resolutions and is built with a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for more stable video synthesis with reduced unrealistic camera movements and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The model is available in multiple versions, including T2V-A14B for text-to-video, I2V-A14B for image-to-video, and TI2V-5B for high-compression text-image-to-video. It is open-sourced under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards. The model has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and includes multi-GPU and single-GPU inference options, as well as support for prompt extension and negative prompts. Computational efficiency tests show that the model can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, making it one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model is evaluated on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, demonstrating superior performance compared to leading closed-source commercial models.

instruction: I need a clear and well-lit face image analyzed for age and gender. Can you do that?
description: A state-of-the-art Vision Transformer model for simultaneous age estimation and gender classification, achieving 94.3% gender accuracy and 4.5 years age MAE on the UTKFace dataset. The model is designed to predict age and gender from facial images and supports multiple input types, including file paths, URLs, PIL Images, and NumPy arrays. It features a dual-head design with age regression and gender classification, based on the ViT-Base architecture. The model was trained on the UTKFace dataset, which contains 23,687 images, using 15 epochs with the AdamW optimizer and a learning rate of 2e-5. It has 86.8 million parameters and achieves an inference speed of approximately 50 milliseconds per image on a CPU. The model provides detailed output, including predicted age, gender, confidence scores, and probability distributions for male and female classifications. It is optimized for adults aged 16 to 60 years, with reduced accuracy for children under 13 and seniors over 60. For best results, it is recommended to use clear, well-lit, front-facing images. The model includes helper functions to return proper age and gender values, and it is advised to avoid using the standard pipeline approach, which may return raw label outputs such as LABEL_0 or LABEL_1. The model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is available for commercial use with proper attribution.

instruction: I need to know if there are any visible signs of damage in this building image. Can you help?
description: YOLOS is a Vision Transformer (ViT) trained using the DETR loss. It is designed for object detection and is capable of achieving 42 AP on COCO validation 2017, similar to DETR and more complex frameworks such as Faster R-CNN. The model is trained using a 'bipartite matching loss,' where the predicted classes and bounding boxes of each of the N = 100 object queries are compared to the ground truth annotations, padded up to the same length N. The Hungarian matching algorithm is used to create an optimal one-to-one mapping between each of the N queries and each of the N annotations. Standard cross-entropy is used for the classes, and a linear combination of the L1 and generalized IoU loss is used for the bounding boxes to optimize the parameters of the model. The YOLOS model was pre-trained for 300 epochs on ImageNet-1k and fine-tuned for 300 epochs on COCO, a dataset consisting of 118k/5k annotated images for training/validation respectively. This model achieves an AP (average precision) of 28.7 on COCO 2017 validation. The model is intended for use in object detection tasks and supports PyTorch for both the feature extractor and model. The team releasing YOLOS did not write a model card for this model, so this model card has been written by the Hugging Face team.

instruction: What is the most probable jurisdiction associated with this legal text?
description: GEM-ModernBERT HQ Legal is a ModernBERT-base model pre-trained from scratch on a 21GB corpus of Greek legal, parliamentary, and governmental text. The model incorporates ModernBERT's architectural innovations, including Flash Attention 2, StableAdamW optimizer, 1024-token context length, and advanced memory optimization techniques. It is designed for superior performance on Greek legal document understanding tasks. The model uses a quality-based data repetition strategy, with 30% masking probability, trapezoidal learning rate scheduling, and optimized batch sizing to enhance convergence and performance. It supports extended 1024-token context windows for handling longer legal documents while maintaining computational efficiency. The model is optimized for downstream tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER), Text Classification, and Question Answering in the legal domain. The training data includes a 21.12GB corpus, expanded from an original 16.75GB through strategic repetition of higher-quality legal content. The model uses 1024-token sequences to process complete legal articles, full court decisions, complex legislative texts, and parliamentary debates. The model architecture includes a hidden size of 768, 12 attention heads, 12 hidden layers, and approximately 139 million parameters. It has a vocabulary size of 50,373 tokens and supports 1024 max position embeddings. The model was pre-trained for 150,000 steps on 8x NVIDIA H100 80GB GPUs using BFloat16 mixed-precision. Training took 97 hours and 9 minutes to complete. Key training optimizations include a per-device batch size of 16, gradient accumulation steps of 8, and an effective batch size of 1,024. The StableAdamW optimizer was configured with a learning rate of 0.0002, weight decay of 0.1, and trapezoidal learning rate scheduling with 9,000 warmup steps and square-root decay. The model achieved a final training loss of 0.7648 and a final evaluation loss of 0.7751. Training used a 90%/10% train/validation split and 21.12GB of effective training data. Advanced training infrastructure included Flash Attention 2 optimizations, memory optimization settings, and distributed training with NCCL backend and BFloat16 mixed precision. The model supports downstream tasks with extended context and is compatible with the ModernBERT tokenizer.

instruction: I need to know if this image is real or if it was created using digital tools.
description: AIRealNet is a binary image classifier designed to distinguish AI-generated images from real human photographs. It is optimized for detecting conventional AI-generated content while adhering to strict privacy standards, avoiding the use of personal or sensitive images. The model leverages the SwinV2 Tiny architecture as its backbone, achieving high accuracy while maintaining a lightweight design suitable for practical deployment. AIRealNet was fine-tuned on a 14k-image dataset from the `Parveshiiii/AI-vs-Real` repository, which is balanced between AI-generated and real images (60% AI-generated, 40% real human images). The training data was sourced from public datasets and AI generation tools, with intentional exclusion of edited personal photos. The model demonstrates high accuracy and robustness on public datasets, though it has limitations in detecting subtle edits, such as nano-scale modifications, and cannot identify AI-modified real-person images due to privacy and ethical guidelines. Performance may also vary on images from unseen AI generators or unconventional content. AIRealNet is intended for use in detecting AI-generated imagery on social media, research publications, and digital media platforms, assisting content moderators, researchers, and fact-checkers in identifying synthetic media. It is not intended for legal verification without human corroboration. The model preprocesses images to 256x256 resolution, extracts features using the SwinV2 Tiny vision transformer, and outputs binary classification probabilities for AI-generated versus real human images. Ethical considerations include a privacy-first approach, responsible deployment with human review, and transparency in communicating limitations and dataset design. Future work aims to improve detection of subtle AI-generated edits, expand training data with diverse AI generators, and explore multi-modal detection capabilities.

instruction: Write a dialogue between two characters who are debating the ethics of artificial intelligence.
description: gemma-3n-E4B-it is a 6-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or safety of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or suitability for any purpose. Users are solely responsible for any damage or issues arising from the use of the model.

instruction: Please look up with the camera to show the height of the mountain in the background.
description: This model is trained on ModelScope without the use of code. It is designed to support camera control functions, including moving the camera up, down, left, and right, rotating it left and right, looking down or up, and switching to a wide-angle or close-up shot.
