instruction: What objects are visible in this photo of a park with people and animals?
description: YOLOWorld is an SDK that enables efficient open-vocabulary object detection using YOLO-Worldv2 Large. It is optimized for Axera’s NPU-based SoC platforms, including the AX650 Series, AX630C Series, and AX8850 Series, as well as Axera's dedicated AI accelerator. The model supports inference on both host systems and M.2 accelerator cards, such as those used with Raspberry PI 5. It includes two main models: one for object detection (yolo_u16_ax650.axmodel and yolo_u16_ax630c.axmodel) and one for text encoding (clip_b1_u16_ax650.axmodel and clip_b1_u16_ax630c.axmodel). On the AX650 platform, the object detection model has an input shape of 1 x 640 x 640 x 3 and a latency of 9.522 ms, with a CMM usage of 21 MB, while the text encoder has an input shape of 1 x 77 and a latency of 2.997 ms, with a CMM usage of 137 MB. On the AX630C platform, the object detection model has a latency of 43.450 ms and a CMM usage of 31 MB, and the text encoder has a latency of 10.703 ms and a CMM usage of 134 MB. The model is designed to accept text inputs for object detection, such as 'person', 'dog', 'car', 'horse', 'man', 'shoes', 'ball', and others, and it processes images for inference. The SDK includes a Python interface with example scripts for running inference, and it requires specific dependencies installed via pip. The model is deployed using precompiled shared libraries and model files, and it supports both host and AXCL device types for execution. The system outputs detection results in multiple feature maps with varying spatial resolutions. The model is intended for use with Axera hardware and may not be compatible with other platforms.

instruction: How do you generate 3D Gaussians to represent the shape of an object in a scene?
description: HunyuanWorld-Mirror is a versatile feed-forward model for comprehensive 3D geometric prediction. It integrates diverse geometric priors, including camera poses, calibrated intrinsics, and depth maps, and simultaneously generates various 3D representations, such as point clouds, multi-view depths, camera parameters, surface normals, and 3D Gaussians, in a single forward pass. The model consists of two key components: Multi-Modal Prior Prompting, which embeds diverse prior modalities into the feed-forward model using lightweight encoding layers to convert each modality into structured tokens, and Universal Geometric Prediction, a unified architecture capable of handling the full spectrum of 3D reconstruction tasks, including camera and depth estimation, point map regression, surface normal estimation, and novel view synthesis.

instruction: Can you read the text in this image and also understand the spoken words in the audio?
description: Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is a lightweight open multimodal foundation model that leverages the language, vision, and speech research and datasets used for Phi-3.5 and 4.0 models. The model processes text, image, and audio inputs, generating text outputs, and comes with 128K token context length. The model underwent an enhancement process, incorporating both supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to support precise instruction adherence and safety measures. The languages that each modal supports are the following: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model is intended for broad multilingual and multimodal commercial and research use. The model provides uses for general purpose AI systems and applications which require memory/compute constrained environments, latency bound scenarios, strong reasoning (especially math and logic), function and tool calling, general image understanding, optical character recognition, chart and table understanding, multiple image comparison, multi-image or video clip summarization, speech recognition, speech translation, speech QA, and audio understanding. The model is designed to accelerate research on language and multimodal models, for use as a building block for generative AI powered features. The model is not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Developers should consider common limitations of language models and multimodal models, as well as performance difference across languages, as they select use cases, and evaluate and mitigate for accuracy, safety, and fairness before using within a specific downstream use case, particularly for high-risk scenarios. Developers should be aware of and adhere to applicable laws or regulations (including but not limited to privacy, trade compliance laws, etc.) that are relevant to their use case. This release of Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is based on valuable user feedback from the Phi-3 series. Previously, users could use a speech recognition model to talk to the Mini and Vision models. To achieve this, users needed to use a pipeline of two models: one model to transcribe the audio to text, and another model for the language or vision tasks. This pipeline means that the core model was not provided the full breadth of input information – e.g. cannot directly observe multiple speakers, background noises, jointly align speech, vision, language information at the same time on the same representation space. With Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, a single new open model has been trained across text, vision, and audio, meaning that all inputs and outputs are processed by the same neural network. The model employed new architecture, larger vocabulary for efficiency, multilingual, and multimodal support, and better post-training techniques were used for instruction following and function calling, as well as additional data leading to substantial gains on key multimodal capabilities. It is anticipated that Phi-4-multimodal-instruct will greatly benefit app developers and various use cases. The enthusiastic support for the Phi-4 series is greatly appreciated. Feedback on Phi-4 is welcomed and crucial to the model's evolution and improvement. The Phi-4-multimodal-instruct was observed as having strong automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation (ST) performance, surpassing expert ASR model WhisperV3 and ST models SeamlessM4T-v2-Large. It ranked number 1 on the leaderboard with word error rate 6.14% in comparison with the current best model 6.5% as of March 04, 2025. It is the first open-sourced model that can perform speech summarization, and the performance is close to GPT4o. It has a gap with close models, e.g. Gemini-1.5-Flash and GPT-4o-realtime-preview, on speech QA task. Work is being undertaken to improve this capability in the next iterations. Phi-4-multimodal-instruct is capable of processing both image and audio together, and achieves much stronger performance on multiple benchmarks compared to other existing state-of-the-art omni models that can enable audio and visual signal as input. The model has 5.6B parameters and is a multimodal transformer model. The model has the pretrained Phi-4-Mini-Instruct as the backbone language model, and the advanced encoders and adapters of vision and speech. The model's inputs are text, image, and audio. It is best suited for prompts using the chat format. The model's context length is 128K tokens. The model was trained on 512 A100-80G GPUs for 28 days. The training data includes 5 trillion text tokens, 2.3M speech hours, and 1.1T image-text tokens. The model's outputs are generated text in response to the input. The model was trained between December 2024 and January 2025. The model is a static model trained on offline datasets with the cutoff date of June 2024 for publicly available data. The model supports the following languages: Text: Arabic, Chinese, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Finnish, French, German, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Swedish, Thai, Turkish, Ukrainian; Vision: English; Audio: English, Chinese, German, French, Italian, Japanese, Spanish, Portuguese. The model's release date is February 2025. The model's training data includes a wide variety of sources, totaling 5 trillion text tokens, and is a combination of publicly available documents filtered for quality, selected high-quality educational data, and code; newly created synthetic, “textbook-like” data for the purpose of teaching math, coding, common sense reasoning, general knowledge of the world (e.g., science, daily activities, theory of mind, etc.); high quality human labeled data in chat format; selected high-quality image-text interleave data; synthetic and publicly available image, multi-image, and video data; anonymized in-house speech-text pair data with strong/weak transcriptions; selected high-quality publicly available and anonymized in-house speech data with task-specific supervisions; selected synthetic speech data; and synthetic vision-speech data. Focus was placed on the quality of data that could potentially improve the reasoning ability for the model, and the publicly available documents were filtered to contain a preferred level of knowledge. As an example, the result of a game in premier league on a particular day might be good training data for large foundation models, but such information was removed for the Phi-4-multimodal-instruct to leave more model capacity for reasoning for the model's small size. The data collection process involved sourcing information from publicly available documents, with a focus on filtering out undesirable documents and images. To safeguard privacy, image and text data sources were filtered to remove or scrub potentially personal data from the training data. The decontamination process involved normalizing and tokenizing the dataset, then generating and comparing n-grams between the target dataset and benchmark datasets. Samples with matching n-grams above a threshold were flagged as contaminated and removed from the dataset. A detailed contamination report was generated, summarizing the matched text, matching ratio, and filtered results for further analysis. Like other language models, the Phi family of models can potentially behave in ways that are unfair, unreliable, or offensive. Some of the limiting behaviors to be aware of include: Quality of Service: The Phi models are trained primarily on English language content across text, speech, and visual inputs, with some additional multilingual coverage. Performance may vary significantly across different modalities and languages: Text: Languages other than English will experience reduced performance, with varying levels of degradation across different non-English languages. English language varieties with less representation in the training data may perform worse than standard American English. Speech: Speech recognition and processing shows similar language-based performance patterns, with optimal performance for standard American English accents and pronunciations. Other English accents, dialects, and non-English languages may experience lower recognition accuracy and response quality. Background noise, audio quality, and speaking speed can further impact performance. Vision: Visual processing capabilities may be influenced by cultural and geographical biases in the training data. The model may show reduced performance when analyzing images containing text in non-English languages or visual elements more commonly found in non-Western contexts. Image quality, lighting conditions, and composition can also affect processing accuracy. Multilingual performance and safety gaps: We believe it is important to make language models more widely available across different languages, but the Phi 4 models still exhibit challenges common across multilingual releases. As with any deployment of LLMs, developers will be better positioned to test for performance or safety gaps for their linguistic and cultural context and customize the model with additional fine-tuning and appropriate safeguards. Representation of Harms & Perpetuation of Stereotypes: These models can over- or under-represent groups of people, erase representation of some groups, or reinforce demeaning or negative stereotypes. Despite safety post-training, these limitations may still be present due to differing levels of representation of different groups, cultural contexts, or prevalence of examples of negative stereotypes in training data that reflect real-world patterns and societal biases. Inappropriate or Offensive Content: These models may produce other types of inappropriate or offensive content, which may make it inappropriate to deploy for sensitive contexts without additional mitigations that are specific to the case. Information Reliability: Language models can generate nonsensical content or fabricate content that might sound reasonable but is inaccurate or outdated. Limited Scope for Code: The majority of Phi 4 training data is based in Python and uses common packages such as

instruction: I want to visualize a building from all sides—can you help me build a 3D model from photos?
description: AnySplat is a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting model designed to process input from unconstrained views. It is intended to reconstruct 3D scenes from a set of 2D images by inferring camera poses and generating Gaussian representations of the scene. The model supports inference tasks, including the estimation of extrinsic and intrinsic camera parameters and the generation of interpolated video outputs based on the reconstructed 3D data. The architecture is implemented in Python using PyTorch and is designed to be used in a pipeline that includes image preprocessing, model inference, and post-processing for video output. The model is trained to handle multiple input images, which are processed and stacked into a tensor for inference. During inference, the model outputs Gaussian representations of the scene along with predicted camera poses. The model is evaluated using standard practices in 3D reconstruction and is intended for use in applications requiring 3D scene understanding from arbitrary camera views. The model is provided with a license, and users are encouraged to cite the associated research paper for any use of the model or its outputs.

instruction: What is the address and voter ID number on the front of this document?
description: The Indian ID Validator is a computer vision pipeline designed for classifying, detecting, and extracting text from Indian identification documents, including Aadhaar, PAN Card, Passport, Voter ID, and Driving License. The system is powered by YOLO11 models and PaddleOCR, and it supports both front and back images for Aadhaar and Driving License. The pipeline performs three main tasks: classifying the type of ID document using the Id_Classifier model, detecting specific fields on the document using type-specific YOLO11 detection models, and extracting text from the detected fields via PaddleOCR with preprocessing steps such as upscaling, denoising, and contrast enhancement. The supported ID types include Aadhaar (front and back), PAN Card (front), Passport (front), Voter ID (front and back), and Driving License (front and back). The pipeline includes the following models: Id_Classifier, which classifies the type of Indian ID document with a Top-1 accuracy of 0.995 and Top-5 accuracy of 1.0; Aadhaar, which detects fields such as Aadhaar Number, DOB, Gender, Name, and Address, with mAP50 of 0.795, mAP50-95 of 0.553, Precision of 0.777, Recall of 0.774, and Fitness of 0.577; Driving_License, which detects fields such as Address, Blood Group, DL No, DOB, Name, Relation With, RTO, State, and Vehicle Type, with mAP50 of 0.690, mAP50-95 of 0.524, Precision of 0.752, and Recall of 0.669; Pan_Card, which detects fields such as PAN, Name, Father's Name, DOB, and Pan Card, with mAP50 of 0.924, mAP50-95 of 0.686, Precision of 0.902, and Recall of 0.901; Passport, which detects fields such as Address, Code, DOB, DOI, EXP, Gender, MRZ1, MRZ2, Name, Nationality, Nation, and POI, with mAP50 of 0.987, mAP50-95 of 0.851, Precision of 0.972, and Recall of 0.967; and Voter_Id, which detects fields such as Address, Age, DOB, Card Voter ID 1 Back, Card Voter ID 2 Front, Card Voter ID 2 Back, Card Voter ID 1 Front, Date of Issue, Election, Father, Gender, Name, Point, Portrait, Symbol, and Voter ID, with mAP50 of 0.917, mAP50-95 of 0.772, Precision of 0.922, and Recall of 0.873. The models are available in various formats such as PyTorch, ONNX, and TensorRT for deployment in different environments. The system can be used via a Python API or command-line interface, and it provides end-to-end processing for classification, detection, and OCR. The model is released under the MIT License.

instruction: Add a cat sitting beside the person in this video and make sure it blends into the scene naturally.
description: Lucy Edit 1.1 Dev is an open-weight video editing model that performs instruction-guided edits on videos using free-text prompts. It supports a variety of edits, such as clothing and accessory changes, character changes, object insertions, and scene replacements, while preserving the motion and composition of the original video. Built on the Wan2.2 5B architecture, it inherits a high-compression VAE and DiT stack, making it easier to adapt existing scripts and workflows. The model preserves motion and composition perfectly, allowing for precise edits. It offers improved edit reliability compared to common inference time methods. It supports wardrobe and accessory changes, such as changing outfits and adding items like glasses, earrings, and hats. It can also perform character changes, replacing characters with monsters, animals, or known characters. It supports scene replacements, such as transforming a scene into a 2D cartoon. It accepts pure text instructions without requiring finetuning or masks for common edits. It can perform global edits to the background and style, maintaining subject identity. It provides seamless blending with cleaner boundaries and more coherent lighting across edits. It supports the insertion of general objects with improved spatial grounding. The model has approximately 5 billion parameters and is built on top of Wan2.2 5B. Version 1.1 includes better overall quality, higher fidelity details, stronger prompt adherence, and improved identity preservation. It offers more reliable global edits with better subject preservation and control. It provides better blending and seamless edits with cleaner boundaries, fewer artifacts, and improved color and lighting consistency across frames. It supports the addition of general objects, including wearable, handheld, and general scene objects, with improved spatial grounding, occlusion, and shadows. Lucy Edit 1.1 is designed for precise, realistic, and identity-preserving video edits. Prompts with 20–30 descriptive words work best, and using the right trigger words helps the model understand the intent. Supported edit types include clothing changes, human or character replacement, object replacement, color changes, adding objects, and global transformations. The model excels at identity conservation, edit precision, realism, and prompt adherence. Longer prompts describing style, appearance, and context improve results. Frame counts of 81 produce better temporal consistency than shorter clips. Version 1.1 improves boundary blending and temporal coherence across edits. The model is available via a hosted API and is compatible with the Diffusers pipeline. Future integrations include ComfyUI custom nodes and local inference nodes. The model is based on the Wan2.2 5B architecture and acknowledges contributions from the open-source community, including diffusers and Hugging Face.

instruction: Could you turn this image of a room into a 3D model for interior planning?
description: TripoSR is a fast and feed-forward 3D generative model developed in collaboration between Stability AI and Tripo AI. The model incorporates a series of technical advancements over the LRM model in terms of both data curation as well as model and training improvements. We closely follow the network architecture for the model design. The model type is feed-forward 3D reconstruction from a single image, and it is available under the MIT license. We train TripoSR for 5 days on 22 GPU nodes each with 8 A100 40GB GPUs. We use renders from the dataset, utilizing our enhanced rendering method that more closely replicate the distribution of images found in the real world, significantly improving our model’s ability to generalize. We selected a carefully curated subset of the Objaverse dataset for the training data, which is available under the CC-BY license. The model should not be used to intentionally create or disseminate 3D models that people would foreseeably find disturbing, distressing, or offensive; or content that propagates historical or current stereotypes.

instruction: Could you help me identify if this scanned receipt is rotated or not?
description: The text line orientation classification module is designed to distinguish the orientation of text lines and correct them using post-processing. In scenarios such as document scanning and license or certificate photography, the capture device may be rotated, resulting in text lines in various orientations. Standard OCR pipelines are not well-suited to handle such data. By utilizing image classification technology, the orientation of text lines can be predetermined and adjusted, thereby enhancing the accuracy of OCR processing. The PP-LCNet_x1_0_textline_ori is a text line classification model based on PP-LCNet_x0_25, with two classes: 0 degrees and 180 degrees. It achieves an average recognition accuracy of 98.85 percent and has a model storage size of 0.96 megabytes.

instruction: Is it possible to detect a person's arm movement patterns in a video for a gesture-based game?
description: The Person Pose Detection 480x640 model, developed by Synaptics, is a lightweight quantized tflite model designed for the SR100 processor in the Synaptics Astra™ SR MCU Series. It is intended to generate bounding boxes and identify 17 keypoints for each person, corresponding to various body parts. Each keypoint is accompanied by a confidence score that indicates the reliability of the detection, enabling accurate estimation of the person's pose. The model is designed to fit in Flash and is also intended to fit in SRAM. It is a pose detection model with an input size of 480x640 and outputs, for each detected person, bounding box coordinates, 17 keypoint locations, and confidence scores. The model is designed for a single class (person) and is specifically intended for pose estimation of people. It is optimized for deployment on the Astra™ SR100 MCU, with INT8 quantization and preprocessing that requires input images to be resized and quantized to match model requirements. The model is intended for real-time person pose estimation on embedded, resource-constrained edge devices. Typical use cases include human activity recognition and gesture control, fitness and wellness tracking, smart home automation based on body posture, and interactive applications and user interfaces. The model can be evaluated and tested in a hosted environment optimized for the Synaptics SR110 MCU, which provides a seamless sandbox for model evaluation using hardware-specific quantization and runtime settings. The model is distributed under a license that allows flexible use, modification, and distribution.

instruction: I want to create a fun, animated version of a speech—can you help with that?
description: SoulX-FlashTalk is a real-time, infinite streaming model for audio-driven avatars, developed using self-correcting bidirectional distillation. It is designed to generate continuous, real-time animations of avatars based on audio input, supporting a wide range of styles including portrait, animal animation, and fast-paced rap. The model is built with a 14B parameter architecture and is optimized for deployment on high-end GPU systems, such as 8xH800 or higher, to achieve real-time inference speeds. A 4-GPU version is planned for consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. The model relies on components such as the `chinese-wav2vec2-base` for audio processing. Evaluation results and specific performance metrics are not detailed in the provided text. The model is available for download via Hugging Face, and inference requires a Conda environment with specific PyTorch and dependency versions. An online demo is coming soon. The model is based on foundational models and techniques from external sources, and the authors acknowledge these contributions. The model is described as being under active development, with a technical report and project page already released.

instruction: What might be the purpose of the activity shown in this image?
description: BLIP is a Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed to transfer flexibly to both vision-language understanding and generation tasks. It is trained on visual question answering using a large architecture with a ViT large backbone. BLIP addresses the limitations of existing pre-trained models, which typically excel in either understanding-based or generation-based tasks but not both. The model improves performance by effectively utilizing noisy web data through a process of bootstrapping captions, where a captioner generates synthetic captions and a filter removes the noisy ones. BLIP achieves state-of-the-art results on a wide range of vision-language tasks, including image-text retrieval with a 2.7% increase in average recall@1, image captioning with a 2.8% increase in CIDEr score, and visual question answering (VQA) with a 1.6% increase in VQA score. It also demonstrates strong generalization ability when directly transferred to video-language tasks in a zero-shot manner. The model can be used for conditional and un-conditional image captioning. Ethical considerations include the fact that this release is for research purposes only in support of an academic paper. The models, datasets, and code are not specifically designed or evaluated for all downstream purposes. Users are strongly recommended to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. Users should consider the common limitations of AI, comply with applicable laws, and leverage best practices when selecting use cases, particularly for high-risk scenarios where errors or misuse could significantly impact people’s lives, rights, or safety.

instruction: What are the organizations referenced in this document?
description: The bert-base-NER is a fine-tuned BERT model designed for Named Entity Recognition (NER) and achieves state-of-the-art performance for the NER task. It is trained to recognize four types of entities: location (LOC), organizations (ORG), person (PER), and Miscellaneous (MISC). Specifically, this model is a bert-base-cased model that was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset. A larger BERT-large model fine-tuned on the same dataset is also available. The model can be used with the Transformers pipeline for NER. It is limited by its training dataset of entity-annotated news articles from a specific span of time, which may not generalize well for all use cases in different domains. Additionally, the model occasionally tags subword tokens as entities, and post-processing of results may be necessary to handle those cases. The model was fine-tuned on the English version of the standard dataset, which distinguishes between the beginning and continuation of an entity to allow the model to output where the second entity begins. Each token is classified as one of the following classes: O (Outside of a named entity), B-MISC (Beginning of a miscellaneous entity right after another miscellaneous entity), I-MISC (Miscellaneous entity), B-PER (Beginning of a person’s name right after another person’s name), I-PER (Person’s name), B-ORG (Beginning of an organization right after another organization), I-ORG (Organization), B-LOC (Beginning of a location right after another location), and I-LOC (Location). The training dataset was derived from the Reuters corpus, consisting of Reuters news stories. The dataset includes 7140 training examples for LOC, 3438 for MISC, 6321 for ORG, and 6600 for PER. The development set includes 1837 LOC, 922 MISC, 1341 ORG, and 1842 PER examples. The test set includes 1668 LOC, 702 MISC, 1661 ORG, and 1617 PER examples. The training dataset consists of 946 articles, 14,987 sentences, and 203,621 tokens. The development dataset includes 216 articles, 3,466 sentences, and 51,362 tokens. The test dataset includes 231 articles, 3,684 sentences, and 46,435 tokens. The model was trained on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU with recommended hyperparameters from the source that trained and evaluated the model on the CoNLL-2003 NER task. The model achieved an f1 score of 95.1 on the development set and 91.3 on the test set. The precision was 95.0 on the development set and 90.7 on the test set. The recall was 95.3 on the development set and 91.9 on the test set. The test metrics are slightly lower than the official Google BERT results, which encoded document context and experimented with CRF.

instruction: Is it possible to track a person's pose in real-time from a mobile camera feed?
description: Movenet is a model designed for accurate human pose estimation on human images. It is optimized for mobile deployment and is intended to be used on Qualcomm® devices. The model has an input resolution of 192x192 and contains 2.33 million parameters, with a model size of 8.91 MB in float format. Movenet is implemented using PyTorch and can be compiled for on-device deployment using the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench. The model supports performance profiling, accuracy verification, and inference on cloud-hosted Qualcomm® devices. It can be deployed on Android using runtimes such as TensorFlow Lite (`.tflite`) or QNN (`.so`). The model uses the w8a16_mixed_int16 precision format and runs on the CPU. Performance metrics across various devices include inference times ranging from 9.21 ms to 57.706 ms and peak memory usage ranging from 12 MB to 41 MB. The model is part of the Qualcomm® AI Hub and can be accessed through the QAI Hub Models package. A demo is available to run the model on sample input data, with options to run locally or on a cloud-hosted device. The model can be compiled for on-device deployment by tracing the PyTorch model and submitting a compile job. Performance profiling and on-device inference require access to the Qualcomm® AI Hub Workbench. The model's original implementation is licensed separately, and more information about the license can be found in the provided link. Users can join the Qualcomm® community for collaboration and support.

instruction: Is there a way to estimate how far away the trees are from the camera in this picture?
description: Depth Anything 3 (DA3) is a model that predicts spatially consistent geometry from an arbitrary number of visual inputs, with or without known camera poses. It is designed for multi-view depth estimation and camera pose estimation, using a compact foundation model with a unified depth-ray representation. The model is based on a single plain transformer, such as a vanilla DINO encoder, which serves as a backbone without architectural specialization. DA3 uses a singular depth-ray prediction target, eliminating the need for complex multi-task learning. It is trained using a teacher-student training paradigm and achieves a level of detail and generalization comparable to Depth Anything 2 (DA2). The model sets a new state-of-the-art across all tasks on a new visual geometry benchmark, surpassing prior SOTA VGGT by an average of 44.3% in camera pose accuracy and 25.1% in geometric accuracy. It also outperforms DA2 in monocular depth estimation. DA3 supports relative depth estimation, pose estimation, and pose conditioning. The model is developed by ByteDance Seed Team and is based on a vision transformer for visual geometry. It is trained exclusively on public academic datasets and may have limitations on certain domain-specific images. Performance may vary depending on image quality, lighting conditions, and scene complexity.

instruction: Can you describe the improvements in safety and content filtering compared to earlier versions?
description: Gemma 3n is a family of lightweight, state-of-the-art open models developed by Google, built using the same research and technology as the Gemini models. These models are designed for efficient execution on low-resource devices and are capable of handling multimodal input, including text, image, video, and audio, to generate text outputs. They are available in pre-trained and instruction-tuned variants and were trained on data in over 140 spoken languages. Gemma 3n models use selective parameter activation technology to reduce resource requirements, allowing them to operate at an effective size of 2B and 4B parameters, even though the raw parameter count of the model is 8B. This is achieved by offloading low-utilization matrices from the accelerator, resulting in a memory footprint comparable to a traditional 4B model. The models also use a MatFormer architecture that allows nesting sub-models within the E4B model. One sub-model is provided, or users can access a spectrum of custom-sized models. The models support a total input context of 32K tokens and can generate outputs up to 32K tokens in length. They are trained on a dataset totaling approximately 11 trillion tokens, with a knowledge cutoff date of June 2024. The training data includes web documents, code, mathematics, images, and audio, ensuring a broad range of linguistic styles, topics, and formats. Rigorous data filtering methods were applied, including CSAM filtering, sensitive data filtering, and additional content quality and safety filtering. Gemma 3n models were trained using TPUv4p, TPUv5p, and TPUv5e hardware, leveraging JAX and ML Pathways software for efficient training. Evaluation results show strong performance across a variety of benchmarks, including reasoning, factuality, multilingual tasks, STEM and code, and additional metrics. The models demonstrated safe levels of performance in child safety, content safety, and representational harms evaluations, with minimal policy violations and significant improvements over previous Gemma models. However, the models have limitations, including potential biases from training data, challenges with complex or open-ended tasks, difficulties with language ambiguity and nuance, and the risk of generating incorrect or outdated factual statements. Ethical considerations include the potential for bias, misinformation, misuse, and privacy violations. Developers are encouraged to implement content safety safeguards, adhere to privacy regulations, and use the models responsibly. The models are intended for a wide range of applications, including content creation, research, education, and data extraction. They offer superior performance compared to other similarly sized open models, making them a high-performance option for responsible AI development.

instruction: What platform is expected for a 3 PM train heading towards Woburn from North Station?
description: This model predicts which MBTA commuter rail track or platform a train will use. It is a small tabular neural-network ensemble trained on historical track assignments. The model performs multi-class classification across 13 track classes. It is implemented using Keras with a TensorFlow backend and consists of six diverse dense neural networks with embeddings and cyclical time features. At inference, the softmax outputs of the six models are averaged to produce the final prediction. The model accepts several inputs, including categorical features such as `station_id`, `route_id`, and `direction_id`, as well as cyclical time features like `hour_sin`, `hour_cos`, `minute_sin`, `minute_cos`, `day_sin`, and `day_cos`, and a continuous feature `scheduled_timestamp` (normalized in-model). The output is a probability distribution over the 13 track labels. The model is licensed under the MIT license. The ensemble includes six individual models, each saved as a `.keras` file, and best checkpoints from training are also available. Training metrics include an average validation loss of 1.2251 and an average validation accuracy of 0.5957, with individual model accuracies ranging from 0.5812 to 0.6049. The ensemble accuracy has a standard deviation of 0.0087. The training dataset contains 24,832 records, with 310 training steps and 77 validation steps per epoch. The model expects integer indices for `station_id` and `route_id`, and consistent vocabulary mappings are required for accurate inference. The training pipeline uses a dataset loader to define the exact feature mapping, and users must ensure the same vocabulary is used during inference. The model is intended for assisting real-time track/platform assignment predictions for MBTA commuter rail but is not a safety system and should not replace official dispatch or operations. It is sensitive to concept drift and unseen stations or routes. Consistent categorical index mapping between training and inference is required for reliable performance.

instruction: How can I find the posture of a person in this image?
description: YOLO26-N-pose is a variant of the Ultralytics YOLO26 model, specifically optimized for human keypoint detection. It is the latest evolution in the YOLO series, designed from the ground up for edge and low-power devices. The model has an input size of 640 pixels and achieves a mAP Pose (50-95, e2e) of 57.2 and a mAP Pose (50, e2e) of 83.3. It runs at 40.3 ms on CPU (ONNX) and 1.8 ms on T4 TensorRT10, with 2.9M parameters and 7.5B FLOPs. The architecture of YOLO26 is guided by three core principles: simplicity, deployment efficiency, and training innovation. It is a native end-to-end model that produces predictions directly without the need for non-maximum suppression (NMS), which eliminates a post-processing step and makes inference faster, lighter, and easier to deploy. The end-to-end design simplifies integration, reduces latency, and improves deployment robustness across diverse environments. YOLO26 introduces the MuSGD optimizer, a hybrid of SGD and Muon inspired by Moonshot AI's Kimi K2 breakthroughs in LLM training, which provides enhanced stability and faster convergence. The model includes features such as Residual Log-Likelihood Estimation (RLE) for more accurate keypoint localization, optimized decoding for increased inference speed, DFL removal for simplified inference and broader hardware compatibility, and up to 43% faster CPU inference for edge computing. The model can be installed via pip and used with the ultralytics library for inference on images.

instruction: What are some creative ways to repurpose old electronics at home?
description: gemma-3n-E4B-it is a 4-bit quantized version of a model optimized for Apple Silicon using MLX. It is part of the Community Model Program, which highlights new and noteworthy models created by the community. The model is provided by a third party and is not created, owned, or endorsed by LM Studio. LM Studio does not guarantee the accuracy, reliability, or appropriateness of the model's outputs, and disclaims all warranties related to the model's performance, security, or availability. Users are solely responsible for any damage or issues arising from the use of the model.

instruction: Can you explain the relationship between the objects in this image and their spatial arrangement?
description: Qwen3-VL is the most powerful vision-language model in the Qwen series. It provides comprehensive upgrades in text understanding and generation, visual perception and reasoning, context length, spatial and video dynamics comprehension, and agent interaction capabilities. The model is available in Dense and MoE architectures, with Instruct and reasoning-enhanced Thinking editions for flexible deployment. Key enhancements include a Visual Agent that operates PC and mobile GUIs by recognizing elements, understanding functions, invoking tools, and completing tasks. It supports Visual Coding Boost, generating Draw.io, HTML, CSS, and JS from images and videos. Advanced Spatial Perception allows the model to judge object positions, viewpoints, and occlusions, providing stronger 2D grounding and enabling 3D grounding for spatial reasoning and embodied AI. The model supports Long Context and Video Understanding with a native 256K context length, expandable to 1M, enabling it to handle books and hours-long video with full recall and second-level indexing. Enhanced Multimodal Reasoning excels in STEM and Math, providing causal analysis and logical, evidence-based answers. Upgraded Visual Recognition includes broader, higher-quality pretraining, enabling the model to recognize a wide range of subjects such as celebrities, anime, products, landmarks, flora, and fauna. Expanded OCR supports 32 languages, with robust performance in low light, blur, and tilt, and improved handling of rare or ancient characters and jargon, as well as long-document structure parsing. The model achieves text understanding on par with pure LLMs, with seamless text–vision fusion for lossless, unified comprehension. Architectural updates include Interleaved-MRoPE, which enhances long-horizon video reasoning through full-frequency allocation over time, width, and height via robust positional embeddings. DeepStack fuses multi-level ViT features to capture fine-grained details and sharpen image–text alignment. Text–Timestamp Alignment moves beyond T-RoPE to provide precise, timestamp-grounded event localization for stronger video temporal modeling. The model is designed for use with the Hugging Face Transformers library and supports generation hyperparameters for both vision-language and text tasks. The model is intended for deployment in a variety of settings, with options for fine-tuning and customization. The model is part of a series of vision-language models, with prior versions including Qwen2.5-VL, Qwen2-VL, and Qwen-VL, each with their own technical reports and research contributions.

instruction: Can you determine if the phrase 'The weather is warm' logically follows from 'It is currently 25 degrees Celsius'?
description: This multilingual model is designed to perform natural language inference (NLI) on 100 languages, making it suitable for multilingual zero-shot classification. It is based on the mDeBERTa-v3-base model, which was pre-trained by Microsoft on 100 languages. The model was fine-tuned on the multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset and the XNLI validation dataset. The multilingual-nli-26lang-2mil7 dataset contains 2,730,000 NLI hypothesis-premise pairs in 26 languages spoken by more than 4 billion people, with 105,000 text pairs per language. The dataset was created using the latest open-source machine translation models and is based on English datasets. The languages included are: ar, bn, de, es, fa, fr, he, hi, id, it, ja, ko, mr, nl, pl, ps, pt, ru, sv, sw, ta, tr, uk, ur, vi, zh. An additional 105,000 text pairs for English were added, bringing the total to 27 languages. The dataset also includes a random set of 10% of the hypothesis-premise pairs where an English hypothesis is paired with a premise in another language, and vice versa. This mix of languages enables users to formulate a hypothesis in English for a target text in another language. The XNLI validation set consists of 2490 professionally translated texts from English to 14 other languages, totaling 37,350 texts. The XNLI training set was excluded due to quality issues with the 2018 machine translations. For evaluation, three languages—bg, el, th—were excluded from the XNLI training data and included only in the test data to assess the model's performance on languages not seen during NLI fine-tuning but pre-trained on. The total training dataset had 3,287,280 hypothesis-premise pairs. The model was trained using the Hugging Face trainer with the following hyperparameters: 3 training epochs, a learning rate of 2e-05, a per-device train batch size of 32, gradient accumulation steps of 2, a warmup ratio of 0.06, a weight decay of 0.01, and fp16 disabled. The model was evaluated on the XNLI test set in 15 languages (5010 texts per language, 75150 in total) and the English test sets of MNLI, ANLI, FEVER, LING, and WANLI. The model's performance on the XNLI test set in 15 languages achieved an average accuracy of 0.822, with individual language accuracies ranging from 0.744 to 0.871. On English datasets, the model achieved an average accuracy of 0.794. The model's speed varied by language, with the highest speed of 1887 texts per second on a ZH language and the lowest of 374 texts per second on the FEVER test set. The model is capable of cross-lingual transfer, meaning it can perform NLI on languages it has not seen during NLI fine-tuning, but performance is likely lower than for languages seen during fine-tuning. The performance on the languages bg, el, and th is an indicator of this cross-lingual transfer. The model has limitations due to the use of machine-translated data, which may reduce data quality for complex tasks like NLI. Users are advised to consult the original DeBERTa-V3 paper and literature on NLI datasets for potential biases. The model may have issues with older versions of the Hugging Face Transformers library, and using Transformers==4.13 or higher is recommended. The model currently does not support FP16.

instruction: I recorded a short clip of a lecture in English. Can you transcribe it?
description: Whisper is a pre-trained model for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech translation. Trained on 680,000 hours of labelled speech data using large-scale weak supervision, Whisper models demonstrate strong generalization capabilities across many datasets and domains without the need for fine-tuning. Whisper is a Transformer-based encoder-decoder model, also referred to as a sequence-to-sequence model. The models were trained on either English-only data or multilingual data. English-only models were trained for speech recognition, while multilingual models were trained for both speech recognition and speech translation. For speech recognition, the model predicts transcriptions in the same language as the audio. For speech translation, the model predicts transcriptions in a different language. Whisper checkpoints come in five configurations of varying model sizes. The smallest four are trained on either English-only or multilingual data, while the largest checkpoints are multilingual only. The model is used in conjunction with a WhisperProcessor to pre-process audio inputs into log-Mel spectrograms and post-process model outputs into text. The model is informed of the task to perform (transcription or translation) by passing context tokens to the decoder. These context tokens include a transcription token, a language token, a task token, and optionally a timestamp prediction token. The task token can be set to either transcribe or translate. If the tokens are forced, the model is made to predict each token at each position, allowing control over the output language and task. If un-forced, the model predicts the output language and task automatically. Whisper can be used for English to English transcription, French to French transcription, and French to English translation by setting the appropriate context tokens. The model is evaluated using datasets such as LibriSpeech, and performance is measured using metrics like word error rate (WER). Whisper Tiny, for example, achieves a WER of approximately 7.55% on the LibriSpeech test clean dataset. The model is designed to work on audio samples up to 30 seconds in duration, but can be extended to handle longer audio through a chunking algorithm. Whisper can also predict sequence-level timestamps by enabling the return_timestamps option. While the pre-trained model generalizes well, its performance can be improved through fine-tuning, especially for certain languages and tasks. The models are primarily trained and evaluated on ASR and speech translation to English tasks and show strong results in approximately 10 languages. They may exhibit additional capabilities if fine-tuned for tasks like voice activity detection, speaker classification, or speaker diarization, but have not been robustly evaluated in these areas. Users are advised to perform thorough evaluations before deployment. The models are trained on 680,000 hours of audio data collected from the internet, with 65% in English, 18% in non-English audio with English transcripts, and 17% in non-English audio with corresponding non-English transcripts. The non-English data represents 98 different languages. Performance in a given language is correlated with the amount of training data available for that language. Whisper models exhibit improved robustness to accents, background noise, technical language, and zero-shot translation from multiple languages into English. However, due to training on noisy, weakly supervised data, the models may generate hallucinations—texts that are not actually spoken in the audio. Performance is uneven across languages, with lower accuracy observed in low-resource or low-discoverability languages and across different accents and dialects. The models may also exhibit higher word error rates for speakers of different genders, races, or ages. The sequence-to-sequence architecture makes the model prone to generating repetitive texts, which can be partially mitigated by beam search and temperature scheduling. The models are not recommended for use in high-risk domains or for subjective classification tasks. The broader implications of Whisper include potential use in accessibility tools and real-time speech recognition applications. However, the release of Whisper also raises concerns about dual use, including the potential for surveillance technologies. The models may have some capabilities to recognize specific individuals, which could present safety concerns. The cost of transcription is not expected to be a limiting factor in scaling up surveillance projects.

instruction: I need to segment a person from their background in this picture — how should I proceed?
description: SAM 2 is a foundation model developed by FAIR for solving promptable visual segmentation in images and videos. It is designed to perform segmentation based on various types of input prompts, including point clicks, bounding boxes, and masks from previous predictions. The model supports both image and video segmentation tasks, enabling users to segment objects in static images or track them across video frames. SAM 2 can be used for automatic mask generation to segment all objects in an image, as well as for refining segmentation through multiple points or additional clicks. It allows for batch processing of multiple images or objects within a single image, and supports multi-object segmentation in both images and videos. For video segmentation, SAM 2 can track objects across frames and refine tracking with additional input on later frames. The model is built to handle complex scenarios, including streaming video inference and batched processing of multiple objects. It is implemented using PyTorch and integrates with the Hugging Face Transformers library for ease of use. The model's performance includes the ability to generate multiple mask predictions per input and track objects through hundreds of video frames. Users are advised to refer to the official documentation for detailed usage instructions and examples.

description: Qwen-Image-2512-Turbo-LoRA is a 4 or 8-step turbo LoRA for Qwen Image 2512, trained by Wuli Team. This LoRA matches the original model's output quality but is over 20x faster, with 2x speed improvement from CFG-distillation and additional speed gains from a reduced number of inference steps. For users in the Chinese mainland, the model can be directly tried on the team's website, where four images with 2k resolution can be generated in just 5 seconds. The model has undergone several updates: on January 8, 2026, version 3.0 was released with further improved image color and details and is already ComfyUI compatible. On January 2, 2026, version 2.0 was released with improved image color and details and is also ComfyUI compatible. On December 31, 2025, version 1.0 and its ComfyUI compatible version were released, with plans to soon release version 2.0 with enhanced performance. The model can be used with the Diffsynth-Engine, which includes an example code for creating a pipeline, loading the turbo LoRA, changing the scheduler configuration, and sampling an image. One caveat is that generated image quality may degrade when handling extremely complex details, and users can increase the value of num_inference_steps to address this issue.

description: CompassJudger-2 is a series of generalist judge models designed to overcome the narrow specialization and limited robustness of existing LLM-as-judge solutions. The model is trained using a task-driven, multi-domain data curation and synthesis strategy to enhance robustness and domain adaptability. Judgment tasks are supervised with verifiable rewards, guiding the model's intrinsic reasoning through chain-of-thought (CoT) and rejection sampling, with a refined margin policy gradient loss further enhancing performance. CompassJudger-2 achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple judge and reward benchmarks, with the 7B model demonstrating competitive accuracy with significantly larger models. The model series includes CompassJudger-2-7B-Instruct and CompassJudger-2-32B-Instruct, both fine-tuned on the Qwen2.5-Instruct series. A new benchmark, JudgerBenchV2, with 10,000 questions across 10 scenarios, uses a Mixture-of-Judgers (MoJ) consensus for more reliable ground truth. CompassJudger-2 outperforms general models, reward models, and other specialized judge models across a wide range of benchmarks, including JudgerBench V2, JudgeBench, RMB, and RewardBench. The 7B model achieves 60.52 on JudgerBench V2, 63.06 on JudgeBench, 73.90 on RMB, and 90.96 on RewardBench, with an average of 72.11. The 32B model achieves 62.21 on JudgerBench V2, 65.48 on JudgeBench, 72.98 on RMB, and 92.62 on RewardBench, with an average of 73.32. The model is designed to evaluate AI assistant responses based on helpfulness, relevance, accuracy, depth, creativity, and level of detail, providing structured output indicating the preferred response. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

description: PP-OCRv5_server_det is a text detection model from the PP-OCRv5_det series, developed by the PaddleOCR team. It is designed for high-performance applications and supports the detection of text in diverse scenarios, including handwriting, vertical, rotated, and curved text, across multiple languages such as Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, and Japanese. The model is capable of handling complex layouts, varying text sizes, and challenging backgrounds, making it suitable for practical applications like document analysis, license plate recognition, and scene text detection. The model achieves key accuracy metrics, including 0.803 for Handwritten Chinese, 0.841 for Handwritten English, 0.945 for Printed Chinese, 0.917 for Printed English, 0.815 for Traditional Chinese, 0.676 for Ancient Text, 0.772 for Japanese, 0.797 for General Scenario, 0.671 for Pinyin, 0.8 for Rotation, 0.876 for Distortion, 0.673 for Artistic Text, and an overall average of 0.827. The model can be used as part of an OCR pipeline that includes modules for document image orientation classification, text image unwarping, text line orientation classification, text detection, and text recognition. It is also part of the PP-StructureV3 pipeline for layout analysis, which includes layout detection, general OCR, document image preprocessing, table recognition, seal recognition, and formula recognition. The model is implemented using the PaddlePaddle deep learning framework and can be run on GPU or CPU devices. It outputs detection results in the form of text polygons and scores, and supports saving results in image, JSON, and Markdown formats. The model is intended for integration into projects and pipelines for real-world text detection and document analysis tasks.

description: svara-TTS is a multilingual text-to-speech (TTS) model designed for 19 languages, including 18 Indic languages and Indian English. It is built using an Orpheus-style discrete audio token approach, aiming to provide clarity, expressiveness, and low-latency performance on commodity GPUs and CPUs. The model supports lightweight emotion and style control through tags such as `` and `` and allows for simple speaker identities using the format `Language (Gender)`. It also enables zero-shot adaptation for new use cases. The model is trained on over 2000 hours of high-quality speech data from sources including SYSPIN, RASA, IndicTTS, and SPICOR, covering approximately 50 speakers across the 19 languages. The data was selected to support natural prosody, broad language coverage, and stable multilingual transfer. The model is intended for use in multilingual assistants, IVR systems, learning applications, reading aids, accessibility tools, and content localization for education, public information, and civic services. It is also suitable for research on Indic prosody, emotion control, and cross-lingual transfer. It is not intended for impersonation of individuals, deceptive content creation, or safety-critical deployments without human oversight. The model has limitations, including challenges with proper nouns and rare entities, very long sentences, varying emotion strength by language, and code-mixing that is not fully deterministic. These limitations can be improved with targeted LoRA finetuning and better preprocessing. Users are encouraged to follow applicable laws and ethical guidelines, avoid harmful uses such as impersonation or harassment, and disclose synthetic speech to end users where appropriate. The model is licensed under Apache-2.0 and is versioned as v1.0.0, marking its initial public release with support for 19 languages.

instruction: Take this audio file and produce a clear, accurate text version of the content.
description: NVIDIA NeMo Canary-Qwen-2.5B is an English speech recognition model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple English speech benchmarks. With 2.5 billion parameters and running at 418 RTFx, Canary-Qwen-2.5B supports automatic speech-to-text recognition (ASR) in English with punctuation and capitalization (PnC). The model works in two modes: as a transcription tool (ASR mode) and as an LLM (LLM mode). In ASR mode, the model is only capable of transcribing the speech into text, but does not retain any LLM-specific skills such as reasoning. In LLM mode, the model retains all of the original LLM capabilities, which can be used to post-process the transcript, e.g. summarize it or answer questions about it. In LLM mode, the model does not 'understand' the raw audio anymore - only its transcript. This model is ready for commercial use. Canary-Qwen is a Speech-Augmented Language Model (SALM) with FastConformer Encoder and Transformer Decoder. It is built using two base models: `nvidia/canary-1b-flash` and `Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B`, a linear projection, and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) applied to the LLM. The audio encoder computes audio representation that is mapped to the LLM embedding space via a linear projection, and concatenated with the embeddings of text tokens. The model is prompted with 'Transcribe the following: ', using Qwen's chat template. The model is intended for users requiring speech-to-text transcription capabilities for English speech, and/or transcript post-processing capabilities enabled by prompting the underlying LLMs. Typical use-cases: transcription, summarization, answering user questions about the transcript. The model was trained using the NVIDIA NeMo toolkit for a total of 90k steps on 32 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. LLM parameters were kept frozen. Speech encoder, projection, and LoRA parameters were trainable. The encoder's output frame rate is 80ms, or 12.5 tokens per second. The model was trained on approximately 1.3B tokens in total (this number includes the speech encoder output frames, text response tokens, prompt tokens, and chat template tokens). The tokenizer was inherited from `Qwen/Qwen3-1.7B`. The model can be trained using this and . The model is available for use in the NVIDIA NeMo toolkit, and can be used as a pre-trained checkpoint for inference or for fine-tuning on another dataset. Input to Canary-Qwen-2.5B is a batch of prompts that include audio. Input Type(s): Audio, text prompt. Input Format(s): Audio: .wav or .flac files. Text prompt string for ASR mode: 'Transcribe the following: '. Input Parameters(s): Audio: Two-Dimensional (batch, audio-samples); Text: One-Dimensional (string). Other Properties Related to Input: 16000 Hz Mono-channel Audio, Pre-Processing Not Needed. Output Type(s): Text. Output Format: Text transcript as a sequence of token IDs or a string. Output Parameters: One-Dimensional text string. Other Properties Related to Output: May Need Inverse Text Normalization. Our AI models are designed and/or optimized to run on NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems. By leveraging NVIDIA’s hardware (e.g. GPU cores) and software frameworks (e.g., CUDA libraries), the model achieves faster training and inference times compared to CPU-only solutions. Runtime Engine(s): NeMo - 2.5.0 or higher. Supported Hardware Microarchitecture Compatibility: NVIDIA Ampere, NVIDIA Blackwell, NVIDIA Jetson, NVIDIA Hopper, NVIDIA Lovelace, NVIDIA Pascal, NVIDIA Turing, NVIDIA Volta. Preferred/Supported Operating System(s): Linux, Linux 4 Tegra, Windows. Model Version(s): Canary-Qwen-2.5B. Canary-Qwen-2.5B was trained on a total of 234K hrs of publicly available speech data. The datasets include conversations, videos from the web and audiobook recordings. The majority of the training data comes from the English portion of the Granary dataset: YouTube-Commons (109.5k hours), YODAS2 (77k hours), LibriLight (13.6k hours). In addition, the following datasets were used: Librispeech 960 hours, Fisher Corpus, Switchboard-1 Dataset, WSJ-0 and WSJ-1, National Speech Corpus (Part 1, Part 6), VCTK, VoxPopuli (EN), Europarl-ASR (EN), Multilingual Librispeech (MLS EN), Mozilla Common Voice (v11.0), Mozilla Common Voice (v7.0), Mozilla Common Voice (v4.0), AMI, FLEURS. AMI was oversampled during model training to constitute about 15% of the total data observed. This skewed the model towards predicting verbatim transcripts that include conversational speech disfluencies such as repetitions. The training transcripts contained punctuation and capitalization. The ASR performance is measured with word error rate (WER), and we process the groundtruth and predicted text with version 0.1.12. WER on: Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, RTFx 418, Mean 5.63, AMI 10.18, GigaSpeech 9.41, LS Clean 1.60, LS Other 3.10, Earnings22 10.42, SPGISpech 1.90, Tedlium 2.72, Voxpopuli 5.66. The ASR predictions were generated using greedy decoding. Number of characters per minute on 48 hrs eval set (`max_new_tokens=50` following `nvidia/canary-1b-flash` evaluation): Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, 138.1. WER on at different SNR (signal to noise ratio) levels of additive white noise: Version 2.5.0, Model Canary-Qwen-2.5B, SNR 10 2.41%, SNR 5 4.08%, SNR 0 9.83%, SNR -5 30.60%. As outlined in the paper 'Towards Measuring Fairness in AI: the Casual Conversations Dataset', we assessed the Canary-Qwen-2.5B model for fairness. The model was evaluated on the CasualConversations-v1 dataset with inference done on non-overlapping 40s chunks, and the results are reported as follows: Gender Bias: Gender Male, Female, N/A, Other. Num utterances 18471, 23378, 880, 18. % WER 16.71, 13.85, 17.71, 29.46. Age Bias: Age Group (18-30), (31-45), (46-85), (1-100). Num utterances 15058, 13984, 12810, 41852. % WER 15.73, 15.3, 14.14, 15.11. (Error rates for fairness evaluation are determined by normalizing both the reference and predicted text, similar to the methods used in the evaluations found at ). The model has the following limitations: Input length. The maximum audio duration in training was 40s, and the maximum token sequence length was 1024 tokens (including prompt, audio, and response). The model may technically be able to process longer sequences, but its accuracy may be degraded. Exclusively ASR oriented capabilities. The model is not expected to preserve any of the underlying LLM's capabilities into speech modality. English-only language support. The model was trained using English data only. It may be able to spuriously transcribe other languages as the underlying encoder was pretrained using German, French, and Spanish speech in addition to English, but it's unlikely to be reliable as a multilingual model. NVIDIA believes Trustworthy AI is a shared responsibility and we have established policies and practices to enable development for a wide array of AI applications. When downloaded or used in accordance with our terms of service, developers should work with their internal model team to ensure this model meets requirements for the relevant industry and use case and addresses unforeseen product misuse. For more detailed information on ethical considerations for this model, please see the Model Card++ Explainability, Bias, Safety & Security, and Privacy Subcards. Please report security vulnerabilities or NVIDIA AI Concerns.

description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for gene and protein entity recognition in biomedical texts. It is optimized for high-precision identification of gene and protein mentions in clinical documents, research papers, and healthcare records. The model supports tasks such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is trained on the BC2GM dataset, a curated corpus of MEDLINE abstracts with manually annotated gene and protein mentions. The model uses a ModernBERT-large architecture and is implemented using the Hugging Face Transformers framework. It achieves an F1 score of 0.85, precision of 0.85, recall of 0.86, and accuracy of 0.96. The model is validated on clinical benchmarks and is production-ready for integration into clinical and research workflows. It supports the entity types B-GENE/PROTEIN and I-GENE/PROTEIN. The model is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is available for use in applications such as clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is compatible with Hugging Face's pipeline API and supports various aggregation strategies for entity prediction. The model is trained using the AdamW optimizer with learning rate scheduling and validated using cross-validation on a held-out test set. It is designed for efficient batch processing and can be tuned for performance based on hardware capabilities, including CPU and GPU configurations.

description: This is a vision model, and if applicable, mmproj files will be provided. Weighted imatrix quantizations are available. Static quantizations are also available for download. description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video models, incorporating innovations such as an effective Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video and image-to-video generation. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity without increasing computational cost. Wan2.2 includes a high-noise expert for early denoising stages and a low-noise expert for later stages, each with about 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos than its predecessor, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also features a 5B model with a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE, achieving a compression ratio of 16×16×4, supporting both text-to-video and image-to-video generation at 720P resolution with 24fps, and running on consumer-grade graphics cards like 4090. The model supports both 480P and 720P resolutions and is built with a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for more stable video synthesis with reduced unrealistic camera movements and enhanced support for diverse stylized scenes. The model is available in multiple versions, including T2V-A14B for text-to-video, I2V-A14B for image-to-video, and TI2V-5B for high-compression text-image-to-video. It is open-sourced under the Apache 2.0 License, with usage restrictions to ensure compliance with applicable laws and ethical standards. The model has been integrated into ComfyUI and Diffusers, and includes multi-GPU and single-GPU inference options, as well as support for prompt extension and negative prompts. Computational efficiency tests show that the model can generate a 5-second 720P video in under 9 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU, making it one of the fastest 720P@24fps models available. The model is evaluated on the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, demonstrating superior performance compared to leading closed-source commercial models.

instruction: Can I get a high-quality vocal separation from this audio file?
description: Mini-BS-RoFormer-V2 is a model designed for the music source separation task. It has been improved upon previous versions. The model has a total of 46.8 million parameters and uses BF16 weight precision. On the validation set of the MUSDB18HQ dataset, the model achieves an SDR score of 10.03 for overall tracks, 9.68 for bass, 10.58 for drums, 8.99 for other, and 10.86 for vocals. The model uses time dimension downsampling to significantly reduce resource consumption. The computational cost for inference on 30 seconds of audio is 8343.55 GFLOPs. The model requires the transformers library version 4.55.4, as well as the soundfile, einops, and librosa libraries to function properly. The model can separate audio into bass, drums, other, and vocal tracks. If only vocals and instrumental tracks are desired, the bass, drums, and other tracks can be merged. The model was trained using the train and test sets of the MUSDB18HQ dataset. Training was conducted for 310k steps with a constant learning rate of 5e-4 and a batch size of 16. The Muon optimizer was used for training the transformer layers, while the AdamW optimizer was used for other network layers. Key improvements in Mini-BS-RoFormer-V2 compared to previous versions include training with bf16-true precision, using the Muon optimizer for faster convergence, changing the n_fft for the STFT operation from 2048 to 4096 for input audio, switching from 62 frequency bands to 80 mel-based frequency bands, time dimension downsampling with a step size of 4, and additional code modifications.

It has been further fine-tuned using GRPO RL training to improve its performance on math equations, tables, and other challenging OCR cases. It is intended to be used via the olmOCR toolkit, which provides an efficient inference setup using VLLM to handle millions of documents at scale. This model is licensed under Apache 2.0 and is intended for research and educational use in accordance with Ai2's policies.

instruction: Create a sequence of movements for a person doing a morning stretching routine.
description: Semantic Motion Generation (SMoG) is a state-of-the-art text-to-motion synthesis model that leverages CLIP semantics and Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN)-enhanced transformers to generate realistic, diverse 3D human motions from textual descriptions. Unlike conventional approaches, SMoG replaces linear layers in transformer architectures with KANLayers, enabling adaptive nonlinear feature learning and superior motion-text alignment. It generates the sequence of SMPL body model movement for each frame. SMoG is built on MotionCLIP — a 3D motion autoencoder trained to reconstruct poses using natural language. It employs a latent space representing abstract, compressed data features non-trivially present in the input space. This approach reduces reliance on classical data labeling by using contrastive learning to distinguish similarity, identity, or difference between text-motion pairs. During training, action-text pairs are matched for similarity (positive) or dissimilarity (negative), maximizing positive pair similarity and minimizing negative pair similarity. The latent space enables semantic alignment between input text and generated motion, producing meaningful interpretations. For example, inputting 'wings' or a cultural reference could generate a wing-like arm motion without explicit training examples. The model is trained not only to reconstruct motions but also to align their latent representations with text labels in CLIP space. The KANLayer, core to Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KAN), implements a hybrid function approximation approach based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem. This theorem states that any multivariate continuous function can be represented as a superposition of univariate functions, achieved in KANLayer via linear transformations and nonlinear B-spline components. Structurally, the layer has two parallel branches: a base branch performing linear transformation with activation (default: SiLU), and a spline branch adding nonlinearity through adaptive B-splines. The spline branch operates on coefficients 'spline_weight', computed via B-spline interpolation on a fixed grid dividing the input range (e.g., [-1, 1]) into grid_size intervals. The spline order ('spline_order') determines smoothness and complexity. The 'b_splines' method implements the recursive Cox-de Boor formula for basis functions. Spline weight initialization includes noise addition (scale_noise) and least-squares solving in 'curve2coeff' to approximate initial weights. The 'enable_standalone_scale_spline' parameter allows independent scaling of spline coefficients via 'spline_scaler' for training stability. During the forward pass, inputs are processed by both branches: the base branch generates linear output via activation and matrix multiplication, while the spline branch computes nonlinear offsets via B-spline convolution with trainable weights. Outputs are summed to form the final tensor. The model supports multi-scenario motion generation (e.g., dances, sports, daily activities) from free-form text prompts. It supports both CPU and GPU inference, though GPU is significantly faster. The model is trained on the AMASS dataset, which aggregates motion capture data from academic sources to cover diverse actions—from walking to complex gestures. The dataset records 3D joint coordinates using advanced motion-tracking technologies, preserving temporal sequences where each row represents a timestamp and columns store detailed x, y, z coordinates for body joints, formatted in npz. Notably, AMASS integrates action labels, anthropometric parameters, and synchronized sensor data, enabling detailed biomechanical studies and training deep learning models (e.g., LSTMs, Transformers) for 3D mesh pose prediction. A key feature is its scalability, encompassing tens of thousands of motions. AMASS is licensed for academic research, permitting non-commercial use with attribution, while commercial applications require explicit permission. The dataset is accompanied by visualization tools and code examples, allowing researchers to utilize its potential while preserving participant anonymity. The model's performance is evaluated using several metrics, including R@1, R@5, MMD, and FCD. SMoG achieves R@1 of 0.0004158, R@5 of 0.006237, MMD of 0.020108, and FCD of 0.50885. Additional loss metrics include Vel-Loss of 0.00029719, Text Cosine of 0.27195, rcxyz of 0.00066315, rc of 0.0014857, Mixed without CLIP of 0.24461, Mixed with CLIP of 0.73316, Mixed CLIP only of 0.48855, and Image Cosine of 0.2166. The model is implemented in PyTorch and requires specific dependencies, including CLIP, SMPL, SMPL-X, PyTorch3D, and MotionCLIP. The code is distributed under a license that must be followed, and the model depends on other libraries, each with their own respective licenses. The model is trained using a combination of losses, including velocity loss, text cosine loss, rcxyz loss, rc loss, and mixed losses with and without CLIP. The model is evaluated using a script that logs metrics with wandb. The model is trained on a dataset that includes SMPL and SMPL+H model files, and the training process involves a main training loop with fine-tuning options. The model is evaluated using a script that generates metrics and logs them with wandb. The model is trained using a combination of losses, including velocity loss, text cosine loss, rcxyz loss, rc loss, and mixed losses with and without CLIP. The model is evaluated using a script that generates metrics and logs them with wandb. The model is trained using a combination of losses, including velocity loss, text cosine loss, rcxyz loss, rc loss, and mixed losses with and without CLIP. The model is evaluated using a script that generates metrics and logs them with wandb.

description: MeshCoder is a framework that converts 3D point clouds into editable Blender Python scripts, enabling programmatic reconstruction and editing of complex human-made objects. It addresses prior limitations by developing expressive APIs for modeling intricate geometries, building a large-scale dataset of 1 million object-code pairs across 41 categories, and training a multimodal LLM to generate accurate, part-segmented code from point clouds. The approach outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality, supports intuitive shape and topology editing via code modifications, and enhances 3D reasoning capabilities in LLMs.
