description: The `llama-embed-nemotron-8b` is a text embedding model developed by NVIDIA, optimized for tasks such as retrieval, reranking, semantic similarity, and classification. It is designed for multilingual and cross-lingual text retrieval and serves as a foundational component in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. The model is based on the Llama-3.1-8B architecture with a bidirectional attention mechanism and consists of 32 hidden layers and an embedding size of 4096. It was trained on a combination of public and synthetically generated datasets, with a total of 16.4 million query-passage pairs. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MTEB (Multilingual, v2) benchmark as of October 21, 2025, with a Borda rank of 1 and 39,573 Borda votes, along with a mean task performance of 69.46. The model is instruction-aware, meaning it supports custom instructions to improve performance for specific use cases. For retrieval tasks, queries must be accompanied by a task instruction in the format `Instruct: {task_instruction}\nQuery: {query}`, while documents do not require special handling. The model outputs embedding vectors of dimension 4096 for each text input and supports input sequences up to 32768 tokens in length. It is compatible with NVIDIA GPU-accelerated systems, including architectures such as Ampere, Hopper, Lovelace, Pascal, Turing, and Volta, and is optimized for use with software frameworks like TensorRT and Triton. The model is intended for non-commercial and research use only and is available on Hugging Face as of October 21, 2025. It is recommended to use the model with transformers version 4.51.0 and flash-attention for GPU processing. The model can be integrated using libraries such as Sentence Transformers or Hugging Face Transformers. NVIDIA encourages users to ensure the model meets industry and use-case requirements and to report any concerns regarding model quality, risk, or security vulnerabilities.

instruction: Which parts of the human body are described in this section on metabolic disorders?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for anatomical entity recognition, focusing on identifying and extracting anatomical structures and body parts from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. It is optimized for biomedical entity recognition and trained on a curated ANATOMY dataset, making it suitable for applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. The model supports the classification of two entity types: B-Anatomy and I-Anatomy. It is based on the e5-base-v2 architecture and performs token classification for named entity recognition. The model has achieved an F1 score of 0.88, precision of 0.88, recall of 0.89, and accuracy of 0.97. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: What chemicals are described in this part of the clinical documentation?
description: This model is a fine-tuned transformer designed for chemical entity recognition in biomedical literature. It identifies and extracts chemical compounds and substances from clinical texts, research papers, and healthcare documents. The model supports applications such as drug interaction detection, medication extraction from patient records, adverse event monitoring, literature mining for drug discovery, and biomedical knowledge graph construction. It is optimized for high precision in biomedical entity recognition and is trained on the BC4CHEMD dataset, a manually annotated corpus for chemical entity recognition from the BioCreative IV challenge. The BC4CHEMD dataset contains PubMed abstracts with chemical entities annotated according to ChEBI guidelines and is used to benchmark named entity recognition models in identifying chemical compounds, drugs, and other chemical substances in scientific literature. The model can identify and classify the following entity types: B-CHEM and I-CHEM. It achieves an F1 score of 0.95, precision of 0.94, recall of 0.95, and accuracy of 0.99. The model is based on the bge-m3 architecture and is trained using the Hugging Face Transformers framework with AdamW optimizer and learning rate scheduling. It is validated on clinical benchmarks and is compatible with the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem for easy integration. The model is particularly useful for clinical text mining, biomedical research, drug discovery, healthcare analytics, and academic research in biomedical NLP. It is licensed under the Apache License 2.0.

instruction: How would you create a 5-second video of a flower blooming in slow motion with a gentle breeze?
description: Wan2.2 is a major upgrade to foundational video generation models, incorporating innovations such as a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, cinematic-level aesthetics, complex motion generation, and efficient high-definition hybrid text-to-video (TI2V) capabilities. The MoE architecture separates the denoising process across timesteps using specialized expert models, increasing model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency. The high-noise expert handles early-stage layout, while the low-noise expert refines details in later stages. Each expert has approximately 14B parameters, resulting in a total of 27B parameters but only 14B active per step. The model is trained on significantly larger data, with +65.6% more images and +83.2% more videos compared to Wan2.1, enhancing generalization across motion, semantics, and aesthetics. It achieves top performance among open-sourced and closed-sourced models. Wan2.2 also introduces a 5B dense model, TI2V-5B, supported by a high-compression Wan2.2-VAE with a compression ratio of 16×16×4, enabling 720P@24fps video generation on consumer-grade GPUs like the 4090. This model supports both text-to-video and image-to-video tasks and is among the fastest available for 720P@24fps generation. The T2V-A14B model supports 5-second video generation at 480P and 720P resolutions. On the Wan-Bench 2.0 benchmark, the model outperforms leading commercial models across most key evaluation dimensions. The model is available in multiple versions, including T2V-A14B, I2V-A14B, and TI2V-5B, and can be run using ComfyUI or Diffusers integrations. Computational efficiency tests show performance and memory usage across different GPU configurations. The model is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License, and users are responsible for ensuring their usage complies with legal and ethical standards.

instruction: What kind of diving competition is this video most likely from?
description: V-JEPA 2 is a video understanding model developed by FAIR at Meta, extending the pretraining objectives of its predecessors to achieve state-of-the-art video understanding capabilities. It leverages large-scale data and model sizes to enhance performance. The model is based on a ViT-L architecture with a 256-pixel input size and includes a video classification head. It is pretrained on the Diving 48 dataset, which consists of diving-related video content. The model is designed to support video classification tasks, enabling the prediction of class labels for input videos. Performance is demonstrated through inference examples, where the model outputs top predicted class names along with their probabilities. The model requires specific preprocessing steps, including frame sampling and tensor conversion, to prepare input data for inference. Users are advised to install the latest version of the transformers library and use the provided code snippet to load the model and perform video classification. The model's configuration includes a frames_per_clip parameter, which defines the number of frames to sample from each video clip. The model outputs logits, which are then converted into probabilities using softmax to determine the top predicted classes. The model's training and evaluation are detailed in a technical report published by FAIR at Meta in 2025.

instruction: I’d like to see a color-coded version of this image showing each segmented object — can you do that?
description: EoMT (Encoder-only Mask Transformer) is a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture designed for high-quality and efficient image segmentation. It was introduced in the CVPR 2025 highlight paper titled 'Your ViT is Secretly an Image Segmentation Model' by Tommie Kerssies, Niccolò Cavagnero, Alexander Hermans, Narges Norouzi, Giuseppe Averta, Bastian Leibe, Gijs Dubbelman, and Daan de Geus. The key insight is that, given sufficient scale and pretraining, a plain ViT along with additional few parameters can perform segmentation without the need for task-specific decoders or pixel fusion modules. The same model backbone supports semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation with different post-processing. The original implementation is available in a repository, and the HuggingFace model page is also available.

instruction: Please analyze this article about a natural disaster and tell me which code it belongs to.
description: This is an xlm-roberta-large model that has been fine-tuned on multilingual training data in English, German, Hungarian, Spanish, and Slovakian, labeled with media codes. The model incorporates seven additional media codes: State and Local Government Administration (24), Weather and Natural Disaster (26), Fires (27), Sports and Recreation (29), Death Notices (30), Churches and Religion (31), and Other, Miscellaneous and Human Interest (99). The model is designed for text classification tasks and is used by an open-source and free natural language processing tool intended to simplify and speed up projects for comparative research. The model achieves an overall accuracy of 78.8%, with a macro average precision of 0.75, recall of 0.81, and F1-score of 0.77. The weighted average precision is 0.80, recall is 0.79, and F1-score is 0.79. Per-class metrics include precision, recall, F1-score, and support for each label. The model is gated, requiring a token parameter when loading it, and earlier versions of the Transformers package may require the use_auth_token parameter instead. Model performance can be improved by extending the training sets, and submissions of CAP-coded corpora in any domain and language are welcomed. The model uses the sentencepiece tokenizer, and to run it before transformers version 4.27, it must be installed manually. If a RuntimeError occurs when loading the model using the from_pretrained() method, adding ignore_mismatched_sizes=True should resolve the issue.

instruction: Can you locate the section about delivery timelines in this contract?
description: This model was trained from scratch on an unknown dataset. It achieves a loss of 3.7004 on the evaluation set. The model was trained using the following hyperparameters: a learning rate of 5e-05, a training batch size of 4, an evaluation batch size of 8, a seed of 42, the ADAMW_TORCH optimizer with betas of (0.9, 0.999) and epsilon of 1e-08, a cosine learning rate scheduler, 10 training epochs, and native AMP for mixed precision training. During training, the training loss decreased from 4.4903 at step 100 to 0.2683 at step 2200, while the validation loss fluctuated, reaching a minimum of 2.3696 at step 800 and a maximum of 3.7012 at step 2200. The model was developed using Transformers 4.56.1, PyTorch 2.6.0+cu124, Datasets 3.6.0, and Tokenizers 0.22.0.

instruction: Can you extract the main request from this audio and present it in a clear sentence?
description: Voxtral Small 1.0 (24B) is an enhancement of Mistral Small 3, incorporating state-of-the-art audio input capabilities while retaining best-in-class text performance. It is designed to excel at speech transcription, translation, and audio understanding. Voxtral can operate in a pure speech transcription mode to maximize performance, automatically predicting the source audio language and transcribing the text accordingly. It supports long-form context with a 32k token context length, handling audios up to 30 minutes for transcription or 40 minutes for understanding. The model supports built-in Q&A and summarization, allowing users to ask questions directly through audio and generate structured summaries without the need for separate ASR and language models. It is natively multilingual, with automatic language detection and state-of-the-art performance in widely used languages including English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Hindi, German, Dutch, and Italian. Voxtral enables function-calling straight from voice, allowing direct triggering of backend functions, workflows, or API calls based on spoken user intents. It retains the text understanding capabilities of its language model backbone, Mistral Small 3.1. The model can be used with frameworks such as vLLM and Transformers. For vLLM, it is recommended to use version 0.10.0 or higher. The model supports multiple audios per message, multiple user turns with audio, and function calling, though system prompts are not yet supported. Running Voxtral-Small-24B-2507 on GPU requires approximately 55 GB of GPU RAM in bf16 or fp16. The model can be used for audio instruct, transcription, and function calling. It supports text-only, audio-only, and multi-modal inputs, as well as batched inference. The model can be run in a server/client setting using vLLM or natively with Transformers version 4.54.0 or higher. It requires mistral-common version 1.8.1 or higher with audio dependencies installed.

instruction: Generate a 10-second video of a bustling city street with ambient city sounds.
description: LTX-2 is a DiT-based audio-video foundation model designed to generate synchronized video and audio within a single model. It integrates the core building blocks of modern video generation, with open weights and a focus on practical, local execution. The model is developed by Lightricks and is a diffusion-based audio-video foundation model that supports English. It includes several checkpoints, such as the full model in bf16, fp8, and nvfp4 quantizations, a distilled version of the full model, a LoRA version of the distilled model, and upscalers for spatial and temporal resolution enhancement. The model can be used for image-to-video generation and is supported in the Diffusers framework. It is accessible via online demos and can be run locally using the PyTorch codebase, which includes model definitions, pipelines, and training capabilities. The codebase requires Python 3.12 or higher, CUDA version 12.7 or higher, and PyTorch 2.7. For local use, it is recommended to use the built-in LTXVideo nodes in the ComfyUI Manager. The model has several limitations: it is not intended to provide factual information, may amplify societal biases, may fail to generate videos that perfectly match prompts, prompt following is influenced by prompting style, may generate inappropriate or offensive content, and audio generated without speech may be of lower quality. The base model is fully trainable, and it is easy to reproduce the LoRAs and IC-LoRAs published with the model by following the provided instructions. Training for motion, style, or likeness (sound+appearance) can take less than an hour in many settings.

instruction: Can you generate a voice sample where someone is narrating a mystery story with suspenseful pauses?
description: Higgs Audio v2 is a powerful audio foundation model pretrained on over 10 million hours of audio data and a diverse set of text data. It is designed for expressive audio generation and excels in this area despite having no post-training or fine-tuning. The model demonstrates strong performance in expressive audio generation due to its deep language and acoustic understanding. On a specific evaluation, it achieves win rates of 75.7% and 55.7% over 'gpt-4o-mini-tts' on the 'Emotions' and 'Questions' categories, respectively. It also obtains state-of-the-art performance on traditional TTS benchmarks like Seed-TTS Eval and Emotional Speech Dataset (ESD). The model demonstrates capabilities such as automatic prosody adaptation during narration, zero-shot generation of natural multi-speaker dialogues in multiple languages, melodic humming with the cloned voice, and simultaneous generation of speech and background music. Higgs Audio v2 adopts a 'generation variant' architecture. Its strong performance is driven by three key technical innovations: an automated annotation pipeline that leverages multiple ASR models, sound event classification models, and an in-house audio understanding model, which was finetuned on top of Higgs Audio v1 Understanding; a unified audio tokenizer trained from scratch that captures both semantic and acoustic features; and the DualFFN architecture, which enhances the LLM’s ability to model audio tokens with minimal computational overhead. The audio tokenizer runs at 25 frames per second while maintaining or improving audio quality compared to tokenizers with twice the bitrate. It is the first to train on 24 kHz data covering speech, music, and sound events in one unified system. It uses a simple non-diffusion encoder/decoder for fast, batch inference and achieves state-of-the-art performance in semantic and acoustic evaluations. The DualFFN architecture acts as an audio-specific expert, boosting the LLM's performance with minimal computational overhead. The implementation preserves 91% of the original LLM’s training speed with the inclusion of DualFFN, which has 2.2B parameters. The total number of parameters for Higgs Audio v2 is 3.6B (LLM) + 2.2B (Audio Dual FFN), and it has the same training / inference FLOPs as Llama-3.2-3B. Ablation studies show that the model equipped with DualFFN consistently outperforms its counterpart in terms of word error rate (WER) and speaker similarity. Higgs Audio v2 is evaluated on four benchmarks: Seed-TTS Eval, ESD, EmergentTTS-Eval, and a custom Multi-speaker Eval. On Seed-TTS Eval and ESD, it achieves a WER of 2.44 and a SIM of 67.70 on SeedTTS-Eval, and a WER of 1.78 and a SIM (emo2vec) of 86.13 on ESD. On the EmergentTTS-Eval, it achieves win rates of 75.71% and 55.71% on the 'Emotions' and 'Questions' categories, respectively. On the Multi-speaker Eval, it achieves a WER of 18.88 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 51.95 on the 'two-speaker-conversation' subset, a WER of 11.89 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 67.92 on the 'small talk (ref)' subset, and a WER of 14.65 and a mean Sim & Dis-sim of 55.28 on the 'small talk (no ref)' subset. The model is open-sourced and can be used for tasks such as text-to-speech generation, multi-speaker dialogue generation, and audio generation with specific emotional or prosodic characteristics. It is available through an open-source repository and can be installed and run using provided code snippets. The model is licensed under a specific license, which can be found in the repository.

instruction: Help me turn a rough sketch of a spaceship into a high-resolution image with realistic details.
description: ControlNet v1.1 is a neural network structure designed to control diffusion models by adding extra conditions. It is the successor model to ControlNet v1.0 and was developed by Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala. This checkpoint is a conversion into `diffusers` format and is specifically conditioned on MLSD images, which are images with annotated line segments. ControlNet is used in combination with Stable Diffusion to support additional input conditions in text-to-image generation. The model is based on a diffusion-based architecture and is primarily used in English. The license is adapted from the work of Lvmin Zhang and Maneesh Agrawala in the area of responsible AI licensing. The model was trained using a dataset that previously had issues such as duplicated grayscale human images, low-quality images, and incorrect paired prompts. These issues were resolved in the new version. The training dataset was expanded by adding 300K more images containing more than 16 straight lines, and data augmentations such as random left-right flipping were applied. The model was resumed from MLSD 1.0 and continued training for 200 GPU hours using A100 80G. ControlNet supports various conditions including canny edge detection, pixel to pixel instruction, image inpainting, multi-level line segment detection, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, image segmentation, line art generation, anime line art generation, human pose estimation, scribble-based image generation, soft edge image generation, image shuffling, and image tiling. Each condition is associated with a specific type of image input, such as monochrome images with white edges on a black background for canny edge detection, or color-coded images for surface normal estimation. The model is intended to learn task-specific conditions in an end-to-end manner, and the learning process is robust even with small training datasets.

instruction: I want to use the depth map for a VR environment—can you generate one from this image?
description: This ONNX-based DepthPro model generates high-quality depth maps with minimal overhead. Depth values are encoded such that near points are bright and far points are dark, making the output directly usable for stereo and disparity-based applications without additional inversion or preprocessing. The model is optimized for efficient inference on standard hardware. It features a depth-only ONNX export, which significantly reduces model size while preserving full depth quality. It skips field-of-view calibration, outputting raw predicted depth values without post-processing, thereby avoiding normalization artifacts and computational overhead. The output is disparity-ready and compatible with stereo/disparity workflows without conversion. The model uses FP16 weights, optimized for GPU acceleration via DirectML for faster inference. It is configured for batch size 1, as benchmarks show single-image batches deliver optimal throughput, while larger batches are slower. The model uses Opset 21 for broader runtime optimization support and includes aggressive graph optimization to simplify the model graph, reduce computation, and speed up loading. It achieves fast inference with a minimal memory footprint and rapid depth map generation. The input shape is (1, 3, 1536, 1536) in NCHW format, with an input data type of float16 and an input range of [-1.0, 1.0] (normalized RGB). The output shape is (1, 1536, 1536), with an output data type of float16 and an output range of relative depth (higher values indicate closer objects). The model requires approximately 5.2 GB of VRAM, ONNX Runtime version 1.19.0 or higher, and Python version 3.8 or higher. Benchmarks on an AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX using ONNX Runtime v1.23.0 with DirectML show a throughput of 75.7 images per minute and a model size of 1.2 GB. The model is designed without post-processing, which can cause severe artifacts such as crushed contrast and inconsistent results due to extreme outlier depth values and unpredictable normalization effects. The model is licensed under the Apple Machine Learning Research Model License, which restricts use to non-commercial scientific research and academic development. Redistribution is allowed only with the license included. Use of Apple's trademarks, logos, or name to promote derivative models is prohibited. Commercial use, product integration, or service deployment is not allowed.

instruction: Make a visual of a haunted forest with twisted trees and fog rolling in between them.
description: Qwen-Image-2512-Turbo-LoRA is a 4 or 8-step turbo LoRA for Qwen Image 2512, trained by Wuli Team. This LoRA matches the original model's output quality but is over 20x faster, with 2x speed improvement from CFG-distillation and additional speed gains from a reduced number of inference steps. For users in the Chinese mainland, the model can be directly tried on the team's website, where four images with 2k resolution can be generated in just 5 seconds. The model has undergone several updates: on January 8, 2026, version 3.0 was released with further improved image color and details and is already ComfyUI compatible. On January 2, 2026, version 2.0 was released with improved image color and details and is also ComfyUI compatible. On December 31, 2025, version 1.0 and its ComfyUI compatible version were released, with plans to soon release version 2.0 with enhanced performance. The model can be used with the Diffsynth-Engine, which includes an example code for creating a pipeline, loading the turbo LoRA, changing the scheduler configuration, and sampling an image. One caveat is that generated image quality may degrade when handling extremely complex details, and users can increase the value of num_inference_steps to address this issue.

instruction: Can you identify the parties involved in this legal document?
description: LEGAL-BERT is a family of BERT models designed for the legal domain, intended to support legal NLP research, computational law, and legal technology applications. The models were pre-trained on 12 GB of diverse English legal text from various fields, including legislation, court cases, and contracts, collected from publicly available resources. Different sub-domain variants, such as CONTRACTS-BERT-BASE, EURLEX-BERT-BASE, and ECHR-BERT-BASE, as well as the general LEGAL-BERT-BASE, perform better than using BERT out of the box for domain-specific tasks. LEGAL-BERT-SMALL is a lightweight version of BERT-BASE, pre-trained from scratch on legal data, which is 33% the size of BERT-BASE. It achieves comparable performance to larger models while being approximately four times faster and having a smaller environmental footprint. The pre-training corpora include 116,062 documents of EU legislation from EURLEX, 61,826 documents of UK legislation, 19,867 cases from the European Court of Justice, 12,554 cases from the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR), 164,141 cases from various US courts, and 76,366 US contracts from EDGAR. LEGAL-BERT-BASE, also known as LEGAL-BERT-SC, was trained from scratch using a newly created vocabulary by a sentence-piece tokenizer trained on the same legal corpora. LEGAL-BERT-FP models, which rely on the original BERT-BASE checkpoint, have been released in Archive.org and are considered secondary. The models were trained using the official Google BERT code, with a setup of 1 million training steps, batches of 256 sequences of length 512, and an initial learning rate of 1e-4. Training was conducted on a single Google Cloud TPU v3-8. LEGAL-BERT variants have been evaluated on downstream tasks and are described in the paper 'LEGAL-BERT: The Muppets straight out of Law School' by Chalkidis et al. (2020).

instruction: How can I improve the sound quality of this voice memo recorded in a busy café?
description: DPDFNet is a family of causal, single-channel speech enhancement models designed for real-time noise suppression in challenging everyday environments. It extends the DeepFilterNet2 enhancement framework by incorporating Dual-Path RNN (DPRNN) blocks into the encoder, which enhances long-range temporal and cross-band modeling while maintaining a compact, streaming-friendly design. The models are optimized for deployment on mobile and edge devices using TensorFlow Lite (TFLite). The 16kHz models include baseline, dpdfnet2, dpdfnet4, and dpdfnet8, while a 48kHz model, dpdfnet2_48khz_hr, is available for fullband enhancement. These models are characterized by their causal and low-latency design, suitable for streaming applications such as telephony, conferencing, and embedded devices. The Dual-Path RNN integration improves temporal context and frequency-domain interactions, enabling more robust enhancement in difficult noise conditions. The model family is scalable, allowing users to choose between baseline and dpdfnet2/4/8 to balance quality and computational requirements. The models are intended for real-time, single-channel speech enhancement and are targeted for deployment on mobile devices, embedded NPUs, and edge platforms. The 16kHz models process 16kHz mono noisy speech waveforms and output 16kHz mono enhanced speech waveforms, while the 48kHz model processes and outputs 48kHz mono waveforms. Typical applications include voice calls, video conferencing, always-on voice interfaces, and audio devices such as wearables and earbuds. The models were trained using a mixture of public speech and noise datasets, including DNS4 (downsampled), MLS, MUSAN, and FSD50K. The baseline model has 2.31 million parameters, 0.36 gigaflops of multiply-accumulate operations, and a TFLite size of 8.5 MB. DPDFNet-2 has 2.49 million parameters, 1.35 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 10.7 MB. DPDFNet-4 has 2.84 million parameters, 2.36 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 12.9 MB. DPDFNet-8 has 3.54 million parameters, 4.37 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 17.2 MB. The 48kHz model, DPDFNet-2 HR, has 2.58 million parameters, 2.42 gigaflops, and a TFLite size of 11.6 MB. The models are distributed under the Apache-2.0 license.

instruction: Generate a voice-over for a travel documentary with a narrator and local interviewees, each with their own voice.
description: VibeVoice is a novel open-source text-to-speech model designed to generate expressive, long-form, multi-speaker conversational audio, such as podcasts, from text. It addresses challenges in traditional TTS systems, particularly in scalability, speaker consistency, and natural turn-taking. A core innovation is the use of continuous speech tokenizers (Acoustic and Semantic) operating at an ultra-low frame rate of 7.5 Hz, which preserve audio fidelity while improving computational efficiency for long sequences. VibeVoice employs a next-token diffusion framework, combining a Large Language Model (LLM) to understand textual context and dialogue flow with a diffusion head to generate high-fidelity acoustic details. The model can synthesize speech up to 90 minutes long with up to 4 distinct speakers, surpassing the typical 1-2 speaker limits of many prior models. The LLM used is Qwen2.5. The Acoustic Tokenizer is based on a σ-VAE variant with a mirror-symmetric encoder-decoder structure featuring 7 stages of modified Transformer blocks, achieving 3200x downsampling from 24kHz input. Encoder and decoder components are approximately 340M parameters each. The Semantic Tokenizer mirrors the Acoustic Tokenizer's architecture (without VAE components) and is trained with an ASR proxy task. The Diffusion Head is a lightweight module (4 layers, ~600M parameters) conditioned on LLM hidden states, predicting acoustic VAE features using a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) process. It uses Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and DPM-Solver during inference. The model is trained with a curriculum increasing up to 32,768 tokens. Training involves separate pre-training of the Acoustic and Semantic tokenizers, followed by training of the LLM and diffusion head with a curriculum learning strategy for input sequence length (4k -> 16K -> 32K). The model is intended for research purposes only and is not recommended for commercial or real-world applications without further testing and development. It is limited to English and Chinese data, and outputs in other languages may be unintelligible or offensive. The model does not handle background noise, music, or other sound effects, and does not generate overlapping speech segments in conversations. Potential risks include misuse for deepfakes, disinformation, or voice impersonation. To mitigate these risks, an audible disclaimer is embedded in every synthesized audio file, and an imperceptible watermark is added for verification. Inference requests are logged (hashed) for abuse pattern detection and publishing aggregated statistics quarterly. Users are responsible for sourcing datasets legally and ethically, including securing appropriate rights and anonymizing data prior to use with VibeVoice. The model is developed by members of Microsoft Research, and feedback or reports of issues can be sent to VibeVoice@microsoft.com.

instruction: What steps are needed to compress a speech sample for use in a mobile app?
description: Mimi codec is a state-of-the-art audio neural codec that combines semantic and acoustic information into audio tokens running at 12.5Hz and a bitrate of 1.1kbps. It is a high-fidelity audio codec leveraging neural networks, introducing a streaming encoder-decoder architecture with quantized latent space, trained in an end-to-end fashion. It was trained on speech data, which makes it particularly adapted to train speech language models or text-to-speech systems. Mimi can be used directly as an audio codec for real-time compression and decompression of speech signals, providing high-quality audio compression and efficient decoding. The model is not intended to be used to impersonate other people or for any malicious use of any kind. The model has been trained with a few safeguards to try to limit potential toxic usages, however its toxicity analysis shows that it behaves in the middle of existing models with respect to textual generation. It has some bias towards certain domains and topics that are over-represented in the training data. Its capabilities are relatively limited so far and it is trained to produce only one voice to avoid impersonation. Yet, we need the perspective in time to establish the sociotechnical limitations.

instruction: What could someone learn from looking at this picture?
description: BLIP-2 is a model that consists of three components: a CLIP-like image encoder, a Querying Transformer (Q-Former), and a large language model with 2.7 billion parameters. The image encoder and large language model are initialized from pre-trained checkpoints and remain frozen during training, while the Q-Former, a BERT-like Transformer encoder, is trained to map a set of 'query tokens' to query embeddings that bridge the embedding spaces of the image encoder and the language model. The model is designed to predict the next text token given the query embeddings and the previous text. It supports tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering (VQA), and chat-like conversations by providing the image and previous conversation as input. The model can be used for conditional text generation given an image and optional text. BLIP-2 uses an off-the-shelf OPT model as its language model, which inherits the same risks and limitations as described in Meta's model card, including potential biases, safety issues, and generation quality concerns. The model is fine-tuned on image-text datasets collected from the internet, which may result in the model generating inappropriate content or replicating biases present in the training data. It has not been tested in real-world applications and should not be directly deployed without careful evaluation of its safety and fairness in the intended context. This release is for research purposes only and is not designed or evaluated for all downstream applications. Users are advised to evaluate and address potential concerns related to accuracy, safety, and fairness before deployment. The model's memory requirements vary depending on the precision used, with options including float32, float16/bfloat16, int8, and int4. The model can be run on CPU or GPU, with different configurations for full, half, and 8-bit precision.
